topics.title: Fundamentals of Acoustics - General Types of Sound Wave Propagation
Linearized Wave Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Linearized Wave Equation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Linearized Wave Equation** (also called the **Acoustic Wave Equation**) is the fundamental governing partial differential equation that describes the propagation of small-amplitude pressure disturbances (sound waves) through a fluid medium. It is derived by linearizing the non-linear, three-dimensional equations of fluid dynamics (continuity, momentum, and state) about a quiescent (stationary) homogeneous mean state, retaining only first-order perturbation terms. In standard three-dimensional form, the linearized acoustic wave equation is expressed as: $$\nabla^2 p' - \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = 0$$ where: - $p'$ is the **acoustic (fluctuating) pressure** (Pa) - $c$ is the **speed of sound** in the medium (m/s) - $\nabla^2$ is the **Laplacian operator** - $t$ is time (s) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This equation is the cornerstone of NVH (Noise, Vibration and Harshness) analysis. It allows engineers to predict sound pressure levels in vehicle cabins, mufflers, exhaust systems, and acoustic enclosures using analytical and numerical methods like FEM/BEM. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Ripple-on-a-Pond Model Imagine dropping a small pebble into a perfectly still pond. The water was at rest, but the impact creates tiny ripples that travel outward in expanding circles. The pond's surface (mean state) remains essentially flat, and only small disturbances (ripples) move away from the source. The **linearized wave equation** mathematically describes this exact phenomenon for **sound in air**: - The **still pond** = the stationary fluid (air at rest) — this is our *mean state*. - The **ripple height** = the small pressure fluctuation $p'$ that your ear detects as sound. - The **speed at which ripples spread** = the speed of sound $c$ (≈ **343 m/s** in air at 20°C). - The assumption that the pebble is "small" = the **linearization** assumption, meaning disturbances are much smaller than the ambient atmospheric pressure. If you drop a boulder (huge disturbance), the water splashes chaotically and the simple ripple model breaks down — this is the **non-linear** regime, which is why we must *linearize* first. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall):** > - Speed of sound in air at 20°C: **c ≈ 343 m/s** > - Density of air at 20°C: **ρ₀ ≈ 1.204 kg/m³** > - Ratio of specific heats for air: **γ = 1.4** > - Atmospheric pressure (standard): **P₀ = 101,325 Pa** > - Universal gas constant: **R = 287 J/(kg·K)** for air ### 1.3 Why "Linearize"? The full Navier-Stokes equations governing fluid motion are **non-linear** (they contain products of unknowns like $v \cdot \nabla v$). Non-linear PDEs are extremely difficult to solve analytically. However, in most NVH problems (cabin noise, exhaust noise, duct acoustics), the acoustic pressure $p'$ is much smaller than the ambient pressure $P_0$ (typically by factors of $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$). This permits us to **discard higher-order small terms** and obtain a linear PDE — a process called *linearization*. Linear PDEs support the powerful **superposition principle**, allowing complex sound fields to be built from simple building blocks (plane waves, spherical waves). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Governing Fluid Dynamic Equations The derivation begins with three fundamental equations of fluid dynamics applied to a compressible, inviscid fluid. All are then decomposed into mean and perturbation components. #### 2.1.1 Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass) $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$ States that mass is neither created nor destroyed in a fluid element. The product $\rho \vec{v}$ is the **mass flux**. #### 2.1.2 Momentum Equation (Euler's Equation — Inviscid Form) $$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \vec{v} \right) = -\nabla p$$ Newton's second law applied to a fluid element. The non-linear term $\vec{v} \cdot \nabla \vec{v}$ is the source of the non-linearity that linearization removes. #### 2.1.3 State Equation (Equation of State / Constitutive Relation) For an **isentropic** (reversible, adiabatic) process: $$p = p(\rho)$$ For small disturbances, this is expanded via Taylor series to: $$p' = c^2 \rho'$$ where $c^2 = \left( \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial \rho} \right)_s$ is the **isentropic compressibility** — by definition, this is the *square* of the speed of sound. ### 2.2 The Six Assumptions of Linearization (KTU-Favorite!) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Favorite Question:** "State the assumptions made in deriving the linearized wave equation." | # | Assumption | Physical Meaning | |---|------------|------------------| | 1 | **No mean flow** | The fluid is initially at rest: $\vec{v}_0 = 0$ | | 2 | **Homogeneous medium** | Mean density $\rho_0$ is constant in space | | 3 | **Inviscid fluid** | Viscous (friction) effects neglected | | 4 | **No body forces** | Gravity and other body forces are negligible | | 5 | **Small perturbations** | $p' \ll p_0$, $\rho' \ll \rho_0$, $\vert \vec{v}' \vert \ll c$ | | 6 | **Isentropic process** | No heat transfer; reversible adiabatic process | ### 2.3 Perturbation Decomposition Each variable is split into a **steady mean (subscript 0)** plus a **small fluctuating (primed)** component: $$\rho = \rho_0 + \rho'$$ $$p = p_0 + p'$$ $$\vec{v} = \vec{v}_0 + \vec{v}'$$ For a fluid at rest: $\vec{v}_0 = 0$, $\nabla p_0 = 0$, $\dfrac{\partial p_0}{\partial t} = 0$, $\dfrac{\partial \rho_0}{\partial t} = 0$. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Equation / Formula | Symbolic Form | Use / Context | |--------------------|---------------|---------------| | General 3D Wave Equation | $\nabla^2 p' - \dfrac{1}{c^2} \dfrac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = 0$ | Master equation for acoustic pressure | | Speed of sound (general) | $c = \sqrt{\left( \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial \rho} \right)_s}$ | Thermodynamic definition | | Speed of sound (ideal gas) | $c = \sqrt{\gamma \, \dfrac{P_0}{\rho_0}}$ | For air, exhaust gases | | Speed of sound (ideal gas, T) | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | In terms of absolute temperature | | Linearized continuity | $\dfrac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \, \nabla \cdot \vec{v}' = 0$ | Mass conservation, 1st order | | Linearized momentum | $\rho_0 \dfrac{\partial \vec{v}'}{\partial t} = -\nabla p'$ | Euler equation, 1st order | | Linearized state (isentropic) | $p' = c^2 \rho'$ | Energy/state equation, 1st order | | Plane wave solution | $p'(x,t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$ | Forward + backward travelling waves | | Acoustic particle velocity (1D) | $v' = \dfrac{p'}{\rho_0 c}$ | Specific acoustic impedance relation | | 1D Wave Equation | $\dfrac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2} - \dfrac{1}{c^2} \dfrac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = 0$ | Simplified to one spatial dimension | > [!NOTE] > **Table Formatting Safeguard:** The absolute value notation has been replaced with $\vert \cdot \vert$ LaTeX syntax to preserve markdown table integrity, per engine protocol. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The linearized wave equation is the workhorse of: - **Automotive NVH:** Predicting cabin booming, road-noise transmission, and exhaust drone. - **Muffler/Silencer Design:** Wave equation in ducts with boundary conditions predicts Transmission Loss (TL). - **HVAC and Building Acoustics:** Predicting sound propagation in ventilation ducts and rooms. - **Loudspeaker Engineering:** Modeling sound radiation from vibrating surfaces. - **Ultrasonic NDT (Non-Destructive Testing):** Predicting wave propagation for flaw detection. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Complete Derivation: From Navier-Stokes to the Linearized Wave Equation This is the **highest-marks derivation** in Module 1. The full algebraic detail is given below. #### Step 1: Write the Governing Equations **Continuity Equation:** $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$ **Momentum (Euler) Equation:** $$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \vec{v} \right) = -\nabla p$$ **Isentropic State Equation (general form):** $$p = p(\rho) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad p = C \rho^{\gamma}$$ #### Step 2: Expand the Continuity Equation Using the Product Rule Apply the divergence product rule $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \rho$: $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho \, \nabla \cdot \vec{v} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \rho = 0$$ *Conversion Logic:* This expands the divergence of a product so each term contains either $\rho$ or $\vec{v}$ alone — preparing the equation for substitution of perturbation variables. #### Step 3: Decompose Each Variable into Mean + Perturbation Substitute $\rho = \rho_0 + \rho'$, $p = p_0 + p'$, $\vec{v} = \vec{v}_0 + \vec{v}'$. With $\vec{v}_0 = 0$, the mean flow is stationary. #### Step 4: Apply Perturbation to the Continuity Equation After substitution: $$\frac{\partial (\rho_0 + \rho')}{\partial t} + (\rho_0 + \rho') \, \nabla \cdot \vec{v}' + \vec{v}' \cdot \nabla (\rho_0 + \rho') = 0$$ Expand the products: $$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \, \nabla \cdot \vec{v}' + \rho' \, \nabla \cdot \vec{v}' + \vec{v}' \cdot \nabla \rho' = 0$$ *Conversion Logic:* The mean terms $\frac{\partial \rho_0}{\partial t}$ and $\vec{v}_0 \cdot \nabla \rho_0$ vanish because the mean state is steady and homogeneous. #### Step 5: Linearize — Drop the Second-Order Small Terms Identify second-order (product of two primes) terms: $\rho' \, \nabla \cdot \vec{v}' = O(\rho' v')$ and $\vec{v}' \cdot \nabla \rho' = O(v' \rho')$. These are products of two small quantities and are **negligible** compared to first-order terms: $$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \, \nabla \cdot \vec{v}' = 0 \quad \text{(Linearized Continuity — Eq. A)}$$ #### Step 6: Apply Perturbation to the Momentum Equation Substitute into the Euler equation: $$(\rho_0 + \rho') \left( \frac{\partial \vec{v}'}{\partial t} + \vec{v}' \cdot \nabla \vec{v}' \right) = -\nabla (p_0 + p')$$ Expand and recognize $\nabla p_0 = 0$ (homogeneous mean): $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial \vec{v}'}{\partial t} + \rho' \frac{\partial \vec{v}'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 (\vec{v}' \cdot \nabla \vec{v}') + \rho' (\vec{v}' \cdot \nabla \vec{v}') = -\nabla p'$$ #### Step 7: Linearize the Momentum Equation Drop all second-order terms: $\rho' \frac{\partial \vec{v}'}{\partial t}$, $\rho_0 (\vec{v}' \cdot \nabla \vec{v}')$, and $\rho' (\vec{v}' \cdot \nabla \vec{v}')$ are all second-order: $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial \vec{v}'}{\partial t} = -\nabla p' \quad \text{(Linearized Momentum — Eq. B)}$$ #### Step 8: Take the Divergence of Equation (B) Apply $\nabla \cdot$ to both sides: $$\nabla \cdot \left( \rho_0 \frac{\partial \vec{v}'}{\partial t} \right) = -\nabla \cdot \nabla p'$$ Since $\rho_0$ is constant (homogeneous medium), it can be pulled out: $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t} (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}') = -\nabla^2 p'$$ #### Step 9: Take the Time Derivative of Equation (A) Apply $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}$ to both sides of the linearized continuity equation: $$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t} (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}') = 0$$ *Conversion Logic:* This produces a term identical to the one in Step 8, allowing substitution. #### Step 10: Eliminate the Velocity Divergence Term From Step 8: $\rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t} (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}') = -\nabla^2 p'$ Substitute this into Step 9: $$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} - \nabla^2 p' = 0$$ Rearrange: $$\nabla^2 p' = \frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2}$$ #### Step 11: Apply the Linearized Isentropic State Equation For small isentropic perturbations, the state equation gives: $$p' = c^2 \rho' \quad \text{where} \quad c^2 = \left( \frac{\partial p}{\partial \rho} \right)_s$$ Invert to get $\rho' = \dfrac{p'}{c^2}$, then compute the second time derivative: $$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} = \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2}$$ #### Step 12: Final Assembly — The Linearized Wave Equation Substitute the expression for $\dfrac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2}$ from Step 11 into Step 10: $$\nabla^2 p' = \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2}$$ Bring all terms to one side: $$\boxed{\nabla^2 p' - \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = 0}$$ *Conversion Logic:* This is the desired 3D linearized acoustic wave equation. It is **second-order in both space and time**, linear (superposition applies), and propagates disturbances at speed $c$. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Speed of Sound for an Ideal Gas Starting from the ideal gas law: $p = \rho R T$. For an isentropic process: $p/\rho^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$. Differentiate implicitly: $$\frac{dp}{d\rho} = \frac{\gamma p}{\rho}$$ By definition, $c^2 = \dfrac{dp}{d\rho} \Big|_s$, so: $$c^2 = \frac{\gamma p_0}{\rho_0} \implies c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma p_0}{\rho_0}}$$ Since $p_0 = \rho_0 R T$ for an ideal gas: $$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example (1D Wave Equation) **Problem:** Show that $p'(x,t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$ satisfies the 1D wave equation. **Solution:** Compute the partial derivatives using the chain rule. Let $\xi = x - ct$ and $\eta = x + ct$: $$\frac{\partial p'}{\partial x} = f'(\xi) + g'(\eta)$$ $$\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2} = f''(\xi) + g''(\eta)$$ $$\frac{\partial p'}{\partial t} = -c f'(\xi) + c g'(\eta)$$ $$\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = c^2 f''(\xi) + c^2 g''(\eta) = c^2 [f''(\xi) + g''(\eta)]$$ Substitute into the 1D wave equation: $$\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2} - \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = [f''(\xi) + g''(\eta)] - \frac{1}{c^2} \cdot c^2 [f''(\xi) + g''(\eta)] = 0$$ Verified. The first term $f(x - ct)$ represents a wave travelling in the **+x direction**, and $g(x + ct)$ represents a wave travelling in the **−x direction**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Derivation Logic Chain The following diagram traces the logical flow from the fundamental fluid equations through linearization to the final wave equation. ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Step 1: Governing Equations

rho = rho0 + rho-prime, etc."] node4["Step 4: Drop Steady Terms
Use Homogeneity and Quiescence"] node5["Step 5: Linearize
Discard 2nd Order Terms"] node6A["Linearized Continuity Eq."] node6B["Linearized Momentum Eq."] node6C["Linearized State: p-prime = c-squared rho-prime"] node7["Step 6: Differentiate and Combine
Take Divergence of Momentum, Time Derivative of Continuity"] node8["Step 7: Substitute State Equation
Eliminate rho-prime"] node9["Final: Linearized Wave Equation
del-squared p-prime minus 1 over c-squared d2p-prime over dt2 equals 0"] node10["Physical Interpretation
Speed of Sound: c = sqrt gamma R T"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6A node5 --> node6B node5 --> node6C node6A --> node7 node6B --> node7 node6C --> node7 node7 --> node8 node8 --> node9 node9 --> node10 style node9 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 style node10 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Acoustic Wave Propagation System The linearized wave equation models a complete physical input-output system. The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the functional architecture of acoustic wave propagation in an NVH application (e.g., exhaust noise through a muffler). ```mermaid flowchart LR src["Source: Engine / Compressor

Muffler TL, Cabin Booming, Duct Noise"] src -->|Input Disturbance| prop prop -->|Subject to| bc prop --> fd bc --> out fd --> app out --> app style src fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px style prop fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px style app fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style fd fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Schematic: Three Domains of the Wave Equation ```mermaid flowchart TB td["Time Domain Form
General Wave Equation
del-squared p equals 1 over c-squared d2p over dt2"] fd["Frequency Domain Form

p-prime x t equals P x times e to power j omega t"] app["Engineering Use
Acoustic FEM, BEM, Modal Analysis"] td -->|Fourier Transform| hn hn --> fd fd --> app style td fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px style fd fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px style hn fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q:** Define the linearized wave equation. State its significance in NVH analysis. **Model Answer:** The linearized wave equation is a second-order linear partial differential equation governing the propagation of small-amplitude acoustic pressure disturbances $p'$ in a fluid medium. In three dimensions, it is: $$\nabla^2 p' - \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = 0$$ It is "linearized" because it is obtained by discarding second-order perturbation terms from the non-linear Navier-Stokes equations. **Significance in NVH:** It is the foundational equation for predicting sound propagation in vehicle cabins, exhaust mufflers, HVAC ducts, and acoustic enclosures. It enables analytical solutions, superposition, and numerical methods (FEM/BEM) used to control automotive noise, vibration, and harshness. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for NVH significance)* #### Question 2: Assumptions `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** List any six assumptions made in the derivation of the linearized wave equation. **Model Answer:** 1. **No mean flow** — the fluid is initially at rest ($\vec{v}_0 = 0$). 2. **Homogeneous medium** — the mean density $\rho_0$ is spatially uniform. 3. **Inviscid fluid** — viscous effects are neglected. 4. **No body forces** — gravity and other body forces are ignored. 5. **Small perturbations** — $p' \ll p_0$, $\rho' \ll \rho_0$, $\vert \vec{v}' \vert \ll c$. 6. **Isentropic process** — flow is reversible and adiabatic, with no heat transfer. *(3 marks: 0.5 marks per assumption)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]` **Q:** Derive the linearized wave equation for acoustic pressure propagation in a fluid medium, starting from the continuity, momentum (Euler), and state equations. State all assumptions clearly. ##### Part (a): State assumptions and write the three governing equations. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. The fluid is initially at rest (no mean flow): $\vec{v}_0 = 0$. 2. The medium is **homogeneous**: $\rho_0$ is constant in space. 3. The fluid is **inviscid**: viscous forces are neglected. 4. **No body forces** (gravity neglected). 5. The perturbations are **small**: $p' \ll p_0$, $\rho' \ll \rho_0$, $\vert \vec{v}' \vert \ll c$. 6. The process is **isentropic**: reversible and adiabatic. **Governing Equations:** *Continuity Equation:* $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$ *Euler (Momentum) Equation:* $$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \vec{v} \right) = -\nabla p$$ *State Equation:* $$p = p(\rho) \quad \text{(general isentropic form)}$$ *Conversion Logic:* The continuity equation enforces mass conservation, Euler's equation is Newton's second law for fluids, and the state equation couples pressure and density. *(7 marks: 2 for assumptions, 2 for continuity, 2 for Euler, 1 for state)* ##### Part (b): Perform the linearization and derive the final wave equation. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Expand the continuity equation using the product rule: $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \rho = 0$$ **Step 2:** Substitute perturbations: $\rho = \rho_0 + \rho'$, $p = p_0 + p'$, $\vec{v} = \vec{v}'$ (since $\vec{v}_0 = 0$). **Step 3:** Drop steady-state terms and second-order products to obtain the linearized continuity: $$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \, \nabla \cdot \vec{v}' = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$ **Step 4:** Linearize the Euler equation by dropping the non-linear convective term $\vec{v} \cdot \nabla \vec{v}$ and the product $\rho' \dfrac{\partial \vec{v}'}{\partial t}$: $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial \vec{v}'}{\partial t} = -\nabla p' \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 5:** Take the divergence of equation (ii) — since $\rho_0$ is constant: $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \cdot \vec{v}') = -\nabla^2 p' \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ **Step 6:** Differentiate equation (i) with respect to time: $$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \cdot \vec{v}') = 0 \quad \text{...(iv)}$$ **Step 7:** Substitute (iii) into (iv) to eliminate the velocity-divergence term: $$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} - \nabla^2 p' = 0$$ **Step 8:** Apply the linearized isentropic state relation $p' = c^2 \rho'$, so $\dfrac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} = \dfrac{1}{c^2} \dfrac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2}$. **Step 9:** Final substitution yields the **linearized wave equation**: $$\nabla^2 p' - \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = 0$$ *Conversion Logic:* The Laplacian in space balances the second time derivative — the signature of a wave propagating at speed $c$. *(7 marks: 1 per step, plus 1 for final boxed equation)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** (a) Derive the expression for the speed of sound in an ideal gas. (7 marks) (b) Starting from the 3D linearized wave equation, derive the 1D form and show that $p'(x,t) = f(x-ct) + g(x+ct)$ is a general solution. (7 marks) ##### Part (a): Speed of Sound Derivation (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Start with the ideal gas equation of state: $p = \rho R T$. **Step 2:** For an isentropic (reversible adiabatic) process, the Poisson relation holds: $$\frac{p}{\rho^{\gamma}} = \text{constant} \implies p = C \rho^{\gamma}$$ **Step 3:** Differentiate $p$ with respect to $\rho$ implicitly: $$\frac{dp}{d\rho} = C \gamma \rho^{\gamma - 1} = \frac{\gamma p}{\rho}$$ **Step 4:** By the thermodynamic definition of the speed of sound: $$c^2 = \left( \frac{\partial p}{\partial \rho} \right)_s = \frac{\gamma p_0}{\rho_0}$$ **Step 5:** Therefore: $$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma p_0}{\rho_0}} = \sqrt{\gamma R T}}$$ *Conversion Logic:* The second form uses $p_0 = \rho_0 R T$ (ideal gas law), making the speed of sound depend only on temperature for a given gas. *(7 marks: 1 for isentropic relation, 2 for differentiation, 2 for substitution of c-squared, 2 for final boxed answer)* ##### Part (b): 1D Reduction and General Solution (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Assume plane-wave propagation, so $p' = p'(x, t)$ only. The Laplacian reduces to: $$\nabla^2 p' = \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial z^2} \rightarrow \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2}$$ **Step 2:** The 1D wave equation becomes: $$\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2} - \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = 0$$ **Step 3:** Propose the solution $p'(x,t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$ and verify. Let $\xi = x - ct$, $\eta = x + ct$: $$\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2} = f''(\xi) + g''(\eta)$$ $$\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = c^2 f''(\xi) + c^2 g''(\eta)$$ **Step 4:** Substitute into the 1D wave equation: $$[f''(\xi) + g''(\eta)] - \frac{1}{c^2}[c^2 f''(\xi) + c^2 g''(\eta)] = 0 \quad \checkmark$$ *Conversion Logic:* $f(x - ct)$ is a forward-travelling wave at speed $c$; $g(x + ct)$ is a backward-travelling wave. The general solution is a superposition of both — a direct consequence of linearity. *(7 marks: 1 for 1D reduction, 2 for proposing solution, 2 for derivative evaluation, 2 for verification)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > - **Forgetting to state assumptions:** Examiners explicitly allocate 1–2 marks for the six linearization assumptions. Without them, expect a 2-mark penalty. > - **Keeping the non-linear term $\vec{v} \cdot \nabla \vec{v}$:** This is the #1 mistake. It MUST be dropped during linearization; the entire point is to obtain a linear PDE. > - **Confusing $c^2 = \partial p / \partial \rho$ with $c = \partial p / \partial \rho$:** The speed of sound is the *square root* of the compressibility, not the compressibility itself. > - **Forgetting the homogeneous medium condition ($\rho_0$ = constant):** Required to pull $\rho_0$ out of the divergence in Step 5 of the derivation. Marks deducted if not mentioned. > - **Sign errors in the final wave equation:** The standard form is $\nabla^2 p' - \dfrac{1}{c^2} \dfrac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = 0$ (positive Laplacian, negative time term). Reversed signs lose full credit. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Bullet Summary):** - **Master Equation:** $\nabla^2 p' - \dfrac{1}{c^2} \dfrac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = 0$ — memorize verbatim. - **6 Assumptions:** No mean flow, homogeneous, inviscid, no body forces, small perturbations, isentropic — *all six required for full marks.* - **Three Starting Equations:** Continuity, Euler (momentum), and isentropic state — *must be stated at the beginning of the derivation.* - **Linearization Trick:** Replace each variable with mean + primed, drop products of two primes, drop $\vec{v}_0$, drop $\nabla p_0$. - **State Equation Key:** $p' = c^2 \rho'$ — this is the *bridge* that couples pressure and density in the final step. - **Speed of Sound (Ideal Gas):** $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ — depends only on temperature; for air at 20°C, **c ≈ 343 m/s**. - **Speed of Sound (General):** $c = \sqrt{\gamma p_0 / \rho_0} = \sqrt{(\partial p / \partial \rho)_s}$. - **General 1D Solution:** $p'(x,t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$ — forward + backward waves. - **Acoustic Impedance Relation:** $v' = p' / (\rho_0 c)$ — links particle velocity to acoustic pressure. - **Helmholtz Form (Frequency Domain):** $\nabla^2 P + k^2 P = 0$, where $k = \omega / c$ is the wavenumber — used in FEM/BEM codes like COMSOL and LMS Virtual.Lab. - **NVH Applications:** Cabin noise, muffler design, exhaust drone, road noise transmission, HVAC duct acoustics. - **Key Differentiator:** Linear ⇒ Superposition holds ⇒ Complex problems decomposed into simpler wave components (plane, spherical, cylindrical).

topics.title: Acoustic Velocity Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Acoustic Velocity Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acoustic Velocity Potential (φ):** A scalar function of space and time whose spatial gradient yields the instantaneous acoustic particle velocity vector in an inviscid, compressible fluid medium undergoing small-amplitude (linear) disturbances. Mathematically, it is defined as:
>
> $$\vec{v} = \nabla \phi = -\nabla \psi$$
>
> where $\vec{v}$ is the acoustic particle velocity, and $\psi$ is often associated with the displacement potential in Lagrangian formulations.

The acoustic velocity potential is a fundamental construct in **linear acoustics** that transforms the vector wave equation (which has three spatial components) into a single scalar partial differential equation, vastly simplifying analytical and numerical treatment of sound fields.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Assumptions for the Validity of Velocity Potential:**
> - The fluid is **inviscid** (zero viscosity) and **homogeneous** at equilibrium.
> - The acoustic disturbances are **small amplitude** (linear regime, $|\rho - \rho_0| \ll \rho_0$).
> - The flow is **irrotational**, i.e., $\nabla \times \vec{v} = 0$.
> - Heat conduction effects are neglected (**adiabatic process**).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are looking at the surface of a calm pond. If you drop a small pebble, ripples spread outward in concentric circles. Now, instead of tracking every single water particle (a vector problem involving horizontal and vertical motion), suppose there exists an invisible "height map" — a single scalar value at every point on the pond's surface that, when tilted (gradient taken), tells you which direction and how fast the water is moving.

**The acoustic velocity potential is exactly this "height map" for sound waves in air.**

- The **value of $\phi$** at a point = the "height" of an invisible landscape of pressure waves.
- The **slope (gradient)** of that landscape = the direction and speed at which air particles oscillate.
- A **steeper slope** = stronger air particle motion = louder sound.

This is incredibly powerful because instead of solving three coupled equations (for x, y, z particle velocity components), you only need to solve **one equation** for the scalar $\phi$.

## 1.3 Why It Matters in NVH Engineering

In Noise, Vibration and Harshness (NVH) engineering, the velocity potential is the cornerstone for:
- Deriving **Green's functions** (used in Boundary Element Method - BEM).
- Solving **radiation problems** from vibrating surfaces (engine blocks, speaker cones).
- Modeling **interior cavity acoustics** (vehicle cabins, headphones).
- Predicting **far-field sound pressure** from near-field vibration data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard SI Units:**
> - Velocity potential $\phi$ → **m²/s**
> - Particle velocity $\vec{v}$ → **m/s**
> - Sound pressure $p$ → **Pa (N/m²)**
> - Density of air $\rho_0$ → **kg/m³** (≈ **1.21 kg/m³** at 20°C)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Irrotationality Condition — Foundation of the Potential

The existence of a velocity potential requires that the curl of the velocity field vanishes:

$$\nabla \times \vec{v} = 0$$

**Why this matters physically:** In a sound wave, the air particles do not rotate — they only oscillate back and forth. A parcel of air does not spin; it just translates. This is because sound wavelengths are orders of magnitude larger than the molecular mean free path, so viscous torques dominate locally and kill any rotation.

Using vector calculus identities, if $\nabla \times \vec{v} = 0$ and the domain is simply connected, there exists a scalar function $\phi$ such that:

$$\vec{v} = \nabla \phi$$

This is a direct consequence of **Helmholtz's Decomposition Theorem** (the irrotational component of any vector field can be written as the gradient of a scalar).

## 2.2 The Linearized Continuity Equation

Starting from the **conservation of mass** for a fluid element:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$

Linearizing by writing $\rho = \rho_0 + \rho'$ where $\rho' \ll \rho_0$ and ignoring second-order products $\rho' \vec{v}$:

$$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \, \nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

Substituting $\vec{v} = \nabla \phi$:

$$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \, \nabla^2 \phi = 0$$

## 2.3 The Linearized Momentum (Euler) Equation

The momentum conservation (Newton's second law per unit volume) gives:

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} = -\nabla p$$

Substituting $\vec{v} = \nabla \phi$:

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial (\nabla \phi)}{\partial t} = -\nabla p$$

Since space and time derivatives commute:

$$\nabla \left( \rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + p \right) = 0$$

This integrates to:

$$p = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + C(\vec{r})$$

For acoustic problems at rest, the integration constant $C = 0$ (no static pressure gradient in the potential), giving the **fundamental acoustic pressure-potential relation**:

$$\boxed{p = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This single equation is one of the most heavily tested relationships in KTU exams. The negative sign indicates that an **increase** in velocity potential over time corresponds to a **decrease** in pressure (rarefaction).

## 2.4 The Acoustic Wave Equation in Terms of φ

Combining the continuity and momentum equations with the adiabatic equation of state $p = c^2 \rho'$:

$$\nabla^2 \phi - \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

This is the **homogeneous scalar wave equation** for the velocity potential.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Domain of Use |
|---|----------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\vec{v} = \nabla \phi$ | Particle velocity is gradient of potential | All linear acoustics |
| 2 | $p = -\rho_0 \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$ | Pressure from time-derivative of potential | Time-domain and harmonic analysis |
| 3 | $\nabla^2 \phi = \dfrac{1}{c^2} \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2}$ | Scalar wave equation in φ | Free-field, source-free regions |
| 4 | $\nabla^2 \phi + k^2 \phi = 0$ | Helmholtz equation (time-harmonic, $e^{j\omega t}$) | Frequency-domain analysis |
| 5 | $k = \dfrac{\omega}{c} = \dfrac{2\pi f}{c}$ | Wave number relation | All harmonic problems |
| 6 | $\rho' = -\dfrac{\rho_0}{c^2} \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$ | Density fluctuation from potential | Compressible flow acoustics |
| 7 | $\vec{v} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \int \nabla p \, dt$ | Velocity from pressure gradient (general) | Alternative formulation |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = -\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}$ | Neumann boundary condition | Vibrating surface BC |
| 9 | $I = \langle p \cdot v_n \rangle$ | Acoustic intensity (time-averaged) | Energy analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on sign conventions:** KTU follows the engineering convention $e^{j\omega t}$ for time-harmonic fields. Always check whether the textbook uses $e^{j\omega t}$ or $e^{-i\omega t}$ before writing final phasor answers.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practical NVH applications, the velocity potential is the natural variable for:
- **Boundary Element Method (BEM):** Reduces a 3D volume problem to a 2D surface problem.
- **Near-field Acoustic Holography (NAH):** Reconstructs source distributions from measured φ on a hologram plane.
- **Loudspeaker design:** Predicting SPL and directivity using φ on a spherical measurement surface.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Euler's Equation to the Pressure-Potential Relation

We begin with the linearized Euler equation (Newton's second law applied to a fluid element, neglecting body forces and viscosity):

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} = -\nabla p$$

**Step 1:** Apply the irrotationality condition. Since the acoustic flow is irrotational:

$$\vec{v} = \nabla \phi$$

Substitute this into the left-hand side of the Euler equation:

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial (\nabla \phi)}{\partial t} = -\nabla p$$

**Step 2:** Swap the order of differentiation. Because $\nabla$ operates on spatial coordinates and $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}$ operates on time, these operators commute:

$$\rho_0 \, \nabla \left( \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \right) = -\nabla p$$

**Step 3:** Bring both terms to one side as a single gradient:

$$\nabla \left( \rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + p \right) = 0$$

**Step 4:** Integration. The gradient of a function equals zero everywhere if and only if the function is a constant throughout the domain. Thus:

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + p = C(\vec{r})$$

**Step 5:** Determine the integration constant. Far from any source, in the undisturbed fluid, $\phi$ is constant in time and $p$ equals the ambient pressure (which we typically take as the reference zero for acoustic pressure). Therefore, $C(\vec{r}) = 0$, and we get:

$$p = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:** This shows that the acoustic pressure is simply the time-rate-of-change of the velocity potential, scaled by the mean fluid density. The negative sign encodes the physical fact that *increasing* potential corresponds to *rarefaction* (decreased pressure).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: The Scalar Wave Equation for φ

We start with the **linearized continuity equation**:

$$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \, \nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $\vec{v} = \nabla \phi$:

$$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \, \nabla \cdot (\nabla \phi) = 0$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \phi) = \nabla^2 \phi$ (the Laplacian):

$$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \, \nabla^2 \phi = 0$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to time to obtain a second-order equation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} + \rho_0 \, \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla^2 \phi) = 0$$

Since $\nabla^2$ and $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}$ commute:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} + \rho_0 \, \nabla^2 \left( \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \right) = 0$$

**Step 4:** Use the equation of state for an adiabatic process: $p = c^2 \rho'$, and the pressure-potential relation: $p = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$. Equate them:

$$c^2 \rho' = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$$

Therefore:

$$\rho' = -\frac{\rho_0}{c^2} \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate twice with respect to time:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} = -\frac{\rho_0}{c^2} \frac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial t^3}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back into the differentiated continuity equation:

$$-\frac{\rho_0}{c^2} \frac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial t^3} + \rho_0 \, \nabla^2 \left( \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \right) = 0$$

**Step 7:** Divide by $\rho_0$ and rearrange:

$$\nabla^2 \left( \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \right) = \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial t^3}$$

**Step 8:** Integrate with respect to time (the integration "constant" is a spatially-dependent but time-independent function that vanishes in the absence of a mean flow):

$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** The acoustic velocity potential satisfies the **scalar wave equation** at the speed of sound $c$ in the medium.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Solutions in 1D, 2D, and 3D

| Geometry | General Solution Form | Physical Interpretation |
|----------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **1D plane wave** | $\phi(x,t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$ | Right- and left-traveling waves |
| **1D harmonic** | $\phi(x,t) = A e^{j(\omega t - kx)} + B e^{j(\omega t + kx)}$ | Standing wave by superposition |
| **2D cylindrical** | $\phi(r,\theta,t) = [A H_n^{(1)}(kr) + B H_n^{(2)}(kr)] e^{jn\theta} e^{j\omega t}$ | Line source radiation |
| **3D spherical** | $\phi(r,t) = \dfrac{A e^{j(\omega t - kr)}}{r} + \dfrac{B e^{j(\omega t + kr)}}{r}$ | Point monopole source |

> [!NOTE]
> The spherical solution $\phi \sim 1/r$ is the basis of the **monopole source** used in NVH to model vibrating panels, exhaust openings, and engine radiation in free field.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-style)

**Problem:** A harmonic spherical source radiates sound with velocity potential $\phi = \dfrac{A}{r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$ where $A = 0.01$ m²/s, $r = 2$ m, $\omega = 1000\pi$ rad/s, and $c = 343$ m/s. Find the acoustic pressure at $r = 2$ m.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the wave number:

$$k = \frac{\omega}{c} = \frac{1000\pi}{343} \approx 9.16 \text{ rad/m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the time-derivative of $\phi$ (for a harmonic signal, $\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \rightarrow j\omega$):

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = j\omega \cdot \frac{A}{r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)} = j(1000\pi) \cdot \frac{0.01}{2} \cdot e^{j(\omega t - 9.16 \cdot 2)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the pressure-potential relation:

$$p = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = -j(1000\pi)(1.21)\left(\frac{0.01}{2}\right) e^{j(\omega t - 18.32)}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the magnitude:

$$|p| = (1000\pi)(1.21)(0.005) \approx 19.0 \text{ Pa}$$

> [!TIP]
> The factor of $j$ simply indicates a 90° phase shift between pressure and velocity, which is characteristic of traveling waves (pressure and velocity are in phase for standing waves, out of phase by 90° for purely traveling waves in 1D).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Velocity Potential Framework

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **logical flow** of how the velocity potential ties together the fundamental governing equations of acoustics:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Assumption: Linear, Inviscid, Irrotational Flow] --> B[Fluid Element Governing Equations]
    B --> C1[Continuity Equation: Mass Conservation]
    B --> C2[Momentum Equation: Newtons Second Law]
    B --> C3[Energy Equation: Adiabatic State p = c^2 rho]
    C1 --> D[Apply Irrotationality: v = grad phi]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> E[Combine via Pressure-Potential Link]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Pressure from Potential: p = -rho0 dphi/dt]
    F --> G[Scalar Wave Equation: laplacian phi = 1 over c squared times d2phi/dt2]
    G --> H[Solution Methods]
    H --> H1[Analytical: 1D, 2D, 3D Basis Functions]
    H --> H2[Numerical: BEM, FEM]
    H --> H3[Experimental: NAH, Beamforming]
    H1 --> I[NVH Output: SPL, Directivity, Power]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: From Source to Far-Field Pressure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Vibrating Surface: Engine Block] --> BC[Boundary Condition: dphi/dn equals -v normal]
    BC --> WAVE[Wave Equation in Domain]
    WAVE --> RAD[Far-Field Radiation]
    RAD --> P[Acoustic Pressure: p = -rho0 dphi/dt]
    P --> SPL[Sound Pressure Level: SPL = 20 log10 p over p ref]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Gradient of Potential Yields Velocity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Scalar Field phi x y z t] --> B[Compute Gradient]
    B --> C[Vector Field: vx vy vz]
    C --> D[Physical Air Particle Motion]
    A2[Scalar phi] --> E[Time Derivative]
    E --> F[Pressure Field p = -rho0 dphi/dt]
    F --> G[Microphone Measurement]
```

## 4.4 Boundary Condition Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BC[Boundary Conditions on phi] --> B1[Dirichlet: phi equals specified]
    BC --> B2[Neumann: dphi/dn equals -v surface]
    BC --> B3[Robin: Mixed Impedance Type]
    BC --> B4[Sommerfeld Radiation: Outgoing wave at infinity]
    B2 --> B2A[Rigid Wall: dphi/dn = 0]
    B2 --> B2B[Prescribed Velocity: vibrating piston]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Neumann condition** $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = -v_n$ is the most common in NVH because most real sources (engine blocks, panels) are characterized by their normal surface velocity, measured by laser vibrometers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> Define acoustic velocity potential. Why is it a scalar function and not a vector?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The acoustic velocity potential $\phi(\vec{r}, t)$ is a scalar function whose spatial gradient equals the instantaneous acoustic particle velocity: $\vec{v} = \nabla \phi$.
- **Why scalar (1 mark):** Because in linear, inviscid, irrotational acoustic flow, the curl of velocity is zero ($\nabla \times \vec{v} = 0$). A vector field with zero curl in a simply connected region can be uniquely expressed as the gradient of a scalar.
- **Why useful (1 mark):** It reduces the three-component vector problem to a single scalar field, enabling the formulation of the simple scalar wave equation.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> Derive the relation between acoustic pressure $p$ and velocity potential $\phi$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Starting from the linearized Euler equation $\rho_0 \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} = -\nabla p$ **(1 mark)**
- Substitute $\vec{v} = \nabla \phi$: $\rho_0 \nabla \left( \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \right) = -\nabla p$ **(1 mark)**
- Integrating and setting the constant to zero: $\boxed{p = -\rho_0 \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}}$ **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

### (a) Starting from the fundamental governing equations of linear acoustics, derive the scalar wave equation for the velocity potential $\phi$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the linearized continuity equation:

$$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State the linearized Euler equation:

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} = -\nabla p$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Use the irrotational condition $\vec{v} = \nabla \phi$ in Euler's equation to get:

$$p = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Differentiate the continuity equation with respect to time:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla^2 \phi) = 0$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Use adiabatic equation of state: $p = c^2 \rho'$, and differentiate twice with time:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} = \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = -\frac{\rho_0}{c^2} \frac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial t^3}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Substitute and simplify to obtain:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** State physical interpretation: it is a wave equation showing that disturbances in $\phi$ propagate at the speed of sound $c$ in all directions.

### (b) For a 1D plane acoustic wave traveling in the +x direction, the velocity potential is given by $\phi(x,t) = A e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$. Compute the acoustic pressure, particle velocity, and the acoustic intensity. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Acoustic pressure. Using $p = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$:

$$p = -\rho_0 (j\omega) A e^{j(\omega t - kx)} = -j\omega \rho_0 A e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Particle velocity. Using $v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$:

$$v = (-jk) A e^{j(\omega t - kx)} = -jk A e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Specific acoustic impedance (ratio of p to v):

$$Z = \frac{p}{v} = \frac{-j\omega \rho_0}{-jk} = \frac{\omega \rho_0}{k} = \rho_0 c$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Time-averaged intensity for a plane wave:

$$I = \frac{|p|^2}{2 \rho_0 c} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_0 c |v|^2$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted — INTERNAL CHOICE]**

### (a) Explain the concept of acoustic velocity potential. What are the conditions under which it can be defined? List any four boundary conditions used in solving the potential wave equation. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 marks):** Acoustic velocity potential $\phi$ is a scalar function such that the particle velocity vector equals its spatial gradient. It allows acoustic fields to be described by a single scalar satisfying the wave equation.

**Conditions (2 marks):**
- Inviscid, homogeneous fluid
- Small-amplitude (linear) disturbances
- Irrotational flow ($\nabla \times \vec{v} = 0$)
- No mean flow (or, if present, uniform and steady)
- Adiabatic process (no heat exchange)

**Boundary Conditions (3 marks — any 4 of the following):**
- **Dirichlet:** $\phi = \phi_{specified}$ (prescribed potential on a surface)
- **Neumann (rigid wall):** $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = 0$
- **Neumann (prescribed velocity):** $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = -v_n$
- **Impedance (Robin):** $p = Z v_n$ → $-\rho_0 \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = -Z \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n}$
- **Sommerfeld Radiation:** $\lim_{r \to \infty} r \left( \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{c} \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \right) = 0$

### (b) A point source of acoustic monopole strength $Q$ (m³/s) radiates spherical waves. Write the velocity potential in free field, and hence derive expressions for the far-field sound pressure level at distance $r$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Velocity potential for a monopole:

$$\phi(r,t) = \frac{Q}{4\pi r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Pressure:

$$p = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = -j\omega \rho_0 \frac{Q}{4\pi r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Magnitude:

$$|p| = \frac{\omega \rho_0 Q}{4\pi r} = \frac{\rho_0 f Q}{2r}$$

(using $\omega = 2\pi f$)

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Sound Pressure Level:

$$\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{|p|}{p_{ref}} \right) = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{\rho_0 f Q}{2 r \, p_{ref}} \right)$$

where $p_{ref} = 20 \, \mu \text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa. The SPL falls off as $6$ dB per doubling of distance (1/r law).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Pressure Formula:** Forgetting the negative sign in $p = -\rho_0 \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$ is a **2-mark penalty** in most valuation schemes. Always derive and double-check.
> 2. **Confusing Eulerian vs Lagrangian:** The velocity potential is defined in the **Eulerian** frame (observer at a fixed point). Do not confuse with displacement potential, which is Lagrangian.
> 3. **Forgetting the Constant of Integration:** When integrating $\nabla(\cdot) = 0$, the constant may not be zero if there is a hydrostatic pressure gradient. In standard atmospheric acoustics, ambient pressure variation with height is **neglected**.
> 4. **Mixing Up Time Conventions:** $e^{j\omega t}$ vs $e^{-i\omega t}$ — verify with the question's stem. Wrong choice gives wrong sign in $j\omega$ operations.
> 5. **Incomplete Boundary Conditions:** For a vibrating surface, you must specify whether the normal velocity is zero (rigid) or prescribed (Neumann). KTU examiners **specifically check** for this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [x] Velocity potential $\phi$ is **scalar**, defined such that $\vec{v} = \nabla \phi$.
- [x] Valid only under **linear, inviscid, irrotational** assumptions.
- [x] Pressure link: $p = -\rho_0 \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$.
- [x] Density link: $\rho' = -\dfrac{\rho_0}{c^2} \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$.
- [x] Wave equation: $\nabla^2 \phi = \dfrac{1}{c^2} \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2}$.
- [x] Helmholtz form: $\nabla^2 \phi + k^2 \phi = 0$ (for $e^{j\omega t}$).
- [x] Wave number: $k = \dfrac{\omega}{c} = \dfrac{2\pi f}{c}$.
- [x] Monopole solution: $\phi = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$.
- [x] Plane wave: $\phi = A e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$; impedance $Z = \rho_0 c$.
- [x] Boundary conditions: Dirichlet, Neumann, Robin (impedance), Sommerfeld.
- [x] Units: $[\phi] = $ m²/s, $[p] = $ Pa, $[v] = $ m/s.
- [x] Density of air at 20°C: $\rho_0 = 1.21$ kg/m³.
- [x] Speed of sound in air: $c = 343$ m/s (at 20°C).
- [x] Reference pressure: $p_{ref} = 20 \, \mu$Pa.

> **One-line takeaway for the exam hall:**
> *"Velocity potential is the scalar that, when gradient-ed, gives velocity; when time-differentiated (with a minus and $\rho_0$), gives pressure; and when wave-equation-ed, propagates at speed of sound."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propagation of Sound Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Propagation of Sound Waves # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Sound** is a mechanical longitudinal wave that propagates through an elastic medium by means of particle-to-particle interaction, transmitting energy via alternating compressions and rarefactions of the medium. In the context of Noise, Vibration and Harshness (NVH) engineering, **propagation of sound waves** refers to the systematic transmission of acoustic energy from a source to a receiver through a defined medium (solid, liquid, or gas), governed by the fundamental wave equation of continuum mechanics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > Sound is a *pressure wave* — a local pressure disturbance that propagates at the **speed of sound (c)** through a medium. It requires a material medium for transmission; sound cannot travel through a perfect vacuum. The acoustic wave is characterized by three primary parameters: | Parameter | Symbol | SI Unit | Description | |-----------|--------|---------|-------------| | Frequency | $f$ | **Hz (Hertz)** | Number of pressure oscillations per second | | Wavelength | $\lambda$ | **m (metre)** | Spatial distance of one complete cycle | | Speed of Sound | $c$ | **m/s** | Velocity of wavefront propagation in medium | | Period | $T$ | **s (second)** | Time for one complete oscillation | | Sound Pressure | $p$ | **Pa (Pascal)** | Instantaneous pressure deviation from ambient | | Sound Intensity | $I$ | **W/m²** | Acoustic energy flux per unit area | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Domino Effect" 🎯 Imagine a long row of dominoes standing on a table. If you push the **first domino** gently and release it, only that single domino falls. But if you **strike it with energy**, it falls onto the second, the second onto the third, and so on. A *pattern of disturbance* travels down the entire row, even though **no individual domino travels** the full length of the row. Sound behaves identically: - **Air molecules** = dominoes (they oscillate back and forth locally) - **The first push** = the source of vibration (engine block, speaker cone, tyre-road interaction) - **The travelling pattern** = the sound wave carrying energy outward - **No molecule travels** with the wave — they only vibrate around their mean position > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in NVH Engineering** > In an automobile, the engine creates a vibration (disturbance). The vibration propagates through the engine mounts (solid), into the chassis structure (solid), radiates as sound into the cabin air (gas), and is heard as *noise* by the passenger. The same wave-propagation physics governs every step of this energy journey. ## 1.3 The Two Faces of Sound Waves Sound waves manifest in two fundamental configurations in the context of NVH: **1. Longitudinal Waves** (dominant in fluids — air, water, oil) - Particle vibration is **parallel** to the direction of wave propagation - Compressions (high-pressure zones) and rarefactions (low-pressure zones) alternate - Example: Speech, engine exhaust noise, tyre roar transmitted through cabin air **2. Transverse Waves** (dominant in solids and on surfaces) - Particle vibration is **perpendicular** to the direction of wave propagation - Occur in solid media due to shear stiffness - Example: Flexural vibrations of a car body panel, bending waves in a chassis rail > [!TIP] > **Quick Memory Hook:** **L**ongitudinal = **L**ungs (air — parallel to flow); **T**ransverse = **T**wisted string (perpendicular oscillation). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics of Wave Propagation — Step-by-Step A sound wave is generated when a surface or volume undergoes a *local pressure fluctuation*. The propagation mechanism unfolds as follows: 1. **Disturbance Generation** — A vibrating surface (e.g., loudspeaker diaphragm, engine block face) imparts a small displacement to adjacent air molecules. 2. **Compression Phase** — These molecules are pushed inward, raising the local pressure above ambient (compression). They then collide with neighbouring molecules, transferring momentum. 3. **Rarefaction Phase** — As the source surface moves back, the previously compressed region experiences a pressure drop below ambient (rarefaction). 4. **Wavefront Advancement** — This compression-rarefaction pair propagates outward at the local **speed of sound ($c$)**. 5. **Energy Transport** — Although each molecule oscillates only by a microscopic amount (nanometres for sound), the *energy* associated with this motion is transmitted across the entire field. ## 2.2 Fundamental Relationships — The Acoustic Trinity The three pillars of wave kinematics are linked by the master relation: $$c = f \lambda = \frac{\lambda}{T}$$ This single equation, along with its companions, governs *every* acoustic propagation problem in NVH. ## 2.3 Speed of Sound in Different Media The speed of sound depends on the **bulk modulus ($K$)** and the **density ($\rho$)** of the medium: $$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}$$ For gases, this is more conveniently expressed via the adiabatic relationship: $$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$ where $\gamma$ is the ratio of specific heats, $R$ is the specific gas constant, and $T$ is the absolute temperature (in Kelvin). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these formulas — they form the spine of every Module-1 numerical problem.** | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | SI Units | |---|---------|---------------|----------| | 1 | $c = f \lambda$ | Wave equation (universal) | m/s | | 2 | $T = 1/f$ | Period-frequency relation | s | | 3 | $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ | Speed of sound (general medium) | m/s | | 4 | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Speed of sound in ideal gas | m/s | | 5 | $c_{\text{air}} \approx 331.4 + 0.6\,T_c$ | Empirical formula (air, $T_c$ in °C) | m/s | | 6 | $I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c}$ | Sound intensity (plane wave) | W/m² | | 7 | $\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level | dB | | 8 | $\text{SIL} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | Sound Intensity Level | dB | | 9 | $\text{SWL} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | Sound Power Level | dB | | 10 | $I \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}$ | Inverse-square law (spherical wave) | W/m² | | 11 | $p \propto \frac{1}{r}$ | Pressure decay (spherical wave) | Pa | | 12 | $f' = f \cdot \frac{c \pm v_{\text{obs}}}{c \mp v_{\text{src}}}$ | Doppler effect (frequency shift) | Hz | > **Reference Constants (Must be Memorized):** > - $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{Pa}$ > - $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\,\text{W/m}^2$ > - $W_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\,\text{W}$ > - $\gamma_{\text{air}} = 1.4$, $R_{\text{air}} = 287\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$ ## 2.5 Wave Propagation Patterns **(a) Plane Wave** — Pressure amplitude remains constant with distance. Occurs in waveguides, ducts, and far-field of a large source. $$p(x,t) = p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ **(b) Spherical Wave** — Radiates outward from a point source. Pressure decays as $1/r$, intensity as $1/r^2$. $$p(r,t) = \frac{A}{r}\cos(\omega t - kr)$$ where $A$ is a source-strength constant and $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the wavenumber. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility — Why Automobile Designers Care** > At low frequencies, automotive components act as point sources producing spherical waves (engine noise in the cabin). At high frequencies inside a duct (e.g., intake manifold), plane-wave assumptions give accurate predictions. The correct geometric model determines whether your silencer/muffler design will be effective. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The One-Dimensional Wave Equation We begin with the three fundamental principles of continuum mechanics applied to a fluid element of length $dx$ and cross-section $A$: **Step 1 — Continuity (Conservation of Mass):** A pressure increase causes the element to compress. The fractional change in density is: $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$$ where $u$ is the particle velocity. **Step 2 — Equation of State (Linearised, adiabatic):** For small pressure perturbations $p \ll P_0$: $$\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -K \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$$ **Step 3 — Newton's Second Law (Momentum Equation):** The pressure gradient accelerates the fluid: $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$ **Step 4 — Eliminate Particle Velocity:** Differentiate Step 3 with respect to $x$: $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x \partial t} = -\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2}$$ Substitute $\partial^2 u / (\partial x \partial t)$ from Step 2's time derivative: $$-\frac{\rho_0}{K}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = -\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2}$$ **Step 5 — Final Wave Equation:** $$\boxed{\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}, \qquad c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho_0}}}$$ This is the **one-dimensional acoustic wave equation** — the foundation of all NVH propagation analysis. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Speed of Sound in an Ideal Gas Starting from the general speed-of-sound formula: $$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}$$ For an ideal gas undergoing an *adiabatic* (no heat transfer) process, the bulk modulus equals: $$K = \gamma P$$ where $P$ is the absolute static pressure and $\gamma = c_p/c_v$. Substituting: $$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}}$$ Using the ideal-gas equation of state $P = \rho R T$: $$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \rho R T}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}}$$ **Numerical Plug-In (Air at 20°C = 293 K):** $$c = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 293} = \sqrt{117{,}763} \approx 343.2\,\text{m/s}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Inverse-Square Law Consider a point source radiating acoustic power $W$ uniformly in all directions. At distance $r$, the power is distributed over the surface of a sphere of area $4\pi r^2$: $$I(r) = \frac{W}{4\pi r^2}$$ **Hence the intensity scales as the inverse square of the distance:** $$\frac{I_2}{I_1} = \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^{2}$$ Since $I \propto p^2$ (from $I = p_{\text{rms}}^2/(\rho c)$): $$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{r_1}{r_2}$$ **dB form — Doubling the distance from a point source reduces SPL by 6 dB:** $$\Delta \text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -6\,\text{dB}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Exam Insight:** A 6-dB drop per doubling of distance is a *favourite* KTU question. The 6 dB rule applies to **point sources (spherical waves)**. For a **line source (cylindrical wave)**, the rule becomes 3 dB per doubling. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Doppler Effect in Sound The Doppler effect describes the change in observed frequency due to relative motion between source and observer. **Case A — Moving Source, Stationary Observer:** Each successive wavefront is emitted from a slightly closer (or further) position. The wavelength effectively changes to: $$\lambda' = \lambda - v_s T = \frac{c - v_s}{f}$$ The observed frequency is: $$f' = \frac{c}{\lambda'} = \frac{c \cdot f}{c - v_s} = f \cdot \frac{c}{c - v_s}$$ > *Approaching source ($v_s$ positive towards observer) raises the observed frequency.* **Case B — Moving Observer, Stationary Source:** The observer encounters wavefronts at a faster (or slower) rate: $$f' = \frac{c + v_o}{\lambda} = f \cdot \frac{c + v_o}{c}$$ > *Approaching observer ($v_o$ positive towards source) raises the observed frequency.* **Unified Doppler Formula:** $$\boxed{f' = f \cdot \frac{c \pm v_{\text{obs}}}{c \mp v_{\text{src}}}}$$ *Sign convention: upper signs = approach; lower signs = recede.* **Sample Calculation:** A car horn emits $f = 800\,\text{Hz}$. The car approaches a stationary observer at $v_s = 30\,\text{m/s}$. Find the observed frequency. Take $c = 340\,\text{m/s}$. $$f' = 800 \cdot \frac{340}{340 - 30} = 800 \cdot \frac{340}{310} \approx 877.4\,\text{Hz}$$ ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Relationship Between SPL, SIL, and SWL For a plane progressive wave, the intensity and pressure are linked by: $$I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c}$$ Taking logarithms: $$\log I = 2 \log p_{\text{rms}} - \log(\rho c)$$ In dB form (referenced to standard values): $$\text{SIL} = \text{SPL} + 10 \log_{10}(\rho c) - 10 \log_{10}(I_{\text{ref}}/p_{\text{ref}}^{2})$$ For air at standard conditions ($\rho c \approx 406$ rayls, also called the **acoustic impedance** $Z_0$): $$\text{SIL} \approx \text{SPL} - 0.16\,\text{dB} \approx \text{SPL}$$ This confirms the **practical equivalence of SPL and SIL in air** for most engineering work. The acoustic impedance of the medium, $Z_0 = \rho c$, is a key NVH property: $$Z_0 = \rho c \;\;[\text{kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}] = [\text{rayl}]$$ | Medium | Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | Speed $c$ (m/s) | $Z_0$ (rayl) | |--------|------------------------|------------------|---------------| | Air (20°C) | 1.204 | 343 | 413 | | Water (20°C) | 998 | 1480 | 1,477,000 | | Steel | 7850 | 5000 | 39,250,000 | > [!TIP] > **Mass Law Insight:** The enormous impedance mismatch between air and steel (~10,000×) is why sound does not easily transmit from a vibrating steel panel into the cabin air. This is the physical basis for **NVH countermeasures** like damping sheets, constrained-layer damping, and acoustic absorbers. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Sound Wave Propagation Pipeline in an NVH Context The following block architecture illustrates how an acoustic disturbance travels from a physical source to the human receiver — a concept foundational to all NVH problem-solving in automotive systems. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source
Vibrating Surface] --> B[Medium Coupling
Solid or Fluid] B --> C[Wavefront Generation
Compression and Rarefaction] C --> D[Propagation Path
Direct Reflected Refracted] D --> E[Receiver
Sensor Ear or Microphone] E --> F[Signal Processing
SPL SIL or SWL] F --> G[Perception
Loudness Pitch and Tone] H[Temperature T] -.-> C I[Humidity RH] -.-> C J[Atmospheric Pressure P] -.-> C ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **solid arrows** (A → B → C → D → E → F → G) trace the *forward path* of acoustic energy. - The **dotted arrows** (H, I, J → C) represent the *environmental parameters* that modulate the speed and attenuation of sound in real-world conditions. ## 4.2 Schematic — Longitudinal Wave Structure The diagram below depicts the spatial alternation of compressions and rarefactions in a longitudinal sound wave — the very essence of sound propagation in air. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph WAVE[Longitudinal Wave Cross Section] direction LR N1[Node 1
Equilibrium] --> C1[Compression
High Pressure] C1 --> N2[Node 2
Equilibrium] N2 --> R1[Rarefaction
Low Pressure] R1 --> N3[Node 3
Equilibrium] N3 --> C2[Compression
High Pressure] C2 --> N4[Node 4
Equilibrium] end DIR[Direction of Wave Propagation] --> WAVE PD[Particle Displacement
Parallel to Propagation] -.-> WAVE ``` **Interpretation:** - **C-zones** = compressions (molecules bunched together, pressure > ambient) - **R-zones** = rarefactions (molecules spread apart, pressure < ambient) - One full Compression + Rarefaction = **one wavelength $\lambda$** - The wave moves *rightward*, but each individual molecule only oscillates *left-right* around its mean position ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Energy Decay in Spherical Propagation ```mermaid flowchart TB S[Point Source
Acoustic Power W] --> R1[Distance r1
Intensity I1 = W over 4 pi r1 squared] R1 --> R2[Distance r2 equals 2 r1
Intensity I2 = I1 over 4] R2 --> R3[Distance r3 equals 4 r1
Intensity I3 = I1 over 16] R3 --> R4[Distance r4 equals 8 r1
Intensity I4 = I1 over 64] S -.->|SPL at r1| SPL1[SPL1] R2 -.->|SPL at r2 equals SPL1 minus 6 dB| SPL2[SPL2] R3 -.->|SPL at r3 equals SPL1 minus 12 dB| SPL3[SPL3] R4 -.->|SPL at r4 equals SPL1 minus 18 dB| SPL4[SPL4] ``` **Key takeaway:** The SPL of a point source drops by **6 dB per doubling of distance**. This is a critical design rule for placing microphones, locating passengers relative to noise sources, and estimating cabin noise from external engine/tyre sources. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** ### Q1. Define the term *acoustic wavelength* and state its relationship with frequency and speed of sound. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **acoustic wavelength ($\lambda$)** is defined as the spatial distance over which a sound wave completes one full cycle of pressure variation — that is, the distance between two successive compressions or two successive rarefactions. It is measured in **metres (m)**. The relationship with frequency $f$ (in Hz) and speed of sound $c$ (in m/s) is given by: $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad c = f \lambda$$ **Significance (½ mark for application):** At a fixed speed of sound, low-frequency sounds have long wavelengths (e.g., 20 Hz → 17.2 m in air), while high-frequency sounds have short wavelengths (e.g., 20 kHz → 0.017 m). This wavelength-to-geometry ratio determines whether sound *diffracts* around obstacles or is *shadowed* by them — a critical consideration in NVH packaging of vehicle components. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** ### Q2. State the inverse-square law for sound propagation. What is its significance in automotive NVH measurement? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **inverse-square law** states that the acoustic intensity $I$ of a point source in a free field varies inversely as the square of the distance $r$ from the source: $$I \propto \frac{1}{r^2} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{I_2}{I_1} = \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^{2}$$ **Significance in NVH (1 mark):** This law implies that SPL drops by **6 dB** for every doubling of distance from a point source. In automotive testing, it allows engineers to: - Predict interior cabin noise from exterior measurements - Correct microphone data acquired at non-standard distances - Estimate the noise exposure of a passenger relative to a known source (engine, road, exhaust) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 1]** #### (a) Derive the one-dimensional wave equation for sound propagation in a fluid medium. State clearly all assumptions. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. The fluid is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and at rest in the absence of sound. 2. The acoustic disturbance is **small** (linear acoustics): $p \ll P_0$, $\rho' \ll \rho_0$, particle velocity $u \ll c$. 3. The process is **adiabatic** (no heat conduction between adjacent fluid elements). 4. The medium has **no viscosity** (inviscid fluid). **Derivation (5 marks):** Consider a fluid element of cross-section $A$ and length $dx$. Let $P_0, \rho_0$ be the undisturbed pressure and density. After disturbance, they become $P_0 + p$ and $\rho_0 + \rho'$. **Step 1 — Continuity Equation:** The net mass flow into the control volume per unit time must equal the rate of change of mass: $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Linearised Momentum (Euler's) Equation:** $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$ **Step 3 — Linearised Equation of State (adiabatic):** $$p = c^2 \rho' \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = c^2 \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$ **Step 4 — Eliminate Density and Velocity:** Substitute (1) into (3): $$\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -c^2 \rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$$ Differentiate with respect to $x$: $$\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x \partial t} = -c^2 \rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$ Differentiate the momentum equation (2) with respect to $t$: $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = -\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x \partial t}$$ Combine the two results: $$\rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$ **Final Wave Equation:** $$\boxed{\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}, \quad \text{where } c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho_0}}}$$ The general solution is a superposition of two travelling waves: $$p(x,t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$$ representing waves propagating in the +$x$ and −$x$ directions, respectively. --- #### (b) A machine in a factory radiates acoustic power uniformly in all directions. The sound pressure level measured at a distance of 5 m from the machine is 92 dB. Determine: (i) the sound pressure level at 20 m, and (ii) the acoustic power radiated by the machine. Assume free-field conditions. (7 marks) **Given:** SPL at $r_1 = 5\,\text{m}$ is $L_{p1} = 92\,\text{dB}$, $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$. **Step 1 — Find Sound Pressure at $r_1 = 5$ m (1 mark):** $$p_1 = p_{\text{ref}} \times 10^{L_{p1}/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{92/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 39{,}810.7$$ $$p_1 \approx 0.7962\,\text{Pa}$$ **Step 2 — Find Sound Pressure at $r_2 = 20$ m Using Inverse-Square Law (2 marks):** $$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{r_1}{r_2} = \frac{5}{20} = \frac{1}{4}$$ $$p_2 = \frac{p_1}{4} = \frac{0.7962}{4} \approx 0.1991\,\text{Pa}$$ **Step 3 — Compute SPL at 20 m (1 mark):** $$L_{p2} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_2}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.1991}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(9954.2)$$ $$L_{p2} \approx 20 \times 3.998 = 79.96 \approx 80\,\text{dB}$$ **Alternative check (1 mark):** Using the 6-dB per doubling rule — distance increased by factor 4 (= $2^2$), so SPL drops by $2 \times 6 = 12\,\text{dB}$. Hence $L_{p2} = 92 - 12 = 80\,\text{dB}$ ✓ **Step 4 — Find Acoustic Power (2 marks):** Using $I_1 = p_1^2 / (\rho c)$ with $\rho c = 413\,\text{rayl}$ (air): $$I_1 = \frac{(0.7962)^2}{413} = \frac{0.6339}{413} \approx 1.535 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{W/m}^2$$ Acoustic power: $$W = I_1 \times 4\pi r_1^2 = 1.535 \times 10^{-3} \times 4\pi \times 25$$ $$W \approx 0.4822\,\text{W}$$ **Sound Power Level (extra credit):** $$L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(4.822 \times 10^{11}) \approx 116.8\,\text{dB}$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) SPL at 20 m = **80 dB** - (ii) Acoustic power = **≈ 0.48 W** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]** #### (a) With a neat sketch, explain the formation of a longitudinal sound wave. Define the terms: compression, rarefaction, and wavelength. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Sketch (3 marks) —** A horizontal series of alternating closely-spaced and widely-spaced dots representing air molecules, with a horizontal arrow showing the direction of wave propagation. Each cluster of closely-packed molecules is labelled "**Compression (C)**" and each spread-out region is labelled "**Rarefaction (R)**". A double-headed arrow above the figure shows one complete wavelength $\lambda$. **Definition of Compression (1 mark):** A **compression** is a region in a sound wave where the air molecules are pushed closer together than their equilibrium spacing, producing a **local pressure higher than the ambient atmospheric pressure**. It is sometimes called a *condensation*. **Definition of Rarefaction (1 mark):** A **rarefaction** is a region in a sound wave where the air molecules are spread further apart than their equilibrium spacing, producing a **local pressure lower than the ambient atmospheric pressure**. **Definition of Wavelength (1 mark):** The **wavelength $\lambda$** is the distance measured along the direction of propagation between two successive points that are in the same phase — for example, from one compression to the next compression, or from one rarefaction to the next. It is the spatial period of the wave. **Formation Explanation (1 mark):** A vibrating source (such as a tuning fork prong or an engine piston surface) moves alternately towards and away from the surrounding air. During the **forward stroke**, it compresses the adjacent air — creating a compression that travels outward. During the **backward stroke**, it leaves a partial vacuum — creating a rarefaction. The continuous oscillation produces a train of compressions and rarefactions propagating outward, which constitutes a longitudinal sound wave. The particle motion is **parallel** to the propagation direction. --- #### (b) An observer standing on a road hears a car horn that emits a steady 600 Hz tone. (i) Calculate the frequency heard when the car approaches the observer at 72 km/h. (ii) Recalculate the frequency when the car passes by and recedes at the same speed. (iii) What is the *beat frequency* between the approaching and receding cases? Take the speed of sound as 340 m/s. (7 marks) **Given:** $f = 600\,\text{Hz}$, $v_s = 72\,\text{km/h} = 20\,\text{m/s}$, $c = 340\,\text{m/s}$. **Part (i) — Approaching (2 marks):** $$f_{\text{app}} = f \cdot \frac{c}{c - v_s} = 600 \times \frac{340}{340 - 20} = 600 \times \frac{340}{320}$$ $$f_{\text{app}} = 600 \times 1.0625 = 637.5\,\text{Hz}$$ **Part (ii) — Receding (2 marks):** $$f_{\text{rec}} = f \cdot \frac{c}{c + v_s} = 600 \times \frac{340}{340 + 20} = 600 \times \frac{340}{360}$$ $$f_{\text{rec}} = 600 \times 0.9444 = 566.67\,\text{Hz}$$ **Part (iii) — Beat Frequency (1 mark):** $$f_{\text{beat}} = \vert f_{\text{app}} - f_{\text{rec}} \vert = \vert 637.5 - 566.67 \vert = 70.83\,\text{Hz}$$ **Verification (1 mark):** The beat frequency can also be expressed as: $$f_{\text{beat}} = \frac{2 f v_s c}{c^2 - v_s^2} = \frac{2 \times 600 \times 20 \times 340}{(340)^2 - (20)^2} = \frac{8{,}160{,}000}{115{,}200 - 400} = \frac{8{,}160{,}000}{114{,}800} \approx 71.08\,\text{Hz} \;\checkmark$$ **Engineering Note (1 mark):** This phenomenon — a sudden drop in pitch as a vehicle passes — is highly familiar in everyday life. The total *pitch shift* (637.5 − 566.67 ≈ 70.8 Hz) corresponds to roughly 1.1 semitones in musical terms. NVH engineers exploit Doppler-based measurements to estimate the speed of passing vehicles and to validate the acoustic models of their own vehicle's noise signature. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks in this topic:** > > 1. **Confusing dB formulas** — using $20 \log$ for intensity (use $10 \log$) or $10 \log$ for pressure (use $20 \log$). Memorize: *power-like quantities use 10; field quantities (pressure, voltage) use 20.* > 2. **Forgetting the 6-dB vs 3-dB rule** — 6 dB per doubling applies to *point sources*; 3 dB per doubling applies to *line sources*. Always state the assumption. > 3. **Wrong sign in Doppler formula** — use upper signs for *approach* and lower signs for *recede* in both numerator and denominator. Many students flip signs inconsistently. > 4. **Mixing Celsius and Kelvin** — the formula $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ requires $T$ in **Kelvin**. Converting: $T_K = T_C + 273.15$. > 5. **Using $p_0$ instead of $p_{\text{rms}}$** — the intensity formula $I = p^2/(\rho c)$ requires **RMS** pressure, not peak. For a sine wave, $p_{\text{rms}} = p_{\text{peak}}/\sqrt{2}$. > 6. **Skipping the units** — KTU examiners award a half-mark just for correct unit mention. Always end a numerical answer with the unit. --- ## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Propagation of Sound Waves** - ✅ Sound is a **mechanical longitudinal wave** requiring a material medium. - ✅ Master equation: $c = f \lambda$.
