All wave problems begin here. - ✅ Speed of sound in **air** ≈ $331 + 0.6\,T_c\,\text{m/s}$; in general, $c = \sqrt{K/\rho} = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ for ideal gases. - ✅ At 20°C, $c_{\text{air}} \approx 343\,\text{m/s}$ — memorize this benchmark. - ✅ **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** uses $20 \log_{10}(p/p_{\text{ref}})$ with $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$. - ✅ **Sound Intensity Level (SIL)** and **Sound Power Level (SWL)** use $10 \log_{10}$ with $I_{\text{ref}} = W_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}$. - ✅ **Inverse-square law:** $I \propto 1/r^2$, $p \propto 1/r$. Doubling distance → −6 dB for a point source. - ✅ **Acoustic impedance** $Z_0 = \rho c$ controls transmission/reflection at boundaries; high mismatch (steel ↔ air) blocks sound. - ✅ **Longitudinal** waves dominate in fluids (air, water); **transverse** waves require shear stiffness (solids only). - ✅ **Doppler formula:** $f' = f \cdot (c \pm v_{\text{obs}})/(c \mp v_{\text{src}})$ — approach gives higher $f'$. - ✅ Spherical waves decay as $1/r$; plane waves maintain amplitude — choose the right model based on geometry. - ✅ Atmospheric absorption, reflection, refraction, and diffraction all modify the simple inverse-square prediction in real NVH scenarios.

topics.title: Sound Intensity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sound Intensity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sound Intensity ($I$)** is formally defined as the *time-averaged rate of sound energy transmitted through a unit area normal to the direction of sound wave propagation*.

$$I = \frac{dE}{dA \cdot dt} \quad \left[ \text{W/m}^2 \right]$$

It is a **vector quantity**, possessing both magnitude and direction. The direction of the intensity vector points in the direction of sound energy flow (the direction of wave propagation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Sound Intensity is the acoustic counterpart of "luminous intensity" in optics. It describes the *flow* of acoustic energy, whereas Sound Pressure describes the *local fluctuation* of atmospheric pressure. Never confuse the two — they are linked mathematically but are fundamentally different physical concepts.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine standing in a heavy rain shower while holding a square bucket:
- The **rain falling into the bucket per second** = the *sound energy* passing through an area per second.
- The **area of the bucket opening** = the *unit area* (1 m²) over which we measure.
- The **total amount of rain collected per square meter per second** = exactly analogous to **Sound Intensity** (W/m²).

If the wind blows sideways and the rain is now slanting, the *amount of rain* falling straight down into your bucket decreases — even though the rain is still falling heavily. This is precisely why **sound intensity is direction-dependent** (vectorial), while **sound pressure is scalar** and does not depend on direction.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Values

> [!NOTE]
> - **Reference Intensity ($I_{ref}$)** = $10^{-12}$ W/m² (= 1 pW/m²)
> - **Reference Pressure ($p_{ref}$)** = $20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ (= $2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa)
> - These reference values correspond approximately to the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz.

**Key Insight:** The human ear responds over an enormous range of intensities — from $10^{-12}$ W/m² (just audible) up to $10^2$ W/m² (pain threshold). That is a ratio of $10^{14}$ : 1! Because of this vast span, we use logarithmic scales (decibels).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Sound Intensity Works

**Step 1 — Energy Transport in a Sound Wave**
A sound wave transports mechanical energy through a medium via oscillating particles. The instantaneous intensity is the product of instantaneous acoustic pressure and instantaneous particle velocity.

$$I(t) = p(t) \cdot u(t)$$

**Step 2 — Time-Averaging**
Because both $p(t)$ and $u(t)$ are sinusoidal, their instantaneous product oscillates. The physically meaningful quantity is the *time-average over one full period* ($T$):

$$I = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T p(t)\cdot u(t)\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Plane-Wave Simplification**
For a **plane progressive wave** (far-field, free-field condition), the pressure and particle velocity are **in phase**, and their amplitudes are linked by the *characteristic acoustic impedance* of the medium:

$$Z_0 = \rho \cdot c \quad \left[ \text{N·s/m}^3 \text{ or Rayls} \right]$$

where $\rho$ = medium density (kg/m³) and $c$ = speed of sound (m/s). For air at 20 °C: $\rho \approx 1.21$ kg/m³, $c \approx 343$ m/s, giving $Z_0 \approx 415$ Rayls.

**Step 4 — Intensity from Pressure**
Combining these, the time-averaged intensity for a plane wave becomes:

$$I = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho c} = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{Z_0}$$

**Step 5 — Intensity Level (dB)**
Converting to a logarithmic scale:

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{ref}}\right) \quad [\text{dB re } 10^{-12}\text{ W/m}^2]$$

**Step 6 — Pressure–Intensity Level Link**
In a plane wave, $I \propto p^2$, so the level conversion becomes:

$$L_I = L_p \quad \text{(in dB, for plane waves in free field)}$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Sound Intensity | $I = \dfrac{dE}{dA \cdot dt}$ | W/m² | Vector quantity |
| 2 | Instantaneous Intensity | $I(t) = p(t) \cdot u(t)$ | W/m² | Time-domain product |
| 3 | Plane-Wave Intensity | $I = \dfrac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho c}$ | W/m² | Far-field / free-field |
| 4 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z_0 = \rho c$ | Rayls (N·s/m³) | Air ≈ 415 Rayls |
| 5 | Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{ref}}\right)$ | dB | $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W/m² |
| 6 | Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}}\right)$ | dB | $p_{ref} = 20\ \mu$Pa |
| 7 | Level Equivalence | $L_I = L_p$ | dB | Only for plane waves |
| 8 | Inverse: Intensity from Level | $I = I_{ref} \cdot 10^{L_I/10}$ | W/m² | Useful in calculations |
| 9 | Inverse: Pressure from Level | $p_{rms} = p_{ref} \cdot 10^{L_p/20}$ | Pa | Useful in calculations |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap:** Do NOT write $L_I = 20\log(I/I_{ref})$. The 20-prefix belongs to *pressure* (because $p \propto \sqrt{I}$). Intensity is a power-like quantity, so the multiplier is **10**, not 20. This is a very common 1-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Noise Source Identification (Beamforming):** Intensity probes (p–u probes) pinpoint dominant noise sources on machinery, cars, and transformers.
- **Sound Power Determination:** $L_W = L_I + 10\log_{10}(S)$, where $S$ is the measurement surface area — a standard ISO 9614 method.
- **Building Acoustics:** Quantifying flanking sound transmission through walls.
- **Occupational Health:** Mapping worker exposure zones in industrial plants (relevant to OSHA/ISO 1999 standards).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Sound Intensity from Pressure and Particle Velocity

We start from the linearized one-dimensional momentum (Euler) equation for a fluid with small perturbations:

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$

**Step 1 — Assume a Harmonic Plane Wave Solution**

Pressure and velocity are both sinusoidal, propagating in $+x$ direction:

$$p(x,t) = \hat{p} \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

$$u(x,t) = \hat{u} \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

where $\hat{p}$ and $\hat{u}$ are the peak amplitudes, $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency, and $k = \omega/c$ is the wave number.

**Step 2 — Differentiate the Pressure Waveform with Respect to $x$**

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \hat{p} \cdot k \sin(\omega t - kx)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the Velocity Waveform with Respect to $t$**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\hat{u}\,\omega \sin(\omega t - kx)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Linearized Momentum Equation**

$$\rho_0 \bigl(-\hat{u}\,\omega \sin(\omega t - kx)\bigr) = -\hat{p}\,k \sin(\omega t - kx)$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the Common Sinusoidal Term**

Both sides share the factor $\sin(\omega t - kx)$, so we can divide it out (it is non-zero for a propagating wave):

$$\rho_0\,\hat{u}\,\omega = \hat{p}\,k$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $k = \omega/c$ and Simplify**

$$\rho_0\,\hat{u}\,\omega = \hat{p}\,\frac{\omega}{c} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \hat{p} = \rho_0 c\,\hat{u}$$

This confirms the **specific acoustic impedance relation** $\hat{p}/\hat{u} = Z_0 = \rho_0 c$.

**Step 7 — Compute Instantaneous Intensity**

$$I(t) = p(t)\cdot u(t) = \hat{p}\cos(\omega t - kx)\cdot \hat{u}\cos(\omega t - kx) = \hat{p}\hat{u}\cos^2(\omega t - kx)$$

**Step 8 — Time-Average Over One Period**

Using the identity $\langle \cos^2(\theta) \rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}$:

$$I = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \hat{p}\hat{u}\cos^2(\omega t - kx)\,dt = \frac{\hat{p}\hat{u}}{2}$$

**Step 9 — Convert Peak Amplitudes to RMS Values**

Since $p_{rms} = \hat{p}/\sqrt{2}$ and $u_{rms} = \hat{u}/\sqrt{2}$:

$$I = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,p_{rms}\cdot \sqrt{2}\,u_{rms}}{2} = p_{rms}\,u_{rms}$$

**Step 10 — Apply the Impedance Relation $u_{rms} = p_{rms}/(\rho_0 c)$**

$$I = p_{rms}\cdot \frac{p_{rms}}{\rho_0 c} = \frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 c} \quad \blacksquare$$

This is the **master plane-wave intensity formula** tested repeatedly in KTU exams.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Equivalence of Intensity Level and Pressure Level for a Plane Wave

**Step 1 — Start from the Plane-Wave Intensity Relation**

$$I = \frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 c}$$

**Step 2 — Write Intensity Level**

$$L_I = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{ref}}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 c \cdot I_{ref}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Write Pressure Level**

$$L_p = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{p_{ref}^{\,2}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Show They Differ Only by a Constant Offset**

$$L_I = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{p_{ref}^{\,2}}\right) + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{ref}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 c \cdot I_{ref}}\right)$$

The second term is a *constant*:

$$K = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{ref}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 c \cdot I_{ref}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Constant $K$ for Air**

$$K = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{(2\times 10^{-5})^2}{415 \times 10^{-12}}\right) = 10\log_{10}(0.9639) \approx -0.16\ \text{dB} \approx 0\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 6 — Conclude the Equivalence**

Because $K \approx 0$ dB in air under standard conditions:

$$L_I \approx L_p \quad \text{(in free field, plane wave)} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – June 2024 model]:** A plane sound wave in air has an RMS pressure of $0.1$ Pa. Calculate: (a) the sound intensity, (b) the intensity level, and (c) the pressure level. Use $\rho c = 415$ Rayls.

**Solution:**

**(a) Intensity:**

$$I = \frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho c} = \frac{(0.1)^2}{415} = \frac{0.01}{415} = 2.41 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{W/m}^2$$

**(b) Intensity Level:**

$$L_I = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.41 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10\log_{10}(2.41 \times 10^{7}) = 10(7 + 0.382) = 73.82\ \text{dB}$$

**(c) Pressure Level:**

$$L_p = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.1}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = 20\log_{10}(5000) = 20(3.699) = 73.98\ \text{dB}$$

Notice that $L_I \approx L_p \approx 74$ dB, confirming the equivalence for a plane wave in air.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map of Sound Intensity (Knowledge Graph)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Sound Source] --> B[Acoustic Pressure p]
    A --> C[Particle Velocity u]
    B --> D[Instantaneous Intensity I of t]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Time Averaging over T]
    E --> F[Sound Intensity I]
    F --> G[Characteristic Impedance rho c]
    G --> F
    F --> H[Intensity Level L I in dB]
    H --> I[Reference I ref 10 power minus 12 W per m2]
    B --> J[Pressure Level L p in dB]
    J --> K[Reference p ref 20 micro Pa]
    F --> L[Real World Use: Source Identification]
    F --> M[Real World Use: Sound Power ISO 9614]
    F --> N[Real World Use: Industrial Hygiene]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Measurement Chain of a Sound Intensity Probe

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Acoustic Field under test] --> mic1[Microphone A face]
    srcA --> mic2[Microphone B face]
    mic1 --> preA[Preamplifier A]
    mic2 --> preB[Preamplifier B]
    preA --> proc[Two Channel Signal Processor]
    preB --> proc
    proc --> filt[Bandpass Filter 1 over 3 octave]
    filt --> cross[Cross Spectrum p star omega u omega]
    cross --> div[Divide by rho c and surface]
    div --> out[Intensity I and L I Output]
    out --> display[Display in dB re 1 pW per m2]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Relationship Between Pressure, Intensity, and Power

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Acoustic Energy at Source] --> wave[Sound Wave Propagation]
    wave --> pres[Sound Pressure p at point]
    wave --> vel[Particle Velocity u at point]
    pres --> intensity[Sound Intensity I equals p times u averaged]
    vel --> intensity
    intensity --> level[Intensity Level L I in dB]
    level --> power[Sound Power L W equals L I plus 10 log S]
    power --> rating[ISO 9614 Noise Rating]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define sound intensity. State its SI unit. How does it differ from sound pressure?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Sound intensity ($I$) is defined as the *time-averaged rate of sound energy flowing through a unit area normal to the direction of propagation*. **SI unit: W/m²** (1 mark). It is a **vector quantity** with direction along the wave propagation, while sound pressure is a **scalar** quantity (local pressure fluctuation, in Pa) (1 mark). Intensity is a flow/flux quantity describing energy transport, whereas pressure is a state variable at a point (1 mark).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Write the equation for Sound Intensity Level and mention the reference intensity value used.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Sound Intensity Level is given by

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{ref}}\right) \quad [\text{dB}]$$

**Reference intensity: $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W/m² (= 1 pW/m²)** (2 marks for equation + 1 mark for reference value).

---

### Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between sound intensity and sound pressure for a plane progressive wave in a free field. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine radiates sound. At a point 2 m away in the free field, the measured RMS sound pressure is $0.5$ Pa. Calculate:
- (i) the sound intensity at that point,
- (ii) the sound intensity level in dB,
- (iii) the sound pressure level in dB.

Take $\rho c = 415$ Rayls and $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W/m². **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Assumptions: (1) free-field plane progressive wave, (2) harmonic motion, (3) linear, lossless medium, (4) air at standard conditions.

Starting from the linearized momentum equation:

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$

For plane waves $p(x,t) = \hat{p}\cos(\omega t - kx)$ and $u(x,t) = \hat{u}\cos(\omega t - kx)$:

Step 1: Differentiate both:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \hat{p} k \sin(\omega t - kx)$$

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\hat{u}\,\omega \sin(\omega t - kx)$$

Step 2: Substitute into momentum equation and cancel $\sin(\omega t - kx)$:

$$\rho_0\,\hat{u}\,\omega = \hat{p}\,k \quad \text{where } k = \omega/c$$

Step 3: Hence $\hat{p} = \rho_0 c\,\hat{u}$ (1.5 marks)

Step 4: Instantaneous intensity $I(t) = p \cdot u = \hat{p}\hat{u}\cos^2(\omega t - kx)$ (1 mark)

Step 5: Time-average: $I = \hat{p}\hat{u}/2 = p_{rms} \cdot u_{rms}$ (1.5 marks)

Step 6: Using $u_{rms} = p_{rms}/(\rho_0 c)$:

$$I = \frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 c} \quad (7\ \text{marks complete})$$

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

(i) Intensity:

$$I = \frac{(0.5)^2}{415} = \frac{0.25}{415} = 6.024 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{W/m}^2 \quad (2.5\ \text{marks})$$

(ii) Intensity Level:

$$L_I = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{6.024 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10\log_{10}(6.024 \times 10^8) = 10(8.7797) = 87.80\ \text{dB} \quad (2.5\ \text{marks})$$

(iii) Pressure Level:

$$L_p = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.5}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = 20\log_{10}(25000) = 20(4.398) = 87.96\ \text{dB} \quad (2\ \text{marks})$$

Note: $L_I \approx L_p$ (within 0.16 dB), confirming plane-wave equivalence.

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Intensity Level and Pressure Level. With neat derivations, prove that for a plane wave in a free field, $L_I = L_p$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A workshop has a Sound Intensity Level of 90 dB. Find:
- (i) the actual sound intensity in W/m²,
- (ii) the RMS sound pressure in air (assume plane wave, $\rho c = 415$ Rayls),
- (iii) the peak sound pressure $\hat{p}$.

Take $p_{ref} = 20\ \mu$Pa and $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W/m². **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**
Intensity Level $L_I$ is the logarithmic measure of sound intensity relative to the reference intensity $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W/m² (1.5 marks).
Pressure Level $L_p$ is the logarithmic measure of RMS sound pressure relative to $p_{ref} = 20\ \mu$Pa (1.5 marks).

Formulas:

$$L_I = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{ref}}\right), \qquad L_p = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}}\right)$$

For a plane wave: $I = p_{rms}^{\,2}/(\rho_0 c)$ (1 mark).
Substituting:

$$L_I = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 c \cdot I_{ref}}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{p_{ref}^{\,2}}\right) + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{ref}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 c \cdot I_{ref}}\right)$$

The second term evaluates to:

$$10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{(2 \times 10^{-5})^2}{415 \times 10^{-12}}\right) = 10\log_{10}(0.9639) \approx -0.16\ \text{dB} \approx 0\ \text{dB} \quad (2\ \text{marks})$$

Therefore $L_I \approx L_p$ for plane waves in air (1 mark).

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

(i) Intensity from $L_I = 90$ dB:

$$I = I_{ref} \cdot 10^{L_I/10} = 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{9} = 10^{-3}\ \text{W/m}^2 = 1\ \text{mW/m}^2 \quad (2.5\ \text{marks})$$

(ii) RMS Pressure:

$$p_{rms} = \sqrt{I \cdot \rho c} = \sqrt{10^{-3} \times 415} = \sqrt{0.415} = 0.6442\ \text{Pa} \quad (2.5\ \text{marks})$$

(iii) Peak Pressure:

$$\hat{p} = \sqrt{2}\,p_{rms} = 1.414 \times 0.6442 = 0.911\ \text{Pa} \quad (2\ \text{marks})$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT use 20 log for intensity.** Many students mistakenly write $L_I = 20\log(I/I_{ref})$. Lose 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse peak $\hat{p}$ with RMS $p_{rms}$.** Pressure level uses *RMS*, not peak. Mistaking them gives errors of $\sqrt{2}$ (~41% error) — heavy marks deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert log arguments** (e.g., leaving the ratio as $0.5/2 \times 10^{-5}$ without simplifying to 25000) costs full method marks.
> 4. **Assuming $L_I = L_p$ always** is wrong — the equivalence holds only for plane (free-field) waves. In a reverberant field or near field, they differ. State the condition explicitly.
> 5. **No unit in final answer = 0.5 mark cut.** Always write "W/m²", "Pa", or "dB".

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sound Intensity $I$** = sound energy flux per unit area, vector quantity, unit **W/m²**.
- **Intensity Level** $L_I = 10\log(I/I_{ref})$, with $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W/m².
- **Pressure Level** $L_p = 20\log(p_{rms}/p_{ref})$, with $p_{ref} = 20\ \mu$Pa.
- For a **plane progressive wave** in a free field: $I = p_{rms}^{\,2}/(\rho_0 c)$ and $L_I = L_p$.
- **Characteristic acoustic impedance** $Z_0 = \rho_0 c$ ≈ **415 Rayls** for air at 20 °C.
- **Instantaneous intensity** $I(t) = p(t) \cdot u(t)$ → time-average gives the meaningful intensity.
- **Peak vs RMS**: $\hat{p} = \sqrt{2}\,p_{rms}$; $p_{rms} = \hat{p}/\sqrt{2}$.
- **Inverse formulas**: $I = I_{ref}\cdot 10^{L_I/10}$, $p_{rms} = p_{ref}\cdot 10^{L_p/20}$.
- **Plane-wave assumption** must be stated explicitly in derivations to earn full marks.
- **Application pillars**: noise source identification, ISO 9614 sound power, occupational noise mapping.
- **Common log rule**: every 10 dB rise in level = 10× intensity; every 20 dB rise = 10× pressure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Acoustic Energy Density** is defined as the total instantaneous sound energy contained per unit volume of a medium through which a sound wave is propagating. It is the sum of the **kinetic energy density** (due to particle motion) and the **potential energy density** (due to local compressions and rarefactions) of the fluid elements in the acoustic field.

Mathematically, the total instantaneous acoustic energy density is expressed as:

$$w(x,t) = w_k(x,t) + w_p(x,t)$$

where:
- $w_k$ = Kinetic energy per unit volume (J/m³)
- $w_p$ = Potential (acoustic strain) energy per unit volume (J/m³)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of NVH, energy density serves as the foundational link between microscopic particle motion (velocity, displacement) and macroscopic measurable quantities (sound pressure, sound intensity). It is the bridge connecting **vibration mechanics** and **acoustic radiation**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Plain English Explanation:**

Imagine a stadium crowd doing "the wave." At any instant, only some people are standing up (kinetic energy — they are moving), while the compressed crowd between standing sections stores elastic potential energy (like a spring being squeezed). 

Similarly, in a sound wave:
- **Air molecules in motion** carry **kinetic energy** (just like the standing people in the wave).
- **Compressed or rarefied air parcels** store **potential energy** (just like the squeezed sections of the crowd).
- **Energy density** simply asks: "How much of this combined acoustic energy is packed into every cubic meter of air?"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Water Waves:**
> Think of ocean waves. The water particles oscillate up-and-down (kinetic energy), while the elevated water surface stores gravitational potential energy. The energy density tells you how energetic the wave is at each location. A stormy sea has **high energy density**; a calm pond has **low energy density**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard reference values are used throughout NVH analysis:

| Constant | Symbol | Value (Air at 20°C) | Unit |
|----------|--------|---------------------|------|
| Speed of sound | $c$ | **343** | m/s |
| Density of air | $\rho_0$ | **1.204** | kg/m³ |
| Characteristic acoustic impedance of air | $\rho_0 c$ | **413** | Pa·s/m (Rayl) |
| Reference sound pressure | $p_{ref}$ | **20** | µPa |
| Reference sound intensity | $I_{ref}$ | **10⁻¹²** | W/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **characteristic acoustic impedance** $\rho_0 c$ is THE most important constant in NVH because it directly couples particle velocity to acoustic pressure: $p = \rho_0 c \, v$ for plane waves.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

### Step 1: Decompose the Total Energy

A sound wave traveling through a fluid medium carries energy in two forms simultaneously:

- **Kinetic Energy Density ($w_k$):** Arises because the fluid particles oscillate back and forth with a finite velocity $v$. By classical mechanics, anything with mass and velocity carries kinetic energy.

$$w_k = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 v^2$$

- **Potential Energy Density ($w_p$):** Arises because sound waves involve local compressions and rarefactions, which deviate the local pressure from the ambient value. This pressure deviation does work on the fluid, just as a compressed spring stores energy.

$$w_p = \frac{p^2}{2\rho_0 c^2}$$

### Step 2: Why Divide by $\rho_0 c^2$ in the Potential Term?

This arises from the **adiabatic bulk modulus** of the fluid, $B = \rho_0 c^2$. The factor $\rho_0 c^2$ is a measure of the medium's "stiffness" — how much pressure is needed to compress it. A stiffer medium stores more potential energy for the same pressure deviation, which is why dividing by it gives the *energy stored per unit volume*.

### Step 3: The Total Energy Density

Summing both contributions for a plane progressive wave gives:

$$w = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 v^2 + \frac{p^2}{2\rho_0 c^2}$$

### Step 4: Time-Averaging Over One Period

Since acoustic quantities are time-varying, we are usually interested in the **time-averaged (mean) energy density** $\bar{w}$. For a sinusoidal wave, the average of $\cos^2$ over one period is $\frac{1}{2}$:

$$\bar{w} = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho_0 c^2} = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0^2$$

### Step 5: The Intensity Connection

Sound intensity $I$ (power per unit area) is the rate at which energy crosses a unit area. For a plane wave traveling at speed $c$:

$$I = \bar{w} \cdot c = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho_0 c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This simple relationship $I = \bar{w} c$ is why sound intensity meters and sound pressure meters give correlated (but not identical) readings. The factor $c \approx 343$ m/s is what makes intensity numerically much smaller than energy density.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Instantaneous kinetic energy density | $w_k = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 v^2$ | J/m³ | Depends on particle velocity |
| 2 | Instantaneous potential energy density | $w_p = \frac{p^2}{2\rho_0 c^2}$ | J/m³ | Depends on acoustic pressure |
| 3 | Instantaneous total energy density | $w = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 v^2 + \frac{p^2}{2\rho_0 c^2}$ | J/m³ | Sum of both |
| 4 | Time-averaged energy density (plane wave) | $\bar{w} = \dfrac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho_0 c^2}$ | J/m³ | Most used form |
| 5 | Time-averaged energy density (alternative) | $\bar{w} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho_0 v_{rms}^2$ | J/m³ | In terms of velocity |
| 6 | Energy density in terms of displacement amplitude | $\bar{w} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0^2$ | J/m³ | For sinusoidal waves |
| 7 | Sound intensity | $I = \bar{w} \cdot c$ | W/m² | Energy flux |
| 8 | Sound intensity in terms of pressure | $I = \dfrac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho_0 c}$ | W/m² | Used in sound level meters |
| 9 | Specific acoustic impedance | $Z_s = \dfrac{p}{v} = \rho_0 c$ | Pa·s/m | Plane wave value |
| 10 | Total energy of vibrating SDOF system | $E = \frac{1}{2}k x_0^2 = \frac{1}{2}m \omega^2 x_0^2$ | J | Max total energy |
| 11 | Energy density in structural vibration | $w_{struct} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho A^2 \omega^2$ | J/m³ | Per unit volume of plate/beam |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Sanity Check (KTU 2024):** Energy density has units of J/m³. Always carry units through your derivations — KTU examiners award partial credit for correct dimensional analysis even if the final number is wrong.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Energy Density |
|-------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Automotive NVH** | Quantifying cabin noise from road and engine excitation |
| **Aeroacoustics** | Designing quieter aircraft engines and fans |
| **Architectural Acoustics** | Sizing absorbers in concert halls and offices |
| **Structural Dynamics** | Predicting fatigue failure from vibration energy |
| **Loudspeaker Design** | Matching acoustic impedance to maximize energy transfer |
| **Hearing Conservation** | Computing safe exposure levels using energy-based metrics (LEQ) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Relevance:** Today's NVH engineers use **Statistical Energy Analysis (SEA)**, which works entirely with energy density as its primary variable, because it is conserved across subsystem boundaries and is valid at high frequencies where wavelength is much smaller than structure size.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Acoustic Energy Density for a Plane Progressive Sound Wave

### Given (Assumptions for KTU Standard Derivation)
- A plane progressive harmonic sound wave traveling in the $+x$ direction in a lossless fluid medium.
- Ambient density: $\rho_0$ (kg/m³)
- Speed of sound: $c$ (m/s)
- Angular frequency: $\omega$ (rad/s)
- Wave number: $k = \omega / c$ (rad/m)

### Step 1: Express the acoustic pressure

The acoustic (excess) pressure is sinusoidal in both space and time:

$$p(x,t) = p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

where $p_0$ is the pressure amplitude (Pa).

### Step 2: Express the particle displacement

The particle displacement $\xi$ in the medium is related to pressure by the linearized momentum equation:

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial t^2} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$

For a wave traveling in $+x$, the displacement has the form:

$$\xi(x,t) = \xi_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$$

### Step 3: Compute the particle velocity

Differentiating displacement with respect to time:

$$v(x,t) = \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial t} = \omega \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

> **Logic Note:** Velocity is in phase with pressure for a forward-traveling wave — this is a special property of plane progressive waves and is critical for energy propagation.

### Step 4: Compute the kinetic energy density

By definition, kinetic energy per unit volume is $\frac{1}{2} \rho_0 v^2$:

$$w_k(x,t) = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 v^2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0^2 \cos^2(\omega t - kx)$$

### Step 5: Compute the potential energy density

The potential energy density is related to the condensation $\delta$ (fractional change in density) by:

$$w_p = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 c^2 \delta^2$$

Using the adiabatic relation $p = \rho_0 c^2 \delta$, we get $\delta = p / (\rho_0 c^2)$. Substituting:

$$w_p(x,t) = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 c^2 \left(\frac{p}{\rho_0 c^2}\right)^2 = \frac{p^2}{2\rho_0 c^2}$$

Therefore:

$$w_p(x,t) = \frac{p_0^2}{2\rho_0 c^2}\cos^2(\omega t - kx)$$

### Step 6: Sum the two contributions

$$w(x,t) = w_k + w_p = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0^2 \cos^2(\omega t - kx) + \frac{p_0^2}{2\rho_0 c^2}\cos^2(\omega t - kx)$$

### Step 7: Apply the impedance relation to simplify

The relation between pressure amplitude and displacement amplitude is:

$$p_0 = \rho_0 c \,\omega \xi_0$$

> **Derivation of this relation:** From Newton's second law, $-\partial p / \partial x = \rho_0 \partial v / \partial t$. Differentiating pressure: $\partial p/\partial x = p_0 k \sin(\omega t - kx)$. Differentiating velocity: $\partial v/\partial t = -\omega^2 \xi_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$. Substituting: $p_0 k = \rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0$. Since $k = \omega / c$, we get $p_0 \omega / c = \rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0$, hence $p_0 = \rho_0 c \omega \xi_0$.

Therefore $\frac{p_0^2}{2\rho_0 c^2} = \frac{\rho_0^2 c^2 \omega^2 \xi_0^2}{2\rho_0 c^2} = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0^2$

### Step 8: Final instantaneous total energy density

Both terms are now identical:

$$\boxed{w(x,t) = \rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0^2 \cos^2(\omega t - kx)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Exam Favorite):** For a plane progressive sound wave, **kinetic and potential energy densities are equal in magnitude at every instant and at every point**. This is unique to traveling waves. For standing waves, they are 90° out of phase and the average kinetic and potential energies are still equal, but the instantaneous values are not.

### Step 9: Time-average over one period

Since $\langle \cos^2(\omega t - kx) \rangle_T = \frac{1}{2}$:

$$\bar{w} = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0^2 = \frac{p_0^2}{2\rho_0 c^2} = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho_0 c^2}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Sound Intensity and Energy Density

### Step 1: Define sound intensity

Sound intensity $I$ is the time-averaged rate of energy flow through a unit area perpendicular to the direction of propagation.

### Step 2: Energy crossing a unit area in time $\Delta t$

In time $\Delta t$, the wave travels a distance $c \Delta t$. The energy contained in a column of cross-section $A = 1$ m² and length $c\Delta t$ is:

$$E = \bar{w} \cdot A \cdot c \Delta t = \bar{w} \, c \, \Delta t$$

### Step 3: Power per unit area

Dividing by $\Delta t$:

$$I = \frac{E}{A \cdot \Delta t} = \bar{w} \cdot c$$

### Step 4: Substitute the energy density expression

Using $\bar{w} = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho_0 c^2}$:

$$\boxed{I = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho_0 c}}$$

> **Logic Note:** This is the famous result that allows a single sound pressure measurement (with a microphone) to determine sound intensity in a free field, provided the wave is approximately planar.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Energy Density in a Vibrating SDOF System

### Step 1: Consider a spring-mass system

Mass $m$ attached to spring of stiffness $k$, executing simple harmonic motion with displacement $x(t) = X_0 \cos(\omega t)$.

### Step 2: Write the kinetic and potential energies

$$KE = \frac{1}{2}m \dot{x}^2 = \frac{1}{2}m \omega^2 X_0^2 \sin^2(\omega t)$$

$$PE = \frac{1}{2}k x^2 = \frac{1}{2}k X_0^2 \cos^2(\omega t)$$

### Step 3: Total energy (conserved for undamped system)

$$E_{total} = \frac{1}{2}m \omega^2 X_0^2 = \frac{1}{2}k X_0^2 = \text{constant}$$

### Step 4: Energy density in a continuous system

For a uniform rod/plate of density $\rho$ undergoing bending vibration with amplitude $A$ and frequency $\omega$, the kinetic energy per unit volume is:

$$w_k = \frac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 A^2 \cos^2(\omega t)$$

Time-averaged:

$$\bar{w}_{struct} = \frac{1}{4}\rho \omega^2 A^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for NVH:** This is the formula used in **Statistical Energy Analysis (SEA)** to determine the modal energy density of vibrating subsystems, which is the cornerstone of high-frequency NVH prediction in automobiles and aircraft.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Acoustic Energy Density Derivation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input: Plane Sound Wave Equations] --> B[Pressure Field p_xt]
    A --> C[Displacement Field xi_xt]
    B --> D[Compute Potential Energy Density]
    C --> E[Compute Particle Velocity v_xt]
    E --> F[Compute Kinetic Energy Density]
    D --> G[Total Instantaneous Energy Density]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Time Average Over One Period]
    H --> I[Output: Mean Energy Density w_bar]
    I --> J[Multiply by Speed c]
    J --> K[Output: Sound Intensity I]
    I --> L[Application: Sound Level Meters]
    K --> M[Application: Noise Source Identification]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Energy Density in NVH Engineering Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Vibration Source: Engine / Road / Fan] --> B[Structural Energy Density]
    B --> C[Coupling at Boundary]
    C --> D[Radiation Efficiency sigma]
    D --> E[Acoustic Energy Density in Air]
    E --> F[Sound Pressure Level SPL]
    E --> G[Sound Intensity I]
    B --> H[Modal Energy SEA Analysis]
    H --> I[Interior Cabin Response]
    I --> J[Subjective NVH Rating]
    F --> J
    G --> J
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map: Relationship Between Energy Density and Other NVH Quantities

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Acoustic Pressure p] --> V[Particle Velocity v]
    P --> EPD[Potential Energy Density]
    V --> EKD[Kinetic Energy Density]
    EPD --> TED[Total Energy Density w]
    EKD --> TED
    TED --> INT[Sound Intensity I = w times c]
    TED --> SPL[SPL Calculation]
    TED --> SEA[Statistical Energy Analysis]
    P --> IMP[Specific Acoustic Impedance Z_s]
    V --> IMP
    IMP --> POW[Radiated Acoustic Power]
    TED --> POW
```

## 4.4 Energy Density Across a Vibrating Boundary (Radiation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Structural Plate Vibrating with Velocity v_n] --> B[Normal Component Couples to Air]
    B --> C[Acoustic Pressure Generated p = Z times v_n]
    C --> D[Acoustic Energy Density w = p squared over rho times c squared]
    D --> E[Intensity I = w times c]
    E --> F[Total Radiated Power P_rad = integral of I dA]
    F --> G[Far Field SPL at Distance r]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams?**
> Physical drawings of sound waves, particle motion, and energy fields cannot be precisely rendered in Mermaid. These block diagrams serve as the **canonical functional representation** used in KTU board examinations when a question says *"Sketch the energy flow in an acoustic field"* or *"Draw a block diagram of NVH energy transmission."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define acoustic energy density. Write the expression for the time-averaged energy density of a plane progressive sound wave.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Acoustic energy density is defined as the total sound energy (kinetic + potential) contained per unit volume of the medium in which the sound wave propagates. It is measured in J/m³.

**Expression (2 Marks):**

For a plane progressive sound wave with RMS pressure $p_{rms}$ in a medium of density $\rho_0$ and sound speed $c$:

$$\bar{w} = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho_0 c^2}$$

Equivalently, in terms of displacement amplitude $\xi_0$ and angular frequency $\omega$:

$$\bar{w} = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0^2$$

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the relationship between sound intensity and acoustic energy density. Mention its significance in noise measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Relationship (2 Marks):** The sound intensity $I$ is the time-averaged energy density multiplied by the speed of sound:

$$I = \bar{w} \cdot c = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho_0 c}$$

**Significance (1 Mark):** This relationship allows a single sound pressure measurement in a free field to determine the sound intensity, enabling noise source identification in industrial and automotive NVH applications without expensive intensity probes.

---

## PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B (Option A)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the total instantaneous acoustic energy density in a plane progressive sound wave. Show that the kinetic and potential energy densities are equal at every instant.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Hence obtain the expression for sound intensity in terms of RMS acoustic pressure, and compute the intensity of a sound wave of RMS pressure 0.1 Pa propagating in air. Take $\rho_0 = 1.204$ kg/m³ and $c = 343$ m/s.** **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Express the acoustic pressure as $p(x,t) = p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$ and the particle displacement as $\xi(x,t) = \xi_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute the particle velocity by differentiation:

$$v(x,t) = \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial t} = \omega \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Kinetic energy density:

$$w_k = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 v^2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0^2 \cos^2(\omega t - kx)$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Potential energy density using $w_p = \dfrac{p^2}{2\rho_0 c^2}$:

$$w_p = \frac{p_0^2}{2\rho_0 c^2}\cos^2(\omega t - kx)$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Use the impedance relation $p_0 = \rho_0 c \omega \xi_0$ to substitute:

$$\frac{p_0^2}{2\rho_0 c^2} = \frac{(\rho_0 c \omega \xi_0)^2}{2\rho_0 c^2} = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0^2$$

**Step 6 (2 Marks):** Therefore:

$$w_k(x,t) = w_p(x,t) = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0^2 \cos^2(\omega t - kx)$$

Both are equal at every point and every instant. The total is:

$$w(x,t) = \rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0^2 \cos^2(\omega t - kx)$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Time-averaging over one period:

$$\bar{w} = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0^2 = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho_0 c^2}$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Sound intensity is the energy crossing per unit area per unit time:

$$I = \bar{w} \cdot c = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho_0 c}$$

**Step 3 (3 Marks):** Numerical computation:

Given: $p_{rms} = 0.1$ Pa, $\rho_0 = 1.204$ kg/m³, $c = 343$ m/s

$$I = \frac{(0.1)^2}{1.204 \times 343} = \frac{0.01}{413.0} \approx 2.42 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W/m}^2$$

The sound intensity level is:

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_{ref}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2.42 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-12}}\right) \approx 73.8 \text{ dB}$$

---

### **Question B (Option B)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) With necessary mathematical expressions, derive the expression for the total energy and energy density in a single-degree-of-freedom vibrating system and a continuous structural element.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Discuss the significance of energy density in Statistical Energy Analysis (SEA) for high-frequency NVH prediction in automobiles. List any two SEA subsystems in a passenger car.** **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**SDOF System (3 Marks):**

For mass $m$ on spring $k$ with displacement $x(t) = X_0 \cos(\omega t)$:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2}m \omega^2 X_0^2 \sin^2(\omega t)$$

$$PE = \frac{1}{2}k X_0^2 \cos^2(\omega t)$$

Total energy (conserved):

$$E_{total} = \frac{1}{2}k X_0^2 = \frac{1}{2}m \omega^2 X_0^2$$

**Continuous Structural Element (4 Marks):**

For a structural plate of density $\rho$, area $S$, thickness $h$, vibrating with RMS velocity $v_{rms}$:

$$E_{total} = \frac{1}{2}\rho S h v_{rms}^2$$

Energy density (per unit volume):

$$\bar{w}_{struct} = \frac{1}{2}\rho v_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{4}\rho \omega^2 A^2$$

where $A$ is the displacement amplitude.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Significance in SEA (4 Marks):**

Statistical Energy Analysis uses **modal energy density** as its primary variable. At high frequencies (typically above 200–500 Hz in automobiles), the modal density is high and deterministic analysis becomes impractical. SEA uses the principle of **energy conservation** between coupled subsystems:

$$\omega \eta_{ij} (n_i E_i - n_j E_j) = \text{Power flow from } j \text{ to } i$$

where $n_i, n_j$ are modal densities and $\eta_{ij}$ is the coupling loss factor. Predicting cabin noise, road noise, and boom noise are direct applications.

**Subsystems in a Passenger Car (3 Marks):**

1. **Cavity** (cabin interior acoustic volume)
2. **Plate** (roof, floor, side panels treated as bending plates)
3. (Optional) **Ribbed structure** (body-in-white with stiffeners)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2** in the kinetic energy density formula $\frac{1}{2}\rho_0 v^2$. KTU examiners specifically look for this — losing 1 full mark.
> 2. **Confusing RMS pressure with peak pressure.** Use $p_{rms}$ in the energy density formula, NOT $p_0$.
> 3. **Mixing up characteristic impedance** $\rho_0 c$ with mechanical impedance $Z_m = \omega m$. They are different quantities.
> 4. **In SEA questions**, students often forget to mention modal density $n(\omega)$ — at least 1 mark is reserved for this term.
> 5. **Unit errors:** Forgetting that energy density is J/m³ and not J. Always write units explicitly in your final answer.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Energy density = Kinetic + Potential energy per unit volume** → Units: **J/m³** ✓
- **Plane wave formula:** $\bar{w} = \dfrac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho_0 c^2}$ — **MUST remember this** ✓
- **Equivalent form:** $\bar{w} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho_0 \omega^2 \xi_0^2$ ✓
- **Intensity-Energy link:** $I = \bar{w} \cdot c = \dfrac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho_0 c}$ ✓
- **Kinetic = Potential** for plane progressive waves at every instant ✓
- **Characteristic acoustic impedance of air** $\rho_0 c \approx$ **413 Rayl** at 20°C ✓
- **Speed of sound in air** $c \approx$ **343 m/s** at 20°C ✓
- **Impedance relation** $p_0 = \rho_0 c \, \omega \xi_0$ — key bridge formula ✓
- **For SDOF system:** $E_{total} = \frac{1}{2}k X_0^2$ (constant) ✓
- **For continuous plate:** $\bar{w} = \frac{1}{4}\rho \omega^2 A^2$ ✓
- **SEA uses energy density** as its primary variable at high frequencies ✓
- **Reference values:** $p_{ref} = 20$ µPa, $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W/m² ✓
- **Sound intensity level** $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_{ref})$ in dB ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Energy density questions are most often combined with intensity, impedance, or SEA questions in 14-mark Part B problems. Master the **four core formulas** (kinetic, potential, mean energy density, intensity) and you can solve ~80% of the variants.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sound Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Sound Power # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Sound Power (W)** is defined as the total acoustic energy radiated per unit time by a sound source in all directions. It is an intrinsic property of the source and is independent of the surrounding environment, distance from the source, or the acoustic field characteristics. Sound power is measured in **Watts (W)** and quantifies the rate at which a source converts mechanical or electrical energy into acoustic energy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** *"Sound Power is the total sound energy radiated per second by a source, expressed in Watts (W). It is a property of the source only and does not depend on the measurement distance or environment."* **Sound Power Level (SWL or L_W)** is the logarithmic measure of sound power relative to a reference power of **W₀ = 10⁻¹² W (1 picowatt)**, expressed in **decibels (dB)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Light Bulb" Analogy To truly understand sound power, imagine a **light bulb** in a room: | Light Domain | Sound Domain | |---|---| | **Luminous Power (Lumens)** | **Sound Power (Watts)** | | Total light output of the bulb | Total acoustic energy radiated by source | | Depends only on the bulb | Depends only on the source | | Measured inside an integrating sphere | Measured in an anechoic chamber | | Light Domain | Sound Domain | |---|---| | **Illuminance (Lux)** | **Sound Intensity (W/m²)** | | Light falling on a surface | Sound energy flowing per unit area | | Varies with distance | Varies with distance (1/r² for point) | | Measured with a light meter | Measured with an intensity probe | | Light Domain | Sound Domain | |---|---| | **Brightness perceived** | **Sound Pressure (Pa)** | | What your eye senses | What your ear (microphone) senses | | Depends on environment | Depends on environment | Just as a 100-Watt bulb always emits 100 Watts of light regardless of where it is placed, a machine with a sound power of 1 Watt always radiates 1 Watt of acoustic energy, regardless of whether you measure it near or far. The *brightness* (sound pressure) you perceive changes with distance, but the *power* of the source never does. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Sound power is a **cause**, while sound pressure is an **effect**. This distinction is the most fundamental concept in noise measurement and is frequently tested in KTU examinations. ## 1.3 The Three Fundamental Acoustic Quantities - **Sound Power (W):** Energy rate from source → **Watts (W)** - **Sound Intensity (I):** Energy flux through a surface → **W/m²** - **Sound Pressure (p):** Local pressure fluctuation → **Pascals (Pa)** or **N/m²** > [!TIP] > **Reference Standards (must memorize for KTU):** > - Reference Sound Power: **W₀ = 10⁻¹² W** > - Reference Sound Intensity: **I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m²** > - Reference Sound Pressure: **p₀ = 20 × 10⁻⁶ Pa = 20 μPa** > - Acoustic Impedance of Air: **ρc ≈ 400 N·s/m³** (at 20°C, 1 atm) ## 1.4 Physical Significance in Engineering Sound power is the **gold standard** for rating and comparing noise sources (machinery, vehicles, appliances) because it is an absolute, distance-independent measure. International standards such as **ISO 3744**, **ISO 9614**, and **ISO 3741** define procedures to measure sound power in different acoustic environments (anechoic, semi-anechoic, or reverberant rooms). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Acoustic Energy Chain The flow of acoustic energy from a source to a receiver follows a strict, hierarchical chain: **Step 1 — Sound Power Generation (at the source):** - The vibrating surface of a machine (piston, diaphragm, casing) imparts kinetic energy to the surrounding air molecules. - This energy is radiated outward in the form of pressure waves. - The total rate of this radiation is the **Sound Power (W)**. **Step 2 — Sound Intensity (energy transport):** - The radiated energy propagates through a defined surface *S* surrounding the source. - **I = W / S** (W/m²) → This is the time-averaged intensity through that surface. - For a **point source** (spherical radiation): $S = 4\pi r^2$, hence $I = \dfrac{W}{4\pi r^2}$. - For a **line source** (cylindrical radiation): $S = 2\pi r L$, hence $I = \dfrac{W}{2\pi r L}$. **Step 3 — Sound Pressure (the measurable quantity):** - A microphone senses the local pressure fluctuations. - For a **plane progressive wave** or **spherical wave in free field**: $I = \dfrac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho c}$. - Here, $\rho c$ is the **characteristic acoustic impedance** of the medium. > [!NOTE] > **Why the factor of 1/r² matters:** Doubling the distance from a point source reduces intensity (and hence sound pressure) by a factor of 4, which corresponds to a **6 dB drop** in sound pressure level. This is the inverse-square law of acoustics. ## 2.2 Logarithmic (Decibel) Forms Since acoustic quantities span many orders of magnitude, they are expressed logarithmically in decibels (dB). | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Reference Value | |---|---|---|---| | Sound Power Level | $L_W$ | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right)$ dB | $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W | | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I$ | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ dB | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² | | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p$ | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right)$ dB | $p_0 = 20\,\mu$Pa | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note the factor of 10 vs 20:** Sound power and intensity are energy quantities (proportional to amplitude squared), so we use **10 log₁₀**. Sound pressure is an amplitude quantity, so we use **20 log₁₀**. This is a classic KTU board trap question. ## 2.3 Master Formula — Relating Sound Power Level to Sound Pressure Level In a **free field** (anechoic environment), combining the relationships above gives the most important engineering formula: $$L_W = L_p + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{S_0}\right) \text{ dB}$$ where $S_0 = 1$ m² (reference area). For a point source radiating spherically over a hemisphere (typical for a machine on a hard reflecting floor) or a full sphere, substitute $S$ accordingly. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **complete formula set** required for Module 1 Sound Power questions. Memorize it thoroughly. | # | Relationship | Formula | Condition / Field | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sound Power Level | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W / W_0)$ dB | General (source property) | | 2 | Sound Intensity from Power | $I = W / S$ | Any closed surface *S* | | 3 | Point source intensity | $I = W / (4\pi r^2)$ | Spherical radiation, free field | | 4 | Line source intensity | $I = W / (2\pi r L)$ | Cylindrical radiation | | 5 | Intensity → Pressure (free field) | $I = p_{rms}^{\,2} / (\rho c)$ | Plane / spherical wave | | 6 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms} / p_0)$ | General | | 7 | $L_W$ from $L_p$ (free field) | $L_W = L_p + 20 \log_{10}(r) + 11$ dB | Point source, $S = 4\pi r^2$ | | 8 | $L_W$ from $L_p$ (hemispherical) | $L_W = L_p + 20 \log_{10}(r) + 8$ dB | Source on reflecting plane, $S = 2\pi r^2$ | | 9 | Total power from multiple sources | $W_{tot} = \sum W_i$ | Incoherent (uncorrelated) sources | | 10 | $L_I$ relation to $L_p$ (free field) | $L_I \approx L_p$ | Valid only in free / progressive field | | 11 | $L_W$ from $L_I$ | $L_W = L_I + 10 \log_{10}(S)$ | General, $S$ in m² | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** Sound power is used to **(a)** compare noise emissions of competing products, **(b)** predict sound pressure at any distance using the formulas above, **(c)** design noise control treatments, and **(d)** comply with regulatory noise emission standards (e.g., EU Outdoor Noise Directive 2000/14/EC, EPA noise limits). ## 2.5 The "Add 10 vs Add 3 dB" Rule for Multiple Sources When **N identical incoherent sources** operate together: $$L_{total} = L_{single} + 10 \log_{10}(N) \text{ dB}$$ Special cases to remember: - **Doubling sources** (N = 2): Add **3 dB** to $L_{single}$. - **Ten-fold increase** (N = 10): Add **10 dB** to $L_{single}$. - **Doubling the distance** (point source, free field): Subtract **6 dB** from $L_p$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Sound Intensity from Sound Power (Point Source) **Starting Point:** Sound power $W$ is the total acoustic energy radiated per second by a source. **Step 1 — Energy Conservation (Geometric Spreading):** If a point source radiates uniformly in all directions, the energy passes through an imaginary sphere of radius $r$ centered on the source. The surface area of this sphere is: $$S = 4 \pi r^2$$ **Step 2 — Apply Definition of Intensity:** Intensity is defined as power per unit area. Since the power $W$ is distributed uniformly over the spherical surface $S$: $$I = \frac{W}{S}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the Area:** $$I = \frac{W}{4 \pi r^2}$$ **Step 4 — In Decibel Form:** Take $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides: $$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{4 \pi r^2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4 \pi r^2}{1}\right)$$ $$L_I = L_W - 10 \log_{10}(4 \pi r^2)$$ **Step 5 — Simplify the Geometry Term:** $$10 \log_{10}(4 \pi r^2) = 10 \log_{10}(4\pi) + 10 \log_{10}(r^2) = 10.99 + 20 \log_{10}(r)$$ Since $10 \log_{10}(4\pi) \approx 11$ dB: $$\boxed{L_I = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11 \text{ dB}}$$ **Step 6 — Apply Free-Field Equivalence $L_I = L_p$:** In a free (progressive) wave field, the intensity level and sound pressure level are numerically equal: $$L_p = L_I$$ Therefore, the **master KTU formula** becomes: $$\boxed{L_W = L_p + 20 \log_{10}(r) + 11 \text{ dB}}$$ This is the most tested derivation in Module 1. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: $I = p_{rms}^{\,2} / (\rho c)$ for a Plane Progressive Wave **Starting Point:** The instantaneous intensity of a sound wave is the product of instantaneous pressure and particle velocity: $$i(t) = p(t) \cdot u(t)$$ **Step 1 — Express Pressure and Velocity for a Plane Wave:** For a plane progressive wave traveling in the +x direction: $$p(t,x) = p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ $$u(t,x) = \frac{p_0}{\rho c} \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 2 — Form the Instantaneous Intensity:** $$i(t) = p(t) \cdot u(t) = \frac{p_0^{\,2}}{\rho c} \cos^2(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 3 — Time-Average Over One Period $T$:** $$I = \langle i(t) \rangle = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \frac{p_0^{\,2}}{\rho c} \cos^2(\omega t - kx)\, dt$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral:** The time-average of $\cos^2$ over a complete period is exactly $\frac{1}{2}$: $$\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \cos^2(\omega t - kx)\, dt = \frac{1}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute and Simplify:** $$I = \frac{p_0^{\,2}}{2 \rho c}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to RMS Pressure:** By definition, the root-mean-square pressure is: $$p_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T p^2(t)\, dt} = \frac{p_0}{\sqrt{2}}$$ Squaring both sides: $$p_{rms}^{\,2} = \frac{p_0^{\,2}}{2}$$ **Step 7 — Final Result:** $$\boxed{I = \frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho c}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical meaning of $\rho c$:** This is the **specific acoustic impedance** of air. At 20°C, $\rho \approx 1.204$ kg/m³ and $c \approx 343$ m/s, giving $\rho c \approx 413$ N·s/m³ ≈ **400 N·s/m³** (rounded for engineering use). --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: Sound Power of a Machine **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A compressor is measured to have a sound pressure level of **78 dB** at a distance of **10 m** in an open, hard-ground field. Assuming the machine acts as a hemispherical point source, determine: (a) The sound power level of the compressor. (b) The sound pressure level at 3 m from the machine. (c) The sound power in Watts. **Given:** $L_p = 78$ dB, $r = 10$ m, hemispherical radiation. ### Solution (a) — Sound Power Level **Step 1:** Use the hemispherical formula: $$L_W = L_p + 20 \log_{10}(r) + 8 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate $20 \log_{10}(10)$: $$20 \log_{10}(10) = 20 \times 1 = 20 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute values: $$L_W = 78 + 20 + 8 = 106 \text{ dB}$$ ### Solution (b) — Sound Pressure at 3 m **Step 1:** Use the inverse-square relationship. From the master formula, $L_p$ depends on $r$ as: $$L_p = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 8$$ **Step 2:** Calculate the difference between the two positions: $$\Delta L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{3}\right) = 20 \times 0.5229 = 10.46 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 3:** The new $L_p$ at 3 m (closer → louder) is: $$L_p(3\text{ m}) = 78 + 10.46 = 88.46 \approx 88.5 \text{ dB}$$ ### Solution (c) — Sound Power in Watts **Step 1:** Apply the definition of $L_W$: $$L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Solve for $W$: $$\frac{W}{W_0} = 10^{L_W / 10} = 10^{106/10} = 10^{10.6}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W: $$W = 10^{-12} \times 10^{10.6} = 10^{-1.4} = 0.0398 \text{ W} \approx 39.8 \text{ mW}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Note:** Always show the substitution of $W_0$ explicitly. Marks are awarded for: (i) the correct formula, (ii) the numerical substitution, and (iii) the final numerical answer with units. Skipping any step costs 1–2 marks. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example: Multiple Incoherent Sources **Problem Statement:** Four identical fans operate simultaneously in a workshop. Each fan individually produces a sound pressure level of **72 dB** at the operator's location. Calculate the total sound pressure level at the same location. **Step 1 — Convert a Single Source Level to Power:** $$W_{single} = W_0 \times 10^{L_W/10} = 10^{-12} \times 10^{7.2} = 10^{-4.8} \text{ W}$$ **Step 2 — Total Power from 4 Identical Sources:** $$W_{total} = 4 \times W_{single} = 4 \times 10^{-4.8} \text{ W}$$ **Step 3 — Convert Total Power to Total Level:** $$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W_{total}}{W_0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(4 \times 10^{7.2}\right)$$ $$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}(4) + 10 \log_{10}(10^{7.2}) = 6.02 + 72 = 78.02 \text{ dB}$$ **Shortcut Verification:** $L_{total} = L_{single} + 10 \log_{10}(N) = 72 + 10 \log_{10}(4) = 72 + 6.02 = 78.02$ dB ✓ > [!TIP] > **Common Mistake:** Students often mistakenly say "4 sources × 72 dB = 288 dB" or "4 sources → add 4 dB". Neither is correct. Power adds linearly; **decibels do not add linearly**. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequently Asked in KTU) | Parameter | Sound Power ($W$) | Sound Intensity ($I$) | Sound Pressure ($p$) | |---|---|---|---| | **Physical meaning** | Total energy rate from source | Energy flux through area | Local pressure fluctuation | | **Unit** | Watt (W) | W/m² | Pascal (Pa) | | **Reference value** | $10^{-12}$ W | $10^{-12}$ W/m² | $20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa | | **Log form uses** | $10 \log_{10}$ | $10 \log_{10}$ | $20 \log_{10}$ | | **Depends on distance?** | **No** | Yes (1/r² for point) | Yes (1/r for point) | | **Depends on environment?** | **No** | Yes (field type) | Yes (reflections, etc.) | | **Measured by** | Anemometer / calculation in anechoic room | Sound intensity probe | Microphone | # 4.
