KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the term **Sound Power**. State its units and the reference value used in the decibel scale. Why is sound power considered an intrinsic property of a source? **Model Answer:** **Sound Power (W):** Sound power is the total acoustic energy radiated per unit time by a sound source in all directions. It represents the rate at which a source converts non-acoustic energy (mechanical, electrical, fluid) into acoustic energy. **Unit:** **Watt (W)** in SI units. **Reference value:** $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W (1 picowatt). **Sound Power Level:** $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W / W_0)$ in dB. **Why intrinsic:** Sound power is determined solely by the physical operation of the source (its vibration amplitude, surface area, and radiation efficiency). It does **not** depend on: - Distance from the source - The acoustic environment (anechoic, reverberant, outdoor) - The presence of reflecting surfaces - Atmospheric absorption (which only affects the propagated field, not the source) Hence it is a unique "fingerprint" of the source and is the standard metric used in noise emission regulations (ISO 3740 series, EU Directive 2000/14/EC). > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Students often confuse **sound power** (source property, Watts) with **sound pressure** (field property, Pascals). Always check units and the factor in the log formula: **10 log** for power/intensity, **20 log** for pressure. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **Sound Power Level**, **Sound Intensity Level**, and **Sound Pressure Level**. Give the formulas and reference values for each. **Model Answer:** | Level Type | Symbol | Formula | Reference | Quantity Type | |---|---|---|---|---| | Sound Power Level | $L_W$ | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ dB | $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W | Energy rate | | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I$ | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ dB | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² | Energy flux | | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p$ | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_0)$ dB | $p_0 = 20\,\mu$Pa | Amplitude | **Key Distinctions:** - $L_W$ and $L_I$ use a factor of **10** in the log because $W$ and $I$ are proportional to the square of the acoustic amplitude. - $L_p$ uses a factor of **20** in the log because $p$ is linearly proportional to amplitude. - $L_W$ is distance-independent; $L_I$ and $L_p$ vary with distance and environment. - In a free progressive field, $L_I = L_p$ numerically, but their units and physical meaning differ. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the definitions of sound power and sound intensity, derive the relationship between **Sound Power Level ($L_W$)** and **Sound Pressure Level ($L_p$)** for a point source radiating uniformly in a free field. State clearly the assumption made and the value of $\rho c$ for air at standard conditions. **(b) [7 Marks]** A diesel generator is measured to have a sound pressure level of **82 dB** at a distance of **8 m** in a free field. Assuming it behaves as a point source on a hard reflecting ground (hemispherical radiation), calculate: 1. The sound power level of the generator. 2. The total sound power radiated in Watts. 3. The sound pressure level at a residential boundary located **40 m** away. --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Solution (a) — Derivation [7 Marks] **Step 1:** Sound power $W$ is the total energy radiated per second by the source. **Step 2:** For a point source radiating uniformly, the power passes through a sphere of radius $r$ with area $S = 4\pi r^2$. By definition, the intensity is: $$I = \frac{W}{S} = \frac{W}{4\pi r^2}$$ **Step 3:** In a free (progressive) acoustic field, intensity and RMS pressure are related by: $$I = \frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho c}$$ where $\rho c$ is the specific acoustic impedance of the medium. **Step 4:** For air at 20°C and 1 atm: $\rho \approx 1.204$ kg/m³, $c \approx 343$ m/s, so: $$\rho c \approx 1.204 \times 343 \approx 413 \approx \mathbf{400 \text{ N·s/m}^3}$$ **Step 5:** Take $10 \log_{10}$ of the intensity expression: $$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho c \cdot I_0}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\rho c \cdot I_0}{p_0^{\,2}}\right)$$ **Step 6:** In a free field, the term involving $\rho c$ evaluates to zero (by choice of reference values), so: $$L_I = L_p$$ **Step 7:** Equate the two expressions for $L_I$: $$L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11 = L_p$$ **Step 8:** Rearrange to obtain the master formula: $$\boxed{L_W = L_p + 20 \log_{10}(r) + 11 \text{ dB}} \quad \text{(full spherical, free field)}$$ **Assumption stated:** The source radiates uniformly as a point source, and the acoustic field is free (no reflections). #### Solution (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks] **Given:** $L_p = 82$ dB, $r_1 = 8$ m, hemispherical radiation (hard ground). **Part (b.1) — Sound Power Level:** Use the hemispherical formula: $$L_W = L_p + 20 \log_{10}(r_1) + 8 \text{ dB}$$ **Compute** $20 \log_{10}(8) = 20 \times 0.9031 = 18.06$ dB. $$L_W = 82 + 18.06 + 8 = 108.06 \approx \mathbf{108 \text{ dB}}$$ **Part (b.2) — Total Sound Power in Watts:** $$L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) \implies W = W_0 \times 10^{L_W/10}$$ $$W = 10^{-12} \times 10^{108/10} = 10^{-12} \times 10^{10.8} = 10^{-1.2}$$ $$W = 10^{-1.2} \approx 0.0631 \text{ W} \approx \mathbf{63.1 \text{ mW}}$$ **Part (b.3) — Sound Pressure at 40 m:** For hemispherical radiation, the SPL at any distance $r_2$ is: $$L_p(r_2) = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r_2) - 8$$ Or use the difference method: $$\Delta L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{8}{40}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(0.2) = 20 \times (-0.699) = -13.98 \text{ dB}$$ $$L_p(40\text{ m}) = 82 - 13.98 = 68.02 \approx \mathbf{68 \text{ dB}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently use the **wrong sign** for the hemispherical correction. The "+8 dB" term appears when computing $L_W$ **from** $L_p$, but it becomes "−8 dB" when computing $L_p$ **from** $L_W$. Re-derive carefully before plugging numbers. Also, **20 log₁₀(8) = 18.06**, not 19. A common arithmetic error costs 1 mark. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concepts of **sound power**, **sound intensity**, and **sound pressure** with appropriate physical interpretations. Derive the relationship: $$L_W = L_I + 10 \log_{10}(S)$$ where $S$ is the area of a hypothetical surface enclosing the source. Apply this to compute the SWL of a source that produces $L_I = 70$ dB uniformly over a hemisphere of radius $r = 5$ m. **(b) [7 Marks]** Three machines in a factory produce sound power levels of **78 dB, 81 dB, and 84 dB** respectively. Calculate the total sound power level when all three operate simultaneously, assuming their emissions are incoherent. Also find the total radiated sound power in Watts. --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Solution (a) — Conceptual Explanation + Derivation + Application [7 Marks] **Concepts:** - **Sound Power ($W$):** Total acoustic energy radiated per unit time by a source. Unit: Watt. Independent of distance and environment. Represents the *cause* of noise. - **Sound Intensity ($I$):** The time-averaged rate of sound energy flowing *through* a unit area perpendicular to the direction of propagation. Unit: W/m². Vector quantity. Depends on the field geometry and the area through which energy flows. - **Sound Pressure ($p$):** The local fluctuation in static atmospheric pressure due to the passage of a sound wave. Unit: Pascal (Pa). Scalar (signed) quantity. What a microphone measures. **Derivation of $L_W = L_I + 10 \log_{10}(S)$:** By definition: $$I = \frac{W}{S} \implies W = I \cdot S$$ Divide both sides by the reference $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W: $$\frac{W}{W_0} = \frac{I}{I_0} \cdot \frac{S \cdot I_0}{W_0}$$ Take $10 \log_{10}$: $$L_W = L_I + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S \cdot I_0}{W_0}\right)$$ Using $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² and $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W: $$\frac{S \cdot I_0}{W_0} = S \cdot 1 = S$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{L_W = L_I + 10 \log_{10}(S) \text{ dB}}$$ **Application to the numerical problem:** Given: $L_I = 70$ dB, hemisphere with $r = 5$ m. $$S = 2\pi r^2 = 2\pi (5)^2 = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ m}^2$$ $$10 \log_{10}(157.08) = 10 \times 2.196 = 21.96 \text{ dB}$$ $$L_W = 70 + 21.96 = 91.96 \approx \mathbf{92 \text{ dB}}$$ #### Solution (b) — Multiple Incoherent Sources [7 Marks] **Step 1:** Convert each $L_W$ to power in Watts. $$W_1 = 10^{-12} \times 10^{7.8} = 10^{-4.2} = 6.31 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W}$$ $$W_2 = 10^{-12} \times 10^{8.1} = 10^{-3.9} = 1.259 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W}$$ $$W_3 = 10^{-12} \times 10^{8.4} = 10^{-3.6} = 2.512 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W}$$ **Step 2:** Total power (incoherent sources add in power): $$W_{total} = W_1 + W_2 + W_3 = (0.631 + 1.259 + 2.512) \times 10^{-4} = 4.402 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W}$$ **Step 3:** Convert back to decibels: $$L_{W,total} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W_{total}}{W_0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4.402 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(4.402 \times 10^{8})$$ $$= 10 [\log_{10}(4.402) + 8] = 10 [0.6435 + 8] = 10 \times 8.6435 = \mathbf{86.44 \text{ dB}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often attempt to add decibels directly: $78 + 81 + 84 = 243$ dB — this is **completely wrong**. Decibels are logarithmic; powers must first be summed, then converted back. Marks are lost for skipping the linear-summation step. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Sound Power ($W$):** Total acoustic energy per second. Unit: **Watt (W)**. **Intrinsic** to source. Reference: $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W. - [x] **Sound Power Level:** $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ dB — uses **factor of 10** (energy quantity). - [x] **Sound Pressure Level:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_0)$ dB — uses **factor of 20** (amplitude quantity). Reference: $p_0 = 20\,\mu$Pa. - [x] **Sound Intensity:** $I = W/S$. For a point source: $I = W/(4\pi r^2)$ (full sphere) or $W/(2\pi r^2)$ (hemisphere). - [x] **Free-field relation:** $I = p_{rms}^{\,2}/(\rho c)$, and **numerically** $L_I = L_p$. - [x] **Acoustic impedance of air:** $\rho c \approx 400$ N·s/m³ (at 20°C). - [x] **Master free-field formula (full sphere):** $L_W = L_p + 20 \log_{10}(r) + 11$ dB. - [x] **Master hemispherical formula:** $L_W = L_p + 20 \log_{10}(r) + 8$ dB. - [x] **Inverse-square law:** Doubling distance → **−6 dB** drop in $L_p$. Halving distance → **+6 dB**. - [x] **Multiple incoherent sources:** $L_{total} = L_{single} + 10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB. Doubling $N$ adds **3 dB**. - [x] **Power adds linearly, decibels do not.** Always convert to linear power first when adding sources. - [x] **Sound power is the regulatory metric** (ISO 3740, ISO 3741, ISO 3744, ISO 9614, EU Directive 2000/14/EC). - [x] **Always state assumptions:** point source, free field, incoherent sources, hemispherical/full spherical, hard reflecting ground. - [x] **Memorize the three reference values:** $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W, $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m², $p_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa. - [x] **Logarithm rules to recall:** $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.301$, $\log_{10}(4) \approx 0.602$, $\log_{10}(10) = 1$, $\log_{10}(4\pi) \approx 1.099$. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Strategy (KTU 2024 Scheme):** For any sound-power numerical, follow this 4-step ritual: > 1. **Identify geometry** — point/line source, full sphere/hemisphere? > 2. **Write the master formula** explicitly with the right constant (+11 or +8). > 3. **Show log conversion** — compute $20 \log_{10}(r)$ in a separate line. > 4. **Compute final $L_W$ or $W$ in Watts** with explicit units. > > Skipping any of these four steps typically costs 1–2 marks in the KTU board valuation scheme.

topics.title: Inhomogeneous Wave Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inhomogeneous Wave Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Inhomogeneous Wave Equation** is a second-order partial differential equation that governs the propagation of acoustic, mechanical, or electromagnetic disturbances through a medium in the **presence of external sources, forcing terms, or inhomogeneities** within the propagation medium itself. Unlike its homogeneous counterpart (which describes unforced, free-field wave propagation), the inhomogeneous form explicitly incorporates a non-zero right-hand side function that represents energy injection into the system.

In its most general three-dimensional scalar form, the inhomogeneous wave equation is written as:

$$\nabla^2 \Psi(\mathbf{r}, t) - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 \Psi(\mathbf{r}, t)}{\partial t^2} = -F(\mathbf{r}, t)$$

where:
- $\Psi(\mathbf{r}, t)$ is the scalar field variable (acoustic pressure, displacement, or potential),
- $c$ is the phase velocity of wave propagation in the medium (in **m/s**),
- $F(\mathbf{r}, t)$ is the **source function** or forcing distribution,
- $\mathbf{r}$ is the position vector in space, and
- $t$ is the time coordinate (in **seconds**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of **Noise, Vibration and Harshness (NVH)**, the inhomogeneous wave equation is the cornerstone for predicting sound radiation from vibrating structures, analyzing acoustic sources inside engine compartments, modeling structural-acoustic coupling, and studying the acoustic response of vehicles to road-induced excitation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Building Strong Intuition

Imagine a perfectly still pond of water. If you drop a small stone (a **source**), ripples spread outward — this is governed by the **inhomogeneous** wave equation because there is an active input of energy at a localized point. If instead the pond is already disturbed by wind blowing across it and you simply observe the waves traveling and interacting naturally, you are dealing with the **homogeneous** wave equation (no source term on the right-hand side).

Another vivid analogy: think of a guitar string.
- The **homogeneous wave equation** describes how a string vibrates *after* it is plucked (free oscillation, no external force).
- The **inhomogeneous wave equation** describes the situation where a motor or speaker continuously drives the string, *constantly feeding energy* into it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Homogeneous = "the system is left to itself." Inhomogeneous = "something is actively driving the system."

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of sound in air (20°C) | $c$ | **343 m/s** |
| Speed of sound in water (20°C) | $c$ | **1480 m/s** |
| Speed of sound in steel | $c$ | **5960 m/s** |
| Density of air at STP | $\rho_0$ | **1.204 kg/m³** |
| Acoustic source strength | $Q$ | **m³/s** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Structure

The inhomogeneous wave equation is fundamentally a statement of **Newton's Second Law applied to a continuous medium that has distributed sources of momentum or mass injection**. The left-hand side captures the *propagation* physics (inertia + restoring force), while the right-hand side captures the *generation* physics (sources).

### 2.1.1 One-Dimensional Form

For a string, rod, or air column with a distributed transverse force per unit length $f(x,t)$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 y(x,t)}{\partial t^2} - c^2 \frac{\partial^2 y(x,t)}{\partial x^2} = \frac{f(x,t)}{\mu}$$

where $\mu$ is the mass per unit length (in **kg/m**).

### 2.1.2 Three-Dimensional Acoustic Form

For a fluid medium, the inhomogeneous wave equation governs the acoustic pressure $p(\mathbf{r},t)$:

$$\nabla^2 p(\mathbf{r},t) - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p(\mathbf{r},t)}{\partial t^2} = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial q(\mathbf{r},t)}{\partial t}$$

where $q(\mathbf{r},t)$ is the **mass injection rate per unit volume** (in **kg/(m³·s)**).

## 2.2 Classification of Sources

A cornerstone of NVH engineering is the classification of acoustic sources by their **multipole order**. This is the physical interpretation of the right-hand side of the inhomogeneous wave equation.

| Source Order | Physical Realization | Radiated Power $\propto$ | Frequency Dependence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Monopole** (0th order) | Pulsating sphere, reciprocating compressor | $\omega^2$ | Increases with $f^2$ |
| **Dipole** (1st order) | Oscillating rigid sphere, vibrating panel | $\omega^4$ | Increases with $f^4$ |
| **Quadrupole** (2nd order) | Turbulent jet, exhaust muffler | $\omega^6$ | Increases with $f^6$ |
| **Octupole** (3rd order) | Higher-order turbulence | $\omega^8$ | Increases with $f^8$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because of the steep frequency dependence, **low-frequency noise** is dominated by monopole sources (e.g., engine intake), while **high-frequency noise** is dominated by quadrupole sources (e.g., turbulent exhaust flow). This dictates the design philosophy of mufflers and barriers.

## 2.3 The Green's Function Approach

The general solution to the inhomogeneous wave equation is elegantly expressed via the **Green's function** $G(\mathbf{r}, t \mid \mathbf{r}', t')$:

$$\Psi(\mathbf{r}, t) = \Psi_{\text{hom}}(\mathbf{r}, t) + \int\!\!\int G(\mathbf{r}, t \mid \mathbf{r}', t') \, F(\mathbf{r}', t') \, dV' \, dt'$$

The Green's function is the impulse response of the wave equation — it is the field produced at $(\mathbf{r},t)$ by a unit-amplitude point source that fires instantaneously at $(\mathbf{r}', t')$.

For the three-dimensional wave equation in free space:

$$G(\mathbf{r}, t \mid \mathbf{r}', t') = \frac{\delta\left(t - t' - \frac{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert}{c}\right)}{4\pi \vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert}$$

The argument of the Dirac delta is the **retarded time** — information propagates at finite speed $c$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Card — Inhomogeneous Wave Equation**

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nabla^2 p - \dfrac{1}{c^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = -\rho_0 \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t}$ | 3-D acoustic IWE with mass source |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} - c^2 \dfrac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2} = \dfrac{f(x,t)}{\mu}$ | 1-D forced string/rod |
| 3 | $\nabla^2 \Phi - \dfrac{1}{c^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 \Phi}{\partial t^2} = -S(\mathbf{r},t)$ | Velocity potential formulation |
| 4 | $G_{\text{3D}}(\mathbf{r},t) = \dfrac{\delta(t - R/c)}{4\pi R}$ | Free-space 3-D Green's function |
| 5 | $G_{\text{2D}}(\mathbf{r},t) = \dfrac{H(t - R/c)}{2\pi \sqrt{t^2 - (R/c)^2}}$ | Free-space 2-D Green's function |
| 6 | $G_{\text{1D}}(\mathbf{r},t) = \dfrac{c}{2}H(t - R/c)$ | Free-space 1-D Green's function |
| 7 | $p_{\text{mono}}(r,t) = \dfrac{\rho_0 \dot{Q}(t - r/c)}{4\pi r}$ | Monopole pressure field |
| 8 | $W_{\text{mono}} = \dfrac{\rho_0 \omega^2 \vert Q \vert^2}{8\pi c}$ | Monopole radiated power |
| 9 | $t_r = t - \dfrac{R}{c}$ | Retarded time |
| 10 | $\Box \Psi = -F$ | Compact d'Alembertian form |

**Operator notation:** The d'Alembertian (or wave operator) is defined as:

$$\Box \equiv \nabla^2 - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial t^2}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The inhomogeneous wave equation is the **mathematical backbone** of:

1. **Automotive NVH analysis** — predicting cabin noise from engine, tire, and wind sources.
2. **Loudspeaker design** — computing sound fields from diaphragm motion.
3. **Aeroacoustics** — Lighthill's acoustic analogy, where turbulent flow is replaced by an equivalent source term.
4. **Ultrasonic nondestructive testing** — modeling pulse-echo from defects.
5. **Building acoustics** — predicting sound transmission through partitions.
6. **Active noise control** — designing anti-sound sources.
7. **Underwater sonar** — propagation of acoustic pulses in the ocean.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lighthill's Acoustic Analogy (1952):** Treats turbulent flow noise by recasting the Navier-Stokes equations into the form of an inhomogeneous wave equation where the source term is the **Lighthill stress tensor** $T_{ij} = \rho u_i u_j + (p - c_0^2 \rho)\delta_{ij} - \sigma_{ij}$. This is a foundational result in modern aeroacoustics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the 1-D Inhomogeneous Wave Equation for a String

Consider a stretched string of linear mass density $\mu$ (in **kg/m**) under tension $T$ (in **N**), with a distributed transverse external force $f(x,t)$ per unit length.

**Step 1: Kinematic relation.** A small element of the string located at $x$ with length $dx$ is displaced transversely by $y(x,t)$. The transverse displacement is small, so the slope $\partial y/\partial x$ is small.

**Step 2: Forces on the element.** The tension $T$ acts tangentially at each end. The vertical components of the tension are:

$$F_{\text{left}} = T \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x} \quad \text{(acts upward at left end)}$$

$$F_{\text{right}} = -T \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x+dx} \quad \text{(acts downward at right end)}$$

**Step 3: Net transverse force from tension.** Using a Taylor expansion to first order:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{net, tension}} &= T \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x} - T \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x+dx} \\
&= T \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} - T\left(\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2}dx\right) \\
&= -T \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2}dx
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Apply Newton's second law.** The mass of the element is $dm = \mu \, dx$, and the transverse acceleration is $\partial^2 y / \partial t^2$. Including the external force $f(x,t)dx$:

$$-T \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2}dx + f(x,t)dx = (\mu \, dx) \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2}$$

**Step 5: Divide by $\mu \, dx$ and define $c^2 = T/\mu$.**

$$\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} - c^2 \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2} = \frac{f(x,t)}{\mu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **1-D inhomogeneous wave equation**. The right-hand side $\frac{f(x,t)}{\mu}$ is the source term — it represents external energy injection per unit mass.

## 3.2 Derivation of the 3-D Acoustic Inhomogeneous Wave Equation

Starting from the linearized continuity equation, momentum equation, and equation of state for a fluid:

**Step 1: Continuity (linearized).**

$$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} = \rho_0 q(\mathbf{r},t)$$

where $\rho'$ is the density perturbation, $\mathbf{u}$ is the particle velocity, and $q$ is the volumetric mass injection rate per unit mass.

**Step 2: Linearized momentum (Euler).**

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial t} = -\nabla p$$

**Step 3: Equation of state (adiabatic, linearized).**

$$p = c^2 \rho'$$

**Step 4: Eliminate $\mathbf{u}$.** Take the divergence of the momentum equation:

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}) = -\nabla^2 p$$

**Step 5: Eliminate $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}$ using continuity.** From the continuity equation:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} = -\frac{1}{\rho_0}\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + q$$

**Step 6: Take $\partial/\partial t$ of continuity and substitute.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}) &= -\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial q}{\partial t} \\
-\nabla^2 p &= -\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial q}{\partial t}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Use equation of state to replace $\rho'$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla^2 p &= \frac{\partial^2 (p/c^2)}{\partial t^2} - \rho_0 \frac{\partial q}{\partial t} \\
\nabla^2 p - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} &= -\rho_0 \frac{\partial q}{\partial t}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **3-D inhomogeneous acoustic wave equation**. The right-hand side is the source term, equal to $-\rho_0 \partial q/\partial t$. If $q=0$, we recover the homogeneous wave equation.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Monopole Pressure Field

Consider a pulsating sphere of radius $a \ll \lambda$ (compact source) with surface velocity $v_s(t)$. The volume velocity is $Q(t) = 4\pi a^2 v_s(t)$.

**Step 1: Use the source term.** The mass injection rate equivalent to the surface motion is:

$$q(\mathbf{r},t) = Q(t) \delta^3(\mathbf{r})$$

**Step 2: Write the inhomogeneous wave equation for the pressure.** Spherical symmetry gives $\nabla^2 p = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 \frac{\partial p}{\partial r}\right)$:

$$\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 \frac{\partial p}{\partial r}\right) - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = -\rho_0 \dot{Q}(t) \delta^3(\mathbf{r})$$

**Step 3: Assume outgoing spherical wave form.** $p(r,t) = \frac{A(t - r/c)}{r}$.

**Step 4: Compute derivatives.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} &= -\frac{A}{r^2} - \frac{\dot{A}}{c \, r} \\
r^2 \frac{\partial p}{\partial r} &= -A - \frac{\dot{A} r}{c} \\
\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 \frac{\partial p}{\partial r}\right) &= -\dot{A} - \frac{\dot{A}}{c} - \frac{\ddot{A} r}{c^2}
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — the proper calculation with $A$ being a function of retarded time $\tau = t - r/c$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} &= \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial A}{\partial \tau}\frac{\partial \tau}{\partial r} - \frac{A}{r^2} = -\frac{\dot{A}(\tau)}{c \, r} - \frac{A(\tau)}{r^2} \\
\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 \frac{\partial p}{\partial r}\right) &= \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left[-\frac{\dot{A}}{c} r - A\right] = \frac{1}{r^2}\left[-\frac{\dot{A}}{c} - \frac{\ddot{A}}{c^2}r - \dot{A}\frac{\partial \tau}{\partial r}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

After careful evaluation (using $\partial A/\partial r = -\dot{A}/c$ and $\partial \dot{A}/\partial r = -\ddot{A}/c$):

$$\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 \frac{\partial p}{\partial r}\right) = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\ddot{A}(\tau)}{r}$$

**Step 5: Compute time derivative of $p$.**

$$\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = \frac{\ddot{A}(\tau)}{r}$$

**Step 6: Substitute into the wave equation.** For $r \neq 0$ (away from the source), the $\delta$ term vanishes:

$$\frac{\ddot{A}(\tau)}{c^2 r} - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\ddot{A}(\tau)}{r} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

The wave equation is satisfied identically, confirming the form is correct.

**Step 7: Apply the source strength boundary condition.** Integrating the wave equation over a small sphere of radius $\epsilon$ centered at the origin and using Gauss's divergence theorem:

$$\oint_S \nabla p \cdot d\mathbf{S} = -\frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial t^2}\int_V p \, dV - \rho_0 \dot{Q}$$

The flux integral at small $\epsilon$ is dominated by the $-A/r^2$ near-field term:

$$\oint_S \frac{A}{r^2} \cdot 4\pi r^2 = 4\pi A(\tau)\big|_{r \to 0}$$

Setting the integrated source term equal:

$$-4\pi A(t) = -\rho_0 \dot{Q}(t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad A(t) = \frac{\rho_0 \dot{Q}(t)}{4\pi}$$

**Step 8: Write the final monopole field.**

$$\boxed{p_{\text{mono}}(r,t) = \frac{\rho_0 \dot{Q}(t - r/c)}{4\pi r}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The argument $t - r/c$ is the **retarded time** — the field at distance $r$ at time $t$ depends on what the source did at the *earlier* time $t - r/c$ because information travels at finite speed $c$.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Retarded-Time Solution (Kirchhoff's Integral)

We wish to show that the most general solution to the inhomogeneous scalar wave equation with source $F$ is:

$$\Psi(\mathbf{r}, t) = \int \frac{F(\mathbf{r}', t_r')}{4\pi \vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} dV'$$

where $t_r' = t - \vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert / c$ is the retarded time.

**Step 1: Introduce spherical coordinates around the source point** $\mathbf{r}'$. Let $R = \vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert$.

**Step 2: Recognize that the integrand** $F(\mathbf{r}', t - R/c)/(4\pi R)$ is *precisely* a superposition of monopole fields — each volume element $dV'$ acts as an independent monopole source with strength $F(\mathbf{r}', t - R/c) dV'$, and the resulting pressure at $\mathbf{r}$ is the integral of all contributions.

**Step 3: Linearity.** Since the wave equation is linear, the superposition principle applies, and the integral solves the equation. Boundary values of the source and continuity across $t = t_r'$ are preserved by the Dirac-delta structure of the Green's function.

**Step 4: Far-field approximation.** For $r \gg r'$ (observer far from compact source), $R \approx r - \hat{\mathbf{r}} \cdot \mathbf{r}'$, giving:

$$\Psi(\mathbf{r},t) \approx \frac{1}{4\pi r}\int F\!\left(\mathbf{r}', t - \frac{r}{c} + \frac{\hat{\mathbf{r}}\cdot\mathbf{r}'}{c}\right) dV'$$

This is the foundation of **far-field acoustic beamforming** and **acoustic holography**.

## 3.5 Solution via Separation of Variables (with Source)

For a 1-D driven string of length $L$ with fixed ends and a point source at $x_0$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} - c^2 \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2} = \frac{F_0}{\mu}\delta(x - x_0)\sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 1: Assume steady-state response** $y(x,t) = Y(x)\sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 2: Substitute.**

$$-\omega^2 Y(x) - c^2 Y''(x) = \frac{F_0}{\mu}\delta(x - x_0)$$

**Step 3: Solve homogeneous part on each side of $x_0$.** For $x \neq x_0$:

$$Y''(x) + k^2 Y(x) = 0, \quad k = \omega/c$$

Solution: $Y(x) = A\sin(kx) + B\cos(kx)$ for $x < x_0$, and $Y(x) = C\sin(kx) + D\cos(kx)$ for $x > x_0$.

**Step 4: Apply boundary conditions** $Y(0) = Y(L) = 0$ and continuity of $Y$ at $x_0$, with jump in derivative determined by integrating the equation across the delta:

$$Y'(x_0^+) - Y'(x_0^-) = -\frac{F_0}{\mu c^2}$$

**Step 5: Final result** (mode expansion, after applying all conditions):

$$Y(x) = \frac{2F_0}{\mu L c^2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{\sin(k_n x_<)\sin(k_n(L - x_>))}{k_n^2 - k^2} \cdot \frac{\sin(k_n x_0)}{k_n}$$

where $k_n = n\pi/L$ and $x_< = \min(x, x_0)$, $x_> = \max(x, x_0)$.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **resonance** when $\omega = \omega_n = n\pi c/L$ — the denominator $k_n^2 - k^2 \to 0$ vanishes, producing unbounded response. This is the well-known resonance phenomenon in forced systems, and is fundamentally different from the homogeneous case.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Inhomogeneous Wave Equation Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Domain: Source and Medium] --> B[Mathematical Model: Inhomogeneous Wave Equation]
    B --> C{Choose Solution Method}
    C --> D1[D'Alembert's Method - 1D]
    C --> D2[Separation of Variables - Modal]
    C --> D3[Green's Function - Integral]
    C --> D4[Numerical: FEM / BEM / FDTD]
    D1 --> E[Solution: Field Psi(r,t)]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Post-Processing]
    F --> G1[Sound Pressure Level SPL]
    F --> G2[Radiated Power]
    F --> G3[Frequency Spectrum]
    F --> G4[Directivity Pattern]
    G1 --> H[NVH Engineering Decision]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    A --> A1[Monopole Source]
    A --> A2[Dipole Source]
    A --> A3[Quadrupole Source]
    A --> A4[Distributed Source Field]
    A1 --> B
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    A4 --> B
    B --> B1[Left Hand Side: Wave Operator]
    B --> B2[Right Hand Side: Source Term F]
    B1 --> B3[Laplacian + Second Time Derivative]
    B2 --> B4[Mass Injection / Body Force / Lighthill Tensor]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Solution Pathway Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HomogeneousCase["Homogeneous Case: F equals 0"]
        H1[Wave Equation with Zero Source] --> H2[General Solution: A traveling wave plus B traveling wave]
        H2 --> H3[Superposition of Free Modes]
    end
    subgraph InhomogeneousCase["Inhomogeneous Case: F not equal 0"]
        I1[Wave Equation with Source F] --> I2[Homogeneous Solution plus Particular Solution]
        I2 --> I3[Particular Solution via Green Function]
        I3 --> I4[Integral of Green times Source]
    end
    HomogeneousCase --> Comparison[Inhomogeneous: More General and Physically Realistic]
    InhomogeneousCase --> Comparison
    Comparison --> KTU[KTU Exam Focus: Identify Source Type and Apply Correct Formula]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Multipole Source Hierarchy in NVH

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Source[Acoustic Source Classification] --> M0[Monopole: Order 0]
    Source --> M1[Dipole: Order 1]
    Source --> M2[Quadrupole: Order 2]
    M0 --> M0E[Example: Engine Air Intake, Pulsating Pump]
    M0 --> M0P[Power scales as omega squared]
    M0 --> M0F[Strong at LOW frequency]
    M1 --> M1E[Example: Vibrating Panel, Oscillating Piston]
    M1 --> M1P[Power scales as omega to the fourth]
    M1 --> M1F[Strong at MID frequency]
    M2 --> M2E[Example: Turbulent Jet, Exhaust Flow]
    M2 --> M2P[Power scales as omega to the sixth]
    M2 --> M2F[Strong at HIGH frequency]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Causality and Retarded Time Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Fires at t equals 0 at position r equals 0] --> B[Wave Propagates Outward at Speed c]
    B --> C[At time t, Wavefront is at Radius r equals c times t]
    C --> D[Observer at Distance R Detects Signal at t equals R over c]
    D --> E[Field depends on Retarded Time: t subscript r equals t minus R over c]
    E --> F[Information cannot travel faster than c - CAUSALITY]
    F --> G[Implication: Source past determines field present]
```
