# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a bluff body and give two automotive examples. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **bluff body** is a solid object immersed in a fluid flow whose geometry causes the fluid streamlines to *separate* from its surface, forming a turbulent wake. This is in contrast to a *streamlined (aerodynamic) body*, where the flow remains attached.

**Definition Equation (Reynolds-number independent characteristic):**
A body is "bluff" if its frontal area $A_f$ is large relative to its streamwise length $L$, typically $A_f / L^2 > 0.1$.

**Two automotive examples:**
1. **Side-view mirror** — produces an audible whistle at 80–120 km/h.
2. **Antenna mast (whip antenna)** — generates strong Aeolian tones.

*(Acceptable alternatives: windshield wiper blade, roof rack cross-bar, exhaust tip, HVAC grille, license plate.)*

---

### Question 2: What is the Strouhal number? State its typical value for a circular cylinder. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Strouhal number** ($St$) is a dimensionless parameter that characterizes the frequency of vortex shedding from a bluff body. It is defined as:

$$
St = \frac{f_s \, D}{U_\infty}
$$

where:
- $f_s$ = vortex shedding frequency (Hz)
- $D$ = characteristic body dimension (m)
- $U_\infty$ = free-stream velocity (m/s)

**Typical value for a circular cylinder** in the subcritical Reynolds number range ($10^2 < Re < 10^5$):

$$
St \approx 0.21
$$

**Significance in NVH:** It allows engineers to predict the dominant tonal noise frequency from the geometry and flow speed — a critical design tool for avoiding resonance with structural modes.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Key Pattern — Read Carefully:**
> - **Sub-part (a) at 7 marks** typically tests *theory + formula* (definition, derivation, mechanism). Show all derivation steps — examiners award partial credit per step.
> - **Sub-part (b) at 7 marks** typically tests *application / numerical problem*. Always: (i) write the formula, (ii) substitute values *with units*, (iii) show intermediate steps, (iv) box the final answer.
> - **Common mark losses**: Skipping units, not stating assumptions, writing "Ans: 256" instead of "256 Hz" or "f_s = 256 Hz".

---

### ❓ Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the phenomenon of **Kármán vortex shedding** from a circular cylinder. Define the Strouhal number and discuss its dependence on Reynolds number. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **circular antenna** of diameter $D = 8$ mm is mounted on a car traveling at $U_\infty = 120$ km/h. Assuming $St = 0.21$ and standard sea-level air properties ($\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $c_0 = 340$ m/s), determine:
- (i) The vortex shedding frequency $f_s$.
- (ii) The wavelength of the radiated sound.
- (iii) Whether this frequency lies in the *sensitivity band of human hearing* (20 Hz – 20 kHz). **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Sketch (2 marks):** Draw a horizontal cylinder with alternating vortices labeled A, B, A, B in a staggered pattern downstream. Mark the separation points and the wake width $h \approx 0.281 D$ (Kármán's classical result).

**2. Mechanism of vortex shedding (2 marks):**
- The boundary layer separates asymmetrically from the cylinder.
- On one side, a clockwise vortex forms; on the other, a counter-clockwise vortex.
- They shed alternately, producing the **Kármán vortex street**.
- The frequency of shedding is regular and tone-like.

**3. Strouhal number definition (1 mark):**

$$
St = \frac{f_s \, D}{U_\infty}
$$

**4. Dependence on Reynolds number (2 marks):**
- For $Re < 40$: No vortex shedding; flow is steady.
- For $40 < Re < 200$: $St \approx 0.21$ (Kármán regime, **laminar**).
- For $200 < Re < 10^5$: $St$ remains near 0.21, but wake becomes 3D and turbulent.
- For $Re > 10^5$: $St$ begins to drop (drag crisis) as the boundary layer transitions to turbulence before separation.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $D = 8 \text{ mm} = 0.008 \text{ m}$
- $U_\infty = 120 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{120 \times 1000}{3600} = 33.33 \text{ m/s}$
- $St = 0.21$
- $c_0 = 340 \text{ m/s}$

**(i) Vortex Shedding Frequency (3 marks):**

$$
f_s = \frac{St \cdot U_\infty}{D}
$$

$$
f_s = \frac{0.21 \times 33.33}{0.008}
$$

$$
f_s = \frac{7.00}{0.008}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_s \approx 875 \text{ Hz}}
$$

**(ii) Wavelength of Radiated Sound (2 marks):**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c_0}{f_s} = \frac{340}{875}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda \approx 0.389 \text{ m} \approx 38.9 \text{ cm}}
$$

**(iii) Hearing Sensitivity (2 marks):**

Since $20 \text{ Hz} < 875 \text{ Hz} < 20\,000 \text{ Hz}$, the shedding frequency **lies well within the human hearing range**. Furthermore, 875 Hz falls in the most sensitive band of human hearing (1–4 kHz), so the antenna will produce a *highly noticeable* tonal whine — a typical NVH complaint. The antenna's slender whip design is a classic Aeolian-tone problem in automotive NVH.

---

### ❓ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain **Lighthill's acoustic analogy** for sound generated by a stationary solid body in a uniform flow. Why is such a body modeled as an **acoustic dipole**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The side mirror of a car has a projected area $A = 0.04 \text{ m}^2$ and a drag coefficient $C_D = 1.1$. At a vehicle speed of $U_\infty = 30$ m/s in standard air, calculate:
- (i) The drag force on the mirror.
- (ii) The acoustic power radiated (assuming 0.1% of drag power converts to sound).
- (iii) The sound pressure level at a point 3 m from the mirror (assume spherical spreading). **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Lighthill's acoustic analogy (3 marks):**
Lighthill (1952) reformulated the Navier–Stokes equations into an inhomogeneous wave equation:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} - c_0^2 \, \nabla^2 \rho' = \frac{\partial^2 T_{ij}}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}
$$

where $T_{ij}$ is the **Lighthill stress tensor**. The right-hand side acts as an *equivalent acoustic source* term.

**2. Curle's extension for solid bodies (2 marks):**
Curle applied Lighthill's analogy to a stationary rigid body and showed that the body's unsteady surface pressures act as **acoustic sources distributed over the body surface**. The far-field pressure is given by:

$$
p'(\vec{x}, t) = \frac{1}{4\pi c_0^2} \int_S \frac{x_i - y_i}{r^2} \cdot \frac{\partial p_s}{\partial t} \, dS
$$

**3. Why dipole? (2 marks):**
- The source is proportional to $\partial p_s / \partial t$, which is a *first spatial derivative* of an unsteady surface pressure.
- A first-derivative source corresponds to a **dipole** in acoustic terminology.
- The dipole has a $\cos^2\theta$ directivity pattern — strongest broadside to the flow.
- The radiated acoustic power scales as $U^6$ (i.e., $M^5$ for low Mach) — a steep speed dependence.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $A = 0.04 \text{ m}^2$
- $C_D = 1.1$
- $U_\infty = 30 \text{ m/s}$
- $\rho = 1.204 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Conversion efficiency $\eta = 0.001$ (0.1%)

**(i) Drag Force (2 marks):**

$$
F_D = C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2 \cdot A
$$

$$
F_D = 1.1 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 1.204 \times (30)^2 \times 0.04
$$

$$
F_D = 1.1 \times 0.602 \times 900 \times 0.04
$$

$$
F_D = 1.1 \times 21.67
$$

$$
\boxed{F_D \approx 23.84 \text{ N}}
$$

**(ii) Acoustic Power (2 marks):**
Drag power (rate of work done against drag):

$$
P_{\text{drag}} = F_D \cdot U_\infty = 23.84 \times 30 = 715.2 \text{ W}
$$

Acoustic power:

$$
P_{\text{acoustic}} = \eta \cdot P_{\text{drag}} = 0.001 \times 715.2
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{acoustic}} \approx 0.715 \text{ W}}
$$

**(iii) Sound Pressure Level at 3 m (3 marks):**
Assume spherical spreading; intensity at distance $r$:

$$
I = \frac{P_{\text{acoustic}}}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{0.715}{4\pi \times (3)^2}
$$

$$
I = \frac{0.715}{113.1} \approx 6.32 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W/m}^2
$$

Sound pressure level:

$$
SPL = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right), \quad I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12} \text{ W/m}^2
$$

$$
SPL = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{6.32 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(6.32 \times 10^{9})
$$

$$
SPL = 10 \times (9 + \log_{10} 6.32) = 10 \times (9 + 0.801)
$$

$$
\boxed{SPL \approx 98.0 \text{ dB}}
$$

This is uncomfortably loud for a passenger cabin — explaining why modern cars use **aerodynamic shaping, vortex disrupters, and acoustic foam** behind side mirrors.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Most Common Mistakes on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** in numerical problems. Always: $U \text{ [m/s]} = U \text{ [km/h]} \times \frac{5}{18}$.
> 2. **Using wrong Strouhal number** for non-circular sections (e.g., $St \approx 0.15$ for square, $0.12$ for triangular). KTU loves testing this.
> 3. **Confusing $U^5$ (monopole/turbulence) with $U^6$ (dipole/bluff body)** in Lighthill's law. Bluff body = dipole = $U^6$.
> 4. **Mixing up density and kinematic viscosity** in Reynolds number formula.
> 5. **Not specifying $Re$** when stating $St = 0.21$ — examiners deduct for this.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Bluff Body Flow NVH**

- ✅ A **bluff body** causes flow separation; a streamlined body does not.
- ✅ The **Reynolds number** $Re = U_\infty D / \nu$ determines the flow regime and hence the noise type.
- ✅ **Kármán vortex street** is the canonical periodic wake pattern — dominant noise source in the range $40 < Re < 10^5$.
- ✅ The **Strouhal number** $St = f_s D / U_\infty \approx 0.21$ for circular cylinders in the subcritical range.
- ✅ Vortex shedding frequency: $f_s = St \cdot U_\infty / D$ — higher speed = higher pitch; larger body = lower pitch.
- ✅ **Aeolian tone** = the tonal noise emitted by a bluff body due to vortex shedding (e.g., wire in wind, antenna on car).
- ✅ **Lighthill's analogy**: Bluff body = acoustic dipole; directivity $\propto \cos^2\theta$.
- ✅ **Curle's extension**: Far-field pressure $\propto \dfrac{1}{r} \dfrac{\partial p_s}{\partial t}$.
- ✅ **$U^6$ law (or $M^5$)**: Bluff body acoustic power scales steeply with speed — a 30% speed rise = ~3 dB louder.
- ✅ **Drag force** $F_D = C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2 A$ — drives both fuel consumption and structural excitation.
- ✅ **Vortex-Induced Vibration (VIV)**: When $f_s$ matches a structural natural frequency, "lock-in" occurs — risk of fatigue failure (Tacoma Narrows lesson).
- ✅ Flow regimes: Stokes → Steady → **Kármán** → 3D Turbulent → Supercritical — each with distinct NVH signatures.
- ✅ For $Re > 10^5$, **drag crisis** reduces $C_D$ sharply but broadens noise spectrum.
- ✅ **Noise control strategies**: streamlining, fairings, vortex generators, porous covers, cavity liners, active flow control.

> 🎯 **One-line exam mnemonic**: *"Strouhal's song: small bodies sing high, fast flow sings higher, separation is the singer."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Powell Howe Theory of Vortex Sound
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** The Powell Howe Theory of Vortex Sound ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition The **Powell–Howe Theory of Vortex Sound** is a foundational aeroacoustic framework developed originally by **Alan Powell (1964)** and rigorously extended by **M. S. Howe (1975, 2003)**. The theory classifies the **unsteady vortical motion** in a fluid as the principal source of sound. It states that aerodynamic sound is generated wherever the **Lamb vector** is non-conservative, i.e., wherever its curl is non-zero. Howe further reformulated this in terms of the *unsteady work done by the fluid-dynamic (vortical) forces on the irrotational acoustic field*, leading to the celebrated **vortex-sound theorem**. Mathematically, sound is produced in regions where: $$ \nabla \times (\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}) \neq \mathbf{0} $$ where $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \nabla \times \mathbf{v}$ is the **vorticity** vector and $\mathbf{v}$ is the fluid velocity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEAUT755, Module 1):** The theory forms the analytical core of *Lighthill’s acoustic analogy applied to unsteady vortical flows*. It is a direct competitor to Lighthill’s stress-tensor formulation but is far more physically transparent and computationally efficient for incompressible / low-Mach flows. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **whirlpool forming in a calm lake**. The whirlpool itself is a rotating structure (vorticity). It doesn’t make sound in a still, uniform lake. But now tilt the water surface or push the whirlpool through a **non-uniform region** (a contraction, an obstacle, a shear layer). Suddenly, the whirlpool *distorts* — its rotation interacts with the surrounding flow, and the water begins to oscillate as **pressure waves** (sound) radiate outward. That distortion — the *interaction of vorticity with a non-uniform velocity field* — is precisely what the **Lamb vector** $\mathbf{L} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$ quantifies. The Powell–Howe theory tells us: > **"Vortices do not speak. Only *changing* vortices — those that encounter gradients in the surrounding flow — radiate sound."** A silent whistle in a frictionless bath, a vibrating tuning fork, a compressor blade shedding a Karman vortex street — all of these are the *same* physical phenomenon, mathematically captured by Powell and Howe. ### Key Physical Quantities (Bolded Constants & Vectors) | Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | $\mathbf{v}$ | Fluid velocity | m/s | Total (mean + fluctuating) | | $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \nabla \times \mathbf{v}$ | Vorticity | 1/s | Measures local rotation | | $\mathbf{L} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$ | **Lamb vector** | m/s² | Acceleration due to vorticity | | $p$ | Acoustic pressure | Pa | Perturbation pressure | | $\rho_0$ | Mean fluid density | kg/m³ | **1.225 kg/m³** (air at STP) | | $c_0$ | Speed of sound | m/s | **343 m/s** (air at 20 °C) | | $P$ | Acoustic power | W | Radiated sound power | > [!NOTE] > The Lamb vector has units of acceleration. It is the *vortical acceleration* in the flow and is the *kinematic source* of sound in Powell–Howe theory. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Origin of the Theory — From Lighthill to Powell to Howe **Step 1 — Lighthill’s Acoustic Analogy (1952):** Lighthill rearranged the exact Navier–Stokes equations to obtain an inhomogeneous wave equation for the density perturbation $\rho'$: $$ \frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} - c_0^2 \nabla^2 \rho' = \frac{\partial^2 T_{ij}}{\partial x_i \partial x_j} $$ where $T_{ij} = \rho v_i v_j + (p - c_0^2 \rho)\delta_{ij} - \sigma_{ij}$ is **Lighthill’s stress tensor**. This formulation is *exact* but treats the flow as an acoustic quadrupole source distribution — it is difficult to interpret physically. **Step 2 — Powell’s Insight (1964):** Powell recognized that the dominant acoustic source in low-Mach flows is the *vorticity field* itself. Taking the curl of Lighthill’s momentum equation and using vector identities, he arrived at the **Vorticity Transport Equation with Acoustic Source Term**: $$ \frac{\partial \boldsymbol{\omega}}{\partial t} = \nabla \times (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) + \nu \nabla^2 \boldsymbol{\omega} $$ This implies that the **unsteady Lamb vector $\mathbf{L} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$** acts as the *source* of vorticity changes, and therefore of acoustic radiation. **Step 3 — Howe’s Refinement (1975):** Howe separated the velocity field into an irrotational acoustic part $\mathbf{v}_a$ and a rotational vortical part $\mathbf{v}_v$ (with $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}_a + \mathbf{v}_v$). He then showed that the rate of change of total fluid kinetic energy equals the radiated acoustic power: $$ \boxed{\,P_{\text{acoustic}} = \rho_0 \int_V (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) \cdot \mathbf{v}_a \, dV\,} $$ This is the celebrated **Howe Vortex-Sound Theorem**. The term $\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}$ (note the sign reversal — Howe’s convention) is the **vortical force** that does work on the *irrotational* acoustic field. > [!NOTE] > The sign reversal between Powell’s Lamb vector $\mathbf{L} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$ and Howe’s $\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega} = -\mathbf{L}$ is conventional; both formulations are equivalent. The acoustic work is *positive* only when the Lamb vector is aligned with the *convected* acoustic velocity. ### 2.2 Why Vortices Radiate Sound — The Physical ‘How’ A *frozen* vortex in a uniform mean flow is **silent** (potential flow past a cylinder of infinitesimal thickness). Sound appears when: 1. **Vortex stretching / tilting:** Vorticity $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ is stretched or tilted by a non-uniform $\mathbf{v}$, producing a non-zero $\partial \boldsymbol{\omega} / \partial t$. 2. **Vortex–surface interaction:** A vortex passes through a region of spatially varying mean velocity (e.g., a shear layer, a contraction), changing its strength and position. 3. **Vortex–obstacle interaction:** Periodic shedding (Karman vortex street) off a bluff body oscillates the force on the body, radiating *dipole* sound. In each case, the *unsteady* component of $\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}$ is non-zero, and per Howe’s theorem, this produces a non-zero acoustic power. ### 2.3 The Convected Pressure — Howe’s Key Construct Howe introduced the **convected pressure** $p_c$ as the pressure field that, when added to the fluctuating pressure, makes the *total* velocity field *irrotational*. Formally, the acoustic velocity $\mathbf{v}_a$ is given by: $$ \mathbf{v}_a = \nabla \phi, \quad \text{where} \quad p_c = -\rho_0 \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_0 |\nabla \phi|^2 $$ In low-Mach linear acoustics, $p_c \approx p$ (the acoustic pressure). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Meaning | Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \nabla \times \mathbf{v}$ | Vorticity definition | General | | 2 | $\mathbf{L} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$ | Lamb vector (Powell) | Incompressible / low-Mach | | 3 | $\nabla \times (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) \neq 0$ | Source condition for sound | Powell’s criterion | | 4 | $\nabla^2 p - \dfrac{1}{c_0^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = \rho_0 \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\left[\nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})\right]$ | **Howe’s acoustic wave equation** | Incompressible source | | 5 | $P = \rho_0 \int_V (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) \cdot \mathbf{v}_a \, dV$ | **Howe’s Vortex-Sound Power Theorem** | Exact (linear) | | 6 | $p(\mathbf{x},t) = \dfrac{\rho_0}{4\pi}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_V \dfrac{\nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})}{\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \vert}\,dV(\mathbf{y})$ | **Howe’s acoustic far-field pressure** | Compact source | | 7 | $p \sim \dfrac{\rho_0 L^2 U^2 \omega}{4\pi r c_0}$ | Compact dipole scaling | $M = U/c_0 \ll 1$ | | 8 | $P \sim \rho_0 M^5 D^2 U^3$ | Lighthill’s 8th power law (Mach scaling) | Turbulent jet, $M \ll 1$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** The Powell–Howe theorem is the workhorse for predicting fan/compressor noise, HVAC duct noise, helicopter blade-vortex interaction (BVI) noise, and wind-turbin aeroacoustics. It is the analytical backbone of modern industrial **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness)** software. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Aero-engine fan/compressor noise (turbofan nacelles):** Nacelle liners are designed to suppress dipole noise from unsteady blade-row vorticity — directly predicted by Howe’s theorem. - **Helicopter BVI noise:** Blade tip vortices interact with the airframe; the *vortex-sound* formulation captures the impulsive “slap” noise. - **Wind turbine aerodynamic noise:** Trailing edge noise and tip-vortex noise are modeled using Powell–Howe dipole sources. - **Cavity flows & sunroofs/windshield whistle:** Automobile NVH engineers use Howe’s theorem to locate whistling sources. - **Industrial fans and HVAC duct bends:** Vortex shedding at bends radiates tonal noise, predicted by the vortex-sound theorem. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Navier–Stokes to Powell’s Vorticity Transport with Acoustic Source We start from the **incompressible** Navier–Stokes momentum equation: $$ \rho_0 \frac{D\mathbf{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} $$ **Step 1:** Take the curl of both sides. Since the curl of a gradient is zero, $\nabla \times (\nabla p) = 0$. Also use the vector identity: $$ \frac{D\boldsymbol{\omega}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \boldsymbol{\omega}}{\partial t} + \nabla \times (\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}) + \boldsymbol{\omega} (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) $$ **Step 2:** For incompressible flow, $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$, so the last term vanishes. After dividing by $\rho_0$: $$ \frac{\partial \boldsymbol{\omega}}{\partial t} = \nabla \times (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) + \nu \nabla^2 \boldsymbol{\omega} $$ where $\nu = \mu / \rho_0$ is the **kinematic viscosity**. **Step 3:** *Powell’s physical interpretation:* The term $\nabla \times (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})$ is the *inviscid source* of vorticity changes. This is the same Lamb vector that powers the sound radiation. Taking the curl of the Lamb vector gives a non-zero result when vortices are *stretched* or *tilted* by a non-uniform velocity — exactly the acoustic-source condition. > **Physical Logic:** Vorticity is a material quantity in inviscid flow (Kelvin’s theorem). It changes only when (a) viscous diffusion acts, or (b) vortex lines are stretched/tilted by a non-uniform velocity field. The Lamb vector quantifies (b). Since vorticity stretching and tilting are *unsteady*, they radiate sound. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Howe’s Acoustic Wave Equation from the Linearized Momentum Equation **Step 1:** Decompose the total velocity and pressure into *mean* and *perturbation* parts: $$ \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}_0 + \mathbf{v}', \quad p = p_0 + p' $$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the linearized Navier–Stokes (viscous terms dropped for inviscid far-field radiation): $$ \rho_0 \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 (\mathbf{v}_0 \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{v}' + \rho_0 (\mathbf{v}' \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{v}_0 = -\nabla p' $$ **Step 3:** Using the identity $\nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) = \boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \nabla \times \mathbf{v} - \mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla \times \boldsymbol{\omega}$ and noting $\nabla \times \mathbf{v}' = \boldsymbol{\omega}'$: After algebraic manipulation (see Howe 2003, Ch. 2), one arrives at the **incompressible Helmholtz-like equation for acoustic pressure**: $$ \nabla^2 p' - \frac{1}{c_0^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = \rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\bigl[\nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})\bigr] $$ **Step 4:** The right-hand side is the **Howe acoustic source term** — it is a *dipole-like* distribution proportional to the divergence of the Lamb vector cross product. In free space, the retarded-potential solution gives the far-field pressure: $$ p'(\mathbf{x}, t) = \frac{\rho_0}{4\pi}\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_V \frac{\nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})}{\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \vert}\,dV(\mathbf{y}) \bigg|_{t_{\text{ret}}} $$ > **Logic Check:** The source is *linear* in the Lamb vector and *first-order* in time. The $1/\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \vert$ dependence confirms a **dipole** radiation pattern (sound intensity falls as $1/r^2$, power as $1/r$ for a finite source — classic 3-D dipole behaviour). ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Howe’s Vortex-Sound Power Theorem **Step 1:** Start with the kinetic-energy balance for the total flow field: $$ \frac{d}{dt}\int_V \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 \vert \mathbf{v} \vert^2 \, dV = -\oint_S p \, \mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dA $$ **Step 2:** Decompose the velocity into *irrotational* (acoustic) and *rotational* (vortical) parts: $$ \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}_a + \mathbf{v}_v, \quad \text{where} \quad \nabla \times \mathbf{v}_a = 0, \quad \nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}_a \neq 0 $$ **Step 3:** Substitute and isolate the *acoustic* kinetic energy term $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_0 \vert \mathbf{v}_a \vert^2$. After cancellation of cross-terms and using the divergence theorem, one obtains the **Howe Vortex-Sound Theorem**: $$ \boxed{\,P_{\text{acoustic}} = \rho_0 \int_V (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) \cdot \mathbf{v}_a \, dV\,} $$ > **Interpretation:** The integrand $(\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) \cdot \mathbf{v}_a$ is the *instantaneous rate of work* done by the vortical forces on the irrotational acoustic field. Integrated over the entire flow volume, this gives the total radiated acoustic power. ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Compact Dipole Source — Far-Field Pressure Estimate For a *compact* (sub-wavelength) source of characteristic size $L$ and characteristic velocity $U$ at radial distance $r$: **Step 1:** Estimate the source term magnitude: $$ \nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) \sim \frac{U^2}{L^2} $$ (velocity $\sim U$, vorticity $\sim U/L$, Lamb vector $\sim U^2/L$, divergence $\sim 1/L$). **Step 2:** Estimate the volume integral over a *compact* source of size $L$: $$ \int_V \nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})\,dV \sim \frac{U^2}{L^2} \cdot L^3 = U^2 L $$ **Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to time ($\partial/\partial t \sim \omega = 2\pi f$): $$ \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_V \nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})\,dV \sim \omega U^2 L $$ **Step 4:** The far-field pressure from a compact dipole source at distance $r$ scales as: $$ p \sim \frac{\rho_0 \, \omega \, U^2 \, L^2}{4\pi r \, c_0} $$ **Step 5:** Acoustic intensity (dipole): $$ I \sim \frac{p^2}{\rho_0 c_0} \sim \frac{\rho_0 \, \omega^2 \, U^4 \, L^4}{c_0^3 \, r^2} $$ **Step 6:** Acoustic power for a single dipole of area $L^2$: $$ \boxed{\,P \sim \rho_0 \, M^5 \, D^2 \, U^3\,} $$ where $M = U/c_0$ and $D \sim L$. This is **Lighthill’s 8th-power law** for low-Mach dipole sources — a direct *corollary* of the Powell–Howe theory. ### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix | Aspect | Lighthill’s Analogy | Powell’s Vortex Sound | Howe’s Vortex-Sound Theorem | |---|---|---|---| | **Source term** | $\partial^2 T_{ij}/\partial x_i \partial x_j$ | $\nabla \times (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})$ | $\rho_0 \partial_t [\nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})]$ | | **Multipole order** | Quadrupole | Dipole (for $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{L} \neq 0$) | Dipole | | **Mach scaling** | $M^8$ | $M^5$ | $M^5$ | | **Best for** | High-Mach jet noise | Vortex-sound generation | Vortex-obstacle interaction, BVI | | **Computational cost** | High (full $T_{ij}$) | Moderate (vector field) | Moderate (volume integral) | | **Physical clarity** | Low | High | Very High | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Powell–Howe Vortex-Sound Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[VorticalFlowInput

Helmholtz with source term]:::compute E[RetardedPotential
Integral over source region]:::compute F[FarFieldPressure

NVH predictions and NVH design]:::output A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F C --> G F --> H G --> H classDef input fill:#dbe9ff,stroke:#1f3a93,color:#0b1d51 classDef compute fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a37800,color:#3d2c00 classDef output fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1e7a1e,color:#0d3a0d ``` **Interpretation of the Block Diagram:** 1. **Input Block (Blue):** The raw CFD or analytical flow field provides the velocity $\mathbf{v}$ and the vorticity $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \nabla \times \mathbf{v}$. 2. **Compute Block (Yellow):** The Lamb vector $\mathbf{L} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$ is computed pointwise, its divergence is taken, and the inhomogeneous acoustic wave equation is solved (or its retarded-potential integral evaluated). 3. **Output Block (Green):** The far-field acoustic pressure $p'$ and the integrated acoustic power $P = \rho_0 \int (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) \cdot \mathbf{v}_a \, dV$ are delivered as engineering KPIs for NVH analysis. ### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: From Vortex Distortion to Radiated Sound ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Stationary Vortex

As soon as the vortex is *distorted* by a non-uniform mean velocity (e.g., passing through a shear layer, a contraction, or a bluff-body wake), the Lamb vector acquires a non-zero curl, an *acoustic source* is born, and pressure waves propagate outward at $c_0 = 343$ m/s, eventually reaching the observer as measurable sound. ### 4.3 Schematic of the Acoustic Source Geometry ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SG[Source Volume V] V1[Vortex Core 1
omega concentrated]:::vortex V2[Vortex Core 2

This makes it the **preferred method for industrial NVH simulations** of fans, compressors, wind turbines, and helicopter rotors — typically an order of magnitude faster than full Lighthill solvers. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]** > State **Powell’s theorem of vortex sound**. What physical quantity constitutes the acoustic source in this formulation? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Powell’s theorem of vortex sound states that *aerodynamic sound is generated in regions where the curl of the Lamb vector is non-zero*, i.e., where: $$ \nabla \times (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) \neq \mathbf{0} $$ The **physical quantity** constituting the acoustic source is the **Lamb vector** $\mathbf{L} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$, which represents the *acceleration due to vorticity*. The non-conservative part of this Lamb vector (its curl) is the source of sound. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]** > What is the **Lamb vector**? Why is it considered the *kinematic source* of aerodynamic sound in Powell–Howe theory? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Lamb vector** is defined as the cross product of vorticity and velocity: $$ \mathbf{L} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v} $$ It has units of acceleration (m/s²) and physically represents the *unsteady acceleration* imparted to a fluid particle by the local rotational field. In Powell–Howe theory, it is the *kinematic source* of sound because: 1. Its magnitude is non-zero only in regions of rotational flow, i.e., where vorticity exists. **(1 mark)** 2. Its curl $\nabla \times (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) \neq 0$ identifies regions of *vortex stretching/tilting* that radiate sound. **(1 mark)** 3. The integral $\rho_0 \int_V (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) \cdot \mathbf{v}_a \, dV$ gives the *acoustic power* radiated by the flow. **(1 mark)** --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified] (14 Marks)** > **(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Howe acoustic wave equation** for the perturbation pressure $p'$ in an incompressible, unsteady vortical flow, starting from the linearized Navier–Stokes equations. Clearly identify the source term. > > **(b) [7 marks]** State and prove **Howe’s vortex-sound power theorem**. Discuss its physical significance for industrial NVH applications. **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of Howe’s acoustic wave equation [7 marks]:** *Step 1:* Linearize the Navier–Stokes equation about a mean flow $\mathbf{v}_0$. For an inviscid, incompressible flow: $$ \rho_0 \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 (\mathbf{v}_0 \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{v}' + \rho_0 (\mathbf{v}' \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{v}_0 = -\nabla p' $$ *Step 2:* Use the vector identity $(\mathbf{v}' \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{v}_0 = \nabla(\mathbf{v}' \cdot \mathbf{v}_0) - \mathbf{v}' \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{v}_0) - (\mathbf{v}_0 \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{v}'$ and the definition of perturbation vorticity $\boldsymbol{\omega}' = \nabla \times \mathbf{v}'$. After manipulation: $$ \rho_0 \left[\frac{\partial \mathbf{v}'}{\partial t} + (\mathbf{v}_0 \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{v}' + \nabla(\mathbf{v}' \cdot \mathbf{v}_0)\right] - \rho_0 \mathbf{v}' \times \boldsymbol{\omega}_0 = -\nabla p' $$ *Step 3:* Identify the *vortical source term* as $\rho_0 \mathbf{v}' \times \boldsymbol{\omega}_0$. Note that the mean-flow vorticity $\boldsymbol{\omega}_0$ interacts with the fluctuating velocity $\mathbf{v}'$ — this is the *Lamb-vector analogue* in linearized form. *Step 4:* Take the divergence of both sides and use $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}' = -(1/c_0^2)\partial p'/\partial t$ from the continuity equation. After lengthy vector algebra, one arrives at: $$ \boxed{\,\nabla^2 p' - \frac{1}{c_0^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = \rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left[\nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})\right]\,} $$ The right-hand side is the **Howe acoustic source term**. **(7 marks — full step-by-step must be shown for full credit)** **(b) Howe’s vortex-sound power theorem [7 marks]:** *Statement:* The total acoustic power radiated by an unsteady incompressible flow is: $$ P = \rho_0 \int_V (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) \cdot \mathbf{v}_a \, dV $$ *Proof sketch:* - Begin with the kinetic energy theorem: $\frac{d}{dt}\int_V \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_0 \vert \mathbf{v} \vert^2\,dV = -\oint_S p\, \mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n}\,dA$. - Decompose $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}_a + \mathbf{v}_v$ where $\mathbf{v}_a$ is irrotational (acoustic) and $\mathbf{v}_v$ is solenoidal (vortical). - The right-hand side surface integral yields the *acoustic power flux* $-\oint_S p\, \mathbf{v}_a \cdot \mathbf{n}\,dA$. - After cancellation of cross-terms $\mathbf{v}_a \cdot \mathbf{v}_v$, the volume integral reduces to the *Lamb-vector work* on $\mathbf{v}_a$. QED. **(5 marks)** *Physical significance (2 marks):* - Industrial NVH applications: The theorem enables *direct* computation of acoustic power from CFD velocity fields without solving the full Lighthill stress tensor. - Used in fan/compressor noise prediction, helicopter BVI, and wind turbine noise. - Reduces computational cost by ~10× compared to Lighthill methods. - Provides a *physical* interpretation: sound is the work done by vortical forces on the irrotational acoustic field. **(2 marks)** --- **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE (14 Marks)** > **(a) [7 marks]** With a clear derivation, show that **Lighthill’s acoustic analogy reduces to Powell’s vortex-sound formulation** for low-Mach, incompressible flows. Identify the source-term equivalence. > > **(b) [7 marks]** A small compact vortex source of characteristic size $L = 0.1$ m oscillates at frequency $f = 500$ Hz in air ($\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³, $c_0 = 343$ m/s). The fluctuating velocity amplitude is $U = 5$ m/s. Estimate (i) the far-field acoustic pressure at $r = 10$ m, and (ii) the radiated acoustic power. **Model Solution:** **(a) Lighthill → Powell reduction [7 marks]:** *Step 1:* Lighthill’s exact acoustic analogy: $$ \frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} - c_0^2 \nabla^2 \rho' = \frac{\partial^2 T_{ij}}{\partial x_i \partial x_j} $$ *Step 2:* For low-Mach, incompressible flow, $T_{ij} \approx \rho_0 v_i v_j$ (the Reynolds-stress part dominates; viscous and pressure terms are negligible). Thus: $$ \frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} - c_0^2 \nabla^2 \rho' \approx \rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 (v_i v_j)}{\partial x_i \partial x_j} $$ *Step 3:* Use the vector identity $\nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) = \boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \boldsymbol{\omega} - \mathbf{v} \cdot (\nabla \times \boldsymbol{\omega})$ together with the vorticity transport equation to recast the double divergence as: $$ \frac{\partial^2 (v_i v_j)}{\partial x_i \partial x_j} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\bigl[\nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})\bigr] + O(M^2 \text{ terms}) $$ *Step 4:* Substituting back, with $\rho' \approx p'/c_0^2$ in the acoustic limit: $$ \nabla^2 p' - \frac{1}{c_0^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = \rho_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\bigl[\nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})\bigr] $$ This is **Howe’s equation**, and its source is **Powell’s Lamb-vector divergence**. Equivalence established. **(7 marks)** **(b) Numerical estimation [7 marks]:** *Given:* $L = 0.1$ m, $f = 500$ Hz, $U = 5$ m/s, $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³, $c_0 = 343$ m/s, $r = 10$ m. *Step 1 — Mach number:* $$ M = \frac{U}{c_0} = \frac{5}{343} \approx 0.0146 $$ *Step 2 — Angular frequency:* $$ \omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 500 \approx 3141.6 \text{ rad/s} $$ *Step 3 — Far-field pressure (compact dipole scaling):* $$ p \sim \frac{\rho_0 \, \omega \, U^2 \, L^2}{4\pi r \, c_0} $$ *Step 4 — Numerical substitution:* $$ p \sim \frac{1.225 \times 3141.6 \times 25 \times 0.01}{4\pi \times 10 \times 343} $$ Numerator: $1.225 \times 3141.6 \times 0.25 = 962.2$ Denominator: $4\pi \times 10 \times 343 \approx 43{,}098$ $$ p \sim \frac{962.2}{43{,}098} \approx 0.0223 \text{ Pa} \approx 22.3 \text{ mPa} $$ **(i) Answer: $p \approx 0.022$ Pa at $r = 10$ m** (1 mark) *Step 5 — Sound pressure level:* $$ L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.0223}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) \approx 61 \text{ dB} $$ *Step 6 — Acoustic power (dipole, 8th-power-law scaling):* $$ P \sim \rho_0 M^5 D^2 U^3 $$ where $D \approx L = 0.1$ m. *Step 7 — Numerical substitution:* $$ P \sim 1.225 \times (0.0146)^5 \times (0.1)^2 \times (5)^3 $$ $M^5 = (0.0146)^5 \approx 6.79 \times 10^{-10}$ $D^2 = 0.01$ $U^3 = 125$ $$ P \sim 1.225 \times 6.79 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.01 \times 125 \approx 1.04 \times 10^{-9} \text{ W} $$ **(ii) Answer: $P \approx 1$ nW (1 nanowatt)** (1 mark) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes to Avoid:** > 1. **Sign error in the Lamb vector** — students often write $\mathbf{L} = \mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}$ instead of $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$. Both are used in different conventions; **explicitly state the convention** to avoid losing 1 mark. > 2. **Forgetting the time derivative** in the source term of Howe’s equation. The source is $\partial_t [\nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})]$, **not** $\nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})$ alone. (-1 mark) > 3. **Confusing dipole and quadrupole scalings.** Compact vortex sources radiate as **dipoles** ($M^5$), not quadrupoles ($M^8$). The Mach number exponent is the surest way to lose a mark. (-1 mark) > 4. **Skipping derivation steps.** KTU examiners award step-by-step marks. Always show the vector identity used and the substitution chain. A boxed final answer alone earns only ~40% of the marks. > 5. **Units not shown** in numerical problems: KTU deducts 1 mark if the final numerical answer does not have units (Pa, W, dB, etc.). --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Lamb vector:** $\mathbf{L} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$ — the *kinematic* source of aerodynamic sound; has units of acceleration (m/s²). *(High-frequency KTU question)* - **Powell’s criterion:** Sound is generated wherever $\nabla \times (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) \neq \mathbf{0}$. *(Favourite 3-mark short-answer topic)* - **Howe’s acoustic wave equation:** $\nabla^2 p' - \dfrac{1}{c_0^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = \rho_0 \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\bigl[\nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega})\bigr]$. *(High-yield 14-mark derivation)* - **Howe’s vortex-sound power theorem:** $P = \rho_0 \int_V (\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}) \cdot \mathbf{v}_a \, dV$. *(Directly tested in ESE)* - **Convected pressure:** The pressure that makes the total velocity irrotational when added to the fluctuating field; $p_c \approx p$ in linear acoustics. - **Multipole nature:** Powell–Howe sources are **dipole**, scaling as $M^5$ (not quadrupole/$M^8$ like Lighthill). *(Trap question — easy to lose 1 mark)* - **Compact dipole far-field pressure:** $p \sim \dfrac{\rho_0 \omega U^2 L^2}{4\pi r c_0}$. *(Numericals appear regularly)* - **Far-field $1/r$ decay** confirms dipole behaviour. - **Applications (engineering NVH):** Fan/compressor noise, helicopter BVI, wind turbine noise, HVAC duct whistling, automobile cavity tones. - **Sign convention:** Powell uses $\mathbf{L} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$; Howe uses $\mathbf{v} \times \boldsymbol{\omega} = -\mathbf{L}$ for the work-integral — *always state the convention explicitly*. - **Physical insight:** Stationary vortex in uniform flow = silent; vortex distortion by non-uniform mean flow = sound radiation. - **Free-space retarded-potential solution** is the operational tool for converting the Lamb vector field into far-field acoustic pressure. *(Frequently asked in derivations)* > **Final Tip:** When KTU asks you to “state and prove”, always (1) write the formal statement first, (2) start from a *recognised* governing equation (Navier–Stokes or kinetic energy theorem), (3) perform each algebraic step *one line at a time*, and (4) box the final result. This guarantees ≥ 80% of the marks on a 14-mark question.

topics.title: Acoustic Source Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Acoustic Source Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Acoustic Source Model?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> An **Acoustic Source Model** is a mathematical idealization of a sound-producing mechanism that allows engineers to predict, analyze, and quantify the sound field generated by a real physical source. These models describe the spatial and temporal distribution of sound radiation using simplified geometric and physical abstractions such as point, line, and plane radiators.

In the context of **NVH (Noise, Vibration, and Harshness)** engineering, source models are the foundational building blocks for:
- Predicting radiated noise from machinery
- Designing mufflers, silencers, and acoustic enclosures
- Performing structural-acoustic coupling analysis
- Conducting beamforming and acoustic holography

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Lighthill Vision"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Pond Ripple Analogy**
> Imagine dropping different objects into a still pond. A raindrop (tiny sphere) creates **circular ripples spreading equally in all directions** — this is a **monopole**. A vibrating tuning fork pushed into water creates **two lobes of ripples, stronger in front and back, nothing on the sides** — this is a **dipole**. A snapping twig underwater creates a **four-lobed pattern** of ripples — this is a **quadrupole**.

The key insight: **every real-world sound source can be approximated as a combination of these elementary source models** (monopole, dipole, quadrupole), just like every color in nature can be expressed as a combination of red, green, and blue.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Acoustic Sources

Acoustic sources are broadly classified based on their **dimensionality** and **radiation mechanism**:

| Source Type | Dimensionality | Geometric Idealization | Real-World Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Monopole (Point Source)** | 0-D | Pulsating sphere | Loudspeaker in free field, pulsating pipe, exhaust tailpipe |
| **Dipole Source** | 1-D (oriented) | Two opposite monopoles | Vibrating panel, oscillating rigid sphere, tuning fork |
| **Quadrupole Source** | 2-D | Two opposite dipoles | Turbulent jets, rocket exhausts, cavitation bubbles |
| **Line Source** | 1-D | Infinite line of monopoles | Long pipe, roadway traffic noise line, railway corridor |
| **Plane Source** | 2-D | Infinite vibrating piston | Large wall panel, room boundary, highway noise barrier |

---

## 1.4 The Concept of Source Strength

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Source Strength ($Q$)** is the fundamental quantity that characterizes an acoustic source. For a monopole, it is defined as the **time rate of change of volume displacement** of the source, with units of **m³/s**.

$$Q = \frac{dV}{dt} = 4\pi a^2 v_a$$

where:
- $a$ = radius of the equivalent spherical source
- $v_a$ = radial particle velocity at the source surface
- $V$ = instantaneous volume displacement

For higher-order sources, we define **dipole moment** and **quadrupole moment** tensors that describe the spatial distribution of the source strength.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Monopole (Simple Spherical Source)

The **monopole** is the most fundamental acoustic source. Physically, it represents a **uniformly pulsating sphere** of radius $a$, whose surface expands and contracts symmetrically, pushing air outward and inward equally in all directions.

### 2.1.1 Physical Characteristics
- **Directivity:** Omnidirectional (radiates equally in all directions)
- **Phase:** All points equidistant from the source receive sound in phase
- **Wavefront shape:** Spherical
- **Frequency dependence:** At low frequencies ($kr \ll 1$), it is a **compact source**; at high frequencies, it behaves as a directional radiator.

### 2.1.2 Mathematical Foundation

The monopole satisfies the **inhomogeneous Helmholtz equation** (in the frequency domain):

$$\nabla^2 p + k^2 p = -j\omega\rho_0 Q \cdot \delta(\vec{r})$$

where $\delta(\vec{r})$ is the Dirac delta function locating the source at the origin.

The solution is the well-known **free-field Green's function**:

$$p(r) = \frac{j\omega\rho_0 Q}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

---

## 2.2 The Dipole Source

A **dipole** is formed by two monopoles of equal magnitude but **opposite phase**, separated by an infinitesimal distance $d$. It represents a source where the volume displacement has no net change but produces a fluctuating force/momentum.

### 2.2.1 Physical Characteristics
- **Directivity:** Figure-8 pattern (cosine-squared in far field)
- **Wavefront shape:** Spherical with directional bias
- **Real-world analog:** A vibrating piston in a rigid baffle, an oscillating rigid sphere, a tuning fork

### 2.2.2 Mathematical Foundation

The sound pressure for a dipole aligned along the z-axis:

$$p(r,\theta) = \frac{j\omega\rho_0 (Qd) \cos\theta}{4\pi r^2} (1 + jkr) e^{-jkr}$$

where $Qd$ is the **dipole moment** (units: m⁴/s).

### 2.2.3 Near-Field vs Far-Field Behavior

The dipole exhibits two distinct regimes:

| Regime | Condition | Dominant Term | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Near Field** | $kr \ll 1$ | $\frac{1}{r^2}$ | Pressure drops rapidly; reactive (stored energy) |
| **Far Field** | $kr \gg 1$ | $\frac{1}{r}$ | Pressure decays as $1/r$; propagates sound |

---

## 2.3 The Quadrupole Source

A **quadrupole** is formed by two opposite dipoles, producing an even more directional radiation pattern. There are two main configurations:

### 2.3.1 Longitudinal Quadrupole
Two dipoles aligned end-to-end along the source axis. Dominant radiation is along the **perpendicular** axis.

### 2.3.2 Lateral Quadrupole
Two dipoles placed side-by-side. Dominant radiation is along the **source axis** and its perpendicular.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lighthill's Eighth Power Law** — For aerodynamic quadrupole sources (e.g., jet noise), the radiated acoustic power scales as $W \propto \rho_0 U^8 / (\rho_0 c_0^5 r^2)$ where $U$ is the jet velocity. This is why jet noise is so sensitive to exhaust velocity — doubling the speed increases noise by ~256 times!

---

## 2.4 The Line Source

A **line source** represents an infinitely long, coherent line of monopoles, producing **cylindrical waves**. The sound pressure involves the **Hankel function of the second kind of order zero**:

$$p(r) = \frac{j\omega\rho_0 Q_L}{4} H_0^{(2)}(kr)$$

In the **far field** ($kr \gg 1$), this simplifies to:

$$p(r) \approx \frac{\omega\rho_0 Q_L}{4} \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi k r}} \cos\left(kr - \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

### 2.4.1 Practical Significance

- Sound decays as $1/\sqrt{r}$ (slower than $1/r$ for point source)
- Used to model **highway and railway traffic noise**
- Used to model **long duct radiation**

---

## 2.5 The Plane Source

A **plane source** represents an infinite vibrating surface producing **plane waves** with no geometric spreading. The sound pressure is:

$$p(x,t) = A \, e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$$

### 2.5.1 Key Property
- **No attenuation with distance** (in a lossless medium)
- All acoustic energy propagates in a single direction
- Used in **anechoic chambers, duct acoustics, and room modal analysis**

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **must-memorize formulas** for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 examinations on Acoustic Source Models.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Monopole Pressure (far field) | $p = \frac{j\omega\rho_0 Q}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$ | Pa | Compact source assumption |
| 2 | Monopole Sound Power | $W = \frac{\rho_0 \omega^2 Q^2}{4\pi c_0} = \frac{\rho_0 c_0 k^2 Q^2}{4\pi}$ | W | Proportional to $\omega^2$ |
| 3 | Monopole Intensity | $I = \frac{W}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{\rho_0 \omega^2 Q^2}{32\pi^2 r^2 c_0}$ | W/m² | Spreads over sphere of area $4\pi r^2$ |
| 4 | Monopole SPL at distance $r$ | $L_p = L_W - 20\log_{10}(r) - 10.99$ | dB | $-10.99$ dB = $20\log_{10}(4\pi)$ |
| 5 | Dipole Pressure | $p = \frac{j\omega\rho_0 (Qd)\cos\theta}{4\pi r^2}(1+jkr)e^{-jkr}$ | Pa | Dipole moment $= Qd$ |
| 6 | Dipole Directivity Factor | $D(\theta) = \cos^2\theta$ | dimensionless | Maximum at $\theta=0,\pi$ |
| 7 | Dipole Sound Power | $W = \frac{\rho_0 \omega^4 (Qd)^2}{12\pi c_0^3}$ | W | Proportional to $\omega^4$ |
| 8 | Quadrupole Power | $W \propto \frac{\rho_0 \omega^6 M^2}{c_0^5}$ | W | $M$ = quadrupole moment |
| 9 | Lighthill's 8th Power Law (Jet) | $W \propto \rho_0 \left(\frac{U^8}{c_0^5 r^2}\right)$ | W | Valid for high-Mach-number turbulent jets |
| 10 | Line Source Pressure (far field) | $p = \frac{\omega\rho_0 Q_L}{4}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi k r}}\cos(kr - \pi/4)$ | Pa | Cylindrical spreading |
| 11 | Line Source Power | $W = \frac{\rho_0 \omega^2 Q_L^2}{16\pi c_0}$ per unit length | W/m | $Q_L$ is volume velocity per unit length |
| 12 | Plane Wave Pressure | $p = A e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$ | Pa | No geometric decay |
| 13 | Wavenumber | $k = \omega/c_0 = 2\pi/\lambda$ | rad/m | Fundamental acoustic parameter |
| 14 | Compactness Criterion | $ka \ll 1$ (i.e., $a \ll \lambda$) | dimensionless | Source radius $a$ much smaller than wavelength |
| 15 | Far-Field Criterion | $kr \gg 1$ (i.e., $r \gg \lambda/2\pi$) | dimensionless | Beyond this, only $1/r$ term dominates |

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of Source Models

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Source Models Matter in Industry:**
> 1. **Automotive NVH:** Predicting tire noise (line source), engine block vibration (dipole), exhaust radiation (monopole).
> 2. **Aeroacoustics:** Jet noise prediction (quadrupole) using Lighthill's analogy.
> 3. **Building Acoustics:** HVAC duct noise (line/plane source) propagation into rooms.
> 4. **Active Noise Control:** Reference signal generation from source models.
> 5. **Environmental Noise Mapping:** Highway, railway, and airport noise impact assessment.

---

## 2.8 Comparison of Source Radiation Efficiencies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Comparison for KTU:** At the **same source strength amplitude**, the relative radiation efficiency of the three elementary sources varies dramatically with frequency and order.

| Property | Monopole | Dipole | Quadrupole |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency dependence of power | $\omega^2$ | $\omega^4$ | $\omega^6$ |
| Radiation pattern | Sphere | Figure-8 | Four-lobed (clover) |
| Spreading loss (far field) | $1/r^2$ (intensity) | $1/r^2$ (intensity) | $1/r^2$ (intensity) |
| Directivity index (DI) | 0 dB | 4.77 dB | varies with type |
| Typical source | Pulsating sphere | Vibrating panel | Turbulent jet |
| Relative efficiency (low freq) | Most efficient | Less efficient | Least efficient |

This is **why** low-frequency noise is so hard to suppress — a monopole radiates efficiently even at low frequencies, while a quadrupole is essentially "silent" at low frequencies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Monopole Sound Pressure from the Wave Equation

We will derive the acoustic pressure radiated by a simple spherical source by solving the **inhomogeneous wave equation** in spherical coordinates.

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Problem

Consider a sphere of radius $a$ whose surface oscillates uniformly with radial velocity $v_a e^{j\omega t}$. The acoustic pressure $p$ outside the sphere ($r \geq a$) satisfies the **Helmholtz equation**:

$$\nabla^2 p + k^2 p = 0 \quad \text{for } r > a$$

### 3.1.2 Solution by Separation of Variables

Assume spherical symmetry: $p = p(r)$ only. The Laplacian in spherical coordinates for a spherically symmetric field reduces to:

$$\nabla^2 p = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2 \frac{dp}{dr}\right)$$

Substituting into the Helmholtz equation:

$$\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2 \frac{dp}{dr}\right) + k^2 p = 0$$

### 3.1.3 Solving the Differential Equation

Multiply through by $r^2$:

$$\frac{d^2(rp)}{dr^2} + k^2 (rp) = 0$$

This is the **standard 1-D harmonic oscillator equation**. The general solution is:

$$rp(r) = A e^{-jkr} + B e^{jkr}$$

Applying the **Sommerfeld radiation condition** (waves must propagate outward, not inward from infinity), we set $B = 0$:

$$p(r) = \frac{A}{r} e^{-jkr}$$

### 3.1.4 Applying the Boundary Condition

The radial particle velocity at the sphere surface ($r = a$) is given by Euler's equation:

$$v_a = -\frac{1}{j\omega\rho_0}\left.\frac{\partial p}{\partial r}\right|_{r=a}$$

Compute the derivative:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = A\left(-\frac{1}{r^2} e^{-jkr} - \frac{jk}{r} e^{-jkr}\right)$$

At $r = a$:

$$\left.\frac{\partial p}{\partial r}\right|_{r=a} = -\frac{A}{a^2}(1 + jka) e^{-jka}$$

Substituting back:

$$v_a = -\frac{1}{j\omega\rho_0} \cdot \left[-\frac{A}{a^2}(1+jka)e^{-jka}\right] = \frac{A(1+jka)}{j\omega\rho_0 a^2} e^{-jka}$$

Solving for $A$:

$$A = \frac{j\omega\rho_0 a^2 v_a}{1 + jka} e^{jka}$$

### 3.1.5 Introducing the Source Strength

The **source strength** for a pulsating sphere is:

$$Q = 4\pi a^2 v_a$$

Substituting $A$ and using $Q$:

$$p(r) = \frac{j\omega\rho_0 Q}{4\pi r (1 + jka)} e^{-jkr} \cdot e^{jka}$$

### 3.1.6 Compact Source Approximation

For a **compact source** (i.e., the sphere is much smaller than the wavelength), $ka \ll 1$, so $1 + jka \approx 1$. Also, $e^{jka} \approx 1$. This yields the celebrated **monopole pressure formula**:

$$\boxed{p(r) = \frac{j\omega\rho_0 Q}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}}$$

The associated **radial particle velocity** is:

$$v_r(r) = \frac{p(r)}{\rho_0 c_0}\left(1 + \frac{1}{jkr}\right) = \frac{Q}{4\pi r^2} e^{-jkr} + \frac{jkQ}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

This beautifully shows two terms:
- $\frac{Q}{4\pi r^2}$ → **near-field (incompressible) flow**, like a piston pushing volume
- $\frac{jkQ}{4\pi r}$ → **far-field acoustic wave**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Acoustic Power Radiated by a Monopole

### 3.2.1 Acoustic Intensity

The time-averaged acoustic intensity in the radial direction is:

$$I(r) = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}\left[p(r) v_r^*(r)\right]$$

For a compact monopole in the far field:

$$v_r \approx \frac{p}{\rho_0 c_0}$$

Therefore:

$$I(r) = \frac{|p|^2}{2\rho_0 c_0} = \frac{1}{2\rho_0 c_0} \cdot \frac{\omega^2 \rho_0^2 Q^2}{16\pi^2 r^2}$$

$$\boxed{I(r) = \frac{\rho_0 \omega^2 Q^2}{32\pi^2 r^2 c_0}}$$

### 3.2.2 Total Radiated Power

Integrate the intensity over a closed sphere of radius $r$:

$$W = \oint_S I \, dS = I(r) \cdot 4\pi r^2$$

$$\boxed{W = \frac{\rho_0 \omega^2 Q^2}{8\pi c_0} = \frac{\rho_0 c_0 k^2 Q^2}{4\pi}}$$

This is the **fundamental result**: the acoustic power radiated by a compact monopole is proportional to the **square of the frequency** and the **square of the source strength**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Dipole Pressure from Two Monopoles

### 3.3.1 Constructing a Dipole

Place monopole $+Q$ at $(0, 0, +d/2)$ and monopole $-Q$ at $(0, 0, -d/2)$. Using superposition, the pressure at point $(r, \theta)$ in spherical coordinates is:

$$p(r,\theta) = \frac{j\omega\rho_0 Q}{4\pi r_1} e^{-jkr_1} - \frac{j\omega\rho_0 Q}{4\pi r_2} e^{-jkr_2}$$

where $r_1$ and $r_2$ are the distances from the two monopoles to the field point.

### 3.3.2 Geometric Approximation for Small Separation

For $kr \gg 1$ and $d \ll r$:

$$r_1 \approx r - \frac{d}{2}\cos\theta, \quad r_2 \approx r + \frac{d}{2}\cos\theta$$

Taylor expand the exponentials to first order in $d$:

$$e^{-jkr_1} \approx e^{-jkr} e^{+jk(d/2)\cos\theta}$$
$$e^{-jkr_2} \approx e^{-jkr} e^{-jk(d/2)\cos\theta}$$

Subtracting:

$$e^{-jkr_1} - e^{-jkr_2} \approx 2j \sin\left(\frac{kd\cos\theta}{2}\right) e^{-jkr} \approx jkd\cos\theta \, e^{-jkr}$$

(using $\sin x \approx x$ for small $x$)

### 3.3.3 Final Dipole Pressure

Substituting back:

$$p(r,\theta) = \frac{j\omega\rho_0 Q \cdot jkd\cos\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

$$\boxed{p(r,\theta) = \frac{-k\omega\rho_0 (Qd)\cos\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr} = \frac{j\omega\rho_0 (Qd)\cos\theta}{4\pi r^2}(1 + jkr) e^{-jkr}}$$

The factor $(1 + jkr)$ appears when we include the near-field correction from the $1/r$ dependence of the original monopole. The dipole moment is $Qd$ (units: m⁴/s).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Sound Power of a Dipole

For a compact dipole in the far field, the pressure is:

$$p(r,\theta) \approx \frac{-k\omega\rho_0 (Qd)\cos\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

The intensity at angle $\theta$:

$$I(r,\theta) = \frac{|p|^2}{2\rho_0 c_0} = \frac{k^2 \omega^2 \rho_0 (Qd)^2 \cos^2\theta}{32\pi^2 r^2 c_0}$$

Total power (integrate over sphere):

$$W = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} I(r,\theta) \cdot r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

The angular integral:

$$\int_0^{\pi} \cos^2\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta = \frac{2}{3}$$

Therefore:

$$W = 2\pi r^2 \cdot \frac{k^2 \omega^2 \rho_0 (Qd)^2}{32\pi^2 r^2 c_0} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{k^2 \omega^2 \rho_0 (Qd)^2}{24\pi c_0}$$

$$\boxed{W = \frac{\rho_0 \omega^4 (Qd)^2}{24\pi c_0^3}}$$

Comparing with monopole: dipole power scales as $\omega^4$ vs monopole's $\omega^2$. This means **dipoles are very inefficient radiators at low frequencies** — a critical insight for NVH engineers designing low-frequency sound absorbers.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Sound Pressure Level (SPL) of a Monopole at Distance r

Given sound power $W$ of a monopole source, the SPL at distance $r$ in free field is:

$$L_p(r) = L_W + 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{4\pi r^2}\right)$$

Expanding the logarithm:

$$10\log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{4\pi r^2}\right) = -10\log_{10}(4\pi) - 20\log_{10}(r)$$

Since $10\log_{10}(4\pi) \approx 10.99$:

$$\boxed{L_p(r) = L_W - 20\log_{10}(r) - 10.99 \, \text{dB re 1 pPa}}$$

This **inverse-square law in dB form** is heavily tested in KTU numericals: every doubling of distance reduces SPL by **6 dB**.

---

## 3.6 Quick Reference: Key Numerical Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these constants for KTU 2024 exams:**

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air density (standard) | $\rho_0$ | **1.21** | kg/m³ |
| Speed of sound in air | $c_0$ | **343** | m/s |
| Reference pressure | $p_{ref}$ | **20** | µPa |
| Reference power | $W_{ref}$ | **10⁻¹²** | W |
| Acoustic impedance of air | $\rho_0 c_0$ | **415** | Pa·s/m |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Acoustic Source Models

This Mermaid diagram shows the logical taxonomy of acoustic source models and their governing physical mechanism.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ACOUSTIC SOURCE MODELS] --> B[Order 0: Monopole]
    A --> C[Order 1: Dipole]
    A --> D[Order 2: Quadrupole]
    A --> E[Geometric: Line Source]
    A --> F[Geometric: Plane Source]

    B --> B1[Pulsating Sphere]
    B --> B2[Omnidirectional]
    B --> B3[Power ~ omega^2]

    C --> C1[Two Opposite Monopoles]
    C --> C2[Figure-8 Pattern]
    C --> C3[Power ~ omega^4]

    D --> D1[Two Opposite Dipoles]
    D --> D2[Longitudinal Type]
    D --> D3[Lateral Type]
    D --> D4[Power ~ omega^6]

    E --> E1[Cylindrical Wave]
    E --> E2[Decay ~ 1 over sqrt r]
    E --> E3[Traffic Noise Analog]

    F --> F1[No Geometric Spreading]
    F --> F2[Duct / Anechoic Chamber]
    F --> F3[Decay = 0 in Lossless Medium]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture: From Physical Source to Mathematical Model

This block diagram explains the engineering workflow of converting a real physical source into a workable mathematical model.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Real Physical Source e.g. Engine Block] --> S1[Step 1: Identify Dominant Radiation Mechanism]
    S1 --> S2{Compact or Extended?}
    S2 -->|Compact ka less than 1| S3[Model as Monopole / Dipole / Quadrupole]
    S2 -->|Extended| S4[Model as Line or Plane Source]
    S3 --> A1[Apply Helmholtz Equation]
    S4 --> A1
    A1 --> A2[Derive Pressure Field p r theta]
    A2 --> A3[Compute Intensity and Power]
    A3 --> A4[Validate with Measurement SPL]
    A4 --> V{Error less than 3 dB?}
    V -->|Yes| OUT[Valid Acoustic Model]
    V -->|No| S1
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Source Strength and Radiation Pattern

This diagram maps the relationship between source order, radiation pattern, and typical physical mechanism.

```mermaid
graph TD
    M0[MONOPOLE] --> M0A[Directivity: Sphere - Omni]
    M0 --> M0B[Mechanism: Volume Change]
    M0 --> M0C[Symbol: Filled Dot]

    M1[DIPOLE] --> M1A[Directivity: Figure Eight cos squared theta]
    M1 --> M1B[Mechanism: Force Oscillation]
    M1 --> M1C[Symbol: Two Opposite Dots]

    M2[QUADRUPOLE] --> M2A[Directivity: Four Lobed Clover]
    M2 --> M2B[Mechanism: Stress Fluctuation]
    M2 --> M2C[Symbol: Four Dipoles in Quad]

    M3[LINE SOURCE] --> M3A[Directivity: Cylinder 2D Omni]
    M3 --> M3B[Mechanism: Coherent Line of Monopoles]
    M3 --> M3C[Symbol: Vertical Bar]

    M4[PLANE SOURCE] --> M4A[Directivity: Single Direction]
    M4 --> M4B[Mechanism: Infinite Piston]
    M4 --> M4C[Symbol: Wall with Arrows]
```

---

## 4.4 Process Flow: Far-Field Approximation Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Start with Full Pressure Expression] --> Q1{Is kr greater than 1?}
    Q1 -->|No - Near Field| NF[Near Field Region]
    Q1 -->|Yes - Far Field| FF[Apply Far Field Approximation]

    NF --> NF1[Dominant Term: 1 over r squared]
    NF --> NF2[Reactive Energy Storage]
    NF --> NF3[No Net Power Radiation Beyond Surface]

    FF --> FF1[Retain only 1 over r term]
    FF --> FF2[Pressure in phase with Velocity]
    FF --> FF3[Pure Propagating Wave]
    FF --> FF4[Compute Intensity as p squared over 2 rho c]
```
