$$\frac{D^2 \rho}{Dt^2} = \nabla \cdot \nabla p - \frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} \frac{1}{c^2}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce Lighthill's Stress Tensor**

By algebraic manipulation and introduction of the **Lighthill tensor** $T_{ij}$ (which contains all non-linear and turbulent terms), the exact inhomogeneous wave equation becomes:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} - c_0^2 \nabla^2 \rho' = \frac{\partial^2 T_{ij}}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$$

This is the celebrated **Lighthill equation**, identifying $T_{ij}$ as the quadrupole source strength.

**Step 4 — Verify Source Rank**

The double spatial derivative $\dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$ acting on a **rank-2 tensor** is precisely what classifies this as a **quadrupole** source. The acoustic pressure depends on the *second spatial gradient* of the stress field, which is a hallmark of quadrupole radiation.

### Derivation 2: Sound Power Scaling — The 8th Power Law

**Step 1 — Dimensional Analysis of $T_{ij}$**

The dominant term in Lighthill's tensor for high Reynolds number turbulent flow is the **Reynolds stress** $\rho u_i u_j$:

$$T_{ij} \sim \rho_0 U^2$$

where $U$ is a characteristic turbulent velocity.

**Step 2 — Source Volume**

The effective source region has a characteristic dimension $D$ (e.g., jet diameter, shear-layer thickness):

$$V \sim D^3$$

**Step 3 — Sound Pressure Scaling**

From Lighthill's far-field solution, acoustic pressure scales as:

$$p' \sim \frac{\rho_0 U^2 D^3}{c_0^2 r} \cdot \frac{1}{\tau^2}$$

where $\tau \sim D/U$ is the characteristic time scale, so $1/\tau^2 \sim U^2/D^2$:

$$p' \sim \frac{\rho_0 U^4 D}{c_0^2 r}$$

**Step 4 — Sound Power**

Sound power is intensity integrated over a sphere of radius $r$:

$$W \sim I \cdot 4\pi r^2 = \frac{\overline{p'^2}}{\rho_0 c_0} \cdot 4\pi r^2$$

Substituting $p' \sim \rho_0 U^4 D / (c_0^2 r)$:

$$\overline{p'^2} \sim \frac{\rho_0^2 U^8 D^2}{c_0^4 r^2}$$

$$W \sim \frac{\rho_0 U^8 D^2}{c_0^5}$$

This confirms the **8th power law**: $W \propto U^8$, the fingerprint of quadrupole radiation.

### Derivation 3: Directivity of a Lateral Quadrupole

**Step 1 — Geometry**

A lateral quadrupole consists of two opposing dipoles oriented along the $x_1$-axis, separated by a small distance $\delta$ along $x_2$.

**Step 2 — Pressure Field**

The far-field pressure in the direction $\mathbf{n} = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta, 0)$ is:

$$p'(r,\theta) = \frac{Q}{4\pi c_0^2 r} \cdot \frac{\partial^2}{\partial t^2}[\delta(t - r/c_0)] \cdot \sin(2\theta)$$

where $Q$ is the quadrupole strength.

**Step 3 — Directivity Function**

The amplitude directivity in spherical coordinates is:

$$\mathcal{D}(\theta) = \sin(2\theta) = 2 \sin\theta \cos\theta$$

This produces four lobes with **maxima at $\theta = 45°, 135°, 225°, 315°$** and **nulls at $\theta = 0°, 90°, 180°, 270°$**.

**Step 4 — Directivity Index in Decibels**

$$DI(\theta) = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{\sin^2(2\theta)}{\sin^2(2 \cdot 45°)}\right] = 10 \log_{10}\left[\sin^2(2\theta)\right]$$

At $\theta = 0°$: $DI = -\infty$ dB (null)
At $\theta = 45°$: $DI = 0$ dB (maximum)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Acoustic Source Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Acoustic Source] --> B{Net Volume Flux?}
    B -->|Non-zero| C[Monopole]
    B -->|Zero| D{Net Force?}
    D -->|Non-zero| E[Dipole]
    D -->|Zero| F[Quadrupole]
    C --> G[Examples: Pulsating sphere, breathing cavity]
    E --> H[Examples: Vibrating panel, oscillating piston]
    F --> I[Examples: Turbulent jet, exhaust flow, tire-road noise]
    C --> J[Scaling: W proportional to U to power 2]
    E --> K[Scaling: W proportional to U to power 4]
    F --> L[Scaling: W proportional to U to power 8]
```

### Diagram 2: Lighthill Stress Tensor Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Continuity Equation] --> C[Material Derivative]
    B[Momentum Equation] --> C
    C --> D[Lighthill Wave Equation]
    D --> E[Lighthill Stress Tensor T_ij]
    E --> F1[Reynolds Stress term]
    E --> F2[Entropy term]
    E --> F3[Viscous Stress term]
    F1 --> G[Quadrupole Source Field]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Far-field Acoustic Pressure]
    H --> I[Sound Power 8th power law]
```

### Diagram 3: Quadrupole Directivity Pattern

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Lateral Quadrupole Geometry] --> B[Two opposing dipoles along x1]
    B --> C[Separation along x2 axis]
    C --> D[Directivity D theta equal to sin 2 theta]
    D --> E[Four lobes at 45, 135, 225, 315 degrees]
    D --> F[Four nulls at 0, 90, 180, 270 degrees]
    E --> G[Maximum radiation direction]
    F --> H[Minimum radiation direction]
```

### Diagram 4: Source Classification by Mach Number Regime

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fluid Flow Noise Source] --> B{Mach Number M}
    B -->|M less than 0.1| C[Monopole Dominant]
    B -->|M between 0.1 and 0.3| D[Dipole Dominant]
    B -->|M greater than 0.3| E[Quadrupole Dominant]
    C --> F[Vehicle HVAC, low speed]
    D --> G[Vehicle body panels, medium speed]
    E --> H[Exhaust jet, tire noise, high speed]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define an acoustic quadrupole. Give two practical examples relevant to automotive NVH.**

**Model Answer:**
An acoustic quadrupole is a sound source characterized by a second-order spatial variation of fluctuating stresses, where both the monopole and dipole moments vanish. Sound radiation is governed by the second spatial derivative of the Lighthill stress tensor $T_{ij}$.

**Two practical examples:**
1. **Exhaust jet noise** — turbulent mixing in the exhaust tailpipe at high vehicle speeds.
2. **Tire-road interaction noise** — fluctuating contact stresses between tire tread and road surface.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for examples)*

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: State Lighthill's 8th power law and explain its significance in vehicle noise prediction.**

**Model Answer:**
Lighthill's 8th power law states that the sound power radiated by a turbulent jet (quadrupole source) scales as:

$$W \propto \frac{\rho_0 U^8 D^2}{c_0^5}$$

**Significance:** A doubling of exhaust gas velocity results in a **256-fold** increase in radiated noise ($2^8 = 256$). This explains why high-speed driving produces disproportionately loud exhaust noise and guides muffler design strategies that focus on reducing exit velocity or breaking up coherent turbulence.

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for explanation with numerical example)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive Lighthill's acoustic analogy starting from the continuity and momentum equations, and clearly identify the quadrupole source term. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the conservation equations for a compressible, viscous fluid:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_i)}{\partial x_i} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial (\rho v_i)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_i v_j)}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{ij}}{\partial x_j}$$

**Step 2:** Take the material derivative of the continuity equation and combine with the momentum equation. After algebraic manipulation using the equation of state $p = p(\rho, s)$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} - c_0^2 \nabla^2 \rho' = \frac{\partial^2 T_{ij}}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$$

**Step 3:** Identify the Lighthill stress tensor:

$$T_{ij} = \rho v_i v_j + \left[(p - c_0^2 \rho)\right]\delta_{ij} - \sigma_{ij} - \rho v_i v_j \delta_{ij}/2$$

**Step 4:** The double spatial derivative on the RHS is the **quadrupole signature** — this term vanishes for monopole and dipole sources.

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for derivation steps, 2 for identification of quadrupole term)*

**(b)** A turbulent exhaust jet has a diameter $D = 0.05 \text{ m}$ and exit velocity $U = 80 \text{ m/s}$. Using Lighthill's 8th power law, estimate the radiated sound power. Take $\rho_0 = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $c_0 = 340 \text{ m/s}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the 8th power law:**

$$W = K \cdot \frac{\rho_0 U^8 D^2}{c_0^5}$$

Using a typical empirical constant $K \approx 0.01$ for axisymmetric jets:

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$W = 0.01 \times \frac{1.2 \times (80)^8 \times (0.05)^2}{(340)^5}$$

$$W = 0.01 \times \frac{1.2 \times 1.68 \times 10^{15} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4.54 \times 10^{10}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute step-by-step:**

- $U^8 = 80^8 = (8 \times 10)^8 = 8^8 \times 10^8 = 1.677 \times 10^7 \times 10^8 = 1.677 \times 10^{15}$
- $D^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$
- $c_0^5 = 340^5 = 4.54 \times 10^{10}$

**Step 4 — Final calculation:**

$$W = 0.01 \times \frac{1.2 \times 1.677 \times 10^{15} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4.54 \times 10^{10}}$$

$$W = 0.01 \times \frac{5.03 \times 10^{12}}{4.54 \times 10^{10}} = 0.01 \times 110.8 \approx 1.11 \text{ W}$$

**Answer:** $W \approx 1.1 \text{ W}$

*(7 marks: 2 for formula, 3 for substitution, 2 for final answer with units)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the directivity patterns and sound power scaling laws for monopole, dipole, and quadrupole acoustic sources. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| **Source Type** | **Directivity** | **Power Scaling** | **Mach Number Dependence** |
|-----------------|-----------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| Monopole | Omnidirectional | $W \propto U^2$ | $M^0$ |
| Dipole | Figure-eight ($\cos\theta$) | $W \propto U^4$ | $M^2$ |
| Quadrupole | Four-lobe ($\sin 2\theta$) | $W \propto U^8$ | $M^4$ |

**Key insights:**
1. As source order increases, directivity becomes more complex and power scaling becomes steeper.
2. The increasing $M^n$ dependence explains why quadrupoles are negligible at low speeds but dominate at high speeds.
3. In vehicle NVH: HVAC noise = monopole-dominated, body panel vibration = dipole-dominated, exhaust/tire noise at highway speed = quadrupole-dominated.

*(7 marks: 4 for table, 3 for engineering interpretation)*

**(b)** A passenger car tire operating at $90 \text{ km/h}$ generates quadrupole-type noise. If the speed increases to $120 \text{ km/h}$, by what factor does the radiated sound power increase? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use 8th power law for quadrupole sources:**

$$\frac{W_2}{W_1} = \left(\frac{U_2}{U_1}\right)^8$$

**Step 2 — Compute velocity ratio:**

$$\frac{U_2}{U_1} = \frac{120}{90} = \frac{4}{3} = 1.333$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 8th power:**

$$\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^8 = \frac{4^8}{3^8} = \frac{65536}{6561} \approx 9.99$$

**Step 4 — Convert to dB increase:**

$$\Delta L_W = 10 \log_{10}(9.99) \approx 10 \text{ dB}$$

**Answer:** Sound power increases by a factor of **~10**, corresponding to a **+10 dB** increase in sound power level.

*(7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for ratio computation, 2 for 8th power, 1 for dB conversion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing source orders:** Students often misidentify a dipole as a quadrupole. Remember: *monopole = 0th order spatial derivative, dipole = 1st, quadrupole = 2nd*.
> 2. **Forgetting the $M^4$ factor:** When comparing source strengths, always state the Mach number regime. A "small" quadrupole at low Mach can dominate at high Mach.
> 3. **Unit errors in Lighthill's law:** $T_{ij}$ has units of Pa (N/m²), not force. Ensure dimensional consistency in derivations.
> 4. **Skipping the 8th power:** A common error is using $U^4$ for quadrupoles — this is for *dipoles*. Quadrupoles follow $U^8$.
> 5. **Ignoring directivity:** A quadrupole's directivity is **not** figure-eight — it is **four-lobe** ($\sin 2\theta$).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A quadrupole is a 2nd-order acoustic source governed by $\partial^2 T_{ij}/\partial x_i \partial x_j$.
- **Lighthill's Analogy:** Source = Lighthill stress tensor with double spatial derivative = quadrupole.
- **8th Power Law:** $W \propto \rho_0 U^8 D^2 / c_0^5$ — hallmark of turbulent/jet/exhaust noise.
- **Directivity:** Four-lobe pattern, $\sin 2\theta$ for lateral quadrupole; nulls along principal axes.
- **Mach Scaling:** Quadrupole power $\propto M^4$ relative to monopole — explains highway-speed NVH dominance.
- **Automotive Relevance:** Exhaust jet noise, tire-road noise, and high-speed aerodynamic noise are predominantly quadrupole in nature.
- **Curle's Extension:** Allows quadrupole modeling on arbitrary surfaces (useful for BEM/CAA simulations).
- **Lighthill Tensor Terms:** Reynolds stress $\rho u_i u_j$ is typically dominant; entropy and viscous terms are secondary in high-Re flows.
- **Typical Frequency:** $f_c \sim 0.1 U/D$ (Strouhal scaling) for broadband turbulent quadrupole sources.
- **Engineering Mitigation:** Reduce exit velocity (exhaust), use porous liners, optimize flow geometry to suppress coherent turbulence.
- **Equation to Memorize:** $\nabla^2 p' - \dfrac{1}{c_0^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = \dfrac{\partial^2 T_{ij}}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$ — this is the *defining* equation for KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Vibration - Basic Concept of Vibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Vibration - Basic Concept of Vibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** *Fundamentals of Vibration — Basic Concept of Vibration, Terminology, Degrees of Freedom, Classification of Vibration.*

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of mechanical system dynamics, **Vibration** is formally defined as the **oscillatory motion of a particle, body, or system of bodies about an equilibrium position**. Mathematically, it is the time-dependent periodic variation of a displacement, velocity, or acceleration coordinate relative to a reference (static equilibrium) state.

From a control and structural dynamics perspective, vibration is any motion that repeats itself after an interval of time (periodic) or any motion that is oscillatory in nature, even if non-repeating (random). The governing differential equation of a vibrating system in its most general single-degree-of-freedom form is expressed as:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F(t)$$

where:
- $m$ = **mass** of the system (kg)
- $c$ = **damping coefficient** (Ns/m)
- $k$ = **stiffness** (N/m)
- $x(t)$ = **instantaneous displacement** from equilibrium (m)
- $F(t)$ = **external excitation force** (N)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standardized Phrasing):**
> *"Vibration is the oscillatory motion of a system about its mean/equilibrium position, characterized by quantifiable parameters such as amplitude, frequency, phase angle, and damping."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The "Boat on a Wavy Pond" Analogy:**
Imagine you are sitting in a small boat on a calm pond. If you stand up suddenly and shift your weight, the boat begins to rock back and forth across the water's still surface. The boat's vertical bouncing motion eventually settles back to stillness (if no waves disturb it). This rock-and-settle behavior is the **simplest possible vibration**:
- The boat = the **mass** $m$
- The water's restoring push = the **stiffness** $k$
- The friction of water against the hull = the **damping** $c$
- The "stop rocking" moment = **natural decay** (damped vibration)

**Why is this useful for KTU students?**
Almost every mechanical system — a car suspension, a washing machine drum, a turbine blade, a building in an earthquake, the diaphragm of a microphone — is, at its mathematical heart, just a mass connected to a spring connected to a damper. Once you understand vibration, you understand **all** of these.

### 1.3 The Eight Golden Vocabulary Terms (Mandatory for KTU)

| Term | Symbol | Standard Unit | Plain Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Amplitude | $X$ or $x(t)_{\max}$ | **meters (m)** | The maximum distance the body travels from equilibrium. |
| Period | $T$ | **seconds (s)** | Time taken for **one full cycle** of motion. |
| Frequency | $f$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | Number of complete cycles per second. |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega$ | **rad/s** | Rotational analogue; $\omega = 2\pi f$. |
| Phase Angle | $\phi$ | **radians (rad)** | The "starting offset" of the wave at $t = 0$. |
| Natural Frequency | $\omega_n$ | **rad/s** | The frequency at which the system vibrates *freely* if undisturbed. |
| Damping Ratio | $\zeta$ | **dimensionless** | Quantifies how rapidly oscillations die out. |
| Degrees of Freedom (DOF) | $n$ | **integer** | The minimum number of independent coordinates needed to define the system's configuration. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Master Classification Tree of Vibration

KTU examiners frequently ask students to **classify vibration types**. The hierarchy is as follows:

**A. Classification Based on Presence of External Force (Excitation)**
1. **Free Vibration** — System oscillates *without* any external force after an initial disturbance (e.g., plucking a guitar string and letting it ring).
2. **Forced Vibration** — System is driven by a *continuous time-varying external force* (e.g., a motor mounted on a vibrating platform).

**B. Classification Based on Energy Dissipation (Damping)**
1. **Undamped Vibration** — Idealized case where $c = 0$. Amplitude remains constant forever. Mathematically convenient but physically impossible.
2. **Damped Vibration** — Real-world case where $c > 0$. Amplitude decays exponentially due to energy loss.
   - *Underdamped* ($\zeta < 1$): Oscillates with decaying amplitude.
   - *Critically Damped* ($\zeta = 1$): Returns to equilibrium in the shortest possible time without overshoot.
   - *Overdamped* ($\zeta > 1$): Returns to equilibrium *without* oscillation, very slowly.

**C. Classification Based on Repeatability**
1. **Periodic Vibration** — Pattern repeats after a fixed interval $T$.
   - *Harmonic Vibration* — A pure single-frequency sinusoid (most fundamental case).
   - *Non-Harmonic Periodic* — Periodic but composed of multiple harmonics (e.g., square wave).
2. **Non-Periodic Vibration** — Pattern does *not* repeat.
   - *Transient* — Decays to zero over time (e.g., impact hammer blow).
   - *Random* — Statistically described; no deterministic equation (e.g., road roughness).

**D. Classification Based on Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**
1. **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF)** — One coordinate fully describes the motion.
2. **Multi Degree of Freedom (MDOF)** — Two or more coordinates required (e.g., 2-DOF, 3-DOF, n-DOF).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** In Module 2 questions, ~80% of numerical problems target SDOF systems first. Master SDOF thoroughly before attempting MDOF.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step "Why & How" Logic of Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)

**Why** does SHM matter? It is the **mathematical building block** of all vibration. Any complicated vibration can be expressed as a sum of sinusoids via **Fourier series**.

**How** is SHM generated? Whenever a restoring force is *directly proportional* to displacement (Hooke's Law: $F = -kx$), the resulting motion is sinusoidal.

The logic chain:
- **Step 1:** Newton's 2nd Law: $F = ma = m\ddot{x}$
- **Step 2:** Hooke's restoring force: $F = -kx$
- **Step 3:** Equate: $m\ddot{x} = -kx$
- **Step 4:** Rearrange: $\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$, where $\omega_n^2 = k/m$
- **Step 5:** General solution: $x(t) = X\sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$

This is the *fundamental equation of vibration* you must memorize for KTU.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are guaranteed to be mathematically verifiable and frequently appear in KTU ESE papers (2018–2023 trend).**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Frequency-Period Relation | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Convert between Hz and seconds. |
| 2 | Angular Frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | Convert Hz to rad/s for equations of motion. |
| 3 | SHM Displacement | $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | General SHM position-time expression. |
| 4 | SHM Velocity | $\dot{x}(t) = \omega X \cos(\omega t + \phi)$ | Velocity in SHM (90° phase lead over $x$). |
| 5 | SHM Acceleration | $\ddot{x}(t) = -\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | Acceleration (180° out of phase with $x$). |
| 6 | Natural Frequency (SDOF Spring-Mass) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Free vibration of undamped spring-mass system. |
| 7 | Natural Frequency in Hz | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | When answer is required in Hertz, not rad/s. |
| 8 | Period of SHM | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$ | Time for one oscillation cycle. |
| 9 | Damped Natural Frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | When damping is present but system still oscillates. |
| 10 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | Ratio of actual to critical damping. |
| 11 | Critical Damping | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ | Boundary between oscillatory and non-oscillatory decay. |
| 12 | Logarithmic Decrement | $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Used to find damping experimentally. |
| 13 | Magnification Factor (Forced) | $MF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Ratio of dynamic to static amplitude under harmonic force. |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Why Vibration Fundamentals Matter |
|---|---|
| **Automotive NVH** | Tuning suspension and engine mounts to avoid resonant failures. |
| **Civil/Aerospace** | Earthquake-resistant buildings; flutter suppression in wings. |
| **Rotating Machinery** | Balancing rotors; detecting misalignment via vibration spectra. |
| **Manufacturing** | Vibration-assisted machining, ultrasonic cleaning, conveyor feeders. |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Haptic feedback; speaker diaphragm design; hard-drive shock protection. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Equation of Motion for an Undamped SDOF System

**System Description:** Consider a mass $m$ attached to a linear spring of stiffness $k$, free to move horizontally on a frictionless surface. The mass is displaced by an initial amount $X$ and released from rest.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram & Force Identification:**
The only horizontal force acting on the mass is the spring restoring force. By Hooke's Law, when the mass is at displacement $x$ from equilibrium, the spring exerts a force equal to $-kx$ on the mass. The negative sign indicates the force always points *toward* equilibrium (opposite to displacement).

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law of Motion:**
Newton's Second Law states that the sum of forces equals mass times acceleration.

$$F_{net} = m a$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Force Expression:**
Replacing the net force with the spring force expression:

$$m \ddot{x}(t) = -k x(t)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into Standard Form:**
Divide the entire equation by $m$ and bring all terms to the left side:

$$\ddot{x}(t) + \frac{k}{m} x(t) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Define the Natural Frequency:**
By definition, the natural angular frequency $\omega_n$ for an SDOF undamped system is:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Step 6 — Rewrite the Equation in Compact Canonical Form:**

$$\ddot{x}(t) + \omega_n^2 \, x(t) = 0$$

**Step 7 — Solve the Second-Order Linear Homogeneous ODE:**
The standard trial solution is of the form $x(t) = e^{\lambda t}$. Substituting this into the canonical form:

$$\lambda^2 e^{\lambda t} + \omega_n^2 e^{\lambda t} = 0$$

Factor out $e^{\lambda t}$ (which is never zero):

$$\left( \lambda^2 + \omega_n^2 \right) e^{\lambda t} = 0$$

Therefore the auxiliary (characteristic) equation is:

$$\lambda^2 + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

**Step 8 — Solve the Auxiliary Equation for Roots:**
The two roots are complex conjugates:

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \pm \, i \, \omega_n$$

**Step 9 — Write the General Solution:**
Using the Euler identity $e^{i\theta} = \cos(\theta) + i\sin(\theta)$, the real-valued general solution is:

$$x(t) = A \cos(\omega_n t) + B \sin(\omega_n t)$$

**Step 10 — Apply Initial Conditions to Find Constants:**
Let the initial conditions be:
- $x(0) = X_0$ (initial displacement)
- $\dot{x}(0) = V_0$ (initial velocity)

At $t = 0$:
$$x(0) = A \cos(0) + B \sin(0) = A$$
$$A = X_0$$

Differentiating and applying the velocity condition:
$$\dot{x}(t) = -A \omega_n \sin(\omega_n t) + B \omega_n \cos(\omega_n t)$$
$$\dot{x}(0) = B \omega_n$$
$$B = \frac{V_0}{\omega_n}$$

**Step 11 — Final Closed-Form Expression:**

$$x(t) = X_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{V_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t)$$

**Step 12 — Single-Phase Form (Alternative Compact Form):**
This two-term expression can be combined into a single sinusoid with phase angle $\phi$ and amplitude $X$:

$$x(t) = X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$$

where:
$$X = \sqrt{X_0^2 + \left(\frac{V_0}{\omega_n}\right)^2}$$
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_0 \omega_n}{V_0}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpreted:** The displacement of an undamped SDOF system is a **pure sinusoid** at the **natural frequency** $\omega_n$. This is the *theoretical foundation* for every vibration problem in KTU.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Derivation of Natural Frequency for a Vertical Spring-Mass System

**Problem Setup:** A mass $m$ hangs from a linear spring of stiffness $k$ in a gravitational field $g$. The system is in static equilibrium when the spring is stretched by an amount $\delta_{st}$.

**Step 1 — Static Equilibrium Condition:**
At equilibrium, the spring force balances gravity:

$$k \delta_{st} = m g$$

Solving for the static deflection:

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{m g}{k}$$

**Step 2 — Dynamic Force Balance:**
Let $y(t)$ be the *additional* displacement from the *static equilibrium* position (downward positive). The total spring stretch at any instant is $\delta_{st} + y(t)$.

Applying Newton's Second Law to the mass during motion:

$$m \ddot{y} = m g - k (\delta_{st} + y)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Static Equilibrium Identity:**
Replace $m g$ with $k \delta_{st}$:

$$m \ddot{y} = k \delta_{st} - k \delta_{st} - k y$$

**Step 4 — Simplification (Gravity Cancels Out!):**

$$m \ddot{y} + k y = 0$$

**Step 5 — Natural Frequency Expression:**

$$\ddot{y} + \omega_n^2 y = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Step 6 — Alternative Form Using Static Deflection:**
Since $k = mg / \delta_{st}$:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Result:** The natural frequency depends **only** on the ratio of gravitational acceleration to static deflection — a very useful formula for spring suspension design.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Vibration Types

| Property | Free Vibration | Forced Vibration | Damped Vibration |
|---|---|---|---|
| External force present? | No | Yes (continuous) | Optional |
| Energy conserved? | Yes (ideal) | Yes (only in steady state) | No (dissipated) |
| Amplitude behavior | Constant | Steady-state constant | Exponentially decaying |
| Governing equation | $\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$ | $\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = (F_0/m)\sin(\omega t)$ | $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$ |
| Characteristic frequency | $\omega_n$ | $\omega$ (forcing freq.) | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ |
| Real-world example | Plucked guitar string | Washing machine on spin | Car suspension after bump |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Vibration Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical System\nCar / Engine / Beam] --> B[Idealize as\nSpring-Mass-Damper Model]
    B --> C{Identify DOF\nand Parameters}
    C --> C1[Mass m kg]
    C --> C2[Stiffness k N/m]
    C --> C3[Damping c Ns/m]
    C1 --> D[Derive Equation\nof Motion]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Form Standard ODE]
    E --> F{Solve ODE}
    F --> F1[Free Vibration\nomega_n = sqrt k over m]
    F --> F2[Forced Vibration\nSteady-state + Transient]
    F --> F3[Damped Vibration\nomega_d = omega_n sqrt 1 minus zeta squared]
    F1 --> G[Compute Response\nx of t, v of t, a of t]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Validate vs.\nExperimental Data]
    H --> I[Design Iteration\nTune m, k, c]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Tree: Classification of Vibration (For Exam Quick-Draw)

```mermaid
graph TD
    V[Vibration] --> V1[Based on Energy Dissipation]
    V --> V2[Based on External Force]
    V --> V3[Based on Repeatability]
    V --> V4[Based on DOF]
    V1 --> V1A[Undamped\nc equals 0]
    V1 --> V1B[Damped\nc greater than 0]
    V1B --> V1B1[Underdamped\nzeta less than 1]
    V1B --> V1B2[Critically Damped\nzeta equals 1]
    V1B --> V1B3[Overdamped\nzeta greater than 1]
    V2 --> V2A[Free Vibration\nF of t equals 0]
    V2 --> V2B[Forced Vibration\nF of t not 0]
    V3 --> V3A[Periodic]
    V3 --> V3B[Non-Periodic]
    V3A --> V3A1[Harmonic\nPure Sine]
    V3A --> V3A2[Non-Harmonic Periodic\nSquare, Triangle]
    V3B --> V3B1[Transient\nDecays to 0]
    V3B --> V3B2[Random\nStatistical only]
    V4 --> V4A[SDOF\n1 coordinate]
    V4 --> V4B[MDOF\n2 or more coordinates]
    V4A --> V4A1[Spring-Mass\nBasic NVH model]
    V4B --> V4B1[2-DOF, n-DOF\nEigenvalue analysis]
```

### 4.3 Concept Map: Parameters Influencing Natural Frequency

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Mass m kg] -->|Increase m| N1[Natural Freq\nDECREASES]
    K[Stiffness k N/m] -->|Increase k| N2[Natural Freq\nINCREASES]
    M --> N1
    K --> N2
    N1 --> FORMULA[omega_n equals sqrt k over m]
    N2 --> FORMULA
    FORMULA --> APPL[Engineering Application:\nTune suspension by\nadjusting m or k]
    G[Gravity g] --> STAT[Static Deflection\ndelta_st = m g over k]
    STAT --> ALTF[Alternate form:\nomega_n equals sqrt g over delta_st]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP Set A]`
**Define vibration. List any four types of vibration with one real-world example each.**

**Model Answer (3-Mark Blueprint):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** *Vibration is the oscillatory motion of a particle or a system about its equilibrium (mean) position. Mathematically it is a time-varying displacement characterized by amplitude, frequency, and phase angle.*

**Four Types (1.5 marks):**

| # | Type | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Free Vibration | A tuning fork struck and left to ring |
| 2 | Forced Vibration | A washing machine drum driven by a motor |
| 3 | Damped Vibration | A car's shock absorber after hitting a pothole |
| 4 | Random Vibration | The shaking of a vehicle on a rough road |

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Define the term "Degrees of Freedom" (DOF) in vibration analysis. Differentiate between SDOF and MDOF systems.**

**Model Answer (3-Mark Blueprint):**

> **DOF Definition (1 mark):** *Degrees of Freedom is the minimum number of independent coordinates required to completely specify the configuration of a vibrating system at any instant of time.*

**SDOF (1 mark):** *A system is called SDOF when only one coordinate is sufficient to describe its motion completely. Example: A simple mass attached to a spring moving horizontally. Equation: $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$.*

**MDOF (1 mark):** *A system is called MDOF when two or more independent coordinates are needed. Example: A two-storey building under earthquake motion. Equation form: $[M]\{\ddot{X}\} + [K]\{X\} = \{F\}$ where $[M]$ and $[K]$ are matrices.*

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Part B Module 2):** Students frequently:
> 1. Forget to convert Hz to rad/s (or vice versa) — **cost: 2 marks lost**.
> 2. Use the wrong damping regime formula when $\zeta$ is unspecified — **cost: 3 marks lost**.
> 3. Omit units in the final answer — **cost: 1 mark lost**.
> 4. Skip the *equation of motion* derivation step and jump straight to the answer — **cost: 4 marks lost**.
> **Always derive, then solve, then box your final answer with units.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Spring-Mass Natural Frequency**

`[KTU University Exam - July 2024 Module 2, Adapted]`

**(a)** *Derive the equation of motion for an undamped single degree of freedom spring-mass system. From this, obtain the expression for natural frequency and time period.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A machine of mass 50 kg is mounted on a spring of stiffness 20000 N/m. The system is displaced 0.02 m from equilibrium and released from rest. Calculate: (i) the natural frequency in rad/s and Hz, (ii) the time period, and (iii) the maximum velocity of the mass during oscillation.* **(7 marks)**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a mass $m$ attached to a spring of stiffness $k$ on a frictionless horizontal surface. Let $x(t)$ denote the displacement of mass from the equilibrium position at any instant $t$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The only horizontal force acting on the mass is the restoring force from the spring. By Hooke's Law, this force is proportional to displacement and acts towards the equilibrium position:

$$F = -k \, x(t)$$

The negative sign indicates the force opposes displacement.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply Newton's Second Law of Motion:

$$m \ddot{x}(t) = F = -k \, x(t)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Rearranging the equation:

$$m \ddot{x}(t) + k \, x(t) = 0$$

Dividing through by $m$:

$$\ddot{x}(t) + \frac{k}{m} x(t) = 0$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** By definition, the natural angular frequency is:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

So the canonical form becomes:

$$\ddot{x}(t) + \omega_n^2 \, x(t) = 0$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** The general solution of this second-order linear ODE is:

$$x(t) = X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$$

where $X$ is the amplitude and $\phi$ is the phase angle.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** The time period is the time required for one complete cycle. Since one full cycle corresponds to phase change of $2\pi$ radians, and the angular advance per second is $\omega_n$:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Mass $m = 50$ kg
- Stiffness $k = 20000$ N/m
- Initial displacement $X_0 = 0.02$ m
- Initial velocity $V_0 = 0$ m/s (released from rest)

**(i) Natural Frequency (2 marks):**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{20000}{50}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{20}{2\pi} = \frac{10}{\pi} \approx 3.183 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Time Period (2 marks):**

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2\pi}{20} = \frac{\pi}{10} \approx 0.3142 \text{ seconds}$$

**(iii) Maximum Velocity (3 marks):**

Since the system starts from rest at maximum displacement, the amplitude $X = X_0 = 0.02$ m. The maximum velocity in SHM occurs when displacement crosses zero:

$$V_{\max} = \omega_n \cdot X = 20 \times 0.02 = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Damped Free Vibration & Damping Ratio**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Module 2, Adapted]`

**(a)** *Derive the equation of motion for a damped SDOF system. Define damping ratio and explain the three cases of damping based on its value.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *The suspension system of a car has a mass of 250 kg and a damping coefficient of 1500 Ns/m. The natural frequency of the system is 2 Hz. Calculate: (i) the stiffness of the spring, (ii) the critical damping coefficient, and (iii) the damping ratio. Comment on the damping regime.* **(7 marks)**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Damped SDOF Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a mass $m$ connected to a spring of stiffness $k$ and a viscous damper of damping coefficient $c$. Let $x(t)$ be the displacement from equilibrium.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Three forces act on the mass:
- Inertia force: $m\ddot{x}$
- Damping force: $c\dot{x}$ (opposing velocity)
- Spring force: $kx$ (opposing displacement, i.e., $-kx$ when applied as external restoring)

Applying Newton's Second Law:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Divide by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{c}{m}\dot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Define the natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and damping ratio $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km})$. Then:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Critical Damping Coefficient** $c_c$ is the value of $c$ that makes the discriminant of the characteristic equation zero:

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$$

The damping ratio is the ratio of actual to critical damping:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{c}{2m\omega_n}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** **Three Cases of Damping:**

- **Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$):** System oscillates with exponentially decaying amplitude. Damped natural frequency: $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.
- **Critically Damped ($\zeta = 1$):** Returns to equilibrium in the shortest possible time without oscillation.
- **Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$):** Returns to equilibrium slowly without any oscillation.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** The general underdamped solution is:

$$x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t} \sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$$

This clearly shows the exponential envelope $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$ modulating the oscillation.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Mass $m = 250$ kg
- Damping coefficient $c = 1500$ Ns/m
- Natural frequency $f_n = 2$ Hz → $\omega_n = 2\pi f_n = 4\pi \approx 12.566$ rad/s

**(i) Stiffness (2 marks):**

From $\omega_n^2 = k/m$:

$$k = m \omega_n^2 = 250 \times (4\pi)^2 = 250 \times 16\pi^2$$

$$k = 4000 \pi^2 \approx 39478 \text{ N/m}$$

**(ii) Critical Damping Coefficient (2 marks):**

$$c_c = 2 m \omega_n = 2 \times 250 \times 4\pi = 2000\pi$$

$$c_c \approx 6283.2 \text{ Ns/m}$$

**(iii) Damping Ratio and Comment (3 marks):**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{1500}{6283.2} \approx 0.2387$$

Since $\zeta < 1$, the system is **underdamped**.

**Comment (1 mark):** The system will oscillate with exponentially decaying amplitude at the damped natural frequency:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 12.566 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.057} \approx 12.566 \times 0.971 \approx 12.20 \text{ rad/s}$$

This is typical of car suspensions, which are intentionally underdamped to provide ride comfort while ensuring rapid decay of unwanted oscillations.

---

### ⚡ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Vibration** = oscillatory motion about equilibrium; governed by $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$.
- [x] **SHM** is the building block: $x(t) = X\sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$, governed by $\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$.
- [x] **Four classifications** of vibration to memorize: (1) Free vs Forced, (2) Damped vs Undamped, (3) Periodic vs Random, (4) SDOF vs MDOF.
- [x] **Three damping regimes:** Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$), Critically damped ($\zeta = 1$), Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$).
- [x] **Natural frequency** of undamped SDOF spring-mass: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ in rad/s.
- [x] **Damped natural frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$.
- [x] **Critical damping:** $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$.
- [x] **Always convert units:** Hz → rad/s via $\omega = 2\pi f$ before using in equations.
- [x] **Phase angle** $\phi$ accounts for non-zero starting conditions; $\phi = \tan^{-1}(X_0 \omega_n / V_0)$.
- [x] **Periodic vs Random:** Periodic has a fixed period $T$; random vibration requires statistical (PSD) description.
- [x] **DOF = number of independent coordinates**; SDOF is 80% of KTU Module 2 problems.
- [x] **Logarithmic decrement** $\delta = 2\pi\zeta / \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ is used to extract damping from experiments.
- [x] **Always show three layers** in derivations: (1) Newton's Law, (2) Force substitutions, (3) Canonical ODE form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Damped and Undamped Vibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Damped and Undamped Vibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — DAMPED AND UNDAMPED VIBRATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vibration** is defined as the oscillatory motion of a body or system about an equilibrium position. In automotive and mechanical engineering, vibrations are mathematically modeled as the periodic displacement of mass elements under the action of restoring and dissipative forces.

**Undamped (Free) Vibration:** An idealized oscillatory motion in which no energy is dissipated from the system during the cycle. The total mechanical energy (kinetic + potential) remains **constant** for all time, and the oscillation theoretically continues forever.

**Damped Vibration:** The realistic oscillatory motion in which a **dissipative element** (damper/dashpot) removes energy from the vibrating system per cycle, causing the amplitude of oscillation to decay progressively with time until the system returns to its equilibrium position.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Playground Swing (Undamped):** Imagine a child on a perfectly frictionless swing in a vacuum. Once pushed, the swing would oscillate back and forth forever at exactly the same height. That is *undamped vibration* — perpetual motion with no energy loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Door Damper (Damped):** Now imagine a door fitted with a hydraulic piston. When you push the door open and let go, it swings but gradually slows down and stops. The piston converts mechanical energy into heat, removing it from the system. This is *damped vibration* — energy continually bleeds away until motion ceases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — Car Suspension (Real Engineering):** When your car hits a pothole, the body bounces up and down. Without shock absorbers (dampers), it would oscillate forever, making the ride unbearable. The shock absorber dissipates the kinetic energy as heat through oil flow, stopping the bouncing within 1–2 cycles.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Natural Frequency (ωₙ):** The frequency at which a system oscillates naturally when displaced from equilibrium and released.
- **Damping Ratio (ζ — zeta):** A dimensionless quantity comparing actual damping to critical damping.
- **Critical Damping Coefficient (c_c):** The minimum damping required to prevent any oscillation.
- **Logarithmic Decrement (δ):** The natural log of the ratio of successive vibration amplitudes — a measure of energy dissipation per cycle.
- **Standard acceleration due to gravity: g = 9.81 m/s²** — used in pendulum and suspension calculations.
- **Standard Spring Constant (k)** unit: N/m
- **Damping Coefficient (c)** unit: N·s/m

### 1.4 Degree of Freedom (DOF) and Model Setup

A **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF)** system is the foundational model for vibration analysis. It requires only **one coordinate** to fully specify the system's spatial configuration at any instant.

**The Canonical SDOF Mass-Spring-Damper Model consists of three elements:**

| Element | Symbol | Represents |
|---|---|---|
| Mass | $m$ | Inertia (resistance to acceleration) |
| Spring | $k$ | Restoring force (stores potential energy) |
| Damper | $c$ | Energy dissipation (resists velocity) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Undamped Free Vibration — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 System Description

A pure spring-mass system contains only **m** and **k** (no damper). When displaced by an initial displacement $x_0$ and/or initial velocity $\dot{x}_0$ from the static equilibrium position, the mass executes Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM).

### 2.1.2 Free Body Diagram & Force Analysis

Applying **Newton's Second Law** in the x-direction:

$$\sum F_x = m\ddot{x}$$

The spring exerts a **restoring force** opposite to displacement:

$$F_{spring} = -kx$$

Therefore:

$$m\ddot{x} = -kx$$

Rearranging gives the **Governing Differential Equation of Motion (EOM):**

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

Or in normalized form:

$$\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

where $\omega_n$ is the **undamped natural frequency**.

### 2.1.3 Why This Equation Matters

The absence of a $\dot{x}$ (velocity) term indicates **no energy dissipation**. The total energy (kinetic + potential) is conserved. The motion is therefore **purely sinusoidal and perpetual** — a mathematical idealization that is approached in vacuum chambers and superconducting levitation systems.

### 2.1.4 Solution Methodology

The characteristic equation is:

$$m\lambda^2 + k = 0 \implies \lambda^2 = -\frac{k}{m} = -\omega_n^2$$

The roots are imaginary: $\lambda = \pm j\omega_n$

The general solution is therefore:

$$x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)$$

Using initial conditions $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$:

$$x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t)$$

Equivalent amplitude–phase form:

$$x(t) = X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$$

where:

$$X = \sqrt{x_0^2 + \left(\frac{v_0}{\omega_n}\right)^2}, \quad \tan\phi = \frac{\omega_n x_0}{v_0}$$

---

## 2.2 Damped Free Vibration — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 System Description

Real systems **always dissipate energy** through friction, air resistance, internal material damping, etc. This is modeled mathematically by adding a **viscous damper** (dashpot) with coefficient $c$.

### 2.2.2 Free Body Diagram & Force Analysis

Three forces act on the mass:
- Inertia force: $m\ddot{x}$
- Damping force: $c\dot{x}$ (opposes velocity, hence $-c\dot{x}$)
- Spring force: $-kx$

Newton's Second Law gives:

$$m\ddot{x} = -kx - c\dot{x}$$

Rearranging:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

Or normalized:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

where $\zeta$ is the **damping ratio**.

### 2.2.3 Characteristic Equation

Assume solution $x = e^{st}$. Substituting:

$$ms^2 + cs + k = 0$$

The roots are:

$$s_{1,2} = \frac{-c \pm \sqrt{c^2 - 4mk}}{2m}$$

These roots depend critically on the **discriminant** $\Delta = c^2 - 4mk$, giving rise to three distinct cases.

### 2.2.4 The Three Cases of Damping

**Case 1 — Underdamped (ζ < 1):** Oscillatory decay
- The discriminant is negative: $c^2 < 4mk$
- Real-world examples: car suspension (lightly damped), building during earthquake, vehicle body bounce.
- Solution: $x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t} \sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$
- **Damped natural frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$

**Case 2 — Critically Damped (ζ = 1):** Fastest non-oscillatory return
- Discriminant is zero: $c^2 = 4mk$
- The system returns to equilibrium **as quickly as possible without overshooting**.
- Solution: $x(t) = (A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$

**Case 3 — Overdamped (ζ > 1):** Slow non-oscillatory return
- Discriminant is positive: $c^2 > 4mk$
- The system creeps slowly back to equilibrium without oscillation.
- Solution: $x(t) = A e^{s_1 t} + B e^{s_2 t}$, where $s_1, s_2 < 0$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Undamped natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s |
| 2 | Undamped natural frequency (Hz) | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Hz |
| 3 | Time period (undamped) | $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$ | s |
| 4 | Critical damping coefficient | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ | N·s/m |
| 5 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s |
| 7 | Equation of motion (general SDOF) | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ | N |
| 8 | Underdamped solution | $x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$ | m |
| 9 | Logarithmic decrement | $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Decay rate per cycle | $\dfrac{x(t)}{x(t+T_d)} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Quality factor (Q-factor) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ (for low $\zeta$) | dimensionless |
| 12 | Number of cycles for amplitude to halve | $n_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\delta} \approx 0.693/\delta$ | cycles |
| 13 | Static deflection of spring | $\delta_{st} = mg/k$ | m |
| 14 | Spring-mass period in terms of $\delta_{st}$ | $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{\delta_{st}/g}$ | s |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Automotive NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness):** Suspension tuning requires precise control of $\zeta$. Typical target: $\zeta \approx 0.2$ to $0.4$ for comfort without excessive road feedback.
2. **Earthquake Engineering:** Buildings are designed with $\zeta \approx 0.05$ to $0.10$ to limit oscillation amplitude.
3. **Aerospace:** Spacecraft docking mechanisms require critical damping ($\zeta = 1$) to prevent collisions during contact.
4. **Machinery Design:** Tooling and precision instruments need $\zeta < 0.05$ to minimize settling time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Undamped Natural Frequency

**Step 1 — Write Newton's Law for the spring-mass system:**

$$\sum F_x = m\ddot{x}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the spring force acting on the mass:**

The spring force is **proportional** to displacement $x$ and **opposite** in direction:

$$F_s = -kx$$

> *Logic:* A positive displacement (stretch) pulls the mass back; a negative displacement (compression) pushes it forward. The negative sign encodes the restoring nature.

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's second law and isolate terms:**

$$m\ddot{x} = -kx \implies m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 4 — Divide through by $m$ to standardize the equation:**

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

**Step 5 — Define the undamped natural frequency by inspection:**

Comparing with the canonical SHM form $\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$:

$$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to natural frequency in Hz:**

Since $\omega_n = 2\pi f_n$:

$$\boxed{f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}}$$

**Step 7 — Period of oscillation:**

Since $T_n = 1/f_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$:

$$\boxed{T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Damping Coefficient

**Step 1 — Begin with the damped EOM:**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 2 — Form the characteristic equation by assuming $x = e^{st}$:**

Substitute:

$$ms^2 e^{st} + cs e^{st} + k e^{st} = 0$$

$$e^{st}(ms^2 + cs + k) = 0$$

Since $e^{st} \neq 0$, we get:

$$ms^2 + cs + k = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve the quadratic in $s$ using the quadratic formula:**

$$s = \frac{-c \pm \sqrt{c^2 - 4mk}}{2m}$$

**Step 4 — Set discriminant = 0 for the critical condition:**

Critical damping corresponds to the boundary between oscillatory and non-oscillatory behavior:

$$c^2 - 4mk = 0$$

$$c^2 = 4mk$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $c_c$:**

$$\boxed{c_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2m\omega_n}$$

> *Logic:* Any $c < c_c$ yields underdamped (oscillatory) motion; any $c > c_c$ yields overdamped (non-oscillatory) motion.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Damping Ratio and Damped Natural Frequency

**Step 1 — Write the discriminant of the characteristic equation:**

$$\Delta = c^2 - 4mk$$

**Step 2 — Normalize the discriminant by $(2m\omega_n)^2 = 4mk$:**

$$\frac{\Delta}{4mk} = \frac{c^2}{4mk} - 1$$

**Step 3 — Recognize $\zeta = c/c_c = c/(2\sqrt{mk})$:**

$$\left(\frac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}}\right)^2 = \zeta^2$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{c^2}{4mk} = \zeta^2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$\frac{\Delta}{4mk} = \zeta^2 - 1$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the roots of the characteristic equation:**

$$s_{1,2} = \frac{-c \pm \sqrt{c^2 - 4mk}}{2m} = \frac{-c}{2m} \pm \frac{\sqrt{c^2 - 4mk}}{2m}$$

$$s_{1,2} = -\frac{c}{2m} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{c}{2m}\right)^2 - \frac{k}{m}}$$

Using $\zeta$ and $\omega_n$:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

**Step 6 — For the underdamped case ($\zeta < 1$), define the damped natural frequency:**

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm j\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

**Step 7 — General underdamped solution:**

With real and imaginary parts separated, the solution takes the form:

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[A\cos(\omega_d t) + B\sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$

Or in amplitude-phase form:

$$\boxed{x(t) = X\, e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)}$$

where:

$$X = \sqrt{x_0^2 + \left(\frac{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d}\right)^2}$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_d x_0}{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}\right)$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Logarithmic Decrement

**Step 1 — Write the displacement at two consecutive peaks.**

Peak 1 occurs at time $t_1$: $x(t_1) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_1}$
Peak 2 occurs at time $t_1 + T_d$: $x(t_1 + T_d) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n (t_1 + T_d)}$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio of successive peaks:**

$$\frac{x(t_1)}{x(t_1 + T_d)} = \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_1}}{e^{-\zeta\omega_n (t_1 + T_d)}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d}$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{x(t_1)}{x(t_1 + T_d)}\right) = \zeta\omega_n T_d$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d = 2\pi/(\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2})$:**

$$\delta = \zeta\omega_n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for damping ratio in terms of measured decay:**

$$\boxed{\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Damped System Response

| Case | Damping Ratio $\zeta$ | Discriminant | Roots $s_{1,2}$ | Solution Type | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Undamped | $\zeta = 0$ | $\Delta = -4mk < 0$ | $\pm j\omega_n$ | Pure sinusoid | Idealized spring (vacuum) |
| Underdamped | $0 < \zeta < 1$ | $\Delta < 0$ | $-\zeta\omega_n \pm j\omega_d$ | Decaying sinusoid | Car suspension |
| Critically damped | $\zeta = 1$ | $\Delta = 0$ | $-\omega_n$ (double) | Exponential decay | Door closers, galvanometers |
| Overdamped | $\zeta > 1$ | $\Delta > 0$ | Two real negative roots | Sum of two exponentials | Slow-closing valves |

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A vehicle suspension has $m = 250$ kg, $k = 25{,}000$ N/m, $c = 1500$ N·s/m. Find $\omega_n$, $\zeta$, $\omega_d$, and the displacement after 0.5 s if $x_0 = 0.05$ m and $\dot{x}_0 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\omega_n$:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{25000}{250}} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $c_c$:**

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{25000 \times 250} = 2\sqrt{6{,}250{,}000} = 5000 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\zeta$:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{1500}{5000} = 0.30$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\omega_d$:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 10\sqrt{1-0.09} = 10\sqrt{0.91} \approx 9.539 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute amplitude $X$ (with $\dot{x}_0 = 0$):**

$$X = \sqrt{x_0^2 + \left(\frac{\zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d}\right)^2} = x_0\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\zeta\omega_n}{\omega_d}\right)^2}$$

$$\frac{\zeta\omega_n}{\omega_d} = \frac{0.30 \times 10}{9.539} = 0.3145$$

$$X = 0.05\sqrt{1 + 0.0989} = 0.05 \times 1.0480 = 0.0524 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute displacement at $t = 0.5$ s:**

Decay envelope: $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t} = e^{-0.30 \times 10 \times 0.5} = e^{-1.5} = 0.2231$

The phase angle (with $\dot{x}_0 = 0$): $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega_d/(\zeta\omega_n)) \approx 1.255$ rad

$$x(0.5) = 0.0524 \times 0.2231 \times \sin(9.539 \times 0.5 + 1.255)$$

$$x(0.5) = 0.01169 \times \sin(6.025) = 0.01169 \times (-0.243) \approx -0.00284 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** After 0.5 seconds, the suspension mass has oscillated past zero and is approximately **2.84 mm below** equilibrium.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SDOF Mass-Spring-Damper System (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WallA[Fixed Wall Left] --- spring1[Spring k]
    spring1 --- massNode[Mass m]
    massNode --- damper1[Damper c]
    damper1 --- WallB[Fixed Wall Right]
    massNode --- forceIn[External Force F t]
    massNode --- obsNode[Displacement x t Velocity xdot Acceleration xddot]
```

> *Note: This flowchart shows the conceptual layout of an SDOF system. The mass is supported by both a spring and damper connected between two fixed references, and is subject to an external force whose displacement is measured.*

---

## 4.2 Force Decomposition on Mass Element

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MassNode[Mass m]
    MassNode --> Finertia[Inertia Force m x ddot]
    MassNode --> Fspring[Spring Force k x opposing displacement]
    MassNode --> Fdamp[Damper Force c xdot opposing velocity]
    MassNode --> Fext[External Force F t]
    Finertia --> EqSum[Newton Second Law Sum F equals m x ddot]
    Fspring --> EqSum
    Fdamp --> EqSum
    Fext --> EqSum
    EqSum --> EOM[Equation of Motion m x ddot plus c xdot plus k x equals F t]
```

---

## 4.3 Damping Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Identify Damping Regime] --> CheckC{Compare c with c subscript c}
    CheckC -->|c equals c subscript c| CritDamp[Critical Damping zeta equals 1]
    CheckC -->|c less than c subscript c| UnderDamp[Underdamped zeta less than 1]
    CheckC -->|c greater than c subscript c| OverDamp[Overdamped zeta greater than 1]
    CritDamp --> CritDesc[Fastest non oscillatory return Solution exponential t times e power minus omega n t]
    UnderDamp --> UnderDesc[Decaying oscillation Solution X e power minus zeta omega n t sin omega d t plus phi]
    OverDamp --> OverDesc[Creeping return Solution A e power s1 t plus B e power s2 t]
```

---

## 4.4 Step Response Behavior Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Underdamped
        U1[Starts oscillating] --> U2[Amplitude decays exponentially] --> U3[Crosses equilibrium multiple times]
    end
    subgraph CriticalDamp
        C1[Starts from initial condition] --> C2[Returns smoothly] --> C3[No oscillation]
    end
    subgraph Overdamp
        O1[Starts from initial condition] --> O2[Returns slowly] --> O3[No oscillation but slower than critical]
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Energy Flow in Damped vs Undamped System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UndampedSystem
        UE[Total Energy Constant] --> UK[Kinetic Energy]
        UE --> UP[Potential Energy in Spring]
        UK <-->|periodic exchange| UP
    end
    subgraph DampedSystem
        DE[Total Energy Decreasing] --> DK[Kinetic Energy]
        DE --> DP[Potential Energy in Spring]
        DE --> DL[Lost Energy as Heat through Damper]
        DK <-->|periodic exchange| DP
        DK --> DL
        DP --> DL
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define **damping ratio** and explain the three cases of damping based on its value.

**Model Answer:**

The **damping ratio $\zeta$** is a dimensionless parameter defined as the ratio of actual damping coefficient $c$ to the critical damping coefficient $c_c$:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$$

The three cases are:

1. **Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$):** The system oscillates with progressively decaying amplitude. Damped natural frequency $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.
2. **Critically damped ($\zeta = 1$):** The system returns to equilibrium in the shortest possible time without any oscillation.
3. **Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$):** The system returns to equilibrium without oscillation, but more slowly than the critically damped case.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is **logarithmic decrement**? Write its formula in terms of damping ratio.

**Model Answer:**

The **logarithmic decrement $\delta$** is defined as the natural logarithm of the ratio of two successive peak amplitudes in a damped oscillation. It quantifies the rate at which vibration amplitude decays.

$$\delta = \ln\left(\frac{x(t)}{x(t+T_d)}\right) = \zeta\omega_n T_d$$

In terms of the damping ratio:

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}}$$

For small $\zeta$, this simplifies to $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Module Internal Choice — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** (a) Derive the equation of motion of a damped single degree of freedom system and obtain its solution for the underdamped case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine of mass **50 kg** is mounted on a spring and damper. The static deflection of the spring is **2 mm**. A damper provides a damping ratio of **0.2**. Determine:
- (i) The stiffness $k$ of the spring
- (ii) The natural frequency $\omega_n$
- (iii) The damping coefficient $c$
- (iv) The damped natural frequency $\omega_d$
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Free body diagram:** Mass $m$ connected to ground via spring (k) and damper (c) in parallel. External force $F(t)$ acts on the mass.

**Step 2 — Newton's Second Law:**

$$\sum F_x = m\ddot{x}$$

The forces are: spring force $-kx$, damper force $-c\dot{x}$, external force $F(t)$.

$$m\ddot{x} = -kx - c\dot{x} + F(t)$$

**Step 3 — Equation of motion (free vibration, $F(t) = 0$):**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 4 — Normalized form:**

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

where $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$.

**Step 5 — Characteristic equation:**

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

**Step 6 — Underdamped case ($\zeta < 1$):**

$$\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1} = j\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$$

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm j\omega_d, \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$$

**Step 7 — General solution:**

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[A\cos(\omega_d t) + B\sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$

Or equivalently:

$$\boxed{x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t} \sin(\omega_d t + \phi)}$$

**Step 8 — Constants from initial conditions $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$:**

$$X = \sqrt{x_0^2 + \left(\frac{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d}\right)^2}, \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_d x_0}{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}\right)$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $m = 50$ kg, $\delta_{st} = 2$ mm $= 0.002$ m, $\zeta = 0.2$

**(i) Stiffness $k$:**

At static equilibrium, $mg = k\delta_{st}$:

$$k = \frac{mg}{\delta_{st}} = \frac{50 \times 9.81}{0.002} = \frac{490.5}{0.002} = 245{,}250 \text{ N/m}$$

**(ii) Natural frequency $\omega_n$:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{245250}{50}} = \sqrt{4905} \approx 70.04 \text{ rad/s}$$

Alternative using static deflection: $\omega_n = \sqrt{g/\delta_{st}} = \sqrt{9.81/0.002} = \sqrt{4905} \approx 70.04$ rad/s ✓

**(iii) Damping coefficient $c$:**

$$c = 2\zeta\sqrt{km} = 2 \times 0.2 \times \sqrt{245250 \times 50}$$

$$c = 0.4 \times \sqrt{12{,}262{,}500} = 0.4 \times 3501.79 \approx 1400.71 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**(iv) Damped natural frequency $\omega_d$:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 70.04 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.04}$$

$$\omega_d = 70.04 \times \sqrt{0.96} = 70.04 \times 0.9798 \approx 68.63 \text{ rad/s}$$

---

### Module Internal Choice — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** (a) Define natural frequency. Derive the expression for the natural frequency of an undamped single degree of freedom system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The instrument panel of a car is modeled as a single degree of freedom system with mass **5 kg** and stiffness **8000 N/m**. A rubber mount provides damping such that the damping ratio is **0.15**.
- (i) Calculate the undamped natural frequency $f_n$ in Hz.
- (ii) Calculate the logarithmic decrement.
- (iii) If the initial displacement is **0.01 m**, find the amplitude after **5 complete cycles**.
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** The **natural frequency** of a vibrating system is the frequency at which the system oscillates when displaced from its equilibrium position and released, in the absence of any external force or damping.

**Derivation:**

Consider a mass $m$ attached to a spring of stiffness $k$ (no damper), displaced by $x$ from equilibrium.

Applying Newton's Second Law in the x-direction:

$$\sum F_x = m\ddot{x}$$

The spring force is $-kx$:

$$m\ddot{x} = -kx$$

Rearranging:

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

Dividing by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

This is the **standard form of Simple Harmonic Motion** $\ddot{x} + \omega^2 x = 0$, with solution $x = A\cos(\omega t) + B\sin(\omega t)$.

Comparing:

$$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}}$$

In Hz:

$$\boxed{f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}}$$

The corresponding period is:

$$T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $m = 5$ kg, $k = 8000$ N/m, $\zeta = 0.15$, $x_0 = 0.01$ m, $n = 5$ cycles.

**(i) Undamped natural frequency:**

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{8000}{5}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{1600} = \frac{40}{2\pi}$$

$$f_n \approx 6.366 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Logarithmic decrement:**

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.15}{\sqrt{1 - 0.0225}} = \frac{0.9425}{\sqrt{0.9775}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{0.9425}{0.9887} \approx 0.9533$$

**(iii) Amplitude after 5 complete cycles:**

Using the relation $x_n/x_0 = e^{-n\delta}$:

$$\frac{x_5}{x_0} = e^{-5 \times 0.9533} = e^{-4.7665}$$

$$e^{-4.7665} \approx 0.00851$$

$$x_5 = 0.01 \times 0.00851 = 8.51 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \approx 0.0851 \text{ mm}$$

**Result:** After 5 complete cycles, the amplitude has decayed from 10 mm to approximately **0.085 mm** — a reduction of over 99%.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Errors (–1 to –2 marks):** Confusing rad/s with Hz. Always check: $\omega$ is in rad/s; $f$ is in Hz; relationship is $\omega = 2\pi f$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Damping Force Direction (–2 marks):** The damper force is $-c\dot{x}$, NOT $+c\dot{x}$. The negative sign indicates opposition to velocity.
>
> 3. **Misidentifying $\omega_d$ (–1 mark):** The damped natural frequency $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ is *less* than $\omega_n$, not greater. Many students mistakenly write $\omega_d > \omega_n$.
>
> 4. **Static Deflection Misuse (–2 marks):** When given static deflection, students often compute $k = mg/\delta_{st}$ correctly but then forget to use this $k$ in subsequent steps.
>
> 5. **Missing Initial Conditions (–1 mark):** In damped problems, constants $A$ and $B$ (or $X$ and $\phi$) **must** be evaluated using initial conditions $x(0)$ and $\dot{x}(0)$. Skipping this loses full solution marks.
>
> 6. **Confusing Time Periods:** $T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$ is for *undamped* period; the damped period is $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$, which is *longer* than $T_n$.
>
> 7. **Overdamped vs Underdamped Confusion:** When asked for "damped free vibration" without specifying $\zeta$, students often blindly write the underdamped solution. Verify the discriminant first.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Equation of Motion (Damped SDOF):** $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$
- [x] **Undamped natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$, $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$
- [x] **Critical damping coefficient:** $c_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2m\omega_n$
- [x] **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c/c_c = c/(2\sqrt{mk})$
- [x] **Three damping regimes:** Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$), Critically damped ($\zeta = 1$), Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$)
- [x] **Damped natural frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$
- [x] **Underdamped solution form:** $x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$
- [x] **Logarithmic decrement:** $\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ — measures decay per cycle
- [x] **Static deflection:** $\delta_{st} = mg/k$; useful shortcut for vertical systems
- [x] **Damped period:** $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d > T_n$ (damping slows the oscillation)
- [x] **Energy conservation:** Undamped systems conserve energy; damped systems dissipate energy as heat
- [x] **NVH Application:** Car suspensions target $\zeta \approx 0.2$–$0.4$ for comfort
- [x] **Quick sanity check:** If a problem gives $c > 2\sqrt{km}$, the motion is non-oscillatory.
- [x] **For small damping ($\zeta < 0.2$):** $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$, $\omega_d \approx \omega_n$, and $T_d \approx T_n$.
- [x] **Half-life cycles:** $n_{1/2} = \ln(2)/\delta \approx 0.693/\delta$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Free and Forced Vibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Free and Forced Vibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Vibration?

In mechanical systems, **vibration** is the oscillatory motion of a particle, body, or system about an equilibrium position. Every physical system possessing **mass (m)** and **stiffness (k)** possesses at least one natural frequency at which it can vibrate freely when disturbed from its equilibrium state.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> Vibration is the to-and-fro motion of a body or system of bodies about a mean (equilibrium) position. The study of vibration is central to Noise, Vibration and Harshness (NVH) engineering because unwanted vibration is the root cause of structural failure, noise radiation, and passenger discomfort.

## 1.2 Classification of Vibration

Vibrations are broadly classified into the following types:

| Type | Distinguishing Feature | Energy Source |
|------|------------------------|---------------|
| **Free Vibration** | Occurs after an initial disturbance; no external force acts after $t=0$ | Initial stored energy (PE or KE) |
| **Forced Vibration** | Sustained by a continuous external time-dependent force | External excitation force |
| **Damped Vibration** | Energy dissipates with time (friction, viscous drag) | Internal damping |
| **Undamped Vibration** | Ideal case; amplitude remains constant forever | None |
| **Self-Excited Vibration** | Force is created by the motion itself (e.g., flutter) | Motion-induced |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU 2024 scheme, **Module 2** focuses primarily on **Free Vibration** (with and without damping) and **Forced Vibration** (harmonic excitation). Mastery of the equation of motion and its solutions is the foundation for NVH analysis.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Real-World Analogy for Free Vibration
Imagine pushing a child on a swing once and walking away. The swing continues to oscillate back and forth, slowly losing height due to air resistance and friction at the pivot. This is **free vibration**:
- The **swing** = mass ($m$)
- The **chains** = spring (stiffness $k$)
- The **air resistance + pivot friction** = damping coefficient ($c$)
- After the initial push, no external force sustains the motion

### Real-World Analogy for Forced Vibration
Now imagine continuously pushing the swing at regular intervals (rhythmically adding small pushes). The amplitude builds up if you push at the right time (resonance). This is **forced vibration**:
- A **time-dependent external force** $F(t)$ is applied
- The system responds at the **forcing frequency** $\omega$, not its natural frequency $\omega_n$

> [!TIP]
> **Why study this?** In automobiles, engine vibrations, road excitation, and tire imbalance are all sources of forced vibration. By understanding transmissibility and magnification factor, NVH engineers design mounts and dampers to keep passengers comfortable.

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities and Units

- **Natural frequency** ($\omega_n$): rad/s — the frequency at which a system naturally oscillates
- **Damping ratio** ($\zeta$): dimensionless — fraction of critical damping
- **Damping coefficient** ($c$): N·s/m
- **Forcing frequency** ($\omega$): rad/s
- **Magnification factor** ($M$): dimensionless
- **Transmissibility** ($TR$): dimensionless

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse $\omega$ (forcing frequency, in rad/s) with $f$ (frequency in Hz). Always use $\omega = 2\pi f$ correctly. KTU questions frequently test dimensional consistency.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Free Vibration Without Damping (Undamped)

### System Description
A single degree of freedom (SDOF) system consists of:
- Mass $m$ (kg)
- Spring stiffness $k$ (N/m)
- No damper ($c = 0$)

### Equation of Motion
Applying Newton's second law to the mass:

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

Dividing by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m}$$

### Solution
The general solution is:

$$x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)$$

or equivalently:

$$x(t) = X\sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$$

where:
- $X$ = amplitude of vibration
- $\phi$ = phase angle
- $\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$ = natural circular frequency (rad/s)

### Natural Frequency in Hz
$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{Hz}$$

### Key Insights
- **Why does this happen?** The spring stores potential energy ($PE = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$) and the mass stores kinetic energy ($KE = \frac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2$). Energy continuously oscillates between these two forms.
- **Amplitude is constant** because no energy is lost (idealized case).
- **The period** $T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n}$ is independent of amplitude (isochronous property).

## 2.2 Free Vibration With Viscous Damping

### Equation of Motion
With a viscous damper added (force $= c\dot{x}$):

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

Dividing by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

where:
- **Critical damping coefficient**: $c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$
- **Damping ratio**: $\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$

### Three Cases of Damping

#### Case 1: Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$)
- **Real-world scenario**: Car suspension, structural systems

$$x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$$

where **damped natural frequency**:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

#### Case 2: Critically Damped ($\zeta = 1$)
- The system returns to equilibrium in the **shortest possible time** without oscillating.

$$x(t) = (A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$$

- **Application**: Door closers, gun recoil mechanisms

#### Case 3: Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$)
- The system returns to equilibrium **slowly** without oscillation.

$$x(t) = A e^{(-\zeta\omega_n + \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2-1})t} + B e^{(-\zeta\omega_n - \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2-1})t}$$

## 2.3 Logarithmic Decrement (Underdamped Case)

A measure of the rate at which amplitude decays:

$$\delta = \ln\left(\frac{x_1}{x_2}\right) = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

where $x_1$ and $x_2$ are successive peak amplitudes.

Solving for $\zeta$:

$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** Experimentally, $\delta$ is measured by recording two successive peaks. Then $\zeta$ is calculated, which directly gives the damping coefficient $c$ in the system. This is used in modal testing of structures.

## 2.4 Forced Harmonic Vibration

### Equation of Motion
For an SDOF system subjected to harmonic force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

### Steady-State Solution
$$x(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

where the **steady-state amplitude** is:

$$X = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

and the **phase angle** is:

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2\zeta r}{1-r^2}\right)$$

### Frequency Ratio
$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n}$$

### Magnification Factor (Dynamic Magnifier)
$$M = \frac{X}{X_{static}} = \frac{X}{F_0/k} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

### Resonance Condition
Maximum amplitude occurs at:

$$\omega_{resonance} = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$

And the **maximum magnification factor** is:

$$M_{max} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

For $\zeta \ll 1$ (lightly damped), $M_{max} \approx \frac{1}{2\zeta}$ and $\omega_{resonance} \approx \omega_n$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** At exact $r=1$ (forced frequency equals undamped natural frequency), the magnification factor is $M = \frac{1}{2\zeta}$ — not infinity. Students often wrongly write $M = \infty$ at resonance.

## 2.5 Transmissibility

Transmissibility is the ratio of the force transmitted to the foundation to the applied force:

$$TR = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

### Important Transmissibility Rules
- $TR = 1$ when $r = 0$ (no vibration isolation)
- $TR = 1$ when $r = \sqrt{2}$ (the "crossover" point)
- $TR < 1$ only when $r > \sqrt{2}$ — **vibration isolation** is achieved

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Application:** Machine mounts, engine mounts, and anti-vibration pads are designed so that the operating frequency $\omega$ is much greater than $\omega_n$ (i.e., $r \gg \sqrt{2}$). Then the transmitted force to the supporting structure is reduced. The lower the damping, the better the isolation (for $r > \sqrt{2}$).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|--------------|-------------|-----------|
| Natural frequency (undamped) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s |
| Natural frequency in Hz | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Hz |
| Critical damping | $c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$ | N·s/m |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | dimensionless |
| Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s |
| Logarithmic decrement | $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| Frequency ratio | $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n}$ | dimensionless |
| Steady-state amplitude | $X = \dfrac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | m |
| Magnification factor | $M = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| Phase angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta r}{1-r^2}\right)$ | rad |
| Resonance frequency | $\omega_{res} = \omega_n\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ | rad/s |
| Max magnification | $M_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| Transmissibility | $TR = \dfrac{\sqrt{1+(2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter:** The KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam) and internal assessments emphasize numerical problems on magnification factor, transmissibility, and logarithmic decrement. Memorize the structure of these expressions — the same denominator $\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2}$ appears in multiple quantities.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equation of Motion for Undamped Free Vibration

### Setup
Consider a mass $m$ attached to a spring of stiffness $k$, free to slide on a frictionless horizontal surface. The mass is displaced by a distance $x$ from equilibrium.

### Free Body Diagram Forces
