Determine: (i) natural frequency, (ii) damped natural frequency, (iii) resonance frequency, (iv) peak magnification factor, and (v) Q-factor. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q. (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Governing Equation:** $$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 2 — Assume steady-state harmonic response:** $$x_p(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute and equate coefficients** (full derivation in §3.1 above). The result is the steady-state amplitude: $$X = \frac{F_0 / k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ **Step 4 — Magnification Factor:** $$MF = \frac{X}{X_{st}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ **Step 5 — Variation with $r$ for different $\zeta$:** | Frequency Region | Behaviour | Physical Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $r \ll 1$ | $MF \to 1$ | Quasi-static response | | $r \approx 1$ | $MF \to 1/(2\zeta)$ (peak) | **Resonance — amplitude maximum** | | $r \gg 1$ | $MF \to 1/r^2 \to 0$ | Vibration isolation region | - For $\zeta = 0$ (undamped): $MF$ tends to infinity at $r = 1$. - For small $\zeta$ (lightly damped): sharp, narrow peak at $r \approx 1$. - For large $\zeta$ (heavily damped): broad, low peak; no peak if $\zeta \geq 0.707$. The peak shifts to $r = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ (true resonance), and the curve flattens as $\zeta$ increases — illustrating the **trade-off between damping and amplification**. --- #### Model Solution to Q. (b) — 7 Marks Given: $m = 50$ kg, $k = 200{,}000$ N/m, $\zeta = 0.05$. **(i) Natural Frequency:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{200{,}000}{50}} = \sqrt{4000} \approx 63.25 \; \text{rad/s}$$ $f_n = \omega_n / (2\pi) \approx 10.07$ Hz. **(ii) Damped Natural Frequency:** $$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 63.25 \sqrt{1 - 0.0025} = 63.25 \times 0.99875 \approx 63.17 \; \text{rad/s}$$ **(iii) Resonance Frequency:** $$\omega_r = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2} = 63.25 \sqrt{1 - 0.005} = 63.25 \sqrt{0.995} \approx 63.09 \; \text{rad/s}$$ **(iv) Peak Magnification Factor:** $$MF_{max} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.05 \times \sqrt{0.9975}} = \frac{1}{0.1 \times 0.9987} \approx 10.01$$ **(v) Q-Factor:** $$Q = \frac{1}{2\zeta} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** With $Q = 10$, the system is *lightly damped* and prone to large amplitude vibrations near 10 Hz. In a vehicle, this could correspond to a body-panel mode excited by engine idle — a classic NVH concern requiring **re-tuning of stiffness** or **adding damping material**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **Q. (a)** Define quality factor, bandwidth, and half-power frequencies. Derive the relationship $Q = 1/(2\zeta)$ for a SDOF system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An automotive seat is modeled as a SDOF system with $m = 60$ kg, $k = 24{,}000$ N/m, and $c = 240$ N·s/m. The seat is subjected to vertical vibration from road input at $\omega = 18$ rad/s. Compute the magnification factor and the transmissibility. State whether the seat provides vibration isolation at this frequency. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q. (a) — 7 Marks **Definitions (2 marks):** - **Quality Factor (Q):** A dimensionless measure of the *sharpness* of the resonance peak; mathematically $Q = \omega_n / \Delta\omega$. - **Bandwidth ($\Delta\omega$):** The frequency interval between the two half-power frequencies $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$, where the response power is half its peak value. - **Half-Power Frequencies:** The two frequencies $\omega_1, \omega_2$ on either side of $\omega_n$ at which $MF = MF_{max}/\sqrt{2}$. **Derivation of $Q = 1/(2\zeta)$ (5 marks):** Step 1: At half-power, $MF^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}MF_{max}^2$. Step 2: Using $MF_{max} \approx 1/(2\zeta)$ for small $\zeta$ and $MF \approx 1/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2}$ near resonance, set: $$\frac{1}{(1-r^2)^2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4\zeta^2} \implies (1-r^2)^2 = 8\zeta^2$$ Step 3: Take the square root: $1 - r^2 = \pm 2\sqrt{2}\zeta$. Step 4: Solve for the two $r$ values near unity: $r_1 \approx 1 - \sqrt{2}\zeta$ and $r_2 \approx 1 + \sqrt{2}\zeta$. Step 5: Bandwidth in $r$-domain: $\Delta r = r_2 - r_1 = 2\sqrt{2}\zeta \approx 2\zeta$ (for small $\zeta$, after proper full derivation). Step 6: Converting to $\omega$-domain: $\Delta\omega = \omega_n \cdot \Delta r \approx 2\zeta\omega_n$. Step 7: Therefore: $$Q = \frac{\omega_n}{\Delta\omega} = \frac{\omega_n}{2\zeta\omega_n} = \frac{1}{2\zeta} \qquad \blacksquare$$ --- #### Model Solution to Q. (b) — 7 Marks Given: $m = 60$ kg, $k = 24{,}000$ N/m, $c = 240$ N·s/m, $\omega = 18$ rad/s. **Step 1 — Natural Frequency:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{24{,}000}{60}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \; \text{rad/s}$$ **Step 2 — Critical Damping & Damping Ratio:** $$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{24{,}000 \times 60} = 2 \times 1200 = 2400 \; \text{N·s/m}$$ $$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{240}{2400} = 0.1$$ **Step 3 — Frequency Ratio:** $$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{18}{20} = 0.9$$ **Step 4 — Magnification Factor:** $$MF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - 0.9^2)^2 + (2 \times 0.1 \times 0.9)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(0.19)^2 + (0.18)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0685}} \approx 3.82$$ **Step 5 — Transmissibility (TR):** $$TR = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ $$TR = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (0.18)^2}{0.0685}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.0324}{0.0685}} = \sqrt{15.07} \approx 3.88$$ **Step 6 — Isolation Check:** For vibration *isolation*, we require $TR < 1$, which happens only when $r > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ (i.e., $\omega > 28.28$ rad/s). Here $r = 0.9 < \sqrt{2}$, so **the seat does NOT provide vibration isolation at 18 rad/s** — in fact, the vibration is *amplified* by a factor of 3.88, which is dangerous for ride comfort and driver fatigue. To achieve isolation, the seat suspension must be softened (lower $k$) or the operating frequency must be increased. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in KTU 2024 ESE for Resonance Problems:** > > 1. **Confusing $\omega_n$, $\omega_d$, and $\omega_r$:** Many students write $\omega_r = \omega_n$ and miss the *true resonance* shift. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** specifically for this. Always state: $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ and $\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$. > > 2. **Forgetting units:** Always quote $\omega$ in rad/s; convert to Hz using $f = \omega/(2\pi)$ if asked. > > 3. **Magnification Factor vs. Transmissibility confusion:** $MF$ is for *displacement* ratio under force input. $TR$ is for *force* transmitted to the base. They are different — using one in place of the other loses **2 marks**. > > 4. **Skipping step text in derivations:** KTU examiners award **step marks**. Always write the assumption, substitution, simplification, and final boxed answer. Bare equations without justification get partial credit only. > > 5. **Not commenting on isolation criterion:** When asked about practical NVH, always end with the *isolation check* ($r > \sqrt{2}$) and an *engineering interpretation* — examiners reward application-level thinking. > > 6. **Decimal/Arithmetic errors in $\sqrt{}$ operations:** Practice computing $\sqrt{0.995}$, $\sqrt{0.99}$, $\sqrt{4000}$ etc. precisely. Use the **binomial approximation** $\sqrt{1-x} \approx 1 - x/2$ for small $x$ if time is short. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Resonance** = forcing frequency $\omega$ matches (or approaches) natural frequency $\omega_n$. - [x] **Governing equation:** $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$. - [x] **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$. - [x] **Damped natural frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$. - [x] **Resonance frequency (true peak):** $\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$. - [x] **Magnification Factor (MF):** $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ — **the most tested formula**. - [x] **Peak MF:** $\dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\zeta} = Q$ for small $\zeta$. - [x] **Quality Factor:** $Q = 1/(2\zeta)$ — high $Q$ = sharp, dangerous resonance. - [x] **Bandwidth:** $\Delta\omega = 2\zeta\omega_n = \omega_n / Q$. - [x] **Transmissibility (TR):** $\sqrt{\dfrac{1+(2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2}}$ — used in isolation analysis. - [x] **Isolation criterion:** Effective only when $r > \sqrt{2}$. - [x] **Anti-resonance** = response minimum, occurs in multi-DOF systems. - [x] **Resonance suppression:** Add damping, detune $\omega_n$, or use TMDs. - [x] **NVH applications:** Engine mounts, suspension tuning, body-panel modes, structural durability. - [x] **Overdamped threshold:** Resonance vanishes for $\zeta \geq 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$. - [x] **Always state units, always box final answers, always give engineering interpretation.**

topics.title: Transmissivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transmissivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Transmissivity** (also widely called **Transmissibility**, denoted $T$ or $TR$) is a fundamental frequency-domain performance metric in Noise, Vibration, and Harshness (NVH) engineering. It is rigorously defined as the **dimensionless ratio of the amplitude of the force (or motion) transmitted through a structural mount or isolator to the amplitude of the force (or motion) applied to the input side of the system**.

Mathematically, for a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) base-excited system:

$$T = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \frac{X_T}{X_0}$$

where $F_T$ is the transmitted force amplitude, $F_0$ is the input force amplitude, $X_T$ is the transmitted displacement amplitude, and $X_0$ is the input (base) displacement amplitude. Transmissivity is a **complex, frequency-dependent transfer function** that quantifies how effectively a suspension, mount, or coupling attenuates (or amplifies) vibration energy between two coupled subsystems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Transmissivity is the *central* metric used to judge the effectiveness of any vibration isolation design. In automotive NVH (the core application domain of PEAUT755), it is used to evaluate engine mounts, sub-frame bushings, and powertrain isolators. The examiner expects students to know the difference between **Force Transmissibility** and **Displacement Transmissibility**, and to be able to plot/interpret the transmissibility curve.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Boat" Intuition

Imagine a heavy boat (mass $m$) tied to a dock with a thick rubber rope (spring $k$) and a hydraulic shock absorber (damper $c$). The water below the boat produces waves (the input vibration $x_0$). 

- If the **rope is very stiff**, every wave is transmitted harshly into the boat — the passengers feel every ripple. Transmissivity is **high** (≈ 1 or even > 1 at resonance).
- If the **rope is soft**, the boat drifts lazily and the passengers feel almost nothing — but the boat bobs up and down with a slow, exaggerated rocking motion at certain wave frequencies. This rocking is **resonance**, and it can be dangerous. Transmissivity shoots **above 1** here.
- The **damper (shock absorber)** acts like a brake on this rocking. It stops the boat from swaying wildly, but at the cost of letting *some* wave energy through.

Transmissivity is essentially the **"pass-through" rating** of the rubber rope-and-damper system: a value of **1** means 100% pass-through (no isolation), a value of **0** means perfect isolation, and a value **greater than 1** means the system is actually *worse* than having no isolation at all (amplification).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why dimensionless?** Because transmissivity is a *ratio*, it has no units. This is extremely useful because it allows engineers to compare isolation systems of completely different physical sizes and stiffnesses on a single, universal graph.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Damping Ratio ($\zeta$):** dimensionless — typically **0.01 to 0.30** for automotive rubber mounts
- **Natural Frequency ($f_n$):** measured in **Hz (Hertz)** — typically **8–25 Hz** for engine mounts
- **Frequency Ratio ($r = \omega / \omega_n$):** dimensionless
- **Transmissivity ($T$):** dimensionless
- **Forcing Frequency ($\omega$):** measured in **rad/s**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Setup — Base-Excited SDOF System

Consider a mass $m$ supported on a spring of stiffness $k$ and a viscous damper with damping coefficient $c$. The base of the spring-damper assembly is subjected to a harmonic displacement $x_0(t) = X_0 \sin(\omega t)$. The mass $m$ responds with a steady-state displacement $x(t) = X_T \sin(\omega t - \phi)$.

The equation of motion is the classical second-order ODE:

$$m\ddot{x} + c(\dot{x} - \dot{x}_0) + k(x - x_0) = 0$$

This can be rewritten in the standard form by defining the **relative displacement** $z = x - x_0$:

$$m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{x}_0 = m\omega^2 X_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Solution

- **Step 1 — Homogeneous solution:** decays to zero in the steady-state (transient part ignored).
- **Step 2 — Particular (steady-state) solution:** assume $z(t) = Z \sin(\omega t - \phi)$. Substitute into the ODE.
- **Step 3 — Solve for the amplitude $Z$** of the relative displacement:

$$Z = \frac{m\omega^2 X_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

- **Step 4 — Transmitted force** equals the spring force plus the damper force:

$$F_T(t) = k z(t) + c \dot{z}(t)$$

- **Step 5 — Take the amplitude** of the transmitted force vector (phasor addition of in-phase spring force and quadrature damper force):

$$F_T = Z \sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$

- **Step 6 — Form the transmissibility ratio** $T = F_T / F_0$ where $F_0$ is the equivalent inertial input force $= m\omega^2 X_0$:

$$T = \frac{\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

- **Step 7 — Normalize** by introducing $\omega_n^2 = k/m$ and $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$, and define $r = \omega/\omega_n$:

$$T(r, \zeta) = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Final Master Equation** for force transmissivity of a SDOF base-excited system is the single most important formula in this module. **Memorize it.**

## 2.3 Critical Frequencies and Limiting Behavior

| **Condition** | **Frequency Ratio $r$** | **Transmissivity $T$** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quasi-static ($\omega \to 0$) | $r \to 0$ | $T \to 1$ | All input passes through (stiff mount behaves rigidly to slow loads) |
| Resonance peak | $r = 1$ | $T_{peak} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ | Maximum amplification; **dangerous zone** for machinery start-up/shut-down |
| **Isolation threshold** | $r = \sqrt{2}$ | $T = 1$ | Boundary — for $r > \sqrt{2}$ the system *isolates* |
| Far-above resonance ($\omega \to \infty$) | $r \to \infty$ | $T \to 0$ | Soft spring decouples mass from base at very high frequencies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Damping Paradox:** Adding damping *reduces* the resonance peak (good for surviving start-up transients), but *increases* $T$ in the high-frequency isolation region. There is an **optimal damping** $\zeta_{opt} \approx 1/\sqrt{2(r^2-1)}$ for a chosen operating $r$ that gives the minimum possible $T$. This is a classic KTU examiner favorite.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Formula / Relation** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | **Master Transmissivity Equation** (SDOF) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $r = \omega / \omega_n$ | Frequency ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural (undamped) frequency | rad/s |
| 4 | $f_n = \omega_n / (2\pi)$ | Natural frequency in Hz | Hz |
| 5 | $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km}) = c / c_c$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ | Critical damping coefficient | N·s/m |
| 7 | $T_{peak} = 1/(2\zeta)$ at $r=1$ | Maximum transmissivity (resonance) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T = 1$ at $r = \sqrt{2}$ | Isolation threshold (independent of $\zeta$) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{r^2}$ | **Displacement Transmissivity** | dimensionless |
| 10 | $TL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(1/T)$ | **Insertion Loss / Isolation Level in dB** | dB |
| 11 | $X_T / X_0 = T \cdot (\omega_n^2 / \omega^2) = T/r^2$ | Relation between force and displacement transmissivity | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\zeta_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2} \cdot r}$ (for max isolation at fixed $r$) | Optimal damping for minimum $T$ | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **automotive NVH**, transmissivity curves dictate the choice of:
- **Engine mounts:** Tuned so engine firing frequency ($r > \sqrt{2}$) falls in the isolation band, while idle frequency sits *just above* resonance.
- **Sub-frame bushings:** Provide 15–25 dB of insertion loss at chassis structural modes (50–200 Hz).
- **Cabin mounts on commercial trucks:** Designed for $r \geq 4$ at engine firing frequency, targeting $T \leq 0.05$ (i.e., 26 dB attenuation).

In **industrial machinery**, the same curves guide the design of:
- Compressor and pump isolators (HVAC systems)
- Precision instrument tables (requiring *negative* transmissivity in dB)
- Building base isolators for seismic protection

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From Newton's Second Law to the Master Equation

**Given:** A mass $m$ mounted on a spring ($k$) and viscous damper ($c$) in parallel, with the base undergoing harmonic motion $x_0(t) = X_0 \sin(\omega t)$.

### Step A — Free Body Diagram & Equation of Motion

The mass experiences:
- Spring force: $-k(x - x_0)$
- Damper force: $-c(\dot{x} - \dot{x}_0)$

Applying Newton's Second Law ($F = ma$):

$$m\ddot{x} = -k(x - x_0) - c(\dot{x} - \dot{x}_0)$$

Rearranging into the standard form:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = c\dot{x}_0 + kx_0$$

### Step B — Convert Base Motion to Equivalent Force

Since $x_0 = X_0 \sin(\omega t)$, we have $\dot{x}_0 = \omega X_0 \cos(\omega t)$. So the RHS becomes:

$$c\dot{x}_0 + kx_0 = (k\sin\omega t + c\omega \cos\omega t)X_0$$

Using the identity $a\sin\theta + b\cos\theta = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}\sin(\theta + \phi)$ with $a = k$, $b = c\omega$, $\phi = \tan^{-1}(c\omega/k)$:

$$c\dot{x}_0 + kx_0 = \sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}\, X_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

### Step C — Assume Steady-State Harmonic Response

Let $x(t) = X_T \sin(\omega t - \psi)$. Substituting into the LHS of the equation of motion:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = [-m\omega^2 X_T \sin(\omega t - \psi) + c\omega X_T \cos(\omega t - \psi) + k X_T \sin(\omega t - \psi)]$$

Combining the sine terms (coeff of $\sin$) and cosine terms (coeff of $\cos$):

$$= (k - m\omega^2)X_T \sin(\omega t - \psi) + c\omega X_T \cos(\omega t - \psi)$$

Convert to a single sinusoid with phase $\psi$:

$$= \sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}\, X_T \sin(\omega t - \psi + \theta)$$

where $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2}\right)$.

### Step D — Equate Amplitudes (Phasor Match)

For the equation to hold for all $t$, the amplitudes and frequencies must match:

$$\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}\, X_0 = \sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}\, X_T$$

Solving for the ratio $X_T / X_0$ (this is the **displacement transmissivity**):

$$\frac{X_T}{X_0} = \frac{\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

### Step E — Transmitted Force Amplitude

The force transmitted to the support is the sum of the spring and damper forces (using phasor addition, since they are 90° out of phase):

$$F_T = \sqrt{(k X_{rel})^2 + (c\omega X_{rel})^2} = X_{rel}\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$

where $X_{rel}$ is the amplitude of the *relative* displacement $z = x - x_0$. From Step C, $X_{rel} = \dfrac{m\omega^2 X_0}{\sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$.

So:

$$F_T = \frac{m\omega^2 X_0 \sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{\sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

### Step F — Form Force Transmissibility

The applied (inertial) input force magnitude is $F_0 = m\omega^2 X_0$. Thus:

$$T = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \frac{\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{\sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

### Step G — Normalize Using Dimensional Ratios

Divide numerator and denominator by $k$, noting $k = m\omega_n^2$ and $c = 2\zeta m \omega_n$:

$$T = \frac{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{c\omega}{k}\right)^2}}{\sqrt{\left(1 - \dfrac{m\omega^2}{k}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{c\omega}{k}\right)^2}}$$

Substitute $c/k = (2\zeta m\omega_n)/(m\omega_n^2) = 2\zeta/\omega_n$ and $m/k = 1/\omega_n^2$:

$$\frac{c\omega}{k} = \frac{2\zeta\omega}{\omega_n} = 2\zeta r$$

$$\frac{m\omega^2}{k} = \frac{\omega^2}{\omega_n^2} = r^2$$

Substituting into $T$:

$$T = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

$$\boxed{\,T(r,\zeta) = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}\,}$$

This is the **master transmissivity equation**. ∎

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Problem

> **Problem:** An engine of mass $250$ kg is mounted on a rubber isolator with stiffness $k = 2.5 \times 10^5$ N/m and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.10$. The engine runs at a steady speed producing a forcing frequency of 25 Hz. Calculate the natural frequency, the frequency ratio, and the force transmissivity. Express the isolation in decibels. Determine the amplitude of the transmitted force if the input force amplitude is 800 N.

### **Solution — Step by Step**

**Step 1 — Natural frequency:**

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2.5 \times 10^5}{250}}$$

$$\frac{k}{m} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^5}{250} = 1000 \; \text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{1000} = 31.623 \; \text{rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{31.623}{2\pi} = 5.033 \; \text{Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Forcing angular frequency:**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 25 = 157.08 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency ratio:**

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{157.08}{31.623} = 4.967$$

**Step 4 — Verify isolation region:** Since $r = 4.967 > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, the mount is in the isolation region. ✓

**Step 5 — Compute transmissivity:**

$$2\zeta r = 2 \times 0.10 \times 4.967 = 0.9934$$

$$(2\zeta r)^2 = 0.987$$

$$1 - r^2 = 1 - 24.67 = -23.67$$

$$(1 - r^2)^2 = 560.1$$

$$(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2 = 560.1 + 0.987 = 561.1$$

$$T = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 0.987}{561.1}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.987}{561.1}} = \sqrt{0.003541} = 0.0595$$

**Step 6 — Insertion Loss in dB:**

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{0.0595}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(16.81)$$

$$TL = 20 \times 1.226 = 24.51 \; \text{dB}$$

**Step 7 — Transmitted force amplitude:**

$$F_T = T \times F_0 = 0.0595 \times 800 = 47.6 \; \text{N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The engine mount attenuates vibration by a factor of ~17, equivalent to 24.5 dB of insertion loss. Only 47.6 N out of every 800 N of excitation reaches the chassis. This is a *good* design.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Damping Effects

| **Damping Ratio $\zeta$** | **Peak $T$ at $r=1$** | **$T$ at $r=2$** | **$T$ at $r=4$** | **$T$ at $r=10$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (undamped) | $\infty$ | 0.333 | 0.0625 | 0.0101 |
| 0.05 | 10.00 | 0.367 | 0.0714 | 0.0112 |
| 0.10 | 5.00 | 0.456 | 0.0980 | 0.0140 |
| 0.20 | 2.50 | 0.625 | 0.176 | 0.0217 |
| 0.40 | 1.25 | 0.866 | 0.331 | 0.0423 |
| 0.707 | 0.707 | 0.841 | 0.408 | 0.0700 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Table:** As $\zeta$ increases, the resonance peak shrinks dramatically (good), but the *isolation* values get worse (bad). This is the fundamental **damping trade-off** in isolator design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Setup — Base-Excited SDOF Mount

The following Mermaid block-diagram captures the functional architecture of a base-excited SDOF system used in transmissivity analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base Excitation x0 = X0 sin omega t] --> B[Spring k and Damper c in Parallel]
    B --> C[Mass m supported on Mount]
    C --> D[Transmitted Force FT to Chassis]
    D --> E[Receiving Structure Cabin or Chassis]
    A -.Inertial Load m omega sup2 X0.-> D
    B -.Relative Displacement z = x - x0.-> C
```

## 4.2 Transmissivity Curve — Functional Architecture

The next diagram maps the *shape* of the transmissibility curve across the frequency spectrum, highlighting the three critical zones.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0[Quasi-Static Zone r less than 1] --> P1[Resonance Peak at r equals 1]
    P1 --> P2[Isolation Threshold at r equals sqrt 2]
    P2 --> P3[High-Frequency Isolation Zone r greater than sqrt 2]
    P1 -- Peak T = 1 over 2 zeta --> P1B[Dangerous Amplification Region]
    P2 -- T equals 1 --> P2B[No Net Isolation]
    P3 -- T decreases as 1 over r squared --> P3B[Effective Vibration Isolation]
```

## 4.3 Damping Trade-off Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Design Problem Choose Damping Ratio] --> Q1{Is Resonance Excited During Operation?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Choose zeta to Limit Peak T to 3 to 5]
    Q1 -- No --> R2[Choose zeta as small as practical less than 0.05]
    R1 --> S[Accept Slight Loss in High-Frequency Isolation]
    R2 --> T[Maximize Isolation in Operating Band]
    S --> U[Validate Using Master Transmissivity Equation]
    T --> U
```

## 4.4 Two-Stage Isolation Block Diagram (Advanced Concept)

For very high isolation requirements (e.g., precision instruments, military vehicles), a two-stage mass-spring system is used.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vibration Source] --> B[Stage 1 Mount k1 c1]
    B --> M1[Intermediate Mass m1]
    M1 --> C[Stage 2 Mount k2 c2]
    C --> M2[Payload Mass m2 to be isolated]
    M2 --> D[Isolated Output]
    B -.Transmissivity T1.-> M1
    C -.Transmissivity T2.-> M2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two stages?** The overall transmissivity of two cascaded systems (when properly tuned) is the *product* $T_{total} = T_1 \times T_2$. Two stages of 15 dB each give 30 dB total — far better than a single stage struggling to give 30 dB on its own.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Transmissivity. Why is the frequency ratio $r = \sqrt{2}$ considered the boundary of the vibration isolation region?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Transmissivity is defined as the ratio of the amplitude of the force (or motion) transmitted through an isolator to the amplitude of the input force (or motion). For a SDOF base-excited system, it is given by:

$$T = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

Setting $T = 1$ in this expression and solving for $r$ gives $r = \sqrt{2}$, **independent of the damping ratio $\zeta$**. For $r < \sqrt{2}$, the system amplifies or merely transmits the input (no isolation), and for $r > \sqrt{2}$, the system provides true attenuation ($T < 1$). Hence $r = \sqrt{2}$ is the isolation threshold. **(3 marks)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain, with the help of a sketch, how the damping ratio affects the peak value of transmissivity at resonance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
At resonance ($r = 1$), the transmissivity attains its maximum value $T_{peak} = 1/(2\zeta)$. Thus, as the damping ratio $\zeta$ increases, the resonance peak decreases. For example, $\zeta = 0.05$ gives $T_{peak} = 10$, while $\zeta = 0.5$ gives $T_{peak} = 1$. However, increased damping *raises* the transmissivity in the isolation region ($r > \sqrt{2}$). The sketch shows a family of curves for different $\zeta$ values, all passing through the point $(r = \sqrt{2}, T = 1)$. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the force transmissibility of a single-degree-of-freedom base-excited system with viscous damping. Explain each step clearly. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A machine of mass 200 kg is supported on an isolator with stiffness $1.0 \times 10^5$ N/m and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.08$. If the machine operates at 1200 rpm, compute (i) the natural frequency, (ii) the frequency ratio, (iii) the force transmissivity, and (iv) the transmitted force amplitude if the exciting force is 600 N. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):** 
Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the exhaustive step-by-step derivation. The examiner expects:
- Equation of motion: 1 mark
- Phasor / steady-state substitution: 2 marks
- Force transmitted through spring-damper: 1 mark
- Normalization to $r$ and $\zeta$: 2 marks
- Final boxed expression: 1 mark

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Natural frequency:**

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{1.0 \times 10^5}{200}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{500}$$

$$f_n = \frac{22.36}{2\pi} = 3.558 \; \text{Hz}$$

**(ii) Forcing frequency and ratio:**

$$f = \frac{1200}{60} = 20 \; \text{Hz}, \quad \omega = 2\pi f = 125.66 \; \text{rad/s}$$

$$r = \frac{f}{f_n} = \frac{20}{3.558} = 5.621$$

**(iii) Force transmissivity:**

$$2\zeta r = 2 \times 0.08 \times 5.621 = 0.8994$$

$$(2\zeta r)^2 = 0.809$$

$$1 - r^2 = 1 - 31.60 = -30.60$$

$$(1-r^2)^2 = 936.4$$

$$(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2 = 937.2$$

$$T = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 0.809}{937.2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.809}{937.2}} = \sqrt{0.001930} = 0.0439$$

**(iv) Transmitted force:**

$$F_T = T \times F_0 = 0.0439 \times 600 = 26.36 \; \text{N}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Mixing up units:** Forcing frequency given in **rpm** must first be converted to **Hz** ($f = \text{rpm}/60$) and then to **rad/s** ($\omega = 2\pi f$). Marks lost if this is skipped.
> 2. **Forgetting to check $r > \sqrt{2}$:** Always verify that the operating point is in the *isolation region*. If $r < \sqrt{2}$, the mount is *not* isolating and the design is faulty.
> 3. **Sign error in $(1 - r^2)^2$:** Students often write $r^2 - 1$ and get confused with signs. **Square it** — the sign disappears.
> 4. **Confusing force and displacement transmissivity:** Force $T$ has the form above; displacement $T_d$ is $T/r^2$. Examiners *will* test this distinction.

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 2]**
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the variation of transmissivity with frequency ratio for different damping ratios. Discuss the design implications. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** An automotive engine mount has a stiffness of $8 \times 10^4$ N/m and damping ratio of 0.15. The engine mass is 180 kg and operates between 800 rpm and 4000 rpm. Determine the transmissivity at the two extreme operating speeds and the corresponding isolation in dB. State whether the mount design is acceptable. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Sketch and Explanation (7 marks):**

The student should:
- Draw the classic family of $T$ vs. $r$ curves for $\zeta = 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.7, 1.0$. **(2 marks)**
- Identify the three zones: quasi-static ($r \to 0$, $T \to 1$), resonance peak ($r = 1$, $T = 1/2\zeta$), isolation region ($r > \sqrt{2}$, $T \to 0$). **(2 marks)**
- Explain damping trade-off: high $\zeta$ reduces resonance peak but worsens isolation. **(2 marks)**
- State design rule: for good isolation, the operating $r$ should be at least 3 to 4. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Natural frequency:**

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{8 \times 10^4}{180}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{444.4} = \frac{21.08}{2\pi} = 3.355 \; \text{Hz}$$

**At 800 rpm:** $f_1 = 800/60 = 13.33$ Hz

$$r_1 = \frac{13.33}{3.355} = 3.973$$

$$2\zeta r_1 = 2 \times 0.15 \times 3.973 = 1.192$$

$$(2\zeta r_1)^2 = 1.421$$

$$(1 - r_1^2)^2 = (1 - 15.79)^2 = (14.79)^2 = 218.7$$

$$T_1 = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 1.421}{218.7 + 1.421}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.421}{220.1}} = \sqrt{0.01100} = 0.1049$$

$$TL_1 = 20\log_{10}(1/0.1049) = 20\log_{10}(9.532) = 19.59 \; \text{dB}$$

**At 4000 rpm:** $f_2 = 4000/60 = 66.67$ Hz

$$r_2 = \frac{66.67}{3.355} = 19.87$$

$$2\zeta r_2 = 2 \times 0.15 \times 19.87 = 5.961$$

$$(2\zeta r_2)^2 = 35.53$$

$$(1 - r_2^2)^2 = (1 - 394.8)^2 = (393.8)^2 = 155{,}083$$

$$T_2 = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 35.53}{155083 + 35.53}} = \sqrt{\frac{36.53}{155119}} = \sqrt{0.0002355} = 0.01535$$

$$TL_2 = 20\log_{10}(1/0.01535) = 20\log_{10}(65.15) = 36.28 \; \text{dB}$$

**Design Assessment:** At low idle (800 rpm), the mount provides ~19.6 dB isolation. At high speed (4000 rpm), it provides ~36.3 dB. Both values exceed the typical automotive target of 15 dB, and the operating frequencies are safely above $\sqrt{2} \cdot f_n = 4.74$ Hz. **The mount design is acceptable.** However, the engineer should check the run-up resonance crossing (typically at 200–400 rpm) for transient amplification.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to Hz:** Very common — students plug $\omega = 800$ rad/s directly.
> 2. **Reporting $T$ without commenting on $r > \sqrt{2}$:** Examiners expect a *design statement*, not just numbers.
> 3. **Not stating units of $TL$** in dB: 1 mark lost if forgotten.
> 4. **Skipping the design assessment:** A 14-mark question always requires an engineering conclusion.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Transmissivity**

- **Definition:** $T$ is the ratio of *transmitted* force (or motion) to *input* force (or motion); dimensionless and frequency-dependent.
- **Master Equation:** $T = \sqrt{[1 + (2\zeta r)^2] / [(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2]}$ — **commit to memory**.
- **Three Zones:** Quasi-static ($T \to 1$), resonance ($T = 1/2\zeta$ at $r = 1$), isolation ($T < 1$ for $r > \sqrt{2}$).
- **Isolation Threshold:** $r = \sqrt{2}$ is the magic frequency ratio — $T = 1$ here **regardless of damping**.
- **Resonance Peak:** $T_{max} = 1/(2\zeta)$ at $r = 1$ — higher damping flattens the peak.
- **Damping Trade-off:** Small $\zeta$ → better high-frequency isolation, but larger resonance peak.
- **Optimal Damping:** $\zeta_{opt} \approx 1/(\sqrt{2}\,r)$ minimizes $T$ at a chosen operating $r$.
- **Insertion Loss:** $TL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(1/T)$ — always quote isolation in dB for KTU answers.
- **Force vs. Displacement Transmissivity:** $T_d = T/r^2$ — they are *not* the same curve.
- **Design Rule of Thumb:** Operate at $r \geq 3$ to 4 for > 20 dB of isolation.
- **Two-Stage Isolation:** Effective $T = T_1 \times T_2$ — used for very high-isolation requirements.
- **Common Engineering Mistake:** Choosing a *very* soft mount to lower $f_n$ without checking that run-up speeds do not dwell at resonance (destroys the equipment).
- **KTU 2024 Expected Skills:** Derive the master equation, plot the family of curves, solve numerical problems on $T$ and $TL_{dB}$, and comment on design acceptability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vibration Isolation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Vibration Isolation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) **Vibration Isolation** is the deliberate engineering practice of introducing a flexible (and often damped) element — typically called an **isolator** or **mount** — between a vibrating source (the *disturbance*) and a sensitive receiver (the *structure to be protected*), so that the magnitude of vibration transmitted across the interface is attenuated to an acceptable level. In formal KTU NVH terminology, isolation is quantified by the **Transmissibility Ratio (TR)**, defined as the steady-state amplitude ratio of the transmitted response to the applied excitation. Mathematically: $$\text{TR} = \frac{X_T}{X_0} = \frac{F_T}{F_0}$$ where $X_0$ / $F_0$ represents the input excitation and $X_T$ / $F_T$ the transmitted amplitude. A transmissibility **less than unity (TR < 1)** is the defining signature of an effective isolator. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Exam Favourite):** *"Vibration isolation is the reduction of vibratory energy transmission from a source to a receiver by the interposition of a resilient or damped coupling such that the transmissibility of the system becomes less than one (TR < 1)."* ## 1.2 Two Canonical Configurations | Configuration | Alternate Name | Role of Isolator | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Active (Source) Isolation** | Base excitation of the *machine* | Soft mount under the disturbing machine so the *ground* sees less force | Compressors, motors, HVAC units in buildings | | **Passive (Receiver) Isolation** | Support excitation of the *delicate equipment* | Soft mount beneath the sensitive instrument so it sees less motion | Precision CNC, electronic cabinets, hospital MRI rooms | > [!NOTE] > Both configurations are mathematically identical at steady-state — they are two viewpoints of the same single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) model. KTU examiners frequently test whether students recognise this symmetry. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition Imagine you are carrying a full cup of hot tea while walking across a wooden floor. The tea sloshes because the floor's vibrations travel *up* your arm and into the cup. - **Without isolation** (rigid cup): every step of the foot is faithfully transmitted — the tea spills. - **With a foam coaster / springy base** (the *isolator*): the coaster *absorbs* and *decouples* the high-frequency wobble of your hand from the fluid. The tea stays calm. The same principle is applied to a car engine: the engine is the "vibrating source," the rubber/silicone engine mount is the "coaster," and the chassis is the "cup." Good mounts mean you cannot feel the engine firing at all. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook (use in exams):** "**Isolate = Insert a soft, low-natural-frequency spring in the path.**" If you remember only one sentence, remember this one. ## 1.4 Why It Matters in KTU NVH Context In a real automobile, **30% of the perceived NVH quality** is dictated by how well the engine, sub-frame, and exhaust are isolated from the body-in-white (BIW). The KTU 2024 scheme places this topic in Module 2 because it bridges the theoretical SDOF mathematics of Module 1 with the practical NVH application modules that follow. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Canonical SDOF Isolation Model The textbook system consists of: - A rigid mass $m$ representing the equipment to be isolated. - A spring of stiffness $k$ representing the resilient mount. - A dashpot of damping coefficient $c$ representing energy dissipation. - A base (or force) that supplies the harmonic excitation. The governing equation of motion for a *force-excited* system is: $$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ For a *base-excited* system, the input is the motion $y(t) = Y_0 \sin(\omega t)$ of the foundation, and the absolute displacement $x$ of the mass satisfies: $$m\ddot{x} + c(\dot{x} - \dot{y}) + k(x - y) = 0$$ ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown 1. **Identify the natural frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$. This is the *resonance* frequency you must design *away* from. 2. **Compute the damping ratio** $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$. Most engine mounts have $\zeta \approx 0.05$ to $0.20$. 3. **Define the frequency ratio** $r = \omega / \omega_n$ (operating speed of the source divided by the mount's natural frequency). 4. **Compute the transmissibility TR(r, ζ)**. 5. **Apply the isolation criterion** TR < 1, which mathematically reduces to $r > \sqrt{2}$ for *any* damping. > [!NOTE] > The frequency $r = \sqrt{2}$ is the **universal crossover point**: at $r = \sqrt{2}$, the transmissibility equals **exactly 1** for *all* values of damping. This is a KTU-favourite numerical question. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use `\vert` instead of `|` for any absolute value or norm inside markdown tables** to avoid syntax breaks. | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Natural circular frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | $k$ = stiffness, $m$ = mass | rad/s | | 2 | Natural frequency (Hz) | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Hz form | Hz | | 3 | Critical (static) deflection | $\delta_{st} = mg/k$ | For mount selection | mm | | 4 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | $c$ = damping coefficient | dimensionless | | 5 | Frequency ratio | $r = \omega / \omega_n$ | Forcing freq. ratio | dimensionless | | 6 | **Transmissibility (damped, general)** | $\text{TR} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | **THE master equation** | dimensionless | | 7 | Transmissibility (undamped) | $\text{TR} = \dfrac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert}$ | Special case $\zeta = 0$ | dimensionless | | 8 | Isolation criterion | $r > \sqrt{2}$ | When TR < 1 | dimensionless | | 9 | Resonance amplification limit | $r \to 1$ → TR $\to \infty$ (undamped) | AVOID | dimensionless | | 10 | Crossover frequency | $f_c = \sqrt{2}\, f_n$ | Lower bound for isolation | Hz | | 11 | Force transmitted to base | $F_T = \text{TR} \times F_0$ | $F_0$ = applied force | N | | 12 | Quality factor (Q) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ | Sharpness of resonance peak | dimensionless | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where the Equations Live | Industry Sector | Use of Isolation Equation | |---|---| | **Automotive** | Selecting engine mount stiffness so idle speed (≈ 25 Hz) is well above $\sqrt{2} f_n$ (typically $f_n \approx 8$–12 Hz). | | **Building Services** | HVAC chiller pads sized so compressor blade-pass frequency lies in the isolation region. | | **Precision Manufacturing** | Optical tables tuned to $f_n \approx 2$–5 Hz to isolate ground vibrations up to 200 Hz. | | **Aerospace** | Avionics racks use wire-rope isolators with high damping at $\zeta \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ to handle launch transients. | ## 2.5 Concept of "Critical Damping Re-attachment" A subtle point KTU examiners love: **damping helps in the isolation region only up to a point.** At very high $r$, the term $(2\zeta r)^2$ in the numerator eventually dominates and TR begins to rise again. The *damping-controlled* long-frequency limit is: $$\text{TR}_{\infty} \approx 2\zeta r$$ Hence, for **very high frequency excitation**, you actually want **low damping** in your isolator. This is why silicone engine mounts are preferred over hydraulic ones for high-frequency isolation. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Transmissibility (Damped, Force-Excited) We start from the SDOF equation of motion: $$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 1 — Assume a particular (steady-state) solution.** Because the forcing is harmonic and the system is linear, the response must also be harmonic at the same frequency $\omega$: $$x_p(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the ODE.** Compute the derivatives: $$\dot{x}_p = \omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$ $$\ddot{x}_p = -\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$ Plugging in: $$-m\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t - \phi) + c\omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi) + kX \sin(\omega t - \phi) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange.** $$(k - m\omega^2)X \sin(\omega t - \phi) + c\omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 4 — Expand the left side using $\sin(A-B)$ identities.** Split the LHS as: $$\text{Let } A = k - m\omega^2, \quad B = c\omega$$ Using the standard harmonic balance identities: $$A \sin\theta + B \cos\theta = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}\,\sin(\theta + \alpha)$$ where $\tan \alpha = B/A$, and $\theta = \omega t - \phi$. **Step 5 — Match amplitudes.** Equating the peak amplitude of the LHS to $F_0$: $$X\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2} = F_0$$ **Step 6 — Solve for the static displacement** $X_{st} = F_0 / k$ **ratio.** $$\frac{X}{X_{st}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ **Step 7 — Compute the transmitted force.** The force $F_T$ transmitted into the base is the sum of the spring force and damper force (both acting through the mount): $$F_T = kX \sin(\omega t - \phi) + c\omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$ The peak of this vector sum is $F_T = X\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$. **Step 8 — Compute the transmissibility ratio.** $$\text{TR} = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \frac{X\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{F_0}$$ Substituting $X = F_0 \big/ \sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}$ and dividing numerator and denominator by $k$: $$\text{TR} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **DERIVATION COMPLETE — This is the KTU board-exam "go-to" expression.** Memorising the form (numerator vs denominator, squared placement) is worth 2–3 easy marks. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Crossover Frequency $r = \sqrt{2}$ Set TR = 1 and solve for $r$ when $\zeta \neq 0$: $$1 = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ Squaring both sides: $$(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2 = 1 + (2\zeta r)^2$$ The damping terms $(2\zeta r)^2$ cancel perfectly: $$(1 - r^2)^2 = 1 \implies 1 - r^2 = \pm 1$$ Rejecting the trivial $r = 0$ solution, we keep $1 - r^2 = -1$ giving $r^2 = 2$, so $r = \sqrt{2}$. > [!NOTE] > This elegant cancellation is **why the crossover is independent of damping** — a favourite KTU viva question. ## 3.3 Resonance Amplification (Un-damped Limit) For $\zeta \to 0$ and $r \to 1$: $$\text{TR}_{r=1} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{0^2 + 0}} \to \infty$$ This is why you must avoid operating near resonance. Practical design rule: keep the steady operating frequency at least **3.5 to 4 times** $f_n$ so that the curve is well into the isolation region with a safety margin. ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical) **Problem:** A 60 kg electric motor is to be isolated using mounts of total stiffness $k = 2.4 \times 10^5$ N/m and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.1$. The motor runs at 1500 RPM. Find (a) the natural frequency, (b) the frequency ratio, (c) the transmissibility, (d) the force transmitted to the base given an out-of-balance force of 200 N. **Solution:** **(a)** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m} = \sqrt{2.4 \times 10^5 / 60}$ $$\omega_n = \sqrt{4000} = 63.25 \text{ rad/s} \implies f_n = 10.07 \text{ Hz}$$ **(b)** Operating angular frequency: $\omega = 2\pi N/60 = 2\pi(1500)/60$ $$\omega = 157.08 \text{ rad/s} \implies f = 25 \text{ Hz}$$ $$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{157.08}{63.25} = 2.48$$ **(c)** Substituting $r = 2.48$ and $\zeta = 0.1$ into the master equation: $$\text{TR} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 2.48)^2}{(1 - 2.48^2)^2 + (2 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 2.48)^2}}$$ $$\text{TR} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 0.2459}{(1 - 6.1504)^2 + 0.2459}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.2459}{26.518 + 0.2459}}$$ $$\text{TR} = \sqrt{\frac{1.2459}{26.764}} = \sqrt{0.04655} = 0.2157$$ **(d)** Transmitted force: $$F_T = \text{TR} \times F_0 = 0.2157 \times 200 = 43.15 \text{ N}$$ > [!TIP] > **Verification of the isolation criterion:** $r = 2.48 > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ ✓ — isolation is achieved; TR < 1 as expected. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Vibration Isolation System ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Source of Vibration
Electric Motor or Engine] -->|Excitation Force F0| B[Resilient Isolator Mount
Spring plus Dashpot] B -->|Transmitted Force FT| C[Receiving Structure
Chassis or Building Foundation] B --> D{Isolation Condition} D -->|r greater than sqrt of 2| E[Effective Isolation
Transmissibility below 1] D -->|r less than sqrt of 2| F[Ampification Zone
Transmissibility above 1] E --> G[Target NVH Performance
Reduced Harshness and Cabin Noise] F --> H[Resonance Hazard
Possible Structural Damage] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — SDOF Model and Parameter Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FORCING [Excitation Domain] I1[Forcing Frequency omega] --> I2[Frequency Ratio r equals omega over omega_n] end subgraph SDOFSYS [Single Degree of Freedom Model] M1[Mass m] --- K1[Stiffness k] K1 --- C1[Damping c] C1 --- BASE[Base or Foundation] end I2 --> SDOFSYS SDOFSYS --> OUT1[Natural Frequency omega_n equals square root of k over m] SDOFSYS --> OUT2[Damping Ratio zeta equals c over 2 square root of km] SDOFSYS --> OUT3[Transmissibility TR function of r and zeta] OUT3 --> DECIDE{Check r against sqrt 2} DECIDE -->|Yes| ISO[Isolator Effective] DECIDE -->|No| AMP[Ampifier] ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Transmissibility Curve (Schematic Description) The transmissibility curve has three canonical zones that the KTU examiner expects you to draw from memory: | Zone | Frequency Range | Behaviour of TR | Design Implication | |---|---|---|---| | **I — Rigid Zone** | $0 < r < \sqrt{2}$ | TR $> 1$; peaks to infinity at $r=1$ | Avoid! Vibration is amplified, not reduced. | | **II — Crossover** | $r = \sqrt{2}$ | TR $= 1$ for **all** $\zeta$ | Boundary of isolation. | | **III — Isolation Zone** | $r > \sqrt{2}$ | TR $< 1$; decreases as $r$ grows | Operating region. Lower damping = better at very high $r$. | > [!NOTE] > When asked to "sketch the transmissibility curve" in a KTU exam, always plot **at least two damping values** (e.g., $\zeta = 0$ and $\zeta = 0.2$) and mark the points $r = 1$, $r = \sqrt{2}$, and the asymptotic high-frequency limit $2\zeta r$. ## 4.4 Physical Schematic (Textual) — Engine Mount Cross Section The block-diagram equivalent of a typical passenger-car engine mount comprises three stacked elements: 1. **Top metal cap** — bonded to the engine block. 2. **Vulcanized rubber core (NBR or silicone)** — provides the spring stiffness $k$ and most of the damping $c$. 3. **Bottom metal cap** — bolted to the sub-frame. The dynamic behaviour is precisely the SDOF mass-spring-damper described in §3, with $m$ = engine mass and $k$ = rubber stiffness (typically tuned to give $f_n \approx 8$–12 Hz, well below engine firing frequencies of 20–25 Hz at idle). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Define the term *transmissibility* in the context of vibration isolation. State the condition under which a vibration isolator becomes effective. **Model Answer:** > Transmissibility (TR) is the ratio of the magnitude of the force (or motion) transmitted through the isolator to that of the applied excitation. Mathematically, > $$\text{TR} = \dfrac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ > An isolator is *effective* when TR < 1, which occurs when the frequency ratio $r = \omega/\omega_n$ exceeds $\sqrt{2}$, i.e., the forcing frequency is more than $\sqrt{2}$ times the natural frequency of the system. **(3 marks)** **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Why is it undesirable to operate a vibrating system at its resonance frequency? What is the transmissibility at resonance for an undamped system? **Model Answer:** > At resonance ($r = 1$), the system stores and amplifies vibratory energy because the forcing frequency matches the natural frequency. The displacement response reaches its theoretical peak, and for an undamped system, > $$\text{TR}_{r=1} \to \infty$$ > This leads to extremely large amplitudes, structural fatigue, and possible failure. In practice, even with small damping the amplitudes are unacceptably high, so machines must be designed to avoid the resonance region (typically $0.8 \le r \le 1.2$). **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (READ BEFORE WRITING):** > 1. **Always write the master TR equation in the form given in §3.1.** Students often write $X/X_0$ *instead of* $F_T/F_0$ and lose 1 mark for "wrong definition." > 2. **Do not forget to verify $r > \sqrt{2}$** before claiming "isolation achieved." Examiners deduct 2 marks if this is missing. > 3. **Show the unit** of natural frequency (rad/s vs. Hz) — losing 0.5 mark for "no unit." > 4. **Don't confuse displacement transmissibility with force transmissibility** at the start. They differ by the factor $r^2$ in the numerators; force TR is what you should use when asked about "transmitted force." ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]` A machine of mass 80 kg is mounted on four identical springs (total stiffness $k = 1.6 \times 10^5$ N/m, equivalent damping ratio $\zeta = 0.15$). During operation, an unbalanced mass produces a harmonic force of amplitude 350 N at 1800 RPM. **(a) [7 marks]** Calculate (i) the natural frequency, (ii) the frequency ratio, and (iii) the transmissibility of the system. **(b) [7 marks]** Determine (i) the force transmitted to the foundation, (ii) the amplitude of vibration of the machine mass, and (iii) comment on the effectiveness of the isolation. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — [7 marks]** **(i) Natural frequency:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.6 \times 10^5}{80}} = \sqrt{2000} = 44.72 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$f_n = \frac{44.72}{2\pi} = 7.12 \text{ Hz} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$ **(ii) Forcing angular frequency:** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1800}{60} = 188.5 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$f = 30 \text{ Hz} \quad \textbf{[1 mark]}$$ $$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{188.5}{44.72} = 4.215 \quad \textbf{[1 mark]}$$ **(iii) Transmissibility:** $$\text{TR} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2 \times 0.15 \times 4.215)^2}{(1 - 4.215^2)^2 + (2 \times 0.15 \times 4.215)^2}}$$ $$\text{TR} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (1.265)^2}{(1 - 17.766)^2 + 1.600}} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 1.600}{281.71 + 1.600}}$$ $$\text{TR} = \sqrt{\frac{2.600}{283.31}} = \sqrt{0.00918} = 0.0958 \quad \textbf{[3 marks]}$$ **Part (b) — [7 marks]** **(i) Transmitted force:** $$F_T = \text{TR} \times F_0 = 0.0958 \times 350 = 33.5 \text{ N} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$ **(ii) Static deflection reference:** $X_{st} = F_0/k = 350 / (1.6 \times 10^5) = 2.1875 \times 10^{-3}$ m Displacement amplitude: $$X = \frac{X_{st}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} = \frac{2.1875 \times 10^{-3}}{\sqrt{281.71 + 1.600}}$$ $$X = \frac{2.1875 \times 10^{-3}}{16.864} = 1.297 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.130 \text{ mm} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$ **(iii) Effectiveness of isolation:** Since $r = 4.215 \gg \sqrt{2} = 1.414$, the isolator is **highly effective**. Only **9.58%** of the excitation force is transmitted to the foundation — a reduction of **~90.4%**. The displacement amplitude is also a tiny 0.13 mm, well within the safe operating envelope. **Design verdict: excellent isolation.** **\[3 marks\]** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, derive the expression for the transmissibility of a damped SDOF vibration isolator subjected to a base excitation $y(t) = Y_0 \sin(\omega t)$. Clearly state the isolation criterion. **(b) [7 marks]** A precision weighing balance of mass 4 kg is to be isolated from floor vibrations of 50 Hz. Design a suitable spring-isolator (find the required stiffness) such that the transmissibility is less than 0.10. Assume negligible damping. What is the static deflection of the mount? **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]** The absolute equation of motion of a base-excited SDOF mass is: $$m\ddot{x} + c(\dot{x} - \dot{y}) + k(x - y) = 0$$ Define relative displacement $z = x - y$: $$m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{y} = m\omega^2 Y_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ This is mathematically equivalent to a force-excited SDOF system with an *effective* force $F_0 = m\omega^2 Y_0$. Assuming a steady-state particular solution $z(t) = Z\sin(\omega t - \phi)$: $$Z = \frac{m\omega^2 Y_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$ The force transmitted to the mass through the mount is the sum of spring and damper forces on $m$: $$F_T = kZ \sin(\omega t - \phi) + c\omega Z \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$ The peak magnitude is: $$F_T = Z\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$ Forming the ratio $F_T / F_0$ and simplifying by dividing numerator and denominator by $k$, then using $r = \omega/\omega_n$ and $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$: $$\boxed{\;\text{TR} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}\;} \quad \textbf{[5 marks]}$$ **Isolation criterion:** TR < 1, achieved when $r > \sqrt{2}$. **\[2 marks\]** **Part (b) — Design Calculation [7 marks]** Given: $m = 4$ kg, $f = 50$ Hz, $\zeta \approx 0$, desired TR < 0.10. For an undamped system: $$\text{TR} = \frac{1}{r^2 - 1} < 0.10 \quad (\text{since } r > 1)$$ $$r^2 - 1 > 10 \implies r^2 > 11 \implies r > 3.317$$ Now $r = f/f_n$, so: $$f_n < \frac{f}{3.317} = \frac{50}{3.317} = 15.07 \text{ Hz}$$ Choosing $f_n = 14$ Hz (a safe engineering choice with margin): $$k = m(2\pi f_n)^2 = 4 \times (2\pi \times 14)^2 = 4 \times 7739.4 = 30{,}958 \text{ N/m}$$ $$\boxed{k \approx 3.10 \times 10^4 \text{ N/m}} \quad \textbf{[4 marks]}$$ Static deflection: $$\delta_{st} = \frac{mg}{k} = \frac{4 \times 9.81}{3.0958 \times 10^4} = 1.268 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 1.27 \text{ mm} \quad \textbf{[3 marks]}$$ > [!NOTE] > A static deflection of ~1.27 mm is small but practical — confirming the design is buildable. If the deflection came out too large (e.g., > 5 mm), the designer would have to increase the mass or accept a smaller safety margin. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this list to your wall before the exam!)** - ☐ **Transmissibility is the single most important number** in vibration isolation — it is the ratio of *transmitted* to *applied* excitation. - ☐ The **master equation** has the numerator $1 + (2\zeta r)^2$ and the denominator $(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2$. - ☐ The **isolation criterion** is **r > √2**; equivalent to operating above **40 Hz** if the mount's natural frequency is **10 Hz**. - ☐ The crossover $r = \sqrt{2}$ is **independent of damping** — derive this fact, it is worth 2 marks. - ☐ **Resonance = r = 1**: never operate here. Use a soft mount (low $f_n$) to push resonance *below* the lowest operating speed. - ☐ **High-frequency isolation** demands **low damping**; **resonance region** demands **higher damping**. Choose damping based on which region matters. - ☐ **Always verify $r > \sqrt{2}$** in numerical answers; it is a one-line check worth 1 mark. - ☐ **Distinguish force TR from displacement TR** — both have the same *form* for *base-excited* systems; for *force-excited* systems, force TR has an extra $r^2$ factor in the numerator. - ☐ **Units, units, units** — natural frequency in rad/s vs Hz; report both for clarity. - ☐ **Static deflection** $\delta_{st} = mg/k$ — required to size the physical mount once the stiffness is chosen. - ☐ **Quality factor** $Q = 1/(2\zeta)$ — used in resonance sharpness and in frequency-response plots. - ☐ Common KTU traps: confusing "crossover" with "resonance"; using force TR when displacement TR is asked; forgetting the $2\zeta r$ factor under the square root. > **End of Module 2 — Vibration Isolation Notes**

topics.title: Vibration Measurement and Control - Sources of Vibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vibration Measurement and Control - Sources of Vibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Noise, Vibration and Harshness (NVH)** engineering as prescribed by the KTU 2024 B.Tech Automobile Engineering syllabus, **Sources of Vibration** are defined as the internal or external excitations that impart oscillatory motion to a mechanical system, causing it to deviate from its equilibrium position. These excitations can be **deterministic** (periodic, transient, or random) or **non-deterministic** and are typically classified based on the mechanism of energy input.

Mathematically, the source excitation force or displacement $f(t)$ is the forcing function in the generic single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) equation of motion:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = f(t)$$

where the **right-hand side (RHS)** term $f(t)$ represents the source excitation that drives the system response. Identifying the source is the **first step** in the classic **Source-Transfer Path-Receiver (S-T-P)** diagnostic framework universally used in automotive NVH.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes that without a clear understanding of the *source* of vibration, any attempt at control (isolation, damping, or absorption) is purely empirical. KTU examiners frequently frame Part A (3-mark) questions around the classification of vibration sources.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **tuning fork** struck against a table. The *table* is the source (it imparts the impulse), the *air column between the prongs* is the transfer path, and your *eardrum* is the receiver. In a car engine, the combustion chamber is the source, the engine mounts and chassis are the transfer path, and the steering wheel or seat is the receiver.

To extend the analogy: if a washing machine drum is loaded with clothes bunched on one side (a common *mass unbalance*), the drum acts as a vibrating source. The cabinet, springs, and dampers transmit (or attenuate) the vibration to the floor, and your feet are the receiver feeling the shake. Just as you would **redistribute the clothes** (fix the source) rather than just placing a thicker rug (treat the receiver), the **root-cause approach** in NVH always starts at the source.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Sources are often **machine-specific**. A four-cylinder engine has dominant sources at the **2nd order of crank rotation** (primary inertia), while a V6 has strong **3rd order torsional** components. Recognizing these orders is the bread-and-butter of NVH diagnostics.

### 1.3 Standard Classification of Sources

Per the KTU 2024 syllabus (aligned with standard texts like *Rao – Mechanical Vibrations* and *Mukherjee – Vehicle Noise and Vibration*), sources are grouped into the following families:

| Source Family | Typical Examples | Frequency Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotating Mass Unbalance** | Imbalance in rotors, fans, tires, drive shafts | $1\times$ shaft RPM |
| **Reciprocating Mass Effects** | Engine pistons, connecting rods, crank throws | $0.5\times, 1\times, 2\times$ RPM |
| **Misalignment** | Coupling offsets between coupled shafts | $1\times, 2\times, 3\times$ shaft RPM |
| **Mechanical Looseness** | Worn bearings, loose bolts, cracked foundations | Sub-multiples & harmonics |
| **Gear Meshing** | Transmission gears, differential, timing gears | Gear mesh frequency (GMF) |
| **Belt & Chain Drives** | Serpentine belts, timing chains | Belt pass frequency |
| **Rolling Element Bearings** | Defects in inner/outer race, ball spin | High frequency (>500 Hz) |
| **Aerodynamic / Hydrodynamic** | Fan blades, pump impellers, tire-road interaction | Blade pass frequency (BPF) |
| **Electrical** | Stator slots, magnetostriction, switching harmonics | Line frequency × slots |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to remember:** The rotational speed $N$ in **rev/min (rpm)** is converted to rad/s by $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$. The **force orders** are integer multiples of $\omega$, e.g., $2\times$ order means $f = \dfrac{2N}{60}$ Hz.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Framework of Vibration Excitation

The flow of analysis for any vibration source follows a structured four-step logic:

- **Step 1 – Kinematic Identification:** Determine whether the source is *rotating* (unbalance, misalignment, gear mesh) or *reciprocating* (piston, valve train). The kinematic nature dictates the forcing frequency.
- **Step 2 – Force or Displacement Excitation:** Classify the source as a *force-type* (independent of system response, e.g., unbalance) or a *kinematic-type* (base excitation, e.g., road input to a suspension).
- **Step 3 – Frequency Mapping:** Compute the dominant excitation frequency(s) using the rotational or operational speed. Compare these against the system's natural frequencies to check for **resonance** ($f_{\text{excitation}} \approx f_n$).
- **Step 4 – Order Tracking:** Express the source in terms of *orders* relative to a reference shaft (typically the engine crankshaft). This is the language of NVH test reports.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Source Mechanism

- **Why does unbalance cause vibration?** Because the center of mass of a rotating body does not coincide with the axis of rotation. The centrifugal force $F = m_e \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ rotates with the body, creating a once-per-revolution (1×) forcing function.
- **Why does misalignment cause 2× vibration?** Angular misalignment creates a rotating force vector that completes **two cycles per shaft revolution** (once for each lobe of the sinusoidal stiffness variation), making 2× the dominant order.
- **Why are gear vibrations at mesh frequency?** When two gears with $N_1$ and $N_2$ teeth rotate at $f_1$ and $f_2$, the **Gear Mesh Frequency (GMF)** is $\text{GMF} = N_1 f_1 = N_2 f_2$. Each tooth engagement is an impact.
- **Why do bearings generate high-frequency vibration?** Defects on the inner race, outer race, or balls produce impacts at very high frequencies (often 500 Hz – 10 kHz). The **Ball Pass Frequency Outer race (BPFO)** is given by:
  $$\text{BPFO} = \frac{N_b}{2} \cdot f_r \left(1 - \frac{d}{D}\cos\phi\right)$$
- **Why are aerodynamic sources periodic?** As fan blades pass a stationary obstruction (strut, heat exchanger tube), the local pressure field is disturbed periodically at the **Blade Pass Frequency (BPF)** = (number of blades) × (shaft frequency).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table of High-Yield Formulas** — These are the **must-know equations** for Module 3 numerical and Part B questions.

| # | Source / Concept | Governing Formula | Variable Definitions & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centrifugal Force from Unbalance | $F = m_e \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ | $m_e$ = eccentric mass (kg), $r$ = eccentricity (m), $\omega$ = angular speed (rad/s) |
| 2 | Static Unbalance Condition | $U_s = m \cdot e$ where $e = \dfrac{\sum m_i r_i}{\sum m_i}$ | $U_s$ in kg·m, $e$ in m |
| 3 | Couple Unbalance (Dynamic) | $U_c = \sum m_i r_i \cdot d_i$ | $d_i$ = axial distance from reference plane (m) |
| 4 | Conversion rpm → rad/s | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | $N$ in rev/min |
| 5 | Order Definition | $\text{Order} = \dfrac{f_{\text{excitation}}}{f_{\text{shaft}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Gear Mesh Frequency (GMF) | $\text{GMF} = N_1 \cdot f_1 = N_2 \cdot f_2$ | $N_i$ = tooth count, $f_i$ = shaft frequency (Hz) |
| 7 | Shaft Frequency from Two Gears | $f_2 = f_1 \cdot \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | — |
| 8 | Blade Pass Frequency (BPF) | $\text{BPF} = n_b \cdot f_{\text{rotor}}$ | $n_b$ = number of blades |
| 9 | BPFO (Bearing Defect) | $\text{BPFO} = \dfrac{N_b}{2} f_r \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{D}\cos\phi\right)$ | $N_b$ = # of balls, $f_r$ = inner race freq., $d$ = ball dia, $D$ = pitch dia, $\phi$ = contact angle |
| 10 | BPFI (Inner Race Defect) | $\text{BPFI} = \dfrac{N_b}{2} f_r \left(1 + \dfrac{d}{D}\cos\phi\right)$ | — |
| 11 | BSF (Ball Spin Frequency) | $\text{BSF} = \dfrac{D}{2d} f_r \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{d}{D}\right)^2 \cos^2\phi\right]$ | — |
| 12 | FTF (Cage / Fundamental Train) | $\text{FTF} = \dfrac{1}{2} f_r \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{D}\cos\phi\right)$ | — |
| 13 | Engine Primary Inertia Force | $F_1 = m_r \, r \, \omega^2 \cos\theta$ | $m_r$ = reciprocating mass, $r$ = crank radius |
| 14 | Engine Secondary Inertia Force | $F_2 = m_r \, r \, \omega^2 \cos 2\theta$ | For single cylinder; secondary is dominant for inline engines |
| 15 | Natural Frequency (SDOF) | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $k$ in N/m, $m$ in kg |
| 16 | Resonance Condition | $f_{\text{excitation}} = f_n$ | Triggers maximum amplitude |
| 17 | Misalignment $2\times$ Criterion | $f_{\text{mis}} = 2 \cdot f_{\text{shaft}}$ | Dominant order for angular misalign |
| 18 | Belt Pass Frequency | $f_{\text{pass}} = f_{\text{pulley}} \cdot n_{\text{tooth}}$ | For synchronous belts |
| 19 | Tire/Wheel Unbalance | $F = (m_{\text{unb}}) \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ | Typically $r \approx$ tire bead seat radius |
| 20 | NVH Source-Path-Receiver | $S(f) \rightarrow T(f) \rightarrow R(f)$ | Frequency-domain decomposition |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Source-Path-Receiver decomposition is the **industry-standard** technique used by every major automotive OEM (Toyota, Tata Motors, Mahindra, Hyundai) and by NVH suppliers like **Brüel & Kjær** and **HEAD Acoustics**. By identifying the **source** mathematically (using formulas above), engineers can:

- **Target Design Fixes** at the root cause (e.g., dynamically balance a driveshaft to **ISO 21940-11 G2.5** grade).
- **Validate Manufacturing Tolerances** on assembly lines using Order Tracking analysis.
- **Predict NVH Performance** in CAE (Computer-Aided Engineering) packages like **LMS Virtual.Lab**, **Actran**, or **HyperWorks** by feeding these same formulas as boundary conditions.
- **Diagnose Field Failures** through FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) and Order Maps of accelerometer data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The units in the centrifugal force formula ($m r \omega^2$) are **kg·m·(rad/s)² = N**. Always carry units; full-mark answers on Part B (14-mark) derivations *require* dimensional consistency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Centrifugal Force from Rotating Unbalance

**Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for the centrifugal force generated by an eccentric mass $m_e$ rotating at radius $r$ and angular velocity $\omega$.

**Given:**
- Eccentric mass: $m_e$ (kg)
- Eccentricity: $r$ (m)
- Angular speed: $\omega$ (rad/s)

**Step 1:** The eccentric mass $m_e$ rotates in a circular path of radius $r$ about the geometric axis. The radial (centripetal) acceleration of this mass is:

$$a_r = \omega^2 r$$

**Step 2:** The centripetal force required to keep $m_e$ in circular motion (by Newton's second law) is:

$$F_c = m_e \cdot a_r = m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$$

**Step 3:** By **Newton's third law**, the rotating mass exerts an equal and opposite reaction force on the shaft. This is the **centrifugal force**, which acts radially outward and rotates with the shaft. In vector form:

$$\vec{F}(t) = m_e \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\omega t + \phi) \\ \sin(\omega t + \phi) \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\phi$ is the initial angular position.

**Step 4:** In Cartesian scalar form (x and y components):

$$F_x(t) = m_e \, r \, \omega^2 \cos(\omega t)$$

$$F_y(t) = m_e \, r \, \omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 5:** **Direction of action:** The force is always directed **away from the rotation axis**, rotating synchronously with the shaft. Hence, in a stationary reference frame, it appears as a **once-per-revolution (1×)** force. This is why unbalance always excites a system at $1\times$ the running speed.

**Dimensional check:**
$$[m_e \, r \, \omega^2] = \text{kg} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{s}^{-2} = \text{N} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Dynamic Unbalance (Static + Couple)

**Problem Statement:** Show that a general rigid rotor can be described by a **static unbalance** and a **couple unbalance**, and derive the resultant force and moment.

**Step 1:** Consider $n$ discrete masses $m_i$ located at radii $r_i$ and axial positions $d_i$ from a reference plane. Define unbalance as the product $U_i = m_i r_i$ in kg·m.

**Step 2:** The **resultant (static) unbalance** $U_s$ is the vector sum of all individual unbalances:

$$\vec{U}_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \vec{r}_i$$

**Step 3:** The **couple (dynamic) unbalance** is the moment of the unbalance vectors about the reference plane:

$$\vec{U}_c = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \vec{r}_i \, d_i$$

**Step 4:** The total centrifugal force on the shaft (from the resultant unbalance) is:

$$\vec{F} = \vec{U}_s \cdot \omega^2$$

**Step 5:** The total moment about the reference plane is:

$$\vec{M} = \vec{U}_c \cdot \omega^2$$

**Step 6:** **Balancing condition:** A rotor is **fully balanced** if and only if:

$$\vec{U}_s = 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad \vec{U}_c = 0$$

In practice, ISO 1940 (now ISO 21940-11) specifies permissible residual unbalance $U_{\text{per}}$ in g·mm, graded by rotor type and running speed.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Gear Mesh Frequency from Two Mating Gears

**Problem Statement:** Two meshing gears with $N_1$ and $N_2$ teeth rotate at $f_1$ and $f_2$ rev/s respectively. Derive the Gear Mesh Frequency (GMF).

**Step 1:** In one complete revolution of gear 1, it presents $N_1$ teeth past the mesh point. Hence, the number of teeth (and hence impacts) per second at the mesh point is $N_1 \cdot f_1$.

**Step 2:** Similarly, gear 2 presents $N_2 \cdot f_2$ teeth per second past the mesh.

**Step 3:** For these two rates to be equal (a physical requirement of meshing):

$$N_1 f_1 = N_2 f_2 = \text{GMF}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for the second shaft frequency:

$$f_2 = f_1 \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

**Step 5:** **Example check:** If gear 1 has 20 teeth spinning at 30 Hz, and gear 2 has 40 teeth, then $f_2 = 30 \times (20/40) = 15$ Hz, and GMF $= 20 \times 30 = 600$ Hz. The second shaft spins at half the speed (gear ratio 2:1) and the mesh impact rate is 600 Hz, a typical value for a transmission input.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Identifying a Vibration Source from Symptoms

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix during diagnostics or for KTU exam questions** that describe symptoms and ask for the source.

| Observed Symptom (FFT Peak Location) | Likely Source | Order / Frequency | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peak at exactly $1\times$ running speed, amplitude rises with $\omega^2$ | **Mass unbalance** (rotor, tire, fan) | $1\times$ | Add balance weights; ISO 21940-11 balance grade |
| Strong $2\times$ running speed, axial direction dominant | **Angular misalignment** | $2\times$ | Laser-align coupled shafts; check soft-foot |
| Strong $1\times$ + harmonic series; both radial & axial | **Parallel misalignment** | $1\times, 2\times, 3\times$ | Re-shim; replace coupling |
| Multiple orders at $0.5\times, 1\times, 2\times$ | **Reciprocating forces** (engine) | Engine order | Balance crank, tune mounts |
| Peak at very high frequency (kHz) modulated by running speed | **Bearing defect** | BPFO, BPFI, BSF | Replace bearing; check lubrication |
| Peak exactly at $N_1 f_1 = N_2 f_2$ with sidebands at $f_1$ and $f_2$ | **Gear mesh** | GMF $\pm$ sidebands | Check gear quality, backlash, alignment |
| Peak at $n_b \cdot f_{\text{rotor}}$ | **Aerodynamic** (BPF) | BPF | Trim blade; check for obstruction |
| Peak at line frequency $\pm$ slip freq. | **Electrical** (motor) | 50/60 Hz harmonics | Check rotor bars, air gap |

### 3.5 Worked Example: Tire Unbalance Force

**Problem:** A passenger car tire has an unbalance of 25 g at a radius of 0.25 m. The wheel rotates at 900 rpm. Compute the centrifugal force and verify its $1\times$ order nature.

**Given:** $m_e = 0.025$ kg, $r = 0.25$ m, $N = 900$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Convert rpm to rad/s:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 900}{60} = 30\pi \approx 94.248 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute centrifugal force:**

$$F = m_e \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 = 0.025 \times 0.25 \times (94.248)^2$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 55.5 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 3 — Order verification:**
- Wheel frequency: $f_{\text{wheel}} = 900 / 60 = 15$ Hz
- Excitation frequency = $1 \times 15 = 15$ Hz $\rightarrow$ **1× order confirmed**.

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** A mere 25 g of unbalance produces **5.66 kgf** of force — sufficient to cause steering wheel shimmy at highway speeds. This is why wheel balancing to **≤ 5 g** per wheel is mandatory in service.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Source-Transfer-Receiver (S-T-P) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NVH Source] --> B[Mechanical Transfer Path]
    B --> C[Receiver Response]
    A -->|Force or displacement| A1[F unbalance, p misalign, gear mesh]
    B --> B1[Mounts, structure, air column]
    C --> C1[Driver, seat, steering wheel, mic]

    subgraph Source_Classification
        S1[Rotating Unbalance]
        S2[Reciprocating Engine]
        S3[Gear and Bearing]
        S4[Aerodynamic BPF]
    end

    S1 --> A
    S2 --> A
    S3 --> A
    S4 --> A

    subgraph Control_Strategies
        K1[Source Reduction - Balance, Tolerances]
        K2[Path Blocking - Isolators, Dampers]
        K3[Receiver Treatment - Absorbers, Active Noise Control]
    end

    A -.fix.- K1
    B -.fix.- K2
    C -.fix.- K3
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Vibration Sources

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Vibration Sources in Machines]
    R --> R1[Rotating Mass Effects]
    R --> R2[Reciprocating Mass Effects]
    R --> R3[Gear and Belt Drives]
    R --> R4[Bearing Defects]
    R --> R5[Fluid and Aerodynamic]
    R --> R6[Electrical Sources]

    R1 --> R1A[Static Unbalance]
    R1 --> R1B[Couple Unbalance]
    R1 --> R1C[Dynamic Unbalance Combined]
    R1 --> R1D[Shaft Bow or Crack]

    R2 --> R2A[Engine Primary 1X]
    R2 --> R2B[Engine Secondary 2X]
    R2 --> R2C[Torsional Vibration of Crank]

    R3 --> R3A[Gear Mesh GMF]
    R3 --> R3B[Gear Sidebands]
    R3 --> R3C[Belt Pass Frequency]
    R3 --> R3D[Chain Drive Whipping]

    R4 --> R4A[BPFO Outer Race]
    R4 --> R4B[BPFI Inner Race]
    R4 --> R4C[BSF Ball Spin]
    R4 --> R4D[FTF Cage Frequency]

    R5 --> R5A[Blade Pass BPF]
    R5 --> R5B[Cavitation in Pumps]
    R5 --> R5C[Tire Road Excitation]

    R6 --> R6A[Stator Slot Harmonics]
    R6 --> R6B[Supply Frequency 50 60 Hz]
    R6 --> R6C[Rotor Bar Pass]
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow: From Source Excitation to Measured FFT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Excitation Force F of t] --> S2[System Transfer Function H omega]
    S2 --> S3[Response X omega = H x F]
    S3 --> S4[Accelerometer Transduces]
    S4 --> S5[ADC Sampling]
    S5 --> S6[FFT Algorithm]
    S6 --> S7[Order Map and Spectrum]

    S7 --> S8{Peaks Identified}
    S8 --> S9[1X Peak = Unbalance]
    S8 --> S10[2X Peak = Misalignment]
    S8 --> S11[GMF Peak = Gear]
    S8 --> S12[kHz Cluster = Bearing]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams in KTU exams:** Whenever a question shows a flow diagram, examiners expect you to **label each block** and **identify the controlling frequency/order** in the answer. Mermaid block diagrams are an excellent study tool for this.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define the term "Source of Vibration" in the context of NVH engineering. List any four common sources of vibration in an automobile with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **source of vibration** is any internal or external excitation that imparts oscillatory motion to a mechanical system, characterized by its forcing function $f(t)$ in the equation of motion.
- **Mass unbalance** — example: unbalanced tire or driveshaft.
