- **Misalignment** — example: misaligned propeller shaft coupling.
- **Gear mesh** — example: transmission gear pair engagement.
- **Engine reciprocating forces** — example: piston inertia in an inline-4 engine.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for four correctly listed sources with examples.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*State the formula for centrifugal force due to rotating unbalance. Explain why unbalance produces a 1× (once-per-revolution) excitation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Formula:
$$F = m_e \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$
**Reason for 1× excitation:** The eccentric mass $m_e$ at radius $r$ traces a circular path once per shaft revolution, so the centrifugal force vector also completes one full rotation per revolution. The force vector's magnitude is constant, but its **direction** rotates at the shaft speed, producing a **1× order** forcing function in a stationary reference frame.

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for 1× reasoning, 1 mark for the directional nature of the rotating force.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module typically has *one full 14-mark question* with internal choice (a) or (b). Below are **two complete alternative questions** for practice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a)** A rotating shaft carries three discrete masses in three different planes perpendicular to the shaft axis. The masses and their locations are:
- $m_1 = 4$ kg at radius $r_1 = 100$ mm and axial position $d_1 = 0$ mm
- $m_2 = 3$ kg at radius $r_2 = 150$ mm and axial position $d_2 = 200$ mm
- $m_3 = 2$ kg at radius $r_3 = 200$ mm and axial position $d_3 = 400$ mm

The angular positions of $m_1, m_2, m_3$ are $\theta_1 = 0°, \theta_2 = 90°, \theta_3 = 180°$ respectively. The shaft runs at 3000 rpm. Determine:
1. The resultant (static) unbalance.
2. The couple (dynamic) unbalance.
3. The centrifugal force.
4. The balancing mass and its angular position **if placed in plane $L$ at $d_L = 400$ mm and radius $r_L = 150$ mm**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With neat sketches, classify the **various sources of vibration** in rotating and reciprocating machinery. For each, mention the **typical frequency/order** observed. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Resolve each unbalance vector into x and y components:**

$$U_{1x} = 4 \times 0.1 \times \cos 0° = 0.40 \text{ kg·m}$$
$$U_{1y} = 4 \times 0.1 \times \sin 0° = 0.00 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$U_{2x} = 3 \times 0.15 \times \cos 90° = 0.00 \text{ kg·m}$$
$$U_{2y} = 3 \times 0.15 \times \sin 90° = 0.45 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$U_{3x} = 2 \times 0.2 \times \cos 180° = -0.40 \text{ kg·m}$$
$$U_{3y} = 2 \times 0.2 \times \sin 180° = 0.00 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 2 — Resultant (static) unbalance:**

$$U_{sx} = 0.40 + 0.00 - 0.40 = 0.00 \text{ kg·m}$$
$$U_{sy} = 0.00 + 0.45 + 0.00 = 0.45 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$U_s = \sqrt{0^2 + 0.45^2} = 0.45 \text{ kg·m}$$
$$\theta_s = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.45}{0}\right) = 90°$$

**Step 3 — Couple (dynamic) unbalance $U_c = \sum m_i r_i d_i$:**

$$U_{cx} = (0.40)(0) + (0)(0.200) + (-0.40)(0.400) = -0.16 \text{ kg·m}^2$$
$$U_{cy} = (0)(0) + (0.45)(0.200) + (0)(0.400) = +0.09 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

$$U_c = \sqrt{(-0.16)^2 + (0.09)^2} = \sqrt{0.0256 + 0.0081} = \sqrt{0.0337} \approx 0.1836 \text{ kg·m}^2$$
$$\theta_c = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.09}{-0.16}\right) = 180° - 29.36° = 150.64°$$

**Step 4 — Centrifugal force:** $\omega = 2\pi \times 3000 / 60 = 100\pi \approx 314.16$ rad/s

$$F = U_s \cdot \omega^2 = 0.45 \times (314.16)^2 \approx 0.45 \times 98696 \approx 44{,}413 \text{ N} \approx 44.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Balancing mass in plane L at $d_L = 400$ mm, $r_L = 150$ mm:**

For zero static unbalance after adding mass $m_b$ at $\theta_b$:
$$U_{sx, \text{new}} = 0 + m_b \cdot r_L \cos\theta_b = 0$$
$$U_{sy, \text{new}} = 0.45 + m_b \cdot r_L \sin\theta_b = 0$$

From the first equation, $m_b \cos\theta_b = 0 \Rightarrow \theta_b = 90°$ or $270°$. Substituting $\theta_b = 270°$ (to make the y-component negative and cancel +0.45):
$$m_b \cdot 0.15 = 0.45 \Rightarrow m_b = 3.0 \text{ kg at } \theta_b = 270°$$

*(4 marks for steps 1–3, 2 marks for force, 1 mark for balancing mass — total 7 marks for part a.)*

**(b) Model Answer (7 marks):**
Classification of sources (use a table or labeled sketch):
- **Rotating unbalance** (1× order) — driveshaft, fan, tire.
- **Reciprocating** (0.5×, 1×, 2× engine orders) — piston/con-rod.
- **Misalignment** (1×, 2×, 3×) — coupling offset.
- **Looseness** (sub-multiples, harmonics).
- **Gear mesh** (GMF) — transmission.
- **Bearings** (BPFO, BPFI, BSF, FTF) — high frequency.
- **Aerodynamic** (BPF) — fans, blowers.
- **Electrical** (50/60 Hz and harmonics) — motors.
*(Distribute marks: 1 mark for each correctly described source with order, 7 sources → 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2025, Model]**

**(a)** Two meshing gears have $N_1 = 25$ teeth and $N_2 = 60$ teeth. The pinion (gear 1) runs at 1450 rpm. Determine:
1. The frequency of the pinion and the gear in Hz.
2. The Gear Mesh Frequency (GMF).
3. The order of the GMF with respect to the pinion and the gear.
4. If a defect on one tooth of the pinion produces a 1× sideband of the GMF, what is its frequency? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **Source-Transfer-Receiver (S-T-P) framework** of NVH. With a suitable block diagram, explain how each domain is analyzed and controlled. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Frequencies:**
$$f_1 = \frac{1450}{60} \approx 24.17 \text{ Hz}$$
$$f_2 = f_1 \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_2} = 24.17 \times \frac{25}{60} \approx 10.07 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — GMF:**
$$\text{GMF} = N_1 \cdot f_1 = 25 \times 24.17 \approx 604.17 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Orders:**
$$\text{Order}_{\text{vs pinion}} = \frac{604.17}{24.17} = 25 \text{ (equals tooth count)} \quad \checkmark$$
$$\text{Order}_{\text{vs gear}} = \frac{604.17}{10.07} = 60 \text{ (equals tooth count)} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Sideband frequency (assuming 1× pinion = 24.17 Hz sideband separation):**
$$f_{\text{sideband}} = \text{GMF} \pm f_1 = 604.17 \pm 24.17$$
$$\boxed{f_{\text{sideband}} = 579.99 \text{ Hz or } 628.34 \text{ Hz}}$$

*(Award 2, 2, 1.5, 1.5 marks respectively — total 7.)*

**Model Solution (b):**
The **S-T-P framework** decomposes any NVH issue into three sequential domains:
- **Source (S):** Where vibration/noise is generated. Examples: unbalance, gear mesh, engine firing. Controlled by *reducing* the excitation (e.g., balancing, tighter tolerances, helical gears).
- **Transfer Path (T):** Medium through which vibration propagates. Examples: mounts, chassis structure, air column. Controlled by *isolation* or *damping* (rubber mounts, tuned mass dampers).
- **Receiver (R):** The point of perception. Examples: driver seat, steering wheel, microphone. Controlled by *treatment* (active noise control, sound deadening).

A block diagram with three blocks connected in series, each with feedback control loops, is expected. The **best practice** is to fix the source first; if impossible, address the path; only as a last resort treat the receiver.

*(Award 2 marks per domain for description, 1 mark for the diagram — total 7.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s (factor of $2\pi/60$) costs **1–2 marks** in any centrifugal force or unbalance derivation.
> 2. **Order Confusion:** Students often mix up "order" and "frequency." An order is **dimensionless**; frequency has **Hz**. Always write the order relative to a *specific* reference shaft.
> 3. **BPFO vs BPFI mix-up:** BPFO is **outer race** (factor $1 - d\cos\phi/D$); BPFI is **inner race** (factor $1 + d\cos\phi/D$). Mnemonic: **O**uter = **O**pposite sign in formula.
> 4. **Incomplete Source Classification:** Listing only "unbalance" and "misalignment" will not fetch full marks. Cover at least **5 distinct families** for a 7-mark question.
> 5. **Skipping Diagrams:** Any 7-mark question that asks for "with neat sketch" *requires* a labeled diagram; missing it deducts at least 1–2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Source definition** is the **forcing function $f(t)$** in the equation of motion; **not** the response.
- **Unbalance = 1× order**; magnitude scales with $\omega^2$ (so doubling speed → 4× force).
- **Misalignment = 2× dominant** (angular) or 1×, 2×, 3× (parallel).
- **Gear Mesh Frequency (GMF) = $N_1 f_1 = N_2 f_2$**; GMF equals the *number of teeth × shaft frequency*.
- **Bearing defect frequencies** are at very high frequencies (kHz range); use BPFO, BPFI, BSF, FTF.
- **BPF = $n_b \cdot f_{\text{rotor}}$** for aerodynamic sources.
- **Engine primary = 1× crank**, **engine secondary = 2× crank**.
- **Resonance** occurs when $f_{\text{excitation}} = f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ — **always check this first** when vibration is severe.
- **Source-Transfer-Receiver (S-T-P)** is the universal NVH diagnostic framework.
- **Balancing** requires both $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ (static) **and** $\sum m_i r_i d_i = 0$ (couple) for a complete solution.
- **Order** is dimensionless and **shaft-relative**; **frequency** is in Hz and absolute.
- **Rotor balancing grades** per **ISO 21940-11** — e.g., G2.5 for fans, G6.3 for pumps, G16 for heavy machinery.
- **Tire unbalance** as small as 25 g can produce **~55 N** at highway speeds — always specify a balance grade.
- **Defensive tip for KTU:** Always state **assumptions** (e.g., "assume small angular misalignment, neglect gravity") at the start of any 14-mark derivation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vibration Pickups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vibration Pickups

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vibration Pickup (Vibration Transducer):** A device that converts mechanical vibration (displacement, velocity, or acceleration of a vibrating surface) into a proportional electrical signal (voltage or charge) suitable for measurement, recording, display, and spectral analysis in Noise, Vibration and Harshness (NVH) engineering.

A **vibration pickup** (also called a **vibration sensor**, **transducer**, or **probe**) is the essential link between the physical vibrating system (the structure under test) and the electronic signal-conditioning/analysis chain (charge amplifier, DAQ, FFT analyzer, or computer). The output electrical quantity is directly proportional to one of the three primary kinematic descriptors of vibration: **displacement (x)**, **velocity (v)**, or **acceleration (a)**.

In automotive NVH applications, vibration pickups are used on:
- Engine blocks, cylinder heads, and crankcase
- Transmission housings
- Vehicle body panels (floor pan, firewall, dashboard)
- Steering column and seat rails
- Suspension components (springs, dampers, bushings)
- Brake calipers and rotors (squeal/judder studies)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tiny Inertia Inside the Box"

Imagine a small box that you glue to a vibrating wall. Inside the box is a tiny mass suspended on a soft spring, with a pen attached to it. When the wall (and the box) shakes up and down, **the mass inside lags behind because of its inertia**, just like a passenger on a bumpy bus lurches backward when the bus accelerates forward. By measuring how much the mass has moved *relative to the box*, we can figure out how hard the box itself is being shaken.

This is exactly the principle of a **seismic-mass vibration pickup**. The "pen" is replaced by an electrical element — a piezoelectric crystal, a strain gauge, or a coil — that turns the relative motion into a voltage or charge signal. The faster the wall shakes, the larger the relative displacement of the inside mass, and the bigger the electrical signal we read.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** No matter the type, every vibration pickup obeys a basic rule — its output is a faithful copy of the *mechanical input* only when the input frequency is **well above** the natural frequency of the internal mass-spring system. This is the cardinal "operating range" rule that every KTU examiner expects you to remember.

### 1.3 Three Fundamental Output Classes of Vibration Pickups

| Class | Output Quantity | Common Device | Typical Engineering Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Displacement Pickup | Proportional to **x** (m) | LVDT, Eddy-current probe, Laser vibrometer | µm, mm |
| Velocity Pickup | Proportional to **v** (m/s) | Moving-coil geophone | mm/s |
| Acceleration Pickup (Accelerometer) | Proportional to **a** (m/s²) | Piezoelectric, Piezoresistive, MEMS, Capacitive | g, m/s² |

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (bold = physically measurable)

- **Sensitivity** (mV/g, pC/g, V/(m/s))
- **Frequency Range** (Hz)
- **Resonance Frequency** (kHz)
- **Dynamic Range** (linear operating amplitude band)
- **Transverse Sensitivity** (% of axial, ideally < 5%)
- **Mass Loading Effect** (g, should be < 1/10 of test mass)
- **Operating Temperature Range** (°C)
- **Mounting Type** (stud, adhesive, wax, magnetic, hand-held probe)

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 3 of PEAUT755, KTU expects students to clearly distinguish between **contact-type** (piezoelectric, piezoresistive) and **non-contact-type** (eddy current, laser Doppler vibrometer) pickups, and to derive the **frequency-response equations** of a seismic mass-spring-damper system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Universal Seismic Mass-Spring-Damper Model

Nearly every contact-type vibration pickup (whether displacement, velocity, or acceleration type) can be modelled by the same second-order mechanical system:

**Mechanical elements:**
- A **seismic mass (m)** suspended by a **spring of stiffness (k)**
- A **damper (c)** that provides viscous energy dissipation
- A **housing** that is rigidly attached to the vibrating surface

The **relative displacement** $z(t) = x(t) - y(t)$ between the seismic mass (absolute displacement $x$) and the housing (absolute displacement $y$) is the quantity that the internal transducer element converts to an electrical signal.

### 2.2 Derivation of Governing Equation of Motion

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram of the Seismic Mass:**
The forces acting on the mass are: spring force ($-k \cdot z$), damper force ($-c \cdot \dot{z}$), and inertial reaction ($-m \cdot \ddot{x}$). Newton's second law gives:

$$m \ddot{x} + c \dot{z} + k z = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $x = z + y$:**

$$m (\ddot{z} + \ddot{y}) + c \dot{z} + k z = 0$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange with input acceleration on the right:**

$$m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{y}$$

This is the canonical equation that every KTU question on vibration pickups starts from. The right-hand side is a forcing function equal to the **negative of the inertial force** of the input base motion.

**Step 4 — Define Natural Frequency and Damping Ratio:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{and} \quad \zeta = \frac{c}{2 \sqrt{k m}} = \frac{c}{2 m \omega_n}$$

Dividing the equation of motion by $m$:

$$\ddot{z} + 2 \zeta \omega_n \dot{z} + \omega_n^2 z = -\ddot{y}$$

This is identical in form to a forced second-order system with input $-\ddot{y}(t)$.

**Step 5 — Frequency Response for Sinusoidal Base Excitation $y = Y \sin(\omega t)$:**

Assume steady-state solution $z(t) = Z \sin(\omega t - \phi)$. The amplitude ratio $\frac{Z}{Y}$ is:

$$\left\vert \frac{Z}{Y} \right\vert = \frac{\omega^2}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2 \zeta \omega_n \omega)^2}}$$

### 2.3 The Three Operating Regimes of a Seismic Pickup

The beauty of this single equation is that it reveals the operating principle of *three different pickups* depending on which frequency band we use:

| Regime | Frequency Condition | What is Measured | Type of Pickup |
|---|---|---|---|
| Displacement mode | $\omega \gg \omega_n$ | $Z \approx Y$ (relative motion = base motion) | Accelerometer operating in its flat band |
| Velocity mode | $\omega \approx \omega_n$ | $Z \approx \frac{\omega Y}{2 \zeta \omega_n}$ | Velocity geophone (tuned near resonance) |
| Displacement-output pickup | $\omega \ll \omega_n$ | $Z \approx \frac{\omega^2}{\omega_n^2} Y \ll Y$ | Relative-displacement pickup (seismometer) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** A **piezoelectric accelerometer** is designed so that $\omega_n$ is **very high** (typically 20–50 kHz for general-purpose units, > 100 kHz for miniature units). When measuring vibration below ~5 kHz, the system always operates in $\omega \ll \omega_n$ region, where $Z$ is **directly proportional to the input acceleration** $a = -\omega^2 Y \sin(\omega t)$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~80% of the marks in any vibration-pickup question.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency of seismic element | rad/s |
| 2 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency in Hz | Hz |
| 3 | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2 \sqrt{k m}}$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $Z/Y = \dfrac{\omega^2}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2 \zeta \omega_n \omega)^2}}$ | Steady-state amplitude ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $a(t) = -\omega^2 y(t)$ | Kinematic relation for sinusoidal motion | m/s² |
| 6 | $Z \approx a / \omega_n^2$ (when $\omega \ll \omega_n$) | Accelerometer output ∝ input acceleration | m |
| 7 | $Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot k \cdot Z$ | Charge output of piezoelectric crystal (d = charge coefficient) | Coulombs (C) |
| 8 | $V_{out} = Q / C_a$ | Voltage output (where $C_a$ is accelerometer + cable capacitance) | Volts |
| 9 | $S_v = V_{out}/a$ | Voltage sensitivity of accelerometer | V·s²/m or mV/g |
| 10 | $S_q = Q / a$ | Charge sensitivity | pC/g |
| 11 | $\Delta f_{max} = 0.2 \, f_n$ | Recommended upper measurement frequency (20% of $f_n$ for < 5% error) | Hz |
| 12 | $M_{loading} < M_{structure}/10$ | Mass-loading rule (pickup mass < 1/10 of test structure's effective modal mass) | kg |
| 13 | $V = B \ell v$ | EMF in a moving-coil velocity pickup (B = flux density, $\ell$ = wire length, v = velocity) | Volts |
| 14 | $f_{upper,useful} = 0.4 \, f_n$ (when $\zeta = 0.7$) | Practical upper frequency for an accelerometer with optimal damping | Hz |

### 2.5 Piezoelectric Accelerometer — Working Principle

The piezoelectric accelerometer is the workhorse of NVH measurement. It contains a **piezoelectric crystal** (typically Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT) sandwiched between the seismic mass and the base. When the housing vibrates, the inertia of the mass squeezes the crystal, generating an electric charge $Q$ proportional to the applied force $F$:

$$Q = d \cdot F$$

For a sinusoidal excitation, $F = m \cdot a$ where $a$ is the input acceleration, so:

$$Q = d \cdot m \cdot a \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_q = d \cdot m \;\; (\text{pC/g})$$

This charge is converted to a usable voltage by a **charge amplifier** (a high-gain op-amp with a feedback capacitor $C_f$):

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d \cdot m \cdot a}{C_f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why charge amplifiers are preferred over voltage amplifiers:** Cable capacitance between the accelerometer and the readout instrument would otherwise load the high-impedance crystal, distort the low-frequency response, and inject noise. The charge amplifier's virtual-ground input makes the system **immune to cable capacitance** — a key KTU viva question.

### 2.6 Engineering Applications — Where Each Pickup is Used

- **Engine NVH (combustion, piston slap, valvetrain):** High-temperature piezoelectric accelerometers (shear-mode design, > 150 °C rated)
- **Body and chassis (road-induced):** Miniature (1–5 g) accelerometers with adhesive mounting
- **Brake squeal (1–16 kHz):** High-frequency resonant piezoelectric units
- **Modal analysis of structures:** Very light (< 1 g) accelerometers to minimize mass loading
- **Rotating machinery (shafts, bearings):** Eddy-current proximity probes (non-contact)
- **Acoustic holography / speaker diaphragm motion:** Laser Doppler vibrometer (non-contact, μm-resolution)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Frequency Response of a Seismic Pickup

**Given:**
- Seismic mass $m$, spring stiffness $k$, damping coefficient $c$
- Base (housing) motion $y(t) = Y \sin(\omega t)$
- Absolute mass motion: $x(t)$
- Relative displacement: $z(t) = x(t) - y(t)$

**Step 1 — Equation of Motion from Newton's Law:**

The mass is acted upon by the spring force, damper force, and inertia. Summing forces in the direction of motion:

$$m \ddot{x} = -k (x - y) - c (\dot{x} - \dot{y})$$

This is the same equation written with clear physical meaning: net force on mass = sum of restoring (spring) and dissipative (damper) reactions.

**Step 2 — Express in terms of relative displacement $z = x - y$:**

Differentiating twice, $\ddot{x} = \ddot{z} + \ddot{y}$, $\dot{x} = \dot{z} + \dot{y}$. Substituting:

$$m (\ddot{z} + \ddot{y}) + c \dot{z} + k z = 0$$

**Step 3 — Collect $z$ terms on left, base acceleration on right:**

$$m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{y}$$

This is the standard form where the **input** is the base acceleration $\ddot{y}(t)$, and the **output** is the relative displacement $z(t)$.

**Step 4 — Divide through by $m$ and introduce $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$:**

$$\ddot{z} + 2 \zeta \omega_n \dot{z} + \omega_n^2 z = -\ddot{y}$$

where
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{c}{2 m \omega_n}$$

**Step 5 — Assume steady-state sinusoidal response:**

For input $y(t) = Y \sin(\omega t)$, $\ddot{y} = -\omega^2 Y \sin(\omega t)$. The steady-state solution is $z(t) = Z \sin(\omega t - \phi)$. Substituting and matching coefficients of $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$:

$$- \omega^2 Z \sin(\omega t - \phi) + 2 \zeta \omega_n \omega Z \cos(\omega t - \phi) + \omega_n^2 Z \sin(\omega t - \phi) = \omega^2 Y \sin(\omega t)$$

Collecting the $\sin$ and $\cos$ terms separately and squaring and adding:

$$Z^2 [(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2 \zeta \omega_n \omega)^2] = \omega^4 Y^2$$

**Step 6 — Final Amplitude Ratio:**

$$\left\vert \frac{Z}{Y} \right\vert = \frac{\omega^2}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2 \zeta \omega_n \omega)^2}}$$

This is the **transfer function magnitude** of a seismic pickup measuring acceleration. Multiply numerator and denominator by $1/\omega_n^2$:

$$\left\vert \frac{Z}{Y} \right\vert = \frac{(\omega/\omega_n)^2}{\sqrt{[1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2]^2 + [2 \zeta (\omega/\omega_n)]^2}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **The asymptotic limit that KTU loves to test:** When $\omega \ll \omega_n$, the denominator $\to 1$, so $Z \to (\omega/\omega_n)^2 \cdot Y$. Since acceleration amplitude is $a = \omega^2 Y$, we have $Z \to a / \omega_n^2$. This proves that in the low-frequency (well below resonance) regime, the **relative displacement of the mass is directly proportional to the input acceleration** — the working principle of every piezoelectric accelerometer.

### 3.2 Derivation: Sensitivity of a Piezoelectric Accelerometer

**Step 1 — Inertial Force on the Crystal:**

When the housing accelerates at $a$, the seismic mass $m$ experiences an inertial force $F = m \cdot a$. This force is transmitted through the piezoelectric crystal.

**Step 2 — Piezoelectric Charge Generation:**

$$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a$$

where $d$ is the **charge-mode piezoelectric coefficient** (e.g., ~250 pC/N for PZT-5A).

**Step 3 — Charge Sensitivity:**

$$S_q = \frac{Q}{a} = d \cdot m \;\; [\text{pC/g}]$$

**Step 4 — Conversion to Voltage through Charge Amplifier:**

The charge amplifier has feedback capacitor $C_f$ (typically 1 nF). Its output:

$$V_{out} = \frac{Q}{C_f} = \frac{d \cdot m \cdot a}{C_f}$$

**Step 5 — Voltage Sensitivity:**

$$S_v = \frac{V_{out}}{a} = \frac{d \cdot m}{C_f} \;\; [\text{V/g}]$$

**Numerical Example (typical industrial accelerometer):**
- $m = 5 \text{ g}$ (seismic mass)
- $d = 250 \text{ pC/N}$
- $C_f = 1 \text{ nF}$

$$S_q = 250 \times 5 = 1250 \;\; \text{pC/g} \;\; \text{(typical for industrial units)}$$

$$S_v = \frac{1250 \text{ pC/g}}{1000 \text{ pF}} = 1.25 \;\; \text{V/g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Typical industrial accelerometers have sensitivities in the range 1–10 V/g (ICP-type) or 10–1000 pC/g (charge-type). The values above fall comfortably in this band, confirming the derivation.

### 3.3 Derivation: Upper Frequency Limit for < 5% Amplitude Error

For an undamped ($\zeta \to 0$) accelerometer, the amplitude ratio is:

$$\left\vert \frac{Z}{Y} \right\vert = \frac{(\omega/\omega_n)^2}{\vert 1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2 \vert}$$

For **5% error**, we require $|Z/Y| \le 1.05$ (or more precisely, the low-frequency approximation to be within 5%):

$$\frac{(\omega/\omega_n)^2}{1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2} = 1.05$$

$$(\omega/\omega_n)^2 = 1.05 [1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2]$$

$$(\omega/\omega_n)^2 + 1.05 (\omega/\omega_n)^2 = 1.05$$

$$(\omega/\omega_n)^2 = \frac{1.05}{2.05} = 0.512$$

$$\omega/\omega_n = 0.716 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{max} \approx 0.2 \, f_n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 1/5 Rule (the most-asked KTU shortcut):** To keep amplitude error below ~5% and phase error below a few degrees, the **upper usable measurement frequency is approximately one-fifth of the natural frequency** of the accelerometer. So a 25 kHz resonant accelerometer is good for measurements up to about 5 kHz.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pickup Selection for NVH

| Pickup Type | Output Quantity | Frequency Range | Sensitivity | Pros | Cons | Typical NVH Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Piezoelectric accelerometer | Charge / Voltage ∝ a | 0.1 Hz – 10 kHz | 1–1000 pC/g | Wide range, robust, no external power (charge mode) | Cannot measure static (DC) | Engine, body, modal analysis |
| Piezoresistive accelerometer | Resistance change ∝ a | DC – few kHz | mV/V/g | Measures static acceleration | Lower sensitivity, needs excitation | Crash testing, low-freq |
| MEMS capacitive accelerometer | Capacitance change ∝ a | DC – 1 kHz | V/g | Tiny, cheap, low power | Limited bandwidth | Consumer electronics, basic NVH |
| LVDT (displacement) | Voltage ∝ x | DC – 200 Hz | V/mm | Very linear, absolute | Limited to low-freq | Suspension travel, valve lift |
| Moving-coil velocity geophone | EMF ∝ v | 1 – 500 Hz | V/(m/s) | Simple, no power needed | Low sensitivity, fragile | Seismic, large machinery |
| Eddy-current probe | Inductance ∝ gap | DC – 50 kHz | V/µm | Non-contact, very linear | Only for conductive surfaces | Shaft vibration, bearing health |
| Laser Doppler Vibrometer (LDV) | Doppler shift ∝ v | DC – MHz | depends on decoder | Non-contact, ultra-precise, no mass loading | Very expensive | Speaker, MEMS, brake squeal |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Seismic Pickup Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Vibrating Surface] --> B[Housing of Pickup]
    B --> C[Base of Piezo Stack]
    C --> D[Seismic Mass m]
    B --> E[Spring Stiffness k]
    E --> D
    D --> F[Piezoelectric Crystal]
    F --> G[Charge Q = d.F]
    G --> H[Charge Amplifier]
    H --> I[Voltage Output Vout]
    I --> J[DAQ / FFT Analyzer]
    J --> K[Display / Recorder]

    subgraph SG1[Mechanical Subsystem]
        B
        C
        D
        E
    end

    subgraph SG2[Transduction Subsystem]
        F
        G
    end

    subgraph SG3[Signal Conditioning and Analysis]
        H
        I
        J
        K
    end
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Piezoelectric Accelerometer Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Seismic Mass] --> C1[Compression Piezo Element]
    C1 --> C2[Compression Piezo Element]
    C2 --> C3[Compression Piezo Element]
    C3 --> B1[Housing Base]
    C1 --> E1[Electrode 1]
    C2 --> E2[Electrode 2]
    C3 --> E3[Electrode 3]
    E1 --> W1[Output Wire]
    E3 --> W2[Ground Wire]
    E2 --> W1
    W1 --> OUT[Net Charge Q]
    W2 --> GND[Reference Ground]
    OUT --> CA[Charge Amplifier]
    CA --> V[Voltage Output Vout]
    V --> LPF[Low Pass Filter]
    LPF --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> DSP[Digital Signal Processor]
    DSP --> DISP[Display Console]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete NVH Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[Vibrating Source] --> PICK[Vibration Pickup]
    PICK --> AMP[Charge or Voltage Amplifier]
    AMP --> FILT[Anti Aliasing Filter]
    FILT --> DAQ[Data Acquisition Card]
    DAQ --> FFT[FFT Spectrum Analyzer]
    FFT --> MOD[Modal or Order Analysis]
    MOD --> DSP[Display Dashboard]
    FFT --> RCD[Recorder Storage]
    FFT --> OVALL[Overall RMS Calculator]
    OVALL --> ALERT[Alarm and Diagnostic System]
    PICK -.-> CAL[Calibration Source]
    CAL -.-> PICK
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Pickup Selection for NVH Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST[Start Selection] --> Q1{Is the surface conductive and accessible non contact?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> ECP[Choose Eddy Current Probe]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is ultra high precision required at MHz frequencies?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> LDV[Choose Laser Doppler Vibrometer]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is static DC acceleration needed?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> PR[Choose Piezoresistive or MEMS Accelerometer]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is the measurement bandwidth below 1 kHz?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> VEL[Choose Moving Coil Velocity Pickup]
    Q4 -- No --> PZA[Choose Piezoelectric Accelerometer]
    PZA --> Q5{Is temperature above 100 deg C?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> HTA[Choose High Temp Shear Mode Accelerometer]
    Q5 -- No --> STA[Choose Standard Compression Mode]
    ECP --> DONE[Selected Pickup]
    LDV --> DONE
    PR --> DONE
    VEL --> DONE
    PZA --> DONE
    HTA --> DONE
    STA --> DONE
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Frequency Response Behaviour of Seismic Pickup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REG1[Very Low Frequency Region omega much less than omega_n] --> NOTE1[Pickup is a Relative Displacement Sensor Z proportional to omega squared Y]
    REG2[Near Resonance Region omega near omega_n] --> NOTE2[Resonance Peak Magnified Output High Phase Distortion]
    REG3[Very High Frequency Region omega much greater than omega_n] --> NOTE3[Pickup Output Saturation Z approaches Y as Rigid Body]
    REG1 --> MATH1[Z = a divided by omega n squared]
    REG3 --> MATH2[Z = Y nearly equal to base motion]
    REG2 --> MATH3[Z = omega Y divided by 2 zeta omega n]
    MATH1 --> USE1[Used for Accelerometer Mode]
    MATH3 --> USE2[Used for Velocity Geophone Mode]
    MATH2 --> USE3[Avoided in Measurement Band]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
Define a vibration pickup. List the three fundamental kinematic quantities it can measure, and name one typical sensor for each.

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

A **vibration pickup** is a transducer that converts mechanical vibration of a structure into a proportional electrical signal (voltage or charge) for measurement and analysis. It can be designed to measure three fundamental kinematic quantities:

| Kinematic Quantity | SI Unit | Typical Pickup |
|---|---|---|
| Displacement (x) | m | Eddy-current probe, LVDT |
| Velocity (v) | m/s | Moving-coil geophone |
| Acceleration (a) | m/s² | Piezoelectric accelerometer |

The choice depends on the **frequency range** of interest, the **amplitude** of vibration, **temperature environment**, and the **mass-loading constraint** of the test structure.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
What is the natural frequency of the seismic element in a piezoelectric accelerometer, and why is it kept very high?

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

The natural frequency of the seismic mass-spring system inside the accelerometer is:

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

where $k$ is the spring stiffness of the piezo stack and $m$ is the seismic mass. It is kept very high (typically 20–50 kHz for industrial units) so that the accelerometer operates in its **flat response region** (where $\omega \ll \omega_n$). In this region, the relative displacement $Z$ is directly proportional to the input acceleration $a$, allowing faithful measurement of vibration across a wide frequency range (typically up to $0.2 \, f_n$ with < 5% error).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Answer one full question. Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into Part (a) **7 marks** + Part (b) **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [Modeled on KTU University Exam – 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **piezoelectric accelerometer** as a vibration pickup. Derive the expression for its charge sensitivity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A piezoelectric accelerometer has a seismic mass of 8 g and a piezo charge coefficient of 280 pC/N. The charge amplifier uses a feedback capacitor of 1.2 nF. Calculate:
(i) The charge sensitivity in pC/g.
(ii) The voltage sensitivity in mV/g.
(iii) The output voltage when the input acceleration is 5 g (rms). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Working Principle:**

A piezoelectric accelerometer consists of:
- A **piezoelectric crystal** (PZT) sandwiched between a **seismic mass** and the **housing base**
- The housing is bolted or glued to the vibrating surface
- A charge amplifier connected to the crystal's electrodes

When the surface vibrates with acceleration $a$, the seismic mass $m$ exerts an inertial force $F = m a$ on the crystal. By the **direct piezoelectric effect**:

$$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a$$

**Derivation of charge sensitivity:**

The charge sensitivity is defined as the charge produced per unit input acceleration:

$$S_q = \frac{Q}{a} = d \cdot m$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

Given: $m = 8 \text{ g}$, $d = 280 \text{ pC/N}$, $C_f = 1.2 \text{ nF} = 1200 \text{ pF}$, $a_{rms} = 5 \text{ g}$.

**(i) Charge sensitivity:**

$$S_q = d \cdot m = 280 \text{ pC/N} \times 8 \text{ g} = 2240 \text{ pC/g}$$

**(ii) Voltage sensitivity:**

$$S_v = \frac{S_q}{C_f} = \frac{2240 \text{ pC/g}}{1200 \text{ pF}} = 1.867 \text{ V/g} = 1867 \text{ mV/g}$$

**(iii) Output voltage for 5 g rms:**

$$V_{out,rms} = S_v \times a_{rms} = 1.867 \text{ V/g} \times 5 \text{ g} = 9.33 \text{ V (rms)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 14-mark Question:** Most students forget to convert grams to kg (or equivalently, use the units consistently in pC, pF, g). If you write $S_q = 280 \times 8 = 2240$ without units, you lose 1 mark. Also, students often divide pC by pF incorrectly, getting mV/g when they should get V/g — remember $1 \text{ pC} / 1 \text{ pF} = 1 \text{ V}$. Final answer must include the unit **g**, not kg, because accelerations in NVH are conventionally expressed in **g** ($1 g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Modeled on KTU University Exam – 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion of a **seismic mass-spring-damper vibration pickup** subjected to base excitation $y(t) = Y \sin \omega t$. Hence obtain the steady-state amplitude ratio $Z/Y$ and show that the pickup behaves as an accelerometer when $\omega \ll \omega_n$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A seismic pickup has $m = 0.05 \text{ kg}$, $k = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}$, and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.05$. The base is subjected to a sinusoidal vibration of amplitude $Y = 0.5 \text{ mm}$ at frequency $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$. Calculate:
(i) The natural frequency $f_n$.
(ii) The frequency ratio $r = \omega / \omega_n$.
(iii) The relative displacement amplitude $Z$.
(iv) Verify whether the pickup is operating in the accelerometer mode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Refer to the detailed derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps to include in your answer:
1. Free body diagram: $m \ddot{x} = -k(x - y) - c(\dot{x} - \dot{y})$
2. Substitute $z = x - y$: $m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{y}$
3. Standard form: $\ddot{z} + 2 \zeta \omega_n \dot{z} + \omega_n^2 z = -\ddot{y}$
4. For sinusoidal input, derive: $\dfrac{Z}{Y} = \dfrac{\omega^2}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2 \zeta \omega_n \omega)^2}}$
5. For $\omega \ll \omega_n$: $Z \approx (\omega^2 / \omega_n^2) Y = a / \omega_n^2$, i.e., $Z \propto a$, proving accelerometer behaviour.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

Given: $m = 0.05 \text{ kg}$, $k = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}$, $\zeta = 0.05$, $Y = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$.

**(i) Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{1 \times 10^5}{0.05}} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^6} = 1414.2 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1414.2}{2\pi} = 225.08 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Frequency ratio:**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{314.16}{1414.2} = 0.222$$

**(iii) Relative displacement amplitude:**

$$Z = Y \cdot \frac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2 \zeta r)^2}}$$

$$Z = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{(0.222)^2}{\sqrt{(1 - 0.222^2)^2 + (2 \times 0.05 \times 0.222)^2}}$$

$$Z = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{0.0493}{\sqrt{(0.9507)^2 + (0.0222)^2}}$$

$$Z = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{0.0493}{0.9510} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.0518 = 2.59 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

$$Z \approx 25.9 \; \mu\text{m}$$

**(iv) Verification of Accelerometer Mode:**

Since $r = 0.222 \ll 1$, the pickup is operating in the regime $\omega \ll \omega_n$. Therefore, the relative displacement $Z$ is approximately:

$$Z \approx \frac{a}{\omega_n^2} = \frac{\omega^2 Y}{\omega_n^2} = r^2 Y = 0.0493 \times 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0247 \text{ mm} = 24.7 \; \mu\text{m}$$

This matches the more exact value of 25.9 µm (within ~5%), confirming that the pickup is indeed behaving as an **accelerometer** in this frequency range. The output is proportional to the input acceleration:

$$a = \omega^2 Y = (314.16)^2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 49.35 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 5.03 \text{ g}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 14-mark Question:** In Part (a), do **not** skip the free-body diagram — KTU examiners award 2 marks just for the diagram. In Part (b)(iv), students often forget to compute and report the **input acceleration in g**, which is the key step that justifies whether the pickup is in accelerometer mode or not. Always conclude with a sentence: "Since $r \ll 1$, the pickup is in accelerometer mode."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist before the exam. Every bullet is a potential question.**

- ✅ A **vibration pickup** converts mechanical vibration into an electrical signal proportional to **displacement, velocity, or acceleration**.
- ✅ The **seismic mass-spring-damper model** is the universal basis for all contact-type pickups.
- ✅ The governing equation is: $m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{y}$, with $z = x - y$.
- ✅ Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$; Damping ratio: $\zeta = c/(2 m \omega_n)$.
- ✅ Amplitude ratio: $Z/Y = \omega^2 / \sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2 \zeta \omega_n \omega)^2}$.
- ✅ Three regimes: (1) $\omega \ll \omega_n$ → **Accelerometer** ($Z \propto a$); (2) $\omega \approx \omega_n$ → **Velocity**; (3) $\omega \gg \omega_n$ → **Seismometer** ($Z \approx Y$).
- ✅ **Piezoelectric accelerometer charge:** $Q = d \cdot m \cdot a$; Charge sensitivity: $S_q = d \cdot m$.
- ✅ **Charge amplifier output:** $V_{out} = Q / C_f = (d \cdot m \cdot a) / C_f$.
- ✅ **The 1/5 rule:** $f_{upper,useful} \approx 0.2 \, f_n$ keeps amplitude error < 5%.
- ✅ **Mass-loading rule:** Pickup mass < 1/10 of test structure's effective modal mass.
- ✅ **Piezoelectric** = **cannot measure static (DC)** acceleration. For DC, use **piezoresistive** or **MEMS capacitive** accelerometers.
- ✅ **Charge amplifier** is preferred over voltage amplifier because it is **immune to cable capacitance** — vital in field NVH testing with long cables.
- ✅ **Non-contact pickups:** Eddy-current (conductive surfaces only, no DC), Laser Doppler Vibrometer (optical, expensive, no mass loading).
- ✅ **Velocity geophone:** EMF = $B \ell v$; operates near resonance ($\omega \approx \omega_n$).
- ✅ **Sensitivity units to remember:** pC/g, mV/g, V/(m/s), V/µm — never mix them up.
- ✅ Standard $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. In NVH, accelerations are almost always quoted in **g**, not m/s².
- ✅ When asked to **"show that the pickup behaves as an accelerometer"**, derive $Z \approx a/\omega_n^2$ using the limit $\omega \ll \omega_n$.
- ✅ Typical KTU pitfall: Forgetting that $1 g$ acceleration on a $5 \text{ g}$ seismic mass produces a **49 N force** ($5 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81$); the corresponding charge is $d \times 49$ pC, not $d \times 5$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transducers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transducers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Transducer

A **Transducer** is a device that converts energy from one form to another. In the specific context of Noise, Vibration, and Harshness (NVH) engineering, a transducer is a sensing element that converts a physical quantity such as **force, displacement, velocity, acceleration, pressure, or sound** into a proportional electrical signal (voltage, current, or charge) that can be measured, recorded, and analyzed.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a transducer acts as the **primary interface between the physical vibration/acoustic domain and the electronic signal-conditioning domain**. It is the first and most critical link in any measurement chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A transducer is *not* a sensor in isolation — it is the complete energy-conversion element. The associated signal conditioner and display unit together constitute the measurement *system*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are talking to a friend who only understands electricity, but you want to tell them how rough a road feels when you ride a bike over it. You need a "translator" that takes the physical bumpiness and turns it into a language your friend can read. **That translator is the transducer.**

- The **road bumpiness** = physical vibration (mechanical energy)
- The **translator** = transducer
- The **electrical language** = voltage output
- Your **friend** = the data acquisition system (DAQ) and computer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Active vs Passive — A Key Distinction:** Some transducers *generate* their own electricity (e.g., a piezoelectric crystal), while others need external electrical power to work (e.g., a strain gauge in a Wheatstone bridge). This difference is the single most asked concept in KTU examinations.

### 1.3 Classification Overview

Transducers are broadly classified along two axes relevant to NVH applications:

| Axis | Categories | Example in NVH |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | **Active** (self-generating) vs **Passive** (need excitation) | Piezoelectric accelerometer vs Strain gauge |
| Output form | **Analog** vs **Digital** | LVDT vs Digital MEMS accelerometer |
| Measured quantity | Force, Displacement, Velocity, Acceleration, Sound pressure | PZT accelerometer, LVDT, geophone, microphone |
| Operating principle | Resistive, Inductive, Capacitive, Piezoelectric, Electromagnetic | Strain gauge, LVDT, capacitive probe, piezo, coil-magnet |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, the **piezoelectric accelerometer** is the highest-weightage topic. Master its working, equivalent circuit, and charge/voltage mode equations.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are **mandatory** for NVH transducer problems:

- **Charge sensitivity of quartz (d₃₃) ≈ 2.3 × 10⁻¹² C/N** (Coulomb per Newton)
- **Piezoelectric constant of PZT ≈ 250 × 10⁻¹² C/N**
- **Standard accelerometer mass: 2 g to 100 g** (lower mass = less loading error)
- **Frequency Range of human hearing: 20 Hz to 20 kHz**
- **Reference sound pressure: p₀ = 20 μPa**
- **Standard gravity: g = 9.81 m/s²**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Generic Transducer

A transducer performs the energy conversion through the following logical sequence:

- **Step 1 — Sensing Element (Primary):** The physical quantity (acceleration, force, pressure) is directly applied to the active material of the transducer.
- **Step 2 — Energy Conversion Mechanism:** The active material changes one of its electrical properties (resistance, capacitance, inductance, or generates a charge) in proportion to the input.
- **Step 3 — Signal Generation:** A measurable electrical quantity emerges — either a voltage, current, resistance change, or charge.
- **Step 4 — Signal Conditioning:** External circuitry (amplifier, filter, integrator) refines the raw output into a usable signal.
- **Step 5 — Output to DAQ:** The conditioned signal is digitized and recorded for analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> The "Why" behind each step: The sensing element alone produces a tiny, noisy, or non-standard output. Without conditioning (Steps 4–5), the signal cannot drive a recorder or analyzer. **This is why the transducer and conditioner are designed as a matched pair.**

### 2.2 Static and Dynamic Characteristics of Transducers

These characteristics determine whether a transducer is suitable for a given NVH application:

- **Sensitivity (S):** Ratio of output electrical signal to the input physical quantity. Units vary (V/g, mV/Pa, pC/N).
- **Linearity:** Output must be linearly proportional to input across the operating range. Non-linearity is expressed as a percentage of full scale (% FS).
- **Accuracy:** Closeness of the measured value to the true value. Expressed as ±(% of reading + % of FS).
- **Hysteresis:** Maximum difference in output for the same input approached from increasing and decreasing directions.
- **Resolution:** Smallest change in input that produces a detectable change in output.
- **Dynamic Range:** Ratio of the largest to the smallest measurable signal, typically expressed in **dB**.
- **Frequency Response:** Variation of sensitivity with the frequency of the input signal. A flat response over the band of interest is essential.
- **Resonant Frequency (f_n):** Frequency at which the transducer's internal mass-spring system resonates — the usable bandwidth is typically **f_n / 2.6** to **f_n / 5**.
- **Cross-axis Sensitivity:** Output produced by an input applied perpendicular to the sensitive axis. Expressed as a percentage (typically < 5%).
- **Loading Error:** Disturbance caused by the transducer's own mass on the vibrating structure. Mitigated by **low mass-to-test-mass ratio (< 1/10)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = S \cdot Q_{in}$ | Output voltage = Sensitivity × Input quantity | V, V/(unit of Q) |
| 2 | $S_a = V_{out} / a$ | Accelerometer sensitivity | V·s²/m or V/g |
| 3 | $Q = d \cdot F$ | Piezoelectric charge: $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | C, C/N, N |
| 4 | $V = Q / C_p$ | Voltage mode output: $V = dF / C_p$ | V, F (Farad) |
| 5 | $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | Strain gauge: $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $V_{out} = (V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon) / 4$ | Quarter-bridge output (approximate, single active gauge) | V |
| 7 | $e = B \cdot l \cdot v$ | Electromagnetic transducer EMF: $e = B l v$ | V, T, m, m/s |
| 8 | $f_n = (1 / 2\pi) \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency of seismic mass | Hz, N/m, kg |
| 9 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Angular natural frequency | rad/s |
| 10 | $V_{out} = S \cdot \int a \, dt$ | Velocity from integration of accelerometer signal | m/s |
| 11 | $d = V_{out} = K \cdot x$ | LVDT output: $V_{out} = K \cdot x$ | V, V/m, m |
| 12 | $Z_a = \sqrt{(m\omega)^2 + (c\omega)^2 + k^2}$ | Accelerometer mechanical impedance | N·s/m |
| 13 | $f_{max} = f_n / 2.6$ | Maximum usable frequency (5% limit) | Hz |
| 14 | $TL = 20 \log_{10}(p / p_0)$ | Sound pressure level with $p_0 = 20\,\mu Pa$ | dB |
| 15 | $L_w = 10 \log_{10}(W / W_0)$ | Sound power level with $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When writing $\vert \cdot \vert$ for absolute value inside a math row, use the LaTeX command `\vert` so it does not collide with markdown table column separators. For example: $S = \vert V_{out} / a \vert$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Transducers are the **backbone of every NVH test cell, vehicle pass-by-noise test, modal hammer test, and acoustic chamber measurement**.

- **Automotive NVH:** Accelerometers measure powertrain vibration; microphones measure cabin and pass-by noise; force transducers in shaker tables apply controlled excitation.
- **Aerospace:** Piezoelectric accelerometers monitor turbine blade vibration at temperatures exceeding 500 °C.
- **Civil Engineering:** Seismometers (velocity transducers) record ground motion during earthquakes.
- **Consumer Electronics:** MEMS microphones in smartphones use capacitive transducer principles.
- **Industrial Machinery:** Eddy-current and inductive displacement probes monitor shaft vibration in turbomachinery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Natural Frequency of a Seismic-Mass Accelerometer

The seismic-mass accelerometer is the most common NVH transducer. Its operating principle requires that the seismic mass behaves as a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) system.

**Given:**
- Seismic mass = $m$ (kg)
- Spring stiffness = $k$ (N/m)
- Damping coefficient = $c$ (N·s/m)
- Base (case) displacement = $y(t)$
- Relative displacement of mass w.r.t. case = $z(t) = x(t) - y(t)$

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1 — Equation of Motion (Free Body Diagram of Mass):**

The mass is acted upon by the spring force $-k z$ and damping force $-c \dot{z}$. Applying Newton's second law:

$$
m \ddot{x} = -k(x - y) - c(\dot{x} - \dot{y})
$$

This is rewritten using relative coordinate $z = x - y$, giving $x = z + y$, so $\ddot{x} = \ddot{z} + \ddot{y}$:

$$
m(\ddot{z} + \ddot{y}) + c \dot{z} + k z = 0
$$

Rearranging to isolate the base acceleration as input:

$$
m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{y}
$$

**Step 2 — Forcing Function Identification:**

The base acceleration $\ddot{y}$ is the physical quantity being measured. It acts as a forcing function with magnitude $-m$. The right-hand side is the **inertial force** on the mass.

**Step 3 — Sinusoidal Steady-State Assumption:**

Let the base move as $y(t) = Y_0 \sin(\omega t)$, so $\ddot{y}(t) = -\omega^2 Y_0 \sin(\omega t)$. Assume a steady-state solution $z(t) = Z_0 \sin(\omega t - \phi)$.

Substituting and solving the magnitude ratio:

$$
\frac{Z_0}{Y_0} = \frac{( \omega / \omega_n )^2}{\sqrt{\left[ 1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2 \right]^2 + \left[ 2 \zeta (\omega/\omega_n) \right]^2}}
$$

where $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and $\zeta = c / (2 \sqrt{mk})$.

**Step 4 — Deriving the Usable Frequency Range:**

For an accelerometer to measure the *base acceleration* accurately, the relative displacement $z$ must be proportional to $\ddot{y}$. This requires $(\omega / \omega_n)^2 \gg 1$, i.e., the operating frequency must be **well above the natural frequency**.

A common rule of thumb in KTU problems:

$$
f_{max} = \frac{f_n}{2.6} \quad \text{(5% amplitude error limit, }\zeta \approx 0.7\text{)}
$$

**Step 5 — Sensitivity Relation:**

The relative displacement $z$ is converted to voltage by a displacement-to-voltage mechanism (e.g., piezoelectric, capacitive). The accelerometer output is therefore:

$$
V_{out} = S_a \cdot \ddot{y} = S_a \cdot a_{base}
$$

where $S_a$ is the charge or voltage sensitivity in V·s²/m or V/g.

> [!NOTE]
> The "Why" of this derivation: It shows that an accelerometer does **not** directly measure acceleration — it measures the relative displacement between the seismic mass and the case. The acceleration is inferred through the SDOF mechanical model. This is why *frequency range* is the most important specification.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Piezoelectric Charge and Voltage Sensitivity

**Step 1 — Direct Piezoelectric Effect:**

When a force $F$ is applied along the poling axis of a piezoelectric crystal (e.g., quartz, PZT), the resulting charge is:

$$
Q = d_{33} \cdot F
$$

where $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient in **C/N** (Coulomb per Newton).

**Step 2 — Equivalent Circuit Model:**

The piezoelectric element is modelled as a charge source $Q$ in parallel with the capacitance of the crystal $C_p$ and the input capacitance of the cable $C_c$:

$$
C_{total} = C_p + C_c
$$

**Step 3 — Voltage Across the Crystal:**

$$
V = \frac{Q}{C_{total}} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{C_p + C_c}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $F = m a$ for an Accelerometer:**

$$
V = \frac{d_{33} \cdot m \cdot a}{C_p + C_c}
$$

**Step 5 — Voltage Sensitivity $S_v$:**

$$
S_v = \frac{V}{a} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot m}{C_p + C_c}
$$

Units: V·s²/m (or V/g when $g = 9.81$ m/s² is used).

**Step 6 — Charge Sensitivity $S_q$:**

$$
S_q = \frac{Q}{a} = d_{33} \cdot m
$$

Units: pC/g (pico-Coulomb per g).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Charge Mode vs Voltage Mode:** In charge mode, the cable capacitance does not affect sensitivity (because the charge is measured directly with a high-impedance charge amplifier). In voltage mode, long cables *reduce* sensitivity because $C_c$ appears in the denominator. **This is a 3-mark KTU favourite.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Strain Gauge Bridge Output

**Step 1 — Single Active Gauge in Quarter Bridge:**

A strain gauge changes resistance under strain: $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \varepsilon$, where $G$ is the gauge factor (typically ≈ 2.0 for metallic gauges).

**Step 2 — Wheatstone Bridge Output:**

The bridge is excited by $V_{ex}$. With one active gauge and three fixed arms of resistance $R$:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \left[ \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{R}{2R} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Simplification for Small $\Delta R$:**

Since $\Delta R \ll R$, the term $(\Delta R)^2$ in the denominator is negligible:

$$
V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $\Delta R / R = G \varepsilon$:**

$$
V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon}{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Full-Bridge with Four Active Gauges:**

When all four arms are active (two in tension, two in compression), the output is **four times larger**:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The "Why" behind bridge design: A full-bridge configuration also provides **automatic temperature compensation**, because all four gauges experience the same temperature and any thermal strain cancels out. This is why full bridges are preferred in field NVH measurements.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Electromagnetic Velocity Transducer (Geophone)

**Step 1 — Working Principle:**

A coil of $N$ turns moves with velocity $v$ in a uniform magnetic field $B$ of effective length $l$. The induced EMF is:

$$
e = B \cdot l \cdot v \cdot N
$$

For $N$ turns: $e = N B l v$.

**Step 2 — For Sinusoidal Motion $x = X_0 \sin(\omega t)$:**

$$
v = \dot{x} = \omega X_0 \cos(\omega t)
$$

$$
e(t) = N B l \omega X_0 \cos(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3 — Sensitivity in V/(m/s):**

$$
S_v = N B l
$$

Units: V·s/m.

> [!TIP]
> Geophones have a natural frequency (typically 1–10 Hz) **above** which the output voltage is proportional to velocity. Below $f_n$, the output is proportional to displacement. This dual-region behaviour is a common viva question.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Major Transducers

| Transducer | Measured Quantity | Operating Principle | Sensitivity Range | Frequency Range | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Piezoelectric Accelerometer | Acceleration | Piezoelectric effect | 1–1000 pC/g or 1–1000 mV/g | 0.1 Hz to 50 kHz | **Very High** |
| LVDT | Displacement | Variable inductance | 1–1000 mV/V/mm | DC to 1 kHz | High |
| Strain Gauge | Strain | Resistance change | 2 mV/V at 1000 μɛ | DC to 100 kHz | High |
| Geophone | Velocity | Electromagnetic induction | 1–50 V/(m/s) | 1 Hz to 500 Hz | Medium |
| Capacitive Microphone | Sound Pressure | Capacitance change | 1–50 mV/Pa | 20 Hz to 100 kHz | High |
| Eddy-Current Probe | Displacement | Eddy-current loss | 1–10 V/mm | DC to 100 kHz | Medium |
| Force Transducer (Load Cell) | Force | Strain gauge or piezo | 1–10 pC/N or 1–3 mV/V | DC to 50 kHz | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Vibration Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Vibration Source] --> B[Transducer Element]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Unit]
    C --> D[Amplifier and Filter]
    D --> E[Data Acquisition DAQ]
    E --> F[FFT Analyzer]
    F --> G[Display and Recording]
    B -. Feedback .- H[Calibration Signal]
    H --> C
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **Physical Vibration Source** (e.g., engine block, gearbox housing) generates the mechanical input.
- The **Transducer Element** converts this into an electrical signal.
- The **Signal Conditioning Unit** provides excitation (for passive transducers) and impedance matching.
- The **Amplifier and Filter** boosts the signal and removes unwanted noise.
- The **Data Acquisition** digitizes the signal.
- The **FFT Analyzer** converts time-domain data to frequency-domain spectra.
- The **Display and Recording** presents the final result.
- The **Calibration Signal** path ensures the entire chain is traceable to a known reference (e.g., 9.81 m/s² at 159.2 Hz from a back-to-back calibrator).

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Classification of NVH Transducers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T[NVH Transducers]
    T --> ACT[Active Self Generating]
    T --> PAS[Passive Externally Excited]
    ACT --> ACT1[Piezoelectric Accelerometer]
    ACT --> ACT2[Piezoelectric Microphone]
    ACT --> ACT3[Geophone Velocity Pickup]
    ACT --> ACT4[Electromagnetic Shaker Force Gauge]
    PAS --> PAS1[Strain Gauge Load Cell]
    PAS --> PAS2[LVDT Displacement Sensor]
    PAS --> PAS3[Capacitive Microphone]
    PAS --> PAS4[Eddy Current Probe]
    PAS --> PAS5[MEMS Accelerometer]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- All NVH transducers fall into two top-level categories: **Active** (self-generating) and **Passive** (externally excited).
- **Active transducers** include piezoelectric types, geophones, and electromagnetic shakers — these generate voltage or EMF without external power.
- **Passive transducers** include strain gauges, LVDTs, capacitive microphones, eddy-current probes, and MEMS devices — these require excitation voltage, current, or carrier signals.

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Piezoelectric Accelerometer (Internal)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BASE[Base Mounting Stud] --> CASE[Housing Case]
    CASE --> MASS[Seismic Mass m]
    MASS --> PZT[Piezoelectric Crystal PZT]
    PZT --> ELEC[Electrode]
    ELEC --> AMP[Charge or Voltage Amplifier]
    AMP --> OUT[Output Voltage Vout]
    CASE -. Spring k .-> MASS
    MASS -. Damper c .-> CASE
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **Base Mounting Stud** is the interface to the vibrating structure.
- The **Housing Case** moves with the base.
- The **Seismic Mass** lags behind due to inertia, creating a relative displacement.
- The **Piezoelectric Crystal** experiences this relative motion and generates charge.
- The **Electrode** collects the charge and routes it to the amplifier.
- The internal **Spring (k)** and **Damper (c)** govern the dynamic response and set the natural frequency.

---

### 4.4 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right NVH Transducer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Quantity to measure?}
    Q1 -->|Acceleration| A1[Use Piezoelectric or MEMS Accelerometer]
    Q1 -->|Velocity| A2[Use Geophone or Electromagnetic Pickup]
    Q1 -->|Displacement| A3[Use LVDT or Eddy-Current Probe]
    Q1 -->|Force| A4[Use Piezoelectric Force Gauge or Load Cell]
    Q1 -->|Sound Pressure| A5[Use Microphone Capacitive or Piezo]
    A1 --> Q2{Frequency range?}
    Q2 -->|>1 kHz| R1[Choose Piezoelectric]
    Q2 -->|<1 kHz| R2[Choose MEMS or Strain Gauge]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The choice begins with the **physical quantity**.
- For **acceleration above 1 kHz**, piezoelectric is preferred for its high bandwidth.
- For **acceleration below 1 kHz**, MEMS or strain-gauge-based systems are competitive.
- For **velocity**, the geophone is a passive, robust solution.
- For **displacement**, LVDT and eddy-current probes are the workhorses.
- For **force**, piezoelectric is preferred for dynamic measurements, while load cells dominate quasi-static.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]:**
Define a transducer. Differentiate between **active** and **passive** transducers with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A transducer is a device that converts a physical quantity (such as force, displacement, pressure, or sound) into a proportional electrical signal for measurement and analysis.

**Active Transducer:** Generates its own electrical output without requiring any external power source. The energy conversion is one-way (mechanical → electrical).

*Example:* **Piezoelectric accelerometer** — when subjected to acceleration, a piezoelectric crystal produces a charge proportional to the applied force.

**Passive Transducer:** Requires an external electrical excitation (voltage or current) to operate. The output is a change in an electrical property (resistance, inductance, capacitance) of the sensing element.

*Example:* **Strain gauge** — requires a Wheatstone bridge excitation voltage; the output is a small voltage proportional to the strain.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "active" and "passive" without specifying the **direction of energy conversion** or the **external power requirement**. This costs 1 mark. Always state the *external excitation* explicitly.

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]:**
State the working principle of a **piezoelectric accelerometer** and write the expression for its **charge sensitivity**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A piezoelectric accelerometer operates on the **direct piezoelectric effect**. When a seismic mass $m$ is subjected to base acceleration $a$, it exerts an inertial force $F = ma$ on a piezoelectric crystal (PZT or quartz). This force generates a charge $Q$ across the crystal faces, given by:

$$
Q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot m \cdot a
$$

where $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient in **C/N**.

The **charge sensitivity** is defined as the charge generated per unit acceleration:

$$
S_q = \frac{Q}{a} = d_{33} \cdot m \quad \text{(Units: C·s²/m or pC/g)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to define $d_{33}$ and write units incorrectly. Always specify that $d_{33}$ is in **C/N** and the resulting $S_q$ is in **pC/g** when $g = 9.81$ m/s² is used.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a piezoelectric accelerometer**. Derive the expression for its **charge sensitivity** and **voltage sensitivity**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Construction:**

A piezoelectric accelerometer consists of the following components:

- A **piezoelectric crystal** (PZT or quartz) sandwiched between two electrodes.
- A **seismic mass** $m$ resting on top of the crystal, preloaded by a central stud.
- A **base** with a mounting stud fixed to the vibrating structure.
- A **hermetic case** providing mechanical protection and electrical shielding.
- A **connector** for the output cable.

**Working:**

When the accelerometer is mounted on a vibrating surface, the case moves with the surface. The seismic mass $m$ tends to remain at rest due to inertia, producing a relative displacement across the piezoelectric crystal. This generates a charge $Q$ given by the direct piezoelectric effect:

$$
Q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot m \cdot a
$$

The output charge is proportional to the base acceleration $a$.

**Derivation of Charge Sensitivity:**

$$
S_q = \frac{Q}{a} = d_{33} \cdot m
$$

**Derivation of Voltage Sensitivity:**

The piezoelectric element has an inherent capacitance $C_p$. The cable and amplifier add further capacitance $C_c$. The output voltage is:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{Q}{C_p + C_c} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot m \cdot a}{C_p + C_c}
$$

$$
S_v = \frac{V_{out}}{a} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot m}{C_p + C_c}
$$

For charge mode (using a charge amplifier), the cable capacitance is eliminated from the sensitivity calculation, making the system immune to cable length changes.

**(b)** A piezoelectric accelerometer has a seismic mass of $10$ g and a piezoelectric coefficient $d_{33} = 250 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N. The crystal capacitance is $1000$ pF and the cable capacitance is $300$ pF. Calculate the **charge sensitivity** and the **voltage sensitivity** in V/g. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $m = 10 \text{ g} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 0.01 \text{ kg}$
- $d_{33} = 250 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C/N}$
- $C_p = 1000 \text{ pF} = 1000 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$
- $C_c = 300 \text{ pF} = 300 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Charge Sensitivity:**

$$
S_q = d_{33} \cdot m = (250 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 0.01 = 2.5 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C·s}^2/\text{m}
$$

Converting to **pC/g**:

$$
S_q = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}}{9.81 \text{ m/s}^2} = 0.2548 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C·s}^2/\text{m}
$$

$$
S_q = 0.2548 \text{ pC/(m/s}^2) \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2/\text{g} = 2.5 \text{ pC/g}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_q = 2.5 \text{ pC/g}}
$$

**Step 2 — Voltage Sensitivity:**

Total capacitance:

$$
C_{total} = C_p + C_c = 1000 + 300 = 1300 \text{ pF} = 1300 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}
$$

$$
S_v = \frac{d_{33} \cdot m}{C_{total}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-12}}{1300 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.923 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V·s}^2/\text{m}
$$

Converting to **mV/g**:

$$
S_v = 1.923 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \cdot \text{s}^2/\text{m} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2/\text{g} = 18.86 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V/g}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_v \approx 18.86 \text{ mV/g}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Marks lost in this problem:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert g (grams) to kg (SI units) — costs 1 mark.
> 2. Forgetting to convert pF to F (Farads) when computing voltage — costs 1 mark.
> 3. Not multiplying by $g = 9.81$ m/s² to obtain final units in pC/g and mV/g — costs 1 mark.
> 4. Mixing up charge mode vs voltage mode in the formula — costs 2 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of an LVDT** (Linear Variable Differential Transformer). Derive the expression for its **output voltage** in terms of core displacement. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Construction:**

An LVDT consists of:

- A **primary coil** $P$ wound at the centre of a hollow cylindrical former.
- Two **secondary coils** $S_1$ and $S_2$ wound symmetrically on either side of the primary, connected in **series opposition** (i.e., their EMFs subtract).
- A movable **ferromagnetic core** that slides inside the former.
- An **AC excitation** (typically 1–10 kHz, 1–10 V RMS) applied to the primary.

**Working:**

When AC excitation is applied to the primary, it induces voltages $E_{s1}$ and $E_{s2}$ in the two secondaries. The net output is:

$$
V_{out} = E_{s1} - E_{s2}
$$

- When the core is at the **null (central) position**, $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ and $V_{out} = 0$.
- When the core moves toward $S_1$, $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$ and $V_{out}$ is positive.
- When the core moves toward $S_2$, $E_{s2} > E_{s1}$ and $V_{out}$ is negative.

**Derivation:**

For small displacements $x$ from the null position, the output voltage is approximately linear:

$$
V_{out} = K \cdot x
$$

where $K$ is the LVDT sensitivity in **V/m** (or V/mm).

For the differential output, with secondary turns $N_s$ and primary AC voltage $V_p$ at frequency $\omega$:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{N_s \cdot dM}{dx} \cdot \dot{I}_p \cdot x
$$

In magnitude form, for sinusoidal excitation:

$$
V_{out} = K \cdot x \quad \text{(linear region)}
$$

The phase of $V_{out}$ indicates the **direction** of displacement, and the magnitude indicates the **amount** of displacement.

**(b)** A strain gauge with gauge factor $G = 2.1$ and resistance $R = 350\ \Omega$ is bonded to a steel beam. The beam is loaded such that the strain is $\varepsilon = 1000\ \mu\varepsilon = 1000 \times 10^{-6}$. The gauge is connected in a **quarter Wheatstone bridge** with excitation voltage $V_{ex} = 5$ V. Calculate the **output voltage** of the bridge. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Gauge factor $G = 2.1$
- Gauge resistance $R = 350\ \Omega$
- Strain $\varepsilon = 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3}$
- Excitation voltage $V_{ex} = 5$ V
- Configuration: Quarter bridge (single active gauge)

**Step 1 — Change in Resistance:**

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 10^{-3} = 2.1 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\Delta R = R \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon = 350 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-3} = 0.735\ \Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Quarter-Bridge Output Voltage:**

$$
V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting Values:**

$$
V_{out} = \frac{5}{4} \times 2.1 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 1.25 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-3} = 2.625 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{out} = 2.625 \text{ mV}}
$$

**Step 4 — If a Full-Bridge Were Used (Bonus Insight):**

For a full bridge with four active gauges, the output is four times larger:

$$
V_{out,full} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon = 5 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-3} = 10.5 \text{ mV}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Marks lost in this problem:**
> 1. Using $\varepsilon = 1000$ instead of $1000 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3}$ — costs 1 mark.
> 2. Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{4}$ for quarter bridge and using $V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$ instead — costs 1 mark.
> 3. Not stating the units of the final answer in mV or $\mu$V — costs 1 mark.
> 4. Confusing the gauge factor formula with $\Delta R / R = \varepsilon / G$ (inverted) — costs 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Transducers (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Transducer = device that converts physical quantity to electrical signal.
- **Two Top-Level Categories:** Active (self-generating) vs Passive (externally excited).
- **Piezoelectric Effect:** Direct (mech → elec) and Converse (elec → mech).
- **Charge Sensitivity:** $S_q = d_{33} \cdot m$, units **pC/g**.
- **Voltage Sensitivity:** $S_v = d_{33} \cdot m / (C_p + C_c)$, units **mV/g**.
- **Charge Mode vs Voltage Mode:** Charge mode is immune to cable capacitance; voltage mode is sensitive to cable length.
- **LVDT Output:** $V_{out} = K \cdot x$, linear in the central region; phase gives direction.
- **Strain Gauge:** $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \varepsilon$; quarter bridge: $V_{out} = V_{ex} G \varepsilon / 4$; full bridge: $V_{out} = V_{ex} G \varepsilon$.
- **Electromagnetic Transducer:** $e = N B l v$; output is proportional to velocity.
- **Seismic Mass Natural Frequency:** $f_n = (1/2\pi) \sqrt{k/m}$; usable range $f < f_n / 2.6$.
- **Loading Error Rule:** Transducer mass should be less than 1/10 of the test structure's effective mass at the measurement point.
- **Cross-Axis Sensitivity:** Always < 5% of primary axis sensitivity for a quality accelerometer.
- **Key NVH Transducer Specs to Memorize:** Sensitivity, frequency range, resonant frequency, mass, maximum shock limit, operating temperature.
- **Capacitive Microphone:** Operates on diaphragm-plate capacitance change; reference pressure $p_0 = 20\ \mu$Pa.
- **Geophone:** Outputs velocity above its natural frequency; outputs displacement below $f_n$.
- **Common Exam Traps:** Confusing active and passive; mixing up charge and voltage mode; forgetting the $\frac{1}{4}$ factor in quarter bridge; not converting strain to dimensionless form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accelerometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Accelerometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accelerometer (KTU 2024 Definition):** An accelerometer is an electromechanical transducer that converts the **mechanical acceleration** of a vibrating structure into a proportional **electrical signal** (voltage or charge), enabling the quantitative measurement of vibration parameters in Noise, Vibration, and Harshness (NVH) analysis.

In the context of automotive, aerospace, and structural NVH engineering, accelerometers serve as the **primary sensing element** in the measurement chain. The output signal is post-processed to obtain parameters such as displacement, velocity, and acceleration as functions of time or frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 - KTU 2024):** The focus is on the working principle, mathematical model (mass-spring-damper analogy), sensitivity, frequency response, and mounting considerations of accelerometers used for vibration measurement.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Diving Board & Mass" Model:**
> Imagine a small mass glued to a thin, flexible diving board. When you shake the base of the diving board back and forth (the vibrating structure), the mass on top lags behind due to its **inertia**. The bending of the diving board (deflection) is proportional to how violently you shake the base — that is, proportional to **acceleration**.
> 
> If we attach a tiny **piezoelectric crystal** (or any displacement sensor) at the root of the diving board, it converts that bending into an electrical voltage. **Faster shaking → More bending → Larger voltage → Higher measured acceleration.**

## 1.3 Classification of Accelerometers (KTU Syllabus)

| Type | Working Principle | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Piezoelectric** | Charge generation by stress on crystal | General vibration, modal analysis |
| **Piezoresistive** | Resistance change under strain | High-g shock, crash tests |
| **Capacitive (MEMS)** | Capacitance change between plates | Consumer electronics, ECU |
| **Servo (Force-Balance)** | Feedback nulls mass displacement | Low-frequency, seismic, inertial nav |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in Accelerometer Analysis:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Standard gravitational unit in vibration: **1 g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Sensitivity units: $\text{mV/g}$ (voltage output) or $\text{pC/g}$ (charge output)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Mass-Spring-Damper (Seismic) Model

Every accelerometer, regardless of its electrical transduction principle, is fundamentally a **single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) mass-spring-damper system**. The mathematical model is identical — only the electrical readout mechanism differs.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Base Excitation:** The structure on which the accelerometer is mounted moves with displacement $y(t)$, velocity $\dot{y}(t)$, and acceleration $\ddot{y}(t)$.

2. **Inertial Response:** The seismic mass $m$ resists the motion due to its inertia. The relative motion between the mass and the base ($x - y$) creates a spring force and a damper force.

3. **Force Transmission:** The net inertial force $F = m \cdot a$ is transmitted to the sensing element (piezo crystal, strain gauge, capacitive plate, etc.).

4. **Electrical Transduction:** The sensing element converts the mechanical force into an electrical signal — charge, voltage, or resistance change.

5. **Signal Readout:** The output is amplified and fed to a data acquisition system (DAQs, FFT analyzers).

## 2.2 The Seismic Mass Equation of Motion

The governing equation for the seismic mass $m$:

$$m\ddot{x} + c(\dot{x} - \dot{y}) + k(x - y) = 0$$

Rearranging to express the relative displacement $z = x - y$:

$$m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{y}$$

This is the **classic forced SDOF equation** with the right-hand side being $-m \cdot \ddot{y}_{\text{base}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** When the excitation frequency $\omega \gg \omega_n$ (natural frequency), the relative displacement $z$ becomes proportional to $\ddot{y}$ (base acceleration). This is the **"accelerometer region"** of operation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's Law (Inertial force) | $F = m \cdot a$ | N | Fundamental transduction |
| 2 | Charge generated (Piezo) | $q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot m \cdot a$ | pC or C | Piezo sensitivity |
| 3 | Output voltage (Piezo) | $V_{out} = \dfrac{q}{C_p} = \dfrac{d_{33} \cdot m}{C_p} \cdot a$ | V | Voltage-mode IEPE |
| 4 | Sensitivity (charge) | $S_q = \dfrac{q}{a}$ | $\text{pC/g}$ | Charge-mode spec |
| 5 | Sensitivity (voltage) | $S_v = \dfrac{V_{out}}{a}$ | $\text{mV/g}$ | IEPE / MEMS spec |
| 6 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s | Resonance limit |
| 7 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | — | Dynamic response |
| 8 | Transfer function magnitude | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{\omega_n^2}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega)^2}}$ | — | Frequency response |
| 9 | Upper usable frequency | $\omega_{max} \approx 0.3\,\omega_n$ to $0.5\,\omega_n$ | rad/s | Design guideline |
| 10 | Lower usable frequency | Limited by amplifier/electronics | Hz | Charge vs. IEPE choice |
| 11 | Conversion: g to m/s² | $a \left(\text{m/s}^2\right) = a(g) \times 9.81$ | m/s² | Unit conversion |
| 12 | Conversion: rad/s to Hz | $f = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi}$ | Hz | Plotting spectra |
| 13 | Accelerometer mass loading | $f_{err} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{1 + m_s/m_a}}$ | — | Mounting correction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In automotive NVH, accelerometers are mounted on the engine block, transmission housing, body panels, and steering column to measure structure-borne vibration, which correlates with in-cabin noise (BSR — Buzz, Squeak, Rattle). Aerospace applications use servo-accelerometers in **inertial navigation systems (INS)** for measuring sustained accelerations during flight.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Accelerometer Transfer Function

We start from the relative-motion equation of motion (already derived in Section 2):
