- **Aerospace**: Aircraft fuselage skin, helicopter rotor blade, cabin floor panels (must meet FAR 25.853 + 25.856 acoustic burn-through).
- **Rail & Metro**: Composite floor panels, partition walls, HVAC ducts.
- **White goods & Building**: Refrigerator cabinets, elevator walls, lightweight doors.

A sandwich panel that **doubles the loss factor** (e.g. $\eta = 0.02 \to 0.04$) can reduce interior SPL by **3–5 dB(A)** — perceived as a **halving of loudness**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bending Stiffness $D$ of a Symmetric Sandwich Panel

We start from classical laminated plate theory (CLPT) but simplify for a symmetric 3-layer sandwich.

**Setup:**
- Total core thickness = $t_c$, face thickness = $t_f$ (each face).
- Face-to-face distance $d = t_c + t_f$.
- Place origin at the mid-plane of the sandwich.

**Step 1 — Definition of $D$**

$$
D \;=\; \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} E(z)\, z^{2}\, dz
$$

**Step 2 — Split the integral into 3 layers**

The sandwich has total thickness $h = t_c + 2 t_f$. The faces occupy $|z| \in [t_c/2,\; h/2]$; the core occupies $|z| \le t_c/2$.

$$
D \;=\; 2\!\int_{t_c/2}^{h/2}\! E_f\, z^{2}\, dz \;+\; 2\!\int_{0}^{t_c/2}\! E_c\, z^{2}\, dz
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the face integral**

$$
2\!\int_{t_c/2}^{h/2}\! E_f z^{2}\, dz \;=\; \frac{2 E_f}{3}\left[\left(\tfrac{h}{2}\right)^{3} - \left(\tfrac{t_c}{2}\right)^{3}\right]
$$

Expand $\left(\dfrac{h}{2}\right)^{3}$ where $h = t_c + 2t_f$:

$$
\left(\frac{t_c + 2t_f}{2}\right)^{3} - \left(\frac{t_c}{2}\right)^{3} \;=\; \frac{3 t_c^{2} t_f}{8} + \frac{3 t_c t_f^{2}}{4} + t_f^{3}
$$

For a **thin-face sandwich** ($t_f \ll t_c$), the $t_f^3$ and $t_c t_f^2$ terms are negligible, leaving:

$$
2\!\int_{t_c/2}^{h/2}\! E_f z^{2}\, dz \;\approx\; \frac{2 E_f}{3} \cdot \frac{3 t_c^{2} t_f}{8} \;=\; \frac{E_f\, t_f\, t_c^{2}}{4}
$$

But $t_c \approx d = t_c + t_f$ (since $t_f \ll t_c$), so:

$$
\text{face contribution} \;\approx\; \frac{E_f t_f d^{2}}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the core integral**

$$
2\!\int_{0}^{t_c/2}\! E_c z^{2}\, dz \;=\; \frac{2 E_c}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{t_c}{2}\right)^{3} \;=\; \frac{E_c\, t_c^{3}}{12}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the two contributions**

$$
D \;=\; \frac{E_f\, t_f\, d^{2}}{2} \;+\; \frac{E_c\, t_c^{3}}{12}
$$

This is **Formula #1** in the cheat sheet. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> The leading factor of 2 in the first term arises because the integration was doubled for *both* faces symmetrically. This is the standard form found in Allen (1969) and Zenkert (1995) sandwich handbooks.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A car floor-pan sandwich panel has aluminium faces ($E_f = 70 \times 10^{9}$ Pa, $t_f = 0.5$ mm) and a PU foam core ($E_c = 0.2 \times 10^{9}$ Pa, $t_c = 20$ mm). Find (i) bending stiffness $D$, (ii) areal mass $m$, (iii) coincidence frequency $f_c$, (iv) whether the mass law predicts TL at 500 Hz.

**Given:**

$$
E_f = 70 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa}, \quad t_f = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}
$$

$$
E_c = 0.2 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa}, \quad t_c = 20 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\rho_f = 2700\,\text{kg/m}^{3}, \quad \rho_c = 80\,\text{kg/m}^{3}, \quad c_0 = 343\,\text{m/s}
$$

**Step (i) — Bending stiffness $D$**

$$
d = t_c + t_f = 0.0205\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\frac{E_f t_f d^{2}}{2} = \frac{70\times 10^{9} \times 0.5\times 10^{-3} \times (0.0205)^{2}}{2}
$$

$$
= \frac{70 \times 10^{9} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.2025 \times 10^{-4}}{2}
$$

$$
= \frac{14\,708.75}{2} = 7354.4\,\text{N·m}
$$

$$
\frac{E_c t_c^{3}}{12} = \frac{0.2\times 10^{9} \times (0.02)^{3}}{12} = \frac{0.2 \times 10^{9} \times 8\times 10^{-6}}{12} = 133.3\,\text{N·m}
$$

$$
D = 7354.4 + 133.3 = 7487.7 \approx 7.49\,\text{kN·m}
$$

**Step (ii) — Areal mass $m$**

$$
m = 2 \rho_f t_f + \rho_c t_c = 2(2700)(0.0005) + (80)(0.02)
$$

$$
= 2.70 + 1.60 = 4.30\,\text{kg/m}^{2}
$$

**Step (iii) — Coincidence frequency $f_c$**

$$
f_c = \frac{c_0^{2}}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{m}{D}} = \frac{(343)^{2}}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{4.30}{7487.7}}
$$

$$
= \frac{117\,649}{6.2832} \times \sqrt{5.74 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
= 18\,727.2 \times 0.02396 = 448.6\,\text{Hz}
$$

**Step (iv) — Mass law TL at 500 Hz**

$$
500\,\text{Hz} > f_c = 448.6\,\text{Hz} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Above coincidence — mass law NOT valid}
$$

The panel will have a **coincidence dip** near 450 Hz, where TL drops sharply. **Engineering decision:** increase face thickness to 0.7 mm or use stiffer face to push $f_c$ above 1000 Hz.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Foam vs Honeycomb vs Balsa Core

| Property | PU Foam | Aluminium Honeycomb | Nomex Honeycomb | Balsa (end-grain) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho_c$ (kg/m³) | 30–150 | 30–100 | 50–120 | 150–250 |
| $E_c$ (MPa) | 5–50 | 100–1000 | 50–500 | 3000–5000 |
| $G_c$ (MPa) | 3–30 | 40–300 | 20–150 | 100–200 |
| Loss factor $\eta$ | **0.10–0.30** | 0.005–0.02 | 0.02–0.05 | 0.01–0.03 |
| Cost (rel.) | Low | Medium-High | High | Medium |
| Best NVH role | Damping | Stiffness | Stiffness + moderate damping | Compression + eco |
| Typical use | Dash, headliner | Floor, hood | Floor, door | Marine, truck floor |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cross-Sectional Layer Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FC1["FACE SHEET TOP"]
        nA["Aluminium 0.5 mm  E_f = 70 GPa  rho_f = 2700 kg per m3"]
    end
    subgraph CORE["CORE LAYER 20 mm"]
        nB["PU Foam  E_c = 0.2 GPa  G_c = 20 MPa  eta = 0.20  rho_c = 80 kg per m3"]
    end
    subgraph FC2["FACE SHEET BOTTOM"]
        nC["Aluminium 0.5 mm  E_f = 70 GPa  rho_f = 2700 kg per m3"]
    end
    subgraph ADH["ADHESIVE BONDLINE 0.1 mm each"]
        nD1["Epoxy film adhesive tau max 12 MPa"]
        nD2["Epoxy film adhesive tau max 12 MPa"]
    end
    nA --- nD1
    nD1 --- nB
    nB --- nD2
    nD2 --- nC
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — NVH Energy Path Through a Sandwich Panel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["EXTERNAL ACOUSTIC EXCITATION p_in"]:::inp --> B["FACE SHEET 1 Vibration drives the face"]
    B --> C{"CORE DEFORMATION MECHANISM"}
    C -->|Shear strain| D["VISCOELASTIC HEAT DISSIPATION eta_G"]
    C -->|Cell friction| E["ACOUSTIC ABSORPTION alpha_c"]
    C -->|Porous flow| F["THERMAL DISSIPATION via air movement"]
    D --> G["FACE SHEET 2 Lower-amplitude re-radiation"]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H["RADIATED SOUND p_out SPL drop 5 to 10 dB A"]:::out
    classDef inp fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0044aa,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Bending Moment vs Shear Force Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph B["BENDING MOMENT M carried by FACES sigma_f = M y over I"]
        nB1["Top face tension plus compression"]
        nB2["Bottom face tension plus compression"]
    end
    subgraph S["SHEAR FORCE V carried by CORE tau_c = V over A_core"]
        nS1["Core in pure shear G_c gamma = tau"]
    end
    B --- S
    nB1 -->|Lever arm d| nB2
```

> [!TIP]
> This is the single most important *mental model* to draw during a 14-mark derivation question. The faces carry $M$, the core carries $V$, and the core's distance $d$ from the faces amplifies the bending rigidity quadratically.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Q1.** *Define sandwich construction. List any two core materials used in automotive NVH applications with one property of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A sandwich construction is a three-layer structural composite comprising two thin, stiff, high-strength face sheets bonded to a thick, lightweight, low-modulus core. In NVH it is used as a passive damping and acoustic-insulation element.
- **Core materials (1 mark each, pick two):**
  - *Polyurethane (PU) foam* — loss factor $\eta \approx 0.20$, excellent damping.
  - *Aluminium honeycomb* — very high specific stiffness, low density (50–100 kg/m³).
  - *Nomex (aramid) honeycomb* — fire-resistant, used in aircraft floor panels.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** *Explain the term "coincidence frequency" of a panel. Why is it undesirable in NVH?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The coincidence (or critical) frequency $f_c$ is the frequency at which the phase velocity of a free bending wave in the panel matches the speed of sound in air. Below $f_c$, the panel is "acoustically short" and inefficiently radiates sound (high TL); at and above $f_c$, the panel radiates very efficiently, causing a sharp dip in transmission loss called the **coincidence dip**.
- **Why undesirable (1.5 marks):** The dip can be 10–20 dB deep and often falls in the 200–1000 Hz speech band, dramatically worsening cabin NVH. Designers push $f_c$ above the band of interest by increasing $D$ (thicker faces, stiffer core).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, ESE]**

#### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose either A or B.*

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction of a sandwich panel. List any **five failure modes** of a sandwich panel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the bending stiffness $D$ of a symmetric sandwich panel and explain how each term contributes to the total stiffness. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question A (a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A sandwich panel consists of:
1. **Top face sheet** — thin (0.1–1 mm), high-modulus material such as aluminium, steel, GFRP or CFRP; carries bending stresses.
2. **Core** — thick (5–50 mm), low-density material such as PU foam, aluminium honeycomb, or end-grain balsa; carries transverse shear and provides damping.
3. **Bottom face sheet** — identical to top face; together they form a symmetric "I-beam" cross-section.
4. **Adhesive bondlines** — typically epoxy film, ~0.1 mm thick; transfer shear between face and core.

**Sketch (2 marks) — Reproduce this ASCII diagram in the answer booklet:**

```
+-------------------------+   ← y = +h/2
|        TOP FACE         |   t_f (aluminium)
+-------------------------+   ← y = +t_c/2
|                         |
|       CORE (foam/       |   t_c (PU/honeycomb)
|       honeycomb)        |
|                         |
+-------------------------+   ← y = -t_c/2
|       BOTTOM FACE       |   t_f (aluminium)
+-------------------------+   ← y = -h/2
          |←—— d ———→|
```

**Five Failure Modes (2 marks — 0.4 each):**
1. Face yielding (tensile/compressive).
2. Core shear failure.
3. Face wrinkling (local buckling into core).
4. Core crushing/indentation.
5. Debonding (delamination at adhesive layer).

---

**Model Solution — Question A (b) — Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Define bending stiffness for a plate:

$$
D = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} E(z)\, z^{2}\, dz
$$

**Step 2:** Split into 3 layers; for a symmetric panel with thin faces ($t_f \ll t_c$):

$$
D = \underbrace{\frac{E_f t_f (t_c + t_f)^{2}}{2}}_{\text{face contribution}} + \underbrace{\frac{E_c t_c^{3}}{12}}_{\text{core contribution}}
$$

**Step 3:** Since $d = t_c + t_f$ and $t_f \ll t_c$, $d \approx t_c$:

$$
\boxed{\,D \;\approx\; \frac{E_f t_f d^{2}}{2} + \frac{E_c t_c^{3}}{12}\,}
$$

**Contribution analysis (2 marks):**
- The **first term** scales with $d^2$ — the lever arm. Doubling the core thickness **quadruples** the face contribution, which is why thick cores are so efficient.
- The **second term** scales with $t_c^3$ and $E_c$. For low-modulus foam cores ($E_c \ll E_f$), this term is often 1–3 % of the total and can be neglected in design — but for honeycomb cores it is significant.
- For a typical PU-foam / aluminium-faced panel, the face term contributes **~98 %** of $D$.

---

#### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative choice.*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **coincidence frequency** of a sandwich panel and explain its importance in NVH design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel-faced ($\rho_f = 7850$ kg/m³, $E_f = 200$ GPa, $t_f = 0.4$ mm) sandwich panel with a PVC foam core ($\rho_c = 60$ kg/m³, $E_c = 0.1$ GPa, $t_c = 25$ mm) is used as a vehicle dash panel. Calculate (i) the bending stiffness $D$, (ii) the areal mass $m$, (iii) the coincidence frequency $f_c$, and (iv) the mass-law transmission loss at 1 kHz. Comment on whether 1 kHz is above or below the coincidence region. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question B (a):**

**Derivation of $f_c$ (4 marks):**

The coincidence frequency is the frequency at which the **bending-wave phase velocity** in the panel equals the **speed of sound in air** $c_0$.

Bending-wave phase velocity in a plate:

$$
c_{b} = \sqrt{\omega} \cdot \left( \frac{D}{m} \right)^{1/4}
$$

At coincidence, $c_b = c_0$, so:

$$
c_0^{2} = \omega \sqrt{\frac{D}{m}}
$$

Substituting $\omega = 2\pi f_c$:

$$
c_0^{2} = 2\pi f_c \sqrt{\frac{D}{m}}
$$

Solving for $f_c$:

$$
\boxed{\,f_c = \frac{c_0^{2}}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{m}{D}}\,}
$$

**NVH importance (3 marks):**
- Below $f_c$: the panel is **mass-controlled** — TL follows mass law and rises 6 dB/octave with frequency; good sound insulation.
- At $f_c$: the **coincidence dip** appears — TL can drop 10–20 dB because structural and acoustic waves couple efficiently, allowing the panel to radiate sound as a "loudspeaker".
- Above $f_c$: TL recovers, but at a slower rate (~6 dB/oct still, but starting from a deep dip).
- **Design rule:** $f_c$ should be **pushed above the speech band** (≈ 4 kHz) for cabin panels, achieved by increasing $D$ (thicker faces, stiffer cores) or reducing $m$.

---

**Model Solution — Question B (b):**

**Given:** $E_f = 200 \times 10^{9}$ Pa, $t_f = 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $E_c = 0.1 \times 10^{9}$ Pa, $t_c = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\rho_f = 7850$ kg/m³, $\rho_c = 60$ kg/m³, $c_0 = 343$ m/s, $f = 1000$ Hz.

**(i) Bending stiffness $D$ (2.5 marks):**

$$
d = t_c + t_f = 0.0254 \text{ m}
$$

$$
D_1 = \frac{E_f t_f d^{2}}{2} = \frac{200\times 10^{9} \times 0.4\times 10^{-3} \times (0.0254)^{2}}{2}
$$

$$
= \frac{200\times 10^{9} \times 0.4\times 10^{-3} \times 6.452\times 10^{-4}}{2} = \frac{51\,614}{2} = 25\,807\,\text{N·m}
$$

$$
D_2 = \frac{E_c t_c^{3}}{12} = \frac{0.1\times 10^{9} \times (0.025)^{3}}{12} = \frac{0.1 \times 10^{9} \times 1.5625\times 10^{-5}}{12} = 130.2\,\text{N·m}
$$

$$
D = 25\,807 + 130.2 = 25\,937 \approx 25.9\,\text{kN·m}
$$

**(ii) Areal mass $m$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
m = 2 \rho_f t_f + \rho_c t_c = 2(7850)(0.0004) + (60)(0.025) = 6.28 + 1.50 = 7.78\,\text{kg/m}^{2}
$$

**(iii) Coincidence frequency $f_c$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
f_c = \frac{(343)^{2}}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{7.78}{25\,937}} = \frac{117\,649}{6.2832} \times \sqrt{3.00 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
= 18\,727.2 \times 0.01732 = 324.3\,\text{Hz}
$$

**(iv) Mass-law TL at 1 kHz (1 mark):**

$$
TL = 20 \log_{10}(m f) - 47 = 20 \log_{10}(7.78 \times 1000) - 47
$$

$$
= 20 \log_{10}(7780) - 47 = 20 \times 3.891 - 47 = 77.82 - 47 = 30.82\,\text{dB}
$$

**Comment (0.5 marks):** Since $f = 1000$ Hz $\gg f_c = 324$ Hz, the panel is **well above coincidence**. The mass-law formula is technically no longer valid at 1 kHz; the **coincidence dip** near 324 Hz will cause a real-world TL ~5–10 dB lower than predicted. The designer should either thicken the face or use a stiffer CFRP face to push $f_c$ above 1 kHz.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in Sandwich Construction questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the face contribution: writing $D = E_f t_f d^{2}/2$ as $E_f t_f d^{2}/4$. The 2 comes from the *two* faces. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 2. **Using $t_c$ instead of $d = t_c + t_f$** in the lever-arm term. Always use the face-to-face distance, not the core thickness. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 3. **Neglecting the core term** in $D$ even when the core is honeycomb (stiff). Always include both terms and *then* comment on their relative magnitude. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 4. **Confusing coincidence frequency with resonance frequency.** $f_c$ is acoustic-structural coupling; $f_{mn}$ is structural modal frequency. They are *different* quantities and have *different* formulas. **Lose 2–3 marks.**
> 5. **Applying mass law above coincidence** — mass law is valid only for $f < f_c$. State this explicitly. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 6. **Units mismatch** in $D$ (often written as N·m², should be N·m per unit width). **Lose 1 mark.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Sandwich = **2 faces + 1 core**; faces carry moment, core carries shear.
- ✅ Bending stiffness: $\;D = \dfrac{E_f t_f d^{2}}{2} + \dfrac{E_c t_c^{3}}{12}\;$ — **2-term formula, learn it cold**.
- ✅ Thin-face limit: face term dominates (~98 % for foam cores).
- ✅ Areal mass: $\;m = 2 \rho_f t_f + \rho_c t_c\;$.
- ✅ Coincidence freq: $\;f_c = \dfrac{c_0^{2}}{2\pi}\sqrt{m/D}\;$ — high $D$ pushes $f_c$ up.
- ✅ Mass law: $\;TL = 20\log_{10}(mf) - 47\;$ dB — valid **only** below $f_c$.
- ✅ 5 failure modes: face yield, core shear, face wrinkling, core crushing, debonding.
- ✅ Damping ($\eta$) — single most important NVH metric; foam cores give $\eta \approx 0.2$, honeycomb only $\approx 0.01$.
- ✅ Doubling $\eta$ ≈ 3–5 dB(A) interior SPL drop ≈ perceived halving of loudness.
- ✅ Push $f_c$ **above 1 kHz** for cabin panels, **above 3 kHz** for aircraft cabin.
- ✅ Sketch: always draw a **labelled cross-section** in the answer; examiners reward it.
- ✅ KTU-style: derive, then *interpret each term physically* — that's where the easy marks lie.
- ✅ Final tip: if a numerical answer gives $f_c < 500$ Hz, immediately add a line: *"This is below the speech band, hence the panel needs $D$ increased to push $f_c$ above 1 kHz."* — this comment alone is worth **1 bonus mark** in valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vibration Absorbers and Isolators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Vibration Absorbers and Isolators ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Vibration Isolator — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Vibration Isolator (KTU 2024 Definition):** A flexible support element (spring, elastomer, rubber mount, fluid mount, or air spring) intentionally inserted between a vibrating source and the structure (or between a sensitive receiver and a vibrating floor) such that the **transmissibility of force or motion** from one side to the other is reduced to acceptable levels at the operating frequency. The isolated system operates in the **isolation region**, defined by the frequency ratio $r = \omega/\omega_n > \sqrt{2}$, where $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ is the natural frequency of the supported mass on the isolator, and $\omega$ is the forcing (disturbance) frequency. ### 1.2 Vibration Absorber — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vibration Absorber (KTU 2024 Definition):** An auxiliary **secondary mass-spring (and sometimes damper) subsystem** rigidly attached to the **primary vibrating system** to absorb, store, and dissipate vibrational energy so that the primary mass becomes essentially stationary at the tuned excitation frequency. The classical device is the **Frahm undamped tuned absorber** (1909); modern variants include **damped dynamic absorbers**, **centrifugal pendulum absorbers**, and **active absorbers**. **Key conceptual contrast** (commonly tested as a 3-mark question): | Feature | Isolator | Absorber | |---|---|---| | Location | Between source & receiver | Attached directly to the vibrating body | | Function | **Reduces** transmitted force/motion | **Cancels** vibration at the source mass | | Tuning | Designed for $r > \sqrt{2}$ | Tuned so $\omega_a = \omega_{forcing}$ | | Energy path | Reflects/absorbs energy at boundary | Re-routes energy into the secondary mass | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition **Isolator analogy:** Imagine a heavy machine sitting on four rubber pads on a concrete floor. When the machine's motor runs, the rubber pads squish and stretch instead of letting the shaking travel directly into the floor. The rubber "softens" the connection so the neighbour in the room below does not feel the vibration. This is **isolation** — we use a soft mount to *decouple* two bodies. **Absorber analogy:** Now imagine the same machine has a small heavy pendulum hanging from its frame. When the motor tries to shake the frame, the pendulum swings in exactly the opposite phase at exactly the same frequency. The two motions cancel out, and the frame stays almost still. The pendulum *absorbs* the kinetic energy. This is a **dynamic vibration absorber** — we attach a tuned secondary system so that the primary system's motion vanishes. > [!TIP] > **Memory trick for exams:** "**Isolator isolates the path; Absorber absorbs the motion.**" ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard acceleration of gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - **Transmissibility (TR):** Dimensionless ratio of transmitted-to-excited amplitude, dimensionless - **Frequency ratio:** $r = \omega/\omega_n$, dimensionless - **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$, dimensionless - **Mass ratio:** $\mu = m_2/m_1$, dimensionless (absorber) - **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n$ in rad/s; $f_n = \omega_n/(2\pi)$ in Hz ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Vibration Isolation — Operational Logic A single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) system of mass $m$, stiffness $k$, and damping $c$ is excited by a harmonic force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ transmitted through an isolator of stiffness $k$ and damping $c$ to a rigid foundation. **Why does isolation occur?** The isolator acts as a low-pass mechanical filter. At low frequencies ($r < \sqrt{2}$), the motion is amplified (resonance peak near $r=1$). At high frequencies ($r > \sqrt{2}$), the isolator stiffness is so compliant relative to the inertial forces of $m$ that the force that *can* be transmitted is small. Mathematically, **TR drops monotonically as $r$ increases** beyond $\sqrt{2}$. **How is isolation quantified?** Through the **force transmissibility** $T_R = F_T/F_0$ or **motion transmissibility** $X/Y$ where $Y$ is the foundation's motion. The standard textbook form is: $$ T_R \;=\; \frac{F_T}{F_0} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1+(2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} $$ **Design Rule for an isolator (high-yield exam fact):** > [!IMPORTANT] > For **best isolation**, choose the lowest possible $\omega_n$ (softest mount) and the lowest practical $\zeta$ (damping mainly to limit resonance amplification, typically $\zeta \approx 0.05$–$0.20$ for rubber, $\zeta \approx 0$ for steel springs). The isolator stiffness is set by **static deflection** $\delta_{st} = mg/k$, and $\omega_n = \sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$. ### 2.2 Undamped Dynamic Vibration Absorber (Frahm Absorber) A primary SDOF system ($m_1, k_1$) is excited by $F \sin(\omega t)$. A secondary SDOF ($m_2, k_2$) is attached to $m_1$ via the spring $k_2$ (no damper in the undamped case). **Why does it work?** At steady state, $m_2$ oscillates such that the spring force $k_2(x_2 - x_1)$ exerted *on* $m_1$ exactly cancels the external force $F$. Since $m_1$ is then free of net force, it remains stationary ($X_1 = 0$). All energy is traded between $m_2$ and $k_2$ as a pure oscillation. **How to tune?** Choose $k_2$ and $m_2$ such that the **absorber natural frequency equals the excitation frequency**: $$ \omega_a \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{k_2}{m_2}} \;=\; \omega $$ The **mass ratio** $\mu = m_2/m_1$ governs how strongly the absorber can pull the primary. As $\mu$ grows, the operating bandwidth (over which $X_1$ remains acceptably small) widens. The two natural frequencies of the combined system are: $$ \omega_{1,2}^2 \;=\; \frac{\omega_1^2}{2}\left[ (1+\mu) \mp \sqrt{(1+\mu)^2 - 4\mu\left(\frac{\omega_a}{\omega_1}\right)^2} \right] $$ where $\omega_1 = \sqrt{k_1/m_1}$ is the primary natural frequency and $\omega_a$ is the absorber frequency. At exact tuning ($\omega_a = \omega$, the forcing frequency, and the tuned point), the two split frequencies simplify. ### 2.3 Damped Dynamic Vibration Absorber Real absorbers must include damping $c_2$ on the secondary mass to (a) limit the **beat phenomenon** (large $X_1$ when forcing drifts off tuning), and (b) absorb transient energy. **The optimum damping ratio of the absorber** (Den Hartog's result, very high-yield): $$ \zeta_{2,opt} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{3\mu}{8(1+\mu)^3}} $$ At this optimum, the **maximum amplitude of the primary mass over the entire frequency sweep** is minimised. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Cheat Sheet for Module 3 — Vibration Absorbers & Isolators (KTU 2024 Scheme)** | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Frequency ratio | $r = \omega/\omega_n$ | dimensionless | $\omega$ rad/s, $\omega_n$ rad/s | | 2 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ | dimensionless | $c$ N·s/m, $k$ N/m, $m$ kg | | 3 | Mass ratio | $\mu = m_2/m_1$ | dimensionless | absorber only | | 4 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | or $\omega_n = \sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$ | | 5 | **Force transmissibility** | $T_R = \sqrt{(1+(2\zeta r)^2)/((1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2)}$ | dimensionless | identical to motion TR | | 6 | Isolation boundary | $r = \sqrt{2}$ | dimensionless | TR = 1 at this point (undamped) | | 7 | Undamped TR | $T_R = 1/\vert 1-r^2 \vert$ | dimensionless | set $\zeta = 0$ | | 8 | Static deflection | $\delta_{st} = mg/k$ | m | governs mount softness | | 9 | Absorber tuning | $\omega_a = \sqrt{k_2/m_2}$ | rad/s | set equal to $\omega$ forcing | | 10 | Primary amplitude at exact tuning | $X_1 = 0$ | m | undamped, infinite in theory | | 11 | Absorber amplitude (exact tune) | $X_2 = F/k_2$ | m | undamped Frahm | | 12 | Combined natural frequencies | $\omega_{1,2}^2 = \tfrac{\omega_1^2}{2}\!\left[(1+\mu) \mp \sqrt{(1+\mu)^2 - 4\mu(\omega_a/\omega_1)^2}\right]$ | rad²/s² | when $\omega_a = \omega_1$, $\omega_{1,2} = \omega_1\sqrt{(1+\mu\mp\sqrt{\mu})/(1)}$ approx | | 13 | Den Hartog optimum $\zeta_2$ | $\zeta_{2,opt} = \sqrt{3\mu/(8(1+\mu)^3)}$ | dimensionless | damped absorber | | 14 | Centrifugal pendulum length | $l = g/(\omega^2 \cos\alpha)$ | m | tuned at any speed | | 15 | Logarithmic decrement | $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | dimensionless | small $\zeta$ approx | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive engine mounts:** Rubber/elastomer isolators decoupling engine NVH from chassis (passenger cars target isolation above ~25 Hz idle). - **HVAC chiller bases:** Spring isolators ($f_n \approx 3$–$5$ Hz) under 50/60 Hz compressor forcing — well inside isolation region. - **Tuned Mass Dampers (TMD):** Taipei 101 ($660$ tonne steel sphere), Citicorp Center, Millennium Bridge retrofits — civil engineering. - **Centrifugal Pendulum Absorbers (CPA):** Used in Formula 1 engines and Mercedes-Benz ABC balancer shafts to cancel second-order engine torque pulsations across the entire RPM range (auto-tuning). - **Aerospace equipment racks:** Avionics mounted on elastomer isolators to survive rocket launch vibration spectra. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Force Transmissibility for a Damped Isolator **System description.** A rigid mass $m$ is mounted on a spring $k$ and damper $c$ in parallel. The base is excited by displacement $y(t) = Y \sin(\omega t)$. The mass absolute displacement is $x(t)$ measured from the static equilibrium position. The transmitted force to the foundation is the force exerted by the spring and damper on the base. **Step 1 — Equation of motion.** Using Newton's law on the mass, with $z = x - y$ as relative displacement: $$ m \ddot{x} + c(\dot{x} - \dot{y}) + k(x - y) = 0 $$ Equivalently, in terms of relative motion $z$: $$ m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{y} $$ **Step 2 — Harmonic assumption.** Let $y = Y \sin(\omega t)$, so $\ddot{y} = -\omega^2 Y \sin(\omega t)$. The forcing term becomes $m \omega^2 Y \sin(\omega t)$. Steady-state solution $z(t) = Z \sin(\omega t - \phi)$. **Step 3 — Magnitude of relative displacement.** Substituting and equating magnitudes: $$ Z \;=\; \frac{m\omega^2 Y}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}} $$ Divide numerator and denominator by $k$ and introduce $r = \omega/\omega_n$, $\omega_n^2 = k/m$, $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$, $2\zeta r = c\omega/k$: $$ \frac{Z}{Y} \;=\; \frac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} $$ **Step 4 — Identify transmissibility.** The *force* transmitted to the base is $F_T = k z + c \dot{z}$. Its magnitude is: $$ F_T \;=\; Y \sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{k} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}} $$ After algebraic simplification (force transmitted = impedance of mount × base velocity, expressed as ratio), we obtain: $$ T_R \;=\; \frac{F_T}{F_0} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} $$ where $F_0 = m\omega^2 Y$ is the inertial force amplitude. This is the **canonical textbook expression** and is the same as the *motion transmissibility* $X/Y$ for a base-excited SDOF. **Step 5 — Undamped special case.** Set $\zeta = 0$: $$ T_R \;\to\; \frac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert} $$ For $r > 1$, this becomes $T_R = 1/(r^2 - 1)$, which decays monotonically — pure isolation. **Step 6 — Isolation boundary.** For undamped isolator, $T_R = 1$ at $r = \sqrt{2}$. For damped isolator, the minimum TR point is shifted, but $r > \sqrt{2}$ is the textbook *isolation region*. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Steady-State Response of an Undamped Vibration Absorber (Frahm) **System description.** Primary mass $m_1$ on spring $k_1$, excited by $F \sin(\omega t)$. Absorber mass $m_2$ attached to $m_1$ by spring $k_2$. Both displacements measured from static equilibrium of $m_1$. **Step 1 — Equations of motion.** For $m_1$ and $m_2$ respectively: $$ m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + k_1 x_1 + k_2 (x_1 - x_2) \;=\; F \sin(\omega t) $$ $$ m_2 \ddot{x}_2 + k_2 (x_2 - x_1) \;=\; 0 $$ **Step 2 — Harmonic steady-state ansatz.** Assume: $$ x_1(t) = X_1 \sin(\omega t), \qquad x_2(t) = X_2 \sin(\omega t) $$ Substituting $\ddot{x}_i = -\omega^2 x_i$: $$ (-m_1 \omega^2 + k_1 + k_2) X_1 - k_2 X_2 \;=\; F $$ $$ -k_2 X_1 + (-m_2 \omega^2 + k_2) X_2 \;=\; 0 $$ **Step 3 — Solve linear system.** From the second equation: $$ X_2 \;=\; \frac{k_2 X_1}{k_2 - m_2 \omega^2} $$ Substitute into the first: $$ (-m_1 \omega^2 + k_1 + k_2) X_1 - \frac{k_2^2 X_1}{k_2 - m_2 \omega^2} \;=\; F $$ $$ X_1 \left[ (k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega^2) - \frac{k_2^2}{k_2 - m_2 \omega^2} \right] \;=\; F $$ **Step 4 — Combine the bracket over a common denominator.** Multiply top by $(k_2 - m_2 \omega^2)$: $$ X_1 \cdot \frac{(k_1 + k_2 - m_1\omega^2)(k_2 - m_2 \omega^2) - k_2^2}{k_2 - m_2 \omega^2} \;=\; F $$ Expand the numerator: $$ N \;=\; (k_1 + k_2)(k_2 - m_2 \omega^2) - m_1 \omega^2 (k_2 - m_2 \omega^2) - k_2^2 $$ $$ N \;=\; (k_1 + k_2) k_2 - (k_1 + k_2) m_2 \omega^2 - m_1 k_2 \omega^2 + m_1 m_2 \omega^4 - k_2^2 $$ The $(k_1+k_2)k_2 - k_2^2 = k_1 k_2$ terms collapse: $$ N \;=\; k_1 k_2 - (k_1 m_2 + m_1 k_2 + k_2 m_2) \omega^2 + m_1 m_2 \omega^4 $$ So: $$ X_1 \;=\; \frac{F (k_2 - m_2 \omega^2)}{k_1 k_2 - (k_1 m_2 + k_1 m_2 + m_1 k_2) \omega^2 + m_1 m_2 \omega^4} $$ **Step 5 — Apply the tuning condition.** Set $\omega^2 = k_2/m_2$ (i.e., $\omega = \omega_a$). Then the numerator $(k_2 - m_2\omega^2) = 0$, hence: $$ X_1 \;\to\; 0 $$ This is the **Frahm absorber result**: at exact tuning, the primary mass has zero steady-state amplitude. The absorber mass amplitude becomes $X_2 = F/k_2$. **Step 6 — Combined-system natural frequencies.** Setting $F=0$ and solving the determinant of the coefficient matrix: $$ (k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega^2)(k_2 - m_2 \omega^2) - k_2^2 \;=\; 0 $$ Expanding gives a quadratic in $\omega^2$: $$ m_1 m_2 \omega^4 - (k_1 m_2 + k_1 m_2 + m_1 k_2) \omega^2 + k_1 k_2 \;=\; 0 $$ Using $\omega_1^2 = k_1/m_1$ and $\omega_a^2 = k_2/m_2$, divide by $m_1 m_2$: $$ \omega^4 - (\omega_1^2 + \omega_a^2 + \mu \omega_a^2) \omega^2 + \omega_1^2 \omega_a^2 \;=\; 0 $$ Wait — redo carefully. Divide through by $m_1 m_2$: $$ \omega^4 - \left(\frac{k_1}{m_1} + \frac{k_2}{m_1} + \frac{k_2}{m_2}\right)\omega^2 + \frac{k_1 k_2}{m_1 m_2} \;=\; 0 $$ Now $\frac{k_2}{m_1} = \frac{m_2}{m_1} \cdot \frac{k_2}{m_2} = \mu \omega_a^2$, and $\frac{k_1}{m_1} = \omega_1^2$, so: $$ \omega^4 - \left[\omega_1^2 + (1+\mu)\omega_a^2\right]\omega^2 + \omega_1^2 \omega_a^2 \;=\; 0 $$ Solving via the quadratic formula with $u = \omega^2$: $$ \omega_{1,2}^2 \;=\; \frac{\omega_1^2 + (1+\mu)\omega_a^2}{2} \;\mp\; \sqrt{\left[\frac{\omega_1^2 + (1+\mu)\omega_a^2}{2}\right]^2 - \omega_1^2 \omega_a^2} $$ Equivalent compact form used in textbooks: $$ \omega_{1,2}^2 \;=\; \frac{\omega_1^2}{2}\left[(1+\mu) \mp \sqrt{(1+\mu)^2 - 4\mu \left(\frac{\omega_a}{\omega_1}\right)^2}\right] $$ **Step 7 — Two-mode interpretation.** When the absorber is tuned so $\omega_a = \omega$ (forcing), the two natural frequencies split symmetrically about $\omega$ on a log scale only when $\mu$ is small. The amplitude $X_1(\omega)$ therefore has **two resonance peaks** straddling the forcing frequency — this is why a damped absorber is essential in practice. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Device | Disturbance type | Best device | Reason | |---|---|---| | Broad-band random (e.g., road input) | **Damped isolator** | $T_R < 1$ across the entire isolation region | | Pure-tone harmonic (e.g., 1× running imbalance) | **Undamped tuned absorber** | exact cancellation at one frequency | | Slowly varying harmonic (engine RPM sweep) | **Centrifugal pendulum absorber** | auto-tunes via $l = g/(\omega^2 \cos\alpha)$ | | Multi-frequency disturbance | **Multiple TMDs in series** or **active absorber** | each mode addresses one peak | | Sensitive instrument on vibrating floor | **Low-$f_n$ air-spring isolator** | $r \gg \sqrt{2}$ at low forcing frequencies | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Vibration Isolator Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SRC[VIBRATION SOURCE] A1[Engine / Motor / Compressor] end SRC --> B1[Mount Base Plate] B1 --> C1[Isolator Element
Spring plus Damper
k and c] C1 --> D1[Equipment Mass
m] C1 -.Force F sub T.-> E1[Foundation or Floor] D1 --> F1[Sensor: Accelerometer] F1 -.Feedback.-> G1[NVH Analyst] style A1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333 style C1 fill:#9CC3E5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D1 fill:#B5E8B5,stroke:#333 style E1 fill:#D9D9D9,stroke:#333 ``` **Reading the diagram.** The isolator element (highlighted blue) sits **between** the source and the foundation. Its job is to make the force $F_T$ transmitted to the foundation as small as possible at the forcing frequency. ### 4.2 Vibration Absorber Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PRIM[PRIMARY SYSTEM] P1[Base Excitation
F sin omega t] P2[Primary Mass m1] P3[Primary Spring k1] P1 --> P2 P3 --> P2 end P2 --> Q1[Coupling Spring
k2 connects m1 to m2] subgraph ABS[ABSORBER SUBSYSTEM] Q2[Absorber Mass m2] Q3[Absorber Damper c2
optional] end Q1 --> Q2 Q3 --> Q2 style P2 fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#333 style Q2 fill:#B5E8B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Q1 fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333 style Q3 fill:#C7B8EA,stroke:#333 ``` **Reading the diagram.** The absorber mass $m_2$ (green) is **attached to** the primary mass $m_1$ (red). The coupling spring $k_2$ (yellow) transmits a force to $m_1$ that exactly cancels $F$ when tuned. Damping $c_2$ (purple) is optional but strongly recommended in practice. ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow of NVH Vibration Control ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Identify Forcing Frequencies] --> B{Is the source narrow
band or broad band?} B -- Narrow band --> C[Tune Absorber
omega a equals omega] B -- Broad band --> D[Choose Isolator
omega n much less than omega] C --> E[Validate by FRF Test
X1 versus frequency] D --> E E --> F{TR target met?} F -- Yes --> G[Release to Production] F -- No --> H[Iterate Stiffness or Mass] H --> B style A fill:#9CC3E5,stroke:#333 style C fill:#B5E8B5,stroke:#333 style D fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333 style E fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333 style G fill:#C7EBC1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Reading the diagram.** Decision tree for the NVH engineer: identify the forcing spectrum, then choose between an isolator (broadband) or an absorber (narrowband), validate with a Frequency Response Function (FRF) test, and iterate until the transmissibility target is met. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]** Distinguish clearly between a **vibration isolator** and a **vibration absorber**. State one practical application of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Vibration Isolator | Vibration Absorber | |---|---|---| | Function | Reduces the **transmitted force/motion** between source and receiver by interposing a flexible mount | Reduces the **vibration amplitude of a primary mass** by attaching a secondary tuned mass-spring system | | Operating principle | Operates in the **isolation region** ($r > \sqrt{2}$) where $T_R < 1$ | Operates at the **tuning frequency** where the secondary system's reaction force cancels the excitation on the primary mass | | Application | Rubber engine mounts in a car; spring mounts under an HVAC chiller | Tuned mass damper in a tall building (e.g., Taipei 101); centrifugal pendulum absorber in F1 engines | (Marking split: 1 mark distinction table summary, 1 mark principle, 1 mark application — awarding scheme follows KTU's structured 3-mark format.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define **transmissibility** for a vibration isolation system. Show that for an undamped isolator the transmissibility equals $1/\vert 1 - r^2 \vert$ and state the isolation boundary. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Transmissibility** is the ratio of the force (or motion) transmitted to the foundation to the excitation force (or motion). For a damped SDOF base-excited system: $$ T_R = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} $$ Setting $\zeta = 0$ gives $T_R = 1/\vert 1 - r^2 \vert$. The **isolation boundary** is $r = \sqrt{2}$ (i.e., $\omega > \sqrt{2}\,\omega_n$); for $r$ above this, $T_R < 1$ and isolation is achieved. (1 mark definition, 1.5 marks derivation reference, 0.5 mark boundary.) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]` **(a)** Derive the equation of motion of an SDOF system mounted on a damped isolator with base excitation $y(t) = Y \sin(\omega t)$. Hence obtain the force transmissibility expression: $$ T_R = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} $$ **(7 marks)** **(b)** A precision weighing balance of mass **12 kg** is to be isolated from floor vibrations dominated by a **40 Hz** component. Design an undamped isolator (find $k$) such that the transmissibility at 40 Hz is at most **0.10**. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Step 1.** Newton's second law for mass $m$ with absolute displacement $x(t)$ and base displacement $y(t)$: $$ m \ddot{x} + c(\dot{x} - \dot{y}) + k(x - y) = 0 $$ Let $z = x - y$ (relative displacement). Then $\ddot{z} = \ddot{x} - \ddot{y}$, so $\ddot{x} = \ddot{z} + \ddot{y}$, and the equation becomes: $$ m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{y} $$ For $y = Y \sin(\omega t)$, $\ddot{y} = -\omega^2 Y \sin(\omega t)$, hence RHS = $m\omega^2 Y \sin(\omega t)$. (1 mark) **Step 2.** Steady-state solution $z = Z \sin(\omega t - \phi)$. Substitute to get: $$ Z = \frac{m\omega^2 Y}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}} $$ Dividing by $Y$: $$ \frac{Z}{Y} = \frac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} $$ where $r = \omega/\omega_n$, $\omega_n^2 = k/m$, $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$. (2 marks) **Step 3.** The transmitted force to the base is $F_T = k z + c \dot{z}$, with magnitude $F_T = Z \sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$. After substitution and simplification: $$ T_R = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \frac{Z\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{m\omega^2 Y} $$ Insert $Z/Y$ from Step 2 and simplify: $$ T_R = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} $$ (2 marks for algebra; 2 marks for stating interpretation — isolation region, role of $\zeta$, etc.) --- **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Given:** $m = 12$ kg, $f = 40$ Hz $\Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi \times 40 = 251.327$ rad/s, $T_R \le 0.10$. **Step 1.** For an undamped isolator ($\zeta = 0$): $$ T_R = \frac{1}{r^2 - 1} \le 0.10 $$ (taking $r > 1$ so the absolute value becomes $r^2 - 1$). Solving: $$ r^2 - 1 \ge 10 \;\Rightarrow\; r^2 \ge 11 \;\Rightarrow\; r \ge \sqrt{11} \approx 3.317 $$ (2 marks) **Step 2.** $\omega_n = \omega/r = 251.327/3.317 = 75.77$ rad/s, hence $f_n = 12.06$ Hz. (1 mark) **Step 3.** Stiffness: $$ k = m \omega_n^2 = 12 \times (75.77)^2 = 12 \times 5741.1 = 68{,}893 \text{ N/m} \approx 68.9 \text{ kN/m} $$ (2 marks) **Step 4.** Static deflection check: $$ \delta_{st} = \frac{mg}{k} = \frac{12 \times 9.81}{68{,}893} = 1.708 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.71 \text{ mm} $$ (1 mark) **Step 5.** Design remark: at 40 Hz the operating $r = 3.32 > \sqrt{2}$, well inside the isolation region. Adding small damping ($\zeta \approx 0.05$–$0.10$) is recommended to control transient resonance when forcing sweeps through $f_n$. (1 mark) **Answer:** $k \approx 68.9$ kN/m with static deflection $\approx 1.71$ mm. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set C]` *(Alternative to Q-A)* **(a)** With the help of a clear schematic, derive the equations of motion of a primary SDOF system (mass $m_1$, spring $k_1$) to which an undamped dynamic vibration absorber (mass $m_2$, spring $k_2$) is attached. The primary is excited by $F(t) = F \sin(\omega t)$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A machine-tool structure of mass **50 kg** vibrates excessively due to a harmonic excitation of **1200 N** at **25 Hz**. Design an undamped vibration absorber (find $m_2$ and $k_2$) using a mass ratio of **0.20**. Verify that the steady-state amplitude of the primary mass becomes zero at the design frequency. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Schematic:** (Refer to SECTION 4.2 Mermaid block.) Two-degree-of-freedom system with coordinates $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$. (1 mark) **Equations of motion** using Newton's second law: For mass $m_1$: $$ m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + k_1 x_1 - k_2 (x_2 - x_1) = F \sin(\omega t) $$ Rearranging: $$ m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + (k_1 + k_2) x_1 - k_2 x_2 = F \sin(\omega t) $$ (2 marks) For mass $m_2$: $$ m_2 \ddot{x}_2 + k_2 (x_2 - x_1) = 0 $$ (2 marks) **Steady-state assumption:** $x_i = X_i \sin(\omega t)$. Substituting and equating coefficients yields the algebraic system shown in SECTION 3.2 (Steps 2–4). Setting the forcing frequency equal to the absorber frequency $\omega = \sqrt{k_2/m_2}$ yields $X_1 = 0$. (2 marks) --- **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Given:** $m_1 = 50$ kg, $F = 1200$ N, $f = 25$ Hz $\Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi \times 25 = 157.08$ rad/s, $\mu = 0.20$. **Step 1. Absorber mass:** $$ m_2 = \mu \cdot m_1 = 0.20 \times 50 = 10 \text{ kg} $$ (1 mark) **Step 2. Absorber stiffness (tuning condition):** $$ k_2 = m_2 \omega^2 = 10 \times (157.08)^2 = 10 \times 24{,}674 = 246{,}740 \text{ N/m} \approx 246.7 \text{ kN/m} $$ (2 marks) **Step 3. Verify primary amplitude at design frequency.** The undamped steady-state amplitude of the primary is: $$ X_1 = F \cdot \frac{k_2 - m_2 \omega^2}{\Delta(\omega)} $$ where $\Delta(\omega) = m_1 m_2 \omega^4 - (k_1 m_2 + k_2 m_1 + k_2 m_2)\omega^2 + k_1 k_2$. At tuning, $k_2 - m_2 \omega^2 = 0$, hence **numerator is zero**. Therefore $X_1 = 0$. (3 marks) **Step 4. Absorber amplitude at design frequency.** From $X_2 = k_2 X_1/(k_2 - m_2\omega^2)$, the limiting ratio $X_2 = F/k_2$: $$ X_2 = \frac{1200}{246{,}740} = 4.864 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 4.86 \text{ mm} $$ (1 mark) **Answer:** $m_2 = 10$ kg, $k_2 \approx 246.7$ kN/m. The absorber oscillates at $\pm 4.86$ mm while the primary stays still. --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls in KTU Valuation — Vibration Absorbers & Isolators (Module 3)** > > 1. **Confusing $T_R = 1/\vert 1 - r^2 \vert$ (undamped) with the full damped form.** Students often write $T_R = 1/(1-r^2)$ without absolute value, losing a mark if the question assumes $r<1$. > 2. **Writing "isolation occurs when $r > 1$".** This is **wrong by KTU convention**. The textbook isolation boundary is $r > \sqrt{2}$. Examiners specifically deduct for stating $r>1$. > 3. **Forgetting the coupling spring term $k_2(x_1 - x_2)$ in BOTH equations of motion** of the absorber. Symmetric action-reaction is mandatory; losing the sign loses full marks on sub-part (a). > 4. **Stating $X_1 = 0$ without showing the numerator vanishes** in the absorption derivation. The KTU key requires explicit cancellation: $k_2 - m_2\omega^2 = 0 \Rightarrow X_1 = 0$. > 5. **Mixing $\omega$ (forcing) with $\omega_n$ (natural) and $\omega_a$ (absorber).** Use distinct symbols and label them clearly in diagrams. > 6. **Forgetting the factor $g$ when computing static deflection** $\delta_{st} = mg/k$ in isolator design problems. > 7. **In damped absorber problems, not justifying the choice of $\zeta_{2,opt}$.** Mention Den Hartog's formula and the $\mu$-dependence. > 8. **Missing units.** KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit in numerical answers (e.g., "$k$ = 68.9 k" instead of "**68.9 kN/m**"). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Module 3 — Vibration Absorbers & Isolators: Rapid-Revision Checklist** - **Isolator vs Absorber:** Isolator = *between* source and receiver; Absorber = *attached to* the vibrating body. - **Transmissibility formula (master version):** $$ T_R = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} $$ - **Isolation region:** $r > \sqrt{2}$ for $\zeta \approx 0$; shift slightly upward with damping. - **Static-deflection rule of thumb:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$ — useful to *quickly* size mounts from a desired $f_n$. - **Undamped Frahm absorber:** tuned at $\omega_a = \sqrt{k_2/m_2} = \omega$ (forcing); primary amplitude $X_1 \to 0$. - **Mass ratio $\mu = m_2/m_1$** controls bandwidth — larger $\mu$ widens the effective absorption band. - **Damped absorber optimum (Den Hartog):** $\zeta_{2,opt} = \sqrt{3\mu/[8(1+\mu)^3]}$. - **Combined natural frequencies (undamped 2-DOF):** solutions of $\omega^4 - [\omega_1^2 + (1+\mu)\omega_a^2]\omega^2 + \omega_1^2 \omega_a^2 = 0$. - **CPA auto-tuning:** $l = g/(\omega^2 \cos\alpha)$ — unique property that tracks excitation frequency. - **Centrifugal pendulum length relation** holds for any shaft speed — the reason CPAs are used in I.C. engines. - **Design sequence for an isolator:** pick $T_R$ target → compute required $r$ → compute $\omega_n$ → compute $k = m\omega_n^2$ → compute $\delta_{st} = mg/k$ → verify it is feasible. - **Design sequence for an absorber:** identify forcing frequency $\omega$ → choose $\mu$ (typically 0.05–0.25) → compute $m_2$ → set $k_2 = m_2\omega^2$ → optionally size $c_2 = 2\zeta_{2,opt}\sqrt{k_2 m_2}$. - **Practical damping in isolators:** $\zeta \approx 0.05$–$0.20$ for elastomers; aim lower for steel springs. - **FRF validation:** measure $X_1/F$ vs frequency; target should show two well-separated peaks with the forcing frequency in the trough between them. - **Avoid beating:** without damping, the absorber creates large $X_1$ *near* but *not exactly at* tuning — Den Hartog damping cures this. - **KTU favourite 3-mark question:** "*Define transmissibility and derive the undamped form. State the isolation boundary.*" - **KTU favourite 14-mark question:** "*Design an isolator/absorber for given mass, frequency, and TR/amplitude target.*" Memorise the standard 5-step template.

topics.title: Noise Measurement and Control - Sources of Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Noise Measurement and Control - Sources of Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In the context of automotive and mechanical engineering NVH (Noise, Vibration, and Harshness), a **source of noise** is defined as any physical mechanism, component, sub-system, or excitation phenomenon that converts mechanical, fluid, thermal, or electromagnetic energy into acoustic energy (pressure fluctuations in a surrounding medium) perceptible or measurable within a defined frequency range and sound pressure level threshold.

As per **ISO 12001** and **SAE J1924** standards adopted in the KTU 2024 NVH curriculum, a noise source is characterized by three measurable attributes:
- **Sound Power Level (SWL or Lw)** — the total acoustic energy radiated per unit time
- **Frequency Spectrum** — distribution of acoustic energy across the frequency domain
- **Directivity Pattern** — spatial distribution of radiated sound around the source

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme Module 3 explicitly categorizes noise sources into **three primary domains**: *Mechanical/Structure-borne*, *Fluid/Aerodynamic*, and *Combustion/Thermodynamic*. Students must be able to map every real-world automotive noise to one of these domains for full marks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a small pond of still water. When you drop a pebble, ripples (waves) spread outward in concentric circles. The **pebble is the noise source**, the **water is the air medium**, and the **ripples are the sound waves**. Different pebbles (different sources) produce ripples of different sizes (amplitudes) and different spacing between crests (frequencies). Now imagine dropping many pebbles at once — a car engine is exactly this: a hundred "pebbles" (combustion, gears, bearings, valves, fan) all hitting the "water" simultaneously, creating a complex interference pattern that we perceive as the total vehicle noise.

### 1.3 Three-Domain Classification (KTU 2024 Mandate)

| Domain | Energy Conversion | Typical Sources | Dominant Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Kinetic → Acoustic | Gears, bearings, chains, belts, valves | 200 Hz – 10 kHz |
| **Aerodynamic** | Fluid kinetic → Acoustic | Fan, wind, HVAC, exhaust flow | 500 Hz – 8 kHz |
| **Combustion** | Chemical → Thermal → Acoustic | Engine cylinder pressure rise, knock | 30 Hz – 1 kHz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics** (used throughout KTU valuation):
> - Reference sound pressure: **p₀ = 20 μPa** (threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz)
> - Reference sound power: **W₀ = 10⁻¹² W** (picowatt)
> - Speed of sound in air at 20°C: **c = 343 m/s**
> - Air density at 20°C: **ρ = 1.204 kg/m³**
> - Characteristic acoustic impedance: **Z₀ = ρc ≈ 415 Pa·s/m**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchy of Noise Sources in an Automobile

Automotive noise sources are organized in a **Source-Path-Receiver (SPR) model** that is the foundational framework of all NVH engineering. KTU examiners frequently test this hierarchy.

**Step 1 — Primary Excitation Identification**
Identify the *root cause* of vibration or pressure fluctuation (e.g., combustion pressure, gear meshing, tire-road interaction).

**Step 2 — Source Characterization**
Quantify the source as either an *acoustic source* (direct sound radiator) or a *vibration source* (structure that radiates sound when excited).

**Step 3 — Path Identification**
Determine whether the energy travels through *air (airborne path)*, *structure (structure-borne path)*, or *fluid (fluid-borne path)*.

**Step 4 — Receiver Response**
Quantify the sound pressure at the listener's position (driver's ear, passenger cabin, roadside observer).

### 2.2 Detailed Sources in Each Domain

#### A. Mechanical/Structure-Borne Sources
1. **Engine Mechanical Noise** — piston slap, valve train, timing chain/belt, crankshaft, camshaft
2. **Transmission Noise** — gear meshing, bearing whine, synchronizer rattle, driveline clunk
3. **Tire/Road Noise** — tread impact, air pumping, road surface texture excitation
4. **Brake Noise (NVH Brake Squeal)** — rotor-disc interface friction-induced vibration
5. **Accessory Drive** — alternator whine, power steering pump, AC compressor

#### B. Aerodynamic Sources
1. **Wind Noise** — flow over A-pillar, side mirrors, windshield, sunroof
2. **HVAC Blower Noise** — fan blade passage frequency (BPF) tones
3. **Exhaust Flow Noise** — turbulent jet mixing, orifice flow
4. **Intake Noise** — air induction system resonances

#### C. Combustion Sources
1. **Engine Combustion Knock** — auto-ignition of end-gas
2. **Diesel Combustion Rattle** — rapid pressure rise in CI engines
3. **After-fire / Backfire** — unburnt fuel in exhaust

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level | dB re 20 μPa |
| 2 | $L_w = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right)$ | Sound Power Level | dB re 10⁻¹² W |
| 3 | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Sound Intensity Level | dB re 10⁻¹² W/m² |
| 4 | $I = \dfrac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho c}$ | Acoustic Intensity | W/m² |
| 5 | $L_w = L_p + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi r^2}{S_0}\right)$ | Free-field SWL from SPL | r in meters, S₀ = 1 m² |
| 6 | $L_p = L_w - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r}{r_0}\right) - 11$ | Inverse square law (free field) | dB; r₀ = 1 m |
| 7 | $f_{BPF} = \dfrac{N \cdot \Omega}{60}$ | Blade Passage Frequency | Hz, N=blades, Ω=RPM |
| 8 | $f_{gear} = \dfrac{N_1 \cdot f_{shaft}}{N_2}$ | Gear mesh frequency | Hz |
| 9 | $f_{comb} = \dfrac{n \cdot N}{60 \cdot \tau}$ | Engine combustion order | n=cyl, N=RPM, τ=strokes |
| 10 | $L_{A} = L_{C} + \Delta A(f)$ | A-weighting correction | dB(A) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** The factor is **10** for power/intensity ratios but **20** for pressure/amplitude ratios. Many KTU students lose 1–2 marks by misapplying this. Remember: pressure is an *amplitude*, power is proportional to *amplitude squared*.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
The Source-Path-Receiver framework is used by:
- **OEM NVH engineers** to assign noise reduction targets (e.g., −3 dB(A) at driver's ear)
- **Tier-1 suppliers** to benchmark components (e.g., tires, exhausts)
- **Regulatory bodies** for pass-by noise certification (ECE R51, ISO 362)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sound Power Level from Sound Pressure Level (Free Field)

We begin with the relationship between sound intensity, sound pressure, and the radial distance from a *point source* radiating into free space.

**Given relationships:**

$$
I = \frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho c}
$$

For a point source in free field, the total acoustic power passing through a sphere of radius $r$ surrounding the source is:

$$
W = I \cdot 4\pi r^{2}
$$

Substituting the first equation into the second:

$$
W = \frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho c} \cdot 4\pi r^{2}
$$

**Step A** — Divide both sides by the reference power $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W:

$$
\frac{W}{W_0} = \frac{4\pi r^{2}}{\rho c \, W_0} \cdot p_{rms}^{\,2}
$$

**Step B** — Take the base-10 logarithm of both sides:

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi r^{2}}{\rho c \, W_0}\right) + 2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) + 2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_0^{\,2}}{1}\right)
$$

Wait — let us normalize cleanly. Using $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ and $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W, and substituting numerical values for $\rho c \approx 415$ Pa·s/m:

**Step C** — Compute the constant term:

$$
\frac{4\pi r^{2}}{\rho c \, W_0} \cdot p_0^{\,2} = \frac{4\pi r^{2}}{415 \times 10^{-12}} \times (20 \times 10^{-6})^{2}
$$

$$
= \frac{4\pi r^{2} \times 4 \times 10^{-10}}{4.15 \times 10^{-10}} = 12.1 \, r^{2}
$$

Hmm — this gives us the correct numerical form. Re-stating the full equation:

$$
L_w = L_p + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi r^{2}}{S_0}\right) - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\rho c}{400}\right)
$$

where $S_0 = 1 \, \text{m}^2$. For standard air ($\rho c = 400$ Pa·s/m), the last term vanishes and we recover:

$$
L_w = L_p + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(4\pi r^{2}\right)
$$

$$
L_w = L_p + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(4\pi\right) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(r\right)
$$

$$
L_w = L_p + 10.99 + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(r\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,L_w = L_p + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(r\right) + 11\,}
$$

This is the KTU board-exam version of the formula.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Gear Mesh Noise Frequency

A pair of meshing gears with $N_1$ and $N_2$ teeth, rotating at $f_1$ and $f_2$ Hz respectively, produces a fundamental **gear mesh frequency (GMF)** at every tooth contact.

**Given:**
- Pinion rotational frequency: $f_1$ (Hz)
- Gear rotational frequency: $f_2$ (Hz)
- Number of teeth: $N_1$ (pinion), $N_2$ (gear)

**Step 1** — The number of tooth contacts per second on the pinion equals:

$$
\text{Contacts/sec} = N_1 \cdot f_1
$$

**Step 2** — By the gear ratio rule ($N_1 f_1 = N_2 f_2$):

$$
f_{GMF} = N_1 f_1 = N_2 f_2
$$

**Step 3** — Express in terms of pinion RPM ($\Omega_1$):

$$
f_{GMF} = N_1 \cdot \frac{\Omega_1}{60}
$$

**Step 4** — Higher-order harmonics (sidebands) appear at $n \cdot f_{GMF} \pm k \cdot f_{shaft}$ due to manufacturing errors and modulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "what is the noise frequency of a gear", the answer is the **mesh frequency**, NOT the shaft rotation frequency. Each tooth contact is a brief impact — that's the source.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Engine Combustion Order Frequency

For an $n$-cylinder, 4-stroke engine running at $N$ RPM:

$$
f_{\text{comb}} = \frac{n \cdot N}{60 \cdot \tau}
$$

where $\tau = 2$ for 4-stroke and $\tau = 1$ for 2-stroke.

**Example:** 4-cylinder, 4-stroke, 3000 RPM:

$$
f_{\text{comb}} = \frac{4 \times 3000}{60 \times 2} = 100 \, \text{Hz}
$$

This is the **2nd-order** firing frequency (one combustion every 180° of crank rotation).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Source-to-Frequency Mapping

| Source | Excitation Mechanism | Characteristic Frequency Formula | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engine firing | Combustion pressure pulse | $\dfrac{n \cdot N}{60 \cdot \tau}$ | 20 – 200 Hz |
| Gear mesh | Tooth contact impact | $N_g \cdot f_{\text{shaft}}$ | 200 – 4000 Hz |
| Tire/road | Tread element impact | $\dfrac{v}{\text{tread pitch}}$ | 100 – 1000 Hz |
| Fan BPF | Blade wake passage | $\dfrac{N_{\text{blade}} \cdot \Omega}{60}$ | 50 – 2000 Hz |
| Wind noise | Turbulent boundary layer | Broadband | 500 – 8000 Hz |
| Brake squeal | Friction-induced self-excitation | 1 – 16 kHz | High frequency |

---
