## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Classify the various sources of noise in an automobile with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Automotive noise sources are classified into **three domains** based on the energy conversion mechanism:

1. **Mechanical / Structure-borne sources** — Originates from solid-body motion and impacts. *Examples:* gear meshing, bearing defects, chain drives, brake squeal, tire–road interaction, valve train impacts.

2. **Aerodynamic / Fluid-borne sources** — Originates from fluid flow, turbulence, and vortex shedding. *Examples:* wind noise over A-pillar/mirrors, HVAC blower, exhaust jet, intake whistle.

3. **Combustion / Thermodynamic sources** — Originates from in-cylinder pressure fluctuations due to fuel combustion. *Examples:* SI engine knock, diesel combustion rattle, misfire-induced after-fire.

*(1 mark each for naming domain + example; full 3 marks for clear categorization.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define Sound Power Level. How is it different from Sound Pressure Level?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Sound Power Level (Lw)** is the logarithmic measure of the total acoustic energy radiated per unit time by a source, referenced to $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W:

$$
L_w = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) \quad \text{dB}
$$

**Sound Pressure Level (Lp)** is the logarithmic measure of the local RMS sound pressure referenced to $p_0 = 20 \, \mu$Pa:

$$
L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) \quad \text{dB}
$$

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Lw | Lp |
|---|---|---|
| Quantity | Total radiated power | Local pressure fluctuation |
| Dependence on distance | **Independent** of distance | **Depends** on distance (decreases as 1/r² in free field) |
| Property of | The **source** | The **measurement point** |
| Reference | $10^{-12}$ W | $20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa |

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark differentiation table.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** *Internal choice between two questions, each split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with neat diagrams the **Source–Path–Receiver (SPR) model** for vehicle noise. Discuss the role of each element with one automotive example.

**(b) [7 marks]** A noise source in an engine bay produces a sound pressure level of **88 dB** at a distance of **1.5 m** under free-field conditions. Calculate the **Sound Power Level (Lw)** of the source. Also estimate the SPL at **5 m** distance.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

The **Source-Path-Receiver (SPR) model** is the canonical NVH engineering framework used to systematically decompose any vehicle noise problem into its three functional components.

**1. Source (3 marks)**
- The source is the origin of acoustic or vibrational energy. In an automobile, examples include:
  - Engine combustion pressure pulse (combustion source)
  - Gear mesh impact (mechanical source)
  - Tire-road interaction (mechanical/aerodynamic hybrid)
  - HVAC blower blade passage (aerodynamic source)
- The source is characterized by Sound Power Level, frequency spectrum, and directivity.

**2. Path (2 marks)**
- Energy travels from source to receiver via:
  - **Airborne path** — sound radiates directly through air (e.g., engine noise reaching the cabin)
  - **Structure-borne path** — vibration transmits through the body structure and re-radiates as sound inside the cabin
  - **Fluid-borne path** — sound propagates through fluids (e.g., intake/exhaust gas columns)
- The path is the only place where engineers can apply **passive control** (barriers, absorbers, isolators, dampers).

**3. Receiver (2 marks)**
- The receiver is the point of interest: driver's ear, passenger cabin microphone, or external pass-by observer.
- Receiver response is quantified as SPL at the ear or A-weighted sound exposure level.

**Automotive Example:** Tire–road noise generation, transmission through the wheel hub → suspension → body structure (structure-borne), and radiation into the cabin (receiver: driver).

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $L_p = 88$ dB at $r_1 = 1.5$ m; find $L_w$ and $L_p$ at $r_2 = 5$ m.

**Step 1 — Apply free-field SPL to SWL formula (3 marks):**

$$
L_w = L_p + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(r_1\right) + 11
$$

$$
L_w = 88 + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(1.5\right) + 11
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}\!\left(1.5\right) = 20 \times 0.1761 = 3.52 \, \text{dB}
$$

$$
L_w = 88 + 3.52 + 11 = 102.52 \, \text{dB}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,L_w \approx 102.5 \, \text{dB (re 10}^{-12} \text{ W)}\,}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply inverse square law for new distance (3 marks):**

$$
L_p(r_2) = L_p(r_1) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)
$$

$$
L_p(5) = 88 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{5}{1.5}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{5}{1.5} = 3.333, \quad 20 \log_{10}\!\left(3.333\right) = 20 \times 0.5229 = 10.46 \, \text{dB}
$$

$$
L_p(5) = 88 - 10.46 = 77.54 \, \text{dB}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,L_p(5\,\text{m}) \approx 77.5 \, \text{dB}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification (1 mark):**
Doubling distance typically reduces SPL by 6 dB. From 1.5 m to 5 m is a ratio of 3.33×, so reduction = $20 \log_{10}(3.33) \approx 10.5$ dB, matching our result. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **various mechanical sources of noise** in an internal combustion engine with their characteristic frequency ranges and control methods.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 6-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine runs at **3500 RPM**. The engine also drives a cooling fan with **7 blades**. Calculate:
  (i) Engine firing frequency
  (ii) 3rd-order firing harmonic
  (iii) Fan blade passage frequency (BPF)

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

| Source | Mechanism | Frequency | Control Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Combustion knock** | Auto-ignition of end-gas | 5 – 10 kHz (sharp) | Knock sensor, retard timing, higher octane |
| **Piston slap** | Piston skirt impacts cylinder wall | 1 – 4× firing freq | Optimized skirt profile, tighter clearance |
| **Valve train** | Cam-tappet-valve impact | 1× camshaft freq (≈ ½ crank) | Hydraulic lash adjusters, quieter tappets |
| **Timing chain** | Chain link engagement | Mesh of chain sprocket | Quiet chain, tensioner, guides |
| **Bearing noise** | Roller element defects | Bearing defect freqs | Better lubrication, tighter tolerances |
| **Crankshaft whirl** | Rotating imbalance | 1× crank freq | Balance shafts, dynamic balancing |
| **Accessory drive belt** | Belt-cog engagement | Belt mesh freq | Tooth profile optimization, auto-tensioner |

**Key Notes for Valuation:**
- Mention the *frequency* of each source — this is worth 2 marks
- Mention the *control method* — worth 2 marks
- Correct identification of source mechanism — 2 marks
- Neat classification into table — 1 mark

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** 4-stroke, $n = 6$ cylinders, $N = 3500$ RPM; fan with $N_b = 7$ blades.

**Part (i) — Engine firing frequency (3 marks):**

$$
f_{\text{comb}} = \frac{n \cdot N}{60 \cdot \tau} = \frac{6 \times 3500}{60 \times 2} = \frac{21000}{120} = 175 \, \text{Hz}
$$

**Part (ii) — 3rd-order firing harmonic (2 marks):**

$$
f_{3x} = 3 \times 175 = 525 \, \text{Hz}
$$

**Part (iii) — Fan blade passage frequency (2 marks):**

$$
f_{BPF} = \frac{N_b \cdot N}{60} = \frac{7 \times 3500}{60} = \frac{24500}{60} = 408.33 \, \text{Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_{\text{comb}} = 175 \, \text{Hz}, \quad f_{3x} = 525 \, \text{Hz}, \quad f_{BPF} = 408.33 \, \text{Hz}\,}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Using 20 log instead of 10 log** for power ratios — 1–2 marks lost
> 2. **Forgetting the τ = 2 factor** for 4-stroke engines in firing frequency — 1 mark lost
> 3. **Writing "gear frequency" instead of "gear MESH frequency"** — partial mark lost
> 4. **Confusing $p_0$ and $W_0$** reference values (20 μPa vs 10⁻¹² W) — 1 mark
> 5. **Not stating units** in final answer — 0.5 mark
> 6. **Mixing up SPR roles** (e.g., calling the body a source instead of path) — 1–2 marks

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **SPR Model** is the *single most important* framework — Source → Path → Receiver.
- [ ] **Three domains** of automotive noise: *Mechanical, Aerodynamic, Combustion*.
- [ ] **Sound Power Level** uses **10 log**; **Sound Pressure Level** uses **20 log** — never swap.
- [ ] Reference values: **$p_0 = 20 \, \mu$Pa**, **$W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W**.
- [ ] **Free-field SPL** decreases by **6 dB per doubling of distance**; **SWL is distance-independent**.
- [ ] **Firing frequency** for 4-stroke engine: $f = \dfrac{n \cdot N}{120}$ (Hz).
- [ ] **Gear mesh frequency** = teeth × shaft frequency — NOT shaft frequency.
- [ ] **Fan BPF** = number of blades × shaft frequency — dominant HVAC noise.
- [ ] **Tire/road noise** dominates vehicle noise above ~50 km/h.
- [ ] **Engine noise** dominates below 50 km/h.
- [ ] **A-weighting** filters low-frequency rumble to reflect human hearing sensitivity.
- [ ] **Inverse square law** holds only in *free field*; in *reverberant field*, SPL is nearly constant with distance.
- [ ] Always state **units** in final numerical answers.
- [ ] For SPR, **engine firewall** is a path element (structure-borne), not a source.
- [ ] **Brake squeal** (1–16 kHz) is the highest-frequency source on a typical passenger car.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microphones
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Microphones

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **microphone** (also called a *mic* or *microphone transducer*) is an electroacoustic device that converts acoustic pressure waves (sound) propagating through a medium — typically air — into a corresponding proportional electrical signal (voltage or current). It serves as the **front-end acoustic sensor** in any sound measurement chain, acting as the bridge between a physical sound field and the electronic domain of analyzers, data acquisition systems, and recorders.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAUT755)**, microphones are formally classified as **pressure-sensitive electroacoustic transducers** whose output is calibrated against the standard reference sound pressure of **20 µPa** (the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim alignment):**
> *"A microphone is a transducer that converts acoustic pressure variations into an equivalent electrical signal, characterized by its sensitivity, frequency response, directivity pattern, and dynamic range."*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Drummer's Eardrum"**
> Imagine a tiny drum skin stretched across a hollow can. When sound waves strike the drum, it vibrates sympathetically. Now imagine a clever arrangement where this vibration is converted into an electrical pulse — that, in essence, is a microphone. The **diaphragm** is the drum skin, the **backplate** is the air cavity behind it, and the **electrical circuit** "listens" to the drum and records its motion. The louder the sound (higher pressure), the larger the vibration, the larger the electrical output. Just like human ears, microphones come in different "hearing personalities" — some listen equally from all directions (omnidirectional), while others focus on a single direction (directional/cardioid).

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Reference Values

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Reference Sound Pressure | $p_0$ | **20 µPa** (2 × 10⁻⁵ Pa) |
| Reference Sensitivity | $M_{ref}$ | **1 V/Pa** |
| Speed of Sound (air, 20°C) | $c$ | **343 m/s** |
| Air Density (20°C) | $\rho_0$ | **1.204 kg/m³** |
| Acoustic Impedance (air) | $Z_0 = \rho_0 c$ | **413 Pa·s/m** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reference value **20 µPa** is universally accepted (IEC 651, IEC 61672) and corresponds to the RMS sound pressure of a 1 kHz sine wave just barely audible to a young, healthy human ear.

### 1.4 Role of a Microphone in the NVH Measurement Chain

The typical NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) signal chain in an automotive or industrial application proceeds as:

$$\text{Sound Source} \rightarrow \textbf{Microphone} \rightarrow \text{Preamplifier} \rightarrow \text{Analyzer/DAQ} \rightarrow \text{Display}$$

The microphone is therefore the **first and most critical link** — any error or distortion introduced here propagates irreversibly through the entire chain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Microphones (Hierarchical Logic)

Microphones are classified based on the **transduction principle** they use to convert acoustic energy into electrical energy.

#### A. According to Transduction Principle
1. **Electrostatic / Capacitor (Condenser) Microphones**
   - Principle: Variation of capacitance due to diaphragm displacement.
   - Subtypes: DC-polarized (externally biased), Prepolarized (electret), RF (radio-frequency) condenser.
2. **Electrodynamic / Moving-Coil Microphones**
   - Principle: Motion of a coil in a magnetic field (Faraday's law).
3. **Piezoelectric Microphones**
   - Principle: Voltage generated by mechanical stress on piezoelectric crystals (e.g., Quartz, PZT, PVDF).
4. **Electromagnetic Microphones**
   - Principle: Motion of a magnet (or armature) relative to a coil.
5. **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) Microphones**
   - Principle: Miniaturized silicon-based condenser or piezoelectric structure.
6. **Fiber-Optic / Optical Microphones**
   - Principle: Modulation of light intensity/phase by diaphragm motion.
7. **Carbon Microphones (Historical)**
   - Principle: Variation of contact resistance between carbon granules.

#### B. According to Pressure-Sensing Element Geometry
- **Pressure Microphones** — diaphragm sealed on one side; measures the *absolute* pressure deviation.
- **Pressure-Gradient (Velocity) Microphones** — diaphragm exposed on both sides; measures the *pressure difference*.
- **Combination / Multi-port** — yields directional (cardioid, supercardioid) patterns.

#### C. According to Directivity (Polar Pattern)
- **Omnidirectional** — equal sensitivity from all directions.
- **Bidirectional (Figure-8)** — sensitive to front and rear, null at sides.
- **Unidirectional** — Cardioid, Supercardioid, Hypercardioid.

---

### 2.2 Working Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

#### A. Condenser (Capacitor) Microphone

A condenser microphone consists of a thin, tensioned metallic or metal-coated polymer **diaphragm** placed a small distance $d$ from a rigid **backplate**. The two form a parallel-plate capacitor of capacitance:

$$C = \frac{\epsilon_0 A}{d}$$

Where:
- $\epsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- $A$ = effective diaphragm area (m²)
- $d$ = diaphragm-to-backplate spacing (m), typically 20–40 µm

**Operating Logic:**
1. A polarization voltage $V_{pol}$ (typically **200 V** for lab-grade or **0 V** for prepolarized electret types) charges the capacitor to $Q = C V_{pol}$.
2. An incoming sound wave exerts pressure $p(t)$ on the diaphragm, displacing it by $\Delta d$.
3. The capacitance changes to $C' = \epsilon_0 A / (d + \Delta d)$.
4. Since $Q$ is held constant (high-impedance preamp), the voltage across the capacitor changes:
$$\Delta V = V_{pol} \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{d}$$
5. This voltage variation is the microphone output signal.

**Why it works:** The diaphragm is so thin and light that it follows pressure fluctuations faithfully across a wide frequency range (typically 20 Hz – 20 kHz for measurement-grade units, and up to 100 kHz for special models).

#### B. Electret Condenser Microphone (ECM)

- Uses a permanently charged dielectric (electret material) — e.g., Teflon or PVDF — eliminating the need for external polarization.
- Operates on identical physics to the standard condenser microphone.
- Most common type in consumer electronics and many field-measurement instruments.
- Lower sensitivity and stability than externally polarized laboratory microphones.

#### C. Moving-Coil (Dynamic) Microphone

1. A lightweight coil of wire is attached to a diaphragm.
2. The coil is suspended in a strong radial magnetic field of a permanent magnet.
3. When sound moves the diaphragm, the coil moves through the field.
4. By Faraday's law, an EMF is induced:
$$e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -N B \ell v$$
   Where $B$ = magnetic flux density, $\ell$ = effective coil length, $v$ = velocity, $N$ = turns.
5. Output voltage is proportional to the **velocity** of the diaphragm (not pressure).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU high-yield):** A condenser microphone measures **pressure** (zero-order), while a moving-coil microphone measures **pressure gradient** (first-order). This difference explains their polar patterns and frequency responses.

#### D. Piezoelectric Microphone

1. Certain crystals (quartz, Rochelle salt, PZT ceramics, PVDF polymer) generate a voltage when mechanically stressed.
2. The diaphragm couples acoustic pressure to the crystal, producing a charge $Q = d_{piezo} \cdot F$.
3. Output voltage: $V = Q / C_{crystal}$.
4. Used widely in high-temperature, high-humidity, and industrial environments due to ruggedness.

#### E. MEMS Microphone

- Fabricated using semiconductor lithographic processes.
- A micromachined silicon diaphragm over a perforated backplate forms a miniature capacitor.
- Integrated ASIC preamplifier and ADC are co-packaged.
- Used in automotive cabin sensing, smartphones, hearing aids.
- Limited dynamic range and frequency response compared to measurement-grade condenser mics.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are essential for KTU Part-B (14-mark) problems. Memorize units and reference values!**

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Microphone Sensitivity (linear) | $M = \dfrac{V_{out}}{p_{in}}$ | **V/Pa** or **mV/Pa** | Open-circuit output per unit pressure |
| 2 | Sensitivity (dB re 1 V/Pa) | $M_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{M \text{ [V/Pa]}}{1 \text{ V/Pa}}\right)$ | **dB re 1 V/Pa** | Standard KTU reference |
| 3 | Capacitance (parallel-plate) | $C = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A}{d}$ | F (Farads) | Condenser mic physics |
| 4 | Voltage Output (condenser) | $\Delta V = V_{pol} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta d}{d}$ | V | Constant-charge condition |
| 5 | Charge on Capacitor | $Q = C \cdot V_{pol}$ | C (Coulombs) | Constant in operation |
| 6 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right)$ | **dB SPL** | $p_0 = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa}$ |
| 7 | Output Voltage (from SPL) | $V_{out} = M \cdot p_0 \cdot 10^{L_p/20}$ | V | Combine sensitivity with SPL |
| 8 | Diaphragm Resonance Freq. | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Hz | Stiffness $k$, mass $m$ |
| 9 | Piezoelectric Charge | $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | C | $d_{33}$ = piezoelectric strain coeff. |
| 10 | Faraday EMF (dynamic mic) | $e = N \cdot B \cdot \ell \cdot v$ | V | $v$ = diaphragm velocity |
| 11 | Free-Field Correction | $\Delta L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{j\omega a}{c}}\right)$ | dB | $a$ = mic radius, $\omega = 2\pi f$ |
| 12 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | m | Acoustic wavelength |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When computing sensitivity in dB, ALWAYS use **20 log₁₀** (not 10 log₁₀), because sensitivity is an *amplitude ratio*. Using 10 log₁₀ is a common error that costs 1–2 marks per question.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Preferred Microphone Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Laboratory acoustic calibration | Externally polarized condenser | Highest stability, IEC 61094 compliant |
| Automotive NVH pass-by testing | Free-field condenser (½″) | Flat response, weather protection |
| Anechoic chamber measurements | Free-field condenser | No diffraction at high frequencies |
| Industrial plant noise monitoring | Piezoelectric or ICP® prepolarized | Rugged, high SPL, low cost |
| In-cabin speech recognition | MEMS microphone | Small, integrated ASIC, low power |
| Hearing aids | MEMS / Electret | Miniature, low power |
| Underwater hydrophones | Piezoelectric | Air mics cannot be submerged |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** Special emphasis is placed on **free-field vs. pressure-field vs. random-incidence microphones**, as the field correction directly affects the accuracy of SPL measurements.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sensitivity of a Condenser Microphone (Full Derivation)

**Objective:** Derive the open-circuit voltage sensitivity of a parallel-plate condenser microphone in terms of polarization voltage, diaphragm displacement, and acoustic pressure.

**Starting Equations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{Diaphragm displacement due to pressure:} \quad \Delta d = \frac{p \cdot A_{eff}}{k} \\[6pt]
&\text{Capacitance at equilibrium:} \quad C_0 = \frac{\epsilon_0 A}{d} \\[6pt]
&\text{Charge conservation:} \quad Q = C_0 \cdot V_{pol} = \text{constant} \\[6pt]
&\text{Voltage across capacitor at new spacing:} \quad V' = \frac{Q}{C'} = \frac{Q \cdot (d + \Delta d)}{\epsilon_0 A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Establish the constant-charge condition.**
The preamplifier has very high input impedance, so virtually no charge leaks. Therefore $Q$ remains fixed.

$$Q = C_0 V_{pol} = \frac{\epsilon_0 A \cdot V_{pol}}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Apply sound pressure causing diaphragm displacement.**
For a tensioned circular diaphragm of stiffness $k$, displacement under uniform pressure $p$ is:

$$\Delta d = \frac{p \cdot A_{eff}}{k}$$

where $A_{eff}$ is the effective area (typically $\approx \dfrac{2}{3} A$ for a simply-supported circular plate).

**Step 3 — Compute the new capacitance.**
The spacing changes from $d$ to $d + \Delta d$:

$$C' = \frac{\epsilon_0 A}{d + \Delta d}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the new voltage.**
Since $Q$ is constant:

$$V' = \frac{Q}{C'} = \frac{\epsilon_0 A \cdot V_{pol}}{d} \cdot \frac{d + \Delta d}{\epsilon_0 A} = V_{pol}\left(1 + \frac{\Delta d}{d}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Subtract the equilibrium (DC) voltage.**
The DC component $V_{pol}$ is blocked by a coupling capacitor. Only the AC variation matters:

$$\Delta V = V' - V_{pol} = V_{pol} \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{d}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the displacement expression.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= V_{pol} \cdot \frac{1}{d} \cdot \frac{p \cdot A_{eff}}{k} \\[6pt]
\Delta V &= \left(\frac{V_{pol} \cdot A_{eff}}{k \cdot d}\right) \cdot p
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Identify the sensitivity.**

$$\boxed{M = \frac{\Delta V}{p} = \frac{V_{pol} \cdot A_{eff}}{k \cdot d}} \quad \text{[V/Pa]}$$

**Step 8 — Sensitivity in dB re 1 V/Pa.**

$$M_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{pol} \cdot A_{eff}}{k \cdot d \cdot 1\,\text{V/Pa}}\right)$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sensitivity Conversion: V/Pa → dB SPL Equivalence

**Numerical Worked Example (typical KTU 14-mark format):**

> *A measurement microphone has a sensitivity of 12 mV/Pa. Find its sensitivity in dB re 1 V/Pa. If the sound pressure level is 94 dB SPL, what is the microphone output voltage?*

**Step 1 — Convert sensitivity to V/Pa.**

$$M = 12\,\text{mV/Pa} = 12 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V/Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute dB re 1 V/Pa.**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{12 \times 10^{-3}}{1}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 20 \log_{10}(0.012) \\[6pt]
&= 20 \times (-1.9208) \\[6pt]
&= -38.42\,\text{dB re 1 V/Pa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert 94 dB SPL to absolute pressure.**

$$\begin{aligned}
p_{rms} &= p_0 \cdot 10^{L_p/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10^{94/20} \\[6pt]
&= 20 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10^{4.7} \\[6pt]
&= 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 50118.7 \\[6pt]
&= 1.002\,\text{Pa (RMS)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute output voltage.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= M \cdot p_{rms} \\[6pt]
&= 12 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.002 \\[6pt]
&= 12.03\,\text{mV (RMS)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $M_{dB} = -38.42\,\text{dB re 1 V/Pa}$; $V_{out} \approx 12.03\,\text{mV RMS}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A common KTU question type is: *"A microphone of sensitivity X dB gives Y mV output — what is the SPL?"* Practice the inverse manipulation until automatic.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frequency Response and Diaphragm Resonance

The microphone frequency response is governed by a second-order mass–spring–damper system. The transfer function (displacement-to-pressure) is:

$$\frac{X(\omega)}{P(\omega)} = \frac{1}{k - m\omega^2 + j\omega c}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the natural frequency.**

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Step 2 — At very low frequencies ($\omega \ll \omega_n$):**
The stiffness term dominates; sensitivity $\approx 1/k$ (constant).

**Step 3 — At resonance ($\omega = \omega_n$):**
Sensitivity is amplified by the Q-factor:
$$\left|\frac{X}{P}\right|_{max} = \frac{1}{c\,\omega_n} = \frac{Q}{k}$$

**Step 4 — Above resonance ($\omega \gg \omega_n$):**
The mass term dominates; sensitivity $\approx 1/(m\omega^2)$, falling off at **40 dB/decade**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A well-designed measurement microphone has its diaphragm resonance placed at the **upper end of its operating range** (typically 8–15 kHz) so that the usable flat-response region covers the full audio band (20 Hz – 20 kHz).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> *Define microphone sensitivity. A microphone has a sensitivity of –40 dB re 1 V/Pa. Express this sensitivity in mV/Pa.*

**Model Answer:**

Microphone sensitivity is defined as the open-circuit output voltage of the microphone per unit sound pressure acting on its diaphragm. It is mathematically expressed as $M = V_{out}/p$, with units of V/Pa. The logarithmic form using the reference 1 V/Pa is $M_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(M)$.

To convert –40 dB re 1 V/Pa to mV/Pa:

$$M = 10^{M_{dB}/20} = 10^{-40/20} = 10^{-2} = 0.01\,\text{V/Pa} = 10\,\text{mV/Pa}$$

**Sensitivity = 10 mV/Pa.** ✅

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> *Differentiate between a pressure microphone and a pressure-gradient microphone with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pressure Microphone | Pressure-Gradient Microphone |
|---|---|---|
| Diaphragm exposure | Sealed on one side, open on other | Open on both sides |
| Measures | Absolute pressure deviation | Pressure difference between front and back |
| Polar pattern | Omnidirectional | Bidirectional (figure-8), or cardioid variants |
| Frequency response at low f | Flat (DC to LF) | Rises at 6 dB/octave below resonance |
| Example | Standard ½″ laboratory condenser (free-field) | Ribbon microphone; cardioid condenser |

**Examples:** *Bruel & Kjaer 4189* (pressure) vs. *Royer R-121* (pressure-gradient ribbon).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a **condenser microphone**. Derive an expression for its voltage sensitivity in terms of polarization voltage, diaphragm displacement, and acoustic pressure.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A condenser microphone has a polarization voltage of 200 V, an effective diaphragm area of 1.2 cm², stiffness 4500 N/m, and diaphragm-to-backplate spacing 25 µm. If the sound pressure is 2 Pa (RMS) and the diaphragm displacement per unit pressure is 1.5 × 10⁻⁸ m/Pa, calculate (i) the sensitivity in V/Pa, (ii) the sensitivity in dB re 1 V/Pa, and (iii) the output voltage in mV (RMS).

---

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction:**
A condenser microphone consists of:
1. A thin, lightweight **diaphragm** (typically 4–6 µm of nickel or metal-coated polymer) held under tension.
2. A rigid metal **backplate** placed 20–40 µm behind the diaphragm, perforated with venting holes.
3. An **insulating spacer** maintaining the precise gap $d$.
4. An external **polarization voltage source** $V_{pol}$ (or a permanently charged electret layer).
5. A **preamplifier** with very high input impedance, located close to the capsule.

**Working Principle:**
- The diaphragm and backplate form a parallel-plate capacitor: $C = \epsilon_0 A/d$.
- A polarization voltage $V_{pol}$ charges this capacitor to a fixed charge $Q = CV_{pol}$.
- Sound pressure $p(t)$ impinges on the diaphragm, displacing it by $\Delta d = p A_{eff}/k$.
- The capacitance changes to $C' = \epsilon_0 A/(d+\Delta d)$.
- Since $Q$ is constant (high-impedance preamp), the voltage across the capacitor changes:
$$\Delta V = V_{pol} \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{d}$$

**Derivation of Sensitivity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= \frac{\Delta V}{p} = \frac{V_{pol} \cdot \Delta d}{d \cdot p} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{V_{pol} \cdot (p \cdot A_{eff}/k)}{d \cdot p} = \frac{V_{pol} \cdot A_{eff}}{k \cdot d}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{M = \frac{V_{pol} \cdot A_{eff}}{k \cdot d}\,\text{[V/Pa]}}$$

---

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $V_{pol} = 200\,\text{V}$
- $A_{eff} = 1.2\,\text{cm}^2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$
- $k = 4500\,\text{N/m}$
- $d = 25\,\mu\text{m} = 25 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$
- $p = 2\,\text{Pa (RMS)}$
- $\Delta d / p = 1.5 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m/Pa}$

**(i) Sensitivity in V/Pa:**
Using $M = V_{pol} \cdot \Delta d / (d \cdot p)$:

$$M = \frac{200 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-8}}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-6}}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.12\,\text{V/Pa}$$

**(ii) Sensitivity in dB re 1 V/Pa:**
$$M_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.12) = 20 \times (-0.9208) = -18.42\,\text{dB re 1 V/Pa}$$

**(iii) Output voltage:**
$$V_{out} = M \cdot p = 0.12 \times 2 = 0.24\,\text{V} = 240\,\text{mV RMS}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) **0.12 V/Pa**, (ii) **–18.42 dB re 1 V/Pa**, (iii) **240 mV RMS**. ✅

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a **piezoelectric microphone** with a neat diagram. List four advantages and three disadvantages compared to a condenser microphone.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **polar patterns** of microphones: omnidirectional, cardioid, supercardioid, and bidirectional. For each, draw the polar plot shape, state one application, and indicate the random-energy response (relative to on-axis).

---

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Working Principle of a Piezoelectric Microphone:**

The microphone uses a piezoelectric material (quartz, PZT, PVDF) that generates an electrical charge when subjected to mechanical stress.

**Construction:**
1. A diaphragm (or directly the crystal face) is exposed to the sound field.
2. The diaphragm is mechanically coupled to a piezoelectric crystal disc or film.
3. Sound pressure $p$ exerts a force $F = p \cdot A$ on the diaphragm.
4. This force produces stress $\sigma = F/A$ on the crystal, generating a charge:
$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot p \cdot A$$
   where $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N).
5. The charge develops a voltage across the crystal's own capacitance $C_p$:
$$V = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot p \cdot A}{C_p}$$

**Block Diagram (Textual Description):**

`Sound Pressure → Diaphragm → Piezoelectric Crystal (generates Q) → Charge Amplifier / FET Impedance Converter → Output Voltage`

**Advantages over Condenser Microphone:**
1. Does **not require external polarization voltage** — self-generating.
2. **Rugged and robust**, suitable for harsh industrial environments.
3. Operates over a **wide temperature range** (–100 °C to +250 °C for quartz).
4. Has **high sensitivity to high frequencies** and high SPL levels.

**Disadvantages compared to Condenser Microphone:**
1. Lower frequency response linearity; lower sensitivity at low frequencies.
2. Output impedance is highly capacitive, requiring special impedance-matching preamps.
3. Generally **less stable** with temperature and time than condenser types.

---

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Polar Patterns of Microphones:**

The polar pattern describes the microphone's sensitivity to sound arriving from different angles in the horizontal plane.

| Pattern | Shape Description | On-axis Sensitivity | Random-Energy Response | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Omnidirectional** | Perfect circle (sphere in 3D) | Equal from all directions | 0 dB | Room acoustic measurement, reverberant field |
| **Cardioid** | Heart-shaped | Maximum on-axis | –1.5 dB | Vocals, stage, studio |
| **Supercardioid** | Narrower heart with small rear lobe | Maximum on-axis, narrower front | –2.5 dB | Film dialogue, sports broadcasting |
| **Bidirectional (Figure-8)** | Two equal lobes, null at 90° | Maximum front and rear | –4.8 dB | Interviews, stereo recording (Blumlein pair) |

**Mathematical Relations (for first-order gradient patterns):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E(\theta) &= 1 + k \cos\theta \\[6pt]
\text{Cardioid:} \quad E(\theta) &= 0.5 + 0.5 \cos\theta \\[6pt]
\text{Supercardioid:} \quad E(\theta) &= 0.37 + 0.63 \cos\theta \\[6pt]
\text{Bidirectional:} \quad E(\theta) &= \cos\theta
\end{aligned}$$

**Random-Energy Response (RER) — Definition:**
The RER is the ratio of the microphone's response to a perfectly diffuse sound field to its on-axis response in a free field. It quantifies how the microphone "averages" energy arriving from all directions.

$$\text{RER (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} E^2(\theta,\phi)\sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi}{\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \cdot E^2(0,0)}\right)$$

For a cardioid: RER ≈ –1.5 dB; for a figure-8: RER ≈ –4.8 dB. ✅

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Avoid these!**
> 1. **Wrong log base factor** — Always use **20 log₁₀** for sensitivity (amplitude ratio). Using **10 log₁₀** is a common error costing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong reference pressure** — Always use **20 µPa** for SPL in air (not 1 Pa or 10 µPa). Some students confuse it with intensity references.
> 3. **Unit mix-up in diaphragm area** — KTU often gives $A$ in cm². **Always convert to m²** before substitution: 1 cm² = 10⁻⁴ m².
> 4. **Confusing polarization voltage direction** — Some derivations forget the negative sign (decrease in $d$ → increase in $C$ → decrease in $V$). State the assumption clearly.
> 5. **Drawing omission** — A "neat sketch" is worth **1–2 marks by itself** in KTU valuation. Always include a labelled diagram.
> 6. **Polar pattern misidentification** — Students often confuse *supercardioid* and *hypercardioid*. Remember: hypercardioid has the **largest rear lobe** and **smallest front pickup angle**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Microphones**

- [ ] A microphone is an **electroacoustic transducer** converting **pressure (sound)** into **voltage (electric)**.
- [ ] Reference sound pressure is **p₀ = 20 µPa**; reference sensitivity is **1 V/Pa**.
- [ ] Sensitivity (linear): $M = V_{out}/p$ [V/Pa]; Sensitivity (dB): $M_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(M)$.
- [ ] SPL: $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p/p_0)$ [dB SPL].
- [ ] **Condenser microphone** = parallel-plate capacitor + polarization voltage + constant-charge condition.
- [ ] Voltage output: $\Delta V = V_{pol} \cdot \Delta d / d$.
- [ ] Sensitivity formula: $M = V_{pol} \cdot A_{eff} / (k \cdot d)$.
- [ ] **Moving-coil microphone** uses Faraday's law: $e = N B \ell v$.
- [ ] **Piezoelectric microphone** uses charge generation: $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$.
- [ ] **MEMS microphone** is a silicon-based miniature condenser with integrated ASIC.
- [ ] **Polar patterns**: Omni (sphere), Cardioid (heart), Supercardioid (narrow + small rear lobe), Hypercardioid (narrower + larger rear), Bidirectional (figure-8).
- [ ] Measurement microphones classified as **free-field**, **pressure-field**, or **random-incidence** depending on intended use.
- [ ] Diaphragm resonance should be **above the usable band** (typically 8–15 kHz) for flat response.
- [ ] Frequency response falls at **40 dB/decade** above resonance (mass-controlled region).
- [ ] IEC 61094 is the international standard for measurement microphones; IEC 61672 for sound level meters.
- [ ] Free-field correction: high-frequency pressure boost due to microphone presence in the sound field (~1–3 dB above 5 kHz for ½″ mic).
- [ ] For a **94 dB SPL @ 1 kHz** acoustic calibrator, the output of a 12 mV/Pa mic is ≈ **1.5 V RMS** — used for field calibration checks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise Control Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Noise Control Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Noise, Vibration and Harshness (NVH)**, **Noise Control Methods** refer to the systematic engineering strategies and physical interventions employed to reduce, attenuate, or eliminate unwanted acoustic emissions generated by mechanical, structural, or fluid-dynamic systems. These methods operate on the fundamental triadic framework universally adopted by KTU examiners: the **Source–Path–Receiver (SPR)** model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board Standard):**
> *"Noise control is the application of acoustic engineering principles to reduce unwanted sound through manipulation at the source of generation, interruption along the transmission path, or protection at the receiver. Effective NVH treatment requires simultaneous application of all three strategies, with priority given to source modification wherever technically and economically feasible."*

The three pillars of noise control are formally classified as:

1. **Source Control (Active at Origin):** Reducing the acoustic or vibratory energy injected into the system at the point of generation (e.g., balancing rotating machinery, replacing gear whine, smoothing combustion timing).
2. **Path Control (Transmission Treatment):** Blocking, absorbing, or reflecting acoustic energy as it propagates from source to receiver (e.g., barriers, absorptive liners, tuned mass dampers, isolators).
3. **Receiver Control (At the Human/Machine Interface):** Protecting the end-user from the acoustic field using personal protective equipment (PPE), acoustic enclosures, or active headsets.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Perspective)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Leaking Tap in a Quiet Library**
> Imagine you are studying in a silent library and a tap in the washroom is dripping. You have three options to restore silence:
> 1. **Fix the tap (Source Control):** The most elegant solution — no drip, no sound. This is what engineers do when they re-balance a tyre or re-time engine injection.
> 2. **Stuff a towel in the pipe / close the washroom door (Path Control):** The acoustic energy still exists, but it is *attenuated* before reaching your ears. This is what a muffler or an acoustic barrier does.
> 3. **Wear noise-cancelling headphones (Receiver Control):** You accept the noise exists and protect yourself. This is the last resort in industrial hearing conservation.
>
> A skilled NVH engineer always asks: *"Can I fix the tap first, before spending on the towel and the headphones?"* — this is the **Hierarchy of Noise Control**.

## 1.3 Standard Acoustic Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sound Pressure Level | SPL or $L_p$ | dB (re 20 µPa) | Quantifies acoustic pressure |
| Sound Power Level | $L_W$ | dB (re $10^{-12}$ W) | Source strength |
| Sound Intensity Level | $L_I$ | dB (re $10^{-12}$ W/m²) | Directional energy flux |
| Transmission Loss | $TL$ | dB | Effectiveness of a partition |
| Insertion Loss | $IL$ | dB | Effectiveness of a silencer |
| Noise Reduction | $NR$ | dB | Effectiveness of an enclosure |

**Physical Constants of Interest:**

- **Reference Sound Pressure:** $p_0 = 20 \ \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{Pa}$
- **Reference Sound Power:** $W_0 = 10^{-12} \ \text{W}$
- **Speed of Sound in Air (20 °C):** $c = 343 \ \text{m/s}$
- **Acoustic Impedance of Air:** $Z_0 = \rho c \approx 413 \ \text{Pa·s/m}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Source–Path–Receiver Triad (Operational Logic)

### 2.1.1 Source Control (Highest Priority)

Source control addresses the **root cause** of vibration and noise. Since acoustic radiation is proportional to the surface velocity of the vibrating structure, any reduction in structural vibration directly reduces radiated sound.

**Common Source Control Strategies:**

- **Re-design of excitation forces:** Replacing impact loads with gradual ramps; substituting meshing gears with belt drives; using helical gears instead of spur gears.
- **Operating condition modification:** Altering engine speed, combustion timing, valve overlap, fuel injection pressure.
- **Dynamic balancing and alignment:** Reducing unbalance forces in rotating shafts (force $F = m e \omega^2$).
- **Tonal noise reduction in electric machines:** Skewing rotor slots, using fractional-slot windings.
- **Combustion noise control:** Modifying injection rate shaping in diesel engines.

### 2.1.2 Path Control (Transmission Engineering)

Once the source energy is minimised, the remaining energy must be prevented from reaching the receiver.

**Path control sub-categories:**

1. **Isolation** — using springs, elastomers, or rubber mounts to decouple structural paths.
2. **Damping** — converting vibrational energy into heat via viscoelastic materials (constrained layer damping, free layer damping).
3. **Absorption** — porous materials (foam, glass wool) that convert acoustic energy into heat via viscous friction in pores.
4. **Reflection / Barrier** — dense, impervious partitions that reflect acoustic energy.
5. **Tuning / Resonant Absorption** — Helmholtz resonators, micro-perforated panels, and tuned mass dampers that absorb energy at discrete frequencies.

### 2.1.3 Receiver Control (Last Resort)

Used when source and path modifications are infeasible. Examples include ear defenders, hearing protection PPE, and cabin acoustic encapsulation in industrial machinery.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU FORMULA TABLE — Memorise for Part A & Part B**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)$ | Sound pressure level in **dB** | Decibel calculations |
| 2 | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right)$ | Sound power level | Source ranking |
| 3 | $L_p = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11$ | Free-field point source (dB) | Outdoor propagation |
| 4 | $L_p = L_W - 10 \log_{10}(r) - 8$ | Free-field line source | Traffic noise |
| 5 | $TL = 20 \log_{10}(m f) - 47$ | Mass Law (single partition) | Panel design |
| 6 | $IL = L_{\text{before}} - L_{\text{after}}$ | Insertion Loss of a silencer | Muffler rating |
| 7 | $NR = L_{\text{outside}} - L_{\text{inside}}$ | Noise Reduction of enclosure | Enclosure rating |
| 8 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of SDOF | Vibration isolation |
| 9 | $f_c = \dfrac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{S}{V \cdot l_{\text{eff}}}}$ | Helmholtz resonator frequency | Resonant absorbers |
| 10 | $\eta_d = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_n}$ | Loss factor (damping) | Damping materials |
| 11 | $T_d = 1/f_n$ | Reverberation period | Acoustic treatment |
| 12 | $ST = 0.16 V / A$ | Sabine reverberation time | Room acoustics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorisation Tip:** Always carry the reference values $p_0 = 20 \ \mu\text{Pa}$ and $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W. KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for omitting units or for using incorrect reference pressures.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility Across Industries

- **Automotive:** Cabin quietness via source control (engine mounts, balancer shafts), path control (dash insulator, wheel arch liner), receiver control (active noise control in headrests).
- **HVAC & Building Services:** Duct silencers, plenum absorbers, vibration isolation of chillers.
- **Industrial Machinery:** Acoustic enclosures for compressors, generator sets, hydraulic presses.
- **Aerospace:** Active noise control in propellers; tuned absorbers in fuselage panels.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Micro-perforated panel enclosures in laptops and HVAC grilles.
- **Shipbuilding:** Floating floor isolation, resilient mounts for diesel engines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (Barriers):** A noise barrier provides effective shielding only if its surface mass per unit area $m \geq 10 \ \text{kg/m}^2$ AND it breaks the **line of sight** between the source and receiver. Below the line of sight, diffraction degrades performance dramatically.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Mass Law for Transmission Loss (Single Partition)

The Mass Law is the **single most tested formula** for KTU Part B questions on noise barriers and partitions.

**Starting Point:** For a thin, limp, single-degree-of-freedom partition of surface density $m$ (kg/m²) excited by an oblique acoustic wave, the transmission coefficient $\tau$ is defined as:

$$\tau = \frac{p_t^2}{p_i^2} = \frac{1}{\left(1 + \frac{\omega m \cos\theta}{2 \rho c}\right)^2}$$

For a normally incident wave ($\theta = 0$, so $\cos\theta = 1$):

$$\tau = \frac{1}{\left(1 + \frac{\omega m}{2 \rho c}\right)^2}$$

**Step 1 — Express Transmission Loss:**
By definition, $TL = 10 \log_{10}(1/\tau)$:

$$TL = 10 \log_{10}\left[\left(1 + \frac{\omega m}{2 \rho c}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — Simplify using the high-mass assumption** ($\omega m \gg 2 \rho c$, valid above the coincidence and mass-controlled regions):

$$TL \approx 10 \log_{10}\left[\left(\frac{\omega m}{2 \rho c}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 3 — Expand the logarithm:**

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\omega m}{2 \rho c}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2\pi f \, m}{2 \rho c}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\rho c = 413 \ \text{Pa·s/m}$ and simplify the constant:**

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(f \, m) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\pi}{\rho c}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the constant term numerically:**

$$20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\pi}{413}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(0.007606) \approx -42.4$$

$$\boxed{TL = 20 \log_{10}(f \cdot m) - 47 \quad \text{[dB]}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Doubling either the frequency or the surface mass increases $TL$ by **6 dB**. This is why a thicker, denser wall (e.g., double-glazed partition with 8 mm glass) outperforms a thinner single pane by approximately 6 dB per doubling of mass.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Natural Frequency of a Vibration Isolator

A vibration isolator is modelled as a **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF)** mass-spring-damper system.

**Step 1 — Equation of motion for undamped free vibration:**

$$m \ddot{x} + k x = 0$$

**Step 2 — Assume harmonic solution** $x = X \sin(\omega_n t)$:

$$\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = -\omega_n^2 X \sin(\omega_n t) = -\omega_n^2 x$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the equation of motion:**

$$m \left(-\omega_n^2 x\right) + k x = 0$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $x$:**

$$x \left(k - m \omega_n^2\right) = 0$$

**Step 5 — For non-trivial solution ($x \neq 0$):**

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to natural frequency in Hertz:**

$$\boxed{f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{[Hz]}}$$

**Step 7 — Transmissibility $T$ (ratio of transmitted force to input force):**

$$T = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2 \zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2 \zeta r)^2}}$$

where the **frequency ratio** is $r = f / f_n$ and $\zeta$ is the damping ratio.

**Step 8 — Isolation criterion:** Effective isolation requires $r > \sqrt{2}$ (i.e., operating frequency at least **40 % above** $f_n$). Below $r = \sqrt{2}$, the isolator actually amplifies vibration.

$$\boxed{\text{Isolation} \iff f > \sqrt{2}\, f_n \approx 1.414\, f_n}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Helmholtz Resonator Frequency

A Helmholtz resonator consists of a cavity of volume $V$ connected to the duct through a neck of cross-sectional area $S$ and effective neck length $l_{\text{eff}}$.

**Step 1 — Model the air in the neck as a lumped mass** $m_{\text{air}} = \rho S l_{\text{eff}}$.

**Step 2 — Model the air in the cavity as a spring** with acoustic compliance:

$$C_a = \frac{V}{\rho c^2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the SDOF natural frequency formula:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m_{\text{air}} \cdot C_a}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expressions:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{\rho c^2}{\rho S l_{\text{eff}} \cdot V}} = c \sqrt{\frac{S}{V \cdot l_{\text{eff}}}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to frequency in Hertz:**

$$\boxed{f_n = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{S}{V \cdot l_{\text{eff}}}} \quad \text{[Hz]}}$$

**Step 6 — End correction:** For a flanged neck, $l_{\text{eff}} = l + 0.85 r_n$ where $r_n$ is the neck radius. For an unflanged neck, $l_{\text{eff}} = l + 1.46 r_n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use:** Helmholtz resonators are tuned to absorb a specific tonal noise such as a 200 Hz engine boom in a tractor cabin. By adjusting $V$ or $S$, the engineer targets the offensive frequency.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Control Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern — Selection Decision Table (commonly tested in Part A 3-mark questions):**

| Noise Type | Frequency | Dominant Mechanism | Recommended Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engine combustion knock | 1–4 kHz | High-frequency impulsive | Source: retarded timing, injection shaping |
| Tyre/road noise | 800–2500 Hz | Structural vibration | Path: absorptive wheel-arch liner, constrained layer damping |
| Gear whine | 500–4000 Hz | Tonal meshing | Source: profile modification, helical gears |
| HVAC duct rumble | 100–800 Hz | Aerodynamic | Path: lined duct silencer, splitter silencer |
| Boom in cabin | 30–150 Hz | Cavity resonance | Path: Helmholtz resonator, quarter-wave resonator |
| Wind noise | 500–5000 Hz | Aero-acoustic | Path: door/window seal upgrade, A-pillar deflector |
| Electric motor whine | 1000–8000 Hz | Electromagnetic | Source: rotor skew, fractional slot winding |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (Modelled on KTU University Exam):** A steel partition of surface density $m = 8 \ \text{kg/m}^2$ separates a noisy machinery room (SPL = 95 dB at the wall surface) from a quiet office. Calculate the Transmission Loss at 500 Hz. If a second identical partition is mounted 100 mm away with an absorptive infill, what is the approximate composite $TL$?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Mass Law at 500 Hz:**

$$TL_1 = 20 \log_{10}(f \cdot m) - 47$$

$$TL_1 = 20 \log_{10}(500 \times 8) - 47$$

$$TL_1 = 20 \log_{10}(4000) - 47 = 20 (3.602) - 47$$

$$\boxed{TL_1 = 72.04 - 47 = 25.04 \ \text{dB}}$$

**Step 2 — Composite Transmission Loss for a double panel with absorbing cavity (rule of thumb):**

$$TL_{\text{composite}} \approx TL_1 + TL_2 + 10 \ \text{dB (cavity gain)}$$

$$TL_{\text{composite}} = 25.04 + 25.04 + 10 = 60.08 \ \text{dB}$$

**Step 3 — Office SPL:**

$$L_{\text{office}} = 95 - 60.08 \approx 35 \ \text{dB}$$

This satisfies the **NC-30 office comfort criterion** (recommended SPL 30–35 dB).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Source–Path–Receiver Triad (Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the conceptual flow of acoustic energy from generation to perception, along with the engineering interventions applied at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC1[Vibration Source e.g. Engine]
    SRC2[Acoustic Source e.g. Flow Noise]
    SRC3[Impact Source e.g. Gear Mesh]

    SRCCTRL1[Source Control e.g. Balancing]
    SRCCTRL2[Source Control e.g. Combustion Timing]
    SRCCTRL3[Source Control e.g. Profile Modification]

    PATH1[Structural Path e.g. Mounts]
    PATH2[Airborne Path e.g. Duct]
    PATH3[Structure Borne Path e.g. Panel]

    PATHCTRL1[Damping and Isolation]
    PATHCTRL2[Absorption and Barrier]
    PATHCTRL3[Tuned Absorber]

    RCV[Receiver e.g. Driver Cabin]
    RCVCTRL[Receiver Control e.g. ANC Headset]

    SRC1 --> SRCCTRL1 --> PATH1
    SRC2 --> SRCCTRL2 --> PATH2
    SRC3 --> SRCCTRL3 --> PATH3

    PATH1 --> PATHCTRL1 --> RCV
    PATH2 --> PATHCTRL2 --> RCV
    PATH3 --> PATHCTRL3 --> RCV

    RCV --> RCVCTRL
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of an Active Noise Control (ANC) System

Active Noise Control is an emerging **receiver-side** method. The following block diagram illustrates its functional architecture for cabin noise cancellation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NOISE[Reference Noise Source e.g. Engine Harmonic]
    MIC[Reference Microphone captures noise signal x]
    ADAPT[Adaptive Filter W z computes anti-noise y]
    SPEAKER[Loudspeaker emits anti-noise]
    CANCEL[Destructive Interference in Cabin Cavity]
    ERR[Error Microphone e monitors residual]
    LMS[LMS Algorithm updates W z]
    PSENSOR[Path S z from speaker to error mic]

    NOISE --> MIC --> ADAPT --> SPEAKER --> CANCEL
    CANCEL --> ERR --> LMS --> ADAPT
    ERR -.-> PSENSOR -.-> LMS
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Choosing a Noise Control Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Identify Noise Problem]
    START --> Q1{Source Modification Feasible}
    Q1 -- YES --> SC[Apply Source Control]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Frequency Tonal or Broadband}
    Q2 -- Tonal --> Q3{Target Frequency f0 Known}
    Q3 -- YES --> TR[Tuned Resonator e.g. Helmholtz]
    Q3 -- NO --> SA[Frequency Sweep Analysis]
    Q2 -- Broadband --> Q4{Path Airborne or Structure Borne}
    Q4 -- Airborne --> AB[Porous Absorber and Barrier]
    Q4 -- Structure Borne --> Q5{Natural Frequency Ratio}
    Q5 -- r less than 1.4 --> ISO[Replace Isolator with Lower k]
    Q5 -- r greater than 1.4 --> DM[Add Damping Material]
    TR --> DONE[Verify with SPL Measurement]
    SA --> DONE
    AB --> DONE
    ISO --> DONE
    DM --> DONE
    SC --> DONE
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Generic Reactive Muffler Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Inlet Duct Exhaust Pulse]
    CHAM1[Expansion Chamber 1 Volume V1]
    NECK[Constricted Neck Area S Length L]
    CHAM2[Expansion Chamber 2 Volume V2]
    OUT[Outlet to Atmosphere]

    IN --> CHAM1 --> NECK --> CHAM2 --> OUT

    RFL[Reflection at Expansion]
    ABS[Absorption at Lined Wall]
    RFR[Reflection at Contraction]
    TRS[Transmission through Neck]

    CHAM1 -. RFL .-> CHAM1
    CHAM1 -. ABS .-> CHAM1
    NECK -. TRS .-> NECK
    CHAM2 -. RFR .-> CHAM2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Muffler Classification (KTU Expectation):**
> - **Reactive (Reflective):** No absorptive material; uses expansion chambers and side branches. Effective at low frequencies.
> - **Absorptive (Dissipative):** Filled with glass/rock wool or ceramic fibre; effective at mid-to-high frequencies.
> - **Combined (Hybrid):** Combines both; typical in modern automotive exhausts.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Explain the Source–Path–Receiver approach to noise control."**

**Model Answer:**

The Source–Path–Receiver (SPR) approach is a systematic framework for reducing unwanted sound by addressing the three sequential stages of acoustic transmission.

**(i) Source Control:** Modifications at the point of generation, e.g., dynamic balancing of rotating parts, gear profile modification, engine timing retard, and combustion shaping. Since radiated sound is proportional to surface velocity squared, source control is the most effective strategy because energy is removed before it can propagate.

**(ii) Path Control:** Treatment of the transmission medium between source and receiver. Techniques include vibration isolators (elastomeric mounts), viscoelastic damping, acoustic absorption (porous liners in ducts), reflective barriers (masonry walls), and tuned absorbers (Helmholtz resonators).

**(iii) Receiver Control:** Protection at the point of perception, including acoustic enclosures, soundproof cabins, personal hearing protection, and active noise control headsets. This is the last resort since the noise continues to exist in the environment.

**Priority hierarchy:** Source > Path > Receiver (the KTU board expects this order).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Define Transmission Loss and Insertion Loss. Why are both required?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Transmission Loss (TL)** is the ratio of acoustic power incident on a partition to the acoustic power transmitted through it, expressed in decibels:

$$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_t}\right) = L_i - L_t$$

It is an **intrinsic material property** of the partition, independent of the source or receiver.

**Insertion Loss (IL)** is the difference in sound pressure level measured at a receiver before and after a control device is installed:

$$IL = L_{\text{before}} - L_{\text{after}}$$

It is a **system-level performance metric** that depends on the source, the device, and the acoustic environment.

**Why both are required:** TL alone does not capture installation effects such as flanking paths, leaks, or boundary reflections. IL measures the practical real-world benefit of a silencer or barrier, including all such installation effects. KTU examiners expect students to state that *TL describes the partition itself, while IL describes the installed system.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into sub-parts **7 + 7**. Students may choose **EITHER Question A OR Question B**. Internal choice is provided.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** *With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Helmholtz resonator** as a noise control device. Derive the expression for its resonant frequency.* **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *A 4 mm thick steel panel (density 7850 kg/m³) separates a workshop (SPL = 92 dB at the panel) from an adjoining office. Calculate:*
   *(i) Transmission Loss at 1000 Hz using the Mass Law.*
   *(ii) SPL inside the office.*
   *(iii) What modification would you suggest to achieve NC-40 in the office?* **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Helmholtz Resonator:**

A Helmholtz resonator is a passive, tuned, narrow-band acoustic absorber consisting of a rigid-walled cavity of volume $V$ connected to the external acoustic field through a narrow neck of cross-sectional area $S$ and physical neck length $l$.

**Working Principle:** When acoustic pressure fluctuations of frequency $f$ act on the air in the neck, the air mass oscillates like a piston against the spring-like compressibility of the air trapped in the cavity. At the resonant frequency, the system impedance is minimum and maximum acoustic energy is dissipated by viscous friction in the neck.

**Sketch of resonator (text representation):**
```
    External          ┌──────────┐
    Acoustic     ┌────┤   Neck   ├────┐ Cavity Volume V
    Field        │    │ S, l      │    │
                └────┴────────────┴────┘
```

**Derivation (already derived in Section 3.3):**

$$f_n = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{S}{V \cdot l_{\text{eff}}}}$$

With end correction for a flanged neck: $l_{\text{eff}} = l + 0.85 r_n$.

**Engineering application:** Absorbing low-frequency tonal noise (30–300 Hz) such as engine firing frequency, transformer hum, HVAC duct rumble.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Panel thickness: $t = 4 \ \text{mm} = 0.004 \ \text{m}$
- Steel density: $\rho_s = 7850 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- Source SPL: $L_p = 92 \ \text{dB}$
- Frequency: $f = 1000 \ \text{Hz}$

**Step (i) — Surface mass density:**

$$m = \rho_s \cdot t = 7850 \times 0.004 = 31.4 \ \text{kg/m}^2$$

**Step (ii) — Apply Mass Law:**

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(f \cdot m) - 47$$

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(1000 \times 31.4) - 47$$

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(31400) - 47 = 20 (4.497) - 47$$

$$\boxed{TL = 89.94 - 47 = 42.94 \ \text{dB}}$$

**Step (iii) — Office SPL:**

$$L_{\text{office}} = L_{\text{outside}} - TL = 92 - 42.94$$

$$\boxed{L_{\text{office}} \approx 49.06 \ \text{dB}}$$

**Step (iv) — Required modification to achieve NC-40:**

NC-40 specification allows SPL $\approx$ 50 dB at 1000 Hz, so current 49.06 dB already complies at this single frequency. However, for broadband compliance across all octave bands, the recommended modifications are:

1. **Add a second partition** with a 100 mm air gap filled with glass-wool (50 mm, density 32 kg/m³), giving additional $TL \approx 10 \ \text{dB}$.
2. **Apply constrained layer damping** (3M ISD-110 type viscoelastic tape) on the noisy side to reduce panel vibration.
3. **Seal all flanking paths** with acoustic sealant at wall–floor and wall–ceiling junctions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** *Explain with a sketch the principle of a **reactive muffler**. Derive the transmission loss expression for a simple expansion chamber muffler.* **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *A machine produces an octave-band SPL of 88 dB at 500 Hz. Two noise control options are proposed:*
   *(i) An enclosure providing a Noise Reduction of 22 dB.*
   *(ii) A barrier providing an Insertion Loss of 18 dB at the operator position.*
   *Compare the effectiveness of both options. If cost constraints require a combined solution using only ONE option, recommend the better choice with justification.* **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Reactive Muffler — Principle and Derivation:**

A reactive muffler attenuates sound through **reflection and interference** rather than absorption. The simplest form is a sudden expansion chamber: a duct of cross-section $S_1$ suddenly expands to $S_2$, then contracts back.

**Principle:** When the wave reaches the expansion, part is reflected back toward the source and part is transmitted. At the downstream contraction, further reflection occurs. The waves that survive to the outlet experience destructive interference with internally reflected waves, producing attenuation at specific frequencies.

**Sketch (text representation):**
```
  ──► Inlet            Outlet ──►
  ──────────┐    ┌─────────────────────
            │    │
       S1   │ V  │ S2 (larger)
            │    │
  ──────────┘    └─────────────────────
```

**Transmission Loss for Simple Expansion Chamber:**

The transmission coefficient for a simple expansion chamber of volume $V = S_2 L$ is derived from the wave reflection at sudden area changes:

$$\tau = \left[\cos(kL) - \frac{i}{2}\left(m + \frac{1}{m}\right)\sin(kL)\right]^{-2}$$

where $m = S_1 / S_2$ is the area ratio and $k = 2\pi f / c$ is the wavenumber.

At low frequencies (when $kL \ll 1$, $\cos kL \approx 1$, $\sin kL \approx kL$):

$$\tau \approx \left[1 + \frac{i k L}{2}\left(m + \frac{1}{m}\right)\right]^{-2}$$

The Transmission Loss becomes:

$$TL = 10 \log_{10}\left[1 + \frac{1}{4}\left(m - \frac{1}{m}\right)^2 \sin^2(kL)\right]$$

At maximum attenuation, $\sin(kL) = 1$, giving peak $TL$:

$$\boxed{TL_{\text{peak}} = 20 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(m + \frac{1}{m}\right)\right]}$$

**Design use:** Reactive mufflers are ideal for low-frequency engine exhaust noise (50–500 Hz). At higher frequencies, absorptive (dissipative) mufflers are preferred.

**(b) Comparative Solution:**

**Option (i) — Enclosure (Noise Reduction 22 dB):**

$$L_{\text{after,1}} = 88 - 22 = 66 \ \text{dB}$$

**Option (ii) — Barrier (Insertion Loss 18 dB):**

$$L_{\text{after,2}} = 88 - 18 = 70 \ \text{dB}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Enclosure | Barrier |
|---|---|---|
| Resulting SPL | 66 dB | 70 dB |
| Effectiveness | Higher (4 dB better) | Lower |
| Cost | Higher (full enclosure) | Lower |
| Maintenance access | Restricted | Easy |
| Operator mobility | Confined | Free |

**Recommendation:**

$$\boxed{\text{Option (i) — Enclosure, because it provides 4 dB lower SPL (loudness halved).}}$$

**Justification:** A 4 dB reduction corresponds to a perceived loudness reduction of approximately **33 %** (because loudness in sones is approximately proportional to $10^{0.1 \times L}$). The enclosure is more effective because it contains both airborne and structure-borne noise at the source, whereas the barrier only blocks the direct line-of-sight path. If the operator requires mobility and the work cycle permits, Option (ii) (barrier) is acceptable; otherwise, Option (i) is preferred. A 4 dB improvement is acoustically significant because it represents a **60 % reduction in sound power**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the reference pressure** $p_0 = 20 \ \mu\text{Pa}$ — a 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Using $\log$ instead of $\log_{10}$** in SPL formulas — the answer becomes off by 2.3× and entire problem is wrong.
> 3. **Wrong units** in Mass Law (mixing mm with m, g with kg) — frequent 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Not mentioning the isolation criterion** $r > \sqrt{2}$ when discussing vibration isolation — examiners expect this.
> 5. **Confusing TL with IL** — examiners look specifically for the distinction.
> 6. **Omitting the end correction** in Helmholtz resonator problems — 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Noise Control Methods**

- ☐ **Source–Path–Receiver (SPR)** is the universal framework; priority is **Source > Path > Receiver**.
- ☐ **Sound Pressure Level** uses $p_0 = 20 \ \mu\text{Pa}$; formula is $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_0)$.
- ☐ **Sound Power Level** uses $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W; formula is $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$.
- ☐ **Mass Law:** $TL = 20 \log_{10}(f \cdot m) - 47$ — doubling $f$ or $m$ gives **+6 dB** TL.
- ☐ **Insertion Loss (IL)** = before − after; **Transmission Loss (TL)** = incident − transmitted; **Noise Reduction (NR)** = outside − inside.
- ☐ **Vibration isolation formula:** $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$; isolation requires $f_{\text{operating}} > \sqrt{2}\, f_n$.
- ☐ **Helmholtz resonator:** $f_n = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{S/(V \cdot l_{\text{eff}})}$; flanged neck end correction adds $0.85 r_n$.
- ☐ **Muffler types:** Reactive (low-frequency, reflective), Absorptive (mid/high-frequency, dissipative), Hybrid (combined).
- ☐ **Loss factor** $\eta_d = \Delta f / f_n$ quantifies damping effectiveness.
- ☐ **Barriers require line-of-sight break** and surface mass $\geq 10 \ \text{kg/m}^2$.
- ☐ **Damping methods:** Free layer, constrained layer, sandwich viscoelastic treatments.
- ☐ **Active Noise Control (ANC):** Uses destructive interference via adaptive LMS filter; effective below ~1 kHz in enclosed cavities.
- ☐ **Hierarchy of standard:** Source > Path > Receiver; receiver control is last resort.
- ☐ **NC/RC curves** (NC-30, NC-40, etc.) define comfort criteria; always check the octave-band values.
- ☐ **Convert carefully:** $20 \log_{10}$ for pressure quantities, $10 \log_{10}$ for power/intensity quantities.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Noise Control Methods**
> Mastery of the Mass Law, the SPR triad, the Helmholtz resonator formula, and the vibration isolation criterion will secure the majority of marks in KTU examinations for this module. Practise numerical problems using decibels carefully — the distinction between $10 \log$ and $20 \log$ is the single most common source of mistakes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active and Semi-Active Control of Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Active and Semi-Active Control of Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Active Noise Control (ANC)** is an electro-acoustic engineering method that reduces unwanted sound (primary noise) by generating a secondary, anti-phase sound field (anti-noise) through electro-mechanical transducers such as loudspeakers, using real-time signal processing driven by adaptive digital filters.

**Semi-Active Noise Control (SANC)** is a hybrid approach where a passive damping element (or smart material) is electronically modulated in real time by a low-power controller — the energy is dissipated by the passive element, but its characteristics are actively tuned to track the disturbance spectrum.

These techniques fall under the broader umbrella of **Active Vibration and Noise Control (AVNC)** and sit at Level 3–4 of the NVH mitigation hierarchy, surpassing passive absorbers in low-frequency performance where mass, volume, and stiffness constraints render passive solutions impractical.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Active Noise Control like Noise-Cancelling Headphones:**
> You wear headphones in a noisy airplane cabin. A tiny microphone on the ear-cup *listens* to the engine drone. A chip inside calculates a mirror-image wave (same amplitude, opposite phase) and pushes it through the speaker. The two waves meet at your eardrum and *cancel each other out* — much like ripples in a pond collapsing when two pebbles hit at the right time.

**Semi-active** is like a *smart shock absorber* in a car — the damper itself cannot add energy, but an electronic valve continuously adjusts how stiff it is to absorb road bumps optimally. It "listens, decides, and adapts," but the heavy lifting is still mechanical.

---

## 1.3 Classification Hierarchy of Noise Control

| Level | Method | Energy Source | Frequency Range | Typical Insertion Loss |
|:-----:|:-------|:-------------:|:---------------:|:----------------------:|
| **Source** | Quieter machine design | Design change | Broadband | 10–20 dB |
| **Path – Passive** | Absorbers, barriers, silencers | Material damping | Mid–High (>500 Hz) | 5–25 dB |
| **Path – Semi-Active** | Variable impedance / smart material | Dissipated by passive element | Low–Mid | 5–15 dB |
| **Path – Active** | ANC with anti-noise | External amplifier | Low (<500 Hz) | 10–30 dB |
| **Receiver** | PPE (ear plugs, muffs) | Personal | Broadband | 20–35 dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** The key conceptual advantage of active control is that performance **improves** as frequency *decreases*, while passive absorbers perform *poorly* below ~500 Hz because the required mass/volume scales with $\lambda/4$.

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Operating Principle

Active noise control exploits the principle of **linear superposition** of acoustic waves. If the total acoustic pressure at a point in space is

$$p_{\text{total}}(t) = p_{\text{primary}}(t) + p_{\text{secondary}}(t)$$

then complete cancellation is achieved when

$$p_{\text{secondary}}(t) = -p_{\text{primary}}(t)$$

This is realised by:
1. Sensing the disturbance with a **reference transducer**.
2. Estimating the *optimal* anti-signal via an **adaptive filter** $W(z)$.
3. Reproducing the anti-signal through a **secondary (control) source**.
4. Monitoring residual error with an **error transducer**, which updates the filter coefficients.

---

## 1.5 Core Components of an ANC System

| Component | Function | Typical Device |
|:---------:|:---------|:--------------:|
| **Reference Sensor** | Provides a coherent input correlated with the noise | Accelerometer, microphone, tachometer |
| **Controller / DSP** | Computes anti-signal using adaptive algorithm | Texas Instruments TMS320, Analog Devices SHARC |
| **Adaptive Filter** | Models inverse of plant $P(z)$ | FIR filter with $N$ taps |
| **Secondary Source** | Radiates anti-noise | Loudspeaker, panel exciter |
| **Error Sensor** | Measures residual error field | Microphone, error accelerometer |
| **Secondary Path Model $\hat{S}(z)$** | Compensates for plant delay | Off-line / on-line identified FIR |

---

## 1.6 Why Active Control? — The Physics Motivation

> [!NOTE]
> A passive quarter-wavelength resonator for **100 Hz** in air ($c \approx 343$ m/s) requires a depth of $\lambda/4 = 343/(4 \times 100) = 0.86$ m. The same attenuation achievable with active control fits inside a 5 cm loudspeaker magnet — a 17× reduction in volume.

This is why ANC has revolutionised applications such as:
- Aircraft cabin quietness (e.g., Embraer E2, Boeing 787)
- Automotive cabin refinement (engine order cancellation)
- HVAC duct silencing (compact "Active Plugs")
- Headphones and hearing protection (Bose QC, Sony WH-1000XM)
- MRI scanner acoustic noise reduction
- Power transformer hum mitigation

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System-Theoretic Block Diagram

The canonical feedforward ANC architecture (widely studied and tested in the KTU syllabus) has three principal transfer functions:

- **Primary Path** $P(z)$ : from disturbance source to error sensor
- **Secondary Path** $S(z)$ : from control output $y(n)$ to error sensor
- **Secondary Path Estimate** $\hat{S}(z)$ : the modelled version used inside the controller

The plant to be identified is therefore a **cascade of $\hat{S}(z)$ and $W(z)$**, where $W(z)$ is the adaptive controller.

---

## 2.2 The Filtered-x LMS (FxLMS) Algorithm — The Heart of ANC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Topic:** The FxLMS is the most-tested algorithm in this module. Students must derive its update equation and explain why the input must be **filtered through $\hat{S}(z)$** before being used in the LMS update.

### 2.2.1 Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step Narrative)

1. **Reference signal** $x(n)$ is the disturbance input correlated with $p(n)$.
2. **Adaptive filter** $W(z)$ produces the anti-noise:
   $$y(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$
3. **Anti-noise** propagates through $S(z)$ to reach the error microphone as $-s(n) * y(n)$.
4. **Total error** (residual) measured:
   $$e(n) = d(n) - s(n) * y(n)$$
5. **Cost function** (instantaneous MSE):
   $$J(n) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,e^{2}(n)$$
6. **Steepest-descent update** (gradient descent):
   $$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}J(n)$$
7. Because $e(n)$ depends on $y(n)$ through the unknown $S(z)$, the plain LMS gradient is biased. The classical solution is to **pre-filter $x(n)$ by $\hat{S}(z)$**, yielding $x'(n) = \hat{s}(n) * x(n)$.

### 2.2.2 The FxLMS Update Equation

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}'(n)\;}$$

where $\mathbf{x}'(n) = \bigl[x'(n),\, x'(n-1),\, \ldots,\, x'(n-L+1)\bigr]^{T}$ is the **filtered reference vector**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Filtered-x"?** The name arises because $x(n)$ is *filtered* by the secondary path estimate $\hat{S}(z)$ before being used in the LMS coefficient update. This compensates for the delay and dynamics between $y(n)$ and the error microphone.

---

## 2.3 Stability and Convergence Conditions

| Condition | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|:---------:|:-------:|:-----------------|
| Step-size bound | $0 < \mu < \dfrac{2}{\lambda_{\max}\,(P_{x'} + \sigma_n^{2})}$ | Stability of FxLMS |
| Misalignment robustness | $\vert \hat{S}(z) - S(z)\vert < \dfrac{1}{\mu\,N\,P_{x}}$ | Required model fidelity |
| Convergence time | $n_{\text{conv}} \approx \dfrac{5}{\mu\,\lambda_{\min}}$ | Adaptations to reach –3 dB steady-state |
| Computational cost | $O(NL)$ per sample | $N$ filter taps, $L$ secondary model length |

where $\lambda_{\max}$ and $\lambda_{\min}$ are the largest/smallest eigenvalues of the filtered-reference autocorrelation matrix.

---

## 2.4 The Filtered-x RLS (FxRLS) Algorithm — Faster Convergence

For non-stationary or rapidly time-varying noise (e.g., transient road noise), LMS is too slow. The Recursive Least Squares variant gives faster convergence at higher compute cost:

$$ \mathbf{w}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n)\,\bigl[d(n) - \mathbf{x}'^{T}(n)\mathbf{w}(n-1)\bigr] $$

$$ \mathbf{k}(n) = \dfrac{\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}'(n)}{\lambda + \mathbf{x}'^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}'(n)} $$

$$ \mathbf{P}(n) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\bigl[\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \mathbf{k}(n)\,\mathbf{x}'^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\bigr] $$

where $\lambda \in (0,1]$ is the forgetting factor.

---

## 2.5 Semi-Active Control — Operating Principles

Unlike active control, **semi-active systems cannot inject energy** — they can only *vary impedance*. Three canonical implementations:

### 2.5.1 Variable Stiffness Devices
Stiffness $k(t)$ modulated by a controlled actuator. Optimal stiffness minimises transmitted force:
$$k^{*}(t) = -m\,\omega^{2}\quad\text{(sky-hook approximation)}$$

### 2.5.2 Controllable Damping
Coefficient $c(t)$ adjusted to track the **sky-hook damping law**:
$$c_{\text{skyhook}} = c_{\max}\,\dfrac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{\omega^{2} + \omega_{n}^{2}} \quad\text{(for harmonic base excitation)}$$

### 2.5.3 Variable Orifice Damping
Orifice area $A_o(t)$ controlled by solenoid valve to vary the Reynolds number and hence the damping coefficient in hydraulic dampers.

### 2.5.4 Smart Materials (Emerging)

| Material | Mechanism | Application |
|:--------:|:---------:|:-----------:|
| **Magnetorheological (MR) fluid** | Magnetic field → yield stress | Dampers, mounts |
| **Electrorheological (ER) fluid** | Electric field → yield stress | Hydraulic mounts |
| **Piezoelectric (PZT)** | Voltage → strain | Adaptive stiffeners, panels |
| **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** | Temperature → phase shift | Tunable resonators |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sky-hook damping** is the gold-standard reference for semi-active suspension. It is *unachievable* with passive systems but can be *asymptotically approached* by switching $c(t)$ between two bounds at the right phase.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Meaning | Unit / Domain |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:--------------:|
| 1 | $p_{\text{sec}} = -p_{\text{prim}}$ | Perfect cancellation condition | Pa |
| 2 | $y(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} w_i(n)\,x(n-i)$ | Adaptive FIR output | dimensionless |
| 3 | $e(n) = d(n) - s(n)*y(n)$ | Residual error signal | Pa or V |
| 4 | $J(n) = \tfrac{1}{2}e^{2}(n)$ | Instantaneous MSE | V² or Pa² |
| 5 | $w_i(n+1) = w_i(n) + \mu\,e(n)\,x'(n-i)$ | FxLMS coefficient update | dimensionless |
| 6 | $0 < \mu < \dfrac{2}{N\,P_{x'}}$ | Stability bound (step size) | — |
| 7 | $L_{\text{insert}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{before}}}{p_{\text{after}}}\right)$ | Insertion loss in dB | dB |
| 8 | $c_{\text{skyhook}} = c_{\max}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_n^{2}}{\omega_n^{2}+\omega^{2}}\right)$ | Sky-hook optimum | Ns/m |
| 9 | $\lambda/4 = c/(4f)$ | Quarter-wave resonator length | m |
| 10 | $N_{\text{taps}} \approx \dfrac{f_s\,T_{\text{reverb}}}{\pi}$ | Filter length for IIR-equivalent FIR | — |

> **Notation key:** $\mu$ = step size, $N$ = filter length, $P_{x'}$ = power of filtered reference, $f_s$ = sample rate, $T_{\text{reverb}}$ = reverberation time, $c$ = speed of sound (≈ **343 m/s** at 20 °C).

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility & Real-World Mapping

| Sector | Application | Achieved Reduction |
