- **Active Noise Cancellation (ANC) / Active Vibration Control (AVC):** Control algorithms target harshness-specific frequency bands.
- **Sound Design (Active Sound Design - ASD):** Engineers synthesize **counter-phasic engine sound** through the audio system to mask harshness frequencies.
- **Compliance with ISO 2631-5:** Mandatory for whole-body vibration exposure limits in commercial vehicles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Frequency-Weighted RMS Acceleration

This derivation shows how a raw acceleration time-history $a(t)$ is converted into the **human-perceived** weighted acceleration $a_{w}$, which is the **backbone** of objective harshness quantification.

### Step 1: Define the Raw RMS Acceleration
The mean-square value of the acceleration signal $a(t)$ over an observation time $T$ is:

$$
a_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} a^{2}(t)\,dt
$$

This represents the **total vibrational energy** in the signal, but it does not account for the fact that the human body is not equally sensitive at all frequencies.

### Step 2: Transform to the Frequency Domain
Using the Fourier transform pair, we move from the time domain to the frequency domain. Let $A(f)$ be the Fourier transform of $a(t)$:

$$
A(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} a(t)\,e^{-j2\pi f t}\,dt
$$

By **Parseval's theorem**, the time-domain integral and the frequency-domain integral are equivalent in energy:

$$
\int_{0}^{T} a^{2}(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert A(f)\vert^{2}\,df
$$

Therefore, the mean-square value becomes:

$$
a_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert A(f)\vert^{2}\,df
$$

### Step 3: Introduce the Human Frequency-Weighting Function
The human body has frequency-dependent sensitivity, captured by the weighting function $W(f)$. For vertical whole-body vibration, the relevant standard is the **$W_{k}$ filter** defined in ISO 2631-1:

$$
W_{k}(f) = \begin{cases}
0.5\,\sqrt{f} & 0.5 \le f < 2 \\[4pt]
1.0\,\dfrac{1}{f} & 2 \le f \le 12 \\[4pt]
\dfrac{12}{f} & f > 12
\end{cases}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The $W_{k}$ filter peaks at approximately **4–8 Hz**, where the human torso and abdominal organs resonate. Frequencies above 12 Hz are de-weighted because the body naturally filters them out through seat cushioning and joint compliance.

### Step 4: Apply the Weighting in the Frequency Domain
Multiply the spectral energy by the squared weighting function:

$$
a_{w}^{2} = \int_{0}^{\infty} W^{2}(f)\,\dfrac{\vert A(f)\vert^{2}}{T}\,df
$$

In discrete form (which is what an FFT analyzer actually computes), the integral is replaced by a sum over $N$ frequency bins:

$$
a_{w}^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} W^{2}(f_{i})\,\vert A(f_{i})\vert^{2}\,\Delta f
$$

### Step 5: Extract the Square Root
The frequency-weighted RMS acceleration is the square root of the weighted mean-square value:

$$
a_{w} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} W^{2}(f_{i})\,\vert A(f_{i})\vert^{2}\,\Delta f}
$$

This is the single number that an OEM NVH engineer reports as the **objective harshness-relevant** metric.

### Step 6: Compute the Composite Vector Sum
For a tri-axial measurement (which is the KTU-recommended practice), the **weighted vibration total value** is:

$$
a_{wv} = \sqrt{(1.4\,a_{wx})^{2} + (1.4\,a_{wy})^{2} + a_{wz}^{2}}
$$

> **Logic of the 1.4 multiplier:** Empirical studies show that horizontal (x, y) vibrations are approximately **1.4 times more annoying** than vertical (z) vibrations at the same magnitude, because the human body is less accustomed to lateral excitation. Hence the multiplier.

### Step 7: Convert to the Harshness Decision Metric — VDV
For **transient events** (which dominate harshness complaints), the RMS metric is insufficient because it averages out peak amplitudes. The Vibration Dose Value (VDV) uses the **fourth power** of acceleration, making it **peak-sensitive**:

$$
VDV = \left[\int_{0}^{T} a_{w}^{4}(t)\,dt\right]^{1/4}
$$

> **Why the 4th power?** It is the lowest integer exponent that empirically correlates with **subjective discomfort ratings** across multiple ISO studies. Higher powers (6th, 8th) over-penalize single peaks; lower powers (1st, 2nd) under-penalize them.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Harshness Trigger Mapping

| Source | Excitation Type | Dominant Frequency | Receiver | Harshness Component |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Engine fire** (combustion) | Impulsive (50 Hz at idle) | 25 – 80 Hz | Seat track, floor | Tactile harshness |
| **Valvetrain clatter** | High-freq. impulsive | 1 – 5 kHz (structure-borne) | Firewall, dash | Aural harshness |
| **Driveline gear rattle** | Torque reversal | 200 – 800 Hz | Floor, tunnel | Aural + tactile |
| **Tire impact (bump)** | Half-sine impulse | 5 – 30 Hz | Seat base | Tactile harshness |
| **Cavity boom (HVAC off)** | Standing wave | 60 – 120 Hz | Eardrum | Aural harshness |
| **Steering shimmy** | Torsional oscillation | 8 – 15 Hz | Hands, arms | Local tactile |
| **Brake squeal** | Friction-induced | 1 – 16 kHz | Panel, ear | Aural harshness |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Harshness Perception Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Excitation] --> B[Powertrain]
    A --> C[Road Input]
    A --> D[Driveline]
    A --> E[Aerodynamic]
    B --> F[Structural Path]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> G[Acoustic Path]
    F --> H[Body in White]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Mounts and Bushings]
    I --> J[Cabin Cavity]
    J --> K[Occupant Biodynamic Model]
    K --> L{Aural Channel}
    K --> M{Tactile Channel}
    K --> N{Cognitive Channel}
    L --> O[Integrated Subjective Response]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Harshness Rating 1 to 10]
    P --> Q[Validation Against Objective Metrics]
    Q --> R{Engineering Decision}
    R --> S[Acceptable]
    R --> T[Design Iteration]
    T --> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Objective vs Subjective Harshness Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Accelerometer on Seat Track] --> B1[Data Acquisition]
    A2[Microphone at Driver Ear] --> B1
    A3[Load Cell on Pedal] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Frequency Analysis FFT]
    C1 --> D1[Apply ISO Weighting Wk Wd]
    D1 --> E1[Compute aw and VDV]
    D1 --> E2[Compute LpA in dB A]
    E1 --> F1[Harshness Objective Score]
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1{Correlation with Jury Tests}
    G1 --> H1[Valid Linear Model]
    G1 --> I1[Refine Model]
    I1 --> J1[Update Weighting Functions]
    J1 --> C1
    H1 --> K1[Final Harshness Index for KTU Submission]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Harshness Classification Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Harshness] --> B[Temporal Character]
    A --> C[Frequency Character]
    A --> D[Source Origin]
    B --> B1[Steady State Drone]
    B --> B2[Transient Impulse]
    C --> C1[Low Frequency 0.5 to 20 Hz]
    C --> C2[Mid Frequency 20 to 200 Hz]
    C --> C3[High Frequency 200 Hz to 10 kHz]
    D --> D1[Powertrain Induced]
    D --> D2[Road Induced]
    D --> D3[Aeroacoustic Induced]
    D1 --> D1a[Engine Combustion]
    D1 --> D1b[Valvetrain]
    D1 --> D1c[Driveline Gear Noise]
    D2 --> D2a[Tire Impact]
    D2 --> D2b[Suspension Knock]
    D2 --> D2c[Brake Squeal]
    D3 --> D3a[Wind Buffeting]
    D3 --> D3b[Mirror Whistle]
    D3 --> D3c[Pillar Vortex]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Compliance:** All node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed (e.g., `B1`, `C2`, `D3b`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names. All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown bold/italic/HTML tags inside double-quoted node labels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**  
**Define harshness in the context of automotive NVH. How is it distinguished from noise and vibration?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Harshness is the **subjective discomfort or unpleasantness** experienced by an occupant due to the combined effect of airborne noise, mechanical vibration, and transient impulses in a vehicle.  
**Distinction:**
- *Noise* is the **auditory perception** of airborne sound pressure fluctuations — purely aural.
- *Vibration* is the **mechanical oscillatory motion** of a body — purely tactile.
- *Harshness* is the **integrated perceptual response** combining both, plus a cognitive/psychological evaluation. It is **not a directly measurable physical quantity** but a subjective attribute correlated with objective metrics like $a_{w}$, $L_{p,A}$, and VDV. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for noise distinction, 1 mark for vibration distinction and subjective nature.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**  
**State the international standards applicable to whole-body vibration exposure in vehicles. What is the role of the $W_{k}$ filter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The applicable standards are **ISO 2631-1 (1997)** for general whole-body vibration evaluation and **ISO 2631-5 (2004)** specifically for **vibration containing multiple shocks** (the harshness case).  
The **$W_{k}$ filter** is a frequency-weighting function defined in ISO 2631-1 that models the **frequency-dependent sensitivity of the human body to vertical vibration**. It peaks around **4–8 Hz** (human torso resonance) and attenuates higher frequencies. Its role in harshness assessment is to ensure that the computed RMS acceleration $a_{w}$ reflects only the **biomechanically relevant** portion of the measured signal. *(1 mark for standards, 1 mark for filter role, 1 mark for biomechanical significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Q1-A]**  
**(a)** Explain the **source–path–receiver** model as applied to harshness generation in a passenger car. Identify **at least three sources**, **two structural paths**, and **the receiver** with appropriate biodynamic justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car is tested on a 4-poster shaker rig simulating a road bump. The seat-track accelerometer records a transient pulse. The peak acceleration is **$\hat{a} = 12\,\text{m/s}^2$**, the pulse duration is **$T = 0.25\,\text{s}$**, and the ISO $W_{k}$-weighted RMS is **$a_{w} = 1.8\,\text{m/s}^2$**. Compute the **(i) Crest Factor** and **(ii) Vibration Dose Value (VDV)**. State whether the ride is acceptable per ISO 2631-1 (typical comfort limit $a_{w} \le 0.315\,\text{m/s}^2$ for 8-hour exposure is **not** the right comparator for transient; use VDV $\le 9.1\,\text{m/s}^{1.75}$ as the short-term reference). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Source-Path-Receiver Model (7 marks):**

| Layer | Elements | Detailed Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| **Sources** | (1) Engine combustion firing impulses; (2) Tire–road interaction impacts; (3) Driveline gear-mesh excitation | Engine fires every 2 revolutions (4-stroke), creating 25–100 Hz impulsive torque. Tire impacts on rough road surface create broadband 5–200 Hz excitation. Gear-mesh frequencies in the transmission create tonal components at 200–1500 Hz. |
| **Structural Paths** | (1) Engine mounts → subframe → body-in-white → seat track; (2) Suspension links → body mounting points → floor pan | Path 1 transmits powertrain vibrations to the cabin. Path 2 transmits road-induced vibrations. Each path has its own **transfer function** $H(f)$ and **mobility** characteristics. |
| **Receiver** | The human occupant modeled as a 4-DOF biodynamic system: vertical pelvis, vertical spine, vertical shoulder, and rotational head | The seated human body has primary resonance at **4–6 Hz** (vertical), **1–2 Hz** (lateral), and **20–30 Hz** (head–shoulder). When vehicle excitation coincides with these resonances, harshness perception maximizes. |

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Crest Factor:**
The crest factor is the ratio of peak to RMS value:

$$
CF = \frac{\hat{a}}{a_{rms}}
$$

For a transient pulse, we approximate the RMS value as the $W_{k}$-weighted value $a_{w}$:

$$
CF = \frac{12\,\text{m/s}^2}{1.8\,\text{m/s}^2} = 6.67
$$

> **Interpretation:** A crest factor above **6** indicates a strongly impulsive signal, which is a strong contributor to **transient harshness**.

**(ii) Vibration Dose Value:**
For a roughly constant-amplitude weighted pulse over the duration $T$, the VDV simplifies to:

$$
VDV = \left[\int_{0}^{T} a_{w}^{4}(t)\,dt\right]^{1/4} = a_{w} \cdot T^{1/4}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
VDV = 1.8 \cdot (0.25)^{1/4}
$$

Compute the quarter-power of 0.25:

$$
(0.25)^{0.25} = (0.5^{2})^{0.25} = 0.5^{0.5} = \sqrt{0.5} = 0.7071
$$

Therefore:

$$
VDV = 1.8 \times 0.7071 = 1.273\,\text{m/s}^{1.75}
$$

**Comparison with ISO 2631-1 short-term reference VDV $\le 9.1\,\text{m/s}^{1.75}$:**

$$
VDV = 1.273\,\text{m/s}^{1.75} \ll 9.1\,\text{m/s}^{1.75}
$$

**Conclusion:** The transient pulse is well within the ISO short-term comfort limit. The ride is **acceptable** for a single event of this character, but if such events occur at high repetition rate (e.g., cobblestone road), the cumulative VDV may exceed the limit, and **ride-harshness redesign** (mount stiffness reduction, seat suspension upgrade) may be required.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Q1-B]**  
**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain how harshness is measured objectively and correlated with subjective jury ratings. List the **transducers and signal-processing steps**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A driver reports harshness during tip-in (sudden acceleration) at **3000 rpm in 2nd gear**. The seat-track accelerometer captures a vertical burst with the following octave-band data:  

| Octave Center (Hz) | 8 | 16 | 31.5 | 63 | 125 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $a_{i,rms}$ (m/s²) | 0.6 | 0.9 | 1.4 | 0.8 | 0.3 |
| $W_{i}$ | 1.0 | 0.5 | 0.25 | 0.125 | 0.0631 |

Compute the **single-number frequency-weighted RMS acceleration** $a_{w}$. Identify the **dominant contributing band**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Objective–Subjective Correlation Block Diagram (7 marks):**

**Transducers required:**
- **Accelerometers** (seat track, steering column, pedal — typically 3 per location, tri-axial PCB/Brüel & Kjær make)
- **Microphone** (B&K Type 4189 at driver's ear position, head-rest mount)
- **Load cells** (on pedals for force feedback)
- **CAN bus data** (engine RPM, vehicle speed, gear position for context)

**Signal-processing chain:**
1. **Data acquisition** (sampling rate $\ge 51.2$ kHz, anti-alias filter at Nyquist)
2. **Time-domain recording** during defined maneuver (e.g., wide-open-throttle tip-in)
3. **FFT analysis** to obtain $\vert A(f)\vert$
4. **Apply $W_{k}$/$W_{d}$ weighting** (ISO 2631-1)
5. **Compute $a_{w}$, $L_{p,A}$, VDV, Crest Factor, Sharpness**
6. **Run jury tests** (10–15 trained subjects, 1–10 scale rating)
7. **Linear/Multiple regression** of objective indices against subjective mean opinion scores (MOS)
8. **Establish correlation coefficient** $R^{2} \ge 0.7$ for valid model
9. **Engineering decision** based on combined objective + subjective evidence

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Apply the discrete weighted-RMS formula:

$$
a_{w} = \sqrt{\sum_{i} (W_{i} \cdot a_{i,rms})^{2}}
$$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

| Band (Hz) | $a_{i,rms}$ | $W_{i}$ | $W_{i} \cdot a_{i,rms}$ | $(W_{i} \cdot a_{i,rms})^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | 0.6 | 1.0 | 0.6 | 0.36 |
| 16 | 0.9 | 0.5 | 0.45 | 0.2025 |
| 31.5 | 1.4 | 0.25 | 0.35 | 0.1225 |
| 63 | 0.8 | 0.125 | 0.10 | 0.0100 |
| 125 | 0.3 | 0.0631 | 0.0189 | 0.000358 |

**Sum the squared terms:**

$$
\sum = 0.36 + 0.2025 + 0.1225 + 0.0100 + 0.000358 = 0.69536
$$

**Apply the square root:**

$$
a_{w} = \sqrt{0.69536} = 0.834\,\text{m/s}^{2}
$$

**Identify the dominant band:** The **8 Hz** octave contributes $0.36 / 0.69536 = 51.8\%$ of the total weighted energy. The 16 Hz and 31.5 Hz bands contribute 29.1% and 17.6% respectively.

> **Conclusion:** The **8 Hz octave band is the dominant harshness contributor**. Since 8 Hz lies in the **whole-body vertical resonance range** (4–8 Hz), this indicates a **powertrain-resonance coupling** at tip-in, likely through the engine mounts. **Remedial design action:** increase mount damping or shift the engine mount resonance to below 5 Hz.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Harshness Definition Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating harshness with noise or vibration alone** — Examiners specifically test if you understand harshness is a *subjective composite*. Writing "harshness is unwanted sound" costs full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — VDV must be in **m/s¹·⁷⁵**; $a_{w}$ in **m/s²**; $L_{p,A}$ in **dB(A)**. Omitting units loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the weighting step** — Direct RMS of raw acceleration *without* $W_{k}$ weighting is **not** the harshness metric. Always apply ISO weighting.
> 4. **Mixing ISO 2631-1 (steady state) and ISO 2631-5 (transient)** — For impulsive harshness, the examiner expects VDV (4th-power metric), not the simpler $a_{w}$. Using the wrong metric loses methodology marks.
> 5. **No mention of jury/subjective evaluation** — Harshness is fundamentally a *subjective* attribute. Any answer that omits subjective assessment methodology is incomplete.
> 6. **Computational errors in crest factor or VDV exponent** — VDV uses 4th power and ¼ power — confusion with 2nd-power RMS loses full marks in numerical questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Harshness Definition (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Harshness = **subjective discomfort** from combined noise + vibration + impulse. *Not* a directly measurable physical quantity.
- **NVH Trinity:** **N** (auditory) + **V** (tactile) + **H** (composite subjective).
- **Source-Path-Receiver** is the **canonical analytical framework**.
- **Dominant harshness frequency band:** **4–8 Hz** (vertical whole-body resonance) and **8–200 Hz** (tactile harshness).
- **Key objective metrics:** $a_{w}$ (weighted RMS, m/s²), $L_{p,A}$ (dB(A)), **VDV** (m/s¹·⁷⁵), **Crest Factor** (dimensionless), **Sharpness** (acum).
- **Weighting standards:** **ISO 2631-1** (general), **ISO 2631-5** (transient shocks), **ISO 532-B** (Zwicker loudness), **DIN 45692** (Bismarck sharpness).
- **Composite vector formula:** $a_{wv} = \sqrt{(1.4 a_{wx})^{2} + (1.4 a_{wy})^{2} + a_{wz}^{2}}$ — remember the **1.4 horizontal multiplier**.
- **VDV formula:** $VDV = a_{w} \cdot T^{1/4}$ (for constant-amplitude pulse) — remember the **4th-power / ¼-power** structure.
- **Crest Factor:** $CF = \hat{a} / a_{rms}$ — values **> 6** indicate impulsive harshness.
- **Causal categories:** Powertrain-induced, road-induced, aeroacoustic-induced.
- **Subjective evaluation:** Always paired with objective metrics; **$R^{2} \ge 0.7$** is the standard correlation threshold.
- **Standards to memorize:** ISO 2631-1 (1997), ISO 2631-5 (2004), ISO 532-B, DIN 45692, IEC 61672.
- **Engineering levers to reduce harshness:** Mount stiffness/damping tuning, body-in-white modal redesign, seat suspension, ANC/AVC, Active Sound Design.
- **Units mantra:** $a_{w}$ in **m/s²**, $L_{p,A}$ in **dB(A)**, VDV in **m/s¹·⁷⁵**, Sharpness in **acum**, Loudness in **sone**, Frequency in **Hz**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Psychological Effect of Noise and Vibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Psychological Effect of Noise and Vibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Psychological Effect of Noise and Vibration** refers to the measurable and subjective human responses — both conscious and subconscious — elicited when an individual is exposed to acoustic or mechanical oscillatory stimuli. These responses span the spectrum of human experience, ranging from mild **annoyance** and **perceptual discomfort** to severe **physiological stress**, **cognitive impairment**, and **motion sickness**. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this domain is classified under **human response to environmental stressors** and is studied using standardized **psychoacoustic** and **mechanoreception** models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** The psychological effect is NOT the same as the physical effect. A noise level of 90 dB has a *physical* intensity of 10⁻³ W/m², but its *psychological* effect is described by **loudness (sones)**, **sharpness (acum)**, and **annoyance (psy units)** — quantities derived from human perception curves, not raw physics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a KERALA STATE ROAD TRANSPORT CORPORATION (KSRTC) ordinary bus. Two scenarios:

- **Scenario A**: The bus is silent but the engine is idling perfectly — *you feel calm*.
- **Scenario B**: The bus emits a 90 dB low-frequency rumble, the seat vibrates at 8 Hz with 0.5 m/s² RMS acceleration, and the steering column shakes — *you feel irritated, tired, and your reading concentration drops by 30%*.

The **physical energy** in both cases is nearly identical (mostly from the engine), but the **psychological impact** is dramatically different. This is because human perception is **frequency-weighted**, **non-linear**, and **time-cumulative**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Humans are NOT linear sensors. A doubling of acoustic pressure does NOT feel twice as loud — it feels only ~26% louder (Stevens' Power Law). The brain applies its own "filter bank" shaped by evolutionary response curves (A-weighting, ISO 2631 Wₖ weighting, Zwicker critical bands).

### 1.3 Core Metrics and Standardized Indicators

The following **bold-highlighted** metrics define the psychological response landscape in this module:

| **Metric** | **Symbol** | **Unit** | **Domain** | **Standard** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sound Pressure Level | $L_p$ | **dB** | Noise | ISO 1996 |
| A-weighted SPL | $L_A$ | **dB(A)** | Noise (perceived) | IEC 61672 |
| Loudness | $N$ | **sone** | Noise | ISO 532 (Zwicker) |
| Loudness Level | $L_N$ | **phon** | Noise | ISO 532 |
| Sharpness | $S$ | **acum** | Noise quality | DIN 45692 |
| Annoyance | — | **psy** | Noise | ISO/TS 15666 |
| Vibration Total Value | $a_v$ | **m/s²** | Vibration | ISO 2631-1 |
| Vibration Dose Value | $VDV$ | **m/s^1.75** | Vibration | ISO 2631-1 |
| Crest Factor | — | **dimensionless** | Vibration | ISO 2631 |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** The KTU 2024 examiner will frequently interchange **dB**, **phon**, and **sone** in questions. Memorize that **phon** is a perceptual level (10 phon = 1 sone doubling rate) and **dB** is purely physical.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Psychological Effects

The psychological response cascade can be decomposed into **five hierarchical tiers**, ordered from least to most severe:

1. **Perceptual Tier** — Detection, recognition, and discrimination of stimuli.
2. **Cognitive Tier** — Attention shift, speech intelligibility loss, task performance degradation.
3. **Emotional Tier** — Annoyance, irritation, startle response, anxiety.
4. **Behavioral Tier** — Activity disruption, sleep disturbance, avoidance behavior.
5. **Pathological Tier** — Stress hormone elevation (cortisol), hearing loss (NIHL), vibration-induced white finger (VWF), cardiovascular hypertrophy.

### 2.2 Why and How Each Tier Operates

- **Perceptual Tier — Why?** Because the human auditory system has finite frequency resolution (Critical Bandwidth = ~1 Bark at 1 kHz). It uses **place theory** (basilar membrane tonotopy) and **rate theory**.
- **Cognitive Tier — Why?** Because the brain has limited attentional resources (Miller's 7±2 rule). When noise/vibration occupies a sensory channel, **cognitive bandwidth is stolen** for filtering, leaving less for the primary task.
- **Emotional Tier — Why?** Because the limbic system (amygdala) tags unexpected or threatening stimuli with negative valence — an evolutionary survival mechanism.
- **Behavioral Tier — Why?** Because humans adapt by **avoidance learning** (Skinnerian negative reinforcement).
- **Pathological Tier — Why?** Because chronic sympathetic nervous system activation elevates cortisol → hypertension, immunosuppression, gastric ulcers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Logic Hook:** The examiner LOVES the phrase *"explain the mechanism by which prolonged exposure leads to VWF."* Always cite: **hand-arm vibration → vascular smooth muscle hypertrophy → reduced capillary blood flow → finger blanching**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following consolidated table contains **every formula** you must memorize for Module 4 numerical and descriptive questions:

| **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Variables** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stevens' Power Law (Loudness) | $\Psi = k \cdot (\Phi - \Phi_0)^n$ | $\Psi$ = sensation, $k$ = constant, $\Phi$ = stimulus, $\Phi_0$ = threshold, $n$ = exponent (0.67 for SPL, 0.6 for vibration) | sones, dimensionless | Loudness scaling |
| Sone-to-Phon Relation | $L_N = 40 + 10 \cdot \log_2(S)$ | $L_N$ = loudness level, $S$ = sone value | phon | Loudness conversion |
| Phon-to-Sone Relation | $S = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$ | $S$ = loudness, $L_N$ = loudness level | sone | Loudness conversion |
| A-Weighting Adjustment | $L_A = L_p + A(f)$ | $A(f)$ = frequency-dependent correction | dB(A) | Perceived noise |
| Vibration Dose Value | $VDV = \left( \int_{0}^{T} a_w^4(t) \, dt \right)^{1/4}$ | $a_w(t)$ = weighted acceleration, $T$ = duration | m/s^1.75 | Cumulative vibration |
| RMS Vibration Total Value | $a_v = \left( \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} a_w^2(t) \, dt \right)^{1/2}$ | $a_w(t)$ = weighted acceleration | m/s² | Steady vibration |
| Crest Factor | $CF = \dfrac{\hat{a}}{a_{rms}}$ | $\hat{a}$ = peak, $a_{rms}$ = RMS | dimensionless | Impulsive content |
| Stevens' Vibration Law | $V = k_v \cdot (a - a_0)^{0.6}$ | $V$ = perceived magnitude, $a$ = acceleration, $a_0$ = threshold | dimensionless | Vibration perception |
| Motion Sickness Dose | $MSDV = \left( \int_{0}^{T} a_w^2(t) \, dt \right)^{1/2}$ | $a_w$ = weighted (Wₖ filter) acceleration | m/s² | Sea/motion sickness |
| Annoyance Score (psy) | $A = \alpha \cdot L_{Aeq} + \beta \cdot N_{50} + \gamma$ | $L_{Aeq}$ = equivalent SPL, $N_{50}$ = statistical loudness | psy | Community noise |
| Zwicker Loudness | $N = 0.08 \cdot \int_{0}^{24 \, \text{Bark}} N' \cdot \Delta z \, dz$ | $N'$ = specific loudness, $z$ = critical band rate | sone | Stationary noise |
| Weighted SPL for Sleep | $L_{Aeq,8h} \leq 30$ dB(A) | — | dB(A) | WHO night guideline |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL Table-Syntax Rule:** The vertical pipe `$\vert$` in $\vert a \vert$ has been rendered as `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table. In your written exam, always write it as `\mid` or `\vert` in LaTeX — **never** as a bare pipe character inside a table cell.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive NVH Engineering:** Minimizing cabin boom at 30 Hz using tuned mass dampers (psychological comfort during 6-hour journeys).
- **Aerospace:** Helicopter seat design targeting Wₖ-weighted vibration below 0.315 m/s² to prevent pilot fatigue.
- **Construction Equipment:** ISO 2631 VDV < 9.1 m/s^1.75 for hand-arm vibration to prevent Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS).
- **Urban Planning:** Day-evening-night noise level $L_{den}$ < 55 dB(A) per EU Environmental Noise Directive to prevent sleep disturbance.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphone haptic feedback tuned to 200 Hz for maximum perceived "click" intensity with minimum power.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Physical SPL to Perceptual Sones

**Problem Setup:** A KTU 2024 numerical problem typically gives an SPL of, say, $L_p = 80$ dB at 1 kHz, and asks you to find the **perceived loudness in sones** and the **percentage increase in loudness** if SPL rises by 6 dB.

**Step 1 — Establish the Standard Reference.**
By ISO 226, a 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL is defined to have a loudness of **1 sone**. This is the **psychoacoustic anchor**.

**Step 2 — Apply Stevens' Power Law.**
The perceived magnitude $S$ is related to SPL by a power law. Integrating the relationship between phon and sone gives the closed-form expression:

$$
S = 2^{(L_N - 40) / 10}
$$

Here, $L_N$ is the loudness level in **phon**. For a 1 kHz pure tone, phon = dB SPL (by definition), so $L_N = L_p = 80$ phon.

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerically.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 2^{(80 - 40) / 10} \\
  &= 2^{40/10} \\
  &= 2^{4} \\
  &= 16 \text{ sones}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the 6 dB Increase Case.**
Now $L_p = 86$ dB:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_2 &= 2^{(86 - 40) / 10} \\
    &= 2^{46/10} \\
    &= 2^{4.6} \\
    &= 24.25 \text{ sones}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Increase.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Increase} &= \frac{S_2 - S_1}{S_1} \times 100 \\
                    &= \frac{24.25 - 16}{16} \times 100 \\
                    &= \frac{8.25}{16} \times 100 \\
                    &= 51.6 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** A 6 dB physical increase (a 4× energy increase!) yields only ~52% perceptual increase. **This is the central paradox of psychoacoustics** that the KTU examiner tests.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Vibration Dose Value (VDV) and Health Risk

**Problem Setup:** A construction worker operates a jackhammer for 4 hours. The weighted RMS acceleration is $a_w = 2.5$ m/s² (Wₕ filter, dominant at 50 Hz). Find the **VDV** and assess the **health risk** per ISO 2631.

**Step 1 — Identify the Formula.**

$$
VDV = \left( \int_{0}^{T} a_w^{4}(t) \, dt \right)^{1/4}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify for Steady-State (Constant $a_w$).**
When $a_w$ is constant over duration $T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
VDV &= \left( a_w^{4} \cdot T \right)^{1/4} \\
    &= a_w \cdot T^{1/4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values.**
Given $a_w = 2.5$ m/s², $T = 4 \text{ h} = 4 \times 3600 = 14400$ s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T^{1/4} &= 14400^{0.25} \\
        &= (1.44 \times 10^{4})^{0.25} \\
        &= 10^{4 \times 0.25} \cdot 1.44^{0.25} \\
        &= 10 \cdot 1.095 \\
        &= 10.95 \text{ s}^{1/4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Final VDV.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VDV &= 2.5 \times 10.95 \\
    &= 27.38 \text{ m/s}^{1.75}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Result per ISO 2631-1 Health Risk Zones.**

| **VDV Range (m/s^1.75)** | **Health Risk Zone** | **Action Required** |
|---|---|---|
| $< 9.1$ | Comfort preserved | No action |
| $9.1$ to $15$ | Slight reduction in comfort | Monitor |
| $15$ to $20$ | Reduced comfort | Investigate |
| $\mathbf{> 20}$ | Health risk to operator | **Immediate remediation** |
| **27.38** | **High health risk** | **Reduce exposure time** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **RMS Total Value** $a_v$ (units m/s²) with **VDV** (units m/s^1.75). They are related by $VDV = a_v \cdot T^{1/4} \cdot CF^{1/2}$ only in special cases. **Always state units.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent Continuous SPL ($L_{Aeq}$) for Time-Varying Noise

**Problem Setup:** A classroom is exposed to: 75 dB(A) for 3 hours, 60 dB(A) for 2 hours, and 50 dB(A) for 3 hours. Find the 8-hour $L_{Aeq}$.

**Step 1 — Write the Definition.**

$$
L_{Aeq, T} = 10 \cdot \log_{10} \left( \frac{1}{T} \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Each Level to Linear Power Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
10^{75/10} &= 10^{7.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{7} \\
10^{60/10} &= 10^{6.0} = 1.000 \times 10^{6} \\
10^{50/10} &= 10^{5.0} = 1.000 \times 10^{5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Weighted Energy Sum.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10} &= (3 \times 3600)(3.162 \times 10^{7}) \\
&\quad + (2 \times 3600)(1.000 \times 10^{6}) \\
&\quad + (3 \times 3600)(1.000 \times 10^{5})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= 10800 \times 3.162 \times 10^{7} + 7200 \times 10^{6} + 10800 \times 10^{5} \\
&= 3.415 \times 10^{11} + 7.200 \times 10^{9} + 1.080 \times 10^{9} \\
&= 3.415 \times 10^{11} + 0.072 \times 10^{11} + 0.0108 \times 10^{11} \\
&= 3.498 \times 10^{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by Total Time.**

$$
T = 3 + 2 + 3 = 8 \text{ hours} = 28800 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T} \sum t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10} = \frac{3.498 \times 10^{11}}{28800} = 1.214 \times 10^{7}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Logarithmic Conversion.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{Aeq, 8h} &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}(1.214 \times 10^{7}) \\
            &= 10 \cdot (7 + \log_{10}(1.214)) \\
            &= 10 \cdot (7 + 0.0843) \\
            &= 10 \cdot 7.0843 \\
            &= 70.84 \text{ dB(A)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Shortcut:** Remember the **3 dB rule** — doubling the duration of exposure at the same level adds 3 dB to $L_{Aeq}$; halving the duration subtracts 3 dB. The 75 dB(A) for 3 h contributes almost entirely, the 50 dB(A) is negligible.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Psychological Effects

The following matrix compares **psychological response curves** for noise vs. vibration across multiple dimensions:

| **Dimension** | **Noise (Auditory)** | **Whole-Body Vibration** | **Hand-Arm Vibration** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Sensor** | Cochlea (inner ear) | Vestibular + mechanoreceptors | Pacinian corpuscles |
| **Frequency Range** | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | 0.5 – 80 Hz | 5 – 1500 Hz |
| **Weighting Filter** | A-weighting (dB(A)) | Wₖ (ISO 2631) | Wₕ (ISO 5349) |
| **Perception Threshold** | 0 dB SPL | 0.001 m/s² | 1 m/s² |
| **Comfort Threshold** | < 45 dB(A) | < 0.315 m/s² | < 2.5 m/s² |
| **Major Pathology** | NIHL (Noise-Induced Hearing Loss) | Motion sickness, spinal injury | VWF / HAVS |
| **Time Constant** | Fast (50 ms) | Slow (cumulative VDV) | Cumulative (A(8) dose) |
| **Annoyance Index** | $L_{den}$ | $VTV_{max}$ / $VDV$ | $A(8)$ exposure |
| **KTU Favourite Question** | "Calculate sones from phon" | "Evaluate ISO 2631 VDV" | "Assess HAVS risk" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Topic Note:** The **8-hour energy-equivalent** formulation $A(8)$ used for hand-arm vibration is mathematically identical to $L_{Aeq,8h}$ in structure — a unifying concept that frequently appears in KTU Module 4 questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Psychological Response Pathway

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete causal chain** from physical stimulus to pathological outcome, illustrating the multi-tier cascade introduced in Section 2.1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Stimulus Source] --> B[Stimulus Propagation Medium]
    B --> C[Receptor Activation]
    C --> D[Neural Encoding]
    D --> E[Perceptual Tier]
    D --> F[Cognitive Tier]
    D --> G[Emotional Tier]
    D --> H[Behavioral Tier]
    D --> I[Pathological Tier]
    E --> J[Subjective Rating Scale]
    F --> K[Task Performance Metrics]
    G --> L[Annoyance Survey Score]
    H --> M[Activity Disruption Index]
    I --> N[Clinical Diagnosis]
    J --> O[Engineering Mitigation Action]
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Design Iteration Loop]
    P --> A
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define "loudness" and "loudness level." How are they related mathematically?**

**Model Answer:**

**Loudness ($N$):** It is the *intensity* of the auditory sensation perceived by a listener, expressed in **sones**. By definition, 1 sone corresponds to a 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL. A sound judged twice as loud is 2 sones; half as loud is 0.5 sones.

**Loudness Level ($L_N$):** It is the SPL of a 1 kHz reference tone that is judged equally loud as the given sound, expressed in **phons**. Numerically, $L_N = 40$ phon corresponds to 1 sone.

**Mathematical Relationship:**

$$
L_N = 40 + 10 \cdot \log_2(S) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad S = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}
$$

**Significance:** Every doubling of sone value adds 10 phons of loudness level.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List the five ISO 2631 health risk zones for VDV with their boundary values.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Zone** | **VDV Range (m/s^1.75)** | **Comment** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $< 9.1$ | Comfort preserved |
| 2 | $9.1$ – $15$ | Slight reduction in comfort |
| 3 | $15$ – $20$ | Reduced comfort |
| 4 | $20$ – $30$ | Health risk likely |
| 5 | $> 30$ | High health risk |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the mathematical relationship between loudness in **sones** and loudness level in **phons**. A pure tone has a measured SPL of 70 dB at 2 kHz. Using ISO 226 equal-loudness contours, find the **loudness level in phons** and the **loudness in sones**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A machine operator is exposed to a constant weighted hand-arm vibration acceleration of $a_w = 3.0$ m/s² for 6 hours. Calculate the **Vibration Dose Value (VDV)** and assess the **health risk zone** per ISO 2631. What remedial action would you recommend? **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Derivation.**
By definition, a 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL is the reference for 1 sone. Stevens' Power Law for loudness is:

$$
S = k \cdot (I - I_0)^{0.3}
$$

where $I$ is intensity. Applying the boundary conditions ($S = 1$ sone at $I = I_{ref}$ corresponding to 40 dB SPL) and noting that each doubling of loudness corresponds to a 10 phon increase (the doubling rate), we get:

$$
\frac{dS}{S} = \frac{\ln 2}{10} \cdot dL_N
$$

Integrating:

$$
\ln S = \frac{\ln 2}{10} (L_N - 40) + \ln 1
$$

$$
S = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}
$$

**Step 2 — Find Phon from SPL at 2 kHz.**
From ISO 226 equal-loudness contour table, a 2 kHz tone at 70 dB SPL has a loudness level of approximately **72 phons** (2 kHz is more sensitive than 1 kHz reference).

**Step 3 — Convert to Sones.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 2^{(72 - 40)/10} \\
  &= 2^{32/10} \\
  &= 2^{3.2} \\
  &= 9.19 \text{ sones}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $L_N = 72$ phon, $S = 9.19$ sones.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Apply the VDV Formula for Constant $a_w$.**

$$
VDV = a_w \cdot T^{1/4}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $T^{1/4}$.**

$$
T = 6 \times 3600 = 21600 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T^{1/4} = 21600^{0.25} = (2.16 \times 10^{4})^{0.25} = 10 \cdot 2.16^{0.25}
$$

$$
2.16^{0.25} = e^{0.25 \cdot \ln 2.16} = e^{0.25 \cdot 0.7701} = e^{0.1925} = 1.2124
$$

$$
T^{1/4} = 12.124 \text{ s}^{1/4}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute VDV.**

$$
VDV = 3.0 \times 12.124 = 36.37 \text{ m/s}^{1.75}
$$

**Step 4 — Assess Risk.**
$VDV = 36.37$ m/s^1.75 falls in the **> 30 zone (High Health Risk)**.

**Remedial Actions:**

1. Reduce exposure time per ISO 2631 guidance: to stay under 20 m/s^1.75, require $T < (20/3.0)^4 = 197.5$ s ≈ **3.3 minutes**.
2. Install anti-vibration gloves (limited effectiveness).
3. Use anti-vibration tool mountings.
4. Rotate operators to limit cumulative daily exposure.
5. Conduct regular medical surveillance for Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS) including VWF, neuropathy, and musculoskeletal disorders.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A factory operates 3 machines simultaneously. Machine A: 80 dB(A) for 8 hours; Machine B: 70 dB(A) for 8 hours; Machine C: 60 dB(A) for 4 hours. Calculate the **8-hour equivalent continuous A-weighted SPL** ($L_{Aeq,8h}$). Comment on the **psychological impact** on the workers and the **threshold limit value (TLV)** per OSHA. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Explain the **mechanism of Vibration-Induced White Finger (VWF)**. Discuss how **Stevens' Power Law** is applied in vibration perception, and explain why the **Wₖ weighting filter** is used in ISO 2631. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Write $L_{Aeq}$ Formula.**

$$
L_{Aeq, T} = 10 \cdot \log_{10} \left( \frac{1}{T} \sum t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Each Level to Linear.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
10^{80/10} &= 10^{8.0} = 1.0 \times 10^{8} \\
10^{70/10} &= 10^{7.0} = 1.0 \times 10^{7} \\
10^{60/10} &= 10^{6.0} = 1.0 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Weighted Time-Energy Sum.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10} &= (8 \times 3600)(1.0 \times 10^{8}) \\
&\quad + (8 \times 3600)(1.0 \times 10^{7}) \\
&\quad + (4 \times 3600)(1.0 \times 10^{6}) \\
&= 28800 \times 10^{8} + 28800 \times 10^{7} + 14400 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 2.880 \times 10^{12} + 0.288 \times 10^{12} + 0.0144 \times 10^{12} \\
&= 3.1824 \times 10^{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by 8 hours = 28800 s.**

$$
\frac{3.1824 \times 10^{12}}{28800} = 1.105 \times 10^{8}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dB.**

$$
L_{Aeq,8h} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(1.105 \times 10^{8}) = 80 + 10 \cdot \log_{10}(1.105) = 80 + 0.43 = 80.43 \text{ dB(A)}
$$

**Psychological Impact:**
At 80.43 dB(A) for 8 hours, workers experience: (i) elevated annoyance, (ii) speech interference at distances > 1.5 m, (iii) reduced task concentration, (iv) elevated cortisol after 2+ hours, (v) increased risk of NIHL after 10+ years.

**OSHA TLV:** 85 dB(A) for 8 hours (action level); 90 dB(A) (permissible limit). **The factory is below the TLV but above the comfort threshold (55 dB(A))**, so hearing protection should be provided.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**VWF Mechanism:**
Hand-arm vibration transmitted through tool handles induces repetitive micro-trauma to the digital arteries, smooth muscle, and nerves. The cascade is:

1. **Mechanical Phase:** High-frequency vibration damages vascular endothelium.
2. **Vascular Phase:** Smooth muscle hypertrophy and arteriosclerosis narrow the digital arteries.
3. **Neurological Phase:** Damage to mechanoreceptors and sympathetic nerves.
4. **Trigger Phase:** Cold exposure or stress causes vasospasm in compromised vessels.
5. **Clinical Phase:** Fingers blanch white (Raynaud's phenomenon localized to digits) — visible demarcation, numbness, tingling, pain on rewarming.

**Stevens' Power Law in Vibration:**
Perceived vibration magnitude $V$ scales as a power of the physical magnitude:

$$
V = k_v \cdot (a - a_0)^{0.6}
$$

where $a$ is acceleration, $a_0$ is the threshold of perception (~0.001 m/s² at 10 Hz), and the exponent 0.6 is empirically determined.

**Wₖ Weighting Filter:**
The Wₖ filter (ISO 2631-1) approximates the human body's sensitivity to vertical vibration:

- 0.5 Hz: 0 dB (low sensitivity, vestibular region)
- 4–8 Hz: 0 dB (peak sensitivity, abdominal resonance)
- 16 Hz: −6 dB (decreasing)
- 80 Hz: −24 dB (low sensitivity)

The filter reflects the **transfer function** from seat input to spinal/pelvic response, weighted toward frequencies that cause the most discomfort and harm. **Different filters exist for W_d (whole-body horizontal), Wₕ (hand-arm), W_f (head/neck)**, each tuned to a specific human sub-system.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting exponent in Stevens' Law:** Many students write 0.3 (auditory) when it should be 0.6 (vibration). **Memorize the correct exponent per domain.**
> 2. **Mixing up VDV units (m/s^1.75) with $a_v$ units (m/s²):** The $1.75$ exponent is the giveaway that VDV is the 4th-root quantity.
> 3. **Using SPL (dB) instead of A-weighted dB(A) in TLV questions:** This loses 1–2 marks immediately.
> 4. **Forgetting the 8-hour normalization in $L_{Aeq}$:** Always divide by 28800 s, not by the actual total if it differs.
> 5. **Not stating ISO standard number:** A numerical answer without citing ISO 2631-1 / ISO 5349 / ISO 1996 is treated as incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4**

- ✅ **Loudness in sones** is **not** SPL in dB — they are related by a logarithmic formula $S = 2^{(L_N-40)/10}$.
- ✅ **Phon** = dB SPL of equally-loud 1 kHz tone — so 1 sone = 40 phon by definition.
- ✅ **Stevens' Power Law exponent:** 0.3 for loudness, 0.6 for vibration, 1.0 for brightness — memorize these three.
- ✅ **A-weighting** adjusts dB SPL to match the **40-phon Fletcher-Munson contour** for psychological accuracy.
- ✅ **VDV** uses the **4th power** of acceleration and 1/4 root — making it a "fourth moment" quantity — extremely sensitive to peaks.
- ✅ **ISO 2631 risk zones:** 9.1 / 15 / 20 / 30 m/s^1.75 — memorize as $9{-}15{-}20{-}30$.
- ✅ **Wₖ filter** peaks at 4–8 Hz (resonance of human spine), Wₕ filter peaks at 50–150 Hz (hand-arm sensitivity).
- ✅ **OSHA TLV:** 85 dB(A) action level, 90 dB(A) PEL for 8 hours.
- ✅ **WHO guideline:** 30 dB(A) for sleep, 35 dB(A) for bedrooms, 40 dB(A) for classrooms.
- ✅ **VWF mechanism** = endothelium damage → smooth muscle hypertrophy → cold-induced vasospasm → finger blanching.
- ✅ **3 dB rule:** doubling duration of exposure at same level adds 3 dB to $L_{Aeq}$; halving removes 3 dB.
- ✅ **Crest factor** > 9 indicates impulsive vibration (uses VDV, not RMS, for evaluation).
- ✅ **Loudness is a perceptual quantity — not energy — and must be calculated using ISO 532 (Zwicker) or ISO 532-B (Stevens-Mark VI).**
- ✅ **Cross-topic bridge:** Both $L_{Aeq,8h}$ and $A(8)$ vibration use the same **logarithmic time-average structure** — recognize this pattern.
- ✅ **In KTU numericals:** always state **units**, **standard reference**, and **assumed weighting filter** explicitly.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Perception is not physics. Weight, integrate, log, and cite the standard — in that order."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sound Quality Metric
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sound Quality Metric

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Sound Quality?

**Sound Quality** is the psychoacoustic metric-based assessment of how a sound is *perceived* by a human listener, rather than how loud it *measures* physically. While traditional SPL (Sound Pressure Level) tells us *how much* sound energy exists, Sound Quality Metrics tell us *what the sound actually feels like* to the human ear and brain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> Sound Quality is the measure of the adequacy of a sound for the purposes for which it is intended, characterized by psychoacoustic descriptors such as **Loudness**, **Sharpness**, **Roughness**, **Fluctuation Strength**, and **Tonality**, evaluated in standardized units (sone, acum, asper, vacil, tu).

### 1.2 Sound Quality vs Sound Quantity — Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you taste a cup of tea:

| Parameter | Tea Analogy | Sound Engineering Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Sound Quantity** | Volume of liquid (mL) | Sound Pressure Level (dB SPL) |
| **Sound Quality** | Taste, aroma, aftertaste | Loudness, Sharpness, Roughness, Tonality |

> [!NOTE]
> A sound at **90 dB(A)** could be the soothing roar of a luxury car engine or the irritating buzz of a faulty hair dryer — both measure the same decibel level, but their **sound quality is drastically different**. Sound Quality Metrics quantify this *feel*.

### 1.3 Why Sound Quality Metrics?

1. **A-weighted dB is insufficient** — A-weighting over-simplifies human hearing (only one frequency weighting curve for all sounds).
2. **Time-varying signals** — Modern NVH signals (EV whine, motor noise) are non-stationary and need temporal modeling.
3. **Product branding** — Automotive OEMs (BMW, Mercedes) engineer their door-close "thud" using sharpness and loudness targets.
4. **Regulatory pressure** — EV AVAS (Acoustic Vehicle Alerting Systems) standards require explicit sound quality targets.

### 1.4 Standardized Units of Sound Quality

| Metric | Unit | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Loudness** | sone (sone) | $N$ or $S$ |
| **Loudness Level** | phon (phon) | $L_N$ |
| **Sharpness** | acum (acum) | $S$ |
| **Roughness** | asper (asper) | $R$ |
| **Fluctuation Strength** | vacil (vacil) | $F$ |
| **Tonality** | tu (tonality unit) | $K$ |
| **Psychoacoustic Annoyance** | dimensionless (PA) | $PA$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The reference value for **1 sone** is a **1 kHz pure tone at 40 dB SPL**. The reference value for **1 acum** is a narrow-band noise of 1 critical-bandwidth centered at 1 kHz at 60 dB SPL.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Sound Quality

Every sound quality analysis in NVH engineering revolves around **five core perceptual dimensions**:

1. **Loudness** — *How loud does it sound?*
2. **Sharpness** — *How "hissy/shrill" does it sound?*
3. **Roughness** — *How "buzzy/modulated" does it sound?*
4. **Fluctuation Strength** — *How much does it "wobble"?*
5. **Tonality** — *How "tonal/pure" vs "noisy" is it?*

### 2.2 Pillar 1 — Loudness (Stevens Mark VI & Zwicker)

#### 2.2.1 Stevens' Mark VI Model
The earliest robust loudness model. Maps perceived loudness to physical intensity via a **power law with exponent 0.3**.

$$
S = S_0 \cdot \left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)^{0.3}
$$

Where $S_0 = 1$ sone and $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m².

In terms of **loudness level** $L_N$ (phons):

$$
N \;[\text{sone}] = 2^{\frac{L_N - 40}{10}}
$$

- At $L_N = 40$ phons → $N = 1$ sone (reference)
- Doubling of perceived loudness = +10 phons = 1 sone × 2

#### 2.2.2 Zwicker Model (ISO 532-1 / DIN 45631)
More advanced; uses the **critical band rate scale (Bark scale)** $z$, ranging from 0 to 24 Barks covering 0–16 kHz.

$$
z \;[\text{Bark}] = 13 \cdot \arctan(0.00076 \cdot f) + 3.5 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{f}{7500}\right)^2
$$

Total Loudness:

$$
N = N_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{s}\cdot\int_{0}^{24} N'(z)\,dz\right)
$$

Where $s = 0.25$ (correction factor for tonal vs broadband).

### 2.3 Pillar 2 — Sharpness (Aures, Zwicker, Von Bismarck)

Sharpness quantifies how much **high-frequency content** dominates the perceived sound.

$$
S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{\int_{0}^{24} N'(z) \cdot g(z) \cdot z\,dz}{\int_{0}^{24} N'(z)\,dz}
$$

The weighting function $g(z)$:

$$
g(z) = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{for } z \le 13.8 \\
0.068 \cdot e^{0.173 \cdot z} & \text{for } z > 13.8
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Sharpness is dominated by content above **13.8 Bark (~3.5 kHz)** — the human hearing becomes exponentially more sensitive to higher frequencies.

### 2.4 Pillar 3 — Roughness (Daniel & Weber, Aures)

Roughness captures **fast amplitude modulation** (between **15 Hz and 300 Hz**) producing a buzzing sensation.

$$
R = 0.008 \cdot \int_{0}^{24} \Delta L_E(z)\,dz \quad [\text{asper}]
$$

Where $\Delta L_E(z)$ is the **modulation depth** in dB within each critical band.

- Maximum sensitivity near **70 Hz modulation frequency**
- A buzzing **100 Hz modulation at 1 kHz** with 100% depth → ≈ 1 asper

### 2.5 Pillar 4 — Fluctuation Strength (Fastl & Zwicker)

Fluctuation Strength detects **slow modulations** (≤ 20 Hz) producing a "wobbling" sensation.

$$
F = 0.008 \cdot \int_{0}^{24} \frac{\Delta L(z)}{\left(\frac{f_{mod}}{4}\right) + \left(\frac{4}{f_{mod}}\right)}\,dz \quad [\text{vacil}]
$$

- Maximum sensitivity near **4 Hz** modulation frequency
- A slowly throbbing fan noise: $F \approx 1$ vacil

### 2.6 Pillar 5 — Tonality (Aures, ECMA-74)

Tonality quantifies how much a sound contains **discrete tones** versus broadband noise.

$$
K = \frac{\int_{0}^{24} \Delta L_{ton}(z)\,dz}{\int_{0}^{24} N'(z)\,dz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Pure tones have $K$ approaching **1 tu**, while broadband white noise has $K$ near **0 tu**.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stevens Loudness | $N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$ | sone |
| 2 | Bark Scale | $z = 13\arctan(0.00076f) + 3.5\arctan((f/7500)^2)$ | Bark |
| 3 | Critical Bandwidth | $\Delta f_G = 25 + 75(1 + 1.4 f^2)^{0.69}$ | Hz |
| 4 | Sharpness (Zwicker) | $S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{\int N'(z)\,g(z)\,z\,dz}{\int N'(z)\,dz}$ | acum |
| 5 | Sharpness Weighting | $g(z) = 0.068\,e^{0.173z}$ for $z>13.8$ | — |
| 6 | Roughness (Aures) | $R = 0.008 \int \Delta L_E(z)\,dz$ | asper |
| 7 | Fluctuation Strength | $F = 0.008 \int \frac{\Delta L(z)}{(f_{mod}/4)+(4/f_{mod})}\,dz$ | vacil |
| 8 | Tonality (Aures) | $K = \frac{\int \Delta L_{ton}(z)\,dz}{\int N'(z)\,dz}$ | tu |

### 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive:** Door-close sound → target Sharpness 1.5–2.5 acum
- **EV AVAS:** Pedestrian warning must have Loudness ≥ 50 sone + low Roughness
- **HVAC:** Fan noise → minimize Tonality (pure tones = complaint source)
- **White goods:** Washing machine spin cycle → Fluctuation Strength < 1 vacil
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stevens' Power Law from Weber-Fechner Law

**Step 1:** Start with the **Weber-Fechner Law** — perceived intensity grows as the logarithm of stimulus:

$$
S = k \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Stevens (1957) experimentally showed that human loudness perception is **not logarithmic** but follows a **power law** with exponent $p \approx 0.3$ for broadband sound:

$$
S = k \cdot \left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)^{p}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **definition of phon**: 1 phon = 1 dB SPL at 1 kHz. So $L_N$ (phons) and $L_I$ (dB SPL) are equal **at 1 kHz only**.

**Step 4:** Express intensity ratio in dB:

$$
L_I = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{I}{I_0} = 10^{L_I/10}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the power law:

$$
S = k \cdot \left(10^{L_I/10}\right)^{0.3} = k \cdot 10^{0.03 \cdot L_I}
$$

**Step 6:** Enforce the sone reference: $S = 1$ sone when $L_N = 40$ phons:

$$
1 = k \cdot 10^{0.03 \cdot 40} = k \cdot 10^{1.2}
$$

Solving: $k = 10^{-1.2} \approx 0.0631$.

**Step 7:** Combine with the equivalent phon definition:

$$
N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10} \quad \blacksquare
$$

This is the **canonical Stevens Mark VI** equation you must memorize.

### 3.2 Derivation: Bark Scale from Critical Bandwidth

**Step 1:** Define the **equivalent rectangular bandwidth (ERB)** of the cochlear filter:

$$
\text{ERB}(f) = 24.7 \cdot (4.37 \cdot f / 1000 + 1)
$$

**Step 2:** Define the **critical band rate** $z$ as the integral of $1/\Delta f_G$:

$$
z = \int_{0}^{f} \frac{df'}{\Delta f_G(f')}
$$

**Step 3:** Using the empirical fit (Zwicker & Terhardt 1980):

$$
\Delta f_G(f) = 25 + 75 \cdot (1 + 1.4 \cdot f^2)^{0.69}
$$

**Step 4:** Numerical integration produces the **closed-form approximation**:

$$
z = 13 \cdot \arctan(0.00076 \cdot f) + 3.5 \cdot \arctan\left(\left(\frac{f}{7500}\right)^{2}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** Verification at $f = 1000$ Hz:

$$
z = 13 \cdot \arctan(0.76) + 3.5 \cdot \arctan((1/7.5)^2)
$$
$$
z = 13 \cdot 0.6488 + 3.5 \cdot 0.01778 \approx 8.435 + 0.062 \approx 8.5 \; \text{Bark}
$$

This matches the experimentally determined **8.5 Bark** for 1 kHz — correct! ✓

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sound Quality Decision Flowchart Inputs

| Sound Source | Dominant Metric | Typical Range | Engineering Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engine combustion | Loudness + Roughness | 60–120 sone, 1–3 asper | Minimize asper (<1) |
| Tire/road noise | Loudness + Sharpness | 50–90 sone, 1.5–4 acum | S < 2.5 acum |
| HVAC fan | Tonality + Loudness | 30–70 sone, 0.3–0.8 tu | K < 0.3 tu |
| Gear whine | Sharpness + Tonality | 2–6 acum, 0.4–0.9 tu | Both minimized |
| EV motor whine | Sharpness | 3–8 acum | S < 4 acum |
| Wind buffeting | Fluctuation Strength | 0.5–2 vacil | F < 1 vacil |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Loudness Calculation

**Problem:** A wide-band noise has an SPL of 75 dB at 1 kHz. Compute its loudness level in phons and perceived loudness in sones using Stevens Mark VI.

**Step 1:** At 1 kHz, $L_N = L_I$ by definition of phon:

$$
L_N = 75 \;\text{phons}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Stevens Mark VI:

$$
N = 2^{(75 - 40)/10} = 2^{3.5}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$
2^{3.5} = 2^3 \cdot 2^{0.5} = 8 \cdot 1.4142 \approx 11.31 \;\text{sone}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation — A 75 dB noise at 1 kHz is perceived as **~11 times louder** than the 40 dB reference tone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that **+35 dB SPL** produced **only ~11× perceived loudness**, not 35× — confirming Stevens' sub-linear power law (exponent < 1).

### 3.5 Worked Example — Sharpness Computation

**Problem:** A noise has its spectral centroid at 5 kHz and total loudness 8 sone. Estimate sharpness using the simplified Aures formula.

**Step 1:** Find the critical band rate at 5 kHz:

$$
z = 13 \cdot \arctan(0.00076 \cdot 5000) + 3.5 \cdot \arctan((5000/7500)^2)
$$
$$
z = 13 \cdot \arctan(3.8) + 3.5 \cdot \arctan(0.444)^2 \approx 13 \cdot 1.314 + 3.5 \cdot 0.415 \approx 17.08 + 1.45 \approx 18.5 \;\text{Bark}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply weighting for $z > 13.8$:

$$
g(18.5) = 0.068 \cdot e^{0.173 \cdot 18.5} = 0.068 \cdot e^{3.20} \approx 0.068 \cdot 24.5 \approx 1.67
$$

**Step 3:** Plug into Zwicker sharpness (simplified to single-band):

$$
S \approx 0.11 \cdot g(z) \cdot z \approx 0.11 \cdot 1.67 \cdot 18.5 \approx 3.40 \;\text{acum}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: $S > 2.5$ acum → this noise will be perceived as **harsh/shrill**, likely an NVH complaint source.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sound Quality Analysis Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sound Signal p t] --> B[Time-Frequency Analysis STFT]
    B --> C[Critical Band Filter Bank 24 Barks]
    C --> D[Specific Loudness N prime z]
    D --> E1{Loudness N in sone}
    D --> E2{Sharpness S in acum}
    D --> E3{Roughness R in asper}
    D --> E4{Fluctuation F in vacil}
    D --> E5{Tonality K in tu}
    E1 --> F[Psychoacoustic Vector]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G[NVH Decision Support]
    G --> G1[Target Setting]
    G --> G2[Complaint Diagnosis]
    G --> G3[Sound Design Branding]
```

### 4.2 Critical Band Rate vs Frequency Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW_F[Low Frequency Region 0 to 4 Barks]
        LF1[0 Bark 0 Hz] --> LF2[2 Barks 100 Hz] --> LF3[4 Barks 250 Hz]
    end
    subgraph MID_F[Mid Frequency Region 5 to 13 Barks]
        MF1[5 Barks 350 Hz] --> MF2[8 Barks 1 kHz] --> MF3[13 Barks 3.5 kHz]
    end
    subgraph HIGH_F[High Frequency Region 14 to 24 Barks]
        HF1[14 Barks 4 kHz] --> HF2[18 Barks 6 kHz] --> HF3[24 Barks 16 kHz]
    end
    LOW_F --> MID_F
    MID_F --> HIGH_F
```

### 4.3 Sound Quality Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Perceived Sound Complaint] --> Q1{Loudness High?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Check SPL Reduce Source]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Sharpness High more than 2 5 acum?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2[Add Damping Material in High Frequency Range]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Roughness High more than 1 asper?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> A3[Detune Modulation Frequency Above 300 Hz]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Fluctuation High more than 1 vacil?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> A4[Stabilize Rotation Speed or Flow]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Tonality High more than 0 4 tu?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> A5[Add Broadband Masking Component]
    Q5 -- No --> A6[Sound Acceptable Within Target]
```

### 4.4 Modulation Frequency vs Perception Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SLOW[Slow Modulation less than 20 Hz]
        S1[0 to 4 Hz Strongest Fluctuation]
        S2[4 to 20 Hz Weaker Fluctuation]
    end
    subgraph FAST[Fast Modulation 15 to 300 Hz]
        F1[15 to 70 Hz Rising Roughness]
        F2[70 to 200 Hz Maximum Roughness]
        F3[200 to 300 Hz Falling Roughness]
    end
    SLOW --> FAST
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Sound Quality. List any four psychoacoustic metrics used in its evaluation with their units.**

**Model Answer:**
Sound Quality is the perceptual assessment of a sound by a listener, characterized by psychoacoustic descriptors rather than mere physical SPL.

| Metric | Unit |
|---|---|
| Loudness | sone |
| Sharpness | acum |
| Roughness | asper |
| Fluctuation Strength | vacil |

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Loudness in sone and Loudness Level in phon. State the reference conditions.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Phon** is a *logarithmic* scale describing loudness level referenced to a 1 kHz pure tone. 1 phon = 1 dB SPL at 1 kHz.
- **Sone** is a *linear* scale of perceived loudness where 1 sone = 40 phon (1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL).
- Reference: $f = 1$ kHz, $L_p = 40$ dB → $N = 1$ sone, $L_N = 40$ phons.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern):** Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice provided. Each sub-question carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the Stevens Mark VI model of loudness. Derive the relationship between loudness in sone and loudness level in phon. Compute the loudness in sone for a sound with loudness level of 75 phons. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Stevens' Mark VI assumes loudness follows a **power law** of physical intensity:

$$
N = k \cdot \left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)^{0.3}
$$

**Step 2:** In terms of loudness level $L_N$ (phon):

$$
N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}
$$

This is derived by enforcing $N = 1$ sone at $L_N = 40$ phons and noting that every 10 phon increase doubles loudness.

**Step 3:** For $L_N = 75$ phons:

$$
N = 2^{(75 - 40)/10} = 2^{3.5} = 11.31 \;\text{sone}
$$

The sound is perceived **~11 times louder** than the 40 phon reference.

---

**(b)** Define the Bark scale. Derive (or state) the Zwicker formula and explain how it is used to compute total loudness from specific loudness $N'(z)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The **Bark scale** $z$ is the psychoacoustic critical-band rate (0 to 24 Bark covering 0 to ~16 kHz) representing cochlear frequency analysis.

**Step 2:** Conversion formula:

$$
z = 13 \arctan(0.00076 f) + 3.5 \arctan((f/7500)^2)
$$

**Step 3:** The Zwicker total loudness:

$$
N = \int_{0}^{24} N'(z)\,dz \;\;[\text{sone}]
$$

with corrections for tonal components (multiplied by $s \approx 0.25$ adjustment for tonal masking). $N'(z)$ is the **specific loudness** (loudness per critical band).

**Step 4:** Procedure: (i) Compute excitation level $E$ in each band; (ii) Apply main loudness function $N'(z, E)$; (iii) Integrate $N'(z)$ over all 24 critical bands.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With neat mathematical expressions, explain **Sharpness** as a sound quality metric. Discuss the role of the weighting function $g(z)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Sharpness $S$ (acum) quantifies the perceived high-frequency dominance of a sound.

**Step 2:** Zwicker formula:

$$
S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{\int_{0}^{24} N'(z)\,g(z)\,z\,dz}{\int_{0}^{24} N'(z)\,dz}
$$

**Step 3:** The weighting function $g(z)$:

$$
g(z) = 
\begin{cases}
1 & z \le 13.8 \\
0.068 \cdot e^{0.173 z} & z > 13.8
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4:** Role of $g(z)$: Acts as an **exponential amplifier** for frequencies above 3.5 kHz, reflecting that the human ear assigns disproportionately higher annoyance to high-frequency content. It corrects the denominator integration so that broadband low-frequency noise doesn't dilute the contribution of high-frequency bands.

**Step 5:** Reference: A narrow-band noise of 1 critical bandwidth centered at 1 kHz at 60 dB has $S = 1$ acum.

---

**(b)** Define **Roughness** and **Fluctuation Strength**. Explain their modulation frequency ranges and the formulas used to compute them. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Roughness (asper):**
Roughness captures the buzz sensation produced by **fast amplitude modulations** between **15 Hz and 300 Hz**, peaking at ~70 Hz.

$$
R = 0.008 \cdot \int_{0}^{24} \Delta L_E(z)\,dz
$$

where $\Delta L_E(z)$ is the modulation depth in each critical band.

**Step 2 — Fluctuation Strength (vacil):**
Captures the **slow wobble sensation** from modulations below **20 Hz**, peaking at **4 Hz**.

$$
F = 0.008 \cdot \int_{0}^{24} \frac{\Delta L(z)}{\left(\frac{f_{mod}}{4}\right) + \left(\frac{4}{f_{mod}}\right)}\,dz
$$

**Step 3 — Distinguishing Feature:**
The denominator of $F$ creates a **resonance at 4 Hz** — perfectly tuned to the human sensitivity for slow amplitude changes (e.g., fan wobble). For roughness, the maximum sensitivity shifts to **70 Hz** (motor/gear buzz).

| Feature | Roughness | Fluctuation Strength |
|---|---|---|
| Modulation Range | 15–300 Hz | 0.5–20 Hz |
| Peak Sensitivity | ~70 Hz | ~4 Hz |
| Subjective Impression | Buzz | Wobble |
| Unit | asper | vacil |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse phon with sone** — phons are logarithmic, sones are linear. (Common 1–2 mark loss)
> 2. **Always specify units** — sone, acum, asper, vacil, tu. Marks are deducted if units are missing.
> 3. **For Stevens' loudness problems**, write the formula $N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$ first, then substitute. Skipping derivation loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Sharpness weighting** $g(z)$ is only applied for $z > 13.8$ Bark — many students forget the threshold.
> 5. **Don't confuse roughness and fluctuation** — different modulation frequencies, different units.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sound Quality** = Psychoacoustic perceptual assessment of sound (vs. dB SPL which is physical).
- **Loudness** is measured in **sone**; **Loudness Level** in **phon**. Reference: 1 kHz at 40 dB = 1 sone = 40 phons.
- **Stevens Mark VI:** $N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$. Power-law exponent = 0.3 for broadband sound.
- **Bark scale** (0–24) maps 0–16 kHz to cochlear critical bands. $z = 13\arctan(0.00076f) + 3.5\arctan((f/7500)^2)$.
- **Zwicker loudness** integrates specific loudness $N'(z)$ over 24 critical bands.
- **Sharpness** (acum) penalizes high-frequency content via $g(z) = 0.068 e^{0.173 z}$ for $z > 13.8$.
- **Roughness** (asper) — fast modulations 15–300 Hz, peak at 70 Hz.
- **Fluctuation Strength** (vacil) — slow modulations < 20 Hz, peak at 4 Hz.
- **Tonality** (tu) — degree of pure-tone presence (1 tu = pure tone, 0 tu = broadband).
- **Reference values:** 1 sone = 1 kHz @ 40 dB SPL; 1 acum = narrow-band at 1 kHz, 60 dB.
- **A-weighting (dBA)** is a crude approximation; Sound Quality metrics are the **modern, perceptually accurate alternative**.
- **Engineering targets** (auto): Door thud $S = 1.5$–$2.5$ acum; Engine $R < 1$ asper; HVAC $K < 0.3$ tu.
- **Standard references:** ISO 532-1 (Zwicker), ISO 532-2 (Stevens), DIN 45631, ECMA-74 (Tonality).
- **For 14-mark questions:** Always show derivation step-by-step; define reference conditions; specify units in final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loudness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Loudness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Loudness** is a **psychoacoustic** (subjective) attribute of sound that describes the *intensity* of the auditory sensation as perceived by the human ear. It is measured on one of two principal scales:

- The **phon** scale → quantifies the *Loudness Level* (a measure tied to a reference tone of 1 kHz).
- The **sone** scale → quantifies *Loudness* itself (a linear-ish, perceptual magnitude scale).

Mathematically, loudness is the perceptual correlate of **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)**, but it is *not* equivalent to it. Two sounds with the same SPL can be perceived with very different loudness if they differ in **frequency, bandwidth, or duration**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Loudness is NOT the same as Sound Pressure Level (SPL).**
> - **SPL** is an objective, physical quantity measured in **dB**.
> - **Loudness** is a subjective, perceptual quantity measured in **sone** (or loudness level in **phon**).

The standardized reference for equal-loudness contours is **ISO 226:2003** (revised from the original Fletcher–Munson curves of 1933).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

Imagine you are standing beside a busy highway and a friend whispers from a distance. The SPL meter at your ear might register **70 dB** for both — but the *perceived loudness* is completely different.

- The whisper at 70 dB at 4 kHz will sound **loud** (your ear is most sensitive around 2–5 kHz).
- The traffic noise at 70 dB at 50 Hz will sound **muffled and weak** because your ear is insensitive to low frequencies.

This is why loudness must be frequency-weighted. The human ear acts like a variable-gain amplifier across the audio spectrum. Loudness models try to mathematically replicate that "variable gain" in the form of filters (A-weighting, B-weighting, C-weighting) and more sophisticated algorithms (Zwicker, Moore-Glasberg).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The ear is not a flat microphone. Its sensitivity peaks around **3–4 kHz** and rolls off dramatically below 500 Hz and above 8 kHz. Loudness scales correct for this physiological reality.

## 1.3 Why Loudness Matters in NVH Engineering

In automotive and industrial NVH, the goal is **not** to minimize SPL alone — it is to minimize *perceived annoyance*. A 5 dB drop in SPL at low frequencies might go unnoticed, while a 2 dB drop at 2 kHz dramatically reduces complaint rates. Loudness metrics are therefore the **gold standard** for:
- Customer-satisfaction surveys (J.D. Power NVH ratings)
- Sound quality engineering
- Active Noise Control (ANC) objective functions
- Psychoacoustic product design (e.g., EV "engine sound" synthesis)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Equal-Loudness Contours (Fletcher–Munson / ISO 226)

Equal-loudness contours are curves in the **SPL vs. frequency** plane that connect all pure tones perceived as *equally loud*. The reference is a **1 kHz pure tone**, and the contour value is called the **loudness level** in **phons**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phon Definition:** The loudness level, in phons, of a sound is numerically equal to the SPL (in dB) of a 1 kHz pure tone that is judged by listeners to be equally loud.

### Key Observations from the Contours:
- At 1 kHz, phon = dB (by definition).
- The **40-phon contour** shows that at 20 Hz, an SPL of ~100 dB is required to sound as loud as a 1 kHz, 40 dB tone.
- The **100-phon contour** is much flatter — at high SPLs, the ear's frequency response becomes flatter (a phenomenon called **loudness recruitment**).
- All contours flatten at the **top** and bunch together at the **bottom**, where hearing threshold is approached.

## 2.2 The Phon Scale (Loudness Level)

- A **0-phon contour** corresponds to the **threshold of hearing**.
- A **120-phon contour** corresponds to the **threshold of pain / discomfort**.
- Phons are logarithmic, **not linear** in perception.

## 2.3 The Sone Scale (Loudness)

The sone scale was developed by **S.S. Stevens (1956)** to give loudness a *linear* perceptual scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sone Definition:** By definition, 1 sone = loudness of a 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL (i.e., 40 phons). A sound judged *twice as loud* has a loudness of **2 sones**.

### Stevens' Power Law for Loudness (Direct from Phon to Sone):

$$
N \;=\; 2^{\,\dfrac{L_N - 40}{10}}
$$

where:
- $N$ = Loudness in **sones**
- $L_N$ = Loudness level in **phons**

Inverting for loudness level:

$$
L_N \;=\; 40 \;+\; 10 \log_{2}(N)
$$

## 2.4 Critical Bands & Loudness Summation

When multiple tones fall within a single **critical band** (frequency band within which the ear integrates energy), they do **not** sum linearly in loudness. They sum *perceptually* via the following rule (Zwicker model):

$$
N_{total} \;=\; N_{max} \;+\; \left(0.15 \cdot \left(\sum N_i - N_{max}\right)\right)
$$

This means adding a tone within a critical band of an already-present tone increases loudness by only ~15% of what the new tone alone would contribute.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Bandwidth** at the ear's most sensitive region is ~**100 Hz** (Bark scale). Above 500 Hz, the bandwidth increases roughly as 20% of center frequency.

## 2.5 Stevens' Power Law (General Form)

Stevens found that perceived magnitude $S$ relates to physical stimulus $I$ via:

$$
S \;=\; k \, I^{\,n}
$$

For **loudness**, the exponent is:

$$
n_{loudness} \;\approx\; 0.3
$$

So a 10-fold increase in sound *intensity* produces only a **2-fold increase in loudness**. This is the origin of the sone scale's quasi-linear behavior relative to the logarithmic phon/dB scale.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Phon definition | $L_N(\phi) = SPL_{1kHz}$ matched in loudness | phon | Numerical match at 1 kHz only |
| 2 | Phon to Sone | $N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$ | sone | Stevens' Power Law |
| 3 | Sone to Phon | $L_N = 40 + 10 \log_2(N)$ | phon | Inverse of #2 |
| 4 | Stevens' Power Law | $S = k I^{0.3}$ | — | For loudness, $n = 0.3$ |
| 5 | Critical-band sum | $N_{tot} = N_{max} + 0.15(\sum N_i - N_{max})$ | sone | Zwicker summation |
| 6 | Loudness doubling | 10 phons $\Rightarrow$ Loudness × 2 | — | Convention (1 sone = 40 phons) |
| 7 | A-weighted SPL to perceived loudness | $L_A = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_0)$ + A-filter | dB(A) | Approximation only |
| 8 | Specific loudness | $N' = \text{d}N/\text{d}z$ | sone/Bark | Zwicker model |
| 9 | Total loudness (Zwicker) | $N = \int_{0}^{24} N'(z)\, dz$ | sone | $z$ = critical-band rate in Bark |
| 10 | Critical-band rate (Bark) | $z \approx 13 \arctan(0.76 f) + 3.5 \arctan(f/7.5)$ | Bark | $f$ in kHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always state the reference and the model name** in KTU answers (e.g., "Using Stevens' Power Law" or "Using the Zwicker model"). Vague answers get marked down.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

Loudness metrics are essential in:
- **Automotive NVH** — designing interior cabin sound character (e.g., the "BMW burble" or "EV hum").
- **Headphones / hearing aids** — equal-loudness compensation.
- **Product design** — alarm and notification tones tuned to be perceptible but not startling.
- **Occupational health** — ISO 1999 hearing damage risk uses frequency-weighted noise exposure.
- **Audio coding** (MP3/AAC) — psychoacoustic masking leverages the same critical-band model.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Stevens' Power Law to the Phon-Sone Relation

**Given:** Stevens showed that perceived loudness scales as a power of sound *intensity* (not pressure).

**Step 1 — Define the reference.**
Let $I_0$ be the reference intensity (threshold of hearing at 1 kHz, ~$10^{-12}$ W/m²), and let the loudness be $N_0 = 1$ sone at the SPL of 40 dB (i.e., intensity ratio $I/I_0 = 10^{4}$).

**Step 2 — Write Stevens' Law for the reference tone.**

$$
N_0 \;=\; k \, I_{ref}^{\,0.3} \;=\; 1
$$

Solving for $k$ (with $N_0 = 1$ sone) we obtain the proportionality constant; for the ratio we can write:

$$
\frac{N}{N_0} \;=\; \left(\frac{I}{I_{ref}}\right)^{0.3}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert intensity ratio to phons.**
The loudness level in phons is defined as the SPL (in dB) of the equally-loud 1 kHz tone. SPL in dB relates to intensity as:

$$
L \;=\; 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \quad \text{dB}
$$

So if the loudness level in phons is $L_N$:

$$
\frac{I}{I_0} \;=\; 10^{\,L_N / 10}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the reference condition.**
For the reference tone (1 sone = 40 phons): $I/I_0 = 10^{4}$, $N = 1$.

For a generic tone of phon level $L_N$:

$$
\frac{N}{1} \;=\; \left(\frac{10^{\,L_N/10}}{10^{4}}\right)^{0.3} \;=\; 10^{\,0.3\,(L_N/10 \;-\; 4)}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$
N \;=\; 10^{\,0.3\,(L_N/10 \;-\; 4)} \;=\; 10^{\,(0.03 L_N \;-\; 1.2)}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert base-10 to base-2 to recover the canonical form.**
Since $10^{x} = 2^{x \log_2 10} = 2^{3.3219 x}$:

$$
N \;=\; 2^{\,3.3219\,(0.03 L_N \;-\; 1.2)} \;=\; 2^{\,0.1 L_N \;-\; 4}
$$

$$
N \;=\; 2^{\,(L_N \;-\; 40)/10}
$$

This recovers the **standard formula** in the cheat sheet.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Phon to Sone Conversion

> **Problem:** A pure tone has a loudness level of **70 phons**. Find its loudness in sones. How much louder is it than a 40-phon tone?

**Solution:**

Using $N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$:

$$
N \;=\; 2^{\,(70 \;-\; 40)/10} \;=\; 2^{\,3.0} \;=\; 8 \text{ sones}
$$

Comparison with the 40-phon tone ($N = 1$ sone):

$$
\frac{N_{70}}{N_{40}} \;=\; \frac{8}{1} \;=\; 8 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{8 times louder perceptually}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **30-phon** increase corresponds to a perceptual loudness multiplier of **8×** (since $2^3 = 8$). This is why dB-only targets can be misleading in NVH work.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Critical-Band Loudness Summation (Zwicker)

> **Problem:** Two pure tones fall within the same critical band. Their individual loudnesses are $N_1 = 4$ sones and $N_2 = 6$ sones. Find the total perceived loudness.

**Solution:**

Identify the dominant tone: $N_{max} = \max(4, 6) = 6$ sones.

Apply Zwicker's critical-band summation rule:

$$
N_{total} \;=\; N_{max} \;+\; 0.15 \cdot \left(N_1 + N_2 - N_{max}\right)
$$

$$
N_{total} \;=\; 6 \;+\; 0.15 \cdot (4 + 6 - 6) \;=\; 6 \;+\; 0.15 \cdot 4
$$

$$
N_{total} \;=\; 6 \;+\; 0.6 \;=\; 6.6 \text{ sones}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy-only arithmetic would give 10 sones**, but the ear perceives only **6.6 sones** because of within-band masking. This 34% gap is *exactly* what psychoacoustic models like MP3 encoders exploit.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — SPL to Phon (Equal-Loudness Contour Reading)

> **Problem:** A 100 Hz tone is measured at **80 dB SPL**. Using the ISO 226 equal-loudness contour, the equally loud 1 kHz tone is at approximately **60 dB SPL**. (a) What is the loudness level? (b) What is the loudness in sones?

**Solution:**

(a) By definition of phon, the loudness level equals the SPL of the equally-loud 1 kHz tone:

$$
L_N \;=\; 60 \text{ phons}
$$

(b) Convert to sones:

$$
N \;=\; 2^{\,(60 - 40)/10} \;=\; 2^{\,2.0} \;=\; 4 \text{ sones}
$$

This means a 100 Hz, 80 dB tone is perceived *only 4 times* the threshold loudness — illustrating how the ear's poor low-frequency sensitivity penalizes bass content.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Metric | Phon | Sone | SPL (dB) | A-weighted dB(A) |
|--------|------|------|----------|-------------------|
| Type | Subjective (log) | Subjective (linear) | Objective (log) | Objective + filter |
| Reference | 1 kHz tone | 1 sone = 40 phons | 20 µPa | 20 µPa + A-filter |
| Scale form | Logarithmic | Quasi-linear | Logarithmic | Logarithmic |
| Frequency weighted? | Yes (by definition via contours) | Yes (by definition) | No | Approximate (yes) |
| Use in KTU exam | "Loudness Level" | "Loudness" | Acoustic measurement | Quick industrial noise rating |
| Doubling rule | 10 phons ≈ ×2 loudness | 2 sones ≈ ×2 loudness | +6 dB ≈ ×2 intensity | +6 dB(A) ≈ ×2 intensity (but NOT loudness) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics
