## 4.1 Loudness Perception Pipeline (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Sound Pressure Signal p of t"] --> B["Frequency Analysis via DFT or Filter Bank"]
    B --> C["Critical Band Grouping via Bark Scale"]
    C --> D["Excitation Pattern Computation"]
    D --> E["Application of Hearing Threshold Curve"]
    E --> F["Specific Loudness N prime per Bark"]
    F --> G["Integration over Critical Band Rate"]
    G --> H["Total Loudness N in sone"]
    H --> I["Compute Loudness Level Ln in phon"]
    I --> J["Output to NVH Decision Block"]
    J --> K["Apply Weighting per Application: Automotive HVAC, EV interior, Industrial OSH"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block represents a transformation the ear (or a model like Zwicker/ISO 532) performs. The pipeline mirrors the actual cochlear processing chain: mechanical filtering → tonotopic mapping → neural transduction → central integration.

## 4.2 Phon–Sone Inter-Conversion Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Loudness Level Ln in phon"] -->|Stevens Power Law| Q["Exponent Step: subtract 40, divide by 10"]
    Q --> R["Apply Base 2 Power Function"]
    R --> S["Loudness N in sone"]
    S -->|Inverse Transform| T["Multiply by 10, add 40"]
    T --> P
```

## 4.3 Equal-Loudness Contour — Conceptual Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["X Axis: Frequency 20 Hz to 20 kHz logarithmic scale"] --> B["Y Axis: SPL in dB"]
    B --> C["Contour Family: 0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120 phons"]
    C --> D["Lower contours show deep dip near 4 kHz ear resonance"]
    C --> E["Upper contours become flatter as ear saturates"]
    D --> F["Engineering Implication: Penalize SPL targets at low frequencies"]
    E --> G["Engineering Implication: Small dB changes at high SPL cause large loudness changes"]
```

## 4.4 Critical-Band Masking Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Multiple Spectral Tones"] --> B{"Do tones fall within a single critical band?"}
    B -->|Yes| C["Apply Zwicker Summation: N max plus 15 percent of rest"]
    B -->|No| D["Apply Linear Summation: Total N equals sum of N_i"]
    C --> E["Output: Reduced perceived loudness due to masking"]
    D --> F["Output: Full perceived loudness"]
    E --> G["Use in Active Noise Control weight tuning"]
    F --> G
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Node ID Rule Applied:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric, contain no reserved words (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) as standalone names, and node labels contain no markdown bold/italic tags inside double quotes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "loudness" and explain how it differs from sound pressure level. Mention the units used to quantify each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Loudness (1 mark):** Loudness is the *subjective* (psychoacoustic) attribute of sound that corresponds to the perceived intensity of an auditory sensation. It depends on frequency, SPL, duration, and bandwidth — not on physical sound pressure alone.
- **SPL (1 mark):** Sound Pressure Level is the *objective*, physical measure of acoustic pressure relative to a reference of **20 µPa**, expressed in decibels (dB).
- **Units (1 mark):** Loudness is measured in **sones** (linear) or loudness level in **phons**; SPL is measured in **dB** re 20 µPa.
- **Distinction:** Two sounds of identical SPL can have different loudness because of the ear's frequency-dependent sensitivity. SPL is a property of the field; loudness is a property of the listener.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by an "equal-loudness contour"? Why are the contours closer together at low SPL than at high SPL?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** An equal-loudness contour (per ISO 226) is a curve on the SPL–frequency plane that joins all pure tones judged equally loud by a typical listener. The contour's value in phons is defined by the SPL of the 1 kHz reference tone.
- **Low-SPL behavior (1 mark):** At low SPL, the ear is highly frequency-selective, so small frequency shifts cause large perceived loudness changes — contours spread out.
- **High-SPL behavior (0.5 marks):** At high SPL, the basilar-membrane response saturates, frequency selectivity is reduced, contours flatten and bunch together. Above ~100 phons, the ear's response is nearly flat across frequency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Derive the relation between loudness (sone) and loudness level (phon) starting from Stevens' Power Law. State the assumptions clearly.

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Assumption 1:** Stevens' Power Law holds for loudness:

$$
S \;=\; k \cdot I^{\,0.3}
$$

**Assumption 2:** The reference loudness is defined as $N_0 = 1$ sone when the loudness level is 40 phons, i.e., the SPL of the matching 1 kHz tone is 40 dB.

**Step 1 — Express the ratio of loudnesses.**
For two stimuli of intensities $I$ and $I_0$:

$$
\frac{N}{N_0} \;=\; \left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)^{0.3}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert intensity ratio to phons.**
SPL in dB satisfies $L = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$. For a loudness level $L_N$ phons:

$$
\frac{I}{I_0} \;=\; 10^{\,L_N/10}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the reference condition $N_0 = 1$ sone at $L_N = 40$ phons:**

$$
N \;=\; \left(\frac{10^{\,L_N/10}}{10^{4}}\right)^{0.3} \;=\; 10^{\,0.3\,(L_N/10 - 4)}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert base-10 to base-2:**

$$
N \;=\; 2^{\,(L_N - 40)/10}
$$

This is the required relation. (Final boxed result gets full credit.)

#### (b) [7 Marks] A vehicle interior is measured at 75 dB(A). Using the ISO 226 equal-loudness contour, the perceived loudness level is approximately 73 phons. (i) Find the loudness in sones. (ii) If a second harmonic at the same critical band adds a perceptual contribution equivalent to 0.8 sone, compute the total loudness using Zwicker's critical-band summation. (iii) Comment on the result.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Phon to Sone (3 marks):**

$$
N \;=\; 2^{\,(73 - 40)/10} \;=\; 2^{\,3.3}
$$

$$
2^{3.3} \;=\; 2^{3} \cdot 2^{0.3} \;=\; 8 \cdot 1.231 \;=\; 9.85 \text{ sones}
$$

**(ii) Zwicker Summation (3 marks):**
Here $N_{max} = 9.85$ sones and the additional contribution is $0.8$ sone. Treating the added tone as $N_2$:

$$
N_{total} \;=\; 9.85 \;+\; 0.15 \cdot (0.8) \;=\; 9.85 + 0.12
$$

$$
N_{total} \;\approx\; 9.97 \text{ sones}
$$

(Note: 0.8 sone is *added* to the original 9.85 to form $\sum N_i = 10.65$ sones, then $N_{max}$ is subtracted and 15% applied: $9.85 + 0.15 \times 0.8 = 9.97$ sone. Both interpretations are accepted if consistent.)

**(iii) Comment (1 mark):**
Although SPL is unchanged by the harmonic, the within-band contribution increases perceived loudness by only ~1.2%. The ear masks close-frequency harmonics efficiently, which is why NVH treatments targeting harmonically related tones yield diminishing returns above a certain point.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) [7 Marks] With the help of a neat sketch, describe the equal-loudness contours (Fletcher–Munson curves). Explain why the 1 kHz tone is chosen as the reference.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

- **Sketch (3 marks):** Draw SPL (dB) on Y-axis, frequency (Hz, log scale) on X-axis, plot contours at 0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120 phons. Mark the 1 kHz vertical line where the phon value equals the SPL. Show that contours dip near 3–4 kHz and rise at low frequencies.
- **Description (2 marks):** Each contour represents equal perceived loudness. As phon level increases, the contours flatten, indicating reduced frequency selectivity at high SPL.
- **Why 1 kHz? (2 marks):**
  1. The ear has near-maximum sensitivity at 1 kHz.
  2. 1 kHz tones are easily generated and reproduced.
  3. The phon scale is *defined* by the SPL of a 1 kHz tone matched in loudness to the test tone — so the reference must be unique and well-defined.
  4. Historically, 1 kHz was the first standardized audiometric frequency (ANSI/ISO).

#### (b) [7 Marks] Two interior noise sources in a cabin are measured separately. Source A produces a perceived loudness of **3 sones** in its critical band. Source B produces **5 sones** in a different (non-overlapping) critical band. (i) Compute the total loudness. (ii) If the SPL of Source A is increased by 6 dB, find its new loudness in sones and the new total loudness. (iii) State your conclusion regarding the relationship between SPL and perceptual loudness.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Non-overlapping critical bands → linear summation (2 marks):**

$$
N_{total} \;=\; 3 + 5 \;=\; 8 \text{ sones}
$$

**(ii) SPL +6 dB on Source A (4 marks):**
A 6 dB SPL increase doubles the intensity. By Stevens' Power Law:

$$
N_{new} \;=\; N_{old} \cdot 2^{0.3} \;\approx\; 3 \cdot 1.231 \;\approx\; 3.69 \text{ sones}
$$

(Alternative accepted: doubling intensity gives a $\sim$23% increase in loudness.)

The new total loudness (non-overlapping bands):

$$
N_{total}' \;=\; 3.69 + 5 \;=\; 8.69 \text{ sones}
$$

**(iii) Conclusion (1 mark):**
A 6 dB SPL increase — which doubles the *acoustic energy* — raises perceived loudness by only ~23%. **Therefore, SPL is a poor proxy for perceived loudness.** Loudness-aware NVH targets are essential.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "phon" and "sone"** — they are not interchangeable. Phon is logarithmic (level), sone is quasi-linear (magnitude). Always write the unit explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1 kHz reference** in phon definitions — examiners deduct 1 mark if the reference tone is omitted.
> 3. **Applying linear (energy) summation when critical bands overlap** — students often write $N_{total} = N_1 + N_2$ blindly. You must check whether the tones fall in the *same* critical band first.
> 4. **Using A-weighted dB as a loudness metric** — A-weighting is a crude approximation; for KTU answers, mention its limitations explicitly.
> 5. **Unit errors** — dB is dimensionless but expresses a ratio; loudness has units of **sone** and loudness level has units of **phon**.
> 6. **Skipping derivation steps** in 7-mark sub-questions — KTU valuation requires every algebraic step, not just the final expression.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Loudness** = subjective, perceptual magnitude; **SPL** = objective, physical pressure ratio.
- **Phon** = loudness level (logarithmic); **Sone** = loudness (quasi-linear).
- **1 sone ≡ 40 phons ≡ 40 dB SPL at 1 kHz** (reference triplet).
- **Stevens' Power Law** for loudness: $S = k I^{0.3}$ → exponent is **0.3**, not 0.5 or 1.0.
- **Phon → Sone:** $N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$.
- **Sone → Phon:** $L_N = 40 + 10 \log_2(N)$.
- **Equal-loudness contours** are referenced to **1 kHz** (ISO 226:2003 is the current standard).
- **Contours flatten at high SPL** (loudness recruitment effect).
- **Critical-band sum (Zwicker):** dominant tone + 15% of the rest.
- **Non-overlapping critical bands** sum *linearly* in sones.
- **Doubling rule:** +10 phons = ×2 perceived loudness.
- **A-weighting (dB(A))** is an engineering shortcut; **loudness models** (Zwicker, Moore-Glasberg) are the gold standard.
- **Bark scale** $z = 13\arctan(0.76 f) + 3.5\arctan(f/7.5)$ is the modern critical-band rate scale.
- **Common applications:** automotive cabin sound design, EV exterior sound regulation (UN R138), hearing-aid compression, occupational noise (ISO 1999).
- **Famous result:** A 10 dB SPL increase ≈ ×2 perceived loudness (approximately true only near 1 kHz at moderate SPL).
- **Examiner's mantra:** Always state the **reference**, the **scale**, and the **model** in your KTU answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Roughness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Roughness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Roughness** is a subjective NVH descriptor that characterizes the low-to-mid frequency structural vibration perceived through the steering wheel, seat track, pedals, and floor pan of an automobile. It is typically associated with the frequency band of **3 Hz to 30 Hz** (some industry standards extend the upper limit to **80 Hz** when discussing structural idle shake and body boom coupling).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy of vehicle NVH complaints, *Roughness* is firmly separated from related descriptors such as **Shake**, **Boom**, **Buzz**, and **Harshness**. While *Harshness* deals with high-frequency harsh impacts and *Buzz* is tied to interior trim/sound radiation in the 100–500 Hz range, *Roughness* specifically captures the **low-frequency mechanical excitation transmitted as tactile vibration to the driver and occupants**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Roughness is perceived primarily through the **kinesthetic senses** (touch via seat, hands on wheel, feet on floor), NOT primarily through the ears. This makes it a structural-mechanical attribute rather than an acoustical one.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine sitting in a diesel auto-rickshaw idling at a traffic signal. You feel a deep, rhythmic *thrum-thrum-thrum* running through the seat cushion and steering rim — your body vibrates, but the sound itself is not particularly loud. That deep, throbbing, "busy" tactile sensation is **Roughness**.

Compare this with the higher-pitched "rattle-tick-tick" sound from a loose dashboard panel — that is **Buzz**. And compare the one-shot "bump" when the vehicle crosses a pothole — that is **Shock** or **Harshness**. Roughness is the **continuous, low-frequency, body-coupled vibration that the driver perceives during steady-state driving or idling**.

### 1.3 Industry-Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

The following engineering quantities are the primary **objective metrics** used by NVH engineers to quantify and correlate with subjective roughness ratings:

- **Weighted RMS Acceleration** ($a_{w,RMS}$) — units: **m/s²**
- **Vibration Dose Value (VDV)** — units: **m/s¹·⁷⁵**
- **Crest Factor** (dimensionless ratio)
- **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** peak in the **3–30 Hz band**
- **Overall Vibration Total Value (OVTV)** as per **ISO 2631-1**

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 board examinations, always cite **ISO 2631-1:1997 / 2003** when discussing human exposure to whole-body vibration, as this is the globally accepted standard for vehicle roughness evaluation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Roughness — Layered Logic

The complete roughness phenomenon can be decomposed into four logical stages:

**Stage 1 — Excitation Generation:**
The primary excitation sources that produce roughness are operating at low rotational orders of the powertrain:
- Engine firing frequency at idle (e.g., 4-cylinder at 800 RPM → ~26.7 Hz)
- Driveline imbalance and shaft runout
- Tire non-uniformities (radial force variation – RFV)
- Road macro-texture excitation through suspension bushings

**Stage 2 — Structural Transmission:**
Vibration energy travels from the source through the body-in-white (BIW) structure. The floor pan, firewall, and steering column act as the principal transmission paths. Structural modes of the body in the **5 Hz – 50 Hz** range get excited, amplifying or attenuating the signal based on resonance coincidence.

**Stage 3 — Human Reception:**
The seated occupant is sensitive to vertical vibration at the seat-pan interface in the **4 Hz – 8 Hz** range (resonance of the human lumbar spine) and to hand-arm vibration through the steering wheel in the **8 Hz – 16 Hz** range.

**Stage 4 — Subjective Judgment:**
The driver integrates the multi-axial vibration over time and assigns a numerical rating (typically on a **1–10 scale**, where 10 = "imperceptible" and 1 = "unacceptable").

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

- **Why 3–30 Hz?** Below 3 Hz, the body perceives motion as displacement, not vibration. Above 30 Hz, vibration becomes localized and is no longer perceived as whole-body roughness.
- **Why powertrain idle dominates?** Idle operating points have the least combustion damping, the largest torque fluctuations per cycle, and the lowest NVH counter-measure effectiveness from active systems.
- **Why RMS alone is insufficient?** Random and transient events may have low RMS but high peak energy. Hence **VDV** (4th-power law) is preferred for cumulative exposure assessment.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **high-yield equations** for KTU 2024 Module 4 examinations. All vertical bars have been escaped as `\vert` to preserve markdown syntax integrity.

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$a_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} a^{2}(t)\,dt}$$ | Root Mean Square acceleration — basic roughness intensity indicator |
| 2 | $$VDV = \left[\int_{0}^{T} a_{w}^{4}(t)\,dt\right]^{1/4}$$ | Vibration Dose Value — ISO 2631, captures transient shocks |
| 3 | $$CF = \dfrac{a_{peak}}{a_{RMS}}$$ | Crest Factor — ratio of peak to RMS; high CF implies impulsive content |
| 4 | $$VTV = \left[\int_{0}^{T} a_{w}^{4}(t)\,dt\right]^{1/4} \cdot k$$ | Total Vibration Value (with frequency-weighting factor $k$) |
| 5 | $$\text{Overall Level} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{a_{RMS}}{a_{ref}}\right)$$ | Logarithmic dB reference level with $a_{ref} = 10^{-6}\,\text{m/s}^{2}$ |
| 6 | $$f_{e} = \dfrac{n \cdot N}{60}$$ | Engine firing frequency where $n$ = cylinders/2 for 4-stroke, $N$ = RPM |
| 7 | $$\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$$ | Natural frequency of single-DOF structural mode |
| 8 | $$\dfrac{a_{transmitted}}{a_{input}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^{2}}{(1-r^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta r)^{2}}}$$ | Transmissibility ratio where $r = \omega/\omega_{n}$ |
| 9 | $$\text{RMS order level} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\tilde{a}_{order}}{a_{ref}}\right)$$ | Order-tracking level in dB for RPM-locked roughness components |
| 10 | $$\text{Subjective Rating} = f\!\left(a_{w,RMS}, f, \text{duration}\right)$$ | Empirical correlation function linking objective metric to driver rating |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Calibration Teams** use roughness metrics to validate engine mount tuning — softer mounts lower high-frequency buzz but may *increase* low-frequency roughness due to larger static deflection under torque reversal.
- **Body Structure Designers** use the 3–30 Hz band to position body-in-white (BIW) modes away from major powertrain firing orders.
- **Suspension Engineers** correlate seat-track RMS to bushing stiffness and shock absorber tuning curves.
- **Regulatory Bodies** reference **ISO 2631-1** Health Guidance Caution zones (peak VDV > 8.5 m/s¹·⁷⁵).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — RMS Acceleration from Time-History Data

**Starting Point:** A discrete accelerometer time history $a_i$ sampled at $N$ points with sampling interval $\Delta t$.

**Step 1 — Express the continuous integral in discrete form.** The continuous RMS integral is replaced by a discrete summation:

$$a_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_{i}^{2}\,\Delta t}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that $\Delta t$ is constant, hence factor it out.** Since $T = N\Delta t$:

$$\begin{aligned}
a_{RMS} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta t}{N\Delta t}\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_{i}^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_{i}^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion.** The RMS of an acceleration time-history reduces to the square root of the arithmetic mean of the squared samples. **Units**: m/s². **Note**: This is the standard roughness intensity metric used at the seat-pan and steering-wheel measurement points.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Vibration Dose Value (VDV) from 4th-Power Law

The VDV is derived from the human body's **frequency-weighted, fourth-power dose-response model**, which reflects the fact that vibration discomfort grows faster than linearly with amplitude.

**Step 1 — Apply the frequency weighting filter $W_k$ to the raw acceleration.** The weighted acceleration is:

$$a_{w}(t) = W_k \star a(t)$$

where $\star$ denotes convolution with the ISO 2631-1 weighting filter (different filters for $W_k$, $W_d$, $W_f$ for seat, seat-back, feet).

**Step 2 — Raise to the 4th power and integrate over exposure time $T$.** This is the **dose integral**:

$$D = \int_{0}^{T} a_{w}^{4}(t)\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Take the 4th root.** This normalizes the dose back to acceleration units:

$$VDV = D^{1/4} = \left[\int_{0}^{T} a_{w}^{4}(t)\,dt\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example for clarity.** Suppose a driver experiences $a_{w}(t) = 0.5\,\text{m/s}^{2}$ for $T = 4$ hours $= 14400\,\text{s}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= (0.5)^{4} \cdot 14400 \\
&= 0.0625 \cdot 14400 \\
&= 900\,\text{(m/s}^{2})^{4}\cdot\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

Then,

$$\begin{aligned}
VDV &= 900^{0.25} \\
&= (900)^{1/4} \\
&\approx 5.48\,\text{m/s}^{1.75}
\end{aligned}$$

This value lies **above the ISO 2631-1 Health Guidance Caution level of 8.5 m/s¹·⁷⁵ only if exposure time increases**, illustrating why VDV is **time-dependent** and superior to RMS for cumulative exposure assessment.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Engine Firing Frequency (Theoretical Basis of Idle Roughness)

For a 4-stroke, 4-cylinder engine at $N$ RPM, the firing order is the **2nd order** of crankshaft rotation.

**Step 1 — Crankshaft angular frequency.** Convert RPM to Hz:

$$f_{crank} = \dfrac{N}{60}$$

**Step 2 — Apply 4-stroke firing rule.** In a 4-stroke engine, each cylinder fires once every **2 revolutions**, so the firing frequency for the *engine as a whole* (sum of all cylinders) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{firing} &= \dfrac{n}{2} \cdot f_{crank} \\
&= \dfrac{n}{2} \cdot \dfrac{N}{60} \\
&= \dfrac{n \cdot N}{120}
\end{aligned}$$

where $n$ is the number of cylinders.

**Step 3 — Verification for typical case.** For a 4-cylinder engine at 800 RPM:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{firing} &= \dfrac{4 \cdot 800}{120} \\
&= \dfrac{3200}{120} \\
&\approx 26.67\,\text{Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on KTU relevance.** A 26.67 Hz firing excitation at idle lies *inside* the roughness band (3–30 Hz). This is why idle roughness of inline-4 engines is a critical NVH issue and why V6 / V8 engines (with lower firing frequencies) feel smoother at idle despite having more cylinders.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of NVH Descriptors

| Descriptor | Frequency Band | Primary Path | Perception Channel | Typical Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Roughness** | **3 – 30 Hz** | Floor, seat, steering | Touch (kinesthetic) | Engine idle, driveshaft imbalance |
| Shake | 1 – 10 Hz | Whole vehicle body | Sight + touch (oscillation) | Tire imbalance, driveshaft 1st order |
| Boom | 30 – 80 Hz | Cabin cavity | Hearing + chest pressure | Exhaust/driveshaft 2nd order acoustic |
| Buzz | 100 – 500 Hz | Trim panels | Hearing | Powertrain harmonics radiating trim |
| Harshness | Single-event transient | Suspension links | Hearing + seat jolt | Road impacts, shock absorber valving |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow — Roughness Generation & Mitigation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["EXCITATION SOURCES"] --> nodeB["ENGINE FIRING ORDERS"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["DRIVELINE IMBALANCE"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["TIRE RFV AND ROAD INPUT"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["STRUCTURAL TRANSMISSION PATH"]
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF["BODY IN WHITE MODES 5 TO 50 HZ"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["RECEPTION INTERFACES"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["SEAT PAN 4 TO 8 HZ"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["STEERING RIM 8 TO 16 HZ"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["FLOOR PAN 12 TO 25 HZ"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["HUMAN SUBJECTIVE RATING"]
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL["COUNTERMEASURE FEEDBACK"]
    L --> nodeM["ENGINE MOUNT TUNING"]
    L --> nodeN["BIW STIFFNESS MODIFICATION"]
    L --> nodeO["DAMPING MATERIAL ADDITION"]
```

### 4.2 Frequency-Band Allocation Map (NVH Landscape)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["BELOW 3 HZ"] --> nodeA1["DISPLACEMENT MOTION - NOT VIBRATION"]
    node2["3 TO 10 HZ"] --> nodeA2["SHAKE BAND"]
    node3["10 TO 30 HZ"] --> nodeA3["ROUGHNESS CORE BAND"]
    node4["30 TO 80 HZ"] --> nodeA4["BOOM BAND"]
    node5["80 TO 300 HZ"] --> nodeA5["BUZZ AND DRUM BAND"]
    node6["ABOVE 300 HZ"] --> nodeA6["HARSHNESS AND RATTLE BAND"]
```

### 4.3 ISO 2631-1 Weighting Filter Response (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRaw["RAW ACCELEROMETER SIGNAL"] --> nodeW1["APPLY W_K WEIGHTING SEAT PAN"]
    nodeRaw --> nodeW2["APPLY W_D WEIGHTING SEAT BACK"]
    nodeRaw --> nodeW3["APPLY W_F WEIGHTING FEET"]
    nodeW1 --> nodeRMS["CALCULATE RMS AND VDV"]
    nodeW2 --> nodeRMS
    nodeW3 --> nodeRMS
    nodeRMS --> nodeISO["COMPARE AGAINST ISO 2631-1 EXPOSURE ZONES"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams follow the **Mermaid alphanumeric-node convention**. All node IDs are prefixed alphabetically to avoid any reserved-keyword collision with Mermaid's parser, and all labels are kept in plain uppercase text without any inline markdown tags to guarantee clean rendering inside the diagram.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define vehicle roughness. State the typical frequency band and the primary perception channel associated with it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Roughness is the subjective descriptor used in NVH engineering to characterize low-frequency structural vibration transmitted to the vehicle occupants, primarily perceived through tactile feedback at the seat, steering wheel, and floor.
- **Frequency band (1 mark):** The standard industry-accepted band is **3 Hz to 30 Hz**.
- **Perception channel (1 mark):** Primarily the **kinesthetic (touch)** sense via the seat-pan, hands, and feet — *not* primarily hearing.

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between Roughness and Harshness with respect to (i) frequency range and (ii) human perception mechanism.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (i) **Frequency Range (1.5 marks):** Roughness occupies the **3–30 Hz** band and is dominated by steady-state low-frequency structural excitation. Harshness is associated with **transient, broadband high-frequency events (> 80 Hz)** such as suspension impacts over bumps.
- (ii) **Perception (1.5 marks):** Roughness is perceived via **continuous whole-body tactile sensation** at the seat and steering wheel; Harshness is perceived as a **sharp, single-event jolt** combined with a transient sound burst.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **frequency-weighted, fourth-power VDV metric** as defined in **ISO 2631-1** for assessing vehicle roughness. Derive the VDV expression and comment on why the 4th-power law is preferred over RMS for cumulative exposure assessment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine idles at **900 RPM**. Calculate the **firing frequency**, identify whether it lies in the roughness band, and suggest **two engineering countermeasures** to reduce the resulting cabin roughness. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** ISO 2631-1 prescribes frequency-weighting filters $W_k$, $W_d$, $W_f$ corresponding to seat-pan, seat-back, and foot positions respectively.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Frequency-weighted acceleration is computed by convolution:

$$a_{w}(t) = W_{k} \star a(t)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The VDV is defined as the 4th root of the time-integrated 4th-power dose:

$$VDV = \left[\int_{0}^{T} a_{w}^{4}(t)\,dt\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The 4th-power law reflects the **non-linear growth of human discomfort** with vibration amplitude — a doubling of acceleration produces ~16× the dose, matching psychophysical Stevens' power-law responses.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Firing frequency formula:

$$f_{firing} = \dfrac{n \cdot N}{120} = \dfrac{4 \times 900}{120} = 30\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Since 30 Hz falls at the upper edge of the 3–30 Hz roughness band, the excitation is definitely perceived as roughness. (Strictly within band.)

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Two countermeasures:
1. **Retune engine mounts** — shift the mount natural frequency away from 30 Hz (typically target $\omega_n < \sqrt{2} f_{firing}$, i.e., below ~21 Hz) to maximize isolation.
2. **Stiffen floor-pan local panels or add damping patches** to suppress BIW mode amplification at ~30 Hz. Alternative: introduce **active engine mounts** with counter-vibration actuators.

---

#### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** With the aid of a labeled block diagram, describe the **complete chain of roughness generation, transmission, and perception** in a passenger car. Identify at least **three excitation sources** and **two structural transmission paths**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A driver experiences a steady vertical seat-track vibration of **0.4 m/s² (RMS)** for **30 minutes**. Using the **VDV formulation**, calculate the cumulative dose and comment on whether this falls within the **ISO 2631-1 health guidance caution zone** ($VDV > 8.5\,\text{m/s}^{1.75}$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 (3 marks):** Chain description:
- *Excitation Generation* → powertrain firing orders, driveline imbalance, tire RFV
- *Structural Transmission* → body-in-white modes, floor pan, steering column
- *Reception* → seat-pan (4–8 Hz sensitive), steering wheel (8–16 Hz)
- *Subjective Rating* → driver assigns a 1–10 score

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Three excitation sources: (i) Engine firing impulses, (ii) Driveshaft imbalance 1st order, (iii) Tire radial force variation.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Two transmission paths: (i) Engine → mount → subframe → floor pan, (ii) Wheel → suspension → firewall → steering column.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply the VDV definition with $a_w = 0.4\,\text{m/s}^{2}$ and $T = 1800\,\text{s}$:

$$D = a_{w}^{4} \cdot T = (0.4)^{4} \cdot 1800$$

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Evaluate:

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.4)^{4} &= 0.0256 \\
D &= 0.0256 \cdot 1800 \\
&= 46.08\,\text{(m/s}^{2})^{4}\cdot\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute VDV:

$$VDV = D^{1/4} = 46.08^{0.25} \approx 2.60\,\text{m/s}^{1.75}$$

**Step 4 (0 marks — comment only):** Since **2.60 m/s¹·⁷⁵ < 8.5 m/s¹·⁷⁵**, the exposure is **well below** the ISO 2631-1 health guidance caution zone — no health risk for this short exposure.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Roughness Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Students often write VDV as "m/s²" — the correct unit is **m/s¹·⁷⁵** (note the **1.75 exponent**, not 2). Loss: up to **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing Roughness with Buzz:** Writing the frequency band as "100–500 Hz" immediately marks the answer as incorrect for roughness. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting Frequency Weighting:** Reporting VDV without applying the $W_k$ filter loses **1–2 marks** depending on part weightage.
> 4. **Engine Firing Formula Confusion:** Forgetting the factor of 2 (4-stroke) — writing $f = nN/60$ instead of $f = nN/120$. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 5. **Skipping Units in Numerical Problems:** Always quote units at the **final step** — KTU examiners deduct **½ mark** per missing unit when explicitly required.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Roughness = Low-frequency tactile vibration = 3 Hz to 30 Hz band.**
- **Primary perception channel: Touch (seat, steering, floor), NOT hearing.**
- **Key metrics: $a_{RMS}$, VDV, Crest Factor, PSD peak in 3–30 Hz band.**
- **Governing standard: ISO 2631-1** for whole-body vibration exposure.
- **Engine firing frequency (4-stroke):** $f = \dfrac{n \cdot N}{120}$ Hz — verify against roughness band at idle.
- **VDV unit is m/s¹·⁷⁵** — NOT m/s².
- **ISO caution threshold: VDV > 8.5 m/s¹·⁷⁵** triggers health concern.
- **Countermeasures:** Mount tuning, BIW stiffening, damping material addition, active mounts.
- **4th-power law** in VDV reflects non-linear human discomfort growth.
- **Always compare subjective rating against objective metric** — never judge NVH quality from a single sensor point.
- **KTU 2024 Board Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, structure answers into **definition → formula → numerical step → engineering interpretation** to maximize the CO (Course Outcome) mapping score.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sharpness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sharpness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sharpness (S)** is a psychoacoustic metric that quantifies the subjective impression of the **brightness, harshness, or high-frequency content** of a sound. It is calculated as the **critical-band-rate-weighted sum of specific loudness** divided by the total loudness.

**Sharpness** describes the "edge" or "bite" of a sound. A sound with high sharpness is perceived as **shrill, piercing, or harsh**, while a sound with low sharpness sounds **dull, mellow, or rumbling**. It is one of the most important metrics in **Sound Quality Engineering** because human hearing is highly sensitive to the high-frequency components that drive sharpness.

**Standard Unit:** **acum** (named after *acoustical cumulus* of sharpness)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Definition:** By definition, **1 acum** corresponds to the sharpness of a **narrow-band noise centered at 1 kHz** with a sound pressure level of **60 dB**. This is the calibration anchor of the Zwicker sharpness model.

**Subjective Scale of Sharpness (S):**

| Sharpness (acum) | Subjective Impression |
|---|---|
| < 0.7 | Dull / Muffled |
| 0.7 – 1.0 | Neutral / Balanced |
| 1.0 – 1.5 | Slightly Sharp |
| 1.5 – 2.5 | Sharp / Harsh |
| 2.5 – 4.0 | Very Sharp |
| > 4.0 | Extremely Sharp / Penetrating |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a concert and hear two different drum strikes:

- **Kick Drum (Boom)** — A deep "thud" that you feel in your chest. Lots of low-frequency energy, almost no high frequencies. This is **low sharpness** (dull sound).
- **Hi-Hat Cymbal (Tsss)** — A bright, hissing sound that hits your ears sharply. Lots of high-frequency energy above 3 kHz. This is **high sharpness** (harsh sound).

**Sharpness is the "needle prick" factor of sound.** Just as a needle prick feels sharp on the skin because its force is concentrated at a tiny high-pressure point, a sound is sharp perceptually because its acoustic energy is concentrated in the high-frequency critical bands where human hearing is most sensitive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does sharpness matter in automobiles?**
> Modern car buyers complain when the door-closing sound is "tinny" or when the wiper motor sounds "piercing." These complaints are quantified using sharpness. NVH engineers deliberately tune components (e.g., add felt liners, change gear meshing patterns) to bring sharpness into a target range of **0.8–1.6 acum** for a perceived "premium" sound.

## 1.3 Physical Standard Metrics

The formal relationship between sharpness and the underlying psychoacoustic quantities is:

- **Specific Loudness (N′):** Expressed in **sones/Bark**
- **Critical Band Rate (z):** Expressed in **Bark**
- **Total Loudness (N):** Expressed in **sones**
- **Sharpness Weighting Function (g(z)):** A dimensionless multiplier that amplifies the contribution of high critical bands
- **Sharpness (S):** Expressed in **acum**

The standard sharpness model is named after **Eberhard Zwicker**, the German psychoacoustician who, along with Hugo Fastl, formalized it in the landmark reference *Psychoacoustics: Facts and Models*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The Zwicker sharpness model is built on three psychoacoustic pillars:

1. **The Bark Scale (Critical Band Rate):** The frequency range from 0 Hz to 16 kHz is divided into **24 critical bands** of hearing. Each band has a number $z$ measured in **Bark**. Critical bands widen with frequency, mirroring how the cochlea resolves pitch.

2. **Specific Loudness (N′):** This is the loudness "density" per critical band, with units of **sones/Bark**. It captures how loud each frequency region is perceived.

3. **The Weighting Function g(z):** Because human hearing is **disproportionately sensitive to high frequencies**, Zwicker introduced a weighting function that grows monotonically with critical band rate. Higher critical bands (high pitches) contribute disproportionately to sharpness.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Zwicker Sharpness Model

**Step 1 — Acquire the Sound Pressure Signal:**
- Measure or simulate the time signal of the sound.
- Convert to the frequency domain via FFT.
- Compute the **one-third-octave or critical-band sound pressure levels** $L_i$ (in dB).

**Step 2 — Convert SPL to Specific Loudness N′(z):**
- For each critical band $z_i$, convert the band sound pressure level to specific loudness using the **Zwicker loudness model** (ISO 532-1 / IEC 60268-7).

**Step 3 — Apply the Weighting Function g(z):**
- Weight each specific loudness value using:

$$
g(z) = 
\begin{cases}
0.063 \cdot e^{0.29 \cdot z} & \text{for } 0 \le z \le 15.5 \\[4pt]
10^{0.31 \cdot (z - 15.5)} & \text{for } 15.5 < z \le 23.5
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Total Loudness:**
- Integrate the specific loudness over the full critical band range:

$$
N = \int_{0}^{24 \text{ Bark}} N'(z) \, dz \quad [\text{sones}]
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Sharpness:**

$$
S = \dfrac{0.11 \cdot \displaystyle\int_{0}^{24} g(z) \cdot N'(z) \cdot dz}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{24} N'(z) \cdot dz} \quad [\text{acum}]
$$

**Step 6 — Verify Calibration:**
- The constant **0.11** is calibrated so that a narrow-band noise at **1 kHz, 60 dB** yields exactly **1 acum**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the numerator weighted but not the denominator?**
> The weighting function $g(z)$ emphasizes high-frequency loudness in the **numerator** (sharpness), while the **denominator** (total loudness) is unweighted. This means that for two sounds of equal total loudness, the one with more high-frequency energy is judged sharper.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = 0.11 \cdot \dfrac{\int_{0}^{24} g(z) \cdot N'(z) \, dz}{\int_{0}^{24} N'(z) \, dz}$ | Zwicker Sharpness | **acum** |
| 2 | $g(z) = 0.063 \cdot e^{0.29 z}$ for $z \in [0, 15.5]$ | Weighting function (low/mid bark) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $g(z) = 10^{0.31(z - 15.5)}$ for $z \in [15.5, 23.5]$ | Weighting function (high bark) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $N = \int_{0}^{24} N'(z) \, dz$ | Total Loudness (Zwicker) | **sones** |
| 5 | $z \approx 13 \cdot \arctan(0.00076 \cdot f) + 3.5 \cdot \arctan\left[(f/7500)^2\right]$ | Hz to Bark conversion | **Bark** |
| 6 | $S_5 = S \cdot (1 - e^{-N})$ | Sharpness for time-varying signals (5 %-percentile cap) | **acum** |
| 7 | $S_{Aures} = \dfrac{0.11}{\ln(1.066)} \cdot \dfrac{\int N'(z) \cdot \ln(0.0005 + g(z)) \, dz}{\int N'(z) \, dz}$ | Aures Sharpness (alternative model) | **acum** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Always remember that **the weighting function g(z) is piecewise exponential**, with a **transition at z = 15.5 Bark** (≈ 3.15 kHz). This transition corresponds to the high-frequency region where human ears become sharply sensitive.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Sharpness in Engineering

| Industry | Application | Target Sharpness |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive NVH** | Door-closing, wiper-motor, seat-adjustment sound | 0.8 – 1.6 acum |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Laptop fan noise, vacuum cleaner hum | 0.6 – 1.2 acum |
| **Home Appliances** | Washing-machine spin cycle, dishwasher | 1.0 – 1.5 acum |
| **Power Tools** | Drill, grinder acoustic signature | 1.5 – 2.5 acum |
| **HVAC Systems** | Indoor blower tonal noise | 0.5 – 1.0 acum |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Sharpness is one of the **direct inputs to Sound Quality Indices** such as the **Psychoacoustic Annoyance (PA)** and the **Zwicker Loudness Model-based Sound Quality (Zwicker-SQ)**. Lower sharpness with the same loudness is almost universally perceived as more pleasant.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sharpness Formula from First Principles

### 3.1.1 Why a Weighted Average?

**Step 1 — Define the goal.**
We want a single scalar $S$ that captures how "sharp" a sound is. Sharpness is purely about the **distribution of loudness across frequency**, not its absolute magnitude.

**Step 2 — Start with total loudness.**
Total loudness $N$ is the unweighted integral of specific loudness:

$$
N = \int_{0}^{24} N'(z) \, dz
$$

**Step 3 — Define a "weighted loudness."**
Multiply each $N'(z)$ by the weighting function $g(z)$ and integrate:

$$
N_w = \int_{0}^{24} g(z) \cdot N'(z) \, dz
$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio.**
The ratio $N_w / N$ has units of $g(z)$ — dimensionless. To convert this into the cum/acum scale, multiply by the calibration constant **0.11**:

$$
S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{N_w}{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the calibration anchor.**
A narrow-band noise at 1 kHz, 60 dB occupies approximately $z \approx 7.4$ Bark. Its specific loudness is approximately 1 sone/Bark concentrated at one band. With $g(7.4) = 0.063 \cdot e^{0.29 \cdot 7.4} \approx 1.0$, we get:

$$
S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{1.0 \cdot 1.0}{1.0} = 1.0 \text{ acum} \;\; \checkmark
$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Numerical Example

**Problem:** A noise has specific loudness $N'(z)$ uniformly equal to **1 sone/Bark** between $z = 10$ Bark and $z = 16$ Bark, and zero elsewhere. Compute the sharpness.

**Given Data:**
- $N'(z) = 1$ for $z \in [10, 16]$
- $N'(z) = 0$ elsewhere

**Step A — Compute Total Loudness (Denominator).**

$$
N = \int_{10}^{16} 1 \, dz = (16 - 10) = 6 \text{ sones}
$$

**Step B — Compute Weighted Loudness (Numerator).**

For $z \in [10, 15.5]$, we use the lower piece $g(z) = 0.063 \cdot e^{0.29z}$:

$$
\int_{10}^{15.5} 0.063 \cdot e^{0.29z} \cdot 1 \, dz
$$

Let us integrate:

$$
\int 0.063 \cdot e^{0.29z} \, dz = 0.063 \cdot \frac{e^{0.29z}}{0.29} = \frac{0.063}{0.29} \cdot e^{0.29z}
$$

Numerically:

$$
\frac{0.063}{0.29} = 0.21724
$$

Evaluate from 10 to 15.5:

$$
0.21724 \cdot \left( e^{0.29 \cdot 15.5} - e^{0.29 \cdot 10} \right)
$$

Compute the exponentials:

$$
0.29 \cdot 15.5 = 4.495 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{4.495} \approx 89.49
$$

$$
0.29 \cdot 10 = 2.90 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{2.90} \approx 18.17
$$

Therefore:

$$
\int_{10}^{15.5} g(z) \cdot 1 \, dz = 0.21724 \cdot (89.49 - 18.17) = 0.21724 \cdot 71.32 \approx 15.49
$$

For $z \in [15.5, 16]$, we use the upper piece $g(z) = 10^{0.31(z - 15.5)}$:

$$
\int_{15.5}^{16} 10^{0.31(z - 15.5)} \cdot 1 \, dz
$$

Let $u = z - 15.5$, so $du = dz$, $u \in [0, 0.5]$:

$$
\int_{0}^{0.5} 10^{0.31 u} \, du
$$

We use:

$$
\int 10^{a u} \, du = \frac{10^{a u}}{a \cdot \ln(10)}
$$

With $a = 0.31$:

$$
= \left[ \frac{10^{0.31 u}}{0.31 \cdot \ln(10)} \right]_{0}^{0.5}
$$

Numerically:

$$
0.31 \cdot \ln(10) = 0.31 \cdot 2.3026 = 0.7138
$$

Evaluate:

$$
10^{0.31 \cdot 0.5} = 10^{0.155} \approx 1.4302
$$

Therefore:

$$
\int_{15.5}^{16} g(z) \, dz = \frac{1.4302 - 1}{0.7138} \approx \frac{0.4302}{0.7138} \approx 0.6027
$$

**Step C — Sum the Two Pieces.**

$$
N_w = 15.49 + 0.603 \approx 16.09
$$

**Step D — Compute Sharpness.**

$$
S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{N_w}{N} = 0.11 \cdot \frac{16.09}{6} = 0.11 \cdot 2.682 \approx 0.295 \text{ acum}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even though the total loudness is a moderate 6 sones, the sound is in the mid-to-high frequency range, giving a sharpness of about **0.3 acum** — on the dull side. This shows that the *frequency distribution* of loudness matters more than total loudness for sharpness.

### 3.1.3 Sensitivity Analysis: Doubling the Frequency of the Same Noise

Suppose the same loudness profile of 1 sone/Bark is now uniformly distributed between $z = 18$ Bark and $z = 22$ Bark (high frequencies).

**Denominator:**

$$
N = \int_{18}^{22} 1 \, dz = 4 \text{ sones}
$$

**Numerator (both pieces in upper zone):**

For $z \in [18, 22]$, $g(z) = 10^{0.31(z - 15.5)}$:

$$
\int_{18}^{22} 10^{0.31(z - 15.5)} \, dz
$$

Let $u = z - 15.5$, so $u \in [2.5, 6.5]$:

$$
\int_{2.5}^{6.5} 10^{0.31 u} \, du = \frac{1}{0.7138} \left[ 10^{0.31 u} \right]_{2.5}^{6.5}
$$

Compute:

$$
10^{0.31 \cdot 6.5} = 10^{2.015} \approx 103.5
$$

$$
10^{0.31 \cdot 2.5} = 10^{0.775} \approx 5.957
$$

Therefore:

$$
N_w = \frac{103.5 - 5.957}{0.7138} \approx \frac{97.54}{0.7138} \approx 136.65
$$

**Sharpness:**

$$
S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{136.65}{4} \approx 0.11 \cdot 34.16 \approx 3.76 \text{ acum}
$$

**Result:** The same loudness in high frequencies gives a sharpness of **3.76 acum** — in the "very sharp" range — versus only **0.295 acum** for the mid-band version. This confirms the strong frequency dependence of sharpness and justifies the **piecewise exponential weighting function**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Frequency on Sharpness

| Critical Band Range | Approx. Frequency | $g(z)$ Value (approx) | $S$ (1 sone/Bark uniform) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 – 5 Bark | 0 – 500 Hz | 0.07 – 0.22 | 0.01 – 0.02 acum |
| 5 – 10 Bark | 500 Hz – 1.5 kHz | 0.22 – 1.0 | 0.02 – 0.15 acum |
| 10 – 15.5 Bark | 1.5 kHz – 3.15 kHz | 1.0 – 7.1 | 0.15 – 1.2 acum |
| 15.5 – 20 Bark | 3.15 kHz – 6.5 kHz | 1.0 – 5.6 | 1.2 – 4.5 acum |
| 20 – 24 Bark | 6.5 kHz – 16 kHz | 5.6 – 21 | 4.5 – 10+ acum |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sharpness Calculation Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Time Signal p t] --> B[FFT Analysis]
    B --> C[One-Third Octave SPLs L i in dB]
    C --> D[Convert Frequency Hz to Critical Band Rate z in Bark]
    D --> E[Zwicker Loudness Model ISO 532-1]
    E --> F[Specific Loudness N prime z in sones per Bark]
    F --> G[Apply Weighting Function g of z]
    G --> H[Weighted Specific Loudness g z times N prime z]
    F --> I[Total Loudness N integral of N prime z dz]
    H --> J[Weighted Loudness N w integral of g z times N prime z dz]
    I --> K[Compute Ratio N w over N]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Multiply by Calibration Constant 0.11]
    L --> M[Sharpness S in acum]
    M --> N[Subjective Classification Table]
    N --> O{Dull Neutral Sharp Harsh}
    O --> P[NVH Engineering Decision]
```

## 4.2 Sharpness Model Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Psychoacoustic Descriptors] --> B[Zwicker Loudness N]
    A --> C[Sharpness S]
    A --> D[Fluctuation Strength F]
    A --> E[Roughness R]
    B --> F[Specific Loudness N prime z]
    F --> G[Weighting g of z]
    G --> C
    F --> D
    F --> E
    C --> H[Sound Quality Index PA]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    H --> I[NVH Engineering Decision]
```

## 4.3 Mapping Frequency to Perceived Sharpness

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LowFreq[Low Frequency Region]
        LF1[0 to 500 Hz] --> LF2[Dull Sub 0.2 acum]
    end
    subgraph MidFreq[Mid Frequency Region]
        MF1[500 Hz to 2 kHz] --> MF2[Balanced 0.2 to 0.8 acum]
    end
    subgraph HighFreq[High Frequency Region]
        HF1[2 kHz to 5 kHz] --> HF2[Sharp 0.8 to 2.5 acum]
    end
    subgraph VeryHighFreq[Very High Frequency Region]
        VF1[5 kHz to 16 kHz] --> VF2[Penetrating Above 2.5 acum]
    end
    LowFreq --> MidFreq
    MidFreq --> HighFreq
    HighFreq --> VeryHighFreq
```

## 4.4 Sharpness in Automotive Door-Closure Sound Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Target Premium Sound] --> B[Door Closure Event]
    B --> C[Sensor Measurement Microphone]
    C --> D[FFT to Spectrum]
    D --> E[Zwicker Loudness N]
    E --> F[Sharpness S]
    F --> G{Target Range 0.8 to 1.6 acum}
    G -- Yes --> H[Approved Design]
    G -- No Too Sharp --> I[Add Felt Liner]
    G -- No Too Dull --> J[Increase High Freq Stiffness]
    I --> K[Re measure]
    J --> K
    K --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:** This functional block diagram shows the closed-loop engineering process for tuning sharpness in vehicle door-closure acoustics. The decision node tests whether the computed sharpness falls inside the brand's target band. If not, the loop iterates through material/component changes until the target is achieved.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define sharpness. State its units. Mention the calibration reference used for sharpness.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Sharpness (S)** is a psychoacoustic descriptor that quantifies the subjective impression of the brightness or high-frequency dominance of a sound. It is calculated as the **critical-band-rate-weighted sum of specific loudness divided by total loudness**.
>
> **Unit:** **acum**
>
> **Calibration Reference:** **1 acum** corresponds to the sharpness of a **narrow-band noise centered at 1 kHz at 60 dB SPL**, by definition. This anchors the Zwicker model and is fundamental to all sound-quality applications.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for unit, 1 for calibration reference)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Why is sharpness important in NVH engineering? Give two real-world examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Sharpness is critical because human hearing is highly sensitive to high-frequency content, and excessive sharpness is perceived as **harshness, annoyance, or "tinny" sound quality**. Two examples:

1. **Automotive door-closure sound:** Target sharpness is **0.8–1.6 acum**. Sounds above this are perceived as "metallic" or "cheap."
2. **Washing-machine spin cycle:** Target sharpness is **1.0–1.5 acum**; higher values cause consumer complaints of "piercing" noise.

*(3 marks: 1 for importance, 2 for examples with target values)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for sharpness $S$ as formulated by Zwicker. Clearly state the weighting function and its two regimes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A noise has a uniform specific loudness of 1 sone/Bark over the range $z = 12$ to $z = 18$ Bark. Compute its total loudness and sharpness. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Definition of Sharpness (Zwicker):**

$$
S = 0.11 \cdot \dfrac{\int_{0}^{24 \text{ Bark}} g(z) \cdot N'(z) \, dz}{\int_{0}^{24 \text{ Bark}} N'(z) \, dz} \quad [\text{acum}]
$$

**Weighting Function (two regimes):**

$$
g(z) = 
\begin{cases}
0.063 \cdot e^{0.29 z} & \text{for } 0 \le z \le 15.5 \\[4pt]
10^{0.31(z - 15.5)} & \text{for } 15.5 < z \le 23.5
\end{cases}
$$

**Reasoning behind the structure:**

- The constant **0.11** is the calibration factor that ensures a 1 kHz, 60 dB narrow-band noise has sharpness = 1 acum.
- The function $g(z)$ grows **exponentially** with critical-band rate, reflecting the disproportionate contribution of high frequencies to sharpness.
- The transition at $z = 15.5$ Bark (≈ 3.15 kHz) marks the change from "loudness-weighted" to "very-high-frequency-weighted" perception.
- The numerator captures **weighted loudness**; the denominator is **total loudness**, so the ratio is a pure **distribution measure**, independent of overall sound level.

*(7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for weighting function, 3 for reasoning)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Total Loudness:**

$$
N = \int_{12}^{18} 1 \, dz = 18 - 12 = 6 \text{ sones}
$$

**Step 2 — Weighted Loudness.**

For $z \in [12, 15.5]$, use $g(z) = 0.063 \cdot e^{0.29z}$:

$$
\int_{12}^{15.5} 0.063 \cdot e^{0.29z} \, dz = \frac{0.063}{0.29}\left(e^{0.29 \cdot 15.5} - e^{0.29 \cdot 12}\right)
$$

$$
= 0.21724 \cdot (e^{4.495} - e^{3.48}) = 0.21724 \cdot (89.49 - 32.46) = 0.21724 \cdot 57.03 \approx 12.39
$$

For $z \in [15.5, 18]$, use $g(z) = 10^{0.31(z - 15.5)}$:

$$
\int_{15.5}^{18} 10^{0.31(z - 15.5)} \, dz = \frac{1}{0.31 \cdot \ln(10)} \left[10^{0.31 u}\right]_{0}^{2.5}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{0.7138} \cdot (10^{0.775} - 1) = \frac{1}{0.7138} \cdot (5.957 - 1) = \frac{4.957}{0.7138} \approx 6.944
$$

**Step 3 — Sum:**

$$
N_w = 12.39 + 6.944 \approx 19.33
$$

**Step 4 — Sharpness:**

$$
S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{19.33}{6} = 0.11 \cdot 3.222 \approx 0.354 \text{ acum}
$$

**Interpretation:** A uniform loudness distribution in the upper-mid Bark range yields a sharpness of about 0.35 acum — perceived as **dull to neutral**, since the bulk of the loudness is below 3 kHz.

*(7 marks: 1 for total loudness, 4 for weighted loudness in two segments, 1 for sharpness calculation, 1 for interpretation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of the critical band rate (Bark scale). How does it relate to sharpness calculation? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the Zwicker, Aures, and Von Bismarck sharpness models. Mention their respective formulas and use cases. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Critical Band Rate (Bark):**

The human cochlea behaves like a bank of **24 band-pass filters** of unequal bandwidth. Each filter is called a **critical band**, and its index is the **critical band rate $z$ in Bark**.

- $z = 0$ Bark corresponds to 0 Hz.
- $z = 24$ Bark corresponds to ≈ 16 kHz (limit of human hearing).
- A widely used conversion is:

$$
z \approx 13 \cdot \arctan(0.00076 \cdot f) + 3.5 \cdot \arctan\left[\left(\frac{f}{7500}\right)^2\right] \quad f \text{ in Hz}
$$

**Relationship to Sharpness:**

- Sharpness is **fundamentally a function of critical band rate**, not raw frequency in Hz.
- Each critical band has its own **specific loudness** $N'(z)$ — this is the building block.
- The Bark scale captures the **non-linear frequency resolution** of the cochlea: at low frequencies, the bands are narrow (good pitch resolution); at high frequencies, the bands widen.
- The **weighting function $g(z)$** is defined on the Bark scale precisely because perceptual "sharpness" depends on which critical band the energy occupies, not on raw Hz.

**Why this matters:**

Without the Bark scale, sharpness would be a meaningless linear-frequency average. The Bark scale aligns the model with the inner-ear filtering that determines what humans actually hear.

*(7 marks: 2 for concept of critical band, 2 for conversion equation, 3 for relation to sharpness)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

| Model | Formula (simplified) | Weighting Philosophy | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zwicker (1965)** | $S = 0.11 \cdot \dfrac{\int g(z) N'(z)\,dz}{\int N'(z)\,dz}$ | Exponential weighting of high-Bark loudness | **Standard ISO/ANSI**; baseline for all NVH work |
| **Aures (1985)** | $S = \dfrac{0.11}{\ln(1.066)} \cdot \dfrac{\int N'(z) \ln(0.0005 + g(z))\,dz}{\int N'(z)\,dz}$ | Logarithmic weighting → flatter response | **Pleasantness studies**, music perception |
| **Von Bismarck (1974)** | $S = \dfrac{\int z \cdot N'(z)\,dz}{\int N'(z)\,dz} - 0.7$ | Linear weighting in Bark | **Quick approximations** for tonal noise |

**Discussion:**

- The **Zwicker model** is the **industrial standard** and is what most NVH software (HEAD ArtemiS, Brüel & Kjær Sound Quality, Siemens TecWare) implement.
- The **Aures model** softens the contribution of very-high-frequency bands, making it better for **musical sounds** where excessive sharpness is judged less harshly.
- The **Von Bismarck model** is the simplest — linear in Bark — and is used only as a **first-order estimate** for sounds dominated by isolated tones.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for three models with formulas, 2 marks for weighting philosophy, 2 marks for use cases)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Sharpness Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the calibration constant 0.11.** Many students write $S = \int g N' / \int N'$ and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong piece of g(z).** For $z > 15.5$ Bark, you **must** use the second piece $10^{0.31(z-15.5)}$; using $0.063 \cdot e^{0.29z}$ beyond 15.5 Bark is a common error.
> 3. **Mixing units of N′ (sones/Bark) with N (sones).** N′ is a density; integrating it over dz gives total N.
> 4. **Stating sharpness as a frequency-weighted quantity in dB.** Sharpness is **not** a dB-level metric; it is a psychoacoustic metric in **acum**.
> 5. **Ignoring the Bark-scale conversion.** If the problem gives frequencies in Hz, you **must** convert to Bark before applying $g(z)$.
> 6. **Confusing sharpness with roughness or fluctuation strength.** Sharpness = spectral location; roughness = temporal modulation (~70 Hz); fluctuation strength = slower modulation (~4 Hz).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Sharpness (S)** measures the **subjective high-frequency content / brightness** of a sound.
- [ ] **Unit:** **acum**; **Calibration:** 1 acum = 1 kHz narrow-band noise at 60 dB SPL.
- [ ] **Zwicker Formula:** $S = 0.11 \cdot \dfrac{\int g(z) N'(z) dz}{\int N'(z) dz}$
- [ ] **Weighting Function:** Piecewise exponential — $0.063 e^{0.29 z}$ for $z \le 15.5$; $10^{0.31(z-15.5)}$ for $z > 15.5$.
- [ ] **Bark Scale:** 0 to 24 critical bands; converts Hz to perceptual frequency.
- [ ] **Sharpness ≠ Loudness:** Two sounds of equal loudness can have very different sharpness depending on frequency distribution.
- [ ] **Standard Models:** Zwicker (industrial standard), Aures (music), Von Bismarck (tones).
- [ ] **Target Ranges:** Automotive NVH = 0.8–1.6 acum; HVAC = 0.5–1.0 acum; power tools = 1.5–2.5 acum.
- [ ] **Engineering Use:** Inputs to **Psychoacoustic Annoyance (PA)** and overall **Sound Quality (SQ)** indices.
- [ ] **Always convert Hz → Bark** before computing $g(z)$ — this is the most common student mistake.
- [ ] **5%-percentile sharpness ($S_5$)** is used for time-varying signals (e.g., pass-by vehicle noise).
- [ ] The transition at **z = 15.5 Bark (≈ 3.15 kHz)** is the **perceptual hot zone** where sharpness skyrockets.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluctuation Strength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Fluctuation Strength ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Fluctuation Strength (F)** is a standardized **psychoacoustic metric** (perceived loudness variation metric) that quantifies the subjective impression produced by *slow* amplitude modulations of a sound, typically in the modulation frequency range of **0.5 Hz to 20 Hz**. It describes how strongly a listener perceives the *rise and fall* of loudness over time in a sound signal. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEAUT755 – Module 4):** > Fluctuation Strength is one of the four pillar psychoacoustic descriptors of sound quality, alongside **Loudness (N)**, **Sharpness (S)**, and **Roughness (R)**. Where Roughness handles fast modulations (15–300 Hz), Fluctuation Strength is restricted to *slow* modulations (< 20 Hz) which the human ear-brain system resolves as discrete loudness changes rather than as a continuous buzz. - **Standard Reference**: Zwicker & Fastl model; standardized in **ISO 532-1 / DIN 45631**. - **Unit**: **Vacil** (symbol: **vacil**), where 1 vacil corresponds to a 100% modulated 1 kHz tone at 4 Hz modulation frequency and **60 dB SPL** producing a modulation depth of 1 dB. - **Range of Perception**: 0 vacil (steady sound) → ~1.0 vacil (strongly fluctuating). > [!NOTE] > **Critical Modulation Frequency:** Human hearing is **maximally sensitive to loudness fluctuations at 4 Hz**. This means a sound fluctuating at 4 Hz feels more "pulsating" than the same depth of modulation at 1 Hz or 20 Hz. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Flickering Candle" Mental Model Imagine you are reading a book in a room. A steady lamp gives constant, comfortable light. Now imagine that same lamp is connected to a faulty dimmer switch and is *slowly* brightening and dimming at a rate of about 4 cycles per second. - You do not perceive the light as "humming" or "buzzy" (that would be *roughness*). - You clearly *perceive* the pulses of brightness — this conscious awareness of pulsing is exactly what **Fluctuation Strength** measures for sound. - If the dimmer were flickering at 30 Hz, your eye would average it out — similarly, the ear averages out loudness fluctuations above ~20 Hz into **roughness** instead of **fluctuation**. **Engineering Translation:** In a car cabin, if the HVAC blower produces a 4 Hz "whoosh-whoosh" pulsation, that pulsation has high fluctuation strength and is judged as annoying. A smooth blower with no pulsation has near-zero fluctuation strength. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Reference Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Critical Modulation Frequency | $f_{crit}$ | **4 Hz** | | Upper Modulation Limit | $f_{max}$ | **20 Hz** | | Lower Modulation Limit | $f_{min}$ | **0.5 Hz** | | Unit of Fluctuation Strength | F | **vacil** | | Reference Tone | — | 1 kHz @ **60 dB SPL** | | Reference Modulation Depth | $\Delta L_0$ | **1 dB** | ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — How Fluctuation Strength is Computed The calculation of Fluctuation Strength follows a deterministic **Zwicker-stage signal processing pipeline**. The conceptual steps are: 1. **Stage 1 — Time-Domain Pressure Acquisition:** The acoustic signal $p(t)$ is captured. 2. **Stage 2 — Spectral Analysis into Critical Bands:** The signal is decomposed into 24 critical-band rates (Bark scale), $z = 0 \dots 24$. 3. **Stage 3 — Specific Loudness Calculation:** For each Bark band $z$ and each time instant $t$, the specific loudness $N'(z, t)$ is computed using the stationary loudness model (Zwicker). 4. **Stage 4 — Temporal Derivative of Specific Loudness:** The rate of change of $N'(z, t)$ with respect to time is computed, $\dfrac{dN'(z, t)}{dt}$. 5. **Stage 5 — Local Fluctuation Strength per Band:** The squared time-derivative is normalized by a threshold function. 6. **Stage 6 — Integration:** The local contributions are integrated across all Bark bands and the modulation period to yield total Fluctuation Strength $F$ in vacil. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why derivative-based?** The ear-brain system detects fluctuation strength from the *speed* at which loudness changes. A 1 dB change in 0.1 s feels stronger than the same 1 dB change in 1 s. Hence, the metric fundamentally depends on $\dfrac{dN'}{dt}$, not on $\Delta L$ alone. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following is the **board-exam relevant** compact formula set. All equations use $\vert \cdot \vert$ as a vertical bar — to maintain table integrity on KTU digital answer sheets, the boundary bars have been written as `\vert`. | # | Formula (using $\vert$ for abs. value) | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F = \displaystyle\int\_{0}^{24}\int\_{0}^{T}\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{dN'(z,t)}{dt}\right)^{2}}{\left(\dfrac{dN'(z,t)}{dt}\right)^{2} + (2\pi \cdot 4 \cdot N'\_{0})^{2}}\,\dfrac{dt}{N'\_{0}}\,dz$ | Master Zwicker definition of Fluctuation Strength | vacil | | 2 | $f\_{crit} = 4\,\text{Hz}$ | Critical modulation frequency of maximum sensitivity | Hz | | 3 | $N'\_{0} = 0.0082\,\text{acum/Bark}$ | Reference specific loudness normalization | acum/Bark | | 4 | $\dfrac{dN'}{dt} \approx \dfrac{\Delta N'}{\Delta t}$ | Finite-difference approximation of loudness slope | acum/s | | 5 | $F\_{max}\ \text{at}\ f\_{mod} = 4\,\text{Hz}$ | Empirical maximum of human fluctuation perception | vacil | | 6 | $F \approx k \cdot \dfrac{\Delta L^{2}}{f\_{mod}^{2} + 16}$ | Simplified engineering approximation | vacil | | 7 | $\Delta L = 20\log\_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p\_{max}}{p\_{min}}\right)$ | Modulation depth in dB | dB | | 8 | $f\_{mod} = \dfrac{1}{T\_{mod}}$ | Modulation frequency from period | Hz | > **Where:** > - $N'(z, t)$ = time-varying specific loudness at critical-band rate $z$ and time $t$. > - $N'_0$ = a small reference specific loudness that sets the absolute scale (so the result is dimensionless in "vacil"). > - $\Delta L$ = modulation depth of the sound pressure level. > - $k$ = empirical constant (typically $k \approx 0.0082$). ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Fluctuation Strength is a primary **subjective acceptance criterion** in: - **Electric Vehicle (EV) Acoustic Design:** Traction-motor torque ripple creates 4–20 Hz tonal modulation; engineers target $F < 0.1$ vacil for premium-brand acceptance. - **HVAC System NVH:** Blower-fan pass-by tones induce pulsations — minimizing $F$ improves "premium" feel. - **Power-Steering Whine:** 6–18 Hz whine components map directly to fluctuation-strength annoyance. - **Wiper and Washer Noise:** The 1–3 Hz stroke of a wiper blade is a textbook fluctuation-strength problem. - **Tire Cavity Resonance:** Tonal hum at 200–250 Hz with envelope modulation is felt as both roughness and fluctuation strength. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Modulation-Depth Approximation We start with a **sinusoidally amplitude-modulated** sound pressure: $$ p(t) = p_0 \left[1 + m\sin(2\pi f_{mod} t)\right]\cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ where: - $p_0$ = carrier amplitude, - $m$ = modulation index ($0 \le m \le 1$), - $f_c$ = carrier (tonal) frequency, - $f_{mod}$ = modulation frequency (0.5–20 Hz). **Step 1 — Compute the modulation depth in dB.** The peak pressure is $p_0(1+m)$ and the trough pressure is $p_0(1-m)$: $$ \Delta L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_0(1+m)}{p_0(1-m)}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1+m}{1-m}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Relate specific-loudness variation to modulation depth.** For a moderate-level tone (around 40–80 dB SPL), specific loudness varies approximately linearly with sound pressure level. Therefore, the *peak-to-trough* specific-loudness difference is: $$ \Delta N' = \alpha\,\Delta L $$ where $\alpha$ is the local slope of the $N'$–$L$ curve at the operating point (typically $0.01$ to $0.05$ acum/Bark per dB). **Step 3 — Compute the rate of change of specific loudness.** For a sinusoidal modulation of $N'(t)$: $$ \dfrac{dN'}{dt}\bigg|_{max} = 2\pi f_{mod}\,\Delta N' \cos(2\pi f_{mod} t)\,\bigg|_{max} = 2\pi f_{mod}\,\Delta N' $$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the Zwicker master definition.** Inserting the above into Formula (1) of the table, time-averaging over one modulation period, and recognizing that the time-derivative squared peaks at $(2\pi f_{mod}\Delta N')^2$: $$ F = \int_0^{24} \dfrac{(2\pi f_{mod}\Delta N')^2}{(2\pi f_{mod}\Delta N')^2 + (2\pi \cdot 4 \cdot N'_0)^2}\,\dfrac{dz}{N'_0} $$ **Step 5 — Simplify for a single-band case (carrier well-localized in one critical band).** Let $N'_0$ be the reference scale and define the normalized modulation $\beta = \dfrac{2\pi f_{mod}\Delta N'}{2\pi \cdot 4 \cdot N'_0}$. Then: $$ F = \dfrac{\beta^2}{1 + \beta^2} $$ **Step 6 — Expand $\beta$.** $$ \beta = \dfrac{f_{mod}\Delta N'}{4 N'_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{f_{mod}\Delta N'}{4 N'_0}\right)^2}{1 + \left(\dfrac{f_{mod}\Delta N'}{4 N'_0}\right)^2} $$ **Step 7 — Linearize for small modulations ($\beta \ll 1$).** When $\Delta N'$ is small, the denominator $\approx 1$, and we obtain the simplified engineering form: $$ F \approx \left(\dfrac{f_{mod}\,\Delta N'}{4\,N'_0}\right)^2 $$ Substituting $N'_0 = 0.0082$ acum/Bark and $\Delta N' = \alpha\,\Delta L$: $$ F \approx \left(\dfrac{f_{mod}\,\alpha\,\Delta L}{4 \times 0.0082}\right)^2 $$ **Step 8 — Identify the 4 Hz peak.** The factor $f_{mod}^2$ in the numerator increases $F$ with $f_{mod}$, but the Zwicker master equation's denominator (which was simplified away in the small-signal limit) contains a $(4)^2$ term that **dampens** $F$ above 4 Hz. The net combined transfer function has a maximum at exactly $f_{mod} = 4$ Hz, which is the experimentally observed peak of human fluctuation sensitivity. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A 1 kHz tone at 60 dB SPL is amplitude-modulated with depth $\Delta L = 3$ dB at $f_{mod} = 4$ Hz. Compute the approximate Fluctuation Strength. **Given:** - $f_{mod} = 4$ Hz - $\Delta L = 3$ dB - $f_c = 1$ kHz (reference tone, $\alpha \approx 0.04$ acum/Bark per dB for moderate levels) - $N'_0 = 0.0082$ acum/Bark **Step A — Compute $\Delta N'$.** $$ \Delta N' = \alpha\,\Delta L = 0.04 \times 3 = 0.12\ \text{acum/Bark} $$ **Step B — Compute the normalized modulation $\beta$.** $$ \beta = \dfrac{f_{mod}\,\Delta N'}{4\,N'_0} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.12}{4 \times 0.0082} = \dfrac{0.48}{0.0328} \approx 14.63 $$ **Step C — Apply the exact Zwicker equation for the single band.** $$ F = \dfrac{\beta^2}{1 + \beta^2} = \dfrac{(14.63)^2}{1 + (14.63)^2} = \dfrac{214.0}{215.0} \approx 0.995\ \text{vacil} $$ **Step D — Interpretation.** A 3 dB depth at the critical 4 Hz modulation on a 1 kHz tone gives almost the maximum possible fluctuation strength (1 vacil), confirming strong perceived pulsation. > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** If the modulation were at $f_{mod} = 20$ Hz (well above the critical 4 Hz), the ear begins to integrate the fluctuation and the metric falls. The same calculation at 20 Hz would yield a *lower* $F$ once the full Zwicker filter (Formula 1) is applied — illustrating the 4 Hz peak. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fluctuation Strength vs. Roughness | Property | Fluctuation Strength (F) | Roughness (R) | |---|---|---| | Modulation Frequency Band | **0.5 – 20 Hz** | **15 – 300 Hz** | | Critical Frequency | **4 Hz** | **70 Hz** | | Perceptual Character | Slow loudness pulsation | Fast buzz / modulation | | Unit | **vacil** | **asper** | | Used for | HVAC, motor whine, wipers | Engine, gear whine, electric motor harmonics | | Formula Sensitivity | $(dN'/dt)^2$ weighting | $\dfrac{dN'}{dt}$ weighting | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fluctuation Strength Calculation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Sound Signal p(t)] --> B[Fourier / Critical-Band Filterbank
24 Bark bands, z = 0 to 24] B --> C[Stationary Loudness Model
Zwicker ISO 532-1
N(z) for each band] C --> D[Time-Windowed Specific Loudness
N prime of z and t
computed every 2 ms] D --> E[Numerical Derivative
dN prime by dt
finite difference] E --> F[Zwicker Weighting Function
bandpass peaking at 4 Hz] F --> G[Integrate over Time T
and Bark z = 0 to 24] G --> H[Output: Fluctuation Strength F
Unit: vacil] H --> I{Engineering Decision} I -- F less than 0.1 vacil --> J[Acceptable
low pulsation perception] I -- F between 0.1 and 0.4 vacil --> K[Borderline
requires subjective verification] I -- F greater than 0.4 vacil --> L[Unacceptable
redesign source / add absorber] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The 4 Hz Sensitivity Peak ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LowFreq [Modulation less than 4 Hz] A1[1 Hz mod] --> A2[Slow gentle pulse
low F value] end subgraph PeakFreq [Modulation around 4 Hz] B1[4 Hz mod] --> B2[Maximal F value
pulsation most annoying] end subgraph HighFreq [Modulation greater than 4 Hz up to 20 Hz] C1[15 to 20 Hz mod] --> C2[Ear starts integrating
F falls, R rises] end LowFreq --> PeakFreq PeakFreq --> HighFreq B2 -. transition zone.-> C2 ``` ### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Zwicker's Two-Stage Model ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Stage A
Stationary Loudness
Produces N prime of z] --> S2[Stage B
Temporal Derivative
Computes dN prime by dt] S2 --> S3[Stage C
4 Hz Band-Pass Filter
Zwicker weighting] S3 --> S4[Stage D
Squaring and Smoothing
fluctuation energy] S4 --> S5[Stage E
Double Integration
over t and over z] S5 --> OUT[Final F in vacil] style S3 fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#ff8c00 style OUT fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Diagram:** The 4 Hz band-pass filter (Stage C, highlighted) is the **mathematical fingerprint** of the 4 Hz sensitivity peak. Any equivalent diagram in textbooks and software (ArtemiS SUITE, HEAD Artemis, Brüel & Kjær Sound Quality) follows the same pipeline. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]` **Define Fluctuation Strength. State its unit and the critical modulation frequency at which the human ear is most sensitive to it.** **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Fluctuation Strength (F) is a psychoacoustic descriptor that quantifies the subjective impression of *slow* amplitude fluctuations in a sound, typically in the modulation frequency range of **0.5 Hz to 20 Hz**. It measures how strongly a listener perceives the rise and fall of loudness over time. - **Unit:** **vacil** - **Critical modulation frequency:** **4 Hz** (at this frequency, the human auditory system is maximally sensitive to loudness fluctuations). > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Insight:** A 3-mark answer requires the *definition*, the *unit*, AND the *critical frequency*. Omitting any of the three is a guaranteed 1-mark loss. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]` **Differentiate between Fluctuation Strength and Roughness in terms of modulation frequency range, critical frequency, and unit.** **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Fluctuation Strength | Roughness | |---|---|---| | Modulation Frequency Range | **0.5 – 20 Hz** (slow) | **15 – 300 Hz** (fast) | | Critical Frequency | **4 Hz** | **70 Hz** | | Unit | **vacil** | **asper** | Fluctuation Strength applies to slow loudness pulsations, whereas Roughness applies to the buzz-like character of fast modulations. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each 14-mark question carries two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Two full alternative question sets (Q-A and Q-B) are provided below for full internal-choice coverage. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]` **(a)** Explain the **Zwicker model** for the calculation of Fluctuation Strength. Derive the simplified engineering expression for the case of a single tonal component with sinusoidal amplitude modulation. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer (Step-by-step):** 1. **State the master Zwicker equation:** $$ F = \int_{0}^{24}\int_{0}^{T}\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{dN'(z,t)}{dt}\right)^{2}}{\left(\dfrac{dN'(z,t)}{dt}\right)^{2} + (2\pi \cdot 4 \cdot N'_0)^{2}}\,\dfrac{dt}{N'_0}\,dz $$ 2. **Explain each variable:** - $N'(z,t)$ = specific loudness at critical-band rate $z$ and time $t$. - $N'_0 = 0.0082$ acum/Bark = reference normalization. - The factor $(2\pi \cdot 4 \cdot N'_0)^2$ in the denominator sets the **4 Hz critical-frequency** peak. 3. **State the modulated signal assumption:** $$ p(t) = p_0\left[1 + m\sin(2\pi f_{mod} t)\right]\cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ 4. **Compute $\Delta L$ in dB** (Equation 7 from Section 2.2). 5. **Linearize $N'$ vs $L$** for moderate levels: $\Delta N' = \alpha\,\Delta L$. 6. **Differentiate to get $dN'/dt$** and substitute into the master equation. 7. **Simplify for single-band case** to obtain: $$ F = \dfrac{\beta^2}{1+\beta^2},\quad \beta = \dfrac{f_{mod}\,\Delta N'}{4\,N'_0} $$ 8. **Small-signal limit yields:** $$ F \approx \left(\dfrac{f_{mod}\,\alpha\,\Delta L}{4\,N'_0}\right)^2 $$ 9. **Conclude** that $F$ is maximized when $f_{mod} \to 4$ Hz (the critical frequency). --- **(b)** A 2 kHz tone at 70 dB SPL is amplitude-modulated sinusoidally with a modulation depth of 4 dB at a modulation frequency of 4 Hz. Using the Zwicker model with $N'_0 = 0.0082$ acum/Bark and $\alpha = 0.04$ acum/Bark per dB, calculate the Fluctuation Strength in vacil. Comment on the perceptual result. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Given Data:** - $f_{mod} = 4$ Hz - $\Delta L = 4$ dB - $\alpha = 0.04$ acum/Bark per dB - $N'_0 = 0.0082$ acum/Bark **Step 2 — Compute $\Delta N'$:** $$ \Delta N' = \alpha\,\Delta L = 0.04 \times 4 = 0.16\ \text{acum/Bark} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the normalized modulation $\beta$:** $$ \beta = \dfrac{f_{mod}\,\Delta N'}{4\,N'_0} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.16}{4 \times 0.0082} = \dfrac{0.64}{0.0328} \approx 19.51 $$ **Step 4 — Apply the Zwicker single-band equation:** $$ F = \dfrac{\beta^2}{1 + \beta^2} = \dfrac{(19.51)^2}{1 + (19.51)^2} = \dfrac{380.6}{381.6} \approx 0.997\ \text{vacil} $$ **Step 5 — Perceptual Comment:** A 4 dB modulation at the critical 4 Hz frequency produces a Fluctuation Strength approaching the upper limit of perception (~1 vacil). The listener will perceive a *strongly pulsating* sound, which is generally judged as **annoying** in passenger-cabin NVH applications. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to *square* $\beta$ in the Zwicker equation, or confuse $N'_0$ with the total loudness $N$. Both errors cost 2–3 marks. Always re-check: the answer should be a dimensionless value between 0 and 1 vacil. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]` **(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the **psychoacoustic pipeline** for computing Fluctuation Strength from a raw sound pressure signal. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The calculation pipeline consists of **five sequential stages**: 1. **Stage 1 — Critical-Band Filterbank:** The input signal $p(t)$ is decomposed into 24 critical bands (Bark scale, $z = 0 \dots 24$) using band-pass filters whose bandwidths approximate the ear's frequency resolution. 2. **Stage 2 — Stationary Loudness Calculation:** For each band and each short time window (typically 2 ms), the Zwicker loudness model is applied to compute the specific loudness $N'(z, t)$. 3. **Stage 3 — Temporal Differentiation:** The time derivative $\dfrac{dN'(z, t)}{dt}$ is computed via finite differences. 4. **Stage 4 — 4 Hz Band-Pass Weighting:** A filter that peaks at 4 Hz weights the derivative, mathematically encoding the 4 Hz critical-frequency peak of human hearing. 5. **Stage 5 — Squaring, Smoothing, and Double Integration:** The weighted derivative is squared, time-smoothed, and integrated over both time and Bark bands to give the final Fluctuation Strength $F$ in vacil. > (Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for a graphical rendering.) --- **(b)** An EV cabin has a power-steering whine at 800 Hz with an envelope modulation of 2 dB at 6 Hz. The whine has a per-band specific-loudness slope factor $\alpha = 0.03$ acum/Bark per dB.
