Using $N'_0 = 0.0082$ acum/Bark, calculate the Fluctuation Strength and recommend an engineering fix if the value exceeds 0.1 vacil. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Given Data:** - $\Delta L = 2$ dB - $f_{mod} = 6$ Hz - $\alpha = 0.03$ acum/Bark per dB - $N'_0 = 0.0082$ acum/Bark **Step 2 — Compute $\Delta N'$:** $$ \Delta N' = \alpha\,\Delta L = 0.03 \times 2 = 0.06\ \text{acum/Bark} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the normalized modulation $\beta$:** $$ \beta = \dfrac{f_{mod}\,\Delta N'}{4\,N'_0} = \dfrac{6 \times 0.06}{4 \times 0.0082} = \dfrac{0.36}{0.0328} \approx 10.98 $$ **Step 4 — Apply the Zwicker single-band equation:** $$ F = \dfrac{\beta^2}{1 + \beta^2} = \dfrac{(10.98)^2}{1 + (10.98)^2} = \dfrac{120.6}{121.6} \approx 0.992\ \text{vacil} $$ **Step 5 — Engineering Recommendation:** The computed $F \approx 0.99$ vacil vastly exceeds the 0.1 vacil premium-cabin threshold. Recommended actions: 1. **Tune the power-steering pump switching frequency** to shift the whine away from 4–8 Hz envelope modulation. 2. **Add an absorptive liner** in the steering column tunnel to damp the 800 Hz component. 3. **Use a Helmholtz resonator or active noise control** to cancel the envelope modulation specifically. 4. **Verify the fix** by re-measuring $F$ after each modification until $F < 0.1$ vacil is achieved. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Error:** Using 800 Hz as $f_{mod}$ instead of the envelope modulation (6 Hz). The fluctuation calculation **always uses the modulation frequency**, *not* the carrier frequency. This is the single most common error in KTU exam answers on this topic. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 3 Mark-Loss Traps in Fluctuation Strength Questions:** > 1. **Using carrier frequency instead of modulation frequency** — always read the "envelope" or "modulation" frequency from the question, not the tonal frequency. > 2. **Forgetting the unit "vacil"** — KTU answer sheets are strict; writing "vacils" or "vacil" without context loses 0.5 mark. > 3. **Confusing Fluctuation Strength (vacil) with Roughness (asper)** — they differ in band, peak frequency, and unit. Examiners specifically test this distinction. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ Fluctuation Strength quantifies the **subjective perception of slow loudness pulsations** in a sound. - ✅ Unit: **vacil**; range typically 0 to ~1 vacil. - ✅ Applicable modulation frequency band: **0.5 Hz – 20 Hz**. - ✅ **Critical (peak-sensitivity) frequency: 4 Hz.** - ✅ The Zwicker master equation depends on the **square of the time-derivative of specific loudness**, $N'(z, t)$. - ✅ For a single-tone modulated signal: $F = \dfrac{\beta^2}{1+\beta^2}$ where $\beta = \dfrac{f_{mod}\,\Delta N'}{4\,N'_0}$ and $N'_0 = 0.0082$ acum/Bark. - ✅ **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** $F < 0.1$ vacil is the *premium-cabin acceptance criterion*; $F > 0.4$ vacil is *unacceptable*. - ✅ Distinguish from **Roughness (R, asper)** which is for *fast* modulations (15–300 Hz) and peaks at 70 Hz. - ✅ Pipeline: $p(t) \to$ critical-band filterbank $\to N'(z,t) \to dN'/dt \to$ 4 Hz weighting $\to$ integration $\to F$. - ✅ Common applications: EV whine, HVAC blowers, wiper noise, power-steering whine, tire cavity resonance. - ✅ Always **use modulation frequency in calculations, never the carrier (tonal) frequency.**

topics.title: Psychoacoustic Indexes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Psychoacoustic Indexes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Psychoacoustic Indexes?

**Psychoacoustic Indexes** (also called *psychoacoustic metrics* or *perceptual noise metrics*) are quantitative descriptors that correlate a physical sound stimulus with its **subjective human perception**. Unlike physical sound pressure level (in dB), which only measures the magnitude of acoustic pressure, psychoacoustic indexes mathematically model how the human auditory system (outer ear → cochlea → auditory nerve → brain) actually *interprets* a sound.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Psychoacoustic indexes are mathematical transformations of sound pressure spectra into single-number or multi-dimensional quantities that predict the **perceived magnitude of sensation** evoked in a human listener, expressed in standardized perceptual units (sone, acum, asper, vacil, etc.) as defined in **ISO 532, DIN 45692, and ECMA-74**.

## 1.2 Why Do We Need Them?

A pure **100 dB SPL** signal at 100 Hz and a 100 dB SPL signal at 4 kHz have *identical* physical magnitudes. However, the 4 kHz tone is perceived as nearly **eight times louder** by the human ear. This discrepancy exists because:

- The human ear is **non-linear** in frequency response.
- The ear performs **frequency analysis** via the basilar membrane (critical bands).
- Temporal effects (modulation) alter perception.
- Masking occurs between adjacent frequencies.

> [!NOTE]
> **The dB Scale Alone is Insufficient for NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) Evaluation.** Modern automotive and consumer product industries require *perceptual* evaluation because regulatory dB limits no longer differentiate between "acceptable" and "annoying" sounds of equal SPL.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Spice Rating"

Imagine rating a dish:
- **Physical measurement (dB):** Measures the raw amount of chili (in grams) in the food.
- **Psychoacoustic index (Loudness/Sharpness):** Measures how *spicy* it actually *tastes* to you.

A dish with 5 g of ghost pepper (high "physical" spiciness) might feel less intense than 2 g of habanero (lower "physical" spiciness) because of *where* the heat hits your tongue (frequency critical band) and *how it evolves* over time (temporal modulation). Psychoacoustic indexes do exactly this conversion — turning raw acoustic "grams" into human "taste."

## 1.4 The Five Primary Psychoacoustic Indexes

| # | Index | Unit | Symbol | Captures |
|---|-------|------|--------|----------|
| 1 | **Loudness** | sone | $N$ or $N'$ | Perceived intensity |
| 2 | **Sharpness** | acum | $S$ | Perceived "high-frequency emphasis" |
| 3 | **Roughness** | asper | $R$ | Fast modulation (15–300 Hz) annoyance |
| 4 | **Fluctuation Strength** | vacil | $F$ | Slow modulation (≤ 20 Hz) annoyance |
| 5 | **Tonality** | tu or dB | $K$, $T$ | Tonal/pure-tone content |

> [!NOTE]
> Secondary indexes such as **Impulsiveness**, **Articulation Index (AI)**, and **Speech Transmission Index (STI)** are also used in ISO 1996 and IEC 60268-16 contexts.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Loudness ($N$)

**Loudness** is the perceptual correlate of sound intensity. It follows **Stevens' Power Law**.

### 2.1.1 Stevens' Mark VI Method (for pure tones, free field)
Loudness in **sone** is computed from SPL via:

$$N = 2^{\left(\frac{L_N - 40}{10}\right)}$$

where $L_N$ is the **Loudness Level** in **phon** (Loudness Level = SPL of an equally-loud 1 kHz tone). The reference point is **1 sone = 40 phon = 40 dB SPL at 1 kHz**.

### 2.1.2 Zwicker's Method (ISO 532 Part 1 / 2, critical band basis)
The Zwicker method divides the frequency axis into **24 critical bands** (Bark scale, $z$). Loudness is calculated as:

$$N' = 0.08 \left(\frac{E_T}{E_0}\right)^{0.23} \cdot \left[(0.5 + 0.5 \cdot \frac{E}{E_T})^{0.23} - 1 \right]$$

Wait — the *core* per-band equation Zwicker uses is the specific loudness density (Loudness per Bark):

$$N'(z) = N_0 \cdot \left(\frac{E_T(z)}{E_0}\right)^{\alpha} \left[\left(1 - s(z) + s(z) \cdot \frac{E(z)}{E_T(z)}\right)^{\alpha} - 1\right]$$

where:
- $N_0 = $ normalization constant (Zwicker: ~0.0631, Moore-Glasberg: 0.0048 for diffuse field)
- $E(z)$ = excitation at critical band rate $z$
- $E_T(z)$ = excitation at threshold in quiet at band $z$
- $s(z)$ = slope of the masking function
- $\alpha \approx 0.23$

Total loudness is the **integral over all critical bands**:

$$N = \int_{0}^{24\text{ Bark}} N'(z)\, dz \quad \text{[sone]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** Doubling the **sone** value corresponds to roughly a **10 dB increase** in SPL perception (per Stevens' law). A sound at **4 sone** is twice as loud as one at **2 sone**.

---

## 2.2 Sharpness ($S$)

**Sharpness** quantifies the "high-frequency, cutting, piercing" character of a sound. A value of **$S = 1$ acum** is defined for a narrowband noise of 1 kHz at 60 dB (bandwidth = 1 critical band).

**Von Bismarck's formula (the most common):**

$$S = c \cdot \frac{\int_{0}^{24} N'(z) \cdot g(z) \cdot z\, dz}{\int_{0}^{24} N'(z)\, dz} \quad \text{[acum]}$$

where:
- $g(z) = 1$ for $z \le 15.8$ Bark; $g(z) = 0.066 \cdot e^{0.171 \cdot z}$ for $z > 15.8$ Bark
- $c$ = normalization constant (typically **0.11** so that 1 kHz, 60 dB narrowband gives $S = 1$ acum)
- $N'(z)$ = specific loudness (Zwicker or Moore-Glasberg)

**Aures' weighted version** uses $g(z) = 0.078 \cdot \frac{e^{0.173 \cdot z}}{0.5 + 0.25 \cdot e^{0.173 \cdot z}}$ for greater low-frequency sensitivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "**S**harpness **S**preads energy toward high Bark." High $z$ values in the numerator push $S$ up.

---

## 2.3 Roughness ($R$)

**Roughness** quantifies the rapid amplitude modulation sensation (15–300 Hz) — the "buzzy" or "scratchy" character. A **$R = 1$ asper** is defined for a 1 kHz tone at 60 dB SPL, 100 % modulated at 70 Hz.

**Daniel & Weber (1997) model:**

$$R = 0.3 \cdot \frac{f_{\text{mod}}}{1000\, \text{Hz}} \cdot \int_{0}^{24\text{ Bark}} N'(z) \cdot \Delta L_E(z)\, dz \quad \text{[asper]}$$

where:
- $f_{\text{mod}}$ = modulation frequency (Hz), valid 15–300 Hz
- $\Delta L_E(z)$ = modulation depth (in dB) of the excitation at band $z$

A widely-used alternative (Aures, Terhardt):

$$R = k \cdot \int_{0}^{24} N'(z) \cdot \Delta L(z) \cdot e^{-b \cdot f_{\text{mod}}} \cdot (1 - e^{-b \cdot f_{\text{mod}}})\, dz$$

---

## 2.4 Fluctuation Strength ($F$)

**Fluctuation Strength** captures slow modulation (≤ 20 Hz) — the "whooshing" or "pulsing" character. Reference: **$F = 1$ vacil** for a 1 kHz tone at 60 dB with 100 % amplitude modulation at 4 Hz.

**Zwicker's equation:**

$$F = \frac{0.008 \cdot \int_{0}^{24} \Delta L_E(z)\, dz}{\frac{f_{\text{mod}}}{4\, \text{Hz}} + \frac{4\, \text{Hz}}{f_{\text{mod}}}}$$

where $\Delta L_E(z)$ is the modulation depth of the excitation at band $z$ in **dB**, and $f_{\text{mod}} \le 20$ Hz.

---

## 2.5 Tonality ($K$, $T$)

**Tonality** quantifies how much a sound contains identifiable pure-tone (tonal) components versus being noise-like.

**DIN 45681 / ECMA-74 Tonality (Tonhaltigkeitsmaß $T$):**

$$T = \left\vert \text{SPL}_{\text{total}} - \text{SPL}_{\text{noise floor after notching out tone}} \right\vert \quad \text{[tu]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> 1 **tu** (tonality unit) ≈ 1 dB of tonal-to-noise prominence. ECMA-74 uses the **Prominence Ratio** approach: $T \ge 0$ tu indicates a clearly tonal sound.

**Aures' Tonality** (extension of Zwicker):

$$K = \frac{c_2}{\sum N_i} \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i \cdot N_i \cdot \Delta z_i$$

where $s_i$ is the slope of the excitation pattern at the tone, $\Delta z_i$ is the bandwidth, and $c_2$ is a constant.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Index | Formula | Unit | Reference | Key Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Loudness (Stevens) | $N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$ | sone | 1 kHz, 40 dB SPL | 0 – ∞ |
| Loudness (Zwicker) | $N = \int_0^{24} N'(z)\, dz$ | sone | 24 critical bands | 0 – ∞ |
| Loudness Level | $L_N = 40 + 10\log_2(N)$ | phon | Equal-loudness to 1 kHz | 0 – 130 |
| Sharpness (Bismarck) | $S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{\int N'(z)\,g(z)\,z\,dz}{\int N'(z)\,dz}$ | acum | 1 kHz, 60 dB NB → 1 acum | 0.1 – 5+ |
| Roughness (Daniel-Weber) | $R = 0.3 \cdot \frac{f_{mod}}{1000}\int N'(z)\Delta L_E dz$ | asper | 1 kHz, 60 dB, 70 Hz, 100% AM → 1 asper | 0 – 1+ |
| Fluctuation Strength | $F = 0.008 \int \Delta L_E dz \div [(f_{mod}/4)+(4/f_{mod})]$ | vacil | 1 kHz, 60 dB, 4 Hz, 100% AM → 1 vacil | 0 – 1+ |
| Tonality (DIN) | $T = \vert L_{total} - L_{notched} \vert$ | tu | 1 tu ≈ 1 dB prominence | 0 – 5+ |
| Critical Band Rate | $z = 13 \arctan(0.76 f/1000) + 3.5 \arctan((f/7500)^2)$ | Bark | Zwicker | 0 – 24 |
| Bark from Hz | $z \approx 7 \cdot \text{asinh}(f/650)$ | Bark | Traunmüller | 0 – 24 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LATEX RULE:** When expressing absolute value or modulus in this table, use `\vert ... \vert` or `\mid ... \mid`. **Never use a bare pipe `\|`** in tables — it breaks KTU's markdown compiler.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive NVH:** Manufacturers target $N \le 40$ sone cabin loudness, $S \le 2.5$ acum (to avoid "tinny" perception), and $R \le 0.5$ asper.
- **HVAC / Consumer Appliances:** Sharpness reduction = quieter-sounding fan even at same SPL.
- **Psychoacoustic Annoyance (PA):** Combined metric $PA = N \cdot (1 + \sqrt{g} + \sqrt{s})$ (where $g$ = roughness factor, $s$ = sharpness factor) used in **ISO/PAS 20065**.
- **Hearing Aid Design:** Optimizes signal clarity using Articulation Index weighting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stevens' Power Law for Loudness

### Starting Point
Experimental observation: A sound judged *twice as loud* requires approximately a **10 dB increase** in SPL. This empirical "rule" must be elevated to a continuous mathematical law.

### Step 1: Define the reference point
By definition, **1 sone** corresponds to a 1 kHz tone at **40 dB SPL**, and this also equals **40 phon** (Loudness Level).

### Step 2: Encode the 10-dB-per-doubling rule
A doubling of sone for every 10 phon increment is written as:

$$N = 2^{k \cdot (L_N - 40)}$$

where $k$ is a constant to be determined.

### Step 3: Apply the reference boundary condition
Substitute $N = 1$ sone, $L_N = 40$ phon:

$$1 = 2^{k \cdot (40 - 40)} = 2^{0} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

Any $k$ satisfies this, so we need a *second* condition.

### Step 4: Apply the doubling rule
When $L_N$ increases by 10, $N$ must double:

$$2 = 2^{k \cdot 10} \implies k \cdot 10 = 1 \implies k = 0.1$$

### Step 5: Final Stevens' Mark VI formula

$$\boxed{N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}}$$

### Step 6: Inverse transformation (Loudness Level from Sone)
Solving for $L_N$:

$$L_N = 40 + 10 \log_2(N) = 40 + \frac{10}{\ln 2} \ln(N)$$

### Worked Numerical Example
A motor produces a cabin tone of 60 phon. Find its loudness in sone.

$$N = 2^{(60 - 40)/10} = 2^{2} = 4 \text{ sone}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **50 phon** tone gives $N = 2^{1} = 2$ sone. From 2 sone → 4 sone, loudness doubled with only a 10 dB increase — exactly Stevens' law in action.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sharpness via Von Bismarck's Method

### Step 1: Motivation
Loudness alone cannot distinguish a 1 kHz tone (mellow) from a 6 kHz tone of the *same* loudness (piercing). We need a "center of gravity" along the Bark axis, weighted toward high frequencies.

### Step 2: Define specific loudness
Use Zwicker's $N'(z)$ (sone/Bark) from the previous calculation step.

### Step 3: Define the high-frequency weighting
For $z \le 15.8$ Bark, $g(z) = 1$ (no extra weight). For $z > 15.8$ Bark:

$$g(z) = 0.066 \cdot e^{0.171 z}$$

This grows rapidly past the **15.8 Bark** mark (~3.7 kHz), matching the ear's increasing sensitivity to high frequencies.

### Step 4: Compute the weighted first moment

$$S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{24} N'(z) \cdot g(z) \cdot z \, dz}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{24} N'(z) \, dz}$$

### Step 5: Normalization constant $c = 0.11$
This constant is *not arbitrary*; it is chosen so that a 1 kHz narrowband noise (1 critical band wide) at 60 dB SPL produces $S = 1$ acum. The Zwicker loudness of such a stimulus is $N \approx 4.21$ sone. The integral ratio for that stimulus is $1/0.11 \approx 9.09$, so $0.11 \cdot 9.09 = 1.0$ acum.

### Step 6: Numerical Worked Example
Suppose a 1/3-octave band of noise centered at **6 kHz**, 70 dB SPL.
- $z$ at 6 kHz $\approx 14.6$ Bark
- Since $z < 15.8$, $g(z) = 1$ (no extra weighting *yet*).

But the next 1/3-octave at 8 kHz has $z \approx 16.4$ Bark, where $g = 0.066 \cdot e^{0.171 \cdot 16.4} \approx 0.066 \cdot e^{2.804} \approx 0.066 \cdot 16.50 \approx 1.089$.

Thus the high-frequency tail contributes disproportionately — this is precisely why the **engineer's "6 kHz problem"** (gear whine, injector click) feels piercing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Daniel-Weber Roughness Model

### Step 1: Empirical observation
Pure tones modulated at frequencies between 15 and 300 Hz produce the *buzz* sensation. Below 15 Hz: fluctuation; above 300 Hz: roughness drops because the ear cannot temporally resolve.

### Step 2: Modulation depth definition
For an AM signal $p(t) = p_0 (1 + m \cos 2\pi f_{mod} t)$:

$$\Delta L_E(z) = 20 \log_{10}(1 + m) \quad \text{(in dB, per critical band)}$$

### Step 3: Roughness density
For each Bark band:

$$R'(z) \propto N'(z) \cdot \Delta L_E(z) \cdot f_{mod}$$

### Step 4: Total roughness

$$R = c_{DW} \int_0^{24} N'(z) \cdot \Delta L_E(z) \cdot \frac{f_{mod}}{1000}\, dz \quad \text{[asper]}$$

with $c_{DW} = 0.3$.

### Step 5: Band-pass shape
A more accurate model (Aures) uses a band-pass weighting in modulation frequency:

$$R = \int N'(z) \cdot \Delta L(z) \cdot \underbrace{\left( \frac{f_{mod}}{f_1} \right) e^{-f_{mod}/f_2}}_{\text{modulation BP}}\, dz$$

with $f_1 = 70$ Hz, $f_2 = 130$ Hz — maximizing around 70 Hz.

### Step 6: Numerical Example
A 1 kHz tone at 60 dB SPL, 100 % AM ($m = 1$, $\Delta L_E = 6.02$ dB), $f_{mod} = 70$ Hz.

$$R = 0.3 \cdot \frac{70}{1000} \cdot \int_0^{24} N'(z) \cdot 6.02\, dz = 0.021 \cdot 6.02 \cdot N = 0.126 \cdot N$$

For a 60 dB, 1 kHz pure tone, $N \approx 4$ sone. Therefore $R \approx 0.504$ asper — close to the design anchor of 1 asper when full definition is used.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Fluctuation Strength (Zwicker)

### Step 1: Slow-modulation assumption
$f_{mod} \le 20$ Hz. The ear perceives the modulation as a *rhythmic pulsing*.

### Step 2: Reference point
$F = 1$ vacil for 1 kHz, 60 dB, 100 % AM at **4 Hz**.

### Step 3: Frequency dependence
Maximum sensitivity at **4 Hz**; falls off symmetrically:

$$w(f_{mod}) = \frac{1}{(f_{mod}/4) + (4/f_{mod})}$$

At $f_{mod} = 4$ Hz: $w = 1/(1 + 1) = 0.5$. At 1 Hz or 16 Hz: $w \approx 0.25$ (half sensitivity).

### Step 4: Total fluctuation strength

$$F = 0.008 \int_0^{24} \Delta L_E(z)\, dz \cdot w(f_{mod}) \quad \text{[vacil]}$$

The constant 0.008 is calibrated for the reference tone.

### Step 5: Example
A 1 kHz tone, 60 dB SPL, 100 % AM at 4 Hz has $\Delta L_E \approx 6$ dB and total excitation across 24 Bark = some value giving $F = 1$ vacil by definition. If we shift to 8 Hz modulation, the same signal gives $F = 0.5$ vacil — half the fluctuation sensation.

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Index Behavior

| Stimulus | Loudness $N$ | Sharpness $S$ | Roughness $R$ | Fluctuation $F$ | Tonality $T$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 kHz, 40 dB tone | 1 sone | 1.0 acum | 0 asper | 0 vacil | high |
| 1 kHz, 60 dB tone | 4 sone | 1.0 acum | 0 asper | 0 vacil | high |
| 6 kHz, 60 dB tone | 4.3 sone | 2.5 acum | 0 asper | 0 vacil | high |
| White noise, 60 dB | 8 sone | 1.7 acum | 0 asper | 0 vacil | low |
| 1 kHz, 60 dB + AM 70 Hz, 100% | 4 sone | 1.0 acum | 1 asper | 0 vacil | mid |
| 1 kHz, 60 dB + AM 4 Hz, 100% | 4 sone | 1.0 acum | 0 asper | 1 vacil | mid |
| Tire noise, 70 dB broadband | ~16 sone | 2.2 acum | 0.3 asper | 0.1 vacil | low |
| Electric motor whine, 80 dB | ~30 sone | 4.0 acum | 0.1 asper | 0 vacil | very high |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Loudness is essentially *energy-weighted*, sharpness is *centroid* along Bark, roughness is *modulation × loudness product*, and fluctuation is *slow modulation alone*. Tonality is *orthogonal* to all four.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Functional Block Diagram — Psychoacoustic Index Computation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Time-Domain Sound Signal x t] --> B[FFT / Spectral Analysis]
    B --> C[One-Third Octave Band SPL L z]
    C --> D[Excitation Pattern E z via Critical Band Filters]
    D --> E[Specific Loudness N prime z]
    
    E --> F1[Loudness N = Integral of N prime over 0 to 24 Bark]
    E --> F2[Sharpness S = Weighted First Moment of N prime]
    E --> F3[Roughness R = N prime x Modulation Depth Integral]
    E --> F4[Fluctuation F = Slow Modulation Integral]
    E --> F5[Tonality K = Tone vs Noise Slope Analysis]
    
    F1 --> G[Perceptual Output Vector]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    
    G --> H[Engineering Decision: Accept Reject Redesign]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Index

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Sound Complaint Received] --> Q1{Is the issue intensity?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Loud[Use Loudness N in sone]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is it a piercing hiss?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Sharp[Use Sharpness S in acum]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is it a buzz or rattle?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Rough[Use Roughness R in asper]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is it a whoosh or pulse?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Fluc[Use Fluctuation F in vacil]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Is it a whine or hum?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Ton[Use Tonality K or T]
    Q5 -->|No| Complex[Use Combined PA or Zwicker Annoyance]
```

## 4.3 Critical Band / Bark Scale Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[0 Bark - 0 Hz] --> B[5 Bark - 500 Hz]
    B --> C[10 Bark - 1.1 kHz]
    C --> D[15.8 Bark - 3.7 kHz]
    D --> E[20 Bark - 6.5 kHz]
    E --> F[24 Bark - 12 kHz]
    
    A -.->|Low rumble| G[Low frequency zone]
    F -.->|High hiss| H[High frequency zone]
    
    D --> I[Sharpness Weighting Onset]
    I --> J[High-frequency boost g z activates]
```

## 4.4 Modulation Frequency vs. Sensation Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[0.5 to 20 Hz] -->|Slow pulsation| F[Fluctuation Strength]
    B[15 to 300 Hz] -->|Buzz and roughness| R[Roughness]
    C[300+ Hz] -->|Pure tone| T[Tonality]
    
    A --> FDetail[Max sensitivity at 4 Hz]
    B --> RDetail[Max sensitivity at 70 Hz]
    C --> TDetail[Tonal content emerges]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — ISO 532 / ECMA-74 Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Monaural Audio Input] --> B[Anti-aliasing Filter]
    B --> C[Time-windowed FFT - 50 percent overlap]
    C --> D[1/3-Octave Spectrum]
    D --> E[Critical Band Aggregation into 24 Bark]
    E --> F[Zwicker Excitation Pattern - Outer and Inner Ear Transfer]
    F --> G[Specific Loudness N prime z]
    G --> H1[Loudness N]
    G --> H2[Sharpness S]
    G --> H3[Roughness R]
    G --> H4[Fluctuation F]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Loudness and Sharpness. State the units used to express them and write the reference values used for calibration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Loudness** is the perceptual correlate of sound intensity, expressed in **sones**. It is computed from the specific loudness $N'(z)$ integrated over the 24 critical bands of the Bark scale. **Reference:** 1 sone corresponds to a 1 kHz pure tone at 40 dB SPL (or 40 phon).
- **Sharpness** is a psychoacoustic descriptor of the "high-frequency emphasis" or "piercing" character of a sound, expressed in **acum**. It is the weighted first moment of specific loudness along the Bark scale. **Reference:** 1 acum corresponds to a narrowband (1 critical band wide) noise centered at 1 kHz at 60 dB SPL.

(Each definition: 1 mark; units: 0.5 mark; reference: 0.5 mark per index.)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Roughness and Fluctuation Strength. Mention the modulation frequency ranges for which each is significant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Roughness ($R$) | Fluctuation Strength ($F$) |
|---|---|---|
| Unit | asper | vacil |
| Modulation range | 15 – 300 Hz | 0.5 – 20 Hz |
| Reference | 1 asper for 1 kHz, 60 dB, 100% AM at **70 Hz** | 1 vacil for 1 kHz, 60 dB, 100% AM at **4 Hz** |
| Sensation | Buzz, rattle, scratchiness | Whoosh, rhythmic pulse |

(Differences: 2 marks; range: 1 mark.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the procedure for computing **Loudness** using **Zwicker's method** as per **ISO 532**. Define all the variables involved. (7 marks)

**(b)** A sound source radiates a tone of 1 kHz at 70 dB SPL. Another source radiates a tone of 4 kHz at 60 dB SPL. Using **Stevens' Mark VI** loudness model, compute the **loudness in sone** and **loudness level in phon** for each. Compare them and comment. (7 marks)

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the flowchart from Section 4.5 of these notes, showing: Audio Input → FFT → 1/3-Octave SPL → Critical Band Aggregation (Bark) → Excitation Pattern via Outer/Middle ear transfer function → Specific Loudness $N'(z)$ → Integrate over Bark → Total Loudness $N$ in sone.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Acquire time-domain signal, window with Hann, 50 % overlap.
2. Compute FFT and convert to 1/3-octave band SPL.
3. Apply **outer ear transfer function (OETF)** and **middle ear transfer function (METF)** to obtain excitation $E(z)$ at each critical band.
4. Compute **specific loudness**:

$$N'(z) = N_0 \left(\frac{E_T(z)}{E_0}\right)^{\alpha} \left[\left(1 - s(z) + s(z)\cdot\frac{E(z)}{E_T(z)}\right)^{\alpha} - 1\right]$$

5. **Integrate** $N'(z)$ over 0 – 24 Bark to get total loudness $N$ in **sone** (free field) or $N_D$ in sone (diffuse field).

**Variables (2 marks):** $E(z)$ = excitation at band $z$; $E_T(z)$ = excitation at threshold in quiet; $s(z)$ = slope of the masking function; $\alpha = 0.23$ (Zwicker exponent); $N_0$ = reference specific loudness.

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Tone 1: 1 kHz, 70 dB SPL**

For a 1 kHz pure tone, the **phon** equals the SPL in dB. So $L_N = 70$ phon.

Loudness:

$$N_1 = 2^{(70 - 40)/10} = 2^{3} = 8 \text{ sone}$$

**Tone 2: 4 kHz, 60 dB SPL**

For 4 kHz, the **Equal-Loudness Contour** (ISO 226) shows that 4 kHz at 60 dB SPL corresponds to a loudness level of approximately **65 phon** (the ear is *more* sensitive at 4 kHz).

Using $L_N = 65$ phon:

$$N_2 = 2^{(65 - 40)/10} = 2^{2.5} = 5.66 \text{ sone}$$

**Comparison (2 marks):**
- Tone 1 (1 kHz, 70 dB): 8 sone, 70 phon
- Tone 2 (4 kHz, 60 dB): 5.66 sone, 65 phon

**Comment:** Even though Tone 2 has **10 dB less** SPL, it is only **~30 % less loud** (5.66 vs 8 sone). This is because the human ear is far more sensitive at 4 kHz (corresponding to the ear canal resonance at ~2.7 kHz). Hence **dB SPL alone is not a reliable indicator of loudness perception** — psychoacoustic metrics are required.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define **Roughness** and **Sharpness**. Derive the expression for **Von Bismarck's Sharpness** model and explain the role of the weighting function $g(z)$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A gear-mesh whine in a transmission produces a dominant tone at **3.5 kHz** with a sideband modulation of **120 Hz** at **100 % amplitude modulation** (i.e., $m = 1$). The measured loudness at the listener's ear is **8 sone**. Estimate the **roughness in asper** using the Daniel-Weber model. (7 marks)

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Definitions (1 mark each):**
- **Roughness:** Perceived rapid amplitude modulation in the range 15–300 Hz; expressed in **asper**. 1 asper = 1 kHz, 60 dB tone, 100% AM at 70 Hz.
- **Sharpness:** High-frequency "piercing" character of a sound; expressed in **acum**. 1 acum = 1 kHz narrowband noise, 60 dB SPL.

**Derivation of Von Bismarck Sharpness (4 marks):**

The starting point is the specific loudness $N'(z)$ from Zwicker's method. To capture *where* in the frequency domain the loudness is concentrated, we compute a **weighted first moment** along the Bark axis:

$$S = c \cdot \frac{\int_0^{24} N'(z) \cdot g(z) \cdot z \, dz}{\int_0^{24} N'(z) \, dz}$$

**Weighting function $g(z)$ (2 marks):**
- For $z \le 15.8$ Bark: $g(z) = 1$.
- For $z > 15.8$ Bark: $g(z) = 0.066 \cdot e^{0.171 z}$.

The function $g(z)$ **rises rapidly** above 15.8 Bark (~3.7 kHz) to mimic the ear's increasing sensitivity to high frequencies. It ensures that the *same* loudness concentrated at high Bark (e.g., 6 kHz) yields a higher sharpness than if it were concentrated at low Bark (e.g., 500 Hz). The constant $c = 0.11$ is a normalization so that 1 kHz, 60 dB NB gives exactly $S = 1$ acum.

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given:
- Carrier (sideband center) $f_c = 3.5$ kHz
- Modulation frequency $f_{mod} = 120$ Hz
- Modulation depth $m = 1$ (100 %)
- Total loudness $N = 8$ sone

**Step 1: Compute modulation depth in dB**

$$\Delta L_E = 20 \log_{10}(1 + m) = 20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.02 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2: Approximate excitation distribution**
The 3.5 kHz tone excites a small region around $z \approx 13.5$ Bark. For an approximate analysis, treat $\Delta L_E$ as concentrated near the carrier and equal to 6.02 dB.

**Step 3: Apply Daniel-Weber formula**

$$R = 0.3 \cdot \frac{f_{mod}}{1000} \cdot \int_0^{24} N'(z) \cdot \Delta L_E(z)\, dz$$

Since $\Delta L_E$ is approximately uniform and equal to 6.02 dB in the excited region, the integral becomes:

$$\int_0^{24} N'(z) \cdot \Delta L_E(z)\, dz \approx \Delta L_E \cdot \int_0^{24} N'(z)\, dz = 6.02 \cdot N$$

Substituting:

$$R = 0.3 \cdot \frac{120}{1000} \cdot 6.02 \cdot 8 = 0.3 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 6.02 \cdot 8$$

**Step 4: Numerical evaluation**

$$R = 0.3 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 6.02 \cdot 8$$
$$R = 0.3 \cdot 0.12 = 0.036$$
$$R = 0.036 \cdot 6.02 = 0.2167$$
$$R = 0.2167 \cdot 8 = 1.734 \text{ asper}$$

**Final Answer:** $R \approx 1.73$ asper — this is **highly annoying** and would warrant design intervention (e.g., helical gears, NVH absorber, sideband detuning).

**Comment (1 mark):** Roughness greater than 1 asper is generally perceived as "very rough" and objectionable in vehicle cabins. A 120 Hz modulation at 100 % AM on a 3.5 kHz gear whine is a classic NVH problem in manual transmissions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Zones — Psychoacoustic Index Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing phon with dB:** Phon is the **Loudness Level**, not SPL. They are equal *only* at 1 kHz. At other frequencies, use the equal-loudness contour (ISO 226). Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong reference for Sharpness:** Sharpness = 1 acum is for **narrowband noise** (1 critical band wide) at **60 dB**, not a pure tone. Loss: 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Mixing modulation ranges:** Do NOT use the Roughness formula for $f_{mod} = 4$ Hz (use Fluctuation Strength) or vice versa. Loss: 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting units in final answer:** Always append sone / acum / asper / vacil / tu. Loss: 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Wrong constants:** The Zwicker constant is 0.08 (not 0.0631) and the Daniel-Weber prefactor is 0.3. Mixing Moore-Glasberg and Zwicker constants will give wildly wrong answers.
> 6. **Neglecting equal-loudness contours:** At 4 kHz, 60 dB SPL ≠ 60 phon. Use the lookup table or ISO 226 contour.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Loudness $N$ (sone):** Integral of specific loudness $N'(z)$ over 24 Bark. Stevens: $N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$. Reference: 1 sone = 1 kHz, 40 dB SPL.
- ✅ **Loudness Level $L_N$ (phon):** SPL of equally loud 1 kHz tone. Equals SPL at 1 kHz only.
- ✅ **Sharpness $S$ (acum):** $S = 0.11 \cdot (\int N'(z) g(z) z \, dz) / \int N'(z) \, dz$. Weighting $g(z)$ activates past 15.8 Bark.
- ✅ **Roughness $R$ (asper):** Modulation 15–300 Hz. Daniel-Weber: $R = 0.3 (f_{mod}/1000) \int N' \Delta L_E \, dz$. Peak at 70 Hz.
- ✅ **Fluctuation Strength $F$ (vacil):** Modulation ≤ 20 Hz. Peak at 4 Hz. Reference: 1 vacil = 1 kHz, 60 dB, 100% AM at 4 Hz.
- ✅ **Tonality $T$ (tu):** 1 tu ≈ 1 dB prominence of tone over background (DIN 45681 / ECMA-74).
- ✅ **Bark Scale:** $z = 13 \arctan(0.76 f/1000) + 3.5 \arctan((f/7500)^2)$ for $f$ in Hz.
- ✅ **Critical Insight:** Doubling sone ↔ 10 dB SPL increase; doubling acum ≈ 5–6 dB shift toward higher frequency.
- ✅ **Engineering Anchors:** Automotive target $S \le 2.5$ acum, $R \le 0.5$ asper, cabin loudness $N \le 40$ sone.
- ✅ **Combined Metric:** **Psychoacoustic Annoyance** $PA = N (1 + \sqrt{g} + \sqrt{s})$ per ISO/PAS 20065.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceptable Degree of Harshness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Acceptable Degree of Harshness # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Harshness** is the subjective psychological and physiological response of a vehicle occupant to the **intermittent, transient, and high-frequency mechanical disturbances** (typically between **5 Hz to 80 Hz**) transmitted through the vehicle's body structure, suspension, and powertrain. Unlike *Noise* (audible) and *Vibration* (physical oscillation), Harshness is **perceptually weighted** — it describes how *unpleasant* or *rough* a ride feels. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PEAUT755 – Module 4), the **Acceptable Degree of Harshness** refers to the **maximum tolerable limit** of these transient disturbances beyond which the human occupant experiences discomfort, fatigue, or loss of vehicle control. It is defined using standardized objective metrics such as **Root Mean Square (RMS) acceleration**, **Crest Factor**, **Vibration Dose Value (VDV)**, and the **Ride Number (RN)**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for KTU Examiners:** > - **Noise** = Sound Pressure Level (dB), frequency 20 Hz – 20 kHz > - **Vibration** = Steady-state or periodic oscillation, typically below 50 Hz > - **Harshness** = Transient, impulsive, high-frequency events (above 5 Hz), measured by human discomfort index --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are driving two different cars over the **same pothole**: - **Car A (Smooth)**: The suspension absorbs the impact. You feel a gentle dip. You barely register the event. - **Car B (Harsh)**: The suspension bottoms out, you hear a metallic "thud," you feel a sharp upward kick in your spine, and you instinctively brace yourself. The *physical* input (the pothole) is identical. The **difference lies in the vehicle's response and the human's interpretation of that response**. That interpretation — *how rough, jarring, or unpleasant the event feels* — is **Harshness**. The **threshold** below which 80% of test subjects report "acceptable" or "comfortable" is the **Acceptable Degree of Harshness**. > [!TIP] > **Simple Memory Hook for Students:** Harshness = *Roughness of ride*, while Acceptable Degree = *the limit your body says "enough!"* --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used (Quick Reference) | Metric | Symbol | Unit | Threshold of Acceptability | |---|---|---|---| | RMS Acceleration | $a_{rms}$ | **m/s²** | < **0.315 m/s²** (ISO 2631) | | Vibration Dose Value | $VDV$ | **m/s^1.75** | < **9.1 m/s^1.75** | | Crest Factor | $CF$ | dimensionless | < **6 (typical comfort)** | | Ride Number | $RN$ | dimensionless | > **3.0 (acceptable)** | > **Bold convention**: All primary physical constants and standard acceptability thresholds are highlighted in **bold** as per KTU 2024 syllabus emphasis. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Harshness Harshness is evaluated across **three distinct layers**, each requiring different analytical tools: ### Layer 1 — Source Characterization (Objective) - Captures the **raw mechanical signal** (acceleration, force, displacement) at the seat-track, steering wheel, or floor pan. - Uses **accelerometers** sampled typically at **≥ 200 Hz** to capture transient shocks. ### Layer 2 — Human Response Modeling (Psychophysical) - Applies **frequency weighting functions** $W_b$, $W_d$, $W_k$ (per **ISO 2631-1:1997** and **BS 6841**). - These weightings model how the human body **amplifies or attenuates** different vibration frequencies. - Peak sensitivity for seated occupants lies in the **4 Hz – 8 Hz** range (vertical) and **1 Hz – 2 Hz** (horizontal). ### Layer 3 — Subjective Rating (Subjective) - 10-point **rating scales** where trained evaluators rate ride quality: - **1 = Unbearable**, **5 = Acceptable**, **10 = Excellent** - The **80th percentile rating ≥ 5** defines the *acceptable threshold* per **SAE J1492** and **SAE J1540**. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **every formula** that has appeared in KTU past papers for Module 4 of PEAUT755. Memorize these for guaranteed marks. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | RMS Acceleration | $$a_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} a^{2}(t)\,dt}$$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Time-domain form | | 2 | RMS (Discrete form) | $$a_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i^{2}}$$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | For sampled data | | 3 | Crest Factor | $$CF = \dfrac{a_{peak}}{a_{rms}}$$ | dimensionless | Pure sine $= \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$ | | 4 | Vibration Dose Value | $$VDV = \left[\int_{0}^{T} a^{4}(t)\,dt\right]^{1/4}$$ | $\text{m/s}^{1.75}$ | 4th-power average | | 5 | VDV (Discrete) | $$VDV = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i^{4}\,\Delta t\right]^{1/4}$$ | $\text{m/s}^{1.75}$ | More sensitive to peaks | | 6 | Root Mean Quad (RMQ) | $$a_{rmq} = \left[\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} a^{4}(t)\,dt\right]^{1/4}$$ | $\text{m/s}^{1.75}$ | Alternative form | | 7 | Ride Number (RN) | $$RN = 100 - 10\,a_{w,rms}$$ (approx.) | dimensionless | Higher = better | | 8 | Frequency Weighting (Vertical, $W_k$) | $$W_k(f) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+(f/f_1)^2}}$$ for $f_1 = 12.5$ Hz | dimensionless | ISO 2631 | | 9 | Weighted RMS | $$a_w = \sqrt{\sum_{j}(W_j \cdot a_{rms,j})^{2}}$$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Across 1/3-octave bands | | 10 | Comfort Boundary (ISO) | $a_w \leq 0.315$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | "Not uncomfortable" | > **Note on Notation**: In formulas 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, the symbol $a(t)$ denotes the **frequency-weighted instantaneous acceleration**. Avoid using $\vert a \vert$ inside markdown tables — use $\mid a \mid$ or $\lvert a \rvert$ instead. --- ## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Formula — Engineering Insight ### 2.3.1 Why RMS and not Peak? The human body responds to **energy content** of vibration, not instantaneous peaks. The squared term in RMS integrates energy, while a single peak can be misleading due to sensor noise or impulse spikes. ### 2.3.2 Why the 4th Power for VDV? Transient shocks (pothole strikes, brake judder) deliver **disproportionately high discomfort** compared to steady-state vibration of the same RMS. The 4th power **over-weights peaks**, making VDV the **most sensitive indicator of harshness**. This is why VDV catches events RMS misses. ### 2.3.3 Why Frequency Weighting? The human body has **resonant frequencies**: - Spine (seated): **~4–6 Hz** - Head/shoulder: **~5–8 Hz** - Hand-arm (steering): **~8–12 Hz** Weighting curves **filter out frequencies the body can't perceive**, leaving only the perceptually relevant signal. ### 2.3.4 Real-World Utility - **OEM Ride Development**: Engineers tune shock absorber damping, bushing stiffness, and tire spring rate so $VDV < 9.1$ and $a_w < 0.315$. - **Quality Control**: Every production vehicle on the assembly line is statistically sampled (SPC charts) to ensure harshness stays within limits. - **Active Suspension Systems**: Skyhook, CDC, and MR dampers use VDV as the **real-time control objective**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Acceleration to Vibration Dose Value (VDV) > [!NOTE] > This derivation has appeared in **3 previous KTU University Exams**. Practice writing it out in full — examiners award marks for **every line of working**. **Given:** An accelerometer mounted at the driver's seat measures the following acceleration samples during a 4-second pothole strike (sampling interval $\Delta t = 0.01$ s): $$a_i = \{0.5,\ 1.2,\ 3.5,\ 5.0,\ 4.2,\ 2.1,\ 1.0,\ 0.6,\ 0.4,\ 0.3\}\ \text{m/s}^2$$ with $N = 10$ samples. **Find:** (a) RMS acceleration, (b) Crest Factor, (c) VDV. --- ### Part (a) — RMS Acceleration Derivation The RMS of a discrete signal is defined by squaring each sample, averaging, and taking the square root. **Step 1:** Square each acceleration value: $$ \begin{aligned} a_1^{2} &= (0.5)^{2} = 0.25 \\ a_2^{2} &= (1.2)^{2} = 1.44 \\ a_3^{2} &= (3.5)^{2} = 12.25 \\ a_4^{2} &= (5.0)^{2} = 25.00 \\ a_5^{2} &= (4.2)^{2} = 17.64 \\ a_6^{2} &= (2.1)^{2} = 4.41 \\ a_7^{2} &= (1.0)^{2} = 1.00 \\ a_8^{2} &= (0.6)^{2} = 0.36 \\ a_9^{2} &= (0.4)^{2} = 0.16 \\ a_{10}^{2} &= (0.3)^{2} = 0.09 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Sum the squared values: $$\sum_{i=1}^{10} a_i^{2} = 0.25 + 1.44 + 12.25 + 25.00 + 17.64 + 4.41 + 1.00 + 0.36 + 0.16 + 0.09 = 62.60$$ **Step 3:** Divide by N and take the square root: $$ \begin{aligned} a_{rms} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{62.60}{10}} \\ &= \sqrt{6.260} \\ &\approx \mathbf{2.502\ \text{m/s}^2} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** This RMS is **8× higher** than the ISO 2631 comfort limit ($0.315$ m/s²). Hence, the vehicle **fails the acceptable harshness criterion**. --- ### Part (b) — Crest Factor Derivation **Step 1:** Identify the peak acceleration from the dataset: $$a_{peak} = \max(a_i) = 5.0\ \text{m/s}^2$$ **Step 2:** Apply the crest factor formula: $$ \begin{aligned} CF &= \dfrac{a_{peak}}{a_{rms}} \\ &= \dfrac{5.0}{2.502} \\ &\approx \mathbf{1.998} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** $CF \approx 2$ indicates the signal is **moderately transient**. A pure sine wave has $CF = 1.414$, while sharp impacts have $CF > 5$. Our signal is a single-pulse transient typical of road irregularities. --- ### Part (c) — Vibration Dose Value (VDV) Derivation The VDV uses the **4th power** of acceleration, making it highly sensitive to peaks. **Step 1:** Compute $a_i^{4}$ for each sample: $$ \begin{aligned} a_1^{4} &= (0.5)^{4} = 0.0625 \\ a_2^{4} &= (1.2)^{4} = 2.0736 \\ a_3^{4} &= (3.5)^{4} = 150.0625 \\ a_4^{4} &= (5.0)^{4} = 625.0000 \\ a_5^{4} &= (4.2)^{4} = 311.1696 \\ a_6^{4} &= (2.1)^{4} = 19.4481 \\ a_7^{4} &= (1.0)^{4} = 1.0000 \\ a_8^{4} &= (0.6)^{4} = 0.1296 \\ a_9^{4} &= (0.4)^{4} = 0.0256 \\ a_{10}^{4} &= (0.3)^{4} = 0.0081 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Sum and multiply by $\Delta t$: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum a_i^{4} &= 0.0625 + 2.0736 + 150.0625 + 625.0000 + 311.1696 \\ &\quad + 19.4481 + 1.0000 + 0.1296 + 0.0256 + 0.0081 \\ &= 1108.9796 \end{aligned} $$ $$1108.9796 \times \Delta t = 1108.9796 \times 0.01 = 11.0898$$ **Step 3:** Take the 4th root: $$ \begin{aligned} VDV &= (11.0898)^{1/4} \\ &= (11.0898)^{0.25} \\ &\approx \mathbf{1.823\ \text{m/s}^{1.75}} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** $VDV = 1.823$ m/s^1.75. Although RMS was high, VDV is also elevated. For a **continuous ride over time $T$**, VDV scales roughly as $T^{1/4}$. Over a 1-hour drive, the **cumulative VDV** could rise significantly, approaching the 9.1 m/s^1.75 threshold. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Scenario | $a_{rms}$ (m/s²) | $CF$ | $VDV$ (m/s^1.75) | Verdict | |---|---|---|---|---| | Smooth highway cruising | **0.10** | 1.5 | **2.5** | Excellent | | City road, mild bumps | **0.32** | 2.0 | **6.8** | Just acceptable | | Rough rural road | **0.55** | 3.5 | **12.5** | Uncomfortable | | Pothole strike (single) | **0.40** | 6.0 | **15.0** | Harsh event | | Off-road / unpaved | **1.20** | 4.5 | **22.0** | Highly uncomfortable | > **Key Insight:** Notice that **CF distinguishes transient events** (CF = 6) from continuous roughness (CF = 3.5) even when RMS is similar. This is why **multiple metrics** are mandatory for KTU-level analysis. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Harshness Evaluation Below is a Mermaid block diagram representing the **signal flow** from physical disturbance through human perception to final acceptability verdict. This is a **functional architecture** as required by the KTU protocol for topics where complex physical drawings are involved. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Road Surface Input
Pothole Bump Joint Roughness] A2[Vehicle Dynamics Layer
Tire Suspension Bush Body] A3[Sensor Array
Accelerometer Seat Track Floor] A4[Signal Conditioning
Amplifier Filter Anti Alias] A5[Time Domain Metrics
RMS Crest Factor VDV] A6[Frequency Domain Layer
FFT 1/3 Octave PSD] A7[Weighting Module
Wk Wb Wd ISO 2631] A8[Comfort Indices
aw rms Ride Number] A9[Subjective Rating
1 to 10 Scale] A10{Acceptability Test
VDV less than 9.1} A11[Acceptable Harshness Zone] A12[Reject Tune Re Engineer] A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 A4 --> A5 A4 --> A6 A5 --> A7 A6 --> A7 A7 --> A8 A8 --> A10 A9 --> A10 A10 -->|YES| A11 A10 -->|NO| A12 ``` --- ## 4.2 Diagram Reading Guide (For KTU Answer Writing) | Block | KTU Mapping | Real-World Function | |---|---|---| | **Road Surface Input** | Excitation source | The "cause" | | **Vehicle Dynamics Layer** | Transfer function $H(\omega)$ | Filters input | | **Sensor Array** | Measurement | Captures response | | **Time / Frequency Domain** | Analysis method | Reduces data | | **Weighting Module** | Human perception filter | Mimics body | | **Subjective Rating** | SAE J1492 test | Human judgment | | **Acceptability Test** | Threshold comparison | Decision gate | > [!TIP] > In a 14-mark KTU answer, **always include this block flow** as part of your explanation. Examiners allocate **2–3 marks** specifically for diagrams showing the *process architecture*. --- ## 4.3 Acceptable Harshness Envelope (Conceptual Plot Description) Imagine a 2-D plot where: - **X-axis**: Vibration frequency in Hz (logarithmic, 0.5 Hz to 100 Hz) - **Y-axis**: Weighted acceleration $a_w$ in m/s² (logarithmic, 0.01 to 10) - **Curves drawn**: - Lower curve: **ISO "Reduced Comfort" boundary** at $a_w = 0.315$ m/s² - Upper curve: **ISO "Health and Safety" boundary** at $a_w = 1.0$ m/s² - Frequency peaks at 4–8 Hz representing human resonance The **acceptable region** is the band between these two curves, where 50%–80% of test subjects find the ride acceptable. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** ### Question A.1 (3 Marks) **Q:** Define the term **"Harshness"** in the context of vehicle NVH. How is it **distinguished** from noise and vibration? **Model Answer:** Harshness is the **subjective human perception** of intermittent, transient, and high-frequency mechanical disturbances transmitted through the vehicle structure, typically in the **5–80 Hz** range. It is distinct from: - **Noise**: which is audible (20 Hz–20 kHz) and measured in dB. - **Vibration**: which is physical oscillation, often steady-state. Harshness combines both **physical severity** and **psychological discomfort**, requiring both objective metrics (VDV, RMS) and subjective rating (1–10 scale). **(3 marks)** --- ### Question A.2 (3 Marks) **Q:** What is the **Vibration Dose Value (VDV)**? Write its mathematical expression and state its **acceptability limit** as per ISO 2631. **Model Answer:** VDV is a **4th-power averaged measure** of vibration exposure that accounts for transient shocks. Its formula is: $$VDV = \left[\int_{0}^{T} a^{4}(t)\,dt\right]^{1/4}$$ For continuous exposure, the **upper acceptability limit** is **VDV = 9.1 m/s^1.75** (approximately), beyond which ride is rated "highly uncomfortable" per ISO 2631-1. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified as per 2024 Scheme]** --- ### Question B – Option A (14 Marks) **Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **objective methods** used to quantify vehicle harshness. Discuss **RMS acceleration**, **Crest Factor**, and **Vibration Dose Value (VDV)** with their mathematical formulations and significance. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A vehicle's seat-track accelerometer records the following vertical acceleration values (in m/s²) during a sharp road bump: $a = \{0.2,\ 0.8,\ 2.5,\ 4.0,\ 3.2,\ 1.5,\ 0.6,\ 0.3\}$ over $T = 0.08$ s with sampling interval $\Delta t = 0.01$ s. Calculate the **RMS acceleration**, **Crest Factor**, and **VDV**. Comment on whether the ride falls within the **ISO 2631 acceptable harshness zone**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to B(a): The **objective quantification** of vehicle harshness relies on three principal time-domain metrics, each capturing a different aspect of the disturbance. **1. RMS Acceleration $a_{rms}$:** Represents the **energy content** of the vibration over a measurement window $T$. $$a_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} a^{2}(t)\,dt}$$ It is the **most widely used metric** in industry but **fails to capture peak shocks** adequately. **2. Crest Factor $CF$:** The ratio of peak to RMS acceleration: $$CF = \dfrac{a_{peak}}{a_{rms}}$$ A pure sine wave gives $CF = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. Transients (pothole strikes) give $CF > 5$. **Significance:** Distinguishes impulsive events from steady-state vibration. **3. Vibration Dose Value $VDV$:** A 4th-power integral that **over-weights peaks**, making it the **gold standard for harshness assessment**: $$VDV = \left[\int_{0}^{T} a^{4}(t)\,dt\right]^{1/4}$$ For long-duration exposure, $VDV$ grows approximately as $T^{1/4}$. The **acceptability threshold** is **$VDV \leq 9.1$ m/s^1.75**. Values between 9.1 and 15 indicate "fairly uncomfortable," and above 15 indicate "very uncomfortable." **Conclusion:** Together, these three metrics provide a **complete objective picture** of harshness: RMS gives energy, CF gives impulsiveness, and VDV gives cumulative discomfort. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to B(b): Given: $a = \{0.2,\ 0.8,\ 2.5,\ 4.0,\ 3.2,\ 1.5,\ 0.6,\ 0.3\}$ m/s², $\Delta t = 0.01$ s, $N = 8$. **Step 1 — Compute $a_i^{2}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} (0.2)^2 &= 0.04 \\ (0.8)^2 &= 0.64 \\ (2.5)^2 &= 6.25 \\ (4.0)^2 &= 16.00 \\ (3.2)^2 &= 10.24 \\ (1.5)^2 &= 2.25 \\ (0.6)^2 &= 0.36 \\ (0.3)^2 &= 0.09 \\ \end{aligned} $$ $$\sum a_i^{2} = 35.87$$ **Step 2 — RMS:** $$ \begin{aligned} a_{rms} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{35.87}{8}} \\ &= \sqrt{4.484} \\ &\approx \mathbf{2.117\ \text{m/s}^2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Peak and Crest Factor:** $$a_{peak} = 4.0\ \text{m/s}^2$$ $$CF = \dfrac{4.0}{2.117} \approx \mathbf{1.889}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $a_i^{4}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} (0.2)^4 &= 0.0016 \\ (0.8)^4 &= 0.4096 \\ (2.5)^4 &= 39.0625 \\ (4.0)^4 &= 256.0000 \\ (3.2)^4 &= 104.8576 \\ (1.5)^4 &= 5.0625 \\ (0.6)^4 &= 0.1296 \\ (0.3)^4 &= 0.0081 \\ \end{aligned} $$ $$\sum a_i^{4} = 405.5315$$ **Step 5 — VDV:** $$ \begin{aligned} VDV &= \left[\sum a_i^{4} \cdot \Delta t\right]^{1/4} \\ &= \left[405.5315 \times 0.01\right]^{1/4} \\ &= (4.0553)^{0.25} \\ &\approx \mathbf{1.418\ \text{m/s}^{1.75}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Verdict:** | Metric | Value | Threshold | Status | |---|---|---|---| | $a_{rms}$ | 2.117 m/s² | < 0.315 m/s² | **Fails** | | $CF$ | 1.889 | < 6 | Acceptable impulsiveness | | $VDV$ | 1.418 m/s^1.75 | < 9.1 m/s^1.75 | **Acceptable** | **Conclusion:** The **RMS acceleration greatly exceeds** the ISO 2631 comfort limit, indicating **unacceptable harshness**. However, the **VDV is below the limit** because the event is **short-duration**. The vehicle's suspension tuning must be revised. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B – Option B (14 Marks) **Q: (a)** Discuss the **subjective evaluation** of vehicle harshness using the **SAE J1492 / J1540 rating methodology**. Explain the rating scale, the role of **frequency weighting**, and the procedure for establishing the **acceptable degree of harshness**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A car's suspension is tested on a **shaker rig** with sinusoidal excitation. The RMS acceleration measured at the seat is **0.45 m/s²** at **6 Hz**, and **0.20 m/s²** at **25 Hz**. The frequency weighting factors at these frequencies (per ISO 2631 $W_k$ curve) are **0.95** and **0.40** respectively. Compute the **weighted RMS acceleration** $a_w$ and determine whether the ride is acceptable. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to B(a): **Subjective Evaluation of Harshness — SAE Methodology:** The **Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE)** standardized subjective evaluation in **SAE J1492** (Recommended Subjective Rating Practice) and **SAE J1540** (Operational Definition of Ride Quality). **Rating Scale:** Evaluators rate ride quality on a **10-point scale**: - **1** = Worst imaginable (intolerable) - **5** = Just acceptable (threshold) - **10** = Best imaginable (excellent) **Procedure:** 1. A panel of **20–30 trained evaluators** is selected. 2. Each evaluator drives the vehicle over standardized test routes (highway, urban, rough road). 3. Ratings are collected and a **mean opinion score (MOS)** computed. 4. The **80th percentile rating ≥ 5** defines acceptable harshness. **Role of Frequency Weighting:** Frequency weightings $W_b$ (vertical, ISO 2631), $W_d$ (horizontal), and $W_k$ (high-frequency, for harshness) **filter** the raw signal so only the **perceptually relevant** frequencies remain. For **harshness specifically**, the $W_k$ weighting is used because it amplifies the **5–80 Hz** range where human sensitivity to transients is highest. **Acceptable Degree Established by:** $$a_w \leq 0.315\ \text{m/s}^2 \quad (\text{ISO "Reduced Comfort Boundary"})$$ If $a_w$ exceeds this value, more than 50% of evaluators report "uncomfortable," and the vehicle is **rejected for production**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to B(b): Given: - At $f_1 = 6$ Hz: $a_{rms,1} = 0.45$ m/s², $W_k(f_1) = 0.95$ - At $f_2 = 25$ Hz: $a_{rms,2} = 0.20$ m/s², $W_k(f_2) = 0.40$ **Step 1 — Apply weighting to each band:** $$ \begin{aligned} a_{w,1} &= W_k(f_1) \times a_{rms,1} \\ &= 0.95 \times 0.45 \\ &= 0.4275\ \text{m/s}^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} a_{w,2} &= W_k(f_2) \times a_{rms,2} \\ &= 0.40 \times 0.20 \\ &= 0.0800\ \text{m/s}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Combine weighted bands (root-sum-square):** $$ \begin{aligned} a_w &= \sqrt{a_{w,1}^{2} + a_{w,2}^{2}} \\ &= \sqrt{(0.4275)^2 + (0.0800)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{0.1828 + 0.0064} \\ &= \sqrt{0.1892} \\ &\approx \mathbf{0.435\ \text{m/s}^2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compare against ISO 2631 threshold:** $$a_w = 0.435\ \text{m/s}^2 \quad > \quad 0.315\ \text{m/s}^2$$ **Verdict:** The ride **exceeds** the ISO comfort boundary by **38%**. The vehicle is classified as **"Not Uncomfortable"** borderline; it is **NOT acceptable** as per ISO 2631-1 reduced comfort criteria. **Design modifications needed** — likely stiffen the suspension bushings or retune the shock absorber. **(7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing RMS with VDV**: RMS uses the 2nd power; VDV uses the 4th power. Mixing them up costs **3 marks immediately**. > 2. **Forgetting the time interval $\Delta t$ in VDV**: The discrete VDV formula has $\Delta t$ multiplying the sum. Omitting it gives a **dimensionally wrong** answer. > 3. **Using peak instead of RMS**: When a problem says "compute $a_{rms}$," students sometimes use $a_{peak}$. Read the question **twice**. > 4. **Applying $W_k$ at wrong frequency**: The $W_k$ weighting is for **vertical vibration 0.5–80 Hz**, not for whole-body horizontal. Mismatched weighting = **wrong context**. > 5. **Not stating units**: KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** if final answer has no unit (e.g., "1.823" without "m/s^1.75"). > 6. **Skipping the verdict**: A 7-mark calculation question **always requires a concluding statement** comparing the answer to the threshold. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Acceptable Degree of Harshness** - **Harshness** is **subjective**, transient, high-frequency (>5 Hz), measured by **human discomfort**. - **Noise** ≠ **Vibration** ≠ **Harshness** — these three are **distinct** in NVH analysis. - **RMS acceleration** $a_{rms}$ measures **energy content** of vibration. - **Crest Factor** $CF = a_{peak} / a_{rms}$ measures **impulsiveness** (sharpness). - **VDV** uses **4th-power averaging**, ideal for **transient shocks**. - **ISO 2631-1** provides the standard **acceptability thresholds**: - $a_w \leq 0.315$ m/s² → "Not uncomfortable" - $a_w \leq 0.5$ m/s² → "A little uncomfortable" - $a_w \leq 1.0$ m/s² → "Fairly uncomfortable" - $a_w > 1.0$ m/s² → "Very uncomfortable" (health risk) - **VDV limit** ≈ **9.1 m/s^1.75** for a typical daily commute. - **Frequency weighting $W_k$** peaks at **4–8 Hz** (human spine resonance). - **Subjective rating** uses **SAE J1492** (10-point scale); 80th percentile ≥ 5 = acceptable. - **Higher Ride Number (RN)** = Better ride comfort. - **OEM tuning goal**: minimize $a_{rms}$, $VDV$, and $CF$ simultaneously through bushing stiffness, damping, and seat isolation. - **Always write the unit** with your final answer. - **Always conclude** with a comparison to the relevant ISO/SAE threshold. --- **End of Module 4 — Topic: Acceptable Degree of Harshness** *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, PEAUT755 — Noise, Vibration and Harshness*

topics.title: Sound Quality Indexes for High Frequency Tonal Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sound Quality Indexes for High Frequency Tonal Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sound Quality (SQ)** is the perceptually relevant, psychoacoustic characterization of a sound, expressed through a set of **metric indices** that correlate with human auditory sensation — *not* merely with sound pressure level (SPL). For **High-Frequency Tonal Noise (HFTN)** — narrowband pure-tone-like components typically residing in the upper third of the audible spectrum (≈ **2 kHz – 20 kHz**) — the dominant SQ indexes are: **Sharpness (S)**, **Tonality (K)**, **Prominence Ratio (PR)**, **Tone-to-Noise Ratio (TNR)**, and **Specific Loudness (N′)**.

In NVH engineering, **high-frequency tonal noise** arises from sources such as:
- **Gear whine** in transmission systems
- **Tire cavity resonance** (the "humming" road noise)
- **Bearing race frequencies**, **compressor whistles**, **flow-induced tonal components**
- **Pulsating HVAC ducts**

These pure-tone-like disturbances are perceptually more annoying than broadband noise of the **same A-weighted SPL**, because the human ear is highly sensitive to *conspicuous* narrowband components in an otherwise flat spectrum.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are sitting in a quiet library (broadband ambient ≈ 30 dB) and someone starts **whistling a single high-pitched note** at 80 dB. Although the whistle and a noisy vacuum cleaner may both read "80 dB" on a sound level meter, the **whistle is far more irritating** — you can hear it *above* everything else, and your brain tags it as "sharp, piercing, intrusive."

**Why?** Because three psychoacoustic facts coexist:
1. The ear has a **built-in high-frequency emphasis** between 2–5 kHz (the **4 kHz notch / Equal-Loudness Contour peak**).
2. A narrow tone occupies a *tiny* frequency range but contains *lots* of acoustic energy → it stands out (high **Tonality**, **Prominence Ratio**).
3. High-frequency tones excite the **basilar membrane at its basal end** — a region densely populated with neural sensors → perception is amplified.

Hence, engineers cannot rely on **dB(A)** alone. They need a *vector* of SQ metrics, each capturing one psychological attribute.

---

## 1.3 The Five SQ Indexes — Quick-Reference Snapshot

| # | Index | Symbol | Captures | Unit |
|---|-------|--------|----------|------|
| 1 | **Sharpness** | $S$ | High-frequency *emphasis* / "brightness" | **acum** |
| 2 | **Tonality (Aures)** | $K$ | Pure-tone *content* relative to broadband | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Prominence Ratio** | $PR$ | Tone *conspicuousness* above background | **dB** |
| 4 | **Tone-to-Noise Ratio** | $TNR$ | Tone energy vs. local noise energy | **dB** |
| 5 | **Specific Loudness** | $N'$ | Per-Bark loudness density | **sone/Bark** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory hook:** For high-frequency tonal noise, *"S-T-P-T-L"* — **S**harpness, **T**onality, **P**rominence ratio, **T**NR, **L**oudness. These five indices together describe **why** a noise is judged *annoying* rather than just *loud*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sharpness (S) — The "High-Frequency Heaviness" Meter

**Zwicker's definition (DIN 45692 / ISO 532-3):**

$$S = 0.11 \cdot \dfrac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{24\ \text{Bark}} N'(z)\cdot g(z)\cdot z\,dz}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{24\ \text{Bark}} N'(z)\,dz} \quad [\text{acum}]$$

Where:
- $N'(z)$ = **specific loudness** in sone/Bark at critical-band rate $z$ (Bark)
- $z$ = critical-band rate (Bark)
- $g(z)$ = **binaural weighting function** emphasizing high Bark bands:

$$g(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z \le 13.8\ \text{Bark} \\[4pt] 0.066 \cdot e^{0.171\,(z-13.8)}, & z > 13.8\ \text{Bark} \end{cases}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $S < 1$ acum → "dull" sound (low-pass-like, e.g., engine rumble)
- $1 \le S \le 2$ → typical broadband NVH
- $S > 2$ → "sharp", high-frequency dominant (gear whine, brake squeal, whistle)
- $S > 4$ acum → perceptually **very piercing**

> [!NOTE]
> Sharpness *alone* does not reveal whether the high-frequency content is **tonal or broadband**. A pink-noise-like hiss can score high in $S$ if its spectrum is tilted upward.

---

## 2.2 Tonality (K) — Aures' Weighted Tone-to-Broadband Ratio

**Aures' formulation:**

$$K = c \cdot \left[ \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_t}{N}\right) \right]$$

Where:
- $N_t$ = **loudness of the tonal component**
- $N$ = **total loudness of the sound**
- $c = 1$ for narrow tones (bandwidth $\Delta f < 1/3$ octave), decreasing for wider tones
- For very broadband noise, $K \to 0$.

A more rigorous form (von Bismarck):

$$K = \dfrac{\Delta L_N}{a_N}$$

Where $\Delta L_N$ is the loudness-level shift caused by adding the tone and $a_N \approx 1\ \text{sone} \rightarrow 0.4\ \text{phon}$ correction.

---

## 2.3 Prominence Ratio (PR) — ISO 7779 / ECMA-74 Method

**Concept:** A tone is *prominent* if its sound pressure level rises meaningfully above a smoothed (masked) noise floor.

$$PR(f_i) = L_{p}(f_i) - L_{s}(f_i) + \Delta L$$

Where:
- $L_{p}(f_i)$ = SPL in the **tone-containing critical band** at frequency $f_i$
- $L_{s}(f_i)$ = SPL of the **smoothed adjacent-band average** (background)
- $\Delta L$ = correction for the bandwidth of the tone (typically $0$ to $+10$ dB)

**Interpretation:**
- $PR < 7$ dB → tone is **inaudible / masked**
- $7 \le PR < 15$ dB → **perceptible**
- $PR \ge 15$ dB → **highly conspicuous**

---

## 2.4 Tone-to-Noise Ratio (TNR)

$$TNR = L_{\text{tone}} - L_{\text{noise\,band}} \quad [\text{dB}]$$

Computed in the tone's critical band. Conceptually simpler than $PR$ — does *not* apply background-smoothing. Used in **gear-noise** and **compressor-siren** diagnostics.

---

## 2.5 Specific Loudness $N'$ (Zwicker / ISO 532-1,2,3)

The *density* of loudness along the basilar membrane, obtained from the **excitation pattern**:

$$N'(z) = \text{non-linear function of}\ E(z)$$

Where the **excitation level** $E$ (in dB) for each Bark band is:

$$E(z) = \dfrac{\rho c}{\pi \cdot 10^{-12}} \cdot \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[1 + s \cdot \dfrac{\vert H(z,f)\vert^2}{\vert H(z,f_c)\vert^2}\right] \cdot 10^{L_{eq}(f)/10}\,df$$

- $\rho c$ = acoustic impedance of air ≈ **400 N·s/m³ (Pa·s/m)**
- $s = \text{signal/noise-detection threshold} \approx 0\ \text{dB}$
- $H(z,f)$ = **basilar-membrane transfer function** at Bark position $z$ for input frequency $f$
- $f_c$ = characteristic frequency

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Table for Exam Writing.** Replace every $\vert\cdot\vert$ with the word "magnitude of" when writing in exam scripts to avoid the forbidden pipe-character.

| Quantity | Formula | Symbol | Unit | Remarks |
|----------|---------|--------|------|---------|
| **Sharpness** | $S = 0.11 \cdot \dfrac{\int N'(z) g(z) z\, dz}{\int N'(z)\, dz}$ | $S$ | acum | Zwicker / DIN 45692 |
| **Weighting** $g(z)$ | $g(z) = 0.066 e^{0.171(z-13.8)}$ for $z > 13.8$ | $g$ | — | unity below 13.8 Bark |
| **Bark scale** | $z = 13\arctan(0.00076 f) + 3.5\arctan((f/7500)^2)$ | $z$ | Bark | Traunmüller (1990) |
| **Aures Tonality** | $K = c \cdot \log_{10}(N_t/N)$ | $K$ | tu | $c = 1$ for pure tones |
| **Prominence Ratio** | $PR = L_p - L_s + \Delta L$ | $PR$ | dB | ISO 7779 / ECMA-74 |
| **TNR** | $TNR = L_{\text{tone}} - L_{\text{nb}}$ | $TNR$ | dB | Critical-band local |
| **Specific Loudness** | $N'(z) = \Psi(E(z))$ | $N'$ | sone/Bark | from excitation $E$ |
| **Binaural loudness** | $N = m \cdot N_{\text{right}} + (1-m) N_{\text{left}}$ | $N$ | sone | $m = 0.5$ for diffuse field |
| **Critical-band rate→Hz** | $f = 700\cdot(10^{0.1 z}-1)$ | $f$ | Hz | Approx. Greenwood |

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility (Real-World Application)

| Industry Application | SQ Index Most Used | Why |
|----------------------|--------------------|-----|
| Automotive cabin NVH | $S$, $PR$ | Tire/road tonality & HVAC whistles |
| Electric-vehicle (EV) motor whine | $K$, $S$ | High-order harmonics of e-machine |
| Gear transmission diagnostics | $PR$, $TNR$ | Gear-mesh tones, sidebands |
| HVAC duct design | $PR$, $S$ | Flow-induced tonal components |
| Psychoacoustic annoyance (ISO 12913) | Combined $S \cdot K$ model | Soundscape design |

> [!NOTE]
> A standard **annoyance metric** combining these indices is **Zwicker's psychoacoustic annoyance** model:
> $$PA \approx N \cdot \left(1 + \sqrt{w_S^2 + w_{FR}^2}\right) \cdot (1 + w_K K)$$
> where $w_S, w_{FR}, w_K$ are calibrated weights. For high-frequency tonal problems, the **$w_K$** contribution dominates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Converting Frequency (Hz) to Critical-Band Rate (Bark)

The Bark scale partitions the audible spectrum into ~**24 critical bands**, each ≈ 1 Bark wide and corresponding to ~**1.3 mm** of basilar-membrane length.

**Starting point — Traunmüller's empirical fit (1990):**

$$\text{Original equation:}\quad z = \dfrac{26.81\cdot f}{1960 + f} - 0.53$$

**More accurate form (used in ISO 532):**

$$z = 13 \arctan(0.00076 f) + 3.5 \arctan\!\left(\left(\dfrac{f}{7500}\right)^{2}\right)$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A gear-mesh tone is recorded at $f = 4000$ Hz. Compute its critical-band rate $z$.

**Step 1:** Compute $0.00076 \times 4000 = 3.04$.

**Step 2:** $\arctan(3.04)$.
In radians: $\arctan(3.04) \approx 1.2522$ rad.

**Step 3:** First term: $13 \times 1.2522 = 16.278$.

**Step 4:** Compute $(4000/7500)^{2} = (0.5333)^{2} = 0.2844$.

**Step 5:** $\arctan(0.2844) \approx 0.2768$ rad.

**Step 6:** Second term: $3.5 \times 0.2768 = 0.9688$.

**Step 7:** Sum: $z = 16.278 + 0.9688 = 17.247$ Bark.

**Interpretation:** A 4 kHz tone lies at $z \approx 17.2$ Bark — well **above 13.8 Bark**, so it enters the **high-frequency weighting region** of the sharpness integrand.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Sharpness of a Pure Tone at 4 kHz, 70 dB SPL

### Setup
A pure tone: $f = 4$ kHz, $L_p = 70$ dB SPL. Find $S$.

### Step A — Compute $z$ and $g(z)$
We already have $z = 17.247$ Bark. Because $z > 13.8$ Bark, the exponential weighting applies:

$$g(z) = 0.066 \cdot e^{0.171 \cdot (17.247 - 13.8)}$$

**Step A1:** $17.247 - 13.8 = 3.447$.

**Step A2:** $0.171 \times 3.447 = 0.5894$.

**Step A3:** $e^{0.5894} = 1.8030$.

**Step A4:** $g(z) = 0.066 \times 1.8030 = 0.1190$.

### Step B — Specific Loudness $N'$
For a pure tone at $z = 17.247$ Bark, $L_p = 70$ dB, the excitation level is roughly:

$$E(z) \approx L_p - a(z)$$

Where the attenuation factor from the tone's frequency to the excitation level is $a(z) \approx 7$ dB for the critical-band self-masking case. So:

$$E = 70 - 7 = 63 \text{ dB}.$$

Zwicker's specific-loudness approximation (simplified for $E > 40$ dB):

$$N' \approx 2^{\,(E - 40)/10}$$

$$N' \approx 2^{\,23/10} = 2^{2.3} = 4.92\ \text{sone/Bark}$$

The lobe is centered at $z = 17.247$ Bark and spans approximately $\pm 0.5$ Bark. We approximate $N'(z)$ as a triangle with peak $4.92$ sone/Bark:

$$N'(z) = \begin{cases} 4.92 - 9.84\,(z - 17.247), & z < 17.247 \\ 4.92 - 9.84\,(17.247 - z), & z > 17.247 \end{cases}$$

### Step C — Numerator and Denominator

**Denominator (total loudness, single tone):**

$$\int N'(z) dz = 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 4.92 = 2.46\ \text{sone}$$

(This is the well-known rule: a single 70 dB tone ≈ 1–2 sone.)

**Numerator:**

$$\int N'(z) g(z) z\, dz \approx g(17.247) \cdot z_{\text{peak}} \cdot \int N'(z)\, dz$$

$$\int N'(z) g(z) z\, dz \approx 0.1190 \times 17.247 \times 2.46$$

$$\int N'(z) g(z) z\, dz \approx 5.048\ \text{sone·Bark}$$

### Step D — Final Sharpness

$$S = 0.11 \times \dfrac{5.048}{2.46} = 0.11 \times 2.052 = 0.226\ \text{acum}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This *low* value for a *single isolated* 4 kHz tone is a classic **trap** in KTU exams. Sharpness depends on the *loudness distribution*, not just frequency. A 4 kHz tone produces localized excitation — but if the tone is **immersed in a broadband spectrum tilted toward high frequencies**, $S$ rises dramatically (because the *denominator* stays constant while the *high-frequency portion of the numerator* grows).

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Prominence Ratio of a 6 kWh Tire Cavity Tone

### Given
- SPL at the tone (1/3-octave band centered 200 Hz around 315 Hz): $L_p = 65$ dB
- Mean SPL of three adjacent bands (250, 315, 400 Hz center): $L_s = 52$ dB
- Tone bandwidth ≈ 1/12 octave → correction $\Delta L = +3$ dB

### Step 1
$$PR = L_p - L_s + \Delta L$$
$$PR = 65 - 52 + 3$$
$$\boxed{PR = 16\ \text{dB}}$$

### Step 2 — Interpret
$PR = 16$ dB ⇒ **highly conspicuous tone**. Driver complaint very likely.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Aures' Tonality for a 5 kHz Tone Embedded in 60 dB Pink Noise

### Given
- Pink-noise total loudness: $N = 4$ sone
- Tone loudness (extracted by masking the tone and re-computing): $N_t = 2$ sone
- Pure tone ⇒ $c = 1.0$

### Step 1 — Loudness ratio
$$\dfrac{N_t}{N} = \dfrac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

### Step 2 — Tonality
$$K = 1.0 \cdot \log_{10}(0.5) = -0.301\ \text{tu}$$

> [!WARNING]
> KTU students frequently **forget** that when $N_t < N$, $K$ becomes **negative**. Tonality is **signed**: negative ⇒ broadband-dominant, positive ⇒ tone-dominant. For noise control, you actually want a *positive* $K$ to *trigger* a tonal-noise flag in software.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Index Selection Decision Tree (Text-Mapped)

| Spectral Pattern | Best Index | Reason |
|------------------|------------|--------|
| Single pure tone + broadband | $K$, $PR$ | Detects the *conspicuous* peak |
| Many closely spaced harmonics (motor whine) | $PR$ + $S$ | Sidebands need PR; broadband $S$ rises |
| Hiss / broadband-only | $S$ | Tonality useless — no discrete peaks |
| Multi-tone (gear mesh + sidebands) | $PR$ + $TNR$ | Multiple PRs computed for each order |
| A-weighted SPL screening | All — final arbiter | dB(A) is correlated but not causal |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Frequency Tonal Noise — SQ Index Computational Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Microphone Signal x of t] --> B[A weighted SPL Lp]
    A --> C[FFT Spectrum L eq of f]
    C --> D[Critical Band Rate Conversion Hz to Bark]
    D --> E[Excitation Level E of z]
    E --> F[Specific Loudness N prime of z]
    F --> G[Total Loudness N]
    F --> H[Sharpness S via Zwicker integral]
    C --> I[Tonal Peak Detection]
    I --> J[Prominence Ratio PR]
    I --> K[Tone to Noise Ratio TNR]
    F --> L[Loudness with and without tone]
    L --> M[Aures Tonality K]
    G --> N[Psychoacoustic Annoyance PA]
    H --> N
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 SQ Index Decision-Flow for NVH Engineer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Problem Statement High frequency complaint] --> Q{Are there discrete spectral peaks above 2 kHz?}
    Q -- No --> R[Compute Sharpness S only]
    Q -- Yes --> S[Compute PR for each peak]
    S --> T{PR greater than 15 dB?}
    T -- Yes --> U[Compute Tonality K and TNR]
    T -- No --> V[Compute Sharpness S]
    U --> W[Combined Psychoacoustic Annoyance PA]
    V --> W
    R --> W
    W --> X[Targeted Countermeasure Frequency reshaping Absorption Active Noise Control]
```

## 4.3 Mapping of SQ Indexes to Human Auditory Attributes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AA[Acoustic Pressure Pa] --> AB[Equal Loudness Contour ISO 226]
    AB --> AC[Perceived Loudness N]
    AC --> AD[Sharpness S]
    AC --> AE[Tonality K]
    AA --> AF[Spectrum Shape]
    AF --> AD
    AF --> AE
    AD --> AG[Psychoacoustic Annoyance PA]
    AE --> AG
    AF --> AH[Prominence Ratio PR]
    AH --> AG
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SQ Analyzer Module

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Input: Time Signal from Head] --> B2[Block 1: ADC and Calibration]
    B2 --> B3[Block 2: 1/3 Octave FFT]
    B3 --> B4[Block 3: Bark Mapping and Excitation]
    B4 --> B5[Block 4: Loudness N and Specific Loudness N prime]
    B5 --> B6[Block 5: Sharpness Integrator]
    B3 --> B7[Block 6: Tonal Peak Extractor]
    B7 --> B8[Block 7: Prominence Ratio and TNR]
    B5 --> B9[Block 8: Aures Tonality K]
    B6 --> B10[Block 9: Annoyance Combiner]
    B8 --> B10
    B9 --> B10
    B10 --> B11[Output: SQ Vector equals S, K, PR, TNR, PA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block in **4.4** represents a *software module* in commercial SQ analyzers (e.g., **HEAD ArtemiS**, **Brüel & Kjær Sound Quality**, **Psychoacoustic Toolbox MATLAB**). The architecture shows *why* SQ is computationally heavier than dB(A) — it requires FFT, Bark mapping, *and* multiple integrations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Sharpness. Why is it the most critical SQ index for high-frequency tonal noise? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**
Sharpness $S$ (acum) is the psychoacoustic descriptor of the *high-frequency emphasis* of a sound, computed by Zwicker's formula weighting specific loudness $N'(z)$ with the steeply rising function $g(z) = 0.066 e^{0.171(z-13.8)}$ for $z > 13.8$ Bark. For high-frequency tonal noise (gear whine, tire cavity resonance), $S$ directly captures the *brightness* and *piercing* quality that dB(A) misses, making it the dominant index for HFTN diagnosis.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Prominence Ratio and Tonality. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**

| Aspect | Prominence Ratio (PR) | Tonality (K) |
|--------|----------------------|--------------|
| Definition | Tone SPL above smoothed adjacent bands | Ratio of tone loudness to broadband loudness |
| Unit | dB | dimensionless (tu) |
| Reference | ISO 7779 / ECMA-74 | Aures / Zwicker |
| Output | Single value per detected peak | Single global value |
| Sensitivity | Pure-tone only | Spectral shape of band |

PR is computed *peak-by-peak*; K is a *global* psychoacoustic descriptor. Both are needed for HFTN: PR isolates offending frequencies; K quantifies perceptual tone strength.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (Two Full Alternatives)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive Zwicker's sharpness formula starting from the definition of specific loudness $N'(z)$. State and explain the role of the weighting function $g(z)$ for high-frequency bands. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of $N'$:**
The specific loudness $N'(z)$ is the loudness per unit critical-band rate, evaluated along the basilar membrane. Mathematically:

$$N'(z) = \left(\dfrac{E(z)}{E_0}\right)^{\alpha} \cdot \Psi(z)$$

with $E_0$ the reference excitation level and $\alpha = 0.23$ for $E(z) < E_0$ and $\alpha = 0.23$ growing non-linearly above.

**Step 2 — Definition of $N$:**

$$N = \int_{0}^{24\ \text{Bark}} N'(z)\, dz \quad [\text{sone}]$$

**Step 3 — Weighted first moment:**
Sharpness is defined as the *Bark-weighted* first moment of $N'$:

$$S \propto \dfrac{\int z \cdot N'(z) \cdot g(z)\, dz}{\int N'(z)\, dz}$$

**Step 4 — Calibration constant:**
The constant **0.11** is chosen such that $S = 1$ acum for a **1 kHz, 60 dB pure tone**. This is Zwicker's calibration reference:

$$\boxed{S = 0.11 \cdot \dfrac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{24} N'(z)\, g(z)\, z\, dz}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{24} N'(z)\, dz} \quad [\text{acum}]}$$

**Step 5 — Role of $g(z)$:**
For $z \le 13.8$ Bark (≤ 4 kHz), $g(z) = 1$ — no extra weighting. For $z > 13.8$ Bark, $g(z)$ grows **exponentially** at $0.171$ per Bark:

$$g(z) = 0.066 \cdot e^{0.171(z - 13.8)}$$

This reflects the **equal-loudness contours** which show that for the *same* SPL, high-Bark tones are perceived as *more piercing*. Without $g(z)$, sharpness would under-predict high-frequency annoyance.

**(b)** A 6 kHz tonal component is detected in the cabin of an electric vehicle with $L_p = 72$ dB SPL. The background broadband level (smoothed across adjacent bands) is $L_s = 55$ dB. The tone is a single pure tone (no sidebands). Compute: (i) the critical-band rate $z$, (ii) the Sharpness $S$, (iii) the Prominence Ratio $PR$, and (iv) state whether the tone is *highly conspicuous*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Critical-band rate $z$ at 6000 Hz:**
$$z = 13\arctan(0.00076 \times 6000) + 3.5\arctan\!\left(\left(\dfrac{6000}{7500}\right)^{2}\right)$$

$$z = 13\arctan(4.56) + 3.5\arctan(0.64)$$

$$z = 13 \times 1.3557 + 3.5 \times 0.5699 = 17.624 + 1.995 = 19.619\ \text{Bark}$$

**(ii) Sharpness $S$:**
Compute $g(z)$:

$$g(z) = 0.066 \cdot e^{0.171 \times (19.619 - 13.8)} = 0.066 \cdot e^{0.9946} = 0.066 \times 2.704 = 0.1785$$

For a 72 dB pure tone at $z = 19.6$ Bark, excitation $E \approx 65$ dB. Then:

$$N' \approx 2^{(65 - 40)/10} = 2^{2.5} = 5.66\ \text{sone/Bark}$$

Approximating the loudness lobe as a triangle of width 1 Bark centered at $z = 19.62$:

$$\int N'(z)\, dz = 0.5 \times 1 \times 5.66 = 2.83\ \text{sone}$$

$$\int N'(z)\, g(z)\, z\, dz \approx g(z_{\text{peak}}) \cdot z_{\text{peak}} \cdot 2.83$$

$$\int N'(z)\, g(z)\, z\, dz \approx 0.1785 \times 19.62 \times 2.83 = 9.91\ \text{sone·Bark}$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.11 \times \dfrac{9.91}{2.83} = 0.385\ \text{acum}}$$

(Note: For a *single isolated* pure tone, sharpness is naturally low. In a real EV cabin, broadband road/wind noise adds to the denominator, *reducing* $S$ further while the tone contributes an isolated loudness peak — making the **PR** more diagnostic.)

**(iii) Prominence Ratio $PR$ (pure tone → $\Delta L = 0$):**

$$PR = L_p - L_s + 0 = 72 - 55 = 17\ \text{dB}$$

**(iv) Conspicuousness decision:**
