$PR = 17$ dB $> 15$ dB threshold ⇒ **highly conspicuous tone**. Engineering action: target the **motor-controller switching frequency** modulation (e.g., random PWM) or apply **active noise control** in the cabin at the 6 kHz frequency.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain Aures' Tonality index. Starting from the definition, derive its mathematical form. Why is $K$ a *signed* quantity, and what does a *negative* $K$ mean physically? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Aures' Tonality $K$ quantifies the perceptual *tone-ness* of a sound by comparing the loudness of the **tonal** part with the **total** loudness. A broadband (noiselike) sound yields $K \approx 0$; a pure tone dominates $K \to 1$.

**Derivation — Step 1:**
Decompose $N$ into a tonal part $N_t$ and a non-tonal part $N_b$:

$$N \approx N_t + N_b$$

**Derivation — Step 2:**
Define the loudness of the tone (computed by removing the broadband component via spectral smoothing and re-evaluating):

$$N_t = N - N_b$$

**Derivation — Step 3:**
Form the loudness ratio and take a logarithmic scaling (analogous to dB):

$$K = c \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_t}{N_b}\right)$$

Where $c$ depends on the tone bandwidth:
- $c = 1.0$ for $\Delta f < 1/3$ octave (pure tone)
- $c < 1$ for wider tones

**Why signed?** Because $\log_{10}(N_t/N_b)$ can be **positive** (tone loudness > broadband loudness) or **negative** (broadband loudness > tone loudness). A *negative* $K$ indicates a **broadband-dominant sound** — useful as a *neutral* baseline for soundscape engineering.

**Physical meaning of $K$:** It is the perceptual counterpart of the **SNR** in loudness-domain — i.e., a "tonality SNR."

**(b)** A gear-mesh measurement yields the following 1/3-octave SPLs around a 5 kHz tone:

| Band Center (Hz) | SPL (dB) |
|---|---|
| 4000 | 60 |
| 5000 (tone band) | 78 |
| 6300 | 62 |

Compute: (i) the *smoothed* adjacent-band average $L_s$, (ii) the prominence ratio $PR$ (use $\Delta L = +3$ dB for narrow band tone), (iii) the *Bark* value of the 5 kHz tone, (iv) the *qualitative* sharpness judgement (high or low?) with justification. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Smoothed adjacent-band average:**
Arithmetic mean of the two adjacent bands (4000 and 6300 Hz):

$$L_s = \dfrac{60 + 62}{2} = 61\ \text{dB}$$

**(ii) Prominence Ratio:**

$$PR = L_p - L_s + \Delta L = 78 - 61 + 3 = 20\ \text{dB}$$

$PR = 20$ dB $\gg 15$ dB threshold → **extremely conspicuous tone**. Driver will definitely complain.

**(iii) Critical-band rate $z$ at 5000 Hz:**

$$z = 13 \arctan(0.00076 \times 5000) + 3.5 \arctan\!\left(\left(\dfrac{5000}{7500}\right)^{2}\right)$$

$$z = 13 \arctan(3.80) + 3.5 \arctan(0.4444)$$

$$z = 13 \times 1.3140 + 3.5 \times 0.4158 = 17.082 + 1.455 = 18.537\ \text{Bark}$$

**(iv) Sharpness judgement:**
Since the tone lies at $z = 18.5$ Bark **and** the adjacent bands (4000 Hz → $z = 17.08$ Bark; 6300 Hz → $z = 19.78$ Bark) also contribute loudness, the *specific loudness* distribution is concentrated in the high-Bark region. $z > 13.8$ Bark for all bands ⇒ **g(z) exponential region active**. Therefore, **Sharpness will be HIGH** (qualitatively $S > 2$ acum). Justification: excitation is concentrated above 13.8 Bark, the exponential weighting amplifies its contribution to the sharpness integral, and the listener will perceive the gear-mesh tone as *sharp/piercing*. Quantitative refinements require FFT-based specific-loudness computation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on this topic (verified by KTU answer-key patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.11 calibration constant** in $S$ — answer becomes dimensionally correct but numerically wrong by 11×.
> 2. **Confusing dB(A) with sharpness** — sharpness is in **acum**, not dB. Never write "$S = 5$ dB".
> 3. **Using absolute pipe characters** `| |` in the sharpness integrand inside markdown tables — breaks the table in the digital answer sheet.
> 4. **Reporting PR without the smoothing correction** $\Delta L$ — examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 5. **Sign error in Aures' $K$** — writing $K = +0.301$ when $N_t/N < 1$ gives a *negative* value.
> 6. **Drawing a "diagram" of sharpness as a circle of arrows** — examiners want either a **schematic of the $g(z)$ curve**, or a **block flow of the SQ analyzer** (per Section 4 of this note).
> 7. **Mixing up Bark and Hz** — converting *only one way*; remember $z = 13\arctan(0.00076 f) + 3.5 \arctan((f/7500)^2)$.
> 8. **Treating $S$ as frequency-only** — Sharpness is a **loudness-weighted** metric; a *silent* 8 kHz tone has $S \to 0$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Sound Quality (SQ)** is the *psychoacoustic* characterization of noise, not just SPL.
- **High-Frequency Tonal Noise (HFTN)** = discrete narrow peaks in the **2–20 kHz** band — gear whine, motor, whistles, tire cavity.
- **Five indices to remember: S, K, PR, TNR, N′.**
- **Sharpness (S, acum)** = Zwicker's $0.11$-calibrated, $g(z)$-weighted first moment of $N'$.
- **$g(z) = 1$ for $z \le 13.8$ Bark**; **$g(z) = 0.066 e^{0.171(z - 13.8)}$ for $z > 13.8$ Bark**.
- **Critical-band rate $z$ (Bark)** is computed from $f$ (Hz) via Traunmüller's two-arctangent formula.
- **Aures' Tonality $K$** = $c \cdot \log_{10}(N_t/N_b)$; **signed** — negative ⇒ broadband dominant.
- **Prominence Ratio PR (dB)** = $L_p - L_s + \Delta L$ per ISO 7779; **threshold of audibility ≈ 7 dB**; **highly conspicuous ≈ 15 dB**.
- **TNR (dB)** = $L_{\text{tone}} - L_{\text{noise\,band}}$ in the tone's own critical band.
- **Specific Loudness $N'$** (sone/Bark) is the *density* of loudness along the basilar membrane.
- **Combined Psychoacoustic Annoyance** model combines $N$, $S$, $K$ with calibrated weights.
- For an **EV motor whine**: focus on $K$ + $S$ + $PR$.
- For **gear mesh diagnostics**: focus on $PR$ + $TNR$.
- For **HVAC duct whistle**: focus on $PR$ + $S$.
- For **broadband hiss (no tone)**: only $S$ matters; $K \to 0$.
- **Calibration references:**
  - Sharpness $S = 1$ acum for a **1 kHz, 60 dB tone**
  - Loudness $N = 1$ sone for a **1 kHz, 40 dB tone**
- **Units to remember:**
  - $S$ → **acum**
  - $N$ → **sone**, $N'$ → **sone/Bark**
  - $K$ → **tu** (tonality unit, dimensionless)
  - $PR$, $TNR$ → **dB**
- **Examiner's pitfall:** never confuse acum with dB; never use `|x|` notation in tables.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Sound Quality Indexes in NVH Analysis
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT755 - NOISE, VIBRATION AND HARSHNESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application of Sound Quality Indexes in NVH Analysis

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Sound Quality?

**Sound Quality (SQ)** is a branch of psychoacoustics that goes beyond simple **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** measurements to evaluate how a sound is *perceived* by the human ear and brain. While SPL quantifies *how loud* a sound is physically, Sound Quality quantifies *how good (or annoying) it sounds subjectively* to a human listener.

In NVH (Noise, Vibration, and Harshness) engineering, Sound Quality is the discipline of engineering a sound to match a target perception — for example, making a sports car "powerful," a luxury sedan "refined," or an electric vehicle "whisper-quiet."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> Sound Quality is defined as the *subjective response of a listener* (annoyance, pleasantness, comfort) to an acoustic stimulus, measured objectively through **psychoacoustic metrics** (also called Sound Quality Indexes or SQIs) that correlate with human perception.

## 1.2 What are Sound Quality Indexes (SQIs)?

**Sound Quality Indexes (SQIs)** are objective, calculable numerical quantities derived from a sound signal that predict the *perceptual* attributes of that sound. They bridge the gap between physical measurement (mic signal in Pa) and human perception (annoyance, sharpness, etc.).

| Index | Perceptual Attribute |
|---|---|
| **Loudness, $N$** | Perceived intensity |
| **Sharpness, $S$** | High-frequency "edge" |
| **Roughness, $R$** | Fast amplitude modulation |
| **Fluctuation Strength, $F$** | Slow amplitude modulation |
| **Tonality, $K$** | Tonal/pure-tone content |
| **Impulsiveness, $I$** | Impact/transient character |
| **Articulation Index (AI)** | Speech intelligibility |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Why SQIs Matter

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Coffee" Example:**
> Imagine two cups of coffee. Both have the same *temperature* (say 60 °C) — this is analogous to SPL. But one is smooth Arabica (pleasantly perceived) and the other is burnt Robusta (harsh, sharp, unpleasant). The "quality" of the experience depends on *more* than just intensity.
> **SPL = Temperature** (physical). **SQIs = Flavor profile** (perceptual).
> In a car, two engines at the *same* SPL can sound completely different — one sporty, one crude — because of differences in **sharpness, roughness, and tonality**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** A *lower* SPL does **not** always mean a *better* sounding product. Many buyers complain that EVs (electric vehicles) are "too quiet" or "boring" precisely because the engine roar — which sells emotion — is missing. Sound Quality allows engineers to *design* the desired sound, not merely minimize the SPL.

## 1.4 Why SQIs are Needed in Modern NVH

- **Regulatory limits** (dB) are saturated — almost all products pass them. *Differentiation* now comes from *quality*.
- **Customer perception** is the ultimate buying factor in automotive, HVAC, home appliances, and consumer electronics.
- **Active Sound Design (ASD)** and **Electric Vehicle Acoustic Vehicle Alerting Systems (AVAS)** are impossible without SQI inputs.
- **Subjective jury testing** is expensive and slow; SQIs provide rapid, repeatable objective surrogates.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Psychoacoustic Foundation

The human ear does not respond linearly to sound. Two key physiological facts govern all SQIs:

1. **Frequency selectivity** — The cochlea acts as a bank of band-pass filters, each tuned to a narrow region of the basilar membrane. These are modeled as **Critical Bands (CB)**, numbered in **Bark scale** (1 Bark = one CB, 24 Bark = entire audible range).
2. **Non-linear loudness perception** — A 10 dB increase is perceived as a *doubling* of loudness (Stevens' Power Law), not as a numerical doubling.

These two facts together produce every SQI.

## 2.2 Zwicker's Loudness Model (ISO 532-1 / DIN 45631)

**Loudness $N$** is the most fundamental SQI. Zwicker's model is the international standard (ISO 532-1).

**Step-by-step logic:**

- **Step 1:** Apply **A-weighting or Z-weighting** to obtain the free-field frequency response curve.
- **Step 2:** Divide the frequency range into **24 critical bands** (Bark scale $z = 0$ to $24$).
- **Step 3:** Calculate the **excitation pattern** $E(z)$ — the sound pressure level within each critical band, accounting for upward masking spread.
- **Step 4:** Apply a **non-linear transformation** to convert $E(z)$ to specific loudness $N'(z)$ (sone/Bark).
- **Step 5:** Integrate $N'(z)$ over all Bark bands to get total loudness:

$$
N = \int_{0}^{24 \text{ Bark}} N'(z)\, dz \quad [\text{sone}]
$$

**Specific loudness** (loudness per critical band) follows the relationship:
$$
N'(z) = c \cdot \left[ E(z) \cdot \left(1 + s \cdot 0.0456 \cdot 10^{0.0356\,E(z)} \right) \right]^{0.23} \quad [\text{sone/Bark}]
$$

where $s$ is a scaling factor for the band and $E(z)$ is in phons (loudness level).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit convention:** $1 \text{ sone} = 40 \text{ phons}$ at 1 kHz. Doubling of sone ≈ 10 phon increase. This is exam-critical.

## 2.3 Sharpness (DIN 45692, Aures / Bismarck / von Bismarck models)

**Sharpness $S$** measures the *centroid* of loudness on the Bark scale — i.e., how much the loudness is concentrated in the high-frequency critical bands.

**Why it matters:** A sound rich in 2–5 kHz content (where the ear is most sensitive) is perceived as "sharp," "harsh," or "edgy." Gear whine, brake squeal, and turbo whistle have high sharpness.

$$
S = 0.11 \, \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{24} N'(z) \cdot g(z) \cdot z \, dz}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{24} N'(z) \, dz} \quad [\text{acum}]
$$

where the weighting function $g(z)$ boosts high-band contributions:
$$
g(z) = \begin{cases} 1 & z \le 13.6 \text{ Bark} \\ 10^{(z - 13.6)/5} & z > 13.6 \text{ Bark} \end{cases}
$$

**Unit:** **acum** (acum = sharpness unit). Typical values:
- $S < 1$ acum → dull, soft
- $S = 1$–$2$ acum → neutral
- $S > 2.5$ acum → sharp, annoying

## 2.4 Roughness $R$ (Daniel & Weber, 1997)

**Roughness $R$** quantifies the perception of fast amplitude modulation (AM), typically in the range **$f_{\text{mod}} = 15$ Hz to $300$ Hz** (peak sensitivity near **70 Hz**).

**Why it matters:** Engine firing at idle, electric motor torque ripple, and gearbox rattle produce roughness. It is the dominant "annoyance" metric for diesel engines.

$$
R = 0.3 \, f_{\text{mod}} \int_{0}^{24} \frac{\Delta L_E(z) \, dz}{(f_{\text{mod}}^2 + (0.3\,f_{\text{mod}})^2)} \quad [\text{asper}]
$$

**Unit:** **asper** (1 asper ≈ threshold of roughness). Typical:
- $R < 0.5$ asper → smooth
- $R = 1$ asper → clearly rough
- $R > 2$ asper → very rough/annoying

> [!NOTE]
> **Roughness vs Impulsiveness:** Roughness = *fast* modulation (>15 Hz). Impulsiveness = *single, isolated* transient events (impacts, clicks). They are *not* the same.

## 2.5 Fluctuation Strength $F$ (Fastl & Zwicker)

**Fluctuation Strength $F$** quantifies *slow* AM perception, for modulation frequencies $f_{\text{mod}} < 20$ Hz (peak sensitivity at **4 Hz**).

**Why it matters:** Helicopter blade slap, HVAC cycling, and combustion instabilities produce fluctuation. It is the key metric for "wobble" or "pulsing" complaints.

$$
F = \frac{0.008 \displaystyle\int_{0}^{24} \Delta L_E(z)\, dz}{(f_{\text{mod}}/4) + (4/f_{\text{mod}})} \quad [\text{vacil}]
$$

**Unit:** **vacil**.

## 2.6 Tonality $K$ — Prominence Ratio & Tone-to-Noise Ratio

**Tonality $K$** captures the presence of *distinct tones* (pure tones, harmonics) emerging from a noise background.

Two standard approaches:
1. **Prominence Ratio (PR)** — difference in dB between the tone peak and the average of its 5 adjacent critical bands:
$$
\text{PR} = L_{\text{tone}} - \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} L_{\text{adj},i} \quad [\text{dB}]
$$

2. **Tone-to-Noise Ratio (TNR):**
$$
\text{TNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{tone}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right) \quad [\text{dB}]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A-weighting cannot replace tonality.** A 1 kHz tone at 60 dB(A) is far more annoying than broadband noise at 60 dB(A). Tonality is required by regulations (e.g., ECMA-74, ISO 7779) for IT equipment noise testing.

## 2.7 Impulsiveness $I$ and Articulation Index (AI)

- **Impulsiveness, $I$:** Detected by comparing the *short-term* (40 ms) SPL with the *long-term* SPL. A *spike* of $\Delta L > 6$ dB is classified as impulsive. Unitless or dB.
- **Articulation Index (AI):** Predicts the percentage of speech cues that survive masking. Used in **office acoustics** and **voice communication** design (in-cabin voice assistants).

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | SQI | Formula (compact) | Unit | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Loudness** | $N = \int_{0}^{24} N'(z)\,dz$ | **sone** | $N$ | 0–$\infty$ |
| 2 | **Sharpness** | $S = 0.11\,\dfrac{\int N'(z)\,g(z)\,z\,dz}{\int N'(z)\,dz}$ | **acum** | $S$ | 0.5–4 |
| 3 | **Roughness** | $R = 0.3\,f_{\text{mod}}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{\Delta L_E(z)\,dz}{f_{\text{mod}}^2 + (0.3 f_{\text{mod}})^2}$ | **asper** | $R$ | 0–4 |
| 4 | **Fluctuation Strength** | $F = 0.008\displaystyle\int \dfrac{\Delta L_E(z)\,dz}{f_{\text{mod}}/4 + 4/f_{\text{mod}}}$ | **vacil** | $F$ | 0–4 |
| 5 | **Prominence Ratio** | $\text{PR} = L_{\text{tone}} - \dfrac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} L_{\text{adj},i}$ | **dB** | PR | 0–15 |
| 6 | **Tone-to-Noise Ratio** | $\text{TNR} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{tone}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | **dB** | TNR | 0–25 |
| 7 | **Stevens' Power Law** | $\psi = k\,I^n$ (n≈0.67 for SPL) | — | $\psi$ | — |
| 8 | **A-weighting ref.** | $A(f) = \dfrac{12194^2 f^4}{(f^2 + 20.6^2)(f^2 + 12194^2)\sqrt{(f^2+107.7^2)(f^2+737.9^2)}}$ | dB | $A$ | -∞ to +∞ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These SQIs are *the* language of NVH reports in industry. OEMs (Tata, Mahindra, Toyota, BMW) set target ranges for each SQI at every operating point (idle, WOT, cruise). Exceeding the target = subjective failure regardless of how good the SPL is.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stevens' Power Law (Foundation of Loudness)

**Statement:** The perceived loudness $\psi$ (in sone) is related to the physical intensity $I$ (in W/m²) by a power law.

**Step 1:** Begin with the *sone-phon* definition.
By definition, a sound of $L = 40$ phons (1 kHz tone) has loudness $\psi_0 = 1$ sone.

**Step 2:** A 10 dB increase corresponds to a doubling of sone.
By definition:
$$
\psi = 2^{(L - 40)/10} \quad [\text{sone}]
$$

**Step 3:** Express in terms of intensity $I$.
The reference intensity is $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m². The SPL is:
$$
L = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{I}{I_0} = 10^{L/10}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the sone equation:
$$
\psi = 2^{(L - 40)/10} = 2^{\log_{10}(I/I_0)/10 - 4}
$$

**Step 5:** Use the identity $2^{\log_{10} x} = x^{\log_{10} 2}$ and note $\log_{10} 2 \approx 0.3010$.

$$
\psi = \left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)^{0.3010} \cdot 2^{-4} = \left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)^{0.3010} \cdot \frac{1}{16}
$$

**Step 6:** Combining the constants $1/(16 I_0^{0.301})$ into a single constant $k$:
$$
\boxed{\;\psi = k \cdot I^{0.301} \;\approx\; k \cdot I^{0.3}\;}
$$

This is **Stevens' Power Law** for loudness (often the exponent is written as $0.67$ for $L$ vs $I$, equivalent form). It is the mathematical basis of every A-weighting and Zwicker loudness curve.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Zwicker Specific Loudness (from Excitation Pattern)

The specific loudness $N'(z)$ in any critical band $z$ depends on the excitation level $E(z)$ (in phons, the loudness level of the band). The Zwicker formula is:

$$
N'(z) = c \cdot \left[ E(z) \cdot \left(1 + s\,b\,10^{0.0356\,E(z)} \right) \right]^{0.23}
$$

**Derivation logic:**

**Step 1 — Threshold correction:** Pure tones have an absolute hearing threshold that must be subtracted.
Define $E_{\text{THR}}(z)$ as the threshold excitation in band $z$. Then the *effective* excitation is:
$$
E_{\text{eff}}(z) = E(z) - E_{\text{THR}}(z)
$$

**Step 2 — Slope correction:** Above 40 phons, masking curves have a slope of about 1 dB/dB. Below 40 phons, the slope is steeper. The slope factor $s$ accounts for this:
$$
s = \begin{cases} 0 & E(z) \le 4 \text{ phons} \\ \left(\dfrac{E(z) - 4}{30}\right) & 4 < E(z) < 34 \\ 1 & E(z) \ge 34 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Above-threshold amplification:** The bracketed term $\left(1 + s\,b\,10^{0.0356 E}\right)$ represents the *gain* in loudness due to the band being well above threshold. The constant $b = 0.0456$ was fitted empirically from Zwicker's loudness-matching experiments.

**Step 4 — Power compression:** The exponent $0.23$ compresses the loudness range so that a 10 phon increase (a factor of $10^{0.0356 \cdot 10} = 2.24$ in the inner term) becomes a doubling of sone.

**Step 5 — Total loudness** by integrating over all 24 critical bands:
$$
N = \int_{0}^{24} N'(z)\, dz
$$

This derivation is the *core exam derivation* for any KTU question on the Zwicker model.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — A-Weighting Curve from the 40-phon Equal-Loudness Contour

The A-weighting filter is an electrical approximation of the *inverse of the 40-phon Fletcher-Munson equal-loudness contour*, normalized to 1 kHz.

**Step 1:** The 40-phon contour is given (empirically) as a function of frequency:
$$
L_{40}(f) = a(f) \quad [\text{dB SPL needed for 40 phons}]
$$

**Step 2:** The A-weighting is defined as the *negative* of this contour, normalized:
$$
A(f) = L_{40}(1\,\text{kHz}) - L_{40}(f) = 0 - L_{40}(f) = -L_{40}(f) \quad [\text{dB}]
$$

**Step 3:** The transfer function magnitude in analytic form is:
$$
A(f) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left[ \frac{f_4^2 \cdot f^2}{\sqrt{f^2 + f_1^2}\sqrt{f^2 + f_2^2}\,(f^2 + f_3^2)} \right]
$$

where (in Hz): $f_1 = 20.6$, $f_2 = 107.7$, $f_3 = 737.9$, $f_4 = 12194$.

**Step 4 — Quick numerical checks (board favourites):**

| Frequency $f$ (Hz) | $A(f)$ (dB) |
|---|---|
| 31.5 | -39.4 |
| 63 | -26.2 |
| 125 | -16.1 |
| 250 | -8.6 |
| 500 | -3.2 |
| **1000** | **0 (reference)** |
| 2000 | +1.2 |
| 4000 | +1.0 |
| 8000 | -1.1 |
| 16000 | -6.6 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Hint:** Most students lose marks by stating the A-weighting formula incorrectly. Memorize the **four corner frequencies** ($20.6, 107.7, 737.9, 12194$ Hz) and the reference dB at 1 kHz = 0.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Sharpness Calculation (Bark Method)

A broadband engine sound has the following *specific loudness* in three critical bands:

| Band $z$ (Bark) | $N'(z)$ (sone/Bark) | $g(z)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | 1.0 | 1 |
| 10 | 1.5 | 1 |
| 20 | 2.0 | $10^{(20-13.6)/5} = 10^{1.28} = 19.05$ |

Compute the sharpness $S$.

**Step 1:** Compute the numerator:
$$
\text{Num} = \int N'(z)\,g(z)\,z\,dz \approx \sum N'(z)\,g(z)\,z\,\Delta z
$$
Using $\Delta z = 1$ Bark for each band:
$$
\text{Num} = (1.0)(1)(5) + (1.5)(1)(10) + (2.0)(19.05)(20)
$$
$$
= 5 + 15 + 762 = 782
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the denominator:
$$
\text{Den} = \int N'(z)\,dz \approx 1.0 + 1.5 + 2.0 = 4.5
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the sharpness formula:
$$
S = 0.11 \cdot \frac{782}{4.5} = 0.11 \cdot 173.78 = 19.1 \,\text{acum}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
$S = 19.1$ acum is **extremely sharp** (typical: passenger car interior ranges $1.5$–$3$ acum). The third band (z=20 Bark, ≈ 6 kHz) dominates. **Design action:** filter or damp the 6 kHz region.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sound Quality Analysis — End-to-End Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Microphone Signal xPaT] --> B[Time-Frequency STFT]
    B --> C[Critical Band Filter Bank 24 Bark]
    C --> D[Excitation Pattern Ez in phons]
    D --> E1[Loudness N sone]
    D --> E2[Sharpness S acum]
    D --> E3[Roughness R asper]
    D --> E4[Fluctuation F vacil]
    D --> E5[Tonality K dB]
    B --> F1[Modulation Spectrum 4 to 300 Hz]
    F1 --> E3
    F1 --> E4
    E1 --> G[Multivariate SQI Vector]
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    E4 --> G
    E5 --> G
    G --> H[Subjective Jury Correlation]
    H --> I{Target Met}
    I -->|Yes| J[Release Product]
    I -->|No| K[Active Sound Design / Source Modification]
    K --> B
```

## 4.2 Zwicker Psychoacoustic Model — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input pLt] --> B[A or Z Pre-Weighting Filter]
    B --> C[Critical Band Filter Bank 24 Bands]
    C --> D[Masking Spread Function EfT]
    D --> E[Excitation Pattern Ez]
    E --> F[Threshold Correction Ethrz]
    F --> G[Non-linear Transform N prime z]
    G --> H[Integrate over 0 to 24 Bark]
    H --> I[Total Loudness N in sone]
    E --> J[Sharpness Weighting gz times z]
    J --> K[Divide by Total Loudness]
    K --> L[Sharpness S in acum]
    E --> M[Modulation Detector 4 to 300 Hz]
    M --> N[Roughness R in asper]
    M --> O[Fluctuation F in vacil]
```

## 4.3 SQI Decision Map — Choosing the Right Index

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify NVH Complaint] --> Q1{Is the issue loudness only?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Loud[Use Loudness N and Zwicker]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is sound hissy or edgy?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Sharp[Use Sharpness S]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is sound rattly or buzzy?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Rough[Use Roughness R]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is sound pulsing or wobbling?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Fluc[Use Fluctuation Strength F]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Is there a distinct whine or tone?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Ton[Use Tonality K and Prominence Ratio]
    Q5 -->|No| Q6{Is the sound impulsive or clicking?}
    Q6 -->|Yes| Imp[Use Impulsiveness I]
    Q6 -->|No| Multi[Use Multivariate SQI Vector with PCA]
```

## 4.4 Application Map — Where Each SQI is Used in Industry

| Application Domain | Dominant SQI | Engineering Goal |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive engine (Diesel) | **Roughness, R** | Reduce combustion modulation |
| Automotive engine (Petrol) | **Sharpness, S** | Refine intake/exhaust timbre |
| EV Interior (motor whine) | **Tonality, K** | Mask 8 kHz / 16 kHz switching tones |
| EV Exterior AVAS | **Loudness + Tonality** | Pedestrian alert without annoyance |
| HVAC / Refrigerator | **Fluctuation, F** | Eliminate compressor cycling |
| Power tools / Hand drills | **Roughness, R + Sharpness, S** | Lower operator fatigue |
| Wind turbine noise | **Tonality, K (low freq.)** | Avoid amplitude modulation complaints |
| Office equipment (printers) | **Impulsiveness, I** | Damp paper-feed impacts |
| Aircraft cabin | **Loudness + Articulation Index** | Comfort + speech intelligibility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three-Step Method for any NVH SQI Complaint:**
> 1. **Record** the sound (head or binaural microphones, 44.1 kHz minimum).
> 2. **Compute** the full SQI vector using software (ArtemiS SUITE, HEAD Acoustics, Brüel & Kjær Sound Quality, Loudness.online).
> 3. **Correlate** with jury tests (e.g., Pearson $r > 0.8$ required) and act on the worst SQI.
